with qζ′′qζ′′​ as a correction.

Ok continue pushing

I pushed it further, and the situation is now pretty clear.

The six-jet statistic is real, explicit, and simpler than before — but it still does not collapse to a pure value statistic by any obvious generic symmetry. The best way to keep pushing is to isolate the combinations of endpoint jets that appear, then either neutralize the q′q′-sector by a refined statistic or exploit a local symmetry of the zeta profile.

Here is the clean next layer.
1. Rewrite the six-jet form in symmetric/antisymmetric coordinates

2. What actually dominates?

Now the answer is sharper.
Value sector

The value term depends only on
z+=qζ(T1)+qζ(T2).
z+​=qζ​(T1​)+qζ​(T2​).

Its contribution is
1s2∥A∥F2 z+2,∥A∥F2=4−cos⁡(2Δˉ)2Q4.
s21​∥A∥F2​z+2​,∥A∥F2​=2Q44−cos(2Δˉ)​.

So the clean leading value-only piece is
4−cos⁡(2Δˉ)2Q4 s2 z+2.
2Q4s24−cos(2Δˉ)​z+2​.​
First-derivative sector

The first derivatives split into two geometrically different channels:

    p+=p1+p2p+​=p1​+p2​, coupled by B+B+​, which is proportional to sin⁡ΔˉsinΔˉ;

    p−=p1−p2p−​=p1​−p2​, coupled by B−B−​, which has both cos⁡ΔˉcosΔˉ and sin⁡ΔˉsinΔˉ pieces.

So unless some special relation is imposed, the q′q′-sector is genuinely present at the same Q−2Q−2/Q−3Q−3 order as the value sector.
Second-derivative sector

The q′′q′′-sector comes with Q−4Q−4, so it is one background order smaller. It is real, but subleading in the slowly varying-background regime.

So the most honest summary is:
After phase-gap subtraction, the dominant six-jet statistic is values plus first derivatives; second derivatives are lower order.
After phase-gap subtraction, the dominant six-jet statistic is values plus first derivatives; second derivatives are lower order.​

That is the first important simplification.
3. Special regimes that actually simplify

There are three useful ones.
Regime A: slowly varying zeta perturbation

If
∣pj∣≪Q∣zj∣,∣rj∣≪Q2∣zj∣,
∣pj​∣≪Q∣zj​∣,∣rj​∣≪Q2∣zj​∣,

then the value term dominates and
R~\sym(δ)[×]≈4−cos⁡(2Δˉ)2Q4 s2 z+2.
R
\sym(δ)​[×]≈2Q4s24−cos(2Δˉ)​z+2​.

In this regime the statistic is basically a weighted square of
qζ(T1)+qζ(T2).
qζ​(T1​)+qζ​(T2​).
Regime B: symmetric local spike around a midpoint

Suppose the local zeta feature is approximately symmetric about the midpoint m=(T1+T2)/2m=(T1​+T2​)/2. Then
z1≈z2,p1≈−p2,r1≈r2.
z1​≈z2​,p1​≈−p2​,r1​≈r2​.

So
z−≈0,p+≈0,r−≈0.
z−​≈0,p+​≈0,r−​≈0.

Then the statistic simplifies to
L~\sym(qζ)≈1s(Az++B−p−+C+r+).
L
\sym​(qζ​)≈s1​(Az+​+B−​p−​+C+​r+​).

This is useful because one entire derivative channel p+p+​ disappears automatically. You are left with the value sector, one odd derivative sector, and one even second-derivative sector.
Regime C: choose ΔˉΔˉ specially

The background phase gap matters.

If
sin⁡Δˉ≈0,
sinΔˉ≈0,

then B+B+​ vanishes to first order, so the symmetric derivative channel is suppressed.

If
cos⁡Δˉ≈0,
cosΔˉ≈0,

then parts of AA, B−B−​, and C±C±​ simplify, but the derivative sector does not disappear entirely.

So the best practical simplification is not just special ΔˉΔˉ, but special ΔˉΔˉ plus symmetric local placement.
4. Does zeta give an arithmetic identity on these six jets?

Still not from the explicit formula alone.

The explicit formula gives
qζ(t)=Bζ(t)+∑ρfβρ,γρ(t),
qζ​(t)=Bζ​(t)+ρ∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t),

so
zj, pj, rj
zj​, pj​, rj​

are sums of Poisson-pair values and derivatives.

That gives positivity and decay information, but not a universal linear relation among
z+,p+,p−,r+,r−.
z+​,p+​,p−​,r+​,r−​.

So the explicit formula alone does not force the six-jet quadratic form to vanish or collapse.

What it does suggest is that the most arithmetic-looking quantity left is the value sum
z+=qζ(T1)+qζ(T2),
z+​=qζ​(T1​)+qζ​(T2​),

because qζqζ​ itself is a sum of positive Poisson-pair contributions from zeros. If one wants a prime-side relation later, that is probably the first sector to inspect.

So the arithmetic bottleneck has narrowed from seven quantities to, effectively, three channels:
z+,p−,r+,
z+​,p−​,r+​,

with p+p+​ and r−r−​ removable or suppressible under symmetric local placement.

That is progress.
5. What I think the next concrete move should be

There are two good options now.
Option 1: design a refined statistic that kills p−p−​

Because the value sector and the antisymmetric first-derivative sector are the two main survivors under symmetric placement, the cleanest next statistic would be one that cancels the p−p−​-contribution.

A natural way is to take a second nearby symmetric configuration and solve a 2×22×2 linear system to eliminate p−p−​ while keeping z+z+​.

For example, use two separations
s1, s2
s1​, s2​

around the same midpoint. Then you get two scalar statistics
R~\sym(δ)(s1),R~\sym(δ)(s2).
R
\sym(δ)​(s1​),R
\sym(δ)​(s2​).

Their leading coefficients depend differently on the background gap Δˉ(s)Δˉ(s), so in principle you can combine them to suppress the derivative channel.

That is the cleanest purely geometric next move.
Option 2: look for arithmetic control of z+z+​

If you do not redesign the statistic, then the simplest leading term is already
4−cos⁡(2Δˉ)2Q4 s2 z+2.
2Q4s24−cos(2Δˉ)​z+2​.

So the next arithmetic question becomes:

Can the zeta phase derivative
qζ(T1)+qζ(T2)
qζ​(T1​)+qζ​(T2​)

be related or bounded in some special way by the Euler product or explicit formula in the local regimes of interest?

That is much more concrete than asking for a mysterious identity in all six jets.
Bottom line

After pushing further, the picture is:
R~\sym(δ)[×]=1s2∥Az++B+p++B−p−+C+r++C−r−∥F2+smaller terms.
R
\sym(δ)​[×]=s21​∥Az+​+B+​p+​+B−​p−​+C+​r+​+C−​r−​∥F2​+smaller terms.​

And in the most promising symmetric/slowly-varying regime, this reduces further to roughly
R~\sym(δ)[×]≈1s2∥Az++B−p−+C+r+∥F2.
R
\sym(δ)​[×]≈s21​∥Az+​+B−​p−​+C+​r+​∥F2​.​

So the problem has narrowed from a seven-variable mess to a very explicit three-channel competition:

    value sum z+z+​,

    antisymmetric first derivative p−p−​,

    symmetric second derivative r+r+​.

That is the cleanest place we have reached so far.

The next best move is probably to build the p−p−​-neutralized statistic using two symmetric separations.

Ok lets try all options

Good. Let’s try all of them, but in the order that gives the fastest signal.

I would do:

    the p−p−​-neutralized statistic,

    the z+z+​ arithmetic route,

    then the r+r+​ channel.

Because if we can kill p−p−​, the whole picture simplifies a lot.
1. Neutralize p−p−​ using two symmetric separations

We are in the phase-gap-subtracted, symmetric-placement, slowly varying-background regime, where the leading form is
R~\sym(δ)(s)≈1s2∥A(s) z++B−(s) p−+C+(s) r+∥F2.

\sym(δ)​(s)≈s21​
​A(s)z+​+B−​(s)p−​+C+​(s)r+​
​F2​.

This is awkward because it is a squared norm, so the channels mix quadratically. But to first order in the matrix itself, define
Ω~(s):=Ω×(s)−Ω\sym(s)−D(s) δ.
Ω
(s):=Ω×​(s)−Ω\sym​(s)−D(s)δ.

Ω~(s)≈1s(A(s) z++B−(s) p−+C+(s) r+).

sk Ω~(sk)≈Akz++Bkp−+Ckr+,k=1,2,

Now choose scalars λ1,λ2λ1​,λ2​ so that
λ1B1+λ2B2≈0.

∥λ1B1+λ2B2∥F.
∥λ1​B1​+λ2​B2​∥F​.

Ω~val(s1,s2):=λ1s1Ω~(s1)+λ2s2Ω~(s2).

So this is the first refined statistic: it suppresses the antisymmetric first-derivative channel.

Then take the scalar
Rval(s1,s2):=∥Ω~val(s1,s2)∥F2.
Rval​(s1​,s2​):=∥Ω
val​(s1​,s2​)∥F2​.​

This is now much closer to a value-dominated statistic.

A symmetric companion can be built to suppress z+z+​ instead and isolate p−p−​:
Ω~der(s1,s2):=μ1s1Ω~(s1)+μ2s2Ω~(s2),
Ω
der​(s1​,s2​):=μ1​s1​Ω
(s1​)+μ2​s2​Ω
(s2​),

with μ1,μ2μ1​,μ2​ chosen to minimize
∥μ1A1+μ2A2∥F.
∥μ1​A1​+μ2​A2​∥F​.

Then
Ω~der≈(μ1B1+μ2B2)p−+(μ1C1+μ2C2)r+.
Ω
der​≈(μ1​B1​+μ2​B2​)p−​+(μ1​C1​+μ2​C2​)r+​.

So the first option produces a pair of nearly separated matrix statistics:

    one mostly value-based,

    one mostly derivative-based.

That is the cleanest geometric refinement.
2. The z+z+​ arithmetic route

Now suppose we manage to make the statistic mostly sensitive to
z+=qζ(T1)+qζ(T2).
z+​=qζ​(T1​)+qζ​(T2​).

What can arithmetic say about this?

Using the explicit formula,
qζ(t)=Bζ(t)+∑ρfβρ,γρ(t),
qζ​(t)=Bζ​(t)+ρ∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t),

so
z+=Bζ(T1)+Bζ(T2)+∑ρ(fρ(T1)+fρ(T2)).
z+​=Bζ​(T1​)+Bζ​(T2​)+ρ∑​(fρ​(T1​)+fρ​(T2​)).

A few things are immediate.
Positivity

Each fρ(t)fρ​(t) is positive. So apart from the archimedean/background term,
z+
z+​

is a positive sum over zeros.

That means cancellations here are much more constrained than in p−p−​, where derivatives can change sign.
Symmetric placement

If T1,T2T1​,T2​ are placed symmetrically around a local zero cluster, then z+z+​ is approximately measuring the average local zeta phase density around that midpoint.

That makes it a natural arithmetic channel.
Distant zero suppression

For a distant zero at height γγ,
fρ(T1)+fρ(T2)=O(Γ−2),
fρ​(T1​)+fρ​(T2​)=O(Γ−2),

where ΓΓ is the distance from γγ to the doubled window. So the value channel is genuinely local after phase-gap subtraction.

So if we can isolate z+z+​, the remaining question becomes:

    Can the actual zeta phase density qζqζ​ be too small, too large, or too regular in a way incompatible with an off-line local model?

That is much more concrete than the original seven-variable question.

I do not see a proof yet, but this is the first channel where prime/zero positivity might plausibly matter.
3. The p−p−​ channel

Now for
p−=qζ′(T1)−qζ′(T2).
p−​=qζ′​(T1​)−qζ′​(T2​).

This is the odd derivative channel under symmetric placement, and it is the main nuisance competing with z+z+​.

From the explicit formula,
qζ′(t)=Bζ′(t)+∑ρfρ′(t),
qζ′​(t)=Bζ′​(t)+ρ∑​fρ′​(t),

and each fρ′fρ′​ changes sign across its center. So p−p−​ is measuring local asymmetry of the zeta phase density.

That makes it much less universal than z+z+​. It is sensitive to where the midpoint sits relative to local zero features.

That suggests two strategies:

    either suppress it geometrically using the two-separation trick above,

    or deliberately use it as a detector of local asymmetry.

My feeling is that for RH-facing purposes, suppressing it is better.
4. The r+r+​ channel

Finally
r+=qζ′′(T1)+qζ′′(T2).
r+​=qζ′′​(T1​)+qζ′′​(T2​).

This is lower order in the slowly varying-background regime, and after phase-gap subtraction it is the most plausible one to leave as an error term.

Since
qζ′′(t)=Bζ′′(t)+∑ρfρ′′(t),
qζ′′​(t)=Bζ′′​(t)+ρ∑​fρ′′​(t),

and distant contributions are O(Γ−4)O(Γ−4), this channel is genuinely more local and more suppressed.

So my practical recommendation is:

    do not try to eliminate r+r+​ first,

    treat it as a smaller correction while designing a statistic that kills p−p−​.

5. A concrete next statistic

Here is the most actionable version.

Choose two symmetric separations s1,s2s1​,s2​, same midpoint.

Compute the phase-gap-subtracted matrices
Ω~k:=Ω~(sk),k=1,2.
Ω
k​:=Ω
(sk​),k=1,2.

Then define the value-optimized combination by solving
(λ1,λ2)=arg⁡min⁡λ12+λ22=1∥λ1B−(s1)+λ2B−(s2)∥F.
(λ1​,λ2​)=argλ12​+λ22​=1min​∥λ1​B−​(s1​)+λ2​B−​(s2​)∥F​.

Set
Ω~val:=λ1s1Ω~1+λ2s2Ω~2.
Ω
val​:=λ1​s1​Ω
1​+λ2​s2​Ω
2​.​

Then the leading form is
Ω~val≈Aeffz++Ceffr++small p− term,
Ω
val​≈Aeff​z+​+Ceff​r+​+small p−​ term,

where
Aeff:=λ1A(s1)+λ2A(s2),Ceff:=λ1C+(s1)+λ2C+(s2).
Aeff​:=λ1​A(s1​)+λ2​A(s2​),Ceff​:=λ1​C+​(s1​)+λ2​C+​(s2​).

Finally define
Rval:=∥Ω~val∥F2.
Rval​:=∥Ω
val​∥F2​.​

This is the best “all options” statistic so far.

It uses:

    phase-gap subtraction,

    two-scale/separation cancellation of p−p−​,

    and leaves mostly the value channel z+z+​, with r+r+​ as a smaller correction.

6. What I think the next actual step should be

The best immediate next step is not more abstract reasoning. It is:

    compute A(s),B−(s),C+(s)A(s),B−​(s),C+​(s) numerically or symbolically for two chosen separations;

    build the minimizing (λ1,λ2)(λ1​,λ2​);

    inspect whether AeffAeff​ is still robust while the p−p−​-coefficient is strongly reduced.

If that works, then the arithmetic bottleneck really does reduce to understanding z+z+​ plus a smaller r+r+​ correction.

That would be the first point where the program becomes focused enough to either succeed or fail cleanly.

So: trying all options points to one best combined route — phase-gap subtraction + two-separation derivative suppression + value-channel analysis.

Ok try them one by one

I’m starting with the two-separation p−p−​-suppression first, because that is the one most likely to simplify the whole picture quickly. Then I’ll move to the z+z+​ channel and finally the r+r+​ remainder.

I tried them one by one.

The short answer is:

    Two-separation p−p−​-suppression is partially workable, but not as a perfect cancellation with only two scalar weights. It can reduce the p−p−​ channel, not eliminate it generically.

    The z+z+​ route is the cleanest leading channel in the smooth-background regime.

    The r+r+​ channel really is lower order and should be treated as a remainder unless you sit very near a sharp local spike.

Here is the clean version.
1. Two-separation p−p−​-suppression

After phase-gap subtraction, in the large-ss, slowly varying-background regime, we had
Ω~(s)≈1s(A(s) z++B−(s) p−+C+(s) r+),
Ω
(s)≈s1​(A(s)z+​+B−​(s)p−​+C+​(s)r+​),

where
z+=z1+z2,p−=p1−p2,r+=r1+r2.
z+​=z1​+z2​,p−​=p1​−p2​,r+​=r1​+r2​.

The key structural fact is that these matrices depend on the background phase gap Δˉ(s)Δˉ(s) only through sin⁡ΔˉsinΔˉ and cos⁡ΔˉcosΔˉ. More explicitly,
A(s)=sin⁡Δˉ(s) AS+cos⁡Δˉ(s) AC,
A(s)=sinΔˉ(s)AS​+cosΔˉ(s)AC​,
B−(s)=sin⁡Δˉ(s) BS+cos⁡Δˉ(s) BC,
B−​(s)=sinΔˉ(s)BS​+cosΔˉ(s)BC​,
C+(s)=sin⁡Δˉ(s) CS+cos⁡Δˉ(s) CC,
C+​(s)=sinΔˉ(s)CS​+cosΔˉ(s)CC​,

with fixed QQ-dependent coefficient matrices AS,AC,BS,BC,CS,CCAS​,AC​,BS​,BC​,CS​,CC​.

That means the whole family B−(s)B−​(s) lives in a 2-dimensional matrix span. So with two separations s1,s2s1​,s2​, we can form
Ω~λ:=λ1s1Ω~(s1)+λ2s2Ω~(s2),
Ω
λ​:=λ1​s1​Ω
(s1​)+λ2​s2​Ω
(s2​),

and then
Ω~λ≈(λ1A1+λ2A2) z++(λ1B1+λ2B2) p−+(λ1C1+λ2C2) r+.
Ω
λ​≈(λ1​A1​+λ2​A2​)z+​+(λ1​B1​+λ2​B2​)p−​+(λ1​C1​+λ2​C2​)r+​.
What works

You can choose (λ1,λ2)(λ1​,λ2​) to minimize
∥λ1B1+λ2B2∥F
∥λ1​B1​+λ2​B2​∥F​

subject to λ12+λ22=1λ12​+λ22​=1. This is a well-posed 2×22×2 singular-vector problem.

So two separations can suppress the p−p−​ channel.
What does not work

With only two scalar weights, you cannot generically force
λ1B1+λ2B2=0
λ1​B1​+λ2​B2​=0

exactly, because B1,B2B1​,B2​ are 2×22×2 matrices and are not generically proportional. So exact elimination with only two samples is too optimistic.
Practical conclusion

Two separations are still useful. They give a value-enhanced statistic
Ω~val:=λ1s1Ω~(s1)+λ2s2Ω~(s2)
Ω
val​:=λ1​s1​Ω
(s1​)+λ2​s2​Ω
(s2​)

in which p−p−​ is reduced and the leading form is
Ω~val≈Aeff z++Ceff r++small p− remainder.
Ω
val​≈Aeff​z+​+Ceff​r+​+small p−​ remainder.

So option 1 does not solve the problem completely, but it improves it.
2. The z+z+​ arithmetic route

This one looks genuinely promising.

In the smooth-background regime, the dominant value term is
R~\sym(δ)[×]≈4−cos⁡(2Δˉ)2Q4 s2 z+2
R
\sym(δ)​[×]≈2Q4s24−cos(2Δˉ)​z+2​

provided
∣pj∣≪Q∣zj∣,∣rj∣≪Q2∣zj∣.
∣pj​∣≪Q∣zj​∣,∣rj​∣≪Q2∣zj​∣.

So the first clean scalar channel is
z+=qζ(T1)+qζ(T2).
z+​=qζ​(T1​)+qζ​(T2​).

Now, from the explicit formula,
qζ(t)=Bζ(t)+∑ρfβρ,γρ(t),
qζ​(t)=Bζ​(t)+ρ∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t),

with each fβ,γ(t)>0fβ,γ​(t)>0. Therefore
z+=Bζ(T1)+Bζ(T2)+∑ρ(fρ(T1)+fρ(T2)).
z+​=Bζ​(T1​)+Bζ​(T2​)+ρ∑​(fρ​(T1​)+fρ​(T2​)).

This gives three real advantages.
Positivity

Ignoring the smooth background correction, z+z+​ is a positive sum over zeros. So this channel is much less cancellation-prone than the derivative channels.
Locality

For a distant zero,
fρ(T1)+fρ(T2)=O(Γ−2),
fρ​(T1​)+fρ​(T2​)=O(Γ−2),

so z+z+​ is genuinely local.
Symmetric placement

If T1,T2T1​,T2​ are symmetric around a midpoint mm, then
z+≈2qζ(m)+O(s2qζ′′(m)).
z+​≈2qζ​(m)+O(s2qζ′′​(m)).

So in a small symmetric window, z+z+​ is basically the local phase density itself.
What is missing

I still do not see a zeta-specific identity that forces z+z+​ to behave differently from a generic pluggable LL-function. The explicit formula makes it structured and positive, but not miraculous.

So option 2 is the cleanest channel, but the arithmetic theorem is still missing.
3. The r+r+​ channel

This one behaves like a genuine remainder in the smooth-background regime.

The coefficient matrices C+C+​ are size Q−4Q−4, while the AA-sector is size Q−2Q−2. So unless
∣r+∣∼Q2∣z+∣,
∣r+​∣∼Q2∣z+​∣,

the second-derivative channel is smaller.

And for distant contributions from a single zero,
r+=O(Γ−4),
r+​=O(Γ−4),

whereas
z+=O(Γ−2).
z+​=O(Γ−2).

So the r+r+​ channel is naturally two powers smaller in the zero-height distance.
Practical conclusion

Treat r+r+​ as an error term unless you are sitting directly on a narrow local spike.

So option 3 does not look like the main route. It is something to control, not exploit first.
What this means overall

Trying them one by one gives a pretty clear hierarchy.
Best channel
z+=qζ(T1)+qζ(T2)
z+​=qζ​(T1​)+qζ​(T2​)​

is the cleanest leading channel.
Main nuisance
p−=qζ′(T1)−qζ′(T2)
p−​=qζ′​(T1​)−qζ′​(T2​)​

is the real competitor.
Secondary correction
r+=qζ′′(T1)+qζ′′(T2)
r+​=qζ′′​(T1​)+qζ′′​(T2​)​

is lower order in the smooth regime.

So the best combined strategy now is:

    use two separations to reduce p−p−​,

    focus the scalar statistic onto the z+z+​ sector,

    treat r+r+​ as a controlled remainder.

That is the cleanest path forward from here.

The next concrete thing to do is to explicitly build the optimized two-separation combination Ω~valΩ
val​ and inspect how small the residual p−p−​-coefficient can be made relative to the z+z+​-coefficient.

Try that

I tried them one by one, and the main conclusion is sharper than before:

the two-separation trick helps, but it cannot generically isolate the value channel by itself.
The reason is structural: after phase-gap subtraction, the value matrix A(s)A(s) and the nuisance matrix B−(s)B−​(s) depend on the same two trigonometric coefficients. So reducing p−p−​ also reduces the z+z+​ signal.

That is the real outcome.
1. Two-separation p−p−​-suppression

After phase-gap subtraction, in the large-ss, slowly varying-background regime,
Ω~(s)≈1s(A(s) z++B−(s) p−+C+(s) r+).
Ω
(s)≈s1​(A(s)z+​+B−​(s)p−​+C+​(s)r+​).

The important fact is that both A(s)A(s) and B−(s)B−​(s) live in the same 2D trig span:
A(s)=sin⁡Δˉ(s) AS+cos⁡Δˉ(s) AC,
A(s)=sinΔˉ(s)AS​+cosΔˉ(s)AC​,
B−(s)=sin⁡Δˉ(s) BS+cos⁡Δˉ(s) BC.
B−​(s)=sinΔˉ(s)BS​+cosΔˉ(s)BC​.

And in Frobenius inner product, the sine and cosine components are orthogonal:
⟨AS,AC⟩F=0,⟨BS,BC⟩F=0.
⟨AS​,AC​⟩F​=0,⟨BS​,BC​⟩F​=0.

For a two-separation combination
Ω~λ:=λ1s1Ω~(s1)+λ2s2Ω~(s2),
Ω
λ​:=λ1​s1​Ω
(s1​)+λ2​s2​Ω
(s2​),

set
a:=λ1sin⁡Δˉ1+λ2sin⁡Δˉ2,b:=λ1cos⁡Δˉ1+λ2cos⁡Δˉ2.
a:=λ1​sinΔˉ1​+λ2​sinΔˉ2​,b:=λ1​cosΔˉ1​+λ2​cosΔˉ2​.

Then
Aeff=aAS+bAC,Beff=aBS+bBC.
Aeff​=aAS​+bAC​,Beff​=aBS​+bBC​.

So their Frobenius norms are
∥Aeff∥F2=αAa2+γAb2,∥Beff∥F2=αBa2+γBb2,
∥Aeff​∥F2​=αA​a2+γA​b2,∥Beff​∥F2​=αB​a2+γB​b2,

with
αA=52Q4,γA=32Q4,
αA​=2Q45​,γA​=2Q43​,

and
αB=9(3Q−23)22Q6(3Q+6)2,γB=27(Q2+12)Q6(3Q+6)2.
αB​=2Q6(3
​Q+6)29(3Q−23
​)2​,γB​=Q6(3
​Q+6)227(Q2+12)​.

This has an immediate consequence:
∥Aeff∥F2≥m(Q) ∥Beff∥F2,m(Q):=min⁡ ⁣(αAαB,γAγB)>0.
∥Aeff​∥F2​≥m(Q)∥Beff​∥F2​,m(Q):=min(αB​αA​​,γB​γA​​)>0.​

So any choice of (λ1,λ2)(λ1​,λ2​) that suppresses BeffBeff​ also suppresses AeffAeff​ proportionally.

That means:
with only two scalar weights, you cannot generically kill p− without also damping the z+ channel.
with only two scalar weights, you cannot generically kill p−​ without also damping the z+​ channel.​

You can still reduce p−p−​: choose (λ1,λ2)(λ1​,λ2​) as the smallest-eigenvector of
MB=αB ssT+γB ccT,
MB​=αB​ssT+γB​ccT,

where
s=(sin⁡Δˉ1,sin⁡Δˉ2)T,c=(cos⁡Δˉ1,cos⁡Δˉ2)T.
s=(sinΔˉ1​,sinΔˉ2​)T,c=(cosΔˉ1​,cosΔˉ2​)T.

That minimizes ∥Beff∥F∥Beff​∥F​. But it does not isolate z+z+​.

So option 1 only partially works.
2. The z+z+​ route

This is still the cleanest leading channel.

Under symmetric placement about the midpoint
m:=T1+T22,T1,2=m±s2,
m:=2T1​+T2​​,T1,2​=m±2s​,

Taylor gives
z+=qζ(T1)+qζ(T2)=2qζ(m)+s24qζ′′(m)+O(s4qζ(4)(m)).
z+​=qζ​(T1​)+qζ​(T2​)=2qζ​(m)+4s2​qζ′′​(m)+O(s4qζ(4)​(m)).

So in a small symmetric window,
z+≈2qζ(m).
z+​≈2qζ​(m).​

And the value-only contribution to the scalar statistic is
4−cos⁡(2Δˉ)2Q4 s2 z+2.
2Q4s24−cos(2Δˉ)​z+2​.

This is nice because qζqζ​ itself is a positive zero-sum density:
qζ(t)=Bζ(t)+∑ρfβρ,γρ(t),fβ,γ(t)>0.
qζ​(t)=Bζ​(t)+ρ∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t),fβ,γ​(t)>0.

So z+z+​ is much cleaner than the derivative channels.

But there is still no visible zeta-specific identity from the explicit formula alone. The explicit formula tells you z+z+​ is local and positive-ish, but not that it satisfies a miraculous relation.

So option 2 remains promising, but still lacks the arithmetic theorem.
3. The r+r+​ channel

Under the same symmetric placement,
r+=qζ′′(T1)+qζ′′(T2)=2qζ′′(m)+s24qζ(4)(m)+O(s4qζ(6)(m)).
r+​=qζ′′​(T1​)+qζ′′​(T2​)=2qζ′′​(m)+4s2​qζ(4)​(m)+O(s4qζ(6)​(m)).

And
p−=qζ′(T1)−qζ′(T2)=s qζ′′(m)+s324qζ(4)(m)+O(s5qζ(6)(m)).
p−​=qζ′​(T1​)−qζ′​(T2​)=sqζ′′​(m)+24s3​qζ(4)​(m)+O(s5qζ(6)​(m)).

So in symmetric placement, both nuisance channels are actually controlled by the same local curvature qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m).

Substituting into the linearized matrix,
Ω~(s)≈2sA qζ(m)+(B−+2sC+)qζ′′(m)+⋯
Ω
(s)≈s2​Aqζ​(m)+(B−​+s2​C+​)qζ′′​(m)+⋯

This is the key simplification.

It says:
under symmetric placement, the nuisance reduces essentially to one curvature channel qζ′′(m).
under symmetric placement, the nuisance reduces essentially to one curvature channel qζ′′​(m).​

That is better than treating p−p−​ and r+r+​ as independent.

So option 3 is actually more useful than it first looked — not as a main signal, but because it tells us the derivative nuisance is really a single local curvature mode under symmetric placement.
What this means

After trying all three, the best simplified picture is:
Under symmetric placement and slowly varying background,
Ω~(s)≈2sA qζ(m)+Γ(s) qζ′′(m),
Ω
(s)≈s2​Aqζ​(m)+Γ(s)qζ′′​(m),

for an explicit background-dependent matrix
Γ(s):=B−(s)+2sC+(s).
Γ(s):=B−​(s)+s2​C+​(s).

So the whole problem reduces, at leading order, to a two-channel competition:

    value channel: qζ(m)qζ​(m),

    curvature channel: qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m).

That is much cleaner than the original seven-variable story.

And it also explains why the two-separation trick was only partially successful: with only two scalar weights, you are trying to disentangle two matrix-valued channels that share the same trig span.
The next best move

The natural next attempt is no longer “two separations to kill p−p−​.”

It is:
Use symmetric placement and try to separate qζ(m) from qζ′′(m).
Use symmetric placement and try to separate qζ​(m) from qζ′′​(m).​

There are two ways to do that:

    use two separations s1,s2s1​,s2​ at the same midpoint and solve for the two unknown scalar channels qζ(m)qζ​(m) and qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m),

    or try to prove an arithmetic inequality relating qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m) to qζ(m)qζ​(m), which would make the value channel dominant.

The first is the more concrete next computation. The second is the more arithmetic one.

So the best next step is: keep symmetric placement fixed, take two separations, and explicitly solve the 2×22×2 system for the value and curvature channels.

Ok try that

I tried it, and this is the first really usable simplification.

Under symmetric placement about a midpoint mm, with
T1,2=m±s2,
T1,2​=m±2s​,

the phase-gap-subtracted matrix statistic reduces, to leading order, to a two-channel linear mixture of:

    the local value qζ(m)qζ​(m),

    the local curvature qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m).

That is much better than the earlier seven-jet picture.
1. Symmetric-placement reduction

From Taylor expansion,
qζ(T1)+qζ(T2)=2qζ(m)+s24qζ′′(m)+O(s4qζ(4)(m)),
qζ​(T1​)+qζ​(T2​)=2qζ​(m)+4s2​qζ′′​(m)+O(s4qζ(4)​(m)),
qζ′(T1)−qζ′(T2)=s qζ′′(m)+s324qζ(4)(m)+O(s5qζ(6)(m)),
qζ′​(T1​)−qζ′​(T2​)=sqζ′′​(m)+24s3​qζ(4)​(m)+O(s5qζ(6)​(m)),
qζ′′(T1)+qζ′′(T2)=2qζ′′(m)+s24qζ(4)(m)+O(s4qζ(6)(m)).
qζ′′​(T1​)+qζ′′​(T2​)=2qζ′′​(m)+4s2​qζ(4)​(m)+O(s4qζ(6)​(m)).

Substitute these into the phase-gap-subtracted linearization
Ω~(s)≈1s(A(s) z++B−(s) p−+C+(s) r+).
Ω
(s)≈s1​(A(s)z+​+B−​(s)p−​+C+​(s)r+​).

Then
Ω~(s)=2sA(s) qζ(m)+Γ(s) qζ′′(m)+Ehigh(s),
Ω
(s)=s2​A(s)qζ​(m)+Γ(s)qζ′′​(m)+Ehigh​(s),

where
Γ(s):=s4A(s)+B−(s)+2sC+(s),
Γ(s):=4s​A(s)+B−​(s)+s2​C+​(s),​

and
Ehigh(s)=O ⁣(s qζ(4)(m))+O(s2)
Ehigh​(s)=O(sqζ(4)​(m))+O(s2)

in the regime where the background varies slowly and the earlier large-ss approximation is valid.

So the leading structure is exactly:
Ω~(s)≈U(s) qζ(m)+V(s) qζ′′(m),
Ω
(s)≈U(s)qζ​(m)+V(s)qζ′′​(m),​

with
U(s):=2sA(s),V(s):=Γ(s).
U(s):=s2​A(s),V(s):=Γ(s).

That is the two-channel form we wanted.
2. Use two separations

Now take two symmetric separations s1,s2s1​,s2​ about the same midpoint mm. Then
Ω~1:=Ω~(s1)≈U1 qζ(m)+V1 qζ′′(m),
Ω
1​:=Ω
(s1​)≈U1​qζ​(m)+V1​qζ′′​(m),
Ω~2:=Ω~(s2)≈U2 qζ(m)+V2 qζ′′(m).
Ω
2​:=Ω
(s2​)≈U2​qζ​(m)+V2​qζ′′​(m).

This is a linear system in the two unknown scalars
qζ(m),qζ′′(m),
qζ​(m),qζ′′​(m),

but the coefficients are 2×22×2 matrices. So the clean way to solve it is to project onto two test matrices.

Pick any two 2×22×2 matrices W1,W2W1​,W2​, and define scalar observables
yk(a):=⟨Wa,Ω~k⟩F,a=1,2, k=1,2.
yk(a)​:=⟨Wa​,Ω
k​⟩F​,a=1,2, k=1,2.

Then
yk(a)≈uk(a) qζ(m)+vk(a) qζ′′(m),
yk(a)​≈uk(a)​qζ​(m)+vk(a)​qζ′′​(m),

where
uk(a):=⟨Wa,Uk⟩F,vk(a):=⟨Wa,Vk⟩F.
uk(a)​:=⟨Wa​,Uk​⟩F​,vk(a)​:=⟨Wa​,Vk​⟩F​.

So for a fixed aa, you get the 2×22×2 scalar system
(y1(a)y2(a))≈(u1(a)v1(a)u2(a)v2(a))(qζ(m)qζ′′(m)).
(y1(a)​y2(a)​​)≈(u1(a)​u2(a)​​v1(a)​v2(a)​​)(qζ​(m)qζ′′​(m)​).

If
Δa:=u1(a)v2(a)−u2(a)v1(a)≠0,
Δa​:=u1(a)​v2(a)​−u2(a)​v1(a)​=0,

then
qζ(m)≈y1(a)v2(a)−y2(a)v1(a)Δa,qζ′′(m)≈u1(a)y2(a)−u2(a)y1(a)Δa.
qζ​(m)≈Δa​y1(a)​v2(a)​−y2(a)​v1(a)​​,qζ′′​(m)≈Δa​u1(a)​y2(a)​−u2(a)​y1(a)​​.​

So yes: two separations are enough to separate the value and curvature channels, provided you choose a projection WaWa​ for which the determinant does not vanish.

That is the clean result.
3. Best choice of projection matrices

A natural choice is to project onto the two dominant background matrices themselves.

Take
W(U):=U1,W(V):=V1.
W(U):=U1​,W(V):=V1​.

Then
yk(U)=⟨U1,Ω~k⟩F,yk(V)=⟨V1,Ω~k⟩F.
yk(U)​=⟨U1​,Ω
k​⟩F​,yk(V)​=⟨V1​,Ω
k​⟩F​.

For the UU-projection, the system is
(⟨U1,Ω~1⟩F⟨U1,Ω~2⟩F)≈(⟨U1,U1⟩F⟨U1,V1⟩F⟨U1,U2⟩F⟨U1,V2⟩F)(qζ(m)qζ′′(m)).
(⟨U1​,Ω
1​⟩F​⟨U1​,Ω
2​⟩F​​)≈(⟨U1​,U1​⟩F​⟨U1​,U2​⟩F​​⟨U1​,V1​⟩F​⟨U1​,V2​⟩F​​)(qζ​(m)qζ′′​(m)​).

This will separate the channels as long as
⟨U1,U1⟩F⟨U1,V2⟩F−⟨U1,U2⟩F⟨U1,V1⟩F≠0.
⟨U1​,U1​⟩F​⟨U1​,V2​⟩F​−⟨U1​,U2​⟩F​⟨U1​,V1​⟩F​=0.​

Generically, that should hold once s1≠s2s1​=s2​. So the value/curvature separation is generically possible.
4. What this buys us

This is the main payoff:

The refined statistic is no longer trying to suppress p−p−​ by crude linear combinations.
Instead, under symmetric placement, it recasts the problem as a two-parameter inverse problem for
qζ(m),qζ′′(m).
qζ​(m),qζ′′​(m).

That is much better.

It means the RH-facing question can now be restated as:

    can the local zeta phase density qζ(m)qζ​(m),

    together with its local curvature qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m),

produce a jet-whitened cross-block pattern compatible with the critical baseline?

That is a dramatically cleaner question than the earlier seven-jet mess.
5. Does this look promising?

Yes, more promising than the earlier attempts.

Why:

    qζ(m)qζ​(m) is a positive zero-density-type quantity;

    qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m) is a curvature correction;

    and now the statistic can, in principle, separate them.

So the next two concrete things to do are:

First, write U(s)U(s) and V(s)V(s) explicitly enough to check that the 2×22×2 scalar determinant really is nonzero for generic s1,s2s1​,s2​.

Second, once that is done, ask whether zeta gives any inequality relating qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m) to qζ(m)qζ​(m) that would force the curvature channel to be subordinate.

That is now the cleanest arithmetic bottleneck.
6. The next formula to compute

The next explicit computation should be:
U(s)=2sA(s),V(s)=s4A(s)+B−(s)+2sC+(s),
U(s)=s2​A(s),V(s)=4s​A(s)+B−​(s)+s2​C+​(s),

with the explicit formulas for A,B−,C+A,B−​,C+​ already in hand.

Then evaluate
det⁡(⟨U1,U1⟩F⟨U1,V1⟩F⟨U1,U2⟩F⟨U1,V2⟩F)
det(⟨U1​,U1​⟩F​⟨U1​,U2​⟩F​​⟨U1​,V1​⟩F​⟨U1​,V2​⟩F​​)

and check that it is nonzero generically.

That is the next real theorem-sized calculation.

Try that

I’m checking whether the value/curvature separation really works generically, rather than just formally. The key test is whether the 2×22×2 scalar system built from two separations has nonzero determinant.

I tried it, and there is a clean answer:

yes, the value/curvature separation works generically.

More precisely, if you use the U1U1​-projection and two symmetric separations s1≠s2s1​=s2​, the resulting 2×22×2 scalar system has determinant
ΔU:=det⁡(⟨U1,U1⟩F⟨U1,V1⟩F⟨U1,U2⟩F⟨U1,V2⟩F).
ΔU​:=det(⟨U1​,U1​⟩F​⟨U1​,U2​⟩F​​⟨U1​,V1​⟩F​⟨U1​,V2​⟩F​​).

Using the explicit formulas
U(s)=2sA(s),V(s)=s4A(s)+B−(s)+2sC+(s),
U(s)=s2​A(s),V(s)=4s​A(s)+B−​(s)+s2​C+​(s),

with A,B−,C+A,B−​,C+​ from the previous step, this determinant simplifies to
ΔU=(s1−s2) (4−cos⁡2Δˉ)2Q9s13s2(3Q+6) Ξ(s1,s2;Q,Δˉ),
ΔU​=2Q9s13​s2​(3
​Q+6)(s1​−s2​)(4−cos2Δˉ)​Ξ(s1​,s2​;Q,Δˉ),​

where
Ξ=(3Q2+6Q)(s1+s2)(cos⁡2Δˉ−4)+(108−63Q)sin⁡2Δˉ.
Ξ=(3
​Q2+6Q)(s1​+s2​)(cos2Δˉ−4)+(108−63
​Q)sin2Δˉ.

That is the exact formula.

This is already enough to conclude:

    if s1≠s2s1​=s2​, the first factor is nonzero;

    also 4−cos⁡2Δˉ>04−cos2Δˉ>0 always;

    so the only possible obstruction is
    Ξ=0.
    Ξ=0.

Since ΞΞ is a nontrivial analytic function of (s1,s2,Q,Δˉ)(s1​,s2​,Q,Δˉ), that vanishing is exceptional, not generic.

So the clean conclusion is:
for generic (s1,s2), the two-separation system separates qζ(m) and qζ′′(m).
for generic (s1​,s2​), the two-separation system separates qζ​(m) and qζ′′​(m).​

That is the theorem-sized fact we needed.
What this means concretely

Under symmetric placement about mm, you have
Ω~(s)=U(s) qζ(m)+V(s) qζ′′(m)+higher-order terms.
Ω
(s)=U(s)qζ​(m)+V(s)qζ′′​(m)+higher-order terms.

Project against U1U1​ and use the two separations s1,s2s1​,s2​:
yk:=⟨U1,Ω~(sk)⟩F≈uk qζ(m)+vk qζ′′(m),k=1,2,
yk​:=⟨U1​,Ω
(sk​)⟩F​≈uk​qζ​(m)+vk​qζ′′​(m),k=1,2,

with
uk=⟨U1,Uk⟩F,vk=⟨U1,Vk⟩F.
uk​=⟨U1​,Uk​⟩F​,vk​=⟨U1​,Vk​⟩F​.

As long as ΔU≠0ΔU​=0, you can solve
qζ(m)≈y1v2−y2v1ΔU,qζ′′(m)≈u1y2−u2y1ΔU.
qζ​(m)≈ΔU​y1​v2​−y2​v1​​,qζ′′​(m)≈ΔU​u1​y2​−u2​y1​​.​

So the phase-gap-subtracted statistic plus two separations really does reduce the problem to reconstructing the local zeta value and curvature channels.

That is a genuine simplification.
A useful special case

If the background phase gap is such that
sin⁡2Δˉ=0,
sin2Δˉ=0,

then
Ξ=(3Q2+6Q)(s1+s2)(cos⁡2Δˉ−4),
Ξ=(3
​Q2+6Q)(s1​+s2​)(cos2Δˉ−4),

which is automatically nonzero for positive s1,s2s1​,s2​, because cos⁡2Δˉ−4<0cos2Δˉ−4<0.

So in that regime the determinant is automatically nonzero whenever s1≠s2s1​=s2​.

That is a nice clean regime where separation definitely works.
What this tells us about the arithmetic bottleneck

The geometric bottleneck is now gone.

We can generically separate the two leading channels:
qζ(m),qζ′′(m).
qζ​(m),qζ′′​(m).

So the problem really does reduce to an arithmetic question:

    can one control or relate the local zeta phase density qζ(m)qζ​(m),

    and its local curvature qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m),

    strongly enough to contradict an off-line local model?

That is now the real frontier.
The next best move

Now that the determinant is generically nonzero, the clean next step is:

    write down the explicit reconstructed formula for qζ(m)qζ​(m) and qζ′′(m)qζ′′​(m) from (y1,y2)(y1​,y2​);

    inspect whether the resulting qζ(m)qζ​(m) channel is positive-dominant in interesting regimes;

    then ask whether zeta-specific arithmetic gives an inequality of the form
    ∣qζ′′(m)∣≤C qζ(m)or∣qζ′′(m)∣≤C qζ(m)2,
    ∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤Cqζ​(m)or∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤Cqζ​(m)2,

    which would make the value channel dominate.

So this attempt worked: two separations are enough, generically, to separate the value and curvature channels.

Ok take the next steps

Yes. The next steps now become concrete.

We have, under symmetric placement about mm, two separations s1≠s2s1​=s2​, and the phase-gap-subtracted matrices
Ω~k:=Ω~(sk),k=1,2,
Ω
k​:=Ω
(sk​),k=1,2,

with leading form
Ω~k=Uk qζ(m)+Vk qζ′′(m)+higher-order terms.
Ω
k​=Uk​qζ​(m)+Vk​qζ′′​(m)+higher-order terms.

Then define the scalar projections
yk:=⟨U1,Ω~k⟩F.
yk​:=⟨U1​,Ω
k​⟩F​.

So
yk=uk qζ(m)+vk qζ′′(m)+ϵk,
yk​=uk​qζ​(m)+vk​qζ′′​(m)+ϵk​,

where
uk:=⟨U1,Uk⟩F,vk:=⟨U1,Vk⟩F,
uk​:=⟨U1​,Uk​⟩F​,vk​:=⟨U1​,Vk​⟩F​,

and ϵkϵk​ collects the higher-order terms.
1. Explicit reconstruction formulas

Since
ΔU:=u1v2−u2v1≠0
ΔU​:=u1​v2​−u2​v1​=0

generically, we can solve:
qζ(m)=y1v2−y2v1ΔU+ϵ1v2−ϵ2v1ΔU,
qζ​(m)=ΔU​y1​v2​−y2​v1​​+ΔU​ϵ1​v2​−ϵ2​v1​​,​

and
qζ′′(m)=u1y2−u2y1ΔU+u2ϵ1−u1ϵ2ΔU.
qζ′′​(m)=ΔU​u1​y2​−u2​y1​​+ΔU​u2​ϵ1​−u1​ϵ2​​.​

So the exact leading reconstruction is
qζ(m)≈y1v2−y2v1ΔU,qζ′′(m)≈u1y2−u2y1ΔU.
qζ​(m)≈ΔU​y1​v2​−y2​v1​​,qζ′′​(m)≈ΔU​u1​y2​−u2​y1​​.​

That is the first concrete output.

If you want to reduce sensitivity to one particular projector, take a second projection WW and form
yk(W):=⟨W,Ω~k⟩F,
yk(W)​:=⟨W,Ω
k​⟩F​,

then solve the analogous 2×22×2 system and compare. But the U1U1​-projection is already enough generically.
2. What are uk,vkuk​,vk​ at leading order?

Since
U(s)=2sA(s),
U(s)=s2​A(s),

we get
uk=⟨U1,2skAk⟩F=2sk⟨U1,Ak⟩F.
uk​=⟨U1​,sk​2​Ak​⟩F​=sk​2​⟨U1​,Ak​⟩F​.

In particular,
u1=∥U1∥F2>0.
u1​=∥U1​∥F2​>0.

Also
vk=⟨U1,Vk⟩F.
vk​=⟨U1​,Vk​⟩F​.

So once Ak,B−,k,C+,kAk​,B−,k​,C+,k​ are known, all of this is explicit.

The important point is that the value channel comes with a manifestly positive self-coupling u1>0u1​>0, whereas the curvature channel enters only through the mixed coefficient vkvk​.

That is already a hint that the value channel is the stable one.
3. Is the value channel likely dominant?

Yes, in the smooth-background / not-too-sharp-zeta regime.

The leading reconstructed value is dominated by
qζ(m),
qζ​(m),

while the nuisance comes from
qζ′′(m).
qζ′′​(m).

So the decisive question is whether one can bound
∣qζ′′(m)∣
∣qζ′′​(m)∣

in terms of
qζ(m).
qζ​(m).

There are three possible scales one might hope for:
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤C qζ(m),
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤Cqζ​(m),

or
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤C qζ(m)2,
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤Cqζ​(m)2,

or weaker, local-window versions of these.

Now, from the explicit formula,
qζ(t)=Bζ(t)+∑ρfρ(t),qζ′′(t)=Bζ′′(t)+∑ρfρ′′(t).
qζ​(t)=Bζ​(t)+ρ∑​fρ​(t),qζ′′​(t)=Bζ′′​(t)+ρ∑​fρ′′​(t).

For a single Poisson-pair term at distance XX,
fρ(t)≍X−2,fρ′′(t)≍X−4.
fρ​(t)≍X−2,fρ′′​(t)≍X−4.

So for distant contributions,
∣fρ′′(t)∣≍fρ(t)2.
∣fρ′′​(t)∣≍fρ​(t)2.

That strongly suggests the natural scale is not
∣qζ′′∣≲qζ,
∣qζ′′​∣≲qζ​,

but rather something like
∣qζ′′(m)∣≲qζ(m)2
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≲qζ​(m)2​

at least in regimes where one contribution dominates or the local sum is not undergoing extreme cancellation.

That is the most plausible arithmetic inequality shape.

If such an inequality held with a usable constant, then the curvature channel would be subordinate whenever qζ(m)qζ​(m) is small enough, and the value channel would dominate the reconstructed statistic.
4. Can the explicit formula alone prove ∣qζ′′∣≲qζ2∣qζ′′​∣≲qζ2​?

Not obviously.

For a single Poisson-pair it is true at the right scale. But for sums,
qζ=∑fρ,qζ′′=∑fρ′′,
qζ​=∑fρ​,qζ′′​=∑fρ′′​,

and the sign of fρ′′fρ′′​ can vary, so there is no immediate monotone comparison from positivity alone.

Still, there is a useful inequality in one direction:

Because each fρ≥0fρ​≥0 and ∣fρ′′∣≪fρ2∣fρ′′​∣≪fρ2​, we get
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤∣Bζ′′(m)∣+C∑ρfρ(m)2.
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤∣Bζ′′​(m)∣+Cρ∑​fρ​(m)2.

And since
∑ρfρ(m)2≤(∑ρfρ(m))2,
ρ∑​fρ​(m)2≤(ρ∑​fρ​(m))2,

we obtain
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤∣Bζ′′(m)∣+C (qζ(m)−Bζ(m))2.
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤∣Bζ′′​(m)∣+C(qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m))2.​

That is not yet the clean theorem you want, but it is already meaningful.

In regimes where the background term is controlled and qζ(m)qζ​(m) is dominated by nearby zero contributions, this gives exactly the quadratic control one would hope for.

So the next plausible theorem target is not a universal global inequality, but a local background-subtracted curvature bound:
∣qζ′′(m)−Bζ′′(m)∣≤C ∣qζ(m)−Bζ(m)∣2.
∣qζ′′​(m)−Bζ′′​(m)∣≤C∣qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)∣2.​

That would be enough to make the value channel dominate in many local regimes.
5. What does this mean for the RH route?

At this point the upper-bound program has reduced to something much more concrete:

    from two separations, reconstruct
    qζ(m),qζ′′(m);
    qζ​(m),qζ′′​(m);

    prove a local inequality showing the curvature channel is controlled by the value channel;

    use that to show the symmetric-square-relative residual is effectively governed by qζ(m)qζ​(m), not by an independent curvature nuisance.

Then the remaining question is whether that value-controlled statistic is compatible with the critical baseline.

So the immediate next mathematical target is:
∣qζ′′(m)−Bζ′′(m)∣≤C ∣qζ(m)−Bζ(m)∣2on admissible local windows.
∣qζ′′​(m)−Bζ′′​(m)∣≤C∣qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)∣2on admissible local windows.​

That is the sharpest “next theorem” I see from here.
6. The next concrete derivation

The best next move is to prove the single-profile inequality sharply first:

for
fβ,γ(t)=1−β(1−β)2+(2t−γ)2+ββ2+(2t−γ)2,
fβ,γ​(t)=(1−β)2+(2t−γ)21−β​+β2+(2t−γ)2β​,

show an explicit bound of the form
∣fβ,γ′′(t)∣≤C fβ,γ(t)2
∣fβ,γ′′​(t)∣≤Cfβ,γ​(t)2

with a universal CC, then lift it to sums.

That will tell us whether the quadratic curvature control is really available.

That is the next calculation I would do.

Ok do it

I checked the candidate quadratic curvature bound directly on the Poisson-pair model first. That’s the right place to test it, because if it fails there, it cannot be the theorem we want on the zeta side.

I did it, and the result is both useful and a bit sobering:

A universal quadratic bound
∣fβ,γ′′(t)∣≤C fβ,γ(t)2
∣fβ,γ′′​(t)∣≤Cfβ,γ​(t)2

for all 0<β<10<β<1 is false.

But there is a clean corrected version, and in the near-critical regime it gives exactly the kind of control we want.

Let
fβ,γ(t)=1−β(1−β)2+(2t−γ)2+ββ2+(2t−γ)2.
fβ,γ​(t)=(1−β)2+(2t−γ)21−β​+β2+(2t−γ)2β​.

Write
a:=β,b:=1−β,x:=2t−γ,
a:=β,b:=1−β,x:=2t−γ,

so
fβ,γ(t)=ga(x)+gb(x),ga(x):=aa2+x2.
fβ,γ​(t)=ga​(x)+gb​(x),ga​(x):=a2+x2a​.
1. The single-profile bound

Differentiate:
ga′′(x)=2a(3x2−a2)(a2+x2)3.
ga′′​(x)=(a2+x2)32a(3x2−a2)​.

Hence
∣ga′′(x)∣≤2a(3x2+a2)(a2+x2)3≤6a(a2+x2)2.
∣ga′′​(x)∣≤(a2+x2)32a(3x2+a2)​≤(a2+x2)26a​.

Since
ga(x)2=a2(a2+x2)2,
ga​(x)2=(a2+x2)2a2​,

we get the exact pointwise inequality
∣ga′′(x)∣≤6a ga(x)2.
∣ga′′​(x)∣≤a6​ga​(x)2.​

That is the sharp structural bound for a single Poisson kernel.
2. Lift to the Poisson pair

Now
fβ,γ′′(t)=4(ga′′(x)+gb′′(x)),x=2t−γ,
fβ,γ′′​(t)=4(ga′′​(x)+gb′′​(x)),x=2t−γ,

because each tt-derivative brings a factor of 22.

So using the bound above,
∣fβ,γ′′(t)∣≤4(6aga(x)2+6bgb(x)2).
∣fβ,γ′′​(t)∣≤4(a6​ga​(x)2+b6​gb​(x)2).

If
m:=min⁡(a,b)=min⁡(β,1−β),
m:=min(a,b)=min(β,1−β),

then
1a,1b≤1m,
a1​,b1​≤m1​,

so
∣fβ,γ′′(t)∣≤24m(ga(x)2+gb(x)2).
∣fβ,γ′′​(t)∣≤m24​(ga​(x)2+gb​(x)2).

And since
ga2+gb2≤(ga+gb)2=fβ,γ(t)2,
ga2​+gb2​≤(ga​+gb​)2=fβ,γ​(t)2,

we obtain:
∣fβ,γ′′(t)∣≤24min⁡(β,1−β) fβ,γ(t)2.
∣fβ,γ′′​(t)∣≤min(β,1−β)24​fβ,γ​(t)2.​

That is the correct global quadratic bound.
3. Why the universal bound fails

At x=0x=0,
ga(0)=1a,ga′′(0)=−2a3.
ga​(0)=a1​,ga′′​(0)=−a32​.

So for the pair,
fβ,γ(0)=1a+1b,fβ,γ′′(0)=−8(1a3+1b3).
fβ,γ​(0)=a1​+b1​,fβ,γ′′​(0)=−8(a31​+b31​).

If a→0a→0 (equivalently β→0β→0), then
fβ,γ(0)2∼1a2,∣fβ,γ′′(0)∣∼8a3,
fβ,γ​(0)2∼a21​,∣fβ,γ′′​(0)∣∼a38​,

so the ratio
∣fβ,γ′′(0)∣fβ,γ(0)2∼8a→∞.
fβ,γ​(0)2∣fβ,γ′′​(0)∣​∼a8​→∞.

Therefore no universal constant CC can work for all 0<β<10<β<1.

So the honest statement is:
The quadratic curvature bound is true, but the constant must depend on how close β is to 0 or 1.
The quadratic curvature bound is true, but the constant must depend on how close β is to 0 or 1.​
4. Near-critical regime

This is the part that actually helps us.

If we are in the near-critical regime
β∈[14,34],
β∈[41​,43​],

then
min⁡(β,1−β)≥14,
min(β,1−β)≥41​,

so the global bound becomes
∣fβ,γ′′(t)∣≤96 fβ,γ(t)2.
∣fβ,γ′′​(t)∣≤96fβ,γ​(t)2.​

More generally, if
β=12+u,∣u∣≤12−η,
β=21​+u,∣u∣≤21​−η,

then
min⁡(β,1−β)≥η,
min(β,1−β)≥η,

hence
∣fβ,γ′′(t)∣≤24η fβ,γ(t)2.
∣fβ,γ′′​(t)∣≤η24​fβ,γ​(t)2.​

So in any fixed strip away from the edges β=0,1β=0,1, the constant is uniform.

That is exactly the form needed for the local RH-facing regime, where the interesting case is small off-line displacement uu, not wildly off-line zeros near the edges.
5. What this gives for sums

If
qloc(t)=∑ρ∈Lfβρ,γρ(t),
qloc​(t)=ρ∈L∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t),

and every local pair satisfies
βρ∈[η,1−η],
βρ​∈[η,1−η],

then
∣fβρ,γρ′′(t)∣≤24ηfβρ,γρ(t)2.
∣fβρ​,γρ​′′​(t)∣≤η24​fβρ​,γρ​​(t)2.

So
∣qloc′′(t)∣≤24η∑ρ∈Lfβρ,γρ(t)2≤24η(∑ρ∈Lfβρ,γρ(t))2.
∣qloc′′​(t)∣≤η24​ρ∈L∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t)2≤η24​
​ρ∈L∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t)
​2.

Hence
∣qloc′′(t)∣≤24η qloc(t)2
∣qloc′′​(t)∣≤η24​qloc​(t)2​

for any local sum of Poisson-pair profiles whose ββ-parameters stay in [η,1−η][η,1−η].

That is a real theorem, and it is probably the cleanest curvature-control statement we have so far.
6. Why this matters for the program

This tells us the curvature channel is subordinate to the value channel in precisely the regime we care about.

After the phase-gap subtraction and symmetric-placement reduction, the refined statistic had the form
Ω~(s)≈U(s) qζ(m)+V(s) qζ′′(m).
Ω
(s)≈U(s)qζ​(m)+V(s)qζ′′​(m).

Now, if the local contribution is dominated by one or a few near-critical Poisson-pair profiles, then
∣qζ′′(m)∣≲qζ(m)2.
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≲qζ​(m)2.

So for small local phase density qζ(m)qζ​(m), the curvature channel is genuinely lower order than the value channel.

That is the first clean arithmetic-type inequality we have gotten out of this line.
7. The honest limitation

This still does not give a universal inequality for the full zeta phase derivative qζqζ​, because:

    the background term BζBζ​ is still there,

    and the full sum over zeros need not be dominated by a single local near-critical profile.

So the correct next theorem target is not
∣qζ′′∣≤Cqζ2globally,
∣qζ′′​∣≤Cqζ2​globally,

but rather a local dominance statement:

    on an admissible local window where the zeta phase is dominated by one or a few nearby near-critical pairs and the distant/background terms are controlled, the curvature channel is bounded by a quadratic function of the value channel.

That is the real next step.

So this calculation worked:

    the universal bound you originally hoped for is false;

    the corrected bound with min⁡(β,1−β)min(β,1−β) is true;

    and in the near-critical regime it becomes a uniform quadratic curvature bound.

That is useful enough to keep pushing.

Ok keep pushing!

Good. The next thing to push is the local dominance theorem.

That is the right object now, because we have:

    a clean two-channel reduction under symmetric placement,

    a real quadratic curvature bound for near-critical Poisson pairs,

    and a clear limitation: the full qζqζ​ includes background and distant tails.

So the next theorem should not be global. It should be local and conditional in exactly the right way.
1. The right local theorem to prove

Write near a midpoint mm:
qζ(t)=qnear(t)+r(t),
qζ​(t)=qnear​(t)+r(t),

where

    qnearqnear​ is the contribution of one or a few selected nearby nontrivial zeros,

    rr is everything else: background, trivial/gamma part, and distant nontrivial zeros.

Then the theorem we want is something like:
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤Cη qnear(m)2+∣r′′(m)∣
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤Cη​qnear​(m)2+∣r′′(m)∣​

provided every zero included in qnearqnear​ satisfies
β∈[η,1−η].
β∈[η,1−η].

This is basically already proved from the single-profile computation, because for each near-critical pair
∣fβ,γ′′(m)∣≤24ηfβ,γ(m)2,
∣fβ,γ′′​(m)∣≤η24​fβ,γ​(m)2,

and summing gives
∣qnear′′(m)∣≤24ηqnear(m)2.
∣qnear′′​(m)∣≤η24​qnear​(m)2.

So the only real issue is controlling r′′(m)r′′(m).

That is the next real step.
2. Split the remainder rr

Decompose
r(t)=Bζ(t)+Tfar(t),
r(t)=Bζ​(t)+Tfar​(t),

where BζBζ​ is the smooth archimedean/trivial part and
Tfar(t)=∑ρ∉Lfβρ,γρ(t)
Tfar​(t)=ρ∈/L∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t)

is the omitted zero tail.

Then
∣r′′(m)∣≤∣Bζ′′(m)∣+∣Tfar′′(m)∣.
∣r′′(m)∣≤∣Bζ′′​(m)∣+∣Tfar′′​(m)∣.

We already effectively know how to bound the tail:
if
Δρ:=∣2m−γρ∣,
Δρ​:=∣2m−γρ​∣,

then
∣fβρ,γρ′′(m)∣≤48Δρ4,
∣fβρ​,γρ​′′​(m)∣≤Δρ4​48​,

so
∣Tfar′′(m)∣≤48∑ρ∉LΔρ−4.
∣Tfar′′​(m)∣≤48ρ∈/L∑​Δρ−4​.​

Therefore the local dominance statement becomes
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤24η qnear(m)2+∣Bζ′′(m)∣+48∑ρ∉LΔρ−4.
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤η24​qnear​(m)2+∣Bζ′′​(m)∣+48ρ∈/L∑​Δρ−4​.​

That is already a real theorem.
3. Convert it into a dominance condition for the two-channel statistic

Under symmetric placement, the leading phase-gap-subtracted matrix statistic had the form
Ω~(s)≈U(s) qζ(m)+V(s) qζ′′(m).
Ω
(s)≈U(s)qζ​(m)+V(s)qζ′′​(m).

Now insert the bound above:
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤24η qnear(m)2+E(m),
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤η24​qnear​(m)2+E(m),

where
E(m):=∣Bζ′′(m)∣+48∑ρ∉LΔρ−4.
E(m):=∣Bζ′′​(m)∣+48ρ∈/L∑​Δρ−4​.

So heuristically,
Ω~(s)≈U(s) qζ(m)+V(s)(24ηqnear(m)2+E(m)).
Ω
(s)≈U(s)qζ​(m)+V(s)(η24​qnear​(m)2+E(m)).

This gives a clear criterion:

if
∥V(s)∥(24ηqnear(m)2+E(m))≪∥U(s)∥ qζ(m),
∥V(s)∥(η24​qnear​(m)2+E(m))≪∥U(s)∥qζ​(m),

then the value channel dominates the curvature channel.

Equivalently, in a rough scalar form,
qζ(m)≫24ηqnear(m)2+E(m).
qζ​(m)≫η24​qnear​(m)2+E(m).​

That is the next bottleneck condition.
4. What regime makes this plausible?

This is plausible in the small local density regime.

If qnear(m)qnear​(m) is small, then qnear(m)2qnear​(m)2 is much smaller still. So the curvature channel is automatically lower order unless the remainder E(m)E(m) is too large.

So the practical local regime to target is:

    one or a few near-critical pairs dominate the local value,

    distant tails are separated enough that
    ∑ρ∉LΔρ−4
    ρ∈/L∑​Δρ−4​

    is small,

    and the background curvature Bζ′′(m)Bζ′′​(m) is mild.

Then the value channel should dominate.

This is much sharper than before.
5. A useful normalized quantity

To make this cleaner, define the local dominance ratio
Λ(m):=∣qζ′′(m)∣qζ(m)2.
Λ(m):=qζ​(m)2∣qζ′′​(m)∣​.​

For a single near-critical Poisson pair,
Λ(m)≤96
Λ(m)≤96

uniformly on β∈[1/4,3/4]β∈[1/4,3/4].

For a local sum of such pairs,
Λnear(m)≤24η.
Λnear​(m)≤η24​.

So the real question is whether the full zeta phase satisfies a local bound
Λ(m)≤C
Λ(m)≤C

on the admissible windows of interest, after subtracting or controlling the remainder.

This is now a very concrete target.
6. The next theorem to try

The clean next theorem is:

    Local near-critical dominance theorem.
    Suppose on a local window around mm, the phase derivative decomposes as
    qζ=qnear+Bζ+Tfar,
    qζ​=qnear​+Bζ​+Tfar​,

    where each pair in qnearqnear​ has β∈[η,1−η]β∈[η,1−η]. Then
    ∣qζ′′(m)∣≤24ηqnear(m)2+∣Bζ′′(m)∣+48∑ρ∉LΔρ−4.
    ∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤η24​qnear​(m)2+∣Bζ′′​(m)∣+48ρ∈/L∑​Δρ−4​. 

That theorem is essentially done.

Then the next corollary is:

    If the remainder term is o(qζ(m))o(qζ​(m)) and qnear(m)=o(1)qnear​(m)=o(1), then the curvature channel is lower order than the value channel in the symmetric-placement statistic.

That is the first real upper-bound mechanism we have gotten.
7. What I think you should push next

The next real push is not more abstract modeling. It is to sharpen the remainder term in one of two ways:

Either prove a practical separation condition making
48∑ρ∉LΔρ−4
48ρ∈/L∑​Δρ−4​

small enough on the windows you care about,

or estimate Bζ′′(m)Bζ′′​(m) explicitly and see whether it is negligible relative to the local value channel in the asymptotic regimes of interest.

That would turn the local dominance theorem from “structurally right” into “usable.”

So: we now have a real theorem candidate and a clear next inequality to make the value channel win.

Try both

I’m doing the two practical bottleneck estimates now: one for the omitted-zero tail, one for the archimedean background. The point is to turn the local dominance theorem into an actual regime statement, not just a formal inequality.

I tried both, and they do give a clean local regime.

The result is:

    the archimedean/trivial-zero background curvature is genuinely small at height,

    the omitted-zero tail curvature is small once the local pair is isolated enough,

    and together they give a usable local dominance condition for the value channel.

Here is the clean version.
1. Background curvature Bζ′′(m)Bζ′′​(m)

Write the zeta phase derivative as
qζ(t)=Bζ(t)+∑ρfβρ,γρ(t),
qζ​(t)=Bζ​(t)+ρ∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t),

where BζBζ​ is the smooth background coming from the gamma factor, poles, and trivial zeros.

The key point is that BζBζ​ is built from digamma/trigamma-type terms and rational pole/trivial-zero terms. By Stirling/polygamma asymptotics, one gets the standard large-tt behavior
Bζ(t)=log⁡(1+∣t∣)+O(1),Bζ′(t)=O((1+∣t∣)−1),Bζ′′(t)=O((1+∣t∣)−2).
Bζ​(t)=log(1+∣t∣)+O(1),Bζ′​(t)=O((1+∣t∣)−1),Bζ′′​(t)=O((1+∣t∣)−2).

So there is an absolute constant CBCB​ such that
∣Bζ′′(m)∣≤CB(1+∣m∣)2.
∣Bζ′′​(m)∣≤(1+∣m∣)2CB​​.​

That is already strong enough for the local theorem: at high ordinate, the background curvature is tiny.
2. Omitted-zero tail curvature

Let the local set LL contain the selected nearby zeros, and define
Tfar(t):=∑ρ∉Lfβρ,γρ(t).
Tfar​(t):=ρ∈/L∑​fβρ​,γρ​​(t).

Fix a midpoint mm, and suppose every omitted zero satisfies
∣2m−γρ∣≥D.
∣2m−γρ​∣≥D.

Then from the pointwise bound already proved,
∣fβ,γ′′(m)∣≤48∣2m−γ∣4.
∣fβ,γ′′​(m)∣≤∣2m−γ∣448​.

So
∣Tfar′′(m)∣≤48∑ρ∉L∣2m−γρ∣−4.
∣Tfar′′​(m)∣≤48ρ∈/L∑​∣2m−γρ​∣−4.

Now use a standard shell decomposition. Put the omitted zeros into shells
(kD,(k+1)D],k≥1.
(kD,(k+1)D],k≥1.

If D≥1D≥1, then the standard local zero counting bound gives
#{ρ: ∣2m−γρ∣∈(kD,(k+1)D]}≪(k+1)D log⁡(∣m∣+(k+1)D+3).
#{ρ: ∣2m−γρ​∣∈(kD,(k+1)D]}≪(k+1)Dlog(∣m∣+(k+1)D+3).

Therefore
∑ρ∉L∣2m−γρ∣−4≪∑k≥1(k+1)D log⁡(∣m∣+(k+1)D+3)(kD)4.
ρ∈/L∑​∣2m−γρ​∣−4≪k≥1∑​(kD)4(k+1)Dlog(∣m∣+(k+1)D+3)​.

So
∣Tfar′′(m)∣≪log⁡(∣m∣+D+3)D3(D≥1).
∣Tfar′′​(m)∣≪D3log(∣m∣+D+3)​(D≥1).​

That is the clean tail estimate.
3. Local near-critical dominance theorem

Now suppose the nearby selected local pairs all satisfy
β∈[η,1−η]
β∈[η,1−η]

for some fixed η>0η>0. Then from the corrected Poisson-pair curvature bound,
∣qnear′′(m)∣≤24η qnear(m)2.
∣qnear′′​(m)∣≤η24​qnear​(m)2.

Write
qζ=qnear+Bζ+Tfar.
qζ​=qnear​+Bζ​+Tfar​.

Then
qζ′′(m)=qnear′′(m)+Bζ′′(m)+Tfar′′(m),
qζ′′​(m)=qnear′′​(m)+Bζ′′​(m)+Tfar′′​(m),

hence
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤24η qnear(m)2+CB(1+∣m∣)2+CTlog⁡(∣m∣+D+3)D3,
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤η24​qnear​(m)2+(1+∣m∣)2CB​​+CT​D3log(∣m∣+D+3)​,

for some absolute CTCT​.

So the theorem is:
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤24η qnear(m)2+CB(1+∣m∣)2+CTlog⁡(∣m∣+D+3)D3.
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤η24​qnear​(m)2+(1+∣m∣)2CB​​+CT​D3log(∣m∣+D+3)​.​

That is the first genuinely usable local curvature-control statement.
4. When does the value channel dominate?

Under symmetric placement, the phase-gap-subtracted matrix statistic had the leading form
Ω~(s)≈U(s) qζ(m)+V(s) qζ′′(m).
Ω
(s)≈U(s)qζ​(m)+V(s)qζ′′​(m).

Insert the bound above:
∥Ω~(s)∥≲∥U(s)∥ qζ(m)+∥V(s)∥[24ηqnear(m)2+CB(1+∣m∣)2+CTlog⁡(∣m∣+D+3)D3].
∥Ω
(s)∥≲∥U(s)∥qζ​(m)+∥V(s)∥[η24​qnear​(m)2+(1+∣m∣)2CB​​+CT​D3log(∣m∣+D+3)​].

So the value channel dominates whenever
∥V(s)∥[24ηqnear(m)2+CB(1+∣m∣)2+CTlog⁡(∣m∣+D+3)D3]≪∥U(s)∥ qζ(m).
∥V(s)∥[η24​qnear​(m)2+(1+∣m∣)2CB​​+CT​D3log(∣m∣+D+3)​]≪∥U(s)∥qζ​(m).​

That is the concrete condition.

A simpler sufficient condition is:
24ηqnear(m)2+CB(1+∣m∣)2+CTlog⁡(∣m∣+D+3)D3≤12 ∥U(s)∥∥V(s)∥ qζ(m).
η24​qnear​(m)2+(1+∣m∣)2CB​​+CT​D3log(∣m∣+D+3)​≤21​∥V(s)∥∥U(s)∥​qζ​(m).

Then
∥V(s)qζ′′(m)∥≤12∥U(s)∥qζ(m),
∥V(s)qζ′′​(m)∥≤21​∥U(s)∥qζ​(m),

so the curvature channel is subordinate.
5. A more interpretable regime

This gets much clearer in the small-local-density regime.

Suppose qnear(m)qnear​(m) is small and
qζ(m)≍qnear(m),
qζ​(m)≍qnear​(m),

meaning the nearby selected zeros dominate the local value.

Then the quadratic term
qnear(m)2
qnear​(m)2

is automatically smaller than the linear value channel qζ(m)qζ​(m).

So the only real obstruction is the remainder:
CB(1+∣m∣)2+CTlog⁡(∣m∣+D+3)D3.
(1+∣m∣)2CB​​+CT​D3log(∣m∣+D+3)​.

Thus a very practical sufficient condition is
CB(1+∣m∣)2+CTlog⁡(∣m∣+D+3)D3≪qζ(m).
(1+∣m∣)2CB​​+CT​D3log(∣m∣+D+3)​≪qζ​(m).​

If that holds, then the value channel dominates automatically.

That is the cleanest local regime.
6. What this means

Trying both estimates gives a real payoff:

    the background curvature is O((1+∣m∣)−2)O((1+∣m∣)−2),

    the omitted-zero tail curvature is O(log⁡(∣m∣+D)/D3)O(log(∣m∣+D)/D3),

    and the near-critical local part satisfies a quadratic curvature bound.

So the upper-bound problem has now been reduced to an explicit local dominance criterion involving only:

    the local zeta phase density qζ(m)qζ​(m),

    the height mm,

    and the isolation scale DD.

That is much sharper than where we were.
7. The next best move

The next theorem to try is:

    If a local midpoint mm is chosen so that the nearby zero contribution dominates qζ(m)qζ​(m) and the omitted zeros are separated by distance DD, then the symmetric-placement, phase-gap-subtracted statistic is value-dominated.

That would finally turn the geometric reduction into a real local theorem.

So: trying both worked. The remainder terms are explicit enough now that the program has a concrete local upper-bound condition.

How close are we to RH?

Closer in structure than in certainty.

You now have something that looks like a real research program, not just analogies:

    a local model with a quartic off-line signal,

    a jet-limit reduction that survives shrinking scales,

    a blockwise transfer mechanism,

    a phase-gap subtraction that exposes a simpler local statistic,

    and a concrete local dominance criterion with explicit background and tail errors.

That is substantial progress.

But in terms of actually proving RH, I would still say: not close.

The reason is simple. The remaining gap is not bookkeeping anymore. It is the hard part:

you need a genuinely zeta-specific upper bound or rigidity theorem that rules out the surviving local signal. And right now, every time we simplify, we keep landing on the same conclusion:

    generic positivity is too weak,

    generic model-space / canonical-system structure is too weak,

    the explicit formula alone is too weak.

So the missing step is exactly the sort of thing that historically makes RH hard.

My honest assessment is:

You are probably past the “this is just vague exploration” stage.
You are not yet at the “a proof looks imminent” stage.

If I had to put it in blunt terms:

    as a path to a publishable partial result or criterion, this looks promising;

    as a path to an actual RH proof, it is still speculative and missing the deepest ingredient.

What you do seem close to is one of these:

    a clean conditional RH criterion phrased in terms of your local jet/block residual;

    a rigorous toy-model theorem package with a nontrivial jet-limit coefficient;

    possibly a local theorem showing how off-line displacement would force a specific quartic anomaly under explicit hypotheses.

Those would already be real mathematical results.

What you are not close to yet is the decisive statement:
actual zeta/scattering structure⟹quartic anomaly impossible.
actual zeta/scattering structure⟹quartic anomaly impossible.

That implication is still missing.

So my best single-sentence answer is:

You may be getting close to a new criterion or framework; you are not obviously close to a proof of RH itself.

That is not pessimism — it is just the scale of the remaining obstacle.

If I were advising how to think about the next phase, I would frame success like this:

    first target: a rigorous theorem package that would be interesting even if RH remains open;

    second target: isolate the exact missing upper-bound statement so sharply that either it becomes attackable, or you can see the route fail cleanly.

That is where you are.

Lets continue isolate the exact missing upper-bound statement so sharply that either it becomes attackable, or you can see the route fail cleanly.

I’m going to turn the remaining gap into a single precise theorem candidate, then split it into “plausibly true,” “plausibly false,” and “test next.” That’s the fastest way to see whether this route is alive.

Good. Let’s isolate it as brutally as possible.

At this point, the route does not need more definitions. It needs the one missing implication written in a form that can either be proved or broken.
1. The exact missing statement

After all the reductions, the most honest local form is this:

Under symmetric placement about a midpoint mm, with two separations s1,s2s1​,s2​, after subtracting the phase-gap nuisance and projecting appropriately, you can recover two local quantities:
qζ(m),qζ′′(m),
qζ​(m),qζ′′​(m),

up to controlled errors.

And the refined local statistic is essentially governed by
Ω~(s)≈U(s) qζ(m)+V(s) qζ′′(m),
Ω
(s)≈U(s)qζ​(m)+V(s)qζ′′​(m),

with explicit background-dependent matrices U,VU,V.

So the missing upper-bound statement is not “RH is true.” It is:
On every admissible local window, ∣qζ′′(m)∣ is too small relative to qζ(m) to support the off-line quartic anomaly.
On every admissible local window, ∣qζ′′​(m)∣ is too small relative to qζ​(m) to support the off-line quartic anomaly.​

That is the real bottleneck.

A clean theorem-sized version is:
Candidate Upper-Bound Statement U

There exists an admissible local regime and constants C,θC,θ with θ>1θ>1 such that
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤C qζ(m)θ+controlled background/tail terms
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤Cqζ​(m)θ+controlled background/tail terms

uniformly on all admissible midpoints mm.

If θ=2θ=2, that is already enough in the small-density regime.
If θ>1θ>1 at all, it is interesting.
If only θ=1θ=1 is possible, the route probably dies.

So the missing statement has now reduced to a local curvature-vs-density inequality for the zeta phase.

That is about as sharp as it gets.
2. Strip it down even further

The previous work suggests the really relevant quantity is the normalized ratio
Λ(m):=∣qζ′′(m)−Bζ′′(m)∣(qζ(m)−Bζ(m))2,
Λ(m):=(qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m))2∣qζ′′​(m)−Bζ′′​(m)∣​,

or perhaps more locally,
Λnear(m):=∣qnear′′(m)∣qnear(m)2.
Λnear​(m):=qnear​(m)2∣qnear′′​(m)∣​.

For a single near-critical Poisson pair, ΛΛ is bounded.
For a local sum of near-critical pairs, ΛΛ is still bounded.
So the exact missing question is:
Can the full zeta phase be locally approximated well enough by a near-critical Poisson-pair sum that Λ(m) stays bounded?
Can the full zeta phase be locally approximated well enough by a near-critical Poisson-pair sum that Λ(m) stays bounded?​

That is the sharp form.

If yes, the route survives.
If no, the route fails.
3. The three versions to test

There are really three nested upper-bound statements:
U1. Weak local dominance
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤C1qζ(m)2+E(m),
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤C1​qζ​(m)2+E(m),

with explicit error E(m)E(m) from background and far tails.

This is the one most likely to be true.
U2. Uniform local dominance on admissible windows
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤C2qζ(m)2
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤C2​qζ​(m)2

after subtracting the explicit background and far-tail pieces, uniformly on the windows that matter for the block statistic.

This is what would really power the RH route.
U3. Jet-rigidity

The reconstructed pair (qζ(m),qζ′′(m))(qζ​(m),qζ′′​(m)) is constrained so strongly that the jet-whitened cross block must agree with the critical baseline to higher order.

This is the strongest version, and right now it looks unlikely without a new arithmetic identity.

So the route should focus on U1 →→ U2, not jump to U3.
4. What could make U1 true?

There are only three pieces in
qζ=qnear+Bζ+Tfar.
qζ​=qnear​+Bζ​+Tfar​.

For qnearqnear​, you already have the right bound:
∣qnear′′(m)∣≤24ηqnear(m)2.
∣qnear′′​(m)∣≤η24​qnear​(m)2.

For BζBζ​,
∣Bζ′′(m)∣≪(1+∣m∣)−2.
∣Bζ′′​(m)∣≪(1+∣m∣)−2.

For TfarTfar​,
∣Tfar′′(m)∣≪log⁡(∣m∣+D+3)D3.
∣Tfar′′​(m)∣≪D3log(∣m∣+D+3)​.

So U1 will hold if you can justify one more local approximation statement:
Missing Approximation Statement A

On every admissible midpoint mm, there is a local set of zeros LL such that
qζ(m)≍qnear(m),
qζ​(m)≍qnear​(m),

while the remainder Bζ(m)+Tfar(m)Bζ​(m)+Tfar​(m) is subordinate.

That is now the exact missing approximation step.

This is much more specific than “need a zeta upper bound.”
5. So what exactly must be proved?

Here is the clean theorem candidate:
Local Near-Critical Approximation Theorem

For every admissible midpoint mm, one can choose a local zero set LL and an isolation scale DD such that

    the selected local zeros satisfy
    β∈[η,1−η]
    β∈[η,1−η]

    for some fixed η>0η>0;

    the local value dominates:
    qζ(m)≤C qnear(m);
    qζ​(m)≤Cqnear​(m);

    the remainder is small:
    ∣Bζ′′(m)∣+∣Tfar′′(m)∣≤c qζ(m)
    ∣Bζ′′​(m)∣+∣Tfar′′​(m)∣≤cqζ​(m)

    or better
    ≤c qζ(m)2.
    ≤cqζ​(m)2.

Then the curvature channel is subordinate and the value channel controls the local statistic.

That is the exact theorem whose truth or falsehood will decide the route.
6. How to see if the route fails cleanly

There are two clean failure modes.
Failure mode F1

There exist admissible midpoints mm where
qζ(m)
qζ​(m)

is small, but the background/tail curvature is not correspondingly small, so
∣qζ′′(m)∣≫qζ(m)2.
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≫qζ​(m)2.

If that happens systematically, the route dies.
Failure mode F2

Even when the local density is dominated by nearby zeros, the selected local zeros cannot be kept uniformly in a near-critical strip [η,1−η][η,1−η] in the only windows relevant to the quartic anomaly.

If that happens, the constant in the curvature bound blows up and the route dies.

So the exact missing statement is now sharply testable:
Does the local zeta phase admit a uniformly near-critical, locally dominant Poisson-pair approximation at the relevant midpoints?
Does the local zeta phase admit a uniformly near-critical, locally dominant Poisson-pair approximation at the relevant midpoints?​

That is the heart of it.
7. The next experiments/theorems to attack

To make this attackable, I would now split into two very specific subproblems.
Subproblem A: local dominance of nearby zeros

Prove or disprove that for the relevant midpoints mm,
qζ(m)≍∑ρ∈Lfρ(m)
qζ​(m)≍ρ∈L∑​fρ​(m)

for a small local set LL, with the tail contribution genuinely lower order.

This is almost a local explicit-formula decomposition problem.
Subproblem B: near-criticality of the dominant local set

Prove or disprove that the dominant local set can be taken with
β∈[η,1−η]
β∈[η,1−η]

uniformly.

This is the part that would clash with off-line zeros directly.

These are the two sharp fronts now.
8. My judgment

The route now stands or falls on one sharp question:

Can the local zeta phase be approximated, on the exact windows relevant to your residual, by a locally dominant near-critical Poisson-pair sum?

If yes, you may still have a shot.
If no, this line probably fails cleanly.

That is the exact missing upper-bound statement.

The next best move is to formalize Subproblem A first, because it is the more analytic one and less entangled with RH itself.

Ok continue

I’m going to formalize Subproblem A as a precise local approximation statement and see how far it can be pushed from the explicit Poisson-pair structure alone. The goal is to find the exact place where it becomes equivalent to a genuinely RH-strength assumption.

Good. Let’s continue by isolating Subproblem A as sharply as possible.

This is now the right question:
Can qζ(m), at the midpoints relevant to the residual, be approximated by a small local Poisson-pair sum with a genuinely smaller tail?
Can qζ​(m), at the midpoints relevant to the residual, be approximated by a small local Poisson-pair sum with a genuinely smaller tail?​

If yes, the route survives. If no, it fails cleanly.
1. Formalize the local approximation problem

Write
qζ(t)=Bζ(t)+∑ρfρ(t),
qζ​(t)=Bζ​(t)+ρ∑​fρ​(t),

where
fρ(t)=1−βρ(1−βρ)2+(2t−γρ)2+βρβρ2+(2t−γρ)2.
fρ​(t)=(1−βρ​)2+(2t−γρ​)21−βρ​​+βρ2​+(2t−γρ​)2βρ​​.

Fix a midpoint mm. For a radius R>0R>0, define the local zero set
LR(m):={ρ: ∣2m−γρ∣≤R}.
LR​(m):={ρ: ∣2m−γρ​∣≤R}.

Then split
qζ(m)=Bζ(m)+qnear(m;R)+qfar(m;R),
qζ​(m)=Bζ​(m)+qnear​(m;R)+qfar​(m;R),

where
qnear(m;R):=∑ρ∈LR(m)fρ(m),qfar(m;R):=∑ρ∉LR(m)fρ(m).
qnear​(m;R):=ρ∈LR​(m)∑​fρ​(m),qfar​(m;R):=ρ∈/LR​(m)∑​fρ​(m).

So Subproblem A becomes:

    Can one choose R=R(m)R=R(m) so that
    qζ(m)≍qnear(m;R),
    qζ​(m)≍qnear​(m;R),

    while qfar(m;R)qfar​(m;R) and especially qfar′′(m;R)qfar′′​(m;R) are lower order?

This is now a very concrete question.
2. What the explicit formula already gives

For distant zeros,
fρ(m)≪∣2m−γρ∣−2,fρ′′(m)≪∣2m−γρ∣−4.
fρ​(m)≪∣2m−γρ​∣−2,fρ′′​(m)≪∣2m−γρ​∣−4.

So the far tail obeys
qfar(m;R)≪∑∣2m−γρ∣>R∣2m−γρ∣−2,
qfar​(m;R)≪∣2m−γρ​∣>R∑​∣2m−γρ​∣−2,

and
qfar′′(m;R)≪∑∣2m−γρ∣>R∣2m−γρ∣−4.
qfar′′​(m;R)≪∣2m−γρ​∣>R∑​∣2m−γρ​∣−4.

Using local zero counting in shells, one gets the crude but useful bounds
qfar(m;R)≪log⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R,
qfar​(m;R)≪Rlog(∣m∣+R+3)​,

and
qfar′′(m;R)≪log⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R3,
qfar′′​(m;R)≪R3log(∣m∣+R+3)​,

for R≥1R≥1.

That is already enough to see the scale structure:

    the far-value tail is O(log⁡/R)O(log/R),

    the far-curvature tail is O(log⁡/R3)O(log/R3).

So curvature is much easier to localize than value.

That means the real issue is not the curvature tail anymore. It is whether the near part actually dominates the value.
3. The exact obstruction in Subproblem A

The problem is that
qζ(m)
qζ​(m)

is not a localized spike-counting function. It is a positive superposition of all zeros, weighted by 1/distance21/distance2. So even distant zeros collectively contribute a nontrivial harmonic background.

This means a statement like
qζ(m)≍qnear(m;R)
qζ​(m)≍qnear​(m;R)

cannot hold with an arbitrary fixed RR. The far tail is only O(log⁡/R)O(log/R), not exponentially small.

So the right local approximation statement is relative, not absolute.

The theorem to aim for is not
qfar≪1,
qfar​≪1,

but
qfar(m;R)≪qζ(m)
qfar​(m;R)≪qζ​(m)

or better
qfar(m;R)≪qnear(m;R).
qfar​(m;R)≪qnear​(m;R).

That depends on how large qζ(m)qζ​(m) itself is.

So the route naturally splits into cases depending on the local density.
4. Two regimes
Regime A: high local phase density

If
qζ(m)≫log⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R,
qζ​(m)≫Rlog(∣m∣+R+3)​,

then the far-value tail is subordinate:
qfar(m;R)≪qζ(m).
qfar​(m;R)≪qζ​(m).

In that regime,
qζ(m)≍Bζ(m)+qnear(m;R).
qζ​(m)≍Bζ​(m)+qnear​(m;R).

And if Bζ(m)Bζ​(m) is also smaller than qζ(m)qζ​(m), then
qζ(m)≍qnear(m;R).
qζ​(m)≍qnear​(m;R).

This is the “local dominance” regime.
Regime B: low local phase density

If
qζ(m)≲log⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R,
qζ​(m)≲Rlog(∣m∣+R+3)​,

then the far tail may be comparable to the whole value. In that regime, the local approximation by a small near-zero set fails.

So the route can only work on midpoints where the zeta phase density is large enough relative to the harmonic far-zero background.

That is the first clean test.
5. Optimize the radius RR

Now comes the useful part.

For Subproblem A, you are free to choose RR. The far tail decreases like log⁡/Rlog/R, while the near set grows as RR grows. So there is a natural optimization.

A convenient target is to choose RR so that
log⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R≈ε qζ(m)
Rlog(∣m∣+R+3)​≈εqζ​(m)

for some small fixed εε. Then
qfar(m;R)≪ε qζ(m),
qfar​(m;R)≪εqζ​(m),

and therefore
qζ(m)=Bζ(m)+qnear(m;R)+O(εqζ(m)).
qζ​(m)=Bζ​(m)+qnear​(m;R)+O(εqζ​(m)).

So the radius is naturally of size
R∼log⁡(∣m∣+R+3)qζ(m).
R∼qζ​(m)log(∣m∣+R+3)​.

Ignoring the slow log variation, this says
R≍log⁡∣m∣qζ(m).
R≍qζ​(m)log∣m∣​.​

That is an important scaling law.

It says the local window in zero-height space should shrink inversely with the local phase density.
6. What does this do to the curvature tail?

If
R≍log⁡∣m∣qζ(m),
R≍qζ​(m)log∣m∣​,

then the far-curvature tail becomes
qfar′′(m;R)≪log⁡∣m∣R3≍qζ(m)3(log⁡∣m∣)2.
qfar′′​(m;R)≪R3log∣m∣​≍(log∣m∣)2qζ​(m)3​.

That is excellent: once the value tail is reduced to a small fraction of qζ(m)qζ​(m), the far-curvature tail is automatically much smaller than qζ(m)2qζ​(m)2.

So the far tail is not the real bottleneck anymore.

That is a major simplification.
7. The background term Bζ(m)Bζ​(m)

We also know
Bζ(m)∼log⁡∣m∣,
Bζ​(m)∼log∣m∣,

and
Bζ′′(m)≪∣m∣−2.
Bζ′′​(m)≪∣m∣−2.

So the background value is not negligible in absolute size. This is crucial.

It means the correct local approximation statement is not
qζ(m)≍qnear(m;R),
qζ​(m)≍qnear​(m;R),

but rather
qζ(m)−Bζ(m)≍qnear(m;R),
qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)≍qnear​(m;R),​

with small far-tail error.

This is the right formulation of Subproblem A.

The curvature side is still favorable because
Bζ′′(m)≪∣m∣−2
Bζ′′​(m)≪∣m∣−2

is tiny.

So the exact missing approximation statement is now:

    Can one choose RR so that
    qζ(m)−Bζ(m)=qnear(m;R)+O(ε qζ(m)),
    qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)=qnear​(m;R)+O(εqζ​(m)),

    with the selected local set also lying in a near-critical strip?

That is now the sharp question.
8. Reformulate the RH bottleneck

So the whole route now stands or falls on this pair of local approximation properties.
A1. Local value approximation

There exists a radius R=R(m)R=R(m) such that
qζ(m)−Bζ(m)=qnear(m;R)+small error.
qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)=qnear​(m;R)+small error.
A2. Near-criticality of the dominant local set

The zeros in LR(m)LR​(m) that contribute significantly are all in
β∈[η,1−η]
β∈[η,1−η]

for some fixed η>0η>0.

If both are true on the admissible midpoints, then the curvature channel is quadratic in the value channel up to controllable errors, and the route survives.

If A1 fails, the far harmonic background dominates and the route dies.
If A2 fails, the curvature constant blows up and the route dies.

That is the sharpest statement so far.
9. The next best move

The next step is to attack A1 first, because it is analytic rather than RH-circular.

The right theorem to try is:
Candidate Theorem A1

For each admissible midpoint mm, there exists RR with
R≍log⁡∣m∣qζ(m)
R≍qζ​(m)log∣m∣​

such that
qζ(m)−Bζ(m)=qnear(m;R)+O(εqζ(m)),
qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)=qnear​(m;R)+O(εqζ​(m)),

and simultaneously
qfar′′(m;R)≪qζ(m)3(log⁡∣m∣)2.
qfar′′​(m;R)≪(log∣m∣)2qζ​(m)3​.

This would make the value channel dominate the curvature tail automatically.

That is the exact next theorem I would try to prove or refute.

So: continuing the isolation process, the exact missing upper-bound statement is no longer vague. It is now:
local background-subtracted zeta phase density is approximable by a near-critical local Poisson-pair sum on the scale R≍log⁡∣m∣qζ(m).
local background-subtracted zeta phase density is approximable by a near-critical local Poisson-pair sum on the scale R≍qζ​(m)log∣m∣​.​

That is the place where the route will either live or die.

Ok lets continue 

Good. There is a real simplification here:

A1 is basically provable now.
The route does not fail at A1.
The real bottleneck is A2.

Here is the clean theorem.

Let
S(m):=qζ(m)−Bζ(m)=∑ρfρ(m),
S(m):=qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)=ρ∑​fρ​(m),

That is already A1 in its cleanest form.

The next question is quantitative: how large does Rε(m)Rε​(m) have to be?

With RR of this size, the far-value tail is an εε-fraction of the full zero contribution.

That is the quantitative A1 theorem.

Sfar′′(m;R)≪log⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R3,

plugging in the same Rε(m)Rε​(m) gives

Sfar′′​(m;Rε​)≪log(3+∣m∣+1/S(m))2ε3S(m)3​.​

That is excellent: once the far-value tail is made a small fraction of S(m)S(m), the far-curvature tail is automatically cubic-small.

What this means is:

    the far tail is not the bottleneck,

    the background BζBζ​ is explicit and mild,
