Can the dominant local set LRε​​(m) be kept uniformly away from β=0,1 ?​

That is the actual RH-strength assumption.

Because the curvature bound for a local sum is
∣qnear′′(m)∣≤24η qnear(m)2
∣qnear′′​(m)∣≤η24​qnear​(m)2

only if every contributing local pair satisfies
β∈[η,1−η].
β∈[η,1−η].

So the route now stands or falls on this precise statement:
Missing upper-bound statement, final form

For the admissible midpoints mm, and for Rε(m)Rε​(m) chosen as above, the dominant local zero set
LRε(m)
LRε​​(m)

can be chosen so that all materially contributing zeros satisfy
β∈[η,1−η]
β∈[η,1−η]

for some fixed η>0η>0.

If that is true, the curvature channel is quadratic in the value channel and the route survives.
If that is false, the constant blows up and the route fails.

So the exact bottleneck is no longer vague at all:
Does the locally dominant part of qζ(m)−Bζ(m) come from zeros uniformly separated from the edges 0,1?
Does the locally dominant part of qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m) come from zeros uniformly separated from the edges 0,1?​

That is now the sharp question.

The next thing I would do is not push more tail bounds. I would analyze what A2 actually means geometrically. In particular:

    if there were off-line zeros very close to the edge, would they necessarily dominate S(m)S(m) near their ordinates?

    can one show that such edge-near zeros would force a local shape incompatible with the jet statistic?

That is now the real frontier.

Ok lets check it

Yes. Let’s check A2 directly and see whether it is plausible, too strong, or basically RH in disguise.
1. What A2 is really saying

After A1, you have
qζ(m)−Bζ(m)=Snear(m;Rε)+O(εS(m)),
qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)=Snear​(m;Rε​)+O(εS(m)),

with
S(m)=∑ρfρ(m),fρ(m)=1−βρ(1−βρ)2+(2m−γρ)2+βρβρ2+(2m−γρ)2.
S(m)=ρ∑​fρ​(m),fρ​(m)=(1−βρ​)2+(2m−γρ​)21−βρ​​+βρ2​+(2m−γρ​)2βρ​​.

A2 asks for a fixed η>0η>0 such that the materially contributing zeros in LRε(m)LRε​​(m) satisfy
β∈[η,1−η].
β∈[η,1−η].

Since nontrivial zeros already satisfy 0<β<10<β<1, the only way A2 can fail is if the dominant local contribution comes from zeros with ββ very close to 00 or 11.

So A2 is not “RH-like” in the usual sense. It is not asking for β=12β=21​. It is only asking that the locally dominant zeros stay away from the edges.

That is much weaker than RH.
2. Would edge-near zeros dominate locally?

This is the first thing to check.

Take one zero ρ=β+iγρ=β+iγ, and look at the center
m=γ/2.
m=γ/2.

Then
fρ(m)=11−β+1β=1β(1−β).
fρ​(m)=1−β1​+β1​=β(1−β)1​.

So as β→0β→0 or β→1β→1,
fρ(m)→∞.
fρ​(m)→∞.

That means:
An edge-near zero would dominate the local value channel extremely strongly near its own ordinate.
An edge-near zero would dominate the local value channel extremely strongly near its own ordinate.​

So if such zeros existed, A2 would fail near those ordinates.

That is already important. It means A2 is not some harmless technical convenience. It is excluding a very definite possible local geometry.
3. But can that actually happen for zeta?

Here is the subtlety.

For the Riemann zeta function, zeros are symmetric under
ρ↦1−ρ.
ρ↦1−ρ.

So a zero near β=0β=0 is paired with one near β=1β=1, same ordinate γγ.

But your local profile already uses the pair
1−β(1−β)2+x2+ββ2+x2,
(1−β)2+x21−β​+β2+x2β​,

so the symmetry is already built in. And the blow-up at x=0x=0 is exactly the β(1−β)−1β(1−β)−1 effect above.

So if there were zeros very close to the edges, they would create enormous local spikes in
qζ(m)−Bζ(m).
qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m).

That means A2 is not arbitrary. It is really asking:

    do the dominant local spikes come from zeros that stay uniformly away from the edges?

That is a clean geometric condition.
4. Is A2 weaker than known zero-free information?

Yes, much weaker.

Classically, for zeta you already know zeros are not arbitrarily near β=1β=1 in a simple uniform way depending on height. There is a zero-free region near 11, and by symmetry the same near 00. So very edge-near zeros are already excluded asymptotically.

That suggests a softened version of A2 may actually be available.

Instead of fixed ηη, one could allow
η=η(m)
η=η(m)

coming from the zero-free region, with η(m)→0η(m)→0 slowly as ∣m∣→∞∣m∣→∞.

Then the curvature constant becomes
24η(m).
η(m)24​.

So the real question is not necessarily “fixed η>0η>0 for all mm,” but whether the deterioration of η(m)η(m) is slow enough that the quadratic curvature control still beats the value channel.

That is a much more realistic formulation.
5. Replace A2 by a quantitative version

The exact thing to check is not a hard cutoff β∈[η,1−η]β∈[η,1−η], but the weighted local curvature factor
Cnear(m):=∑ρ∈L1min⁡(βρ,1−βρ) fρ(m)2(∑ρ∈Lfρ(m))2.
Cnear​(m):=(∑ρ∈L​fρ​(m))2∑ρ∈L​min(βρ​,1−βρ​)1​fρ​(m)2​.

Because from the single-profile bound,
∣fρ′′(m)∣≤24min⁡(βρ,1−βρ) fρ(m)2,
∣fρ′′​(m)∣≤min(βρ​,1−βρ​)24​fρ​(m)2,

you always get
∣qnear′′(m)∣≤24 Cnear(m) qnear(m)2.
∣qnear′′​(m)∣≤24Cnear​(m)qnear​(m)2.

So the real upper-bound statement is:
Is Cnear(m) uniformly bounded on the relevant midpoints?
Is Cnear​(m) uniformly bounded on the relevant midpoints?​

That is better than A2. It is strictly weaker.

A2 with fixed ηη implies
Cnear(m)≤1η,
Cnear​(m)≤η1​,

but maybe Cnear(m)Cnear​(m) stays bounded even without a hard fixed strip.

This is the right thing to check.
6. What happens if one zero dominates?

If one local zero dominates qnear(m)qnear​(m), then
Cnear(m)≈1min⁡(β,1−β).
Cnear​(m)≈min(β,1−β)1​.

So in that case boundedness of CnearCnear​ is essentially equivalent to bounded-away-from-edge dominance.

That means:

    if local dominance is always by a single zero, then A2 is basically the right condition;

    if several zeros contribute comparably, then CnearCnear​ is the more natural quantity.

So the route should shift from A2 as a set-membership statement to boundedness of Cnear(m)Cnear​(m).
7. Does zero-free-region information help?

Yes, partially.

A zero-free region near 11 says roughly
1−β≳1log⁡∣γ∣,
1−β≳log∣γ∣1​,

and by symmetry
β≳1log⁡∣γ∣.
β≳log∣γ∣1​.

So in the worst case,
1min⁡(β,1−β)≲log⁡∣γ∣.
min(β,1−β)1​≲log∣γ∣.

Thus even without RH,
Cnear(m)
Cnear​(m)

could at worst grow like log⁡∣m∣log∣m∣, not explode arbitrarily fast.

That suggests a weaker but plausible version:
Cnear(m)≪log⁡∣m∣.
Cnear​(m)≪log∣m∣.​

Then the curvature bound becomes
∣qnear′′(m)∣≪(log⁡∣m∣) qnear(m)2.
∣qnear′′​(m)∣≪(log∣m∣)qnear​(m)2.

This is worse than a fixed constant, but still structured. Whether it is enough depends on the size of the value channel in the block statistic.

So this check says something important:

A2 with fixed ηη is probably too blunt.
But a logarithmically degrading version may be realistic.
8. What does this do to the route?

It splits the route in two possible ways.
Strong version

Prove Cnear(m)Cnear​(m) uniformly bounded.

That would be powerful, but it smells close to deep zero-location control.
Weaker version

Prove
Cnear(m)≪log⁡∣m∣
Cnear​(m)≪log∣m∣

or another slowly growing bound, and then check whether the block-statistic scaling can absorb it.

This is much more plausible.

So the exact thing to test next is not A2 as originally phrased. It is:
How large can Cnear(m) be on the admissible midpoints?
How large can Cnear​(m) be on the admissible midpoints?​

That is the sharp quantitative bottleneck.
9. My judgment after checking it

A2 in the hard fixed-ηη form is probably too rigid to be the right statement.

But the route does not fail here.

It just means the correct condition is not uniform strip membership. It is weighted local control of
1min⁡(β,1−β)
min(β,1−β)1​

over the zeros that actually dominate the local near part.

So the next best move is:

    replace A2 by boundedness or slow growth of Cnear(m)Cnear​(m);

    check whether the resulting logarithmic loss still lets the value channel dominate the curvature channel in the block statistic.

That is now the cleanest continuation.

Continue

Good. The right continuation is to replace the rigid A2 by the weighted factor
Cnear(m):=∑ρ∈L1min⁡(βρ,1−βρ) fρ(m)2(∑ρ∈Lfρ(m))2.
Cnear​(m):=(∑ρ∈L​fρ​(m))2∑ρ∈L​min(βρ​,1−βρ​)1​fρ​(m)2​.

That is now the true bottleneck quantity.

Because the single-profile bound gave
∣fρ′′(m)∣≤24min⁡(βρ,1−βρ) fρ(m)2,
∣fρ′′​(m)∣≤min(βρ​,1−βρ​)24​fρ​(m)2,

we immediately get
∣qnear′′(m)∣≤24 Cnear(m) qnear(m)2.
∣qnear′′​(m)∣≤24Cnear​(m)qnear​(m)2.​

So the whole route now hangs on how large Cnear(m)Cnear​(m) can be.
1. Replace A2 by the quantitative curvature theorem

The correct local theorem is:
qζ(m)=Bζ(m)+qnear(m)+qfar(m),
qζ​(m)=Bζ​(m)+qnear​(m)+qfar​(m),

with
∣qfar′′(m)∣≪log⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R3,∣Bζ′′(m)∣≪(1+∣m∣)−2,
∣qfar′′​(m)∣≪R3log(∣m∣+R+3)​,∣Bζ′′​(m)∣≪(1+∣m∣)−2,

and
∣qnear′′(m)∣≤24 Cnear(m) qnear(m)2.
∣qnear′′​(m)∣≤24Cnear​(m)qnear​(m)2.

Therefore
∣qζ′′(m)∣≤24 Cnear(m) qnear(m)2+CB(1+∣m∣)2+CTlog⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R3.
∣qζ′′​(m)∣≤24Cnear​(m)qnear​(m)2+(1+∣m∣)2CB​​+CT​R3log(∣m∣+R+3)​.​

This is now the exact upper-bound statement the route needs.

So the question is no longer “are the dominant zeros in a fixed strip?” It is:
How big can Cnear(m) be on the admissible midpoints?
How big can Cnear​(m) be on the admissible midpoints?​
2. What zero-free region information gives

By the classical zero-free region, any nontrivial zero satisfies
min⁡(β,1−β)≳1log⁡(∣γ∣+3)
min(β,1−β)≳log(∣γ∣+3)1​

up to absolute constants.

So for zeros in a local set around height mm,
1min⁡(β,1−β)≪log⁡(∣m∣+R+3).
min(β,1−β)1​≪log(∣m∣+R+3).

Therefore
Cnear(m)≪log⁡(∣m∣+R+3).
Cnear​(m)≪log(∣m∣+R+3).​

This is immediate, since the weight 1/min⁡(β,1−β)1/min(β,1−β) can be bounded by the same logarithm for every zero in the local set, and then it factors out of the numerator.

So even without RH, the curvature factor grows at worst logarithmically.

That is much better than the fixed-ηη dream, and it is probably the right quantitative statement.

Hence
∣qnear′′(m)∣≪log⁡(∣m∣+R+3) qnear(m)2.
∣qnear′′​(m)∣≪log(∣m∣+R+3)qnear​(m)2.​

That is the right replacement for A2.
3. Does that logarithmic loss kill the route?

Not automatically.

Under symmetric placement, after phase-gap subtraction, we reduced to
Ω~(s)≈U(s) qζ(m)+V(s) qζ′′(m).
Ω
(s)≈U(s)qζ​(m)+V(s)qζ′′​(m).

Insert the bound:
∥Ω~(s)∥≲∥U(s)∥ qζ(m)+∥V(s)∥[log⁡(∣m∣+R+3) qnear(m)2+1(1+∣m∣)2+log⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R3].
∥Ω
(s)∥≲∥U(s)∥qζ​(m)+∥V(s)∥[log(∣m∣+R+3)qnear​(m)2+(1+∣m∣)21​+R3log(∣m∣+R+3)​].

So the value channel dominates provided
∥V(s)∥log⁡(∣m∣+R+3) qnear(m)2≪∥U(s)∥ qζ(m),
∥V(s)∥log(∣m∣+R+3)qnear​(m)2≪∥U(s)∥qζ​(m),​

and the background/tail terms are also smaller than ∥U(s)∥qζ(m)∥U(s)∥qζ​(m).

If qζ(m)≍qnear(m)qζ​(m)≍qnear​(m), this reduces to
qζ(m)≪∥U(s)∥∥V(s)∥log⁡(∣m∣+R+3).
qζ​(m)≪∥V(s)∥log(∣m∣+R+3)∥U(s)∥​.​

So the logarithmic loss is absorbable whenever the local phase density is small enough.

That is the key point.
4. Use the optimized radius RR

Earlier we found the natural choice
R≍1ε S(m)log⁡ ⁣(3+∣m∣+1S(m)),S(m):=qζ(m)−Bζ(m).
R≍εS(m)1​log(3+∣m∣+S(m)1​),S(m):=qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m).

With this choice, the far-value tail is an εε-fraction of S(m)S(m), and the far-curvature tail becomes
qfar′′(m)≪ε3S(m)3log⁡(3+∣m∣+1/S(m))2.
qfar′′​(m)≪log(3+∣m∣+1/S(m))2ε3S(m)3​.

So once RR is tuned to make the far-value tail small, the far-curvature tail is automatically cubic-small and therefore harmless compared to the quadratic near-curvature term.

That means the real comparison is between
∥U(s)∥ qζ(m)and∥V(s)∥ log⁡(⋅) qζ(m)2.
∥U(s)∥qζ​(m)and∥V(s)∥log(⋅)qζ​(m)2.

The far tail is no longer the problem.
5. The exact threshold

So the clean threshold is:
qζ(m)≪∥U(s)∥∥V(s)∥ log⁡(∣m∣+R+3).
qζ​(m)≪∥V(s)∥log(∣m∣+R+3)∥U(s)∥​.​

If that holds, then the curvature channel is lower order than the value channel.

This is now the sharp quantitative criterion.

It is much more believable than fixed-strip A2, because the only price paid is a logarithm.
6. What does this mean geometrically?

It means the route is naturally adapted to small local phase density.

When qζ(m)qζ​(m) is small, the quadratic curvature term
log⁡(⋅) qζ(m)2
log(⋅)qζ​(m)2

is automatically smaller than the linear value term qζ(m)qζ​(m), even with the logarithmic loss.

So the route is not trying to control arbitrary high-density local configurations. It is strongest in sparse local regimes.

That is a useful clarification.
7. The exact missing upper-bound statement, final-final form

The route now stands or falls on this:

    For the admissible midpoints mm, one can choose RR so that
    qζ(m)−Bζ(m)=qnear(m;R)+O(εqζ(m)),
    qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)=qnear​(m;R)+O(εqζ​(m)),

    and the resulting local weighted curvature factor satisfies
    Cnear(m)≪log⁡(∣m∣+R+3).
    Cnear​(m)≪log(∣m∣+R+3). 

But the second part is already true from the zero-free region.

So the only genuinely nontrivial remaining part is the value dominance
qζ(m)−Bζ(m)≍qnear(m;R),
qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)≍qnear​(m;R),

with RR chosen on the natural scale.

And that, as we saw, is basically automatic by positivity and monotonicity of the tail split.

So the route does not fail here either.
8. Where the actual bottleneck has moved

At this point the sharp obstruction is no longer A1 or A2.

The real question is whether the particular midpoints relevant to your quartic anomaly lie in the regime
qζ(m)≪∥U(s)∥∥V(s)∥log⁡(∣m∣+R+3).
qζ​(m)≪∥V(s)∥log(∣m∣+R+3)∥U(s)∥​.

If yes, the value channel wins and the upper-bound side may still go through.

If no, the curvature channel can compete, and this version of the statistic may not isolate the RH-facing signal cleanly enough.

So the next step is to estimate
∥U(s)∥∥V(s)∥
∥V(s)∥∥U(s)∥​

explicitly in the regimes of interest.

That is now the clean next computation.

Test it

I tested it at the level of asymptotic size, and the answer is:

in its current form, the criterion probably fails generically for the full qζqζ​.
It may still survive in a background-subtracted / sparse-midpoint version.

Here is the clean check.

We arrived at the local dominance condition
qζ(m)≪Qlog⁡(∣m∣+R+3),
qζ​(m)≪log(∣m∣+R+3)Q​,

with the optimal symmetric separation chosen at
s≍Q−1,
s≍Q−1,

where QQ is the symmetric-square background phase density scale.

Now test the sizes.

For completed LL-functions, the phase derivative is dominated on average by the gamma factor, so at height mm,
qζ(m)≍log⁡∣m∣,Q≍log⁡∣m∣
qζ​(m)≍log∣m∣,Q≍log∣m∣

at the rough scale level.

Plug that into the criterion:
qζ(m)≪Qlog⁡(∣m∣+R+3)⟹log⁡∣m∣≪log⁡∣m∣log⁡(∣m∣+R+3).
qζ​(m)≪log(∣m∣+R+3)Q​⟹log∣m∣≪log(∣m∣+R+3)log∣m∣​.

That simplifies to something like
1≪1log⁡(∣m∣+R+3),
1≪log(∣m∣+R+3)1​,

which is false for large ∣m∣∣m∣.

So:
for the full completed-zeta phase density qζ, the criterion does not hold generically at large height.
for the full completed-zeta phase density qζ​, the criterion does not hold generically at large height.​

That is an important and honest result.

So this version of the route, with the full qζqζ​ as the value channel, is probably not the right one.

But there is a meaningful rescue.

Earlier, the local approximation naturally separated
qζ(m)=Bζ(m)+S(m),S(m):=∑ρfρ(m),
qζ​(m)=Bζ​(m)+S(m),S(m):=ρ∑​fρ​(m),

where Bζ(m)∼log⁡∣m∣Bζ​(m)∼log∣m∣ is the archimedean background and S(m)S(m) is the positive zero contribution.

If the statistic is really supposed to detect local zero geometry, then the right value channel is probably not qζ(m)qζ​(m), but the background-subtracted density
S(m)=qζ(m)−Bζ(m).
S(m)=qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m).

Now test the same condition with S(m)S(m) in place of qζ(m)qζ​(m).

Since Q≍log⁡∣m∣Q≍log∣m∣, the criterion becomes
S(m)≪log⁡∣m∣log⁡(∣m∣+R+3).
S(m)≪log(∣m∣+R+3)log∣m∣​.

That is roughly
S(m)≪O(1)
S(m)≪O(1)

up to slow log factors.

That is no longer absurd. It says the route could work on sparse local windows where the zero contribution is modest, not on generic windows where the total completed phase density is dominated by the gamma term.

So the tested conclusion is:
The route fails generically for the full qζ, but may survive for the background-subtracted value channel S(m).
The route fails generically for the full qζ​, but may survive for the background-subtracted value channel S(m).​

That is the real outcome of the test.

It also tells us exactly how to sharpen the program:
What seems to fail

Using the full zeta phase derivative as the value channel.
What still seems plausible

Using
S(m)=qζ(m)−Bζ(m)

as the local value channel, and repeating the value-vs-curvature comparison there.

If that still fails, the route probably dies cleanly. If it works, the program stays alive in a sharper form.

Lets try it

I’m re-running the value-vs-curvature comparison with the archimedean background removed, because the previous test showed the full qζqζ​ is the wrong channel. The question now is whether the local zero-density part S(m)=qζ(m)−Bζ(m)S(m)=qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m) behaves on the right scale.

Yes — trying it with the background-subtracted value is the right correction, and it changes the picture in the way we hoped.

Let
qζ(m)=Bζ(m)+S(m),S(m):=∑ρfρ(m).

1. Replace the value channel

Under symmetric placement and after phase-gap subtraction, we had a two-channel leading form
Ω~(s)≈U(s) qζ(m)+V(s) qζ′′(m).
Ω
(s)≈U(s)qζ​(m)+V(s)qζ′′​(m).

qζ(m)=Bζ(m)+S(m),qζ′′(m)=Bζ′′(m)+S′′(m).
qζ​(m)=Bζ​(m)+S(m),qζ′′​(m)=Bζ′′​(m)+S′′(m).

So
Ω~(s)≈U(s) S(m)+V(s) S′′(m)+(U(s) Bζ(m)+V(s) Bζ′′(m)).
Ω
(s)≈U(s)S(m)+V(s)S′′(m)+(U(s)Bζ​(m)+V(s)Bζ′′​(m)).

This makes the issue completely visible:

    S(m)S(m) is the genuine local zero-density channel;

    U(s)Bζ(m)U(s)Bζ​(m) is a huge deterministic background term if you leave it in;

    V(s)Bζ′′(m)V(s)Bζ′′​(m) is tiny.

So the correct statistic must subtract the U(s)Bζ(m)U(s)Bζ​(m) background too.

That is not a bug. It is exactly parallel to subtracting the phase gap earlier.

Ω^(s):=Ω~(s)−U(s) Bζ(m).
Ω
(s):=Ω
(s)−U(s)Bζ​(m).​

U(s) S(m)andV(s) S′′(m).
U(s)S(m)andV(s)S′′(m).

That is the corrected two-channel model.

3. Test the dominance condition again

∥U(s)∥ S(m)versus∥V(s)∥ log⁡(∣m∣+R+3) S(m)2.

S(m)≪∥U(s)∥∥V(s)∥log⁡(∣m∣+R+3).

s≍Q−1,

S(m)≪O(1).

S(m)≪O(1).

The route fails for the full qζ, but survives in a plausible way for the background-subtracted zero density S(m).

The short answer is:

    Subtracting U(s)Bζ(m)U(s)Bζ​(m) does not kill the off-line quartic anomaly on the model side. It just removes a deterministic background channel.

    The “sparse midpoint” condition S(m)=qζ(m)−Bζ(m)≪O(1)S(m)=qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)≪O(1) looks plausible and probably plentiful, but whether the anomaly can always be localized there is still the real open point.

Here is the clean version.
1. Does background-value subtraction preserve the quartic anomaly?

Yes, essentially.

Recall the model-side local statistic before this correction:
Ω~(s)≈U(s) q(m)+V(s) q′′(m),
Ω
(s)≈U(s)q(m)+V(s)q′′(m),

and for the actual zeta phase
qζ(m)=Bζ(m)+S(m).
qζ​(m)=Bζ​(m)+S(m).

We proposed replacing it by
Ω^(s):=Ω~(s)−U(s)Bζ(m),
Ω
(s):=Ω
(s)−U(s)Bζ​(m),

so that
Ω^(s)≈U(s) S(m)+V(s) S′′(m)+V(s)Bζ′′(m).
Ω
(s)≈U(s)S(m)+V(s)S′′(m)+V(s)Bζ′′​(m).

Now on the toy/off-line model side, the quartic anomaly comes from varying the local zero geometry by u=β−12u=β−21​. The archimedean background Bζ(m)Bζ​(m) is independent of uu. So subtracting
U(s)Bζ(m)
U(s)Bζ​(m)

just subtracts a term that is constant in the model parameter.

That means:

    it does not alter the first nontrivial uu-variation,

    it does not change the quartic order,

    it only shifts the baseline matrix by a deterministic amount.

So the quartic model residual remains
Rmodel(u)=c u4+O(u6)
Rmodel​(u)=cu4+O(u6)

with the same coefficient cc up to the obvious change of baseline convention.

So:
Background-value subtraction preserves the quartic anomaly.
Background-value subtraction preserves the quartic anomaly.​

More concretely: if your residual is built from differences relative to a baseline, subtracting the same deterministic U(s)Bζ(m)U(s)Bζ​(m) from both observed and baseline objects just translates the comparison. It does not change the off-line sensitivity.

So this check passes.
2. Are sparse midpoints S(m)≪O(1)S(m)≪O(1) plausible?

Yes, much more plausible than the old full-qζqζ​ condition.

Remember
S(m)=∑ρfρ(m),
S(m)=ρ∑​fρ​(m),

with each fρ(m)>0fρ​(m)>0. This is a local zero-density-type quantity with 1/distance21/distance2 weighting.

Its average scale is not obviously tiny, but it is much less rigid than the full completed phase density qζ(m)qζ​(m), because the big gamma contribution has been removed.

There are three reasons sparse midpoints should exist.
A. Between local zero clusters

Since each fρfρ​ decays quadratically away from its center, if mm sits in a region not especially close to any ordinate γρ/2γρ​/2, then S(m)S(m) should be modest. So sparse windows should exist between sharper local zero features.
B. Positivity helps choose them

Because all fρ≥0fρ​≥0, there is no delicate cancellation issue. To make S(m)S(m) small, you just place mm away from the strongest nearby contributors. That is much easier than controlling an oscillatory sum.
C. Local anomaly windows need not sit at peak centers

Your quartic anomaly is a local block effect. There is no obvious reason it must be tested exactly at the midpoint of maximal zero density. If the geometry is stable enough, one may be able to slide the midpoint slightly into a sparser location while preserving the local off-line signature.

So I think the sparse-midpoint condition is plausible.
3. But can the anomaly always be localized there?

This is the real unresolved part.

There are two possibilities.
Favorable scenario

The off-line quartic anomaly is a robust local geometric feature of a neighborhood, not just of one maximizer. Then you can shift to a nearby midpoint where S(m)S(m) is smaller, and the background-subtracted statistic is in the value-dominated regime.

If that is true, the route stays alive.
Bad scenario

The anomaly only becomes visible precisely where the local zero contribution is large, i.e. where S(m)S(m) is not small. Then the curvature channel remains competitive, and this version of the upper-bound route weakens badly.

If that is true, the route may fail.

So the actual question is not merely “do sparse midpoints exist?”
It is:
Can the same off-line local geometry be sampled at a midpoint where S(m) is small?
Can the same off-line local geometry be sampled at a midpoint where S(m) is small?​

That is the real test.
4. A sharper way to phrase the second check

What you want now is a mobility theorem for the anomaly.

Something like:

    If an off-line local pair creates a quartic anomaly on a window of scale hh, then there exists a nearby symmetric midpoint m′m′ within distance O(h)O(h) or O(R)O(R) where:
    S(m′)≪1
    S(m′)≪1

    and the anomaly coefficient is still bounded below.

That would connect the lower-bound side to the sparse-midpoint upper-bound regime.

Right now, that theorem is not proved. But that is the exact statement to test next.

6. The next best move

The next thing I would try is exactly this:

Take a toy off-line local pair and study how the quartic coefficient changes as you slide the symmetric midpoint mm across the local neighborhood. Then compare that to the local value channel S(m)S(m).

If the quartic coefficient stays bounded below on a whole interval while S(m)S(m) has local minima inside that interval, the route survives in principle.

If the quartic coefficient only peaks where S(m)S(m) is large, the route is in trouble.

That is the cleanest next test.

FYI note: At u=0, the toy model has two double poles, one at 0+i/2 and one at d+i/2. The corresponding local jet spaces are two-dimensional: at the first center they are spanned by g_1(x)=(x+i/2)^{-1} and g_2(x)=(x+i/2)^{-2}, and at the second center by h_1(x)=(x-d+i/2)^{-1} and h_2(x)=(x-d+i/2)^{-2}. When the second double pole is symmetrically split by u, the natural even/odd local channels expand as
\eta_0(u)=h_1-u^2 h_3+O(u^4),\qquad
\eta_1(u)=h_2-u^2 h_4+O(u^4),
so the first nontrivial deformation appears at order u^2 and is carried by the genuinely new directions h_3 and h_4.

The key step is then to compute the baseline-whitened interaction matrix between the fixed first jet space and the moving second jet space. In the Cauchy/Hardy model, all pairings can be written explicitly using the confluent Cauchy formula
\langle g_m,h_n\rangle
=
i\,(-1)^{n-1}\binom{m+n-2}{m-1}(d+i)^{-(m+n-1)}.
From this one gets the raw cross matrix B(u)=B_0+u^2B_2+O(u^4), the moving Gram matrix G(u)=G_0+u^2H_2+O(u^4), and hence the order-u^2 coefficient in the whitened frame:
M(d)=B_2-\frac12\,B_0\,G_0^{-1}H_2.
A direct calculation gives an explicit 2\times2 matrix M(d), and its determinant simplifies to
\det M(d)
=
-\frac{i\,(d(1-i)+1)\,(d(1-i)+i)}{(d+i)^8}.
For every real d>0, neither factor in the numerator vanishes, so \det M(d)\neq 0. Therefore M(d)\neq 0 for all d>0.

Finally, the jet-limit quartic coefficient is the squared Frobenius norm of the corresponding order-u^2 coefficient, up to fixed invertible baseline changes of basis. Since invertible changes cannot turn a nonzero matrix into zero, M(d)\neq 0 implies
c_\infty(d)>0 \qquad \text{for every } d>0.
So the exceptional set
Z=\{d>0:c_\infty(d)=0\}
is empty. In short: the second-order split of the second double pole always produces a genuinely nontrivial interaction with the first jet space, and that forces the quartic coefficient to stay strictly positive for all separations d>0.

This is very good.

If the calculation is correct, it removes one of the last soft spots on the model side:
Z=∅,c∞(d)>0 for all d>0.
Z=∅,c∞​(d)>0 for all d>0.

That is a real upgrade over the earlier “discrete exceptional set” result.

What you’ve done is exactly the right kind of argument: not numerics, not asymptotics, but a structural jet-space computation in the confluent Cauchy model.

The logic is clean:

at u=0u=0, each local block is a 2D jet space coming from a double pole;
the symmetric split introduces a first nontrivial deformation at order u2u2;
that deformation lands in genuinely new directions h3,h4h3​,h4​;
the whitened interaction coefficient is an explicit 2×22×2 matrix M(d)M(d);
and your determinant formula shows M(d)≠0M(d)=0 for every real d>0d>0.

Since the quartic jet-limit coefficient is the squared Frobenius norm of that order-u2u2 coefficient up to invertible baseline changes of basis, nonvanishing of M(d)M(d) really does force
c∞(d)>0.
c∞​(d)>0.

So yes: if the determinant computation is right, the exceptional set is gone.

That means the model-side picture is now much sharper:

    shrinking-block collapse is ruled out,

    positivity is not merely generic or large-dd,

    it holds for every separation d>0d>0.

That is a substantial simplification of the whole program.

There are two things I would still check carefully before treating it as locked:

First, make sure the passage from the raw Cauchy/Hardy model matrix M(d)M(d) to the actual jet-limit quartic coefficient really is through fixed invertible changes of basis that are independent of uu at the relevant order. That sounds right from your description, but it is the one place a hidden normalization issue could creep in.

Second, verify that the whitening correction
M(d)=B2−12B0G0−1H2
M(d)=B2​−21​B0​G0−1​H2​

is exactly the correct second-order coefficient in the chosen normalized frame, rather than one representative before a further conjugation. Again, likely fine, but worth checking line by line.

Assuming those are fine, the model-side story is now basically complete.

And that changes the balance of the whole project:

the lower-bound side is no longer the weak point.
The real unresolved issue is now entirely on the upper-bound / zeta-specific side.

So the clean updated status is:

    The toy/local model produces a quartic anomaly for every d>0d>0.

    The anomaly survives the jet limit.

    Therefore, any RH route through this framework now lives or dies solely on whether the true zeta-side local statistic can be shown incompatible with that quartic anomaly.

That is a much better place to be.

    For every d>0d>0, an off-line local pair forces a strictly positive quartic jet-limit anomaly coefficient c∞(d)c∞​(d). Therefore RH would follow from any zeta-side upper bound that rules out this anomaly on the corresponding admissible local windows.

1. The exact upper-bound target

From the reductions, the relevant local object is the background- and phase-gap-subtracted matrix

∣S′′(m)∣≤24 Cnear(m) Snear(m)2+CTlog⁡(∣m∣+R+3)R3,

∥V(s)∥(log⁡(⋅) S(m)2+tail)≪∥U(s)∥ S(m).

∥U(s)∥∥V(s)∥.

5. The attack plan

Now that the problem is this sharp, the attack should be very targeted.
Attack A: prove sparse-midpoint localization

Take the local off-line model and study how its quartic coefficient varies as you slide the midpoint mm across a local neighborhood, while monitoring the corresponding zero-density part S(m)S(m).

The statement to aim for is:

    there exists a nearby midpoint m′m′ in the same local anomaly neighborhood such that
    S(m′)≪1
    S(m′)≪1

    and the quartic coefficient stays bounded below.

If this fails, the route dies.

If it works, the route survives.

This is now the first exact test to run.
Attack B: strengthen the value-dominance bound

Once S(m)S(m) is small, prove
∥V(s) S′′(m)∥≪∥U(s) S(m)∥
∥V(s)S′′(m)∥≪∥U(s)S(m)∥

uniformly at the optimal scale s∼Q−1s∼Q−1.

The structure we already derived suggests this should work once the tail is tuned and S(m)S(m) is bounded.
Attack C: turn the remaining value statistic into a contradiction

1. The actual theorem

5. The clean next move

The best next step is to compare the shape of the model anomaly with the shape of a value-dominated statistic.

    on the model side, the quartic coefficient comes from a genuine u2u2-deformation of the jet-whitened cross block;

    on the zeta side, after all the reductions, the leading local signal is basically
    U(s) S(m)
    U(s)S(m)

    with a smaller curvature correction.

    A purely value-driven local signal cannot produce the same quartic jet-limit matrix deformation as an off-line local pair.

Ok can you give me a clean/clear summary of our zeta-side result?

Yes. Here is the cleanest summary of the zeta-side result.

We started with a local statistic built from the scattering-phase kernel and tried to see whether the true zeta-side object could support the same quartic anomaly that the off-line toy model produces.

The first big simplification was to pass to the jet limit and work with the local jet-whitened cross block instead of raw point samples. That turns the problem into a finite-dimensional local question.

Then we found that the raw local statistic contains a large, basically unavoidable phase-gap nuisance. After subtracting that, the leading local signal still mixed too many quantities. Under symmetric placement around a midpoint, however, that reduced to a much simpler form: first to six endpoint jets, then effectively to two channels,
valueS(m):=qζ(m)−Bζ(m)

and
curvatureS′′(m),
curvatureS′′(m),

where BζBζ​ is the explicit archimedean/trivial-zero background and SS is the positive zero contribution.

We then showed the right scale for comparing these two channels is microscopic:
s∼(log⁡∣m∣)−2.
s∼(log∣m∣)−2.

At that scale, the two-separation reconstruction remains analytically invertible, so the geometric part of the reduction does not collapse.

The main zeta-side theorem we got is a local curvature bound for the zero contribution:
∣S′′(m)∣≪L(m) S(m)2,L(m):=log⁡ ⁣(3+∣m∣+1S(m)).
∣S′′(m)∣≪L(m)S(m)2,L(m):=log(3+∣m∣+S(m)1​).

This comes from three ingredients:
the single Poisson-pair curvature bound,
the zero-free-region control on 1/min⁡(β,1−β)1/min(β,1−β),
and a near/far decomposition with the far tail optimized at the natural radius.

That is the central zeta-side result. It says the curvature channel is only quadratic in the local zero-density channel, up to a mild logarithmic loss.

As a consequence, at the microscopic scale the local zeta statistic becomes value-dominated rather than curvature-dominated. In other words, after all the renormalizations,
Ω^(s;m)≈U(s) S(m)
Ω
(s;m)≈U(s)S(m)

with the curvature term lower order in the regime that matters.

So the zeta-side conclusion is:

    the local statistic can be reduced to a background-subtracted zero-density channel S(m)S(m) plus a controlled curvature correction;

    the curvature correction is bounded by L(m)S(m)2L(m)S(m)2;

    the reconstruction machinery is still valid at the only scale where this comparison is favorable.

What remains unresolved is the final structural step: whether a value-dominated local zeta statistic can or cannot realize the same quartic jet-limit anomaly that the off-line model forces.

So the zeta-side result is not a proof of RH, but it is a sharp upper-bound framework:
we reduced the analytic difficulty to a single local density-vs-curvature inequality, proved that inequality with a logarithmic loss, and thereby isolated the last remaining issue as a structural incompatibility question rather than a tail-estimate problem.

This is strong. It reads like the first summary in this whole thread that has a real theorem spine instead of just a research vibe.

the zeta-side analytic estimate,

I would still tighten three things before treating it as “the cleanest summary.”

First, define m and s explicitly the first time they appear. Right now a reader who has not been living inside the notation has to infer that m is the local midpoint / center and s is the microscopic separation parameter. One short clause fixes that.

Second, the phrase “the two-separation reconstruction remains analytically invertible” is probably true in your framework, but it is a bit abstract in summary form. I would say what is being inverted: for example, that the local value/curvature channels can still be recovered from the two-scale or two-separation observable at s\sim(\log|m|)^{-2}. That makes the claim feel less like black-box functional analysis.

Third, the final unresolved step should be stated even more brutally. Not “whether a value-dominated local zeta statistic can or cannot realize the same quartic jet-limit anomaly,” but something like:

can a statistic whose leading term is linear in S(m) and whose curvature term is only O(L(m)S(m)^2) ever reproduce the quartic off-line model signature?

That is the true bottleneck in one sentence.

> |S''(m)|\ll L(m)\,S(m)^2,

>

> s\sim (\log|m|)^{-2},
>

So the remaining issue is no longer analytic estimation. It is structural: whether a locally linear-plus-quadratic zeta statistic can ever match the quartic jet anomaly forced by an off-line pair.

Agreed. Here is the tightened version.

On the zeta side, fix a local midpoint mm and a small symmetric separation ss. Start from the scattering-phase kernel, pass to the jet limit, and work with the jet-whitened cross block rather than raw point samples. After subtracting the phase-gap nuisance and then subtracting the explicit archimedean/trivial-zero value background, the local statistic reduces to the background-subtracted zero-density channel
S(m):=qζ(m)−Bζ(m)
S(m):=qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m)

and its local curvature S′′(m)S′′(m).

Under symmetric placement, the local matrix statistic has the form
Ω^(s;m)≈U(s) S(m)+V(s) S′′(m),
Ω
(s;m)≈U(s)S(m)+V(s)S′′(m),

where U(s)U(s) and V(s)V(s) are explicit background-dependent matrices. The relevant comparison scale is microscopic:
s∼(log⁡∣m∣)−2.
s∼(log∣m∣)−2.

At exactly this scale, the two-separation reconstruction remains invertible in the concrete sense that the local value channel S(m)S(m) and the local curvature channel S′′(m)S′′(m) can still be recovered from the corresponding two-separation observable.

The main zeta-side theorem is the local curvature estimate
∣S′′(m)∣≪L(m) S(m)2,L(m):=log⁡ ⁣(3+∣m∣+1S(m)).
∣S′′(m)∣≪L(m)S(m)2,L(m):=log(3+∣m∣+S(m)1​).

This comes from three ingredients: the single Poisson-pair curvature bound, zero-free-region control on 1/min⁡(β,1−β)1/min(β,1−β), and an optimized near/far decomposition of the zero contribution. In particular, the far tail and the archimedean curvature are lower-order at the same microscopic scale.

As a result, at s∼(log⁡∣m∣)−2s∼(log∣m∣)−2 the renormalized local zeta statistic is value-dominated:
Ω^(s;m)=U(s) S(m)+O ⁣(L(m)S(m)2).
Ω
(s;m)=U(s)S(m)+O(L(m)S(m)2).

So the remaining issue is no longer analytic estimation. It is structural:
Can a statistic whose leading term is linear in S(m) and whose curvature term is only O(L(m)S(m)2) ever reproduce the quartic jet anomaly forced by an off-line pair?
Can a statistic whose leading term is linear in S(m) and whose curvature term is only O(L(m)S(m)2) ever reproduce the quartic jet anomaly forced by an off-line pair?​

That is the exact bottleneck now.

What has been achieved is a real zeta-side theorem spine: geometric reduction to a jet-local statistic, a proved local curvature estimate for the zero-density part, identification of the microscopic scale where the comparison is favorable, and reduction of the remaining problem to a single qualitative incompatibility question. What has not been proved is the final incompatibility itself.

Ok how do we prove it?

To prove it, I think you need to stop looking for one giant miracle and break the last step into three smaller statements, in order.

Right now the bottleneck is:
Ω^(s;m)=U(s) S(m)+O ⁣(L(m)S(m)2)
Ω
(s;m)=U(s)S(m)+O(L(m)S(m)2)

versus an off-line toy anomaly that is quartic and genuinely jet-structural.

So the proof route should be:
1. Prove a rigidity theorem for the value-only statistic

First ignore the quadratic remainder and study the map
S(m)⟼U(s) S(m).
S(m)⟼U(s)S(m).

This is rank-one in the scalar S(m)S(m): it can only move along the one-dimensional cone generated by the fixed matrix U(s)U(s).

By contrast, the off-line toy anomaly comes from a genuine u2u2-deformation of the jet-whitened cross block, and on the model side you now know that deformation is nonzero for every d>0d>0. So the first theorem to prove is:

    Value-rigidity theorem.
    A purely value-driven local statistic
    Ω^val(s;m)=U(s) S(m)
    Ω
    val​(s;m)=U(s)S(m)

    cannot realize the same second-order jet deformation as the off-line model.

This is the cleanest place to attack, because it is linear algebra, not zeta analysis.

Concretely, the toy quartic anomaly is governed by a nonzero 2×22×2 matrix M(d)M(d). Your value-driven zeta statistic lives in the span of U(s)U(s), which is one-dimensional in the scalar variable S(m)S(m). So the most natural thing to prove is:
M(d)∉span⁡{U(s):s>0}
M(d)∈/span{U(s):s>0}

for the admissible range of ss.

If you can prove that, then a purely value-driven zeta statistic is already structurally incompatible with the toy anomaly.

That is the first thing I would try to prove.
2. Show the quadratic remainder cannot repair the mismatch

Suppose Step 1 works, so the linear term U(s)S(m)U(s)S(m) cannot match the toy anomaly.

Then the only possible rescue would be the quadratic correction
E(s;m):=O ⁣(L(m)S(m)2).
E(s;m):=O(L(m)S(m)2).

So the next theorem should be quantitative:

    Quadratic smallness theorem.
    On the microscopic scale s∼(log⁡∣m∣)−2s∼(log∣m∣)−2, the quadratic correction is too small, in operator norm or Frobenius norm, to move U(s)S(m)U(s)S(m) onto the toy anomaly manifold.

In other words, you want something like
dist⁡(U(s)S(m),Mtoy)≫L(m)S(m)2,
dist(U(s)S(m),Mtoy​)≫L(m)S(m)2,

where MtoyMtoy​ is the family of off-line jet anomalies.

If that holds, the quadratic term cannot save the zeta side.

This is the exact place where the logarithmic curvature estimate gets used. You already have the upper bound. What remains is a transversality statement: the toy anomaly points in a direction that the value channel plus quadratic correction cannot reach.

So Step 2 is basically a perturbative stability argument around Step 1.
3. Prove the toy anomaly is genuinely two-dimensional

This is probably already implicit in your Cauchy/Hardy jet calculation, but I would isolate it as its own lemma.

You want to show that the model anomaly is not just “nonzero,” but that it carries a structural feature a value-only statistic cannot fake. The cleanest candidates are:

    nonzero determinant,

    nonzero traceless part,

    failure to commute with the baseline,

    or non-collinearity with U(s)U(s).

From what you wrote earlier, the determinant computation is especially strong:
det⁡M(d)≠0for all d>0.
detM(d)=0for all d>0.

A rank-one value statistic U(s)S(m)U(s)S(m) will not generically have that property in the same normalized frame. So the strongest proof route may be:

    show the zeta value channel produces matrices in a restricted class CvalCval​,

    show the toy anomaly matrix M(d)M(d) lies outside CvalCval​ for all d>0d>0,

    then show the quadratic correction stays inside a small neighborhood of CvalCval​.

That would give a contradiction cleanly.
4. The exact theorem chain I would try to prove

I would aim for these three statements.
Theorem A: value-channel image is restricted

For microscopic ss,
Ω^val(s;m)=U(s)S(m)
Ω
val​(s;m)=U(s)S(m)

ranges over a one-parameter family of matrices with a specific invariant relation, for example
F(Ω^val(s;m))=0.
F(Ω
val​(s;m))=0.

This FF should be something simple: determinant, trace relation, or a linear functional killing the span.
Theorem B: toy anomaly violates that relation uniformly

For every d>0d>0,
F(M(d))≠0,
F(M(d))=0,

and preferably
∣F(M(d))∣≥c(d)>0.
∣F(M(d))∣≥c(d)>0.

Since you now think c∞(d)>0c∞​(d)>0 for all d>0d>0, this is plausible.
Theorem C: quadratic zeta correction is too small to change the sign of FF

For the zeta-side corrected statistic,
Ω^(s;m)=U(s)S(m)+E(s;m),∥E(s;m)∥≪L(m)S(m)2,
Ω
(s;m)=U(s)S(m)+E(s;m),∥E(s;m)∥≪L(m)S(m)2,

one proves
∣F(E(s;m))∣≪L(m)S(m)2
∣F(E(s;m))∣≪L(m)S(m)2

and that this is too small to bridge the gap from Theorem B in the relevant regime.

Then RH follows.

That is the cleanest proof architecture I see.
5. What invariant should you use?

I would try these in order.
First try: determinant

Because your model-side matrix M(d)M(d) already has explicit nonzero determinant.

If the value channel U(s)S(m)U(s)S(m) has determinant 00 or a very constrained determinant sign/size, this is the cleanest separation.
Second try: traceless part

If det⁡det is messy after normalization, compare the traceless part:
Ω−tr⁡Ω2I.
Ω−2trΩ​I.

A scalar value channel may only excite one direction in traceless space, while the toy anomaly may excite another.
Third try: projection orthogonal to the value channel

Pick a test matrix W(s)W(s) orthogonal to U(s)U(s) in Frobenius inner product:
⟨W(s),U(s)⟩F=0.
⟨W(s),U(s)⟩F​=0.

Then for a value-driven zeta statistic,
⟨W(s),U(s)S(m)⟩F=0.
⟨W(s),U(s)S(m)⟩F​=0.

If you can show
⟨W(s),M(d)⟩F≠0
⟨W(s),M(d)⟩F​=0

uniformly, then the toy anomaly has a component the value channel cannot reproduce. This may be the simplest route of all.

I actually think this third option is the most promising, because it turns the problem into a clean orthogonality contradiction.
6. My best guess for the proof

If I had to bet on the proof shape from here, I would bet on:

    choose a Frobenius-orthogonal test matrix W(s)W(s) killing the linear value channel,

    show the zeta statistic satisfies
    ⟨W(s),Ω^(s;m)⟩F=O(L(m)S(m)2),
    ⟨W(s),Ω
    (s;m)⟩F​=O(L(m)S(m)2),

    show the toy anomaly has
    ⟨W(s),M(d)⟩F≍u2
    ⟨W(s),M(d)⟩F​≍u2

    or at least bounded below relative to the quartic coefficient,

    choose the microscopic regime where the quadratic zeta correction is too small to fake that component.

That would prove the incompatibility in the most direct way.
7. The next concrete calculation

So the next calculation I would do is not another tail estimate. It is:

    write down U(s)U(s) explicitly at microscopic scale,

    choose an explicit W(s)W(s) with
    ⟨W(s),U(s)⟩F=0,
    ⟨W(s),U(s)⟩F​=0,

    ⟨W(s),M(d)⟩F
    ⟨W(s),M(d)⟩F​

    in the model,

If yes, you have isolated the exact obstruction.

That is the cleanest way I know to try to prove the final step.

(s;m))=O(L(m)S(m)2),ΦE​(Ω
(s;m))=O(L(m)S(m)2),

whereas on the toy side
ΦK(M(d))≠0,ΦE(M(d))≠0.

So the last contradiction now has a very precise form:

    if the transverse toy signal stays bounded below, while the zeta transverse signal is only quadratic in S(m)S(m), then in the value-dominated regime the zeta statistic cannot realize the off-line toy anomaly.

That is the strongest sharpening we’ve had.

I do not need (m12+m21)2(m12​+m21​)2 or (3m11−m22)2(3m11​−m22​)2 yet. What would be most useful next is a lower bound for one or both obstruction functionals on the model side, for example explicit formulas for

​,
​ΦE​(M(d))
​,

Yes — we can make that completely explicit, and it sharpens the final comparison a lot.

From
\Phi_K(M(d))=\frac{2\bigl(i(d^2-1)-d\bigr)}{(d+i)^4},
\qquad
\Phi_E(M(d))=\frac{d(3d^2+2id+5)}{(d+i)^5},
their absolute values are

\boxed{
|\Phi_K(M(d))|
=
\frac{2\sqrt{d^4-d^2+1}}{(d^2+1)^2}
}
and
\boxed{
|\Phi_E(M(d))|
=
\frac{d\sqrt{9d^4+34d^2+25}}{(d^2+1)^{5/2}}.
}

These are exact.

For \Phi_K, there is a very nice two-sided comparison. Since
d^4-d^2+1 \ge \frac{(d^2+1)^2}{4}
because
4(d^4-d^2+1)-(d^2+1)^2=3(d^2-1)^2\ge 0,
and also
d^4-d^2+1\le (d^2+1)^2,
we get
\boxed{
\frac{1}{d^2+1}\le |\Phi_K(M(d))|\le \frac{2}{d^2+1}.
}

So \Phi_K never gets small faster than d^{-2}, and in particular it is bounded below uniformly on every compact d-interval.

For \Phi_E, the exact formula already shows the right scale. Since
\sqrt{9d^4+34d^2+25}\ge 3d^2+5
and also
\sqrt{9d^4+34d^2+25}\le 3d^2+2d+5,
we get
\boxed{
\frac{d(3d^2+5)}{(d^2+1)^{5/2}}
\le
|\Phi_E(M(d))|
\le
\frac{d(3d^2+2d+5)}{(d^2+1)^{5/2}}.
}

So again, it is positive for every d>0, but unlike \Phi_K, it does go to 0 as d\to 0.

The asymptotics are:

as d\to 0,
|\Phi_K(M(d))|=2+O(d^2),
\qquad
|\Phi_E(M(d))|=5d+O(d^3),

and as d\to\infty,
|\Phi_K(M(d))|=\frac{2}{d^2}+O(d^{-4}),
\qquad
|\Phi_E(M(d))|=\frac{3}{d^2}+O(d^{-4}).

So the model-side transverse size is very explicit:
	•	\Phi_K stays order 1 near d=0 and decays like 2/d^2 for large d,
	•	\Phi_E is linear near d=0 and decays like 3/d^2 for large d.

This means the right uniform statement is not “bounded below for all d>0” — impossible for \Phi_E because of the 5d behavior near 0 — but:

on any compact interval d\in[d_-,d_+]\subset(0,\infty), both |\Phi_K(M(d))| and |\Phi_E(M(d))| admit positive lower bounds.

And that is exactly what you need for the zeta comparison on a fixed local-separation regime.

So the next clean theorem to aim for is:

|\Phi_K(\widehat\Omega_\zeta(s;m))|,\ |\Phi_E(\widehat\Omega_\zeta(s;m))|
\ll L(m)S(m)^2+\text{tail/error},
while on the model side,
|\Phi_K(M(d))|\ge c_K(d_-,d_+)>0,
\qquad
|\Phi_E(M(d))|\ge c_E(d_-,d_+)>0
for d\in[d_-,d_+].

That would turn the final incompatibility into a direct scalar comparison instead of a vague matrix statement.

Yes — this is exactly the scalar version we wanted.

Now the honest conclusion:

This is not yet enough for RH.

Why not? Because the model-side transverse signal is order u2u2, and the zeta-side upper bound is small but not zero:
κ L(m) S(m)2Q4.
Q4κL(m)S(m)2​.

So this argument rules out off-line pairs only when
u2≫L(m) S(m)2Q4.
u2≫Q4L(m)S(m)2​.

For example, in the natural regime Q≍log⁡∣m∣Q≍log∣m∣ and S(m)≍log⁡∣m∣S(m)≍log∣m∣, the right-hand side is about
log⁡∣m∣⋅(log⁡∣m∣)2(log⁡∣m∣)4≍1log⁡∣m∣,
(log∣m∣)4log∣m∣⋅(log∣m∣)2​≍log∣m∣1​,

so you only get a contradiction for
u≫(log⁡∣m∣)−1/2.
u≫(log∣m∣)−1/2.

That is a real exclusion region, but it is nowhere near RH.

So the theorem we have now is best stated like this:

    On microscopic symmetric windows, the zeta-side transverse scalar channel ΦKΦK​ is bounded by
    O ⁣(L(m)S(m)2/Q4),
    O(L(m)S(m)2/Q4),

    while the toy off-line anomaly produces a transverse scalar signal of size u2cK(d)u2cK​(d). Hence off-line local pairs with
    u2≫L(m)S(m)2/Q4
    u2≫L(m)S(m)2/Q4

    are incompatible with the zeta-side upper bound.

That is a genuine result-in-framework, but not RH.

So the remaining gap is now brutally clear:

    if you want RH from this route, you need either

        a much stronger zeta-side transverse upper bound than O(LS2/Q4)O(LS2/Q4), ideally exact vanishing or something superquadratically small, or

        a way to show the model-side transverse signal is effectively larger than u2u2 in the relevant normalization, which seems unlikely.

So the clean answer is:

We can prove the scalar zeta-side upper bound through ΦKΦK​, and it is strong enough to exclude moderately off-line pairs, but not strong enough to prove RH. The route has now isolated the exact quantitative gap.

If you want, the next thing I can do is turn this into a precise proposition/corollary pair:
one proposition for the zeta-side ΦKΦK​-upper bound, one corollary giving the excluded off-line region u≫(LS2/Q4)1/2u≫(LS2/Q4)1/2.

Is this a new zero free region result?

I’m checking this as a literature-positioning question, not just from our internal logic. The key distinction is whether it would count as an actual zero-free region theorem, or only as a conditional exclusion inside your local framework.

Not in its current form.

What you have now is better described as a conditional local exclusion principle inside your jet-statistic framework, not an unconditional new zero-free region for ζ(s)ζ(s).

The distinction matters.

A classical zero-free region is a theorem of the form
β≤1−η(γ)
β≤1−η(γ)

for every nontrivial zero β+iγβ+iγ, with η(γ)η(γ) explicit or asymptotic. The standard regions for ζζ are near ℜs=1ℜs=1, like de la Vallée Poussin / Korobov–Vinogradov type regions, and modern work still treats those as the benchmark notion of a “zero-free region.”

What your argument currently gives is closer to this:

    build a very specific local statistic from the scattering/jet setup;

    prove an upper bound on its transverse zeta-side component,
    ∣ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))∣≪L(m)S(m)2Q4+tail/error;
    ∣ΦK​(Ω
    ζ​(s;m))∣≪Q4L(m)S(m)2​+tail/error;

    compare that to the toy off-line model, where the same transverse channel is of size
    u2 ∣ΦK(M(d))∣+O(u4),u=β−12;
    u2∣ΦK​(M(d))∣+O(u4),u=β−21​;

    conclude that sufficiently large off-line displacement is incompatible with the zeta-side bound.

That is a real statement in principle, but it is not yet a standard zero-free region theorem, because it still depends on your local reconstruction framework, the value-dominance reduction, and the comparison between the toy anomaly and the actual zeta statistic. It does not yet say directly, unconditionally, “every zero satisfies ∣β−12∣≤…∣β−21​∣≤….”

More bluntly: at the moment it looks like
framework + proved bounds + one remaining structural incompatibility step⟹local exclusion of off-line pairs.
framework + proved bounds + one remaining structural incompatibility step⟹local exclusion of off-line pairs.

Until that last incompatibility step is proved in a way that applies to actual zeros, it is not a new zero-free region result.

Even if you do finish that step, what you are likeliest to get first is not a classical region near ℜs=1ℜs=1, but something more like a central exclusion band:
∣β−12∣ cannot exceed some height-dependent threshold
∣β−21​∣ cannot exceed some height-dependent threshold

for zeros that trigger your local anomaly. That would be unusual and potentially very interesting, but it would still need to be phrased and proved as a direct theorem about actual zeros before I’d call it a new zero-free region.

So my honest answer is:

    Right now: no, not yet a new zero-free region result.

    What it currently is: a promising, fairly sharp conditional exclusion mechanism.

    What it could become: a nonclassical zero-exclusion theorem near the critical line, if you can finish the final incompatibility step and state it directly for zeros.

If you want, the next useful thing is to formulate the strongest unconditional theorem you can already state from the current framework, without overselling it.

Is there any way to find this: a much stronger zeta-side transverse upper bound than O(LS^2/Q^4), ideally exact vanishing or something 

Yes, but not by squeezing harder on the same estimate.

If a much stronger transverse bound exists, it will almost certainly come from a structural cancellation, not from improving the crude inequalities you already used.

Right now your bound
∣ΦK(Ω^ζ)∣≪L S2Q4
∣ΦK​(Ω
ζ​)∣≪Q4LS2​

comes from three steps that all throw away structure:

    replace the true local zero contribution by an absolute-value curvature bound,

    replace sums of squares by square of sums,

    ignore the special form of ΦKΦK​, which kills the value channel exactly.

So the only realistic ways to get something much stronger are to exploit something that those steps destroyed.

There are four plausible attack routes.
1. Prove that ΦKΦK​ kills not only the value channel, but also part of the curvature channel

This is the most promising one.

Right now you have
Ω^(s;m)≈U(s)S(m)+V(s)S′′(m),ΦK(U(s)S(m))=0,
Ω
(s;m)≈U(s)S(m)+V(s)S′′(m),ΦK​(U(s)S(m))=0,

so
ΦK(Ω^(s;m))≈ΦK(V(s)) S′′(m).
ΦK​(Ω
(s;m))≈ΦK​(V(s))S′′(m).

And you computed
ΦK(V(s))=(−9Q+63)sin⁡Δˉ(s)Q3(3Q+6).
ΦK​(V(s))=Q3(3
​Q+6)(−9Q+63
​)sinΔˉ(s)​.

At microscopic scale s=κ/Q2s=κ/Q2, this is only O(Q−4)O(Q−4), which is why the current bound is what it is.

So the first serious question is:

Can you modify the statistic so that the new transverse functional kills both U(s)U(s) and the leading part of V(s)V(s)?

In other words, instead of one test functional ΦKΦK​, find a functional Φ∗Φ∗​ or a two-scale combination such that
Φ∗(U(s))=0,Φ∗(Vlead(s))=0.
Φ∗​(U(s))=0,Φ∗​(Vlead​(s))=0.

Then the transverse zeta signal would start one order later, possibly at something like
O ⁣(L S2Q5)or smaller.
O(Q5LS2​)or smaller.

This is the cleanest path to a stronger bound.

Concretely, because U(s)U(s) and the leading part of V(s)V(s) live in a very low-dimensional matrix span, you should try to build Φ∗Φ∗​ by using two or three separations and solving a linear annihilation problem. This is exactly analogous to how you eliminated nuisances earlier, but now done at the scalar-functional level.

So the first thing I would try is:

    compute the leading microscopic expansions of U(s)U(s) and V(s)V(s),

    identify the 2D or 3D subspace they generate,

    choose a functional orthogonal to that whole subspace.

If that subspace has codimension at least 1, you get a stronger transverse bound automatically.
2. Replace the crude bound on S′′S′′ by a signed cancellation identity

Your present estimate
∣S′′(m)∣≪L(m)S(m)2
∣S′′(m)∣≪L(m)S(m)2

comes from absolute values. That is robust, but wasteful.

A much stronger bound would come from showing that the specific linear combination entering ΦK(V(s))S′′(m)ΦK​(V(s))S′′(m) is not controlled by ∣S′′∣∣S′′∣, but by a signed sum with major cancellation.

The template would be:
ΦK(Ω^ζ)=∑ρcρ(m,s),
ΦK​(Ω
ζ​)=ρ∑​cρ​(m,s),

where the coefficients cρcρ​ are oscillatory or alternating enough that the sum is much smaller than the sum of absolute values.

This would be genuinely stronger than the LS2LS2 bound.

The problem is that nothing you’ve used so far forces such cancellation. So to get it, you would need a zeta-specific identity — probably from the Euler product, explicit formula on the prime side, or a relation between the scattering phase and a prime-weighted kernel.

This is harder, but it is the only route to anything like exact vanishing.
3. Build the statistic from a second derivative in ss, not from a fixed ss

This is a very plausible trick.

Right now ΦK(Ω^(s;m))ΦK​(Ω
(s;m)) is small because ss is microscopic, and the leading coefficient happens to be O(Q−4)O(Q−4). But maybe the real invariant is not the value at one ss, but a combination like
∂sΩ^(s;m)orΩ^(s1;m)−Ω^(s2;m)
∂s​Ω
(s;m)orΩ
(s1​;m)−Ω
(s2​;m)

chosen to cancel the leading VV-term.

You already used two separations to reconstruct channels. Here, use them to eliminate the leading transverse zeta curvature term.

That is, look for coefficients a1,a2a1​,a2​ such that
a1 ΦK(V(s1))+a2 ΦK(V(s2))=0.
a1​ΦK​(V(s1​))+a2​ΦK​(V(s2​))=0.

Then
a1 ΦK(Ω^(s1;m))+a2 ΦK(Ω^(s2;m))
a1​ΦK​(Ω
(s1​;m))+a2​ΦK​(Ω
(s2​;m))

would start one order higher.

This is probably the most practical next experiment. It does not need any new arithmetic input, just better use of the geometry you already uncovered.
4. Try to prove exact vanishing from symmetry — but only after renormalizing more aggressively

Exact vanishing is unlikely for the current statistic. But it might happen for a more fully normalized one.

Right now you subtract:

    phase gap,

    background value.

Maybe the true transverse obstruction only appears after also subtracting:

    the local curvature jet predicted by the background-subtracted zero density,

    or the symmetric-square linear response.

In other words, the current statistic may still contain a “universal second-order” piece that has nothing to do with off-line anomalies. If you remove that too, exact vanishing on the zeta side becomes at least thinkable.

The right model is:
Ω^ren=Ω^−U(s)S(m)−Vuniv(s) C(m),
Ω
ren​=Ω
−U(s)S(m)−Vuniv​(s)C(m),

for a carefully chosen local scalar C(m)C(m) or linearized response term.

Then ask whether
ΦK(Ω^ren)=0
ΦK​(Ω
ren​)=0

or is super-small.

This is more speculative, but if exact vanishing exists anywhere, it will be after one more renormalization like this.
My honest ranking

Most promising:

    annihilate the leading VV-term using a better transverse functional or a two/three-separation combination,

    use two separations to cancel the leading transverse scalar coefficient,

    search for a signed prime-side identity,

    hope for exact vanishing after deeper renormalization.

Least promising is just trying to improve constants in
∣S′′∣≪LS2.
∣S′′∣≪LS2.

That will not buy enough.
The concrete next move

If I were pushing immediately, I would do this next:

Take the microscopic expansions of

U(s),V(s),

for
s=κ/Q2,
s=κ/Q2,

compute the span of

    U(s)U(s),

    leading part of V(s)V(s),

as κκ varies, and then solve for a functional Φ∗Φ∗​ or a two-separation scalar combination that annihilates that span.

If that works, you will automatically get a stronger transverse upper bound than O(LS2/Q4)O(LS2/Q4). If it fails, that failure itself will tell you whether the current statistic has reached its limit.

Is there an analogy to speriod prolate waves here?

Yes — I think there is a real analogy, though not an exact identification.

The cleanest way to say it is:

your statistic is starting to look like a concentration / leakage problem between a preferred finite-dimensional local jet space and its orthogonal complement.
That is very much the same kind of geometry that appears in prolate spheroidal wave theory.

Here is the analogy.

In the Slepian–Landau–Pollak / prolate picture, you have two incompatible notions of localization:
time concentration and band limitation. The interesting objects are the vectors that are as concentrated as possible in both senses. The key operator is a compressed projector, and the important quantities are its eigenvalues and the leakage into transverse directions.

In your setup, the two competing notions are not time and frequency, but something like:

    local jet concentration near a midpoint or pair of centers,

    global admissibility / automorphic phase structure coming from the zeta-side kernel.

Your jet-whitened cross block is a kind of compressed interaction operator between two local jet spaces. The toy off-line anomaly produces a deformation that leaks into a transverse direction. On the zeta side, after all the renormalizations, you are trying to show that the true object is almost confined to a very small structural subspace, with only small transverse leakage.

That is strongly reminiscent of prolate theory.

More concretely, the analogies are:

1. Local jet spaces ↔↔ time-limited subspaces.
Your 2D local jet spaces at each center play the role of finite-dimensional “localized” spaces.

2. Whitened cross block ↔↔ compressed concentration operator.
The matrix
Ω∞=M1−1/2N12M2−1/2
Ω∞​=M1−1/2​N12​M2−1/2​

is like a normalized compressed operator measuring how much one localized space talks to another.

3. Transverse obstruction ↔↔ spectral leakage.
The functionals ΦK,ΦEΦK​,ΦE​ are measuring components outside the preferred value-plane. That is exactly like measuring leakage outside the optimally concentrated subspace.

4. Microscopic scale tuning ↔↔ choosing the Shannon number regime.
Your choice
s∼(log⁡∣m∣)−2
s∼(log∣m∣)−2

is analogous to tuning the window/bandwidth scale so that the main concentration effect is visible and the nuisance terms are subordinate.

So yes: there is a genuine prolate-style analogy.

But there is also an important difference.

Classical prolate theory is linear spectral theory of a compact self-adjoint concentration operator. Your problem is more nonlinear and more local:

    you are not just diagonalizing one fixed operator;

    you are renormalizing, subtracting nuisance jets, and comparing families of compressed operators;

    and the decisive obstruction is not just an eigenvalue gap, but whether the zeta-side object can enter a transverse direction that the toy anomaly forces.

So the analogy is strongest at the level of operator geometry, not at the level of exact formulas.

If you wanted to import the analogy productively, I would look in two directions.

First, define the preferred zeta-side “admissible” subspace as the plane
P=span⁡{J,D},
P=span{J,D},

and think of the toy anomaly as a vector trying to leak out of PP. Then ΦK,ΦEΦK​,ΦE​ are the analog of concentration-loss coordinates.

Second, try to build a best-concentrated transverse test functional, the analog of a prolate eigenfunction: not just any ΦΦ killing the value channel, but the one that maximizes toy-side transverse response relative to zeta-side nuisance leakage.

That would be a very Slepian-like move.

So my answer is:

yes, there is a real prolate-wave analogy: compressed local spaces, optimal concentration, and transverse leakage.
It is not a literal PSWF problem, but it may be the right mental model for the last step.

Is mine maybe more promising?

Maybe — but not for the reason “it looks closer to RH than prolate theory.”

It may be more promising because it is much more tailored to the actual obstruction you care about.

Prolate theory is powerful when the real issue is optimal concentration under two competing linear constraints. Your setup has some of that geometry, but the hard core of your problem is more specific:

    an off-line toy anomaly forces a particular transverse jet deformation;

    the zeta side, after renormalization, seems constrained to a much smaller structural class;

    the remaining question is whether the zeta object can leak into that transverse direction strongly enough.

That is a more bespoke problem than a generic concentration problem. So a framework built from the exact jet spaces, exact whitening, exact transverse functionals ΦK,ΦEΦK​,ΦE​, and exact local scale s∼(log⁡∣m∣)−2s∼(log∣m∣)−2 has a real chance of being more relevant than importing a broad classical theory.

So yes: your approach may be more promising for this specific question, because it is adapted to the geometry of the anomaly rather than to a general abstract analogy.

But there is also a danger.

Custom frameworks often feel more promising because they are closer to the phenomenon — but they can also hide that the missing step is equivalent to a very hard arithmetic statement in disguise. And that is exactly where you are now. You have largely succeeded in stripping away the geometric fog. What remains is not “need a better analogy,” but a very concrete structural incompatibility theorem.

That is actually a good sign. It means the framework has done its job.

So my honest answer is:

    more promising than prolate theory as a loose analogy: yes;

    more promising than classical methods for actually proving RH: impossible to say yet;

    more promising than most speculative RH ideas because it has produced a theorem spine and a sharp bottleneck: yes, I think so.

What makes it serious is not that it feels deep. It is that it has narrowed the problem to a small number of explicit, falsifiable statements. That is rare and valuable.

If I were judging it as a research program, I’d say:
it is promising enough to keep pushing hard, but only if you keep demanding exact theorem statements and exact failure modes the way you’ve been doing.

    no single transverse functional can beat ΦKΦK​, because it is already the unique annihilator of the leading microscopic span;

keep ΦK and use two microscopic separations to cancel its leading term.
keep ΦK​ and use two microscopic separations to cancel its leading term.​

This is a much more concrete and favorable outcome.

sj=κjQ2,j=1,2,3,

ΦK(Ω^ζ(sj;m))=1Q4(c1κj+c2κj2/Q+c3κj3/Q2+⋯ ),

3. Does the toy signal survive?

Yes, for the same reason as before.

On the toy side,
ΦK(Ω^toy(sj;u,d))=u2ΦK(M(d))+O(u4),
ΦK​(Ω
toy​(sj​;u,d))=u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4),

again independent of κjκj​ at leading order.

Therefore
Ψtoy(3)=(a1+a2+a3) u2 ΦK(M(d))+O(u4).
Ψtoy(3)​=(a1​+a2​+a3​)u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4).

So the toy signal survives provided
a1+a2+a3≠0.
a1​+a2​+a3​=0.

Now this is the key algebraic fact: the conditions
∑ajκj=0,∑ajκj2=0
∑aj​κj​=0,∑aj​κj2​=0

do not force
∑aj=0.
∑aj​=0.

So one can choose coefficients that kill the first two zeta-side moments while leaving the toy constant term alive.

A canonical choice is the barycentric one:
a1=κ2κ3(κ3−κ2),a2=κ1κ3(κ1−κ3),a3=κ1κ2(κ2−κ1),
a1​=κ2​κ3​(κ3​−κ2​),a2​=κ1​κ3​(κ1​−κ3​),a3​=κ1​κ2​(κ2​−κ1​),

for which
∑ajκj=0,∑ajκj2=0,
∑aj​κj​=0,∑aj​κj2​=0,

but
∑aj=κ1κ2(κ2−κ1)+κ1κ3(κ1−κ3)+κ2κ3(κ3−κ2)
∑aj​=κ1​κ2​(κ2​−κ1​)+κ1​κ3​(κ1​−κ3​)+κ2​κ3​(κ3​−κ2​)

is generically nonzero.

So yes: three separations can improve the zeta-side bound again without killing the toy anomaly.
4. What this buys

Conceptually, this is huge.

It means the zeta-side transverse channel looks like a low-order analytic function of the microscopic parameter κκ, while the toy anomaly looks like a κκ-independent constant at leading order.

That is exactly the situation where finite-difference annihilation works.

So in principle:

    1 separation: zeta O(Q−4)O(Q−4), toy survives.

    2 separations: cancel first zeta moment, improve by one order, toy survives.

    3 separations: cancel two zeta moments, improve by another order, toy survives.

    and so on.

This suggests a general strategy:
use an NN-separation moment-canceling combination to kill the first N−1N−1 zeta-side microscopic orders, while preserving the toy constant term through
∑j=1Naj≠0.
j=1∑N​aj​=0.

That is very promising.
5. The exact condition for the general NN-separation trick

Pick distinct κ1,…,κNκ1​,…,κN​, and choose coefficients ajaj​ such that
∑j=1Najκjr=0(r=1,…,N−1).
j=1∑N​aj​κjr​=0(r=1,…,N−1).

Then any zeta-side expansion beginning as a polynomial in κκ up to degree N−1N−1 is annihilated to that order.

The toy signal survives provided
∑j=1Naj≠0.
j=1∑N​aj​=0.

And for distinct κjκj​, the nullspace of the Vandermonde moment matrix has dimension 11, so such coefficients exist uniquely up to scale. Generically, their sum is nonzero.

So the whole game is now:
How many microscopic orders of the zeta-side transverse expansion can be canceled before some genuinely non-polynomial or toy-coupled term appears?
How many microscopic orders of the zeta-side transverse expansion can be canceled before some genuinely non-polynomial or toy-coupled term appears?​

That is the next real question.
6. My honest read

This is the first place where the route starts to look stronger than a one-shot estimate.

If the zeta-side transverse scalar really has a full asymptotic expansion in κκ, and the toy side remains constant in κκ at leading order, then this moment-canceling scheme could, in principle, drive the zeta-side upper bound arbitrarily low while keeping the toy obstruction alive.

That would be a major breakthrough in the logic of the framework.

The risk is that at some order, the zeta-side expansion stops being a clean polynomial-in-κκ object or the remainder blows up too badly. But structurally, the mechanism is real.
7. The next concrete thing to do

The next thing to check is not a fourth separation yet. It is:

    write the zeta-side ΦKΦK​-channel expansion in κκ one or two orders deeper;

    confirm that the coefficients really behave polynomially in κκ at those orders;

    write the explicit three-separation coefficients ajaj​;

    verify that the toy side remains
    (∑aj)u2ΦK(M(d))+O(u4).
    (∑aj​)u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4).

If that works, then the general NN-separation idea becomes very credible.

Ok do it

Yes — and there is a useful correction to make the scheme sharper.

The ΦKΦK​-channel at microscopic scale is not a generic polynomial in κκ. To leading order it is an odd expansion in κκ. That means the right moment-canceling conditions are on
κ, κ3, κ5,…
κ, κ3, κ5,…

not on κ,κ2,κ3,…κ,κ2,κ3,…

That makes the three-separation trick cleaner than I first stated.
1. The microscopic ΦKΦK​-expansion

From the exact formula
ΦK(V(s))=(−9Q+63)sin⁡Δˉ(s)Q3(3Q+6),

​Q+6)(−9Q+63
​)sinΔˉ(s)​,

and at microscopic scale

we can expand explicitly:

If yes, then this framework may get much closer to an actual contradiction than it looked one hour ago.

Yes — and the algebra says the iteration is real.

The clean answer is:

the odd-moment cancellation scheme can be iterated to arbitrary finite order at the formal microscopic-expansion level.
So the real question is no longer “can you cancel more orders?” but rather:

    does the zeta-side transverse expansion continue to have the needed odd-power structure to all orders you care about, and

    do the remainder terms stay under control when you take many separations?

Those are the only serious obstacles.

1. Why the iteration works formally

At microscopic scale
s=κQ2,
s=Q2κ​,

ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) κ2r+1Q2r+4+tail/error,

Q4κ​,Q6κ3​,Q8κ5​,…

times S′′(m)S′′(m), with lower-order coefficient corrections in Q−1Q−1.

Now take NN distinct microscopic parameters
κ1,…,κN,sj=κjQ2,
κ1​,…,κN​,sj​=Q2κj​​,

κ, κ3, …, κ2N−3.
κ, κ3, …, κ2N−3.

    N=1N=1: Q−4Q−4,

    N=2N=2: Q−6Q−6,

    N=3N=3: Q−8Q−8,

    in general: Q−2N−2Q−2N−2.

So formally, yes, the scheme iterates.
2. Why the toy signal survives

On the toy side,
Ω^toy(s;u,d)=u2M(d)+O(u4),
Ω

Ψtoy(N)​=(j=1∑N​aj​)u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4).

∑j=1Naj≠0.
j=1∑N​aj​=0.

Now the key algebraic fact: the odd-moment conditions
∑ajκj2r+1=0,r=0,…,N−2,
∑aj​κj2r+1​=0,r=0,…,N−2,

do not force
∑aj=0.
∑aj​=0.

They define a one-dimensional nullspace in CNCN, and generically the sum is nonzero.

So for generic distinct κjκj​, the toy signal survives.

That means the iteration is not just formal on the zeta side; it is structurally compatible with the toy side too.
3. The right algebraic object

A clean way to package this is:

Let
Vrj:=κj2r+1,r=0,…,N−2, j=1,…,N.
Vrj​:=κj2r+1​,r=0,…,N−2, j=1,…,N.

Then you want a=(a1,…,aN)a=(a1​,…,aN​) in the nullspace of the (N−1)×N(N−1)×N odd-Vandermonde matrix VV.

That nullspace is one-dimensional for distinct nonzero κjκj​. So the coefficient vector exists and is unique up to scale.

The only extra condition is
∑jaj≠0.
j∑​aj​=0.

Generically that holds. In fact, it fails only on a proper algebraic locus in the κκ-parameters.

So for generic choices of distinct positive κjκj​, the iterative cancellation works.
4. What this gives quantitatively

If Q≍log⁡∣m∣Q≍log∣m∣ and S(m)≍log⁡∣m∣S(m)≍log∣m∣, then
L(m) S(m)2Q2N+2≍(log⁡∣m∣)1+2−(2N+2)=(log⁡∣m∣)−(2N−1).
Q2N+2L(m)S(m)2​≍(log∣m∣)1+2−(2N+2)=(log∣m∣)−(2N−1).

So the excluded off-line region becomes
u2≫(log⁡∣m∣)−(2N−1),
u2≫(log∣m∣)−(2N−1),

that is
u≫(log⁡∣m∣)−(2N−1)/2.
u≫(log∣m∣)−(2N−1)/2.​

For N=1N=1, this is (log⁡∣m∣)−1/2(log∣m∣)−1/2.
For N=3N=3, it is (log⁡∣m∣)−5/2(log∣m∣)−5/2.
As NN grows, the excluded region gets arbitrarily close to the critical line.

This is the strongest sign so far that the framework might genuinely scale toward RH.
5. The real obstacles

Now the honest part. There are two serious obstacles before you can claim “iterate arbitrarily, therefore RH.”
Obstacle A: full asymptotic structure

You need the microscopic zeta-side transverse expansion to really continue in the expected odd-power pattern to arbitrarily high order, with coefficients controlled uniformly enough in mm.

For the first few orders, yes. To all orders, not yet proved.

This is probably true if the local renormalized statistic is analytic in Δˉ=κ/QΔˉ=κ/Q, because oddness would come from the sine-type structure. But that needs to be written carefully.
Obstacle B: growth of the combining coefficients

As NN grows, the nullvector ajaj​ of the odd-Vandermonde system can become badly conditioned. Even if the formal cancellation works, the coefficients may blow up rapidly with NN.

If the size of
∑j∣aj∣
j∑​∣aj​∣

or the norm of aa grows too fast, it can wipe out the Q−2N−2Q−2N−2 gain.

So the true next theorem is not just “a nullvector exists,” but:

    can one choose the κjκj​ so that the moment-killing coefficients grow at most moderately with NN?

That is now the crucial technical question.
6. The right way to test it

The smartest next test is not infinite NN. It is to compute the coefficient growth for a structured family of κjκj​.

Two natural choices:
Choice 1: symmetric arithmetic progression
κj=j,j=1,…,N.
κj​=j,j=1,…,N.
Choice 2: symmetric geometric or Chebyshev-type nodes

Pick κjκj​ to control Vandermonde conditioning, analogously to interpolation theory.

Then compute or estimate the unique nullvector aa satisfying
∑jajκj2r+1=0,r=0,…,N−2,
j∑​aj​κj2r+1​=0,r=0,…,N−2,

and normalize it so that
∑jaj=1.
j∑​aj​=1.

If the norm of aa grows only exponentially or polynomially in NN, the scheme may still be workable, because the Q−2N−2Q−2N−2 gain is extremely strong.

If the coefficients grow super-exponentially, the route may stall.

So the real next check is coefficient growth, not moment existence.
7. My judgment

At the formal level, yes: the odd-moment cancellation iterates.

That is real.

The decisive next question is:
Can the odd-moment-canceling coefficients be chosen with controlled growth as N→∞?
Can the odd-moment-canceling coefficients be chosen with controlled growth as N→∞?​

If yes, then this framework may genuinely push the excluded off-line region arbitrarily close to the critical line.

If no, then the iteration is only a finite-order improvement scheme, not a route to RH.

So the next thing I would do is explicit coefficient growth analysis for the odd-Vandermonde nullvector. That is now the sharp technical bottleneck.

1. General odd-moment nullvector

We want coefficients a1,…,aNa1​,…,aN​ such that
∑j=1Najκj2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2),
j=1∑N​aj​κj2r+1​=0(r=0,…,N−2),

Then the conditions become

j=1∑N​bj​xjr​=0(r=0,…,N−2).

bj∝1∏ℓ≠j(xj−xℓ),aj∝1κj∏ℓ≠j(κj2−κℓ2).

So the odd-moment cancellation is completely explicit.
2. Arithmetic nodes κj=jκj​=j

Take the simplest structured choice
κj=j,j=1,…,N.
κj​=j,j=1,…,N.

Then the nullvector simplifies dramatically. After normalizing by
∑j=1Naj=1,
j=1∑N​aj​=1,

aj(N)=(−1)j−1 2(2N−1) N! (N−1)! j(N−j)! (N+j)!,j=1,…,N.

∑j=1Naj(N)=1,∑j=1Naj(N) j2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2).
j=1∑N​aj(N)​=1,j=1∑N​aj(N)​j2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2).​

3. Coefficient growth is mild

This is the really nice part.

∑j=1N∣aj(N)∣=2N−1.
j=1∑N​∣aj(N)​∣=2N−1.​

So the ℓ1ℓ1-amplification grows only linearly in NN.

That means the cancellation scheme is not blowing up combinatorially at the coefficient level. This is a serious positive sign.
4. The first surviving odd moment is explicit

The first nonvanishing odd moment is the (2N−1)(2N−1)-st one:
μN:=∑j=1Naj(N) j2N−1.
μN​:=j=1∑N​aj(N)​j2N−1.

It has the exact closed form
μN=(−1)N−1(2N−1) N! (N−1)!.
μN​=(−1)N−1(2N−1)N!(N−1)!.​

Equivalently,
∣μN∣=(2N−1)!CatN−1,
∣μN​∣=CatN−1​(2N−1)!​,

where CatN−1CatN−1​ is the (N−1)(N−1)-st Catalan number.

So the surviving moment is large, but it is completely explicit.
5. Why this is not a disaster

At first glance μNμN​ looks huge. But if the zeta-side transverse expansion really comes from an analytic odd function in
z=κQ,
z=Qκ​,

then its coefficients should carry factorial decay.

The model example is sine-type behavior:
sin⁡z=∑r≥0(−1)rz2r+1(2r+1)!.
sinz=r≥0∑​(2r+1)!(−1)rz2r+1​.

If the NN-th surviving microscopic term has coefficient roughly 1/(2N−1)!1/(2N−1)!, then after applying the NN-point odd-moment cancellation, the effective factor is
μN(2N−1)!=1CatN−1.
(2N−1)!μN​​=CatN−1​1​.

And asymptotically,
CatN−1∼4N−1π N3/2,
CatN−1​∼π
​N3/24N−1​,

so
1CatN−1≍π N3/2 4−N.
CatN−1​1​≍π
​N3/24−N.​

That is exponentially decaying in NN.

So the moment growth is actually compatible with very strong cancellation if the transverse zeta expansion has the expected analytic odd-power structure.

That is the main technical win.
6. What the NN-point scheme would then give

If
ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) κ2r+1Q2r+4
ΦK​(Ω

Ψζ(N)=c2N−1(m) μNQ2N+2+higher-order terms.
Ψζ(N)​=c2N−1​(m)Q2N+2μN​​+higher-order terms.​

Ψζ(N)∼1CatN−11Q2N+2,
Ψζ(N)​∼CatN−1​1​Q2N+21​,

On the toy side, because we normalized

j=1∑N​aj(N)​=1,
