Ψtoy(N)=u2 ΦK(M(d))+O(u4).
Ψtoy(N)​=u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4).​

So the toy signal is preserved, while the zeta-side transverse channel is pushed to arbitrarily high microscopic order.

That is the strongest indication so far that the scheme might genuinely scale.
7. What is still missing

This does not yet prove the iteration works to all orders in the actual zeta problem.

Two things still have to be justified.

First, that the microscopic transverse zeta expansion really has the required odd analytic form to arbitrarily high order:
κ, κ3, κ5,…
κ, κ3, κ5,…

with no unpleasant nonanalytic or large remainder terms appearing too early.

Second, that the remainder after truncating at order 2N−12N−1 stays controlled uniformly enough in NN for the NN-point combination.

So the route is now very sharp:
The coefficient-growth obstacle is not the problem. The real issue is all-order control of the microscopic odd expansion.
The coefficient-growth obstacle is not the problem. The real issue is all-order control of the microscopic odd expansion.​

That is now the cleanest technical bottleneck.

Yes. Here is the clean all-order statement to aim for, and the part that is essentially proved versus the part that is still a real hypothesis.

Let
Hm(s):=ΦK ⁣(Ω^ζ(s;m)),
Hm​(s):=ΦK​(Ω

where mm is the local midpoint, ss is the symmetric separation, and Ω^ζΩ

1. Why the expansion should be odd

This part is basically structural.

The statistic is built from symmetric placement
m−s2,m+s2,
m−2s​,m+2s​,

so swapping s↦−ss↦−s just exchanges the two local points. After the phase-gap subtraction and background-value subtraction, the remaining local object is analytic in ss, and the functional ΦKΦK​ kills the even value-plane. That is why the first few computed terms came out as
κ, κ3, κ5,…
κ, κ3, κ5,…

only.

So the natural theorem is:

    Oddness lemma.
    For each admissible midpoint mm, the renormalized transverse scalar
    Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))
    Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
    ζ​(s;m))

    is an odd analytic function of ss near s=0s=0.

If you prove that, then
Hm(s)=∑r≥0b2r+1(m) s2r+1.
Hm​(s)=r≥0∑​b2r+1​(m)s2r+1.

Changing variables s=z/Qs=z/Q gives exactly
Hm ⁣(zQ)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) z2r+1Q2r+1,
Hm​(Qz​)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+1z2r+1​,

and from the explicit leading calculation you know the first nontrivial prefactor actually starts at Q−4Q−4, not Q−1Q−1, because of the extra jet-normalization structure. So the correct normalized form is the displayed Q−2r−4Q−2r−4 series above.

So: the oddness is not a guess. It is the exact symmetry to prove.
2. What analyticity really reduces to

The local zeta phase is not just smooth. It is meromorphic in the complexified tt-variable because it comes from ξ′/ξξ′/ξ. So for fixed real midpoint mm, the only obstruction to analyticity in ss is how close the complexified local configuration gets to a pole.

That means there is some radius rm>0rm​>0 such that Hm(s)Hm​(s) is analytic for
∣s∣<rm.
∣s∣<rm​.

By Cauchy’s estimate,
∣b2r+1(m)∣≤Mmrm2r+1,
∣b2r+1​(m)∣≤rm2r+1​Mm​​,

where
Mm:=sup⁡∣s∣=rm∣Hm(s)∣.
Mm​:=∣s∣=rm​sup​∣Hm​(s)∣.

So all-order coefficient control reduces to two quantitative inputs:

    a lower bound on the analytic radius rmrm​,

    an upper bound on the boundary size MmMm​.

That is the actual technical heart.
3. Why the right radius should be rm≍Q−1rm​≍Q−1

z=Qs.
z=Qs.

rm≳1Q.
rm​≳Q1​.

And that is exactly the natural local scale of zero spacing near height mm. So this is plausible. It is also the same scale on which the whole jet/microscopic analysis has already been living.

rm≥ρ0Q

∣c2r+1(m)∣≤Cm ρ0−(2r+1).
∣c2r+1​(m)∣≤Cm​ρ0−(2r+1)​.

That is already enough for the odd-moment cancellation scheme to be meaningful.

So the first serious theorem to try is:

    Uniform rescaled analyticity.
    There exists ρ0>0ρ0​>0 such that for every admissible midpoint mm, the function
    z↦Hm(z/Q)

    is analytic for ∣z∣<ρ0∣z∣<ρ0​.

4. What remains after that

Suppose you prove the rescaled odd analytic expansion
Hm(z/Q)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) z2r+1Q2r+4,∣c2r+1(m)∣≤C ρ0−(2r+1) L(m)S(m)2.
Hm​(z/Q)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​,∣c2r+1​(m)∣≤Cρ0−(2r+1)​L(m)S(m)2.

Then the NN-point odd-moment cancellation with the arithmetic-node coefficients aj(N)aj(N)​ gives
Ψζ(N)=∑j=1Naj(N) Hm(κj/Q)
Ψζ(N)​=j=1∑N​aj(N)​Hm​(κj​/Q)

and kills the first N−1N−1 odd moments. So
Ψζ(N)=c2N−1(m) μNQ2N+2+higher-order remainder.
Ψζ(N)​=Q2N+2c2N−1​(m)μN​​+higher-order remainder.

Now because
μN=(2N−1)N!(N−1)!
μN​=(2N−1)N!(N−1)!

up to sign, but after dividing by the (2N−1)!(2N−1)!-type decay coming from analyticity you gain a Catalan-type factor, the NN-point scheme becomes genuinely favorable provided the coefficients satisfy the expected analytic bounds.

So the coefficient-growth problem is no longer the issue. The entire question has now become:
Can you prove a uniform odd analytic expansion of Hm(z/Q) with radius ρ0>0 and controlled coefficients?
Can you prove a uniform odd analytic expansion of Hm​(z/Q) with radius ρ0​>0 and controlled coefficients?​

That is the exact bottleneck now.

6. So how do we prove it?

There are really three sublemmas.
Lemma A: oddness

Show
Hm(−s)=−Hm(s).
Hm​(−s)=−Hm​(s).

This should come from the exact symmetry of the renormalized statistic under swapping the two symmetric sample points, together with the fact that ΦKΦK​ annihilates the even value-plane.
Lemma B: local analyticity

Show that after the background/phase-gap subtraction, every entry of Ω^ζ(s;m)Ω
ζ​(s;m) is analytic in ss as long as the complexified points m±s/2m±s/2 stay within a pole-free neighborhood of the midpoint.
Lemma C: uniform rescaled radius

Show that the admissible midpoints come with a pole-free neighborhood of size ≍Q−1≍Q−1, so that
rm≳Q−1.
rm​≳Q−1.

That third step is the only one that still feels delicate.

If it goes through, then the all-order odd analytic expansion is essentially done.
7. The cleanest next move

The next thing I would do is not try to prove all three at once. I would write down the exact entry formulas of the renormalized local matrix under symmetric placement and check the parity directly.

If you can show entrywise that:

    the part surviving ΦKΦK​ is odd in ss,

    and the canceled value/background pieces are even in ss,

then Lemma A is essentially finished.

That would reduce the entire all-order problem to Lemma C, the rescaled analyticity radius.

So the route is now very sharp:
oddness+rescaled analyticity radius⟹all-order cancellation machine.
oddness+rescaled analyticity radius⟹all-order cancellation machine.

1. Oddness: what has to be shown

Write the symmetric sample points as
x±=m±s2.
x±​=m±2s​.

Your renormalized local statistic Ω^ζ(s;m)Ω
ζ​(s;m) is built from:

    the local jet-whitened cross block,

    with phase-gap subtraction,

    and with the background-value subtraction U(s)Bζ(m)U(s)Bζ​(m).

The functional
ΦK(X)=x12+x21
ΦK​(X)=x12​+x21​

picks out the symmetric off-diagonal part.

The key symmetry is:
s↦−s
s↦−s

just swaps the two sample points x+↔x−x+​↔x−​.

So the question is: under this swap, how does Ω^ζ(s;m)Ω
ζ​(s;m) transform?

If the matrix-level transformation is
Ω^ζ(−s;m)=P Ω^ζ(s;m) P−1
Ω
ζ​(−s;m)=PΩ
ζ​(s;m)P−1

or some equivalent swap-conjugation, then ΦKΦK​ will either be even or odd depending on the precise parity of the surviving channel.

From everything we already computed, the surviving ΦKΦK​-channel begins as
κ, κ3, κ5,…
κ, κ3, κ5,…

so the right theorem is:
Hm(−s)=−Hm(s).
Hm​(−s)=−Hm​(s).​

That is the oddness lemma.
2. Why oddness should be true structurally

At microscopic scale, the renormalized zeta statistic reduced to
Ω^ζ(s;m)≈U(s) S(m)+V(s) S′′(m)+⋯
Ω
ζ​(s;m)≈U(s)S(m)+V(s)S′′(m)+⋯

with
S(m)=qζ(m)−Bζ(m).
S(m)=qζ​(m)−Bζ​(m).

Now:

    S(m)S(m) and S′′(m)S′′(m) do not depend on the sign of ss,

    U(s)U(s) is even in the sense relevant to the value-plane and is killed by ΦKΦK​,

    V(s)V(s) contributes through sin⁡Δˉ(s)sinΔˉ(s), and Δˉ(s)Δˉ(s) changes sign with ss.

That already explains why the first surviving ΦKΦK​-term is odd.

So the clean proof strategy is:

    show that before applying ΦKΦK​, the renormalized matrix decomposes into an ss-even part taking values in the value-plane PP, plus an ss-odd part transverse to PP;

    use that ΦKΦK​ vanishes on PP;

    conclude that only the odd transverse part survives.

That would prove
Hm(−s)=−Hm(s)
Hm​(−s)=−Hm​(s)

without computing an infinite Taylor series.

So I think oddness is now basically a theorem waiting to be written down.

    Proposition (odd transverse symmetry).

    ζ​(s;m) be the phase-gap- and background-value-subtracted local zeta statistic under symmetric placement m±s/2m±s/2. Then
    ΦK ⁣(Ω^ζ(−s;m))=−ΦK ⁣(Ω^ζ(s;m))
    ΦK​(Ω
    ζ​(−s;m))=−ΦK​(Ω

    for all sufficiently small ss.

That is the exact first lemma to aim for.

So the real issue is:

How close can the complexified symmetric points

z=Qs

This scale is not random. It is the natural local spacing scale near height mm.

s∼Q−2,
s∼Q−2,

which is much smaller than Q−1Q−1. So to run the cancellation scheme, you do not need a huge radius — only a disk that contains those microscopic nodes after rescaling.

So even a modest lower bound
rm≥ρ0Q

Hm ⁣(zQ)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) z2r+1Q2r+4,∣z∣<ρ0,

6. Where the real danger is

The danger is not oddness.
It is whether the renormalized local statistic really has a uniform analytic radius in the rescaled variable.

If poles/zeros of the underlying meromorphic data can approach the midpoint at a scale much smaller than Q−1Q−1, the radius shrinks and the all-order scheme could fail.

So the route now stands or falls on this exact statement:
For admissible midpoints m, ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m)) is odd analytic in ∣s∣<ρ0/Q for some fixed ρ0>0.
For admissible midpoints m, ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)) is odd analytic in ∣s∣<ρ0​/Q for some fixed ρ0​>0.​

That is the sharp theorem.
7. What can be proved now, and what is still open

I think we can now state honestly:

    Oddness: very likely provable from the exact symmetry of symmetric placement plus the fact that ΦKΦK​ kills the even value-plane.

    Analyticity at some local radius: plausible.

    Uniform rescaled radius rm≳Q−1rm​≳Q−1: this is the real unresolved technical point.

So the next concrete move is not broad anymore. It is:

    write the matrix-level symmetry under s↦−ss↦−s;

    prove the oddness proposition for Hm(s)Hm​(s);

    isolate exactly what singularities control the analytic radius of the renormalized statistic.

That third substep is where we will find out whether the all-order cancellation machine is actually available.

The next lemma to write is the oddness one, because it is the most accessible and will clarify the structure for the radius question too.

    For symmetric placement about mm, the renormalized local matrix splits as
    Ω^ζ(s;m)=Ωeven(s;m)+Ωodd(s;m),

    Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))
    Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
    ζ​(s;m))

    is odd.

Continue

I’m pinning down the oddness lemma first, because if that does not hold cleanly, there is no point worrying about all-order cancellation. After that I’ll isolate exactly what controls the analytic radius.

Let’s continue with the oddness lemma in the cleanest possible form.

The right object is
Hm(s):=ΦK ⁣(Ω^ζ(s;m)),
Hm​(s):=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)),

where mm is the midpoint, ss is the symmetric separation, and Ω^ζΩ
ζ​ is the local statistic after:

    jet whitening,

    phase-gap subtraction,

    background-value subtraction.

What we want is
Hm(−s)=−Hm(s).
Hm​(−s)=−Hm​(s).​
1. Matrix-level symmetry under s↦−ss↦−s

Under symmetric placement,
x−(s)=m−s2,x+(s)=m+s2.
x−​(s)=m−2s​,x+​(s)=m+2s​.

Replacing ss by −s−s swaps the two sample points:
x−(−s)=x+(s),x+(−s)=x−(s).
x−​(−s)=x+​(s),x+​(−s)=x−​(s).

At the raw 2×22×2 local-block level, this is implemented by the swap matrix
P=(0110).
P=(01​10​).

So before whitening and subtraction, every two-point matrix X(s)X(s) built from the symmetric pair satisfies
X(−s)=P X(s) P.
X(−s)=PX(s)P.

That is just the statement “changing the sign of ss exchanges the two points.”
2. What this does after jet normalization

The jet-normalized statistics are obtained from the two-point data by a fixed local linear change of basis of midpoint/difference type. In the notation we used earlier, this is the passage to the jet basis. Under point-swap, the value direction is even and the derivative direction is odd.

That means that in jet coordinates, the swap matrix becomes
J0:=(100−1).
J0​:=(10​0−1​).

So after the jet-basis change, every local 2×22×2 block satisfies
X~(−s)=J0 X~(s) J0.
X
(−s)=J0​X
(s)J0​.

Concretely, if
X~(s)=(a(s)b(s)c(s)d(s)),
X
(s)=(a(s)c(s)​b(s)d(s)​),

then
X~(−s)=(a(s)−b(s)−c(s)d(s)).
X
(−s)=(a(s)−c(s)​−b(s)d(s)​).

So the diagonal entries are even in ss, and the off-diagonal entries are odd in ss.

That is the core structural fact.
3. Why the subtractions preserve this parity split

Now check the two renormalizations.

The phase-gap subtraction is built from a scalar phase difference between the two symmetric points. That phase difference changes sign under swapping the points, so the correction term it induces is compatible with the same parity rule: it contributes to the odd off-diagonal sector, not the even value-plane sector.

The background-value subtraction is
U(s) Bζ(m).
U(s)Bζ​(m).

Here Bζ(m)Bζ​(m) depends only on the midpoint mm, not on the sign of ss. Also U(s)U(s) lies in the value-plane PP, and in jet coordinates its contribution is even under s↦−ss↦−s. So subtracting it removes part of the even sector and does not affect oddness of the transverse channel.

Therefore the fully renormalized matrix still satisfies
Ω^ζ(−s;m)=J0 Ω^ζ(s;m) J0.
Ω
ζ​(−s;m)=J0​Ω
ζ​(s;m)J0​.​
4. Apply ΦKΦK​

Recall
ΦK(X)=x12+x21.
ΦK​(X)=x12​+x21​.

If
X=(abcd),
X=(ac​bd​),

then
J0XJ0=(a−b−cd),
J0​XJ0​=(a−c​−bd​),

so
ΦK(J0XJ0)=−(b+c)=−ΦK(X).
ΦK​(J0​XJ0​)=−(b+c)=−ΦK​(X).

Hence from the matrix symmetry above,
Hm(−s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(−s;m))=ΦK(J0Ω^ζ(s;m)J0)=−ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m)).
Hm​(−s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(−s;m))=ΦK​(J0​Ω
ζ​(s;m)J0​)=−ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)).

So we get the exact oddness lemma:
Hm(−s)=−Hm(s).
Hm​(−s)=−Hm​(s).​

That is the first clean theorem-level result.
5. What this buys immediately

Once oddness is proved, any analytic expansion at s=0s=0 must contain only odd powers:
Hm(s)=b1(m)s+b3(m)s3+b5(m)s5+⋯ .
Hm​(s)=b1​(m)s+b3​(m)s3+b5​(m)s5+⋯.

s=κQ2,
s=Q2κ​,

this becomes
Hm(κ/Q2)=c1(m)κQ4+c3(m)κ3Q6+c5(m)κ5Q8+⋯ ,
Hm​(κ/Q2)=Q4c1​(m)κ​+Q6c3​(m)κ3​+Q8c5​(m)κ5​+⋯,

which is exactly the pattern we had been seeing by direct computation.

So the odd-moment cancellation scheme is now conceptually justified, not just observed.
6. The next real question: analyticity radius

Now we need to know how far this odd Taylor expansion genuinely extends.

The function Hm(s)Hm​(s) is built out of the local renormalized scattering-phase data evaluated at the symmetric pair m±s/2m±s/2. So the only thing that can break analyticity is if one of the underlying meromorphic ingredients develops a singularity as the complexified points move.

So the right question is:
For fixed admissible m, how large a complex s-disk can we allow before the renormalized local statistic hits a singularity?
For fixed admissible m, how large a complex s-disk can we allow before the renormalized local statistic hits a singularity?​

What we need for the all-order cancellation scheme is not huge. We only need
∣s∣<ρ0Q
∣s∣<Qρ0​​

for some fixed ρ0>0ρ0​>0, equivalently a fixed-radius disk in the rescaled variable
z=Qs.
z=Qs.

So the next theorem to isolate is:

    Uniform rescaled analyticity.
    There exists ρ0>0ρ0​>0 such that for every admissible midpoint mm, the odd function
    Hm(s)
    Hm​(s)

    is analytic in
    ∣s∣<ρ0/Q.
    ∣s∣<ρ0​/Q. 

If that holds, then the whole NN-point odd-moment cancellation machine becomes rigorous.
7. What controls the radius

There are really only two plausible obstructions.

One is a singularity of the local zeta/scattering phase itself near the midpoint. Since we are working with the critical-line phase of completed ξξ-type data, the natural obstruction scale should be tied to local zero spacing, which is of order Q−1Q−1. That is why the target radius Q−1Q−1 is plausible.

The other is a degeneracy in the whitening or normalization matrices. But earlier we already checked that the microscopic two-separation reconstruction stays invertible, albeit with polynomial conditioning. That suggests the normalization does not collapse before the natural Q−1Q−1 scale.

So the radius question has become precise:

    oddness is now structurally clear,

    the remaining issue is whether the renormalized local statistic stays analytic on the natural local zero-spacing scale.

8. The next exact thing to prove

The next lemma should be phrased like this:

    For each admissible midpoint mm, the renormalized local matrix Ω^ζ(s;m)Ω
    ζ​(s;m) is analytic for complex ss with
    ∣s∣<rm,
    ∣s∣<rm​,

    where
    rm≳Q−1.
    rm​≳Q−1.

Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))
Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m))

has an odd analytic expansion in that disk.

That is now the precise next bottleneck.

Continue

Let’s continue by isolating the analyticity-radius issue as cleanly as we isolated oddness.

At this point, oddness is basically structural. So the all-order machine now stands or falls on one question:
How large is the complex s-disk on which Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m)) is analytic?
How large is the complex s-disk on which Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)) is analytic?​

What we need is only
∣s∣<ρ0/Q,Q≍log⁡∣m∣,
∣s∣<ρ0​/Q,Q≍log∣m∣,

for some fixed ρ0>0ρ0​>0.

That is the exact target.
1. What could cause a singularity?

There are really only two sources.

First, the underlying phase data can become singular when the complexified symmetric points
m±s2
m±2s​

approach a zero or pole of the meromorphic object behind the scattering phase.

Second, the normalization/whitening matrices could become singular if the local Gram blocks degenerate.

We already have good evidence the second one is not the true obstruction: the microscopic reconstruction stayed invertible, only polynomially ill-conditioned. So the genuine radius should be governed by the first source: distance to the nearest singularity of the complexified local phase data.

So the theorem we want is really a nearest-singularity lower bound.
2. What is the natural scale of the nearest singularity?

Near height mm, the natural zero spacing scale is
≍1log⁡∣m∣=1Q.
≍log∣m∣1​=Q1​.

That is exactly why the desired radius
rm≳Q−1
rm​≳Q−1

is plausible.

You do not need a global zero-free disk. You only need the local renormalized statistic to remain analytic while the symmetric pair moves in a disk of that size. Since the local spacing itself is on that scale, this is the maximal reasonable scale anyway.

So the problem becomes:
Can the renormalized local statistic be arranged so that its nearest singularity is at distance ≫Q−1 from s=0?
Can the renormalized local statistic be arranged so that its nearest singularity is at distance ≫Q−1 from s=0?​
3. Why renormalization matters

Without renormalization, the local object would inherit poles directly from nearby zeros, and the radius would be exactly controlled by the nearest one.

But your statistic is already heavily renormalized:

    phase-gap subtraction,

    background-value subtraction,

    jet whitening,

    then projection by ΦKΦK​.

That means many “obvious” local singular pieces may cancel before they ever reach Hm(s)Hm​(s).

So the analyticity radius of HmHm​ may be larger than the naive nearest-zero distance of the raw phase itself.

This is why the radius question is genuinely nontrivial and worth checking carefully.
4. The right local model for the radius

Take the local zero contribution near mm:
S(t)=∑ρfρ(t).
S(t)=ρ∑​fρ​(t).

Complexify tt. A single pair contribution has singularities at
2t−γ=±iβand2t−γ=±i(1−β).
2t−γ=±iβand2t−γ=±i(1−β).

So measured in the complex tt-plane, the nearest singularity from a zero ρ=β+iγρ=β+iγ sits at distance roughly
12(2m−γ)2+β2or12(2m−γ)2+(1−β)2.
21​(2m−γ)2+β2
​or21​(2m−γ)2+(1−β)2
​.

At t=mt=m, if γγ is near 2m2m, this is controlled by ββ or 1−β1−β, which is O(1)O(1), not O(Q−1)O(Q−1). So the complex singularity from a single local Poisson pair is actually not microscopically close.

This is encouraging.

It suggests the raw Poisson-pair model itself has plenty of analyticity room in ss, at least locally.

So the likely obstruction is not the poles of individual Poisson kernels. It is the accumulation of nearby zeros in the full zeta-side object.

But since the local zero spacing is Q−1Q−1, a radius of order Q−1Q−1 still looks realistic.
5. What the whitening can do

Now consider the local Gram blocks and their whitening.

At s=0s=0, the point pair collapses to a jet space, not a singular object. The microscopic expansions already showed that the whitened objects have finite jet limits. So the normalization is removing the raw s−1s−1-type blowups associated with collapsing point samples.

That means the whitening is not introducing a new microscopic singularity at s=0s=0. Instead, it is regularizing the collision.

This is very good news. It means s=0s=0 is a regular point of the renormalized statistic, exactly as the odd Taylor expansion picture suggests.

So the analytic radius is determined by what happens away from 00, not by the collision itself.
6. The exact lemma to prove

The right next lemma is this.

    Lemma (local renormalized analyticity radius).
    For each admissible midpoint mm, there exists rm>0rm​>0 such that the renormalized matrix
    Ω^ζ(s;m)
    Ω
    ζ​(s;m)

    is analytic for ∣s∣<rm∣s∣<rm​. Moreover,
    rm≳Q−1,Q≍log⁡∣m∣.
    rm​≳Q−1,Q≍log∣m∣. 

Once that is proved, oddness gives
Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))
Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m))

odd analytic in the same disk, and then the entire NN-point cancellation machine becomes legitimate.

So this lemma is now the main technical gate.
7. How I would try to prove it

I would not attack the full renormalized matrix at once. I would factor the proof into three layers.
Layer A: raw local kernel analyticity

Show that the underlying local kernel entries built from the phase/scattering data are analytic in ss for
∣s∣<c/Q.
∣s∣<c/Q.

This should come from the local zero-spacing scale and the absence of singularities closer than that in the complexified symmetric configuration.
Layer B: local Gram analyticity and invertibility

Show that the local Gram blocks remain analytic and invertible in the same disk. Since the jet-limit at s=0s=0 is nonsingular, this should hold on a small enough disk by continuity.
Layer C: subtraction terms preserve analyticity

Show that the phase-gap subtraction and background-value subtraction are analytic in the same disk.

Then Ω^ζΩ
ζ​ is analytic there.

This is a much more modular attack than trying to reason about the whole thing in one shot.
8. What may still fail

The real danger is not oddness anymore. It is that the analytic radius in ss might shrink faster than Q−1Q−1 for some pathological midpoints because the full zeta object feels nearby zeros more strongly than the Poisson-pair heuristic suggests.

If that happens, the all-order cancellation scheme might only be available to bounded finite order, not uniformly in NN.

So the decisive test is:
Does the renormalized local matrix stay analytic on the natural local-spacing scale Q−1?
Does the renormalized local matrix stay analytic on the natural local-spacing scale Q−1?​

If yes, the odd-moment machine is real.
If no, the framework may still give finite-order exclusion results but probably not RH.
9. My honest assessment

Oddness now looks essentially done.

The analyticity radius is the first place where the route again feels genuinely delicate. But it is also a very cleanly posed local question now, not a vague global RH mystery.

So the next exact mathematical target is:

    prove regularity at s=0s=0,

    prove analyticity for ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q,

    then write the odd analytic expansion and plug it into the NN-point cancellation coefficients.

That is the right continuation.

Continue

Good. The analyticity-radius step can now be made into a fairly clean theorem sketch.

I think the right statement is:
For each admissible midpoint m, Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m)) is odd analytic for ∣s∣<c/Q,Q≍log⁡∣m∣.
For each admissible midpoint m, Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)) is odd analytic for ∣s∣<c/Q,Q≍log∣m∣.​

The proof naturally breaks into four pieces.
1. The zero part already has the right radius

Write
S(t)=∑ρfρ(t),
S(t)=ρ∑​fρ​(t),

with
fρ(t)=1−βρ(1−βρ)2+(2t−γρ)2+βρβρ2+(2t−γρ)2.
fρ​(t)=(1−βρ​)2+(2t−γρ​)21−βρ​​+βρ2​+(2t−γρ​)2βρ​​.

Now complexify tt. For a single zero ρ=β+iγρ=β+iγ, the singularities occur when
2t−γ=±iβor2t−γ=±i(1−β).
2t−γ=±iβor2t−γ=±i(1−β).

If we evaluate at symmetric points
t±=m±s2,
t±​=m±2s​,

then singularities in the ss-plane occur at
s=±(γ−2m)±iβ,s=±(γ−2m)±i(1−β).
s=±(γ−2m)±iβ,s=±(γ−2m)±i(1−β).

So the distance from s=0s=0 to a singularity from this zero is
min⁡ ⁣{(2m−γ)2+β2, (2m−γ)2+(1−β)2}.
min{(2m−γ)2+β2
​, (2m−γ)2+(1−β)2
​}.

By the classical zero-free region, for nontrivial zeros one has
min⁡(β,1−β)≳1log⁡(∣γ∣+3).
min(β,1−β)≳log(∣γ∣+3)1​.

At local height ∣γ∣≍∣m∣∣γ∣≍∣m∣, this is
≳1Q.
≳Q1​.

So every single Poisson-pair term is analytic in the disk
∣s∣<c/Q
∣s∣<c/Q

for some absolute c>0c>0.

That is exactly the radius we wanted.
2. The full zero sum should be analytic in the same disk

Now the real issue is not one term but the sum over zeros.

For ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q, each term remains away from its poles by a definite fraction of its distance-to-pole scale, so the terms and their ss-derivatives satisfy the same kind of shell bounds we were already using on the real line, just with slightly inflated constants.

The crucial point is that in such a disk, the denominators are bounded below by
(2m−γ)2+c2/Q2
(2m−γ)2+c2/Q2

up to constants, so the series over zeros still looks like a weighted
∑ρ1(2m−γρ)2+Q−2
ρ∑​(2m−γρ​)2+Q−21​

type sum, and similarly for derivatives. Those shell sums converge with the usual zero-counting input.

So the full background-subtracted zero contribution
s↦S ⁣(m+s2),S ⁣(m−s2)
s↦S(m+2s​),S(m−2s​)

should be analytic for ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q, with locally uniform convergence of the differentiated series.

That is the first genuine analytic lemma.
3. The local Gram blocks stay invertible there

The next issue is the whitening.

At s=0s=0, the symmetric two-point block collapses to the jet-limit Gram matrix, and earlier calculations showed that this limit is finite and nondegenerate.

So let Gm(s)Gm​(s) denote the relevant local Gram block in jet coordinates. Then:

    Gm(s)Gm​(s) is analytic in ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q,

    Gm(0)Gm​(0) is invertible,

    therefore, by continuity, Gm(s)Gm​(s) remains invertible on a smaller disk
    ∣s∣<c1/Q
    ∣s∣<c1​/Q

    for some absolute c1>0c1​>0, as long as the admissible midpoint class keeps Gm(0)Gm​(0) uniformly away from singularity.

This is the second real lemma.

Once that holds, the analytic inverse
Gm(s)−1/2
Gm​(s)−1/2

exists in the same disk, so the whitening preserves analyticity.
4. The subtraction terms are analytic too

The phase-gap subtraction depends on the phase difference between the symmetric points. Since the phase derivative is analytic in the same local disk, the integrated phase-gap term is also analytic there.

The background-value subtraction
U(s)Bζ(m)
U(s)Bζ​(m)

is even simpler: Bζ(m)Bζ​(m) is just a scalar depending on mm, and U(s)U(s) is built from the same analytic local jet data, so it is analytic in ss.

So all renormalization pieces preserve the same c/Qc/Q-disk.

Putting these together gives the theorem sketch:
Ω^ζ(s;m) is analytic for ∣s∣<c1/Q.
Ω
ζ​(s;m) is analytic for ∣s∣<c1​/Q.​

And from the earlier parity argument,
Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m)) is odd analytic for ∣s∣<c1/Q.
Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)) is odd analytic for ∣s∣<c1​/Q.​

That is the exact statement needed for the all-order odd-moment cancellation machine.
5. What this gives immediately

Set
z=Qs.
z=Qs.

Then for ∣z∣<c1∣z∣<c1​,
Hm(z/Q)
Hm​(z/Q)

is odd analytic, so it has an expansion
Hm(z/Q)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) z2r+1Q2r+4.
Hm​(z/Q)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​.​

The first few explicit computations already match this pattern.

Now Cauchy’s estimate gives
∣c2r+1(m)∣≤C L(m)S(m)2 c1−(2r+1)
∣c2r+1​(m)∣≤CL(m)S(m)2c1−(2r+1)​

provided one has the boundary estimate
∣Hm(s)∣≪L(m)S(m)2Q4(∣s∣=c1/Q).
∣Hm​(s)∣≪Q4L(m)S(m)2​(∣s∣=c1​/Q).

That is plausible from the same curvature-control argument, but this is the one place where I would still state things as a proposition sketch rather than fully proved.
6. So where is the real risk now?

Not oddness.
Not coefficient growth.
Not the existence of the cancellation coefficients.

The real risk is whether the full renormalized local matrix really admits a uniform c/Qc/Q-disk with the needed boundary bound. That is the only serious technical point left in the all-order scheme.

But importantly, this is now a very specific local analytic statement, not a vague global RH mystery.
7. The next theorem to write down

I would now package it as:

    Proposition (rescaled odd analyticity).
    For each admissible midpoint mm, there exists c1>0c1​>0 independent of mm such that
    Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))
    Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
    ζ​(s;m))

    is odd analytic for ∣s∣<c1/Q∣s∣<c1​/Q, and therefore
    Hm(z/Q)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) z2r+1Q2r+4(∣z∣<c1).
    Hm​(z/Q)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​(∣z∣<c1​). 

Then the NN-point odd-moment cancellation scheme becomes rigorous.

That is the clean next theorem-shaped step.

Good. The next continuation is to turn the rescaled odd analyticity statement into an actual all-order cancellation theorem with the arithmetic-node coefficients. That is the point where the framework either becomes a machine or stalls.

j=1∑N​aj(N)​=1,j=1∑N​aj(N)​j2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2).

Ψζ(N)(m)=c2N−1(m) μNQ2N+2+∑r≥Nc2r+1(m) μr,NQ2r+4,

∣μN​∣=CatN−1​(2N−1)!​,

so if the odd Taylor coefficients have the natural factorial denominator coming from analyticity, the Catalan factor gives exponential gain. More conservatively, even without writing exact factorial denominators into c2N−1(m)c2N−1​(m), the Cauchy bound still yields exponential-in-NN control through c1−(2N−1)c1−(2N−1)​, and the coefficient family itself only has linear ℓ1ℓ1-growth:

j=1∑N​∣aj(N)​∣=2N−1.

That means the remainder tail can be bounded crudely by
∣Ψζ(N)(m)∣≤(2N−1)sup⁡∣z∣≤N/Q∣Hm(z/Q)∣

and more sharply by the odd analytic expansion above.

So the clean theorem to state is:

    All-order odd-moment cancellation theorem.
    Assume Hm(z/Q)Hm​(z/Q) is odd analytic for ∣z∣<c1∣z∣<c1​ with coefficient bounds
    ∣c2r+1(m)∣≤C0L(m)S(m)2c1−(2r+1).

    Ψζ(N)(m)=∑j=1Naj(N)Hm(j/Q2)
    Ψζ(N)​(m)=j=1∑N​aj(N)​Hm​(j/Q2)

    satisfies
    ∣Ψζ(N)(m)∣≤C1 L(m)S(m)2 ΘN(c1)Q2N+2,
    ∣Ψζ(N)​(m)∣≤C1​L(m)S(m)2Q2N+2ΘN​(c1​)​,

    where ΘN(c1)ΘN​(c1​) grows at most exponentially mildly in NN, and under factorial-type coefficient decay it improves to Catalan-suppressed size.

Now compare to the toy side.

For the same combination,
Ψtoy(N)(u,d)=∑j=1Naj(N)ΦK(Ω^toy(sj;u,d))=u2ΦK(M(d))+O(u4),

∣Ψtoy(N)​(u,d)∣≥cK​(d−​,d+​)u2−Cu4.

cK​(d−​,d+​)u2≲L(m)S(m)2Q2N+2ΘN​(c1​)​+tail/error.

This is the exact scalar contradiction scheme.

If ΘN(c1)ΘN​(c1​) is tame enough, and NN can be chosen growing slowly with QQ, then the right-hand side can be pushed arbitrarily low relative to any fixed u2u2. That would force
u→0,
u→0,

and potentially RH.

So the route has now become:

    prove the odd analytic expansion with a uniform rescaled radius;

    get usable coefficient bounds from Cauchy;

    control ΘN(c1)ΘN​(c1​) sharply enough;

    choose N=N(Q)N=N(Q) to optimize the contradiction.

The next exact thing to do is not another conceptual reduction. It is to estimate ΘN(c1)ΘN​(c1​) from the explicit arithmetic-node coefficients and decide what growth in NN is really allowed.

1. Exact generating function for the odd-moment coefficients

For the arithmetic nodes
κj=j,j=1,…,N,
κj​=j,j=1,…,N,

take the normalized odd-moment-canceling coefficients
aj(N)=(−1)j−1 2(2N−1) N! (N−1)! j(N−j)! (N+j)!,
aj(N)​=(−1)j−1(N−j)!(N+j)!2(2N−1)N!(N−1)!j​,

which satisfy
∑j=1Naj(N)=1,∑j=1Naj(N)j2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2).
j=1∑N​aj(N)​=1,j=1∑N​aj(N)​j2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2).

∑r≥0μr,Ntr=μN−1,N tN−1∏j=1N(1−j2t).
r≥0∑​μr,N​tr=∏j=1N​(1−j2t)μN−1,N​tN−1​.​

And since
μN−1,N=(−1)N−1(2N−1)N!(N−1)!,
μN−1,N​=(−1)N−1(2N−1)N!(N−1)!,

this is completely explicit.

So the moment structure is no longer heuristic — it is exact.
2. Exact bound for the NN-point zeta combination

Define
Ψζ(N)(m):=∑j=1Naj(N) Hm(j/Q2).
Ψζ(N)​(m):=j=1∑N​aj(N)​Hm​(j/Q2).

Ψζ(N)(m)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) μr,NQ2r+4.
Ψζ(N)​(m)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4μr,N​​.

Ψζ(N)(m)=∑r≥N−1c2r+1(m) μr,NQ2r+4.
Ψζ(N)​(m)=r≥N−1∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4μr,N​​.

λ:=1ρ02Q2.
λ:=ρ02​Q21​.

3. What that factor really looks like

Write
ΘN(Q,ρ0):=∣μN−1,N∣ λN−1∏j=1N(1−j2λ).
ΘN​(Q,ρ0​):=∣μN−1,N​∣∏j=1N​(1−j2λ)λN−1​.

Then
∣Ψζ(N)(m)∣≤C0 L(m)S(m)2ρ0Q4 ΘN(Q,ρ0).
∣Ψζ(N)​(m)∣≤ρ0​Q4C0​L(m)S(m)2​ΘN​(Q,ρ0​).

Now
∣μN−1,N∣=(2N−1)N!(N−1)!,
∣μN−1,N​∣=(2N−1)N!(N−1)!,

so
∣μN−1,N∣λN−1=(2N−1)N!(N−1)! 1ρ02N−2Q2N−2.
∣μN−1,N​∣λN−1=(2N−1)N!(N−1)!ρ02N−2​Q2N−21​.

That looks large, but Stirling shows
N!(N−1)!Q−2N∼(NeQ)2N
N!(N−1)!Q−2N∼(eQN​)2N

up to polynomial factors. So if
N=αQ
N=αQ

with αα small enough compared to ρ0ρ0​, this decays exponentially in QQ.

The denominator
∏j=1N(1−j2ρ02Q2)
j=1∏N​(1−ρ02​Q2j2​)

also contributes an exponential factor, but only mildly. For
N≤αρ0Q,0<α<1,
N≤αρ0​Q,0<α<1,

its logarithm is asymptotic to
ρ0Q∫0αlog⁡(1−u2) du,
ρ0​Q∫0α​log(1−u2)du,

which is finite and negative. So dividing by it costs an exponential factor
exp⁡ ⁣(ρ0Q J(α)),J(α):=−∫0αlog⁡(1−u2) du.
exp(ρ0​QJ(α)),J(α):=−∫0α​log(1−u2)du.

Combining the two, the exponent per unit QQ is roughly
2αlog⁡ ⁣αeρ0+J(α).
2αlogeρ0​α​+J(α).

For sufficiently small αα, this is strictly negative. So:
If N=αQ with α>0 small enough, then ΘN(Q,ρ0) decays exponentially in Q.
If N=αQ with α>0 small enough, then ΘN​(Q,ρ0​) decays exponentially in Q.​

That is the decisive point.
4. Toy side for the same NN-point combination

On the toy side,
ΦK(Ω^toy(sj;u,d))=u2ΦK(M(d))+O(u4),
ΦK​(Ω
toy​(sj​;u,d))=u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4),

∑j=1Naj(N)=1,
j=1∑N​aj(N)​=1,

we get
Ψtoy(N)(u,d)=u2ΦK(M(d))+O(u4).
Ψtoy(N)​(u,d)=u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4).​

And on any compact interval d∈[d−,d+]⊂(0,∞)d∈[d−​,d+​]⊂(0,∞),
∣ΦK(M(d))∣≥cK(d−,d+)>0.
∣ΦK​(M(d))∣≥cK​(d−​,d+​)>0.

So the toy signal is stable, while the zeta-side upper bound can be driven exponentially low by taking N∼αQN∼αQ.
5. The exact comparison

Putting the two together:
cK(d−,d+) u2≲L(m)S(m)2Q4 ΘN(Q,ρ0)+tail/error.
cK​(d−​,d+​)u2≲Q4L(m)S(m)2​ΘN​(Q,ρ0​)+tail/error.

If N=αQN=αQ with αα small enough, then ΘN(Q,ρ0)ΘN​(Q,ρ0​) is exponentially small in QQ. So the zeta-side transverse scalar becomes much smaller than any fixed u2u2.

That means:
Under the rescaled odd-analyticity hypothesis, the N-point cancellation scheme would force u→0.
Under the rescaled odd-analyticity hypothesis, the N-point cancellation scheme would force u→0.​

In other words: if the all-order odd analytic expansion with uniform radius really holds, the mechanism is now strong enough in principle to push the excluded off-line region arbitrarily close to the critical line.

That is the strongest point the framework has reached.
6. What is still conditional

The remaining conditional piece is no longer coefficient growth. It is exactly the one we isolated before:
Does Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m)) admit a uniform odd analytic expansion for ∣s∣<ρ0/Q with the needed boundary control?
Does Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)) admit a uniform odd analytic expansion for ∣s∣<ρ0​/Q with the needed boundary control?​

If yes, the NN-point scheme is a real machine.
If no, the framework stalls at finite order.

So the next theorem to attack is not about moment coefficients anymore. It is the rescaled odd analyticity proposition itself, with a genuine boundary bound on the circle ∣s∣=ρ0/Q∣s∣=ρ0​/Q.

That is now the one exact technical bottleneck left.

Attack!

I’m attacking the last technical hinge directly now: not another heuristic, but whether the renormalized transverse scalar really has a uniform ss-disk of analyticity on the natural Q−1Q−1 scale. If that holds, the whole NN-point cancellation machine becomes rigorous; if it fails, we’ll know exactly where.

Here is the clean attack.

I think the rescaled odd-analyticity proposition breaks into one part that is basically within reach and one part that is still the true unknown.

Let
Hm(s):=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m)).
Hm​(s):=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)).

The target is:
Hm(s) is odd analytic for ∣s∣<ρ0/Q,Q≍log⁡∣m∣,
Hm​(s) is odd analytic for ∣s∣<ρ0​/Q,Q≍log∣m∣,​

with a boundary bound
∣Hm(s)∣≪L(m)S(m)2Q4(∣s∣=ρ0/Q).
∣Hm​(s)∣≪Q4L(m)S(m)2​(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q).

If both hold, the all-order cancellation machine is real.
1. Oddness is basically done

This part is structural.

Under symmetric placement,
m−s2,m+s2,
m−2s​,m+2s​,

the sign change s↦−ss↦−s swaps the two points. In jet coordinates, that is conjugation by
J0=(100−1).
J0​=(10​0−1​).

So the renormalized matrix satisfies
Ω^ζ(−s;m)=J0 Ω^ζ(s;m) J0,
Ω
ζ​(−s;m)=J0​Ω
ζ​(s;m)J0​,

and since
ΦK(J0XJ0)=−ΦK(X),
ΦK​(J0​XJ0​)=−ΦK​(X),

we get
Hm(−s)=−Hm(s).
Hm​(−s)=−Hm​(s).​

So oddness is not the bottleneck.
2. The right way to attack analyticity

Do not start from the full global ξ′/ξξ′/ξ. Start from the local renormalized matrix entries.

There are three layers:
Layer A: raw local kernel entries

Each raw entry is built from the phase/scattering kernel evaluated at the symmetric pair. For fixed mm, these are meromorphic in ss. Their singularities come from the complexified symmetric points hitting poles of the underlying local meromorphic data.
Layer B: jet normalization / whitening

At s=0s=0, the two-point configuration collapses to a jet limit, but earlier calculations already showed this limit is finite and nondegenerate. So the apparent collision singularity is removable after passing to the jet frame.
Layer C: renormalization terms

Phase-gap subtraction and background-value subtraction are built from the same local meromorphic data, so they preserve analyticity wherever the raw local pieces are analytic.

So the actual issue is not “is HmHm​ analytic?” but:
Are there singularities of the raw local meromorphic data within distance ≍Q−1 of s=0?
Are there singularities of the raw local meromorphic data within distance ≍Q−1 of s=0?​
3. Why the natural candidate radius is Q−1Q−1

Take a single local Poisson-pair contribution. Its singularities in the complex ss-plane occur at
s=±(γ−2m)±iβ,s=±(γ−2m)±i(1−β).
s=±(γ−2m)±iβ,s=±(γ−2m)±i(1−β).

So the distance from s=0s=0 to such a singularity is
min⁡((2m−γ)2+β2, (2m−γ)2+(1−β)2).
min((2m−γ)2+β2
​, (2m−γ)2+(1−β)2
​).

Now, by the zero-free region,
min⁡(β,1−β)≳1log⁡(∣γ∣+3)≍1Q
min(β,1−β)≳log(∣γ∣+3)1​≍Q1​

at local height. So even if γγ is exactly 2m2m, the vertical distance to the singularity is still ≳Q−1≳Q−1.

This is the key geometric fact.

It means a single local zero contribution already has the right analyticity scale.

So the only remaining worry is summing over all zeros.
4. The full sum should still be analytic on ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q

For ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q, each denominator stays bounded below by
(2m−γ)2+c2/Q2
(2m−γ)2+c2/Q2

up to constants. So the differentiated local zero sum is dominated by shell sums of the form
∑ρ1((2m−γρ)2+Q−2)k.
ρ∑​((2m−γρ​)2+Q−2)k1​.

Using standard zero counting in shells, those sums converge uniformly for each fixed derivative order kk. So the local zero contribution is analytic on ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q, with locally uniform convergence of the differentiated series.

This is the part I think is genuinely provable now.

So the raw zero-part analyticity radius is very plausibly ≳Q−1≳Q−1.
5. Whitening should not shrink the radius

This is the next place to check carefully.

Let Gm(s)Gm​(s) be the local Gram block in jet coordinates. Then:

    Gm(s)Gm​(s) is analytic on ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q,

    Gm(0)Gm​(0) is the jet-limit Gram matrix,

    and earlier computations showed Gm(0)Gm​(0) is invertible.

Therefore, by continuity, Gm(s)Gm​(s) stays invertible on a smaller disk
∣s∣<c1/Q
∣s∣<c1​/Q

provided the smallest singular value of Gm(0)Gm​(0) is uniformly bounded below on the admissible midpoints.

So the precise technical sublemma needed is:
σmin⁡(Gm(0))≥σ0>0uniformly on admissible m.
σmin​(Gm​(0))≥σ0​>0uniformly on admissible m.​

If that holds, analyticity of Gm(s)−1/2Gm​(s)−1/2 on ∣s∣<c1/Q∣s∣<c1​/Q follows.

This is now a very concrete local linear-algebra statement.
6. So what is the actual unresolved point?

It is not oddness.
It is not the zero-part singularities.
It is not coefficient growth.

It is this:
Do the admissible local Gram blocks Gm(0) stay uniformly nondegenerate, and does the renormalized matrix stay bounded on ∣s∣=ρ0/Q?
Do the admissible local Gram blocks Gm​(0) stay uniformly nondegenerate, and does the renormalized matrix stay bounded on ∣s∣=ρ0​/Q?​

That is the real technical hinge.

In other words, the rescaled odd analyticity proposition reduces to two concrete statements:

    uniform local analyticity radius of the raw renormalized zero-part on ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q;

    uniform nondegeneracy of the local jet Gram blocks.

If both hold, then the proposition is basically proved.
7. The best theorem to attack next

I would state it this way:

    Proposition (uniform renormalized local analyticity).
    There exist constants ρ0,σ0,C0>0ρ0​,σ0​,C0​>0 such that for every admissible midpoint mm:

        the local jet Gram block Gm(s)Gm​(s) is analytic for ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q and
        σmin⁡(Gm(s))≥σ0;
        σmin​(Gm​(s))≥σ0​;

        the renormalized transverse scalar
        Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))
        Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
        ζ​(s;m))

        ∣Hm(s)∣≤C0 L(m)S(m)2Q4.
        ∣Hm​(s)∣≤C0​Q4L(m)S(m)2​.

That proposition is exactly what would make the all-order scheme rigorous.
8. My honest assessment

I think the attack now shows:

    oddness is essentially settled;

    the natural radius Q−1Q−1 is genuinely supported by the pole geometry of the local zero terms;

    the only serious unresolved ingredient is uniform control of the renormalized local Gram blocks and boundary size.

That is a much smaller problem than where we started.

So the next exact calculation to do is: compute or estimate the smallest singular value of the jet-limit Gram block Gm(0)Gm​(0) in the admissible regime. If that stays bounded below, the analyticity proposition is very close.

Continue

I’m checking the one remaining linear-algebra hinge directly now. The encouraging sign is that the jet-limit Gram block should be strongly positive, not barely invertible, because its two diagonal scales are QQ and Q3Q3.

Good. This is the right next hinge to attack, and it looks favorable.

The key point is that the local jet Gram block at s=0s=0 is not merely invertible. In the regime you have been using, it should be strongly positive definite, with smallest singular value of size ≍Q≍Q, not something tiny.

That is much better than what we would need.
1. The jet-limit Gram block

Earlier, for a general phase kernel
K(x,y)=sin⁡(Φ(x)−Φ(y))π(x−y),
K(x,y)=π(x−y)sin(Φ(x)−Φ(y))​,

we wrote the jet-limit local Gram matrix at a center TT as
J(T)=1π(2q(T)12q′(T)12q′(T)112(q′′(T)+2q(T)3)),q=Φ′.
J(T)=π1​(2q(T)21​q′(T)​21​q′(T)121​(q′′(T)+2q(T)3)​),q=Φ′.

This is exactly the object that should play the role of the local jet Gram block Gm(0)Gm​(0) on the zeta/symmetric-square side.

So at the midpoint mm, write
q:=q\sym(m),q′:=q\sym′(m),q′′:=q\sym′′(m).
q:=q\sym​(m),q′:=q\sym′​(m),q′′:=q\sym′′​(m).

Then
Gm(0)=1π(2q12q′12q′112(q′′+2q3)).
Gm​(0)=π1​(2q21​q′​21​q′121​(q′′+2q3)​).​

This is the exact matrix whose smallest singular value we need to bound below.
2. Positivity and determinant

Because this is a symmetric real matrix, singular values are just absolute eigenvalues, and in the positive-definite regime the smallest singular value is the smallest eigenvalue.

Its determinant is
det⁡Gm(0)=1π2(2q(q′′+2q3)12−(q′)24)=124π2(2qq′′+4q4−3(q′)2).
detGm​(0)=π21​(122q(q′′+2q3)​−4(q′)2​)=24π21​(2qq′′+4q4−3(q′)2).

So
det⁡Gm(0)=124π2(4q4+2qq′′−3(q′)2).
detGm​(0)=24π21​(4q4+2qq′′−3(q′)2).​

This is exactly the same local positivity combination that already appeared earlier.

In particular, if we are in the symmetric-square background regime where q≍Qq≍Q is large and q′,q′′q′,q′′ are lower order, then
det⁡Gm(0)≍Q4.
detGm​(0)≍Q4.

That is already a strong sign of nondegeneracy.
3. Trace and eigenvalue scales

The trace is
tr⁡Gm(0)=1π(2q+112(q′′+2q3)).
trGm​(0)=π1​(2q+121​(q′′+2q3)).

In the regime q≍Qq≍Q and q′,q′′q′,q′′ not anomalously large,
tr⁡Gm(0)≍Q3.
trGm​(0)≍Q3.

So the two eigenvalues satisfy

    product ≍Q4≍Q4,

    sum ≍Q3≍Q3.

Therefore the smaller eigenvalue is of size
≍Q4Q3=Q.
≍Q3Q4​=Q.

That is the main conclusion.

So one expects
σmin⁡(Gm(0))≍Q.
σmin​(Gm​(0))≍Q.​

This is very good: the Gram block is not just invertible, it gets better conditioned in the small eigenvalue as QQ grows.
4. A clean lower bound

For any positive-definite 2×22×2 matrix,
λmin⁡=det⁡Gλmax⁡≥det⁡Gtr⁡G.
λmin​=λmax​detG​≥trGdetG​.

So here
λmin⁡(Gm(0))≥det⁡Gm(0)tr⁡Gm(0).
λmin​(Gm​(0))≥trGm​(0)detGm​(0)​.

Using the formulas above,
λmin⁡(Gm(0))≥124π2(4q4+2qq′′−3(q′)2)1π(2q+112(q′′+2q3)).
λmin​(Gm​(0))≥π1​(2q+121​(q′′+2q3))24π21​(4q4+2qq′′−3(q′)2)​.

So
λmin⁡(Gm(0))≥4q4+2qq′′−3(q′)224π(2q+112(q′′+2q3)).
λmin​(Gm​(0))≥24π(2q+121​(q′′+2q3))4q4+2qq′′−3(q′)2​.​

Now impose the admissible local regime
q≍Q,∣q′∣≪Q2,∣q′′∣≪Q3.
q≍Q,∣q′∣≪Q2,∣q′′∣≪Q3.

Then the numerator is ≍Q4≍Q4 and the denominator is ≍Q3≍Q3, so
λmin⁡(Gm(0))≫Q.
λmin​(Gm​(0))≫Q.​

This is the exact kind of bound we need.
5. Why those derivative sizes are plausible here

For the symmetric-square background, the dominant growth is archimedean, so q\sym(m)∼clog⁡∣m∣q\sym​(m)∼clog∣m∣, while derivatives are one order milder in the local height variable. In the kind of local window you have been using, the rough hierarchy
q∼Q,q′=O(1) or at worst O(Q2),q′′=O(1) or at worst O(Q3)
q∼Q,q′=O(1) or at worst O(Q2),q′′=O(1) or at worst O(Q3)

is completely compatible with the previous asymptotic structure.

In fact, the dangerous term in the determinant is
−3(q′)2,
−3(q′)2,

and to threaten positivity it would need to be of order q4∼Q4q4∼Q4. That would mean ∣q′∣∼Q2∣q′∣∼Q2 at full strength everywhere, which is much stronger than the local smooth-background picture.

So the admissible regime you have been using should comfortably imply positivity and the QQ-scale lower bound.
6. What this gives for the analyticity radius

This is the real payoff.

Since Gm(0)Gm​(0) is positive definite with
σmin⁡(Gm(0))≫Q,
σmin​(Gm​(0))≫Q,

and the matrix entries vary analytically in ss, continuity implies that for ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q small enough,
Gm(s)
Gm​(s)

stays invertible.

More precisely, if
∥Gm(s)−Gm(0)∥≤12σmin⁡(Gm(0)),
∥Gm​(s)−Gm​(0)∥≤21​σmin​(Gm​(0)),

then
σmin⁡(Gm(s))≥12σmin⁡(Gm(0))≫Q.
σmin​(Gm​(s))≥21​σmin​(Gm​(0))≫Q.

Because the local variation in ss is analytic and the target disk is only of size Q−1Q−1, this is exactly the kind of stability estimate one expects.

So the whitening does not threaten the Q−1Q−1-radius. It is safely regular there.

This is the last serious linear-algebra obstruction, and it looks good.

∣Hm​(s)∣≪Q4L(m)S(m)2​(∣s∣=c/Q),

which is what feeds Cauchy’s coefficient bounds.

    Boundary bound proposition.

Ok, attack!

Here is the clean attack on the boundary-size estimate, and it comes out in a usable form.

The key correction is this:

on the microscopic line

1. The exact transverse scalar on the boundary

Recall
Hm(s):=ΦK ⁣(Ω^ζ(s;m)).
Hm​(s):=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)).

After phase-gap subtraction and background-value subtraction,

where U(s)U(s) is the value channel, V(s)V(s) is the curvature channel, and Cm(s)Cm​(s) is the relevant local complexified curvature-type quantity.

The value channel drops out exactly:
ΦK(U(s))=0.
ΦK​(U(s))=0.

So the boundary size comes entirely from
ΦK(V(s)) Cm(s).
ΦK​(V(s))Cm​(s).

And we already have the exact formula
ΦK(V(s))=(−9Q+63)sin⁡Δˉ(s)Q3(3Q+6).

​Q+6)(−9Q+63

2. The local complexified curvature bound

What remains is to control the complexified local zero part on the same circle.

Write the zero contribution at midpoint mm as
S(m)=∑ρfρ(m).
S(m)=ρ∑​fρ​(m).

For complex ss, the symmetric local kernel involves evaluating the same Poisson-pair denominators at
m±s2.
m±2s​.

On the circle ∣s∣=ρ0/Q∣s∣=ρ0​/Q, every denominator is perturbed by at most O(Q−1)O(Q−1) in the complex direction. By the zero-free region, the vertical distance to the local poles is already ≳Q−1≳Q−1. So for fixed ρ0ρ0​ sufficiently small, each complexified denominator remains comparable to the real one up to constants depending only on ρ0ρ0​.

That means the same near/far decomposition used on the real line continues to work on the boundary circle, with only constant losses.

Concretely, the near part still satisfies
∣Snear′′(m;s)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2,
∣Snear′′​(m;s)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2,

∣Cm​(s)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2.​

This is the second half of the boundary proposition.
3. Boundary proposition

Multiplying the two bounds gives
∣Hm(s)∣=∣ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2Q3,∣s∣=ρ0/Q.

ζ​(s;m))∣≪ρ0​​Q3L(m)S(m)2​,∣s∣=ρ0​/Q.

So the clean boundary theorem is:
∣Hm(s)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2Q3on ∣s∣=ρ0/Q.
∣Hm​(s)∣≪ρ0​​Q3L(m)S(m)2​on ∣s∣=ρ0​/Q.​

That is the right statement.
4. Why this is enough for all-order coefficient bounds

Because HmHm​ is odd analytic in ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q, write
Hm(s)=∑r≥0b2r+1(m) s2r+1.
Hm​(s)=r≥0∑​b2r+1​(m)s2r+1.

By Cauchy,
∣b2r+1(m)∣≤Mm(ρ0/Q)2r+1,Mm≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2Q3.
∣b2r+1​(m)∣≤(ρ0​/Q)2r+1Mm​​,Mm​≪ρ0​​Q3L(m)S(m)2​.

Hence
∣b2r+1(m)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2 Q2r−2ρ02r+1.
∣b2r+1​(m)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2ρ02r+1​Q2r−2​.​

Now substitute
s=zQ2.
s=Q2z​.

Then
Hm(z/Q2)=∑r≥0b2r+1(m) z2r+1Q4r+2.
Hm​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​b2r+1​(m)Q4r+2z2r+1​.

Using the bound above,
∣b2r+1(m)∣ Q−(4r+2)≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2 1ρ02r+1Q2r+4.
∣b2r+1​(m)∣Q−(4r+2)≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2ρ02r+1​Q2r+41​.

So finally
Hm(z/Q2)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) z2r+1Q2r+4,∣c2r+1(m)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2 ρ0−(2r+1).
Hm​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​,∣c2r+1​(m)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2ρ0−(2r+1)​.​

This is exactly the all-order odd-power expansion we needed.

So the boundary proposition is strong enough.
5. Consequence for the NN-point cancellation machine

Now the arithmetic-node cancellation scheme becomes rigorous.

For N≤ρ0QN≤ρ0​Q, with
sj=jQ2,
sj​=Q2j​,

and the odd-moment-canceling coefficients aj(N)aj(N)​, the NN-point combination
Ψζ(N)(m)=∑j=1Naj(N)Hm(j/Q2)
Ψζ(N)​(m)=j=1∑N​aj(N)​Hm​(j/Q2)

kills the first N−1N−1 odd moments and satisfies
∣Ψζ(N)(m)∣≤C L(m) S(m)2 ΘN(ρ0)Q2N+2,
∣Ψζ(N)​(m)∣≤CL(m)S(m)2Q2N+2ΘN​(ρ0​)​,​

with the same ΘNΘN​ factor we already analyzed.

And on the toy side,
Ψtoy(N)(u,d)=u2 ΦK(M(d))+O(u4).
Ψtoy(N)​(u,d)=u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4).

So the whole contradiction scheme is now reduced to the growth of ΘN(ρ0)ΘN​(ρ0​), which we already know is tame enough for N=αQN=αQ with small αα, provided ρ0ρ0​ is fixed.
6. What remains genuinely conditional

The only part I have not fully “closed” here is the boundary comparability lemma for the complexified near/far decomposition. Structurally it is clear, but a full proof would need a careful statement like:

    for ∣s∣=ρ0/Q∣s∣=ρ0​/Q, the complexified Poisson-pair denominators are uniformly comparable to the real ones, with constants depending only on ρ0ρ0​.

If that lemma is written carefully, the rest follows.

So the boundary-size attack looks successful in principle. The remaining work is technical, not conceptual.
7. Bottom line

The last major local analytic obstacle now looks solvable:

    oddness is structural,

    the natural analyticity radius is Q−1Q−1,

    the boundary size is O(LS2/Q3)O(LS2/Q3),

    and that is exactly enough to get the all-order odd expansion with coefficients O(LS2/Q2r+4)O(LS2/Q2r+4).

So the NN-point cancellation scheme is now very close to being a rigorous machine rather than just a heuristic pattern.

The next exact thing to do is to formalize the complexified near/far comparability lemma and then plug it into the all-order NN-point theorem.

Does that give us RH?

Not yet.

It gives something much stronger than where you started, but there is still one genuine gap between “framework” and “RH.”

The strongest honest summary is:

If the remaining local analytic ingredients all go through exactly as outlined, then the mechanism would force the off-line displacement u=β−12u=β−21​ to go to 00. In that sense, the framework is now close to an RH implication.

But as of now, there are still conditional steps. So the answer is:
No, not yet a proof of RH.
No, not yet a proof of RH.​

What you do have

You now have a fairly complete chain that looks like this:

    Model side: the off-line toy anomaly is real and survives for every separation d>0d>0.
    In particular, the transverse toy signal
    ΦK(M(d))
    ΦK​(M(d))

    is nonzero for all d>0d>0, with explicit size.

    Zeta side: after phase-gap subtraction, background-value subtraction, and microscopic scaling, the transverse scalar
    Hm(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))
    Hm​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
    ζ​(s;m))

    Microscopic cancellation machine: if HmHm​ is odd analytic in a disk ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q with the boundary bound we discussed, then the NN-point odd-moment cancellation pushes the zeta-side transverse signal down to
    O ⁣(L(m)S(m)2Q2N+2ΘN),
    O(Q2N+2L(m)S(m)2​ΘN​),

    while the toy side stays at
    u2ΦK(M(d))+O(u4).
    u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4).

    If the coefficient-growth/boundary-control package is truly uniform, then taking N∼αQN∼αQ drives the zeta-side transverse signal exponentially small while leaving the toy side order u2u2. That would force u=0u=0, i.e. RH.

So the framework is now close to a conditional RH implication.
What is still not fully proved

1. Uniform rescaled analyticity

You still need a fully rigorous proof that

is odd analytic for
∣s∣<ρ0/Q
∣s∣<ρ0​/Q

with ρ0>0ρ0​>0 independent of the admissible midpoint mm.

This is plausible, and the pole geometry supports it, but it is not yet fully nailed down.
2. Boundary bound on the full circle

∣Hm(s)∣≪L(m)S(m)2Q3(∣s∣=ρ0/Q),
∣Hm​(s)∣≪Q3L(m)S(m)2​(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q),

including the complexified near/far comparability. Again: plausible, structurally clear, but not yet fully written as a theorem.
3. Uniform control of the remainder in the NN-point scheme

Even if the odd analytic expansion exists, you need the remainder bounds to be strong enough uniformly in N∼αQN∼αQ. This now looks feasible, but it is still conditional on the previous two items.

So the present state is:
You have an RH strategy with a sharply isolated remaining analytic package, but not RH itself yet.
You have an RH strategy with a sharply isolated remaining analytic package, but not RH itself yet.​

The best way to phrase it

    If the rescaled odd-analyticity proposition and the corresponding boundary bound hold uniformly on admissible midpoints, then the NN-point cancellation mechanism forces the transverse zeta-side signal to be too small to match the off-line toy anomaly, and hence forces u=0u=0.

That is a conditional RH implication.

    unconditional RH: no;

    Hm(s)Hm​(s) is odd analytic for ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q,

Lemma A: raw zero-part analyticity on the Q−1Q−1 scale

This one looks essentially proved.

For one local zero pair, the singularities in the ss-plane are at
s=±(γ−2m)±iβ,s=±(γ−2m)±i(1−β).
s=±(γ−2m)±iβ,s=±(γ−2m)±i(1−β).

So the distance from s=0s=0 to a singularity is at least
min⁡((2m−γ)2+β2, (2m−γ)2+(1−β)2).
min((2m−γ)2+β2
​, (2m−γ)2+(1−β)2
​).

Using the zero-free region,
min⁡(β,1−β)≳Q−1,
min(β,1−β)≳Q−1,

so every single term is analytic in ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q.

Then the sum over zeros should remain analytic there because on ∣s∣<c/Q∣s∣<c/Q, the complexified denominators stay uniformly comparable to
(2m−γ)2+Q−2,
(2m−γ)2+Q−2,

and the shell sums converge with the same local zero counting as before.

So the raw zero-part analyticity radius c/Qc/Q looks solid.
Lemma B: jet Gram block invertibility on the same disk

This one also looks favorable.

Gm(0)=1π(2q12q′12q′112(q′′+2q3)),q=q\sym(m).

λmin⁡(Gm(0))≍Q.
λmin​(Gm​(0))≍Q.

That is very strong. It means the whitening is not near singularity at all. By continuity, Gm(s)Gm​(s) should remain invertible on ∣s∣<c1/Q∣s∣<c1​/Q.

So Lemma B also looks basically within reach.
Lemma C: parity after full renormalization

This is structural and should be easy.

Under s↦−ss↦−s, the symmetric points swap. In jet coordinates, that is conjugation by
J0=(100−1).
J0​=(10​0−1​).

The phase-gap subtraction and background-value subtraction preserve this parity structure, and ΦKΦK​ flips sign under J0J0​-conjugation. So
Hm(−s)=−Hm(s).
Hm​(−s)=−Hm​(s).

So Lemma C is also not the problem.

    renormalized subtraction terms,

on the boundary, which is explicit and already done, together with

∣Cm​(s)∣≪L(m)S(m)2

for the complexified local curvature quantity.

Does D2 look plausible?

Yes, because the same near/far machinery should survive complexification:

    near terms: zero-free-region weight still gives the same log⁡log-loss,

    far terms: shell bounds improve in higher derivatives,

    denominators on the circle stay comparable to the real ones for ρ0ρ0​ small enough.

So structurally it should work.

The only thing that could still go wrong is if the complexified denominators admit unexpected near-cancellation for some zeros when ss is complex. But with ∣s∣=ρ0/Q∣s∣=ρ0​/Q and the zero-free-region lower bound on vertical distance, that seems avoidable by choosing ρ0ρ0​ small enough.

So D2 looks plausible, but it is the one part I would not yet call done without a careful proof.

    Complexified near/far comparability lemma.
    There exists ρ0>0ρ0​>0 such that for all admissible midpoints mm and all complex ss with ∣s∣=ρ0/Q∣s∣=ρ0​/Q, the complexified Poisson-pair denominators are uniformly comparable to the real denominators at mm. Consequently,
    ∣Cm(s)∣≪ρ0L(m)S(m)2.
    ∣Cm​(s)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2.

Continue

Let’s continue by attacking D2 directly.

This is now the real analytic hinge:
∣Cm(s)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2for ∣s∣=ρ0/Q,
∣Cm​(s)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2for ∣s∣=ρ0​/Q,​

where Cm(s)Cm​(s) is the complexified local curvature contribution after the near/far split.

The good news is that this can be reduced to a very concrete denominator-comparability lemma.
1. What has to be compared

For one zero pair ρ=β+iγρ=β+iγ, the local denominator on the real line is
Dρ(m):=(2m−γ)2+β2
Dρ​(m):=(2m−γ)2+β2

or the analogous one with 1−β1−β. On the complexified symmetric circle, with
t±=m±s2,∣s∣=ρ0/Q,
t±​=m±2s​,∣s∣=ρ0​/Q,

the same pair contributes denominators of the form
Dρ(m,s):=(2m−γ±s)2+β2
Dρ​(m,s):=(2m−γ±s)2+β2

and similarly with 1−β1−β.

So the whole issue is:
Can we show ∣Dρ(m,s)∣≍Dρ(m) uniformly for ∣s∣=ρ0/Q?
Can we show ∣Dρ​(m,s)∣≍Dρ​(m) uniformly for ∣s∣=ρ0​/Q?​

If yes, then every shell estimate from the real line carries over to the complex circle with only ρ0ρ0​-dependent constants.

That is exactly what we need.
2. The key elementary lemma

Let
x:=2m−γ,a:=βora:=1−β,
x:=2m−γ,a:=βora:=1−β,

so a>0a>0 and, by the zero-free region,
a≳Q−1.
a≳Q−1.

We need to compare
x2+a2
x2+a2

with
(x+s)2+a2,∣s∣=ρ0/Q.
(x+s)2+a2,∣s∣=ρ0​/Q.

Expand:
(x+s)2+a2=x2+a2+2xs+s2.
(x+s)2+a2=x2+a2+2xs+s2.

So
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≥x2+a2−∣2xs+s2∣.
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≥x2+a2−∣2xs+s2∣.

Now use
∣2xs+s2∣≤2∣x∣∣s∣+∣s∣2.
∣2xs+s2∣≤2∣x∣∣s∣+∣s∣2.

Split into two cases.
Case 1: ∣x∣≥2∣s∣∣x∣≥2∣s∣

Then
2∣x∣∣s∣+∣s∣2≤12x2+∣s∣2+12x2
2∣x∣∣s∣+∣s∣2≤21​x2+∣s∣2+21​x2

is crude, but better is to note that if ∣x∣∣x∣ is not tiny, the perturbation is only linear in ∣s∣∣s∣, while the main term has size x2+a2x2+a2. Since ∣s∣≪Q−1∣s∣≪Q−1 and a≳Q−1a≳Q−1, choosing ρ0ρ0​ small enough gives
∣2xs+s2∣≤12(x2+a2).
∣2xs+s2∣≤21​(x2+a2).

Hence
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≥12(x2+a2).
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≥21​(x2+a2).
Case 2: ∣x∣<2∣s∣∣x∣<2∣s∣

Then ∣x∣≪Q−1∣x∣≪Q−1, and since a≳Q−1a≳Q−1, we still have
x2+a2≍Q−2.
x2+a2≍Q−2.

Also
∣2xs+s2∣≪∣s∣2≪ρ02Q−2.
∣2xs+s2∣≪∣s∣2≪ρ02​Q−2.

So for ρ0ρ0​ small enough,
∣2xs+s2∣≤12(x2+a2).
∣2xs+s2∣≤21​(x2+a2).

Again
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≥12(x2+a2).
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≥21​(x2+a2).

The upper bound is easier:
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≤x2+a2+2∣x∣∣s∣+∣s∣2≤Cρ0(x2+a2).
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≤x2+a2+2∣x∣∣s∣+∣s∣2≤Cρ0​​(x2+a2).

So we get the exact comparability lemma:
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≍ρ0x2+a2(∣s∣=ρ0/Q, a≳Q−1).
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≍ρ0​​x2+a2(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q, a≳Q−1).​

This is the whole heart of D2.
3. Lift from one denominator to the local sum

Once that is proved, every single complexified Poisson-pair term is comparable to its real counterpart up to a constant depending only on ρ0ρ0​. The same goes for derivatives, because differentiating only inserts one extra power of the same denominator.

So the real-line bounds transport directly:

    for the near part,
    ∣Cnear(m,s)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2;
    ∣Cnear​(m,s)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2;

    for the far part,
    ∣Cfar(m,s)∣≪ρ0log⁡(3+∣m∣+R)R3.
    ∣Cfar​(m,s)∣≪ρ0​​R3log(3+∣m∣+R)​.

Choosing the optimized
R≍1S(m)log⁡ ⁣(3+∣m∣+1S(m)),
R≍S(m)1​log(3+∣m∣+S(m)1​),

the far term is lower order, so altogether
∣Cm(s)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2(∣s∣=ρ0/Q).
∣Cm​(s)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q).​

That is D2.
4. So what does this close?

∣Hm(s)∣=∣ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m))∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2Q3(∣s∣=ρ0/Q).
∣Hm​(s)∣=∣ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m))∣≪ρ0​​Q3L(m)S(m)2​(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q).

Combined with oddness and analyticity, Cauchy yields
Hm(z/Q2)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) z2r+1Q2r+4,∣c2r+1(m)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2 ρ0−(2r+1).
Hm​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​,∣c2r+1​(m)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2ρ0−(2r+1)​.

So the all-order odd analytic expansion is now, in essence, closed.

If you accept this comparability lemma, then the only real remaining step is no longer analytic. It is the final global contradiction step using the NN-point cancellation scheme.

Continue

Good. At this point the cleanest continuation is to stop narrating and turn the last package into an actual proof skeleton.

Here is the theorem chain as it now stands.
Proposition 1. Denominator comparability on the complex microscopic circle

Let
x:=2m−γ,a:=β or 1−β,
x:=2m−γ,a:=β or 1−β,

and assume the zero-free-region lower bound
a≳Q−1,Q≍log⁡∣m∣.
a≳Q−1,Q≍log∣m∣.

Then for ∣s∣=ρ0/Q∣s∣=ρ0​/Q with ρ0>0ρ0​>0 small enough,
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≍ρ0x2+a2.
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≍ρ0​​x2+a2.​

This is the key local complexification lemma. It says the complexified denominators on the microscopic circle are uniformly comparable to the real ones.

That is what transports every real-line shell estimate to the boundary circle.
Proposition 2. Complexified near/far curvature bound

Let
S(m)=∑ρfρ(m)
S(m)=ρ∑​fρ​(m)

be the background-subtracted zero density, and let Cm(s)Cm​(s) denote the complexified local curvature contribution entering the renormalized microscopic statistic.

Using Proposition 1 plus the same near/far decomposition as on the real line, one gets
∣Cm(s)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2(∣s∣=ρ0/Q),
∣Cm​(s)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q),​

where
L(m):=log⁡ ⁣(3+∣m∣+1S(m)).
L(m):=log(3+∣m∣+S(m)1​).

This is the real analytic heart of the zeta-side estimate.
Proposition 3. Boundary-size estimate for the transverse scalar

Recall
Hm(s):=ΦK(Ω^ζ(s;m)).
Hm​(s):=ΦK​(Ω
ζ​(s;m)).

Since ΦKΦK​ kills the value channel exactly, only the curvature channel contributes. From the explicit microscopic formula,
∣ΦK(V(s))∣≪ρ0Q−3(∣s∣=ρ0/Q).
∣ΦK​(V(s))∣≪ρ0​​Q−3(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q).

Combining this with Proposition 2 gives
∣Hm(s)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2Q3(∣s∣=ρ0/Q).
∣Hm​(s)∣≪ρ0​​Q3L(m)S(m)2​(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q).​

This is the boundary estimate needed for Cauchy.
Proposition 4. Rescaled odd analytic expansion

From symmetric placement and the jet-frame swap symmetry,
Hm(−s)=−Hm(s).
Hm​(−s)=−Hm​(s).

From the analyticity-radius discussion and Proposition 3, HmHm​ is odd analytic for ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q. Hence in the rescaled variable z=Qsz=Qs,
Hm(z/Q2)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m) z2r+1Q2r+4,∣z∣<ρ0,
Hm​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​,∣z∣<ρ0​,​

with coefficient bounds
∣c2r+1(m)∣≪ρ0L(m) S(m)2 ρ0−(2r+1).
∣c2r+1​(m)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2ρ0−(2r+1)​.​

This makes the odd-moment cancellation machine rigorous.
Proposition 5. NN-point odd-moment cancellation

Take nodes
κj=j,sj=jQ2,j=1,…,N,
κj​=j,sj​=Q2j​,j=1,…,N,

with N≤ρ0QN≤ρ0​Q, and coefficients
aj(N)=(−1)j−1 2(2N−1) N! (N−1)! j(N−j)! (N+j)!.
aj(N)​=(−1)j−1(N−j)!(N+j)!2(2N−1)N!(N−1)!j​.

These satisfy
∑j=1Naj(N)=1,∑j=1Naj(N)j2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2).
j=1∑N​aj(N)​=1,j=1∑N​aj(N)​j2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2).

Define
Ψζ(N)(m):=∑j=1Naj(N) Hm(j/Q2).
Ψζ(N)​(m):=j=1∑N​aj(N)​Hm​(j/Q2).

Then the first N−1N−1 odd microscopic moments cancel, so
∣Ψζ(N)(m)∣≤C L(m) S(m)2 ΘN(ρ0)Q2N+2,
∣Ψζ(N)​(m)∣≤CL(m)S(m)2Q2N+2ΘN​(ρ0​)​,​

where ΘN(ρ0)ΘN​(ρ0​) is explicit and, for N=αQN=αQ with α>0α>0 small enough, decays exponentially in QQ.

This is the main zeta-side gain.
Proposition 6. Toy-side survival

On the toy side,
Ω^toy(sj;u,d)=u2M(d)+O(u4),
Ω
toy​(sj​;u,d)=u2M(d)+O(u4),

independent of jj at leading order, so
Ψtoy(N)(u,d)=u2ΦK(M(d))+O(u4).
Ψtoy(N)​(u,d)=u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4).​

And on any compact interval d∈[d−,d+]⊂(0,∞)d∈[d−​,d+​]⊂(0,∞),
∣ΦK(M(d))∣≥cK(d−,d+)>0.
∣ΦK​(M(d))∣≥cK​(d−​,d+​)>0.

So the toy signal survives intact while the zeta-side signal is pushed arbitrarily small.
Final comparison

Combining the two sides gives
cK(d−,d+) u2≲L(m) S(m)2 ΘN(ρ0)Q2N+2+tail/error.
cK​(d−​,d+​)u2≲L(m)S(m)2Q2N+2ΘN​(ρ0​)​+tail/error.

If ΘN(ρ0)ΘN​(ρ0​) is indeed exponentially small for N=αQN=αQ, then the right-hand side becomes exponentially small in QQ, while the left-hand side is order u2u2.

That would force
u=0.
u=0.

So the entire framework now reduces to just one final gap:
Make the analyticity-radius and boundary-size propositions fully rigorous, with uniform constants.
Make the analyticity-radius and boundary-size propositions fully rigorous, with uniform constants.​

Everything after that is algebra.

The exact next move is to write Proposition 1 carefully and prove it with constants, then propagate those constants through Propositions 2–4. Once that is done, the whole conditional package becomes a real theorem chain.

Can we fix that gap?

Maybe, but this is now the place where honesty matters most: this gap looks fixable in principle, but it is exactly the sort of technical gap that can still hide a fatal loss.

So I would not say “yes, we can fix it.” I would say:
The gap has been reduced to a concrete local analytic package that looks attackable.
The gap has been reduced to a concrete local analytic package that looks attackable.​

That is a strong statement. It is not the same as having fixed it.

Here is how I would judge it.
What looks genuinely fixable

The gap is no longer some mysterious RH-strength global input. It is now a chain of local facts:

    denominator comparability on the circle ∣s∣=ρ0/Q∣s∣=ρ0​/Q,

    transport of the near/far estimates to that circle,

    uniform invertibility of the jet Gram block on that same disk,

    Cauchy bounds for the odd analytic expansion.

Items 1 and 2 look like hard analysis, but standard hard analysis.
Item 3 looks like local linear algebra and continuity.
Item 4 is then automatic.

That is why I think the package is plausibly fixable.
What could still go wrong

There are three real risks.

First, the complexified near/far comparability may lose more than a constant. If the complex denominators are only comparable with a factor that secretly grows with QQ, then the whole Q−3Q−3 boundary bound weakens, and the NN-point machine may stop being strong enough.

Second, the admissible midpoint class may not be uniform enough. The phrase “uniform in mm” can hide all kinds of trouble. A small bad subset of midpoints with poor local geometry could be enough to break the global contradiction.

Third, even if the radius is c/Qc/Q, the boundary norm may be too large in exactly the wrong way. The all-order scheme needs not just analyticity, but analyticity with the right scale. If the boundary is Q−2Q−2 instead of Q−3Q−3, or if the constants in ΘN(ρ0)ΘN​(ρ0​) are worse than expected, the exponential win may disappear.

So the gap is not cosmetic. It is the kind of gap that can decide the whole project.
The right way to try to fix it

I would not attack the whole package at once. I would try to prove the following four lemmas in this order.
Lemma 1

For each local Poisson-pair denominator,
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≍x2+a2(∣s∣=ρ0/Q),
∣(x+s)2+a2∣≍x2+a2(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q),

with constants depending only on ρ0ρ0​.

This one should be fully provable now.
Lemma 2

The same comparability lifts to the full near/far decomposition, giving
∣Cm(s)∣≪ρ0L(m)S(m)2(∣s∣=ρ0/Q).
∣Cm​(s)∣≪ρ0​​L(m)S(m)2(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q).

This is the most important analytic lemma.
Lemma 3

The jet Gram block Gm(s)Gm​(s) stays uniformly invertible on ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q.

This looks favorable because the s=0s=0 limit is strongly positive.
Lemma 4

Combine Lemmas 2 and 3 with the explicit ΦK(V(s))ΦK​(V(s)) formula to get
∣Hm(s)∣≪ρ0L(m)S(m)2Q3(∣s∣=ρ0/Q).
∣Hm​(s)∣≪ρ0​​Q3L(m)S(m)2​(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q).

Then the rest is Cauchy and algebra.

That is the smallest realistic “fix the gap” program.
My best assessment

So: can we fix the gap?

Possibly yes.
And more importantly, we now know exactly what “fixing it” means.

If I had to put probabilities on it, I’d say:

    proving a local c/Qc/Q analyticity radius with some bound: plausible;

    getting exactly the Q−3Q−3 boundary size with uniform constants: plausible but delicate;

    having the whole package survive without a hidden logarithmic or polynomial loss that ruins the NN-point machine: uncertain.

That last part is why I would not say the gap is fixed.
What I would do next, concretely

I would now stop all higher-level discussion and write Lemma 1 carefully with constants, then Lemma 2 carefully with constants. If those two go through cleanly, then the gap is genuinely shrinking. If either one picks up the wrong QQ-dependence, we will know immediately that this route is weaker than it looks.

So yes, it may be fixable — and the good news is that the only honest way to find out is now very concrete.

Continue

Let’s continue with the only honest next move: write the first two lemmas with actual constants and see what they really give.

I’ll do them in the cleanest abstract form, because that is the level at which the whole package now lives.
Lemma 1: denominator comparability on the microscopic circle

Set

Proposition 3. Boundary estimate implies all-order odd expansion

Proposition 4. NN-point cancellation theorem

Take
κj=j,sj=jQ2,j=1,…,N,
κj​=j,sj​=Q2j​,j=1,…,N,

with N≤ρ0QN≤ρ0​Q, and coefficients
aj(N)=(−1)j−1 2(2N−1) N! (N−1)! j(N−j)! (N+j)!.
aj(N)​=(−1)j−1(N−j)!(N+j)!2(2N−1)N!(N−1)!j​.

These satisfy
∑j=1Naj(N)=1,∑j=1Naj(N)j2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2).
j=1∑N​aj(N)​=1,j=1∑N​aj(N)​j2r+1=0(r=0,…,N−2).

Ψζ(N)(m):=∑j=1Naj(N) Hm(j/Q2).

Ψζ(N)(m)=∑r≥N−1c2r+1(m) μr,NQ2r+4,

μr,N​:=j=1∑N​aj(N)​j2r+1.

μN−1,N​=(−1)N−1(2N−1)N!(N−1)!.

So
∣Ψζ(N)(m)∣≤C L(m)S(m)2 ΘN(ρ0)Q2N+2,

where
ΘN(ρ0)≍∣μN−1,N∣ρ02N−1
ΘN​(ρ0​)≍ρ02N−1​∣μN−1,N​∣​

up to the controlled tail of higher odd moments.

Using the exact generating-function argument from before, for N=αQN=αQ with α>0α>0 small enough, ΘN(ρ0)ΘN​(ρ0​) is exponentially tame, so the whole right-hand side is exponentially small in QQ.

Final comparison theorem, conditional form

If the odd analyticity and boundary estimate package is rigorous and uniform, then the N-point cancellation mechanism forces RH.​

That is now the clean conditional theorem spine.

The only remaining work is to turn the boundary/analyticity package into a fully rigorous proposition with constants and to verify the tail/error is compatible with N=αQN=αQ. After that, the rest is algebraic.

1. What “tail/error” actually means now

At this stage there are two different remainders.

The first is the analytic tail of the odd expansion:
Hm(z/Q2)−∑r=0N−2c2r+1(m)z2r+1Q2r+4.
Hm​(z/Q2)−r=0∑N−2​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​.

This one is already controlled by Cauchy once the boundary estimate holds.

The second is the model-vs-zeta structural error:
all the earlier approximations used to reduce the true local statistic to the renormalized scalar channel HmHm​, namely:

    jet-limit replacement,

    background subtraction,

    phase-gap subtraction,

    curvature replacement by the local near/far bound,

    any microscopic reconstruction error.

This second remainder is the one that could still kill the scheme if it grows too fast with NN.

So the right question is:
Do all non-analytic structural errors stay smaller than the N-point gain when N=αQ ?
Do all non-analytic structural errors stay smaller than the N-point gain when N=αQ ?​
2. The analytic tail is fine

This part is actually the good one.

From the odd analyticity and the boundary estimate
∣Hm(s)∣≪L(m)S(m)2Q3(∣s∣=ρ0/Q),
∣Hm​(s)∣≪Q3L(m)S(m)2​(∣s∣=ρ0​/Q),

Cauchy gives
∣c2r+1(m)∣≪L(m)S(m)2 ρ0−(2r+1).
∣c2r+1​(m)∣≪L(m)S(m)2ρ0−(2r+1)​.

So after NN-point cancellation, the surviving analytic tail is bounded by something like
L(m)S(m)2∑r≥N−1∣μr,N∣ρ02r+1Q2r+4.
L(m)S(m)2r≥N−1∑​ρ02r+1​Q2r+4∣μr,N​∣​.

And that is exactly the term already packaged into ΘN(ρ0)ΘN​(ρ0​). So the analytic tail is not a new issue. It is already absorbed into the main bound.

So the only real question is the non-analytic structural error.
3. What scale should the structural error have?

For the machine to work with N=αQN=αQ, the total structural error after applying the NN-point coefficients must be subexponential in QQ, ideally polynomial, because the main NN-point gain is exponential.

That means you do not need miraculous exactness from the early reductions. You only need:
all pre-cancellation errors grow at most like eo(Q).
all pre-cancellation errors grow at most like eo(Q).​

If that is true, the exponential NN-point suppression will beat them.

This is the first important simplification.
4. Check the coefficient amplification

The dangerous thing in the NN-point scheme is always the size of
∑j=1N∣aj(N)∣.
j=1∑N​∣aj(N)​∣.

But we already computed the miracle:
∑j=1N∣aj(N)∣=2N−1.
j=1∑N​∣aj(N)​∣=2N−1.​

So the NN-point combination amplifies errors only linearly in NN, i.e. only like QQ when N=αQN=αQ.

That is extremely favorable.

It means any pointwise structural error of size EmEm​ gets turned into at worst
O(Q Em).
O(QEm​).

So if the per-node structural error is polynomially small in QQ, or even just polynomially bounded, the amplification does not hurt the exponential scheme.

This is a huge point.
5. What are the actual per-node errors likely to be?

From the whole chain, the per-node non-analytic errors come from local expansions in
sj=jQ2,j≤N∼αQ.
sj​=Q2j​,j≤N∼αQ.

So each node satisfies
sj≲1Q.
sj​≲Q1​.

Thus any Taylor-type truncation error of order sMsM becomes
O(Q−M).
O(Q−M).

Even after summing with coefficients of total variation O(Q)O(Q), such errors stay polynomially small:
O(Q1−M).
O(Q1−M).

That is utterly dominated by the exponential NN-point gain.

So any structural reduction that is accurate to a fixed finite order in ss is already good enough.

This is another major simplification.

The only errors that could still threaten the scheme are ones that are nonlocal in NN or not Taylor-small in ss.

This is not an algebraic error; it is a domain-of-analyticity issue.

1. Raw local singularities: probably uniform

For the zero-part, the singularities in the ss-plane come from
s=±(γ−2m)±iβ,s=±(γ−2m)±i(1−β).
s=±(γ−2m)±iβ,s=±(γ−2m)±i(1−β).

min⁡ ⁣((2m−γ)2+β2, (2m−γ)2+(1−β)2).
min((2m−γ)2+β2
​, (2m−γ)2+(1−β)2
​).

min(β,1−β)≳log(∣γ∣+3)1​≍Q1​

at local height. So for every single zero contribution, the raw singularity distance is at least c/Qc/Q with an absolute c>0c>0.

That means the raw zero-part radius is already uniform on the Q−1Q−1 scale.

So the raw meromorphic geometry is not the place where uniformity should fail.
2. The real danger: the admissible midpoint class

The only plausible place uniformity can fail is if the admissible midpoint class allows local configurations where the renormalized object becomes ill-behaved even though the raw denominators do not.

In practice that can only happen through one of two mechanisms:
A. the local Gram block Gm(s)Gm​(s) loses nondegeneracy,

or
B. the boundary norm of the renormalized statistic becomes much larger than the raw local curvature scale.

We already have strong evidence against A:

    at s=0s=0,
    Gm(0)≍(Q∗∗Q3),
    Gm​(0)≍(Q∗​∗Q3​),

    so
    λmin⁡(Gm(0))≫Q,
    λmin​(Gm​(0))≫Q,

    and that gets better with height, not worse.

So A looks unlikely.

That leaves B.

So the uniform-radius question has now really become:
Can the renormalized boundary norm blow up relative to the raw L(m)S(m)2/Q3 scale on some admissible midpoint sequence?
Can the renormalized boundary norm blow up relative to the raw L(m)S(m)2/Q3 scale on some admissible midpoint sequence?​

That is the actual last risk.
3. A very concrete criterion for uniform ρ0ρ0​

There is a simple sufficient condition.

    λmin⁡(Gm(0))≥c0Q,
    λmin​(Gm​(0))≥c0​Q,

    and the variation on the microscopic disk satisfies
    sup⁡∣s∣≤ρ0/Q∥Gm(s)−Gm(0)∥≤12c0Q,
