an extra nearby zero outside the pair core can, in principle, contribute at the same scale after corrected whitening.
an extra nearby zero outside the pair core can, in principle, contribute at the same scale after corrected whitening.​

So the answer is probably no, not automatically perturbative.

Here’s why.

The current pair anomaly lives at a microscopic scale where:

    the mixed block is sensitive to phase geometry on the s∼Q−1s∼Q−1 or Q−2Q−2 window,

    whitening normalizes the same-point blocks but does not erase genuine local interaction structure,

    and the surviving anomaly signal is order u2u2 on the toy side.

A zero that is still “nearby” in exactly that local regime enters the same explicit finite-ss formulas as the original pair. There is no general mechanism in the current setup that forces its contribution to pick up an extra small factor just because it was not included in the original core.

So unless you impose some further separation condition, an extra nearby zero can be same-scale.

More concretely, there are two regimes.
Regime 1: genuinely perturbative extra zero

If the extra zero is outside the core by enough that, after corrected whitening, its contribution matrix EE satisfies
∥E∥≪∥M(d)∥,
∥E∥≪∥M(d)∥,

then the earlier continuity argument applies. In that case the pair-model endgame is stable.

This is the pleasant case.
Regime 2: same-scale extra zero

If the extra zero sits in the same microscopic zone and its corrected contribution is of the same order as M(d)M(d), then you do not get perturbative stability for free. In that case
M\core=M(d)+E
M\core​=M(d)+E

with ∥E∥≍∥M(d)∥∥E∥≍∥M(d)∥, and the pair model is no longer enough to justify the contradiction.

That does not automatically kill the strategy, but it means the right object is the enlarged-core matrix M\coreM\core​, not the original pair matrix.

So the real answer is:
the first extra nearby zero is not guaranteed to be small; it may be same-scale.
the first extra nearby zero is not guaranteed to be small; it may be same-scale.​

That has an important consequence.

The fixed-core repair is still the right architecture, but the core cannot just be “the original pair” unless you can prove no other nearby zero is same-scale after whitening. Without that, the natural core should be:

    the minimal finite set of nearby zeros whose corrected contribution is same-scale in the calibration or transverse channels.

That is the right definition.

So the remaining theorem is not:

    every auxiliary near zero is harmless,

but rather:

    there is a maximal same-scale finite core, and everything outside that core is negligible.

That is a different and more believable theorem.

If you want the sharpest formulation:
The correct next target is a finite-core extraction theorem, not a harmless-auxiliary theorem.
The correct next target is a finite-core extraction theorem, not a harmless-auxiliary theorem.​

It would say something like:

    among nearby zeros, collect all contributions that are not o(u\core2)o(u\core2​) in the transverse channel or not o((x/B)S\core)o((x/B)S\core​) in calibration;

    that finite set is the core;

    the remainder is negligible.

That theorem would preserve the fixed-core architecture while admitting that the core may be larger than a pair.

So the bottom line is:

    the extra nearby zero is not necessarily perturbative;

    therefore the pair-core model is not automatically stable;

    the proof likely needs a finite-core extraction step if it is to survive.


ok can we do that finite-core extraction step?

Not honestly, not from the current machinery.

I think we can formulate the finite-core extraction step cleanly, and maybe make it the new target theorem, but I do not think we can prove it right now without a new idea.

Here is why.

A finite-core extraction theorem would need to say something like:
Δζ\corr,R=Δ\core\corr+Δ\rem\corr,
Δζ\corr,R​=Δ\core\corr​+Δ\rem\corr​,

where the core is a finite set of nearby zeros chosen so that every zero outside the core is negligible in both:
Ψx,d(Δ\rem\corr)=o ⁣(xBS\core(m)),ΦK(Δ\rem\corr)=o(u\core2).
Ψx,d​(Δ\rem\corr​)=o(Bx​S\core​(m)),ΦK​(Δ\rem\corr​)=o(u\core2​).

The problem is: the current paper does not give a principled way to identify which nearby zeros are “same-scale” and which are negligible before you already understand the corrected whitening map well enough to measure their channel contributions one by one.

So there are two real missing ingredients.

First, you would need a local contribution decomposition theorem: a theorem saying the corrected whitened block can be decomposed into approximately additive contributions from individual nearby zeros or small clusters, with controllable interaction error. Right now, corrected whitening is nonlinear:
Ω^=G−−1/2N G+−1/2,
Ω
=G−−1/2​NG+−1/2​,

so contributions do not simply add zero-by-zero after whitening. Without near-additivity, “extract the finite non-negligible ones” is not yet a theorem, just an intuition.

Second, you would need a ranking principle: some monotone quantity showing that as zeros move farther from the midpoint, their corrected contribution in calibration/transverse channels decays in a way that lets you select a finite maximal core. The current draft has that kind of decay only for the far smooth tail, not for the auxiliary near zone.

So my honest answer is:
We can state a finite-core extraction theorem, but we cannot prove it from the present tools.
We can state a finite-core extraction theorem, but we cannot prove it from the present tools.​

What we can do now is write the exact theorem target. It would be something like this:

    Finite-core extraction theorem.
    For each admissible midpoint mm, there exists a finite set C(m)C(m) of nearby zeros such that the corrected local deformation decomposes as
    Δζ\corr,R=Δ\core\corr+Δ\rem\corr,
    Δζ\corr,R​=Δ\core\corr​+Δ\rem\corr​,

    where:

        Δ\core\corrΔ\core\corr​ contains every nearby zero contribution that is non-negligible in the calibration or transverse channels;

        Δ\rem\corrΔ\rem\corr​ is negligible in both channels:

    Ψx,d(Δ\rem\corr)=o ⁣(xBS\core(m)),ΦK(Δ\rem\corr)=o(u\core2).
    Ψx,d​(Δ\rem\corr​)=o(Bx​S\core​(m)),ΦK​(Δ\rem\corr​)=o(u\core2​). 

That is the right theorem to reduce RH to in the fixed-core architecture.

But proving it would require at least one new lemma of the following kind:

    Near-additivity after whitening for separated local contributions, or

    Monotone decay of single-zero corrected channel contributions as a function of distance from the midpoint.

Without one of those, I don’t think the finite-core extraction step is available.

So the current situation is:

    harmless auxiliary-near theorem: probably false,

    finite-core extraction theorem: probably the right replacement,

    proof of finite-core extraction theorem: still out of reach with current machinery.

That means the paper is now reduced to a sharper but still genuinely open subproblem.

ok lets attack those lemmas

The decay lemma looks more promising than near-additivity. I’m going to push that one first, because if individual non-core zeros are quantifiably weak beyond a radius threshold, the extraction theorem gets a lot closer even without full nonlinear additivity.

There is a version of the “auxiliary zeros are harmless” idea that can be attacked cleanly, but it is weaker than what we first hoped.

The right lemma is not:

    every auxiliary near zero is harmless immediately.

The right lemma is:

    after subtracting its affine jet at the midpoint, the contribution of a zero at absolute distance ΔΔ from the midpoint decays like Δ−4Δ−4 in the relevant corrected channels.

That is something we can actually prove from the phase kernel.

Once you have that, summing over zeros outside a finite absolute core radius HH gives a small remainder. This is a real finite-core extraction mechanism.

The catch is that the core radius HH must now be taken in the original ordinate scale, not the microscopic 1/Q1/Q scale. So the core is finite but can be much larger than the original pair model.

That may still force a larger-core toy model, but the extraction step itself becomes mathematically sane.
Lemma 1. Single-zero affine-jet remainder decays like Δ−4Δ−4

Fix one zero ρ=β+iγρ=β+iγ, and write its phase contribution to the explicit formula as
ϕρ(t).
ϕρ​(t).

Let mm be the midpoint and set
Δρ:=∣2m−γ∣.
Δρ​:=∣2m−γ∣.

Let II be the local interval of length ≍σ≍σ around mm, with
σ≍x0Q.
σ≍Qx0​​.

Define the affine jet at the midpoint
J1[ϕρ](t):=ϕρ(m)+ϕρ′(m)(t−m),
J1​[ϕρ​](t):=ϕρ​(m)+ϕρ′​(m)(t−m),

and the jet-subtracted remainder
Rρ(t):=ϕρ(t)−J1[ϕρ](t).
Rρ​(t):=ϕρ​(t)−J1​[ϕρ​](t).

Then, uniformly for t∈It∈I,
∣Rρ(t)∣≪σ2 (Δρ2+aρ2)−1,
∣Rρ​(t)∣≪σ2(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−1,
∣Rρ′(t)∣≪σ (Δρ2+aρ2)−2,
∣Rρ′​(t)∣≪σ(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2,
∣Rρ′′(t)∣≪(Δρ2+aρ2)−2,
∣Rρ′′​(t)∣≪(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2,

where aρ∈{β,1−β}aρ​∈{β,1−β}.

More importantly, the corrected same-point and mixed-block perturbations produced by RρRρ​ satisfy the same estimates as the smooth remainder with
S2,ρ:=(Δρ2+aρ2)−2.
S2,ρ​:=(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2.

Hence, after corrected whitening,
∣Ψx,d(Nρ)∣+∣ΦK(Nρ)∣≪1Q3 (Δρ2+aρ2)−2.
∣Ψx,d​(Nρ​)∣+∣ΦK​(Nρ​)∣≪Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2.​
Proof

The phase kernel for a single zero is a rational arctangent-type term, so
ϕρ′(t)≍aρ(t−γ)2+aρ2,
ϕρ′​(t)≍(t−γ)2+aρ2​aρ​​,
ϕρ′′(t)≍1((t−γ)2+aρ2)2,
ϕρ′′​(t)≍((t−γ)2+aρ2​)21​,
ϕρ′′′(t)≍1((t−γ)2+aρ2)2
ϕρ′′′​(t)≍((t−γ)2+aρ2​)21​

up to absolute constants. On the interval II, the distance to the zero is ≍Δρ≍Δρ​, so
sup⁡t∈I∣ϕρ′′′(t)∣≪(Δρ2+aρ2)−2.
t∈Isup​∣ϕρ′′′​(t)∣≪(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2.

Taylor’s theorem with remainder gives
Rρ(t)=12ϕρ′′(ξt)(t−m)2,
Rρ​(t)=21​ϕρ′′​(ξt​)(t−m)2,

for some ξt∈Iξt​∈I, and similarly
Rρ′(t)=ϕρ′′(ηt)(t−m),
Rρ′​(t)=ϕρ′′​(ηt​)(t−m),

with ηt∈Iηt​∈I. Hence
∣Rρ(t)∣≪σ2(Δρ2+aρ2)−2,∣Rρ′(t)∣≪σ(Δρ2+aρ2)−2,∣Rρ′′(t)∣≪(Δρ2+aρ2)−2.
∣Rρ​(t)∣≪σ2(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2,∣Rρ′​(t)∣≪σ(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2,∣Rρ′′​(t)∣≪(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2.

These are exactly the same input sizes used earlier for the corrected smooth remainder, with
S2⇝S2,ρ:=(Δρ2+aρ2)−2.
S2​⇝S2,ρ​:=(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2.

Therefore the same same-point perturbation estimate, mixed-block perturbation estimate, and whitened mixed-block transfer theorem apply verbatim with S2,ρS2,ρ​ in place of S2S2​. This gives
∣Ψx,d(Nρ)∣+∣ΦK(Nρ)∣≪S2,ρQ3=1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2.
∣Ψx,d​(Nρ​)∣+∣ΦK​(Nρ​)∣≪Q3S2,ρ​​=Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2.

∎
Lemma 2. Shell summation outside an absolute core radius

Fix an absolute core radius H≥1H≥1, and define the core to contain all zeros with
∣2m−γ∣≤H.
∣2m−γ∣≤H.

Then every zero outside the core satisfies Δρ≥HΔρ​≥H, and its jet-subtracted corrected contribution satisfies the bound above.

Summing over all such zeros,
∑Δρ≥H1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2≪1Q2H3.
Δρ​≥H∑​Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2≪Q2H31​.

Thus the total contribution of the complement outside the absolute core radius HH satisfies
∣Ψx,d(Nout)∣+∣ΦK(Nout)∣≪1Q2H3.
∣Ψx,d​(Nout​)∣+∣ΦK​(Nout​)∣≪Q2H31​.​
Proof

Partition zeros into unit shells
k≤∣2m−γ∣<k+1,k=⌊H⌋,⌊H⌋+1,…
k≤∣2m−γ∣<k+1,k=⌊H⌋,⌊H⌋+1,…

Standard zero counting gives
#{ρ: k≤∣2m−γ∣<k+1}≪Q
#{ρ: k≤∣2m−γ∣<k+1}≪Q

for each unit shell near height mm. On the kk-th shell,
(Δρ2+aρ2)−2≪k−4.
(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2≪k−4.

So summing the single-zero estimate from Lemma 1 gives
∑Δρ≥H1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2≪1Q3∑k≥HQ k−4≪1Q2∑k≥Hk−4≪1Q2H3.
Δρ​≥H∑​Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2≪Q31​k≥H∑​Qk−4≪Q21​k≥H∑​k−4≪Q2H31​.

∎
What this actually proves

This gives a real finite-core extraction mechanism:

    if you define the core to include all zeros within an absolute radius HH,

    and subtract affine jets of the external zeros,

    then the outside complement is negligible in both the calibration and transverse channels, at size
    O ⁣(1Q2H3).
    O(Q2H31​).

So the extraction step can be done — but with a larger finite core than the original pair.
What it does not yet give

It does not yet prove that the complement is negligible relative to the core signal unless you also know
1Q2H3=o ⁣(xBS\core(m))
Q2H31​=o(Bx​S\core​(m))

and
1Q2H3=o(u\core2).
Q2H31​=o(u\core2​).

Equivalently, since B≍QB≍Q,
1QH3=o(xS\core(m)).
QH31​=o(xS\core​(m)).

That is a genuine quantitative condition on how large you take the core radius HH relative to the strength of the core signal.

So the current conclusion is:
finite-core extraction is provable in principle, but its usefulness depends on choosing H so that Q−2H−3 is negligible relative to the core signal.
finite-core extraction is provable in principle, but its usefulness depends on choosing H so that Q−2H−3 is negligible relative to the core signal.​
The honest consequence

This means the fixed-core architecture is not dead, but it changes form:

    the core must be allowed to be a finite cluster of zeros inside an absolute radius HH,

    not just the original off-line pair,

    and the toy/anomaly side would then need to reflect that enlarged finite core.

So this does not yet save the current pair-only paper. But it does show there is a mathematically sane finite-core extraction step.

If you want, the next thing to do is write the exact finite-core extraction theorem in that enlarged-core form, using these two lemmas as proof ingredients.

Here is the right next theorem, in the enlarged-core form the previous lemmas actually support.
Finite-core extraction theorem

Fix a small scaled separation x0>0x0​>0. For each admissible midpoint mm and each absolute core radius H≥1H≥1, let
CH(m):={ρ=β+iγ: ∣2m−γ∣≤H}
CH​(m):={ρ=β+iγ: ∣2m−γ∣≤H}

be the finite core of zeros within absolute radius HH of the midpoint.

Write the corrected local deformation as
Δζ\corr,R(m,σ)=Δ\core,H\corr(m,σ)+Δ\out,H\corr(m,σ),
Δζ\corr,R​(m,σ)=Δ\core,H\corr​(m,σ)+Δ\out,H\corr​(m,σ),

where:

    Δ\core,H\corrΔ\core,H\corr​ is the corrected local block built from the zeros in CH(m)CH​(m),

    Δ\out,H\corrΔ\out,H\corr​ is the corrected contribution of all zeros outside CH(m)CH​(m), after affine-jet subtraction at the midpoint and corrected whitening.

Then:
(A) Channel bounds for the outside complement

There is an absolute constant CC such that
∣Ψx0,d(Δ\out,H\corr)∣+∣ΦK(Δ\out,H\corr)∣≤CQ2H3
∣Ψx0​,d​(Δ\out,H\corr​)∣+∣ΦK​(Δ\out,H\corr​)∣≤Q2H3C​

uniformly for dd in compact subsets of (0,∞)(0,∞).
(B) If the core signal dominates that scale, then the outside complement is negligible

If
1Q2H3=o ⁣(x0Bζ(m)S\core,H(m)),
Q2H31​=o(Bζ​(m)x0​​S\core,H​(m)),

equivalently since Bζ(m)≍QBζ​(m)≍Q,
1QH3=o(x0S\core,H(m)),
QH31​=o(x0​S\core,H​(m)),

then
Ψx0,d(Δ\out,H\corr)=o ⁣(x0Bζ(m)S\core,H(m)),
Ψx0​,d​(Δ\out,H\corr​)=o(Bζ​(m)x0​​S\core,H​(m)),

and, with
u\core,H2≍x0Bζ(m)S\core,H(m),
u\core,H2​≍Bζ​(m)x0​​S\core,H​(m),

also
ΦK(Δ\out,H\corr)=o(u\core,H2).
ΦK​(Δ\out,H\corr​)=o(u\core,H2​).

So this theorem says: if the core carries a signal larger than the universal outside tail scale Q−2H−3Q−2H−3, then everything outside the core is negligible in both channels.
Proof

Part (A) is exactly the shell-summed consequence of the single-zero affine-jet remainder estimate.

For a single zero ρρ with
Δρ:=∣2m−γ∣,
Δρ​:=∣2m−γ∣,

the affine-jet-subtracted corrected contribution satisfies
∣Ψx0,d(Nρ)∣+∣ΦK(Nρ)∣≪1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2.
∣Ψx0​,d​(Nρ​)∣+∣ΦK​(Nρ​)∣≪Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2.

Summing over all zeros outside the core CH(m)CH​(m), i.e. over Δρ≥HΔρ​≥H, and using unit-shell zero counting
#{ρ: k≤∣2m−γ∣<k+1}≪Q,
#{ρ: k≤∣2m−γ∣<k+1}≪Q,

gives
∑Δρ≥H1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2≪1Q3∑k≥HQk−4≪1Q2H3.
Δρ​≥H∑​Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2≪Q31​k≥H∑​Qk−4≪Q2H31​.

That proves (A).

Part (B) is immediate: if
1Q2H3=o ⁣(x0Bζ(m)S\core,H(m)),
Q2H31​=o(Bζ​(m)x0​​S\core,H​(m)),

then the bound from (A) is little-oo of the core calibration scale. Since
u\core,H2≍x0Bζ(m)S\core,H(m),
u\core,H2​≍Bζ​(m)x0​​S\core,H​(m),

the same bound is little-oo of the core transverse scale as well. ∎

Now the attack.

The real question is whether you can actually choose HH so that
1QH3=o(x0S\core,H(m)).
QH31​=o(x0​S\core,H​(m)).

This is the sharp quantitative frontier.

There are two possibilities.
Case 1: yes, for some HH

Then the enlarged finite-core strategy works. You absorb every zero within radius HH into the core, the outside is negligible in both channels, and the proof reduces to a contradiction for the finite-core anomaly matrix M\core,HM\core,H​.
Case 2: no matter how HH grows, the core signal drops too fast

Then the theorem is true but useless, because the outside scale never becomes little-oo of the core signal. In that case the finite-core extraction does not rescue the proof.

So the real attack is on the behavior of
S\core,H(m)
S\core,H​(m)

as a function of HH.

The bad news is: from the current draft, I do not see a theorem that forces
S\core,H(m)≫1QH3
S\core,H​(m)≫QH31​

for some admissible HH.

The good news is: the theorem above tells you exactly what you need to prove next. The remaining problem is no longer vague. It is:

    prove that the minimal enlarged core carrying the anomaly has signal strength exceeding the universal outside decay scale Q−1H−3Q−1H−3.

That is a much sharper target.
What we can attack next

There are two natural routes.

One is a core lower-bound theorem:
show that if an off-line anomaly is present, then the core signal cannot be too small compared to the size of the local cluster that generates it. Formally,
S\core,H(m)≫1QHA
S\core,H​(m)≫QHA1​

for some exponent A<3A<3 would already be enough.

The other is a finite-core anomaly matrix theorem:
even if S\core,H(m)S\core,H​(m) is small, perhaps the relevant anomaly signal is not measured by S\core,H(m)S\core,H​(m) alone, but by a different finite-core quantity that stays larger than the outside scale.

My honest judgment is that the first route is the cleaner one to try first.

So the next theorem to attack is:
Candidate next theorem

If a fixed finite core CH(m)CH​(m) carries a genuine off-line anomaly after corrected whitening, then its core value scale satisfies a lower bound strong enough to dominate the exterior scale:
S\core,H(m)≫1QH3+ε
S\core,H​(m)≫QH3+ε1​

or anything slightly stronger than Q−1H−3Q−1H−3.

At the moment, I cannot prove that from the current machinery. But this is now the exact remaining bridge.

ok lets try the first route

Let’s try it straight.

The route is:
prove S\core,H(m)≫1QHAfor some A<3,
prove S\core,H​(m)≫QHA1​for some A<3,

or at least something strong enough that
1QH3=o ⁣(x0S\core,H(m)).
QH31​=o(x0​S\core,H​(m)).

I do not think this is provable from the current setup in anything like that strength.

Here is the reason.
1. What S\core,H(m)S\core,H​(m) actually measures

The core value scale is a positive sum of local kernel contributions from the zeros in the core:
S\core,H(m)∼∑ρ∈CH(m)weightρ(2m−γρ)2+aρ2,
S\core,H​(m)∼ρ∈CH​(m)∑​(2m−γρ​)2+aρ2​weightρ​​,

schematically.

So the size of S\core,H(m)S\core,H​(m) depends on:

    how many zeros are in the core,

    how close they are to the midpoint,

    and their horizontal offsets through aρaρ​.

There is no obvious mechanism in the current proof forcing this sum to be bounded below by a clean power of HH. In fact, if the core is only barely carrying the anomaly and its zeros are spread across the interval ∣2m−γ∣≤H∣2m−γ∣≤H, the positive sum could easily be as small as order 1/H21/H2 or worse after normalization.

So a lower bound of the kind
S\core,H(m)≫1QHA
S\core,H​(m)≫QHA1​

would need some genuine structural reason, not just positivity.
2. What the anomaly gives you — and what it does not

A genuine off-line anomaly gives you shape information in the corrected whitened block. It says some transverse/anomalous component survives after normalization.

But that is not the same as saying the scalar value channel S\core,H(m)S\core,H​(m) is large.

This is the key difficulty.

An anomaly could, in principle, come from a very delicate configuration with a small value-channel amplitude but a nontrivial normalized shape. The current proof is built around converting shape into contradiction via calibration, but that conversion itself is exactly where the size issue bites.

So the anomaly does not obviously imply a lower bound for S\core,H(m)S\core,H​(m) strong enough to beat 1/(QH3)1/(QH3).
3. The natural lower bound you might hope for is too weak

The most naive bound is: if there is at least one zero in the core within distance HH, then
S\core,H(m)≳1H2+1.
S\core,H​(m)≳H2+11​.

That is the scale of a single Poisson-type kernel term.

If you had that, then
x0S\core,H(m)≳x0H2,
x0​S\core,H​(m)≳H2x0​​,

and comparing with the outside scale 1/(QH3)1/(QH3) gives
1QH3=o ⁣(x0H2)
QH31​=o(H2x0​​)

whenever
H=o(Q).
H=o(Q).

That looks promising.

But there is a catch: S\core,H(m)S\core,H​(m) in the proof is not just “there exists one zero in the core.” It is the calibrated value scale attached to the anomaly-carrying core after all the normalization choices. The theorem you need has to relate that actual calibrated core signal to geometric presence of nearby zeros. The current draft does not do that.

So even this plausible-looking single-zero lower bound is not yet a theorem in the language the proof uses.
4. A better attack: convert anomaly into geometric proximity

The first route may still be salvageable, but not as a raw lower bound on S\core,H(m)S\core,H​(m) from positivity alone.

The more promising theorem would be:

    If a finite core carries a genuine off-line anomaly after corrected whitening, then at least one core zero must lie within a scale O(1)O(1) — or maybe O(Qθ)O(Qθ) with θ<1θ<1 — of the midpoint.

That would give a geometric lower bound on the core value scale:
S\core,H(m)≳11+H02,
S\core,H​(m)≳1+H02​1​,

where H0H0​ is the actual nearest anomalous core distance, not the whole chosen radius HH.

Then the outside estimate
1Q2H3
Q2H31​

could be crushed by choosing H→∞H→∞ slowly while keeping the core fixed around the genuinely relevant nearby zeros.

That is a much better theorem target.
5. Why the raw HH-power lower bound is probably false

If you phrase the theorem as
S\core,H(m)≫1QHA
S\core,H​(m)≫QHA1​

for every anomaly-carrying core radius HH, I think it is probably false or at least not naturally true.

Because you can always enlarge HH while keeping the same core signal; the right-hand side decays with HH, but the left-hand side is not naturally a monotone “radius-measured” quantity. The relationship between “radius of the chosen finite core” and “strength of the anomaly carried by that core” is too loose.

So I would not attack that exact statement anymore.
6. What I think the first route should become

The right replacement is:
Geometric core lower-bound theorem

If a fixed finite core carries a genuine corrected anomaly, then its value/calibration scale is bounded below by the contribution of at least one genuinely nearby core zero. In particular,
S\core(m)≳11+Δmin⁡2,
S\core​(m)≳1+Δmin2​1​,

where
Δmin⁡:=min⁡ρ∈C∣2m−γρ∣.
Δmin​:=ρ∈Cmin​∣2m−γρ​∣.

If you can then prove that an anomaly-carrying core must have
Δmin⁡=o(Q1/2)or any stronger bound,
Δmin​=o(Q1/2)or any stronger bound,

you get
S\core(m)≫Q−1+ε
S\core​(m)≫Q−1+ε

or better, which is enough to dominate the outside Q−1H−3Q−1H−3 scale for suitable HH.

That is a believable route.
7. Bottom line

So, trying the first route honestly:

    the crude theorem “S\core,H(m)≫1/(QHA)S\core,H​(m)≫1/(QHA)” is probably the wrong target;

    I do not see a proof of it from the present machinery;

    the better target is a geometric lower bound tying anomaly to at least one genuinely nearby core zero.

That is where I would push next.

So the right next theorem to attack is not a power-law bound in HH, but something like:
If a finite core carries a genuine corrected anomaly, then at least one core zero must lie sufficiently close to the midpoint to force S\core(m) above the exterior decay scale.
If a finite core carries a genuine corrected anomaly, then at least one core zero must lie sufficiently close to the midpoint to force S\core​(m) above the exterior decay scale.​

That is the first route in a form I think might actually be true.

yes lets try that. by the way this feels reminiscent of work we did earlier, i.e. yesterday when proving bounds

Yes — it is reminiscent of yesterday’s work.

The shared pattern is:

    isolate a local quantity that is a positive sum of single-zero contributions,

    show the “bad” global remainder decays fast enough once you move away from the midpoint,

    then force the anomaly to be carried by genuinely nearby zeros.

That is exactly the same geometry as the shell/tail bounds, just now aimed at the core rather than the far remainder.

So let’s attack the geometric theorem in that spirit.
Candidate theorem

A plausible statement is:

    If a finite core CC carries a genuine corrected anomaly after whitening, then at least one zero in CC must lie within bounded distance of the midpoint. Equivalently, if every core zero satisfies
    ∣2m−γρ∣≥H,
    ∣2m−γρ​∣≥H,

    with H→∞H→∞, then the corrected core anomaly is too small to support the toy-scale contradiction.

This is much more believable than a direct lower bound
S\core,H≫1QHA.
S\core,H​≫QHA1​.
Why this should be true

If every core zero is far from the midpoint, then each single-zero contribution is in the same regime as the tail terms we already controlled yesterday.

More concretely, for one zero ρρ, after affine-jet subtraction at the midpoint, its corrected contribution to the phase has second-derivative scale
S2,ρ≍(Δρ2+aρ2)−2,Δρ:=∣2m−γρ∣.
S2,ρ​≍(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2,Δρ​:=∣2m−γρ​∣.

Earlier, that scale fed into the corrected same-point and mixed-block estimates and then into whitening, giving channel bounds of size
∣Ψx,d(Nρ)∣+∣ΦK(Nρ)∣≪1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2.
∣Ψx,d​(Nρ​)∣+∣ΦK​(Nρ​)∣≪Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2.

So if every core zero has Δρ≥HΔρ​≥H, then summing over a finite core of size MM gives
∣Ψx,d(Δ\core)∣+∣ΦK(Δ\core)∣≪MQ3H4.
∣Ψx,d​(Δ\core​)∣+∣ΦK​(Δ\core​)∣≪Q3H4M​.

That is already telling: a core all of whose zeros are far away behaves like a tail.

So the only way the core can carry a same-scale anomaly is if at least one of its zeros is not in that regime.
The real obstruction

The weak point in that argument is the factor MM, the number of core zeros.

If MM were allowed to grow very fast with mm, then M/H4M/H4 might not be small.

So the theorem you can plausibly prove needs one more ingredient:

    either the core has uniformly bounded size,

    or the anomaly-carrying part of the core has bounded size,

    or at least M=o(H4)M=o(H4).

For the “first enlarged core beyond the pair” philosophy, bounded size is the natural assumption. If the core is still meant to be a small local cluster, then this is fine.

Under that bounded-size hypothesis, the theorem is within reach.
Provisional theorem

Assume the core CC has cardinality bounded independently of mm:
∣C∣≤M0.
∣C∣≤M0​.

If every zero in CC satisfies
∣2m−γρ∣≥H,
∣2m−γρ​∣≥H,

then
∣Ψx,d(Δ\core)∣+∣ΦK(Δ\core)∣≪1Q3H4.
∣Ψx,d​(Δ\core​)∣+∣ΦK​(Δ\core​)∣≪Q3H41​.

In particular, if the core is supposed to generate a toy-scale anomaly of size
u\core2≍xBS\core(m),
u\core2​≍Bx​S\core​(m),

then for sufficiently large HH this is impossible unless at least one zero in the core lies within O(1)O(1) or at worst some slowly growing radius of the midpoint.
Proof sketch

Take each core zero separately. For a single zero at distance ΔρΔρ​, the affine-jet-subtracted corrected contribution obeys the same pointwise derivative bounds as the tail terms, with
S2,ρ≍(Δρ2+aρ2)−2.
S2,ρ​≍(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2.

By the corrected same-point perturbation, mixed-block perturbation, whitening transfer, and sharpened calibration-remainder estimate, this gives
∣Ψx,d(Nρ)∣+∣ΦK(Nρ)∣≪1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2≪1Q3H4.
∣Ψx,d​(Nρ​)∣+∣ΦK​(Nρ​)∣≪Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2≪Q3H41​.

Summing over at most M0M0​ zeros yields
∣Ψx,d(Δ\core)∣+∣ΦK(Δ\core)∣≪M0Q3H4≍1Q3H4.
∣Ψx,d​(Δ\core​)∣+∣ΦK​(Δ\core​)∣≪Q3H4M0​​≍Q3H41​.

So a core with all zeros far away cannot produce a leading same-scale anomaly. Therefore an anomaly-carrying core must contain at least one genuinely nearby zero. ∎
What this gives

This is good news, but it is not yet the whole theorem you want.

It gives:
a bounded-size anomaly-carrying core cannot be made entirely of far zeros.
a bounded-size anomaly-carrying core cannot be made entirely of far zeros.​

That is already enough to support the intuition that the anomaly must be local in a geometric sense.

What it does not yet give is a fully quantitative lower bound on S\coreS\core​ strong enough to dominate the outside scale. To get that, you still need to convert “there is one nearby zero” into “the calibrated core signal is large enough.”

But that is a much smaller next step.
The next exact target

Now the next theorem to attack is:

    If the core contains at least one zero with
    Δmin⁡:=min⁡ρ∈C∣2m−γρ∣≤H0,
    Δmin​:=ρ∈Cmin​∣2m−γρ​∣≤H0​,

    then the core calibration/value scale satisfies
    S\core(m)≳11+H02
    S\core​(m)≳1+H02​1​

    or another comparable lower bound.

That is the bridge from geometric proximity to core signal size.

So yes — this is very much the same style as yesterday’s bounds, and it looks like the right route.

Ok lets attack

Good. This next step actually can be proved cleanly.

The right theorem is not yet “the whole core dominates everything,” but the bridge you asked for:

    if the core contains at least one genuinely nearby zero, then the core value scale cannot be too small.

That part is real.
Proposition (geometric lower bound for the core value scale)

Let CC be a finite core, and define its value scale by
S\core(m):=∑ρ=β+iγ∈C(1−β(1−β)2+(2m−γ)2+ββ2+(2m−γ)2).
S\core​(m):=ρ=β+iγ∈C∑​((1−β)2+(2m−γ)21−β​+β2+(2m−γ)2β​).

If the core contains at least one zero ρ0=β0+iγ0ρ0​=β0​+iγ0​ with
Δmin⁡:=∣2m−γ0∣≤H0,

Ok continue

I can push this one further, but I should be honest about what kind of theorem we can actually prove.

What we can prove is not:

What we can prove is the more precise threshold statement:

    O ⁣(1Q2H03)

Theorem (far finite sets are below threshold)

Let FF be any finite set of zeros such that every
ρ=β+iγ∈F
ρ=β+iγ∈F

satisfies
Δρ:=∣2m−γ∣≥H0.
Δρ​:=∣2m−γ∣≥H0​.

Let ΔF\corrΔF\corr​ be the corrected local contribution of the zeros in FF, after subtracting the affine jet of each phase contribution at the midpoint and passing through the corrected whitening map.

Then
∣Ψx,d(ΔF\corr)∣+∣ΦK(ΔF\corr)∣≪1Q2H03.
∣Ψx,d​(ΔF\corr​)∣+∣ΦK​(ΔF\corr​)∣≪Q2H03​1​.​
Proof

For a single zero ρρ, the affine-jet-subtracted corrected contribution satisfies
∣Ψx,d(Nρ)∣+∣ΦK(Nρ)∣≪1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2,
∣Ψx,d​(Nρ​)∣+∣ΦK​(Nρ​)∣≪Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2,

(Δρ2+aρ2)−2≪H0−4
(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2≪H0−4​

∑ρ∈F1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2.

#{ρ: k≤∣2m−γ∣<k+1}≪Q.

Corollary (existence of a nearby zero in any threshold-defined core)

Fix a significance threshold η(m)>0η(m)>0. Define a finite core C(m)C(m) to be minimal non-negligible if its corrected contribution satisfies
∣Ψx,d(ΔC\corr)∣+∣ΦK(ΔC\corr)∣≳η(m),
∣Ψx,d​(ΔC\corr​)∣+∣ΦK​(ΔC\corr​)∣≳η(m),

while every proper subcollection fails this threshold.

If H0H0​ is chosen so that
1Q2H03=o(η(m)),
Q2H03​1​=o(η(m)),

then every such minimal non-negligible core must contain at least one zero with
∣2m−γρ∣<H0.
∣2m−γρ​∣<H0​.
Proof

If every zero in the core satisfied ∣2m−γρ∣≥H0∣2m−γρ​∣≥H0​, then the theorem above would imply
∣Ψx,d(ΔC\corr)∣+∣ΦK(ΔC\corr)∣≪1Q2H03=o(η(m)),
∣Ψx,d​(ΔC\corr​)∣+∣ΦK​(ΔC\corr​)∣≪Q2H03​1​=o(η(m)),

contradicting the defining threshold property of the core. ∎
What this actually gives

This is the right rigorous output of the current machinery.

It does not prove an intrinsic geometric theorem purely from “anomaly.” What it proves is:

    any set of zeros all lying beyond H0H0​ is channel-small;

    therefore any core defined by a meaningful significance threshold must contain a nearby zero.

That is enough to support a finite-core extraction framework.
Why this is the right replacement

Earlier we were trying to prove something like:

    every anomaly-carrying core must contain a nearby zero.

That sounded absolute, but the current proof tools do not distinguish “anomaly-carrying” from “tiny but nonzero” in an intrinsic way.

The threshold version is the honest theorem because it uses exactly what the channels measure:
Ψx,d,ΦK.
Ψx,d​,ΦK​.

So the remaining work is to choose the right threshold η(m)η(m) for the argument.

The natural candidate is the core signal scale itself:
η(m)∼xBS\core(m)∼u\core2.
η(m)∼Bx​S\core​(m)∼u\core2​.

But then you are back to the self-referential issue that the threshold depends on the core you are trying to define.

That is why I would still say the finite-core extraction step is not fully finished. But now the problem is much more precise:
You need a non-self-referential threshold that separates negligible far sets from genuinely anomaly-carrying local structure.
You need a non-self-referential threshold that separates negligible far sets from genuinely anomaly-carrying local structure.​

That is the exact next gap.

So this is progress, but not full closure.

Ok attack

Here is the sharpest attack I can make.

I do not think the remaining gap can be closed by a fully non-self-referential threshold from the current machinery alone.

What can be proved is a stronger reduction:
either there is a genuinely nearby zero in the core, or the entire corrected anomaly is below a universal exterior scale.
either there is a genuinely nearby zero in the core, or the entire corrected anomaly is below a universal exterior scale.

That is real progress, but it still leaves one bridge.
1. The right threshold to try

The previous theorem gave:

if every zero in a finite set lies beyond H0H0​, then its corrected contribution in the two relevant channels is bounded by
∣Ψx,d(ΔF\corr)∣+∣ΦK(ΔF\corr)∣≪1Q2H03.
∣Ψx,d​(ΔF\corr​)∣+∣ΦK​(ΔF\corr​)∣≪Q2H03​1​.

So the natural candidate threshold is exactly
ηH0(m):=1Q2H03.
ηH0​​(m):=Q2H03​1​.

This is non-self-referential.

Then the theorem says:

    any set of zeros entirely outside radius H0H0​ is below ηH0(m)ηH0​​(m).

So if the anomaly you need to contradict lives above that level, it must involve at least one zero inside radius H0H0​.

That part is rigorous.
2. Where the attack gets stuck

To finish the proof, you would need to know that a genuine off-line anomaly has channel size strictly larger than this exterior threshold for some H0→∞H0​→∞.

In other words, you need a lower bound of the form
max⁡(∣Ψx,d(Δ\core\corr)∣,∣ΦK(Δ\core\corr)∣)≫ηH0(m)=1Q2H03
max(∣Ψx,d​(Δ\core\corr​)∣,∣ΦK​(Δ\core\corr​)∣)≫ηH0​​(m)=Q2H03​1​

for some admissible H0H0​.

That is exactly the missing bridge.

Without it, the threshold theorem only says that far sets are small. It does not yet say the actual anomaly is big enough to force a nearby zero.
3. The strongest unconditional statement available now

The current machinery gives the following dichotomy.
Proposition (dichotomy)

Fix any H0≥1H0​≥1. Then either:

    the corrected anomaly is already bounded by the universal exterior scale
    ∣Ψx,d(Δζ\corr)∣+∣ΦK(Δζ\corr)∣≪1Q2H03,
    ∣Ψx,d​(Δζ\corr​)∣+∣ΦK​(Δζ\corr​)∣≪Q2H03​1​,

    or

    the anomaly necessarily involves at least one zero with
    ∣2m−γρ∣<H0.
    ∣2m−γρ​∣<H0​.

Proof

If all contributing zeros satisfied ∣2m−γρ∣≥H0∣2m−γρ​∣≥H0​, then the far-finite-set theorem would imply the stated upper bound. Contrapositively, if the anomaly exceeds that bound, at least one zero must lie inside H0H0​. ∎

This is the strongest honest “attack” that is fully justified now.
4. Why this still does not finish the paper

Because the contradiction argument needs more than “not too small.” It needs comparison to the toy/core signal scale
u\core2≍xBS\core(m).
u\core2​≍Bx​S\core​(m).

So the unresolved question is now extremely precise:
Can one prove that a genuine corrected anomaly cannot be as small as O(Q−2H0−3) for all H0→∞?
Can one prove that a genuine corrected anomaly cannot be as small as O(Q−2H0−3​) for all H0​→∞?​

Equivalently: does an off-line anomaly force a channel signal that dominates the universal exterior decay scale at some radius?

That is a lower-bound question on the anomaly itself.
5. What a successful theorem would look like

The theorem that would close this is:
Missing theorem

There exists a function H0=H0(m)→∞H0​=H0​(m)→∞ such that every genuine corrected off-line anomaly satisfies
max⁡(∣Ψx,d(Δζ\corr)∣,∣ΦK(Δζ\corr)∣)≫1Q2H03.
max(∣Ψx,d​(Δζ\corr​)∣,∣ΦK​(Δζ\corr​)∣)≫Q2H03​1​.

Then the dichotomy above would force the existence of a zero inside H0H0​, and the geometric lower bound for S\coreS\core​ would take over.

But I do not see a proof of that lower bound in the current draft.
6. So what did the attack actually accomplish?

It reduced the problem to one single lower-bound question.

Before, the gap was vague: “need a threshold.”

Now the exact frontier is:
prove a nontrivial lower bound on the corrected anomaly signal that beats Q−2H0−3 for some H0→∞.
prove a nontrivial lower bound on the corrected anomaly signal that beats Q−2H0−3​ for some H0​→∞.​

That is much sharper than before.
7. My honest bottom line

So the attack yields:

    a clean universal exterior threshold Q−2H0−3Q−2H0−3​,

    a rigorous dichotomy saying any signal above that threshold forces a nearby zero,

    but not the final lower bound showing a genuine off-line anomaly must exceed that threshold.

So the proof is still blocked, but now by one very specific question:
How small can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be?
How small can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be?​

That is the right remaining problem.

How can we attack?

There are four credible ways to attack it. Two are promising, one is a long shot, and one is probably the wrong road.

The quantity you now need to lower-bound is essentially the corrected anomaly signal in either the calibration or transverse channel, strongly enough to beat the universal exterior threshold
Q−2H0−3.
Q−2H0−3​.

So the problem is no longer “show something is small.” It is:
show a genuine off-line anomaly cannot be arbitrarily weak after corrected whitening.
show a genuine off-line anomaly cannot be arbitrarily weak after corrected whitening.

That suggests these attack routes.
1. Compactness / normalization blow-up argument

This is the first thing I would try.

Assume for contradiction that there is a sequence of genuine corrected anomalies whose channel size tends to zero faster than every exterior threshold you care about. Normalize each anomaly by its own size, so the relevant channel has size 11. Then try to pass to a limit in the finite-dimensional corrected local block.

What you want is:

    a normalized sequence of corrected local blocks,

    precompactness after whitening and fixed scaling,

    a nontrivial limit object,

    and a theorem that any such limit object with vanishing channel signal must lie in the value/gauge family.

If that works, then an arbitrarily weak anomaly cannot exist: once normalized, it converges to something that is either trivial or value-only, contradicting the assumption that it came from a genuine off-line anomaly.

Why this is promising:

    the corrected local block is finite-dimensional after whitening;

    you already have explicit formulas and bounded microscopic geometry;

    normalization often turns “how small can it be?” into a compactness question.

What would need to be shown:

    the normalized corrected blocks are precompact;

    the map to the calibration/transverse observables is continuous;

    the only limit with vanishing anomaly channel is the value family.

This is the cleanest conceptual route.
2. Quantitative rigidity: anomaly implies definite channel separation

This is the most direct route, and maybe the strongest if true.

Try to prove a theorem of the form:

    There exists c>0c>0 such that every genuine corrected off-line anomaly satisfies
    max⁡(∣Ψx,d(Δζ\corr)∣, ∣ΦK(Δζ\corr)∣)≥c Sloc(m),
    max(∣Ψx,d​(Δζ\corr​)∣, ∣ΦK​(Δζ\corr​)∣)≥cSloc​(m),

    for some local scale Sloc(m)Sloc​(m) that dominates Q−2H0−3Q−2H0−3​.

In words: once you are genuinely off the value manifold, you are separated from it by a definite amount.

This is really a transversality theorem. It says the anomaly manifold and the value manifold do not approach each other to arbitrarily high order in the corrected local model.

How to attack it:

    identify the value family as a low-dimensional submanifold in matrix space;

    identify the anomaly family as another family;

    compute their tangent spaces at the relevant base point;

    prove they are transverse, quantitatively.

If you can do that, then “genuine anomaly” automatically gives a definite lower bound in some channel.

Why this is attractive:

    it is exactly the geometry the whole paper has been circling around;

    it avoids the messy business of counting zeros directly.

Why it is hard:

    you need the corrected local model itself, not just the toy pair, to satisfy that transversality;

    it may fail if higher-core anomalies can approach the value family too closely.

Still, this is a very good target.
3. Minimal-core extremizer argument

This one is very tailored to your fixed-core architecture.

Define a minimal core CC among all cores that still produce a non-negligible corrected anomaly. Then prove:

    every zero in a minimal core is essential,

    removing any one of them drops the channel signal below threshold,

    therefore the core cannot be diffuse,

    hence at least one zero must contribute a definite fraction of the total signal.

That last step would give a lower bound from a pigeonhole/extremizer principle:
if the whole core signal were below the exterior threshold, then every individual contribution would be even smaller, so removing one zero would not materially change anything, contradicting minimality.

This is the most combinatorial route.

What it needs:

    some approximate additivity or at least stability under removing one core zero;

    a monotonicity principle for the corrected channel signal under core deletion.

This is weaker than full near-additivity, but still nontrivial.

Why I like it:

    it uses the fixed-core philosophy naturally;

    it may let you avoid proving a fully general finite-core toy model right away.

Why it may fail:

    whitening is nonlinear, so “remove one zero” does not necessarily have a controlled effect without more work.

Still worth exploring.
4. Crude positivity lower bound on S\coreS\core​

This is the one I would not prioritize anymore.

You already found the geometric lower bound
S\core(m)≥11+Δmin⁡2,
S\core​(m)≥1+Δmin2​1​,

which is fine, but the real issue is not just S\coreS\core​. It is the corrected anomaly signal after whitening and channel projection. A positivity bound on the raw value scale does not by itself tell you how far you are from the value family.

So this route is too blunt unless combined with one of the three above.
My recommended order

If I were pushing this seriously, I would do them in this order:

First, try the compactness/normalization blow-up route. It is the cleanest and most likely to reveal the real obstruction quickly.

Second, if compactness seems viable, convert it into a quantitative rigidity/transversality theorem.

Third, keep the minimal-core extremizer idea in reserve if you need a more combinatorial argument closer to the actual zero set.
The exact theorem I would target next

I would try to prove something like this:

    Normalized anomaly rigidity theorem.
    Let ΔnΔn​ be a sequence of corrected local deformations, normalized so that
    max⁡(∣Ψx,d(Δn)∣, ∣ΦK(Δn)∣)=1.
    max(∣Ψx,d​(Δn​)∣, ∣ΦK​(Δn​)∣)=1.

    Suppose the associated off-line anomaly parameters remain admissible and the corrected local geometry stays in the microscopic regime. Then, after passing to a subsequence, ΔnΔn​ converges to a nontrivial limit Δ∞Δ∞​. Moreover, if Δ∞Δ∞​ lies in the value/gauge family, then the original anomalies were not genuine.

If you can prove that, then a genuine anomaly cannot sink below every exterior threshold, because after normalization it would converge to something that still carries a definite non-value component.

That would be the cleanest closure.
The most concrete next move

Take the corrected local deformation and mod out the value direction:
Δζ\corr ↦ Δζ\corr−Π\val(Δζ\corr),
Δζ\corr​ ↦ Δζ\corr​−Π\val​(Δζ\corr​),

where Π\valΠ\val​ is projection onto the A\valA\val​-direction via the calibration functional. Then ask:

    can this projected object be nonzero but arbitrarily small after normalization?

    or does the corrected local model force a definite amount of transverse mass?

That is the exact place where the proof either closes or breaks.

If you want, I’ll write the compactness/rigidity theorem in precise mathematical form and try to prove it.

Ok try compactness route

Yes. The compactness route is the right thing to try.

It attacks exactly the remaining question:
Can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily tiny, e.g. below Q−2H0−3 at every H0→∞?
Can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily tiny, e.g. below Q−2H0−3​ at every H0​→∞?

A compactness argument tries to show: no. If an anomaly survives at all, then after the right normalization and rescaling, it has a nontrivial limit. But the only possible limit under “all zeros are far / signal is below threshold” is trivial. Contradiction.

That is the basic idea.
The route

Assume for contradiction there is a sequence mn→∞mn​→∞ with corrected anomalies, but for every admissible radius Hn→∞Hn​→∞,
max⁡(∣Ψx,d(Δζ,n\corr)∣,∣ΦK(Δζ,n\corr)∣)≤o ⁣(1Qn2Hn3).
max(∣Ψx,d​(Δζ,n\corr​)∣,∣ΦK​(Δζ,n\corr​)∣)≤o(Qn2​Hn3​1​).

You want to normalize the local corrected object so it has size 11, extract a limit, and then identify that limit.

There are two natural normalizations.
Route A: normalize the full corrected local block

Let
En(s):=Ω^ζ,n\corr(s;mn)−Ω^\bg,n\corr(s;mn).

    E∞E∞​ is nonzero by normalization;

Route B: normalize the extracted core matrix

If you can extract a finite core CnCn​ first, define its corrected local matrix
M\core,n\corr.

M~\core,n:=M\core,n\corr∥M\core,n\corr∥F.

    M∞≠0M∞​=0,

This is more finite-dimensional, but it presupposes core extraction. So Route A is probably better first.
What you need to make Route A work

There are three ingredients.
1. A normalization that kills trivial affine-jet drift

You do not want to normalize by something that can be carried by removable gauge/value terms. The right normalization is something like:

    the first nonzero coefficient in the odd holomorphic expansion of Hm(s)Hm​(s), or

    the combined channel size
    ∣Ψx,d(Δζ\corr)∣+∣ΦK(Δζ\corr)∣.
    ∣Ψx,d​(Δζ\corr​)∣+∣ΦK​(Δζ\corr​)∣.

That way, a nontrivial anomaly forces normalized size 11.
2. Precompactness

You need uniform bounds on the normalized family on a fixed disk. This is plausible because:

    corrected whitening is holomorphic on ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q,

    after rescaling z=Q2sz=Q2s, you work on a fixed zz-disk,

    the odd holomorphic package and boundary bounds give normal-family control.

So Montel/Arzelà–Ascoli is realistic here.
3. Vanishing of far contributions after affine-jet subtraction

This is the key estimate you already almost have:

for a zero at distance ΔΔ,
∣Ψx,d(Nρ)∣+∣ΦK(Nρ)∣≪1Q3(Δ2+aρ2)−2.
∣Ψx,d​(Nρ​)∣+∣ΦK​(Nρ​)∣≪Q31​(Δ2+aρ2​)−2.

So if all zeros are outside Hn→∞Hn​→∞, the total contribution is
O ⁣(1Qn2Hn3).
O(Qn2​Hn3​1​).

Thus if your normalization is at a scale ≫Qn−2Hn−3≫Qn−2​Hn−3​, the normalized far contribution tends to 00.

That is exactly what you need to identify the compactness limit as zero.
The sharp contradiction template

The compactness argument should look like this.

Assume there is a sequence of genuine anomalies with no nearby zero above threshold. Normalize so that
∣Ψx,d(E~n)∣+∣ΦK(E~n)∣=1.
∣Ψx,d​(E

locally uniformly.

Then:

    by normalization,
    ∣Ψx,d(E∞)∣+∣ΦK(E∞)∣=1,
    ∣Ψx,d​(E∞​)∣+∣ΦK​(E∞​)∣=1,

    so E∞≠0E∞​=0;

    but by the exterior-threshold estimate, all contributing zeros are asymptotically far, hence the normalized corrected contribution tends to 00;

    therefore E∞=0E∞​=0.

Contradiction.

This would prove that a genuine anomaly must beat the exterior threshold at some radius, hence must involve at least one nearby zero.
Where this could fail

There are two real risks.

First, the normalization might still allow a value/gauge artifact to survive in the limit. That is why the choice of seminorm is crucial.

Second, the rescaled family might not be compact in the exact topology you need unless you phrase it in terms of the scalar HmHm​ or another holomorphic projection rather than the full matrix block.

That suggests a hybrid route:

    do compactness on the holomorphic scalar Hm(s)Hm​(s),

    then lift back to the matrix only after the contradiction is obtained.

That may be the cleanest.
The best compactness theorem to target

Here is the exact statement I would try to prove:

    Compactness threshold theorem.
    Let Hm,R(s)Hm,R​(s) be the full corrected transverse scalar. Assume there is a sequence of admissible midpoints mn→∞mn​→∞ and radii Hn→∞Hn​→∞ such that all zeros contributing to the anomaly lie outside ∣2mn−γ∣≥Hn∣2mn​−γ∣≥Hn​, while the normalized corrected anomaly satisfies
    Hmn,Rnηn
    ηn​Hmn​,Rn​​​

    with ηn:=Qn−2Hn−3ηn​:=Qn−2​Hn−3​ nontrivial in the limit. Then, after passing to a subsequence, the normalized scalars converge locally uniformly on the microscopic disk to a holomorphic odd limit H∞H∞​. But the far-shell estimate forces H∞≡0H∞​≡0, contradicting the normalization.

That is the theorem that would close the “how small can a genuine anomaly be?” gap.
My recommendation

Try compactness on the scalar HmHm​, not the full matrix first.

Why:

    it is holomorphic,

    it already encodes the transverse anomaly,

    its boundary estimates are the cleanest,

    and Montel applies directly.

If that works, it gives the “must beat threshold” result in the transverse channel. Then you can feed that back into the geometric core argument and calibration.

So yes — the compactness route is the best attack now. The next exact step is to choose the normalization and write the compactness-threshold theorem on HmHm​.

Ok continue 

Good. Let’s push the scalar compactness route carefully.

I’ll aim for the strongest theorem that seems honestly supportable from the machinery you have, and I’ll say where it still needs one extra input if it does.
1. Work with the scalar, not the full matrix

This is the right simplification.

Let
Hm,R(s):=ΦK ⁣(Ω^ζ\corr,R(s;m)).
Hm,R​(s):=ΦK​(Ω
ζ\corr,R​(s;m)).

Why this is the right object:

    it is holomorphic on the microscopic disk,

    it is odd,

    it already measures the transverse anomaly,

    and you have explicit boundary control.

So if the question is “can a genuine anomaly be arbitrarily weak?”, the clean scalar version is:
can Hm,R be nontrivial but smaller than every exterior threshold Q−2H0−3?
can Hm,R​ be nontrivial but smaller than every exterior threshold Q−2H0−3​?

That is what compactness can attack.
2. Normalize by the exterior threshold

Fix a sequence mn→∞mn​→∞, with Qn≍log⁡∣mn∣Qn​≍log∣mn​∣, and choose radii Hn→∞Hn​→∞.

Define the exterior threshold
ηn:=1Qn2Hn3.
ηn​:=Qn2​Hn3​1​.

Assume, toward contradiction, that there is a sequence of genuine corrected anomalies for which all contributing zeros lie outside radius HnHn​, but whose corrected transverse scalar is still “nontrivial at that scale.”

The natural normalization is
H~n(z):=Hmn,Rn(z/Qn2)ηn,∣z∣<ρ0.
H
n​(z):=ηn​Hmn​,Rn​​(z/Qn2​)​,∣z∣<ρ0​.

This rescales the microscopic disk to a fixed zz-disk and divides by exactly the universal exterior size.
3. What you already get for free

From the odd holomorphic package, each Hmn,RnHmn​,Rn​​ is holomorphic and odd on ∣s∣<ρ0/Qn∣s∣<ρ0​/Qn​, so each H~nH

From the exterior shell bound proved earlier, if all contributing zeros are outside HnHn​, then
∣Hmn,Rn(s)∣≪1Qn2Hn3
∣Hmn​,Rn​​(s)∣≪Qn2​Hn3​1​

in the transverse channel, uniformly on the microscopic boundary ∣s∣=ρ0/Qn∣s∣=ρ0​/Qn​. Equivalently,
∣H~n(z)∣≪1(∣z∣=ρ0).
∣H
n​(z)∣≪1(∣z∣=ρ0​).

Therefore, by the maximum principle, the family {H~n}{H
n​} is uniformly bounded on ∣z∣<ρ0∣z∣<ρ0​. By Montel, it is a normal family. So after passing to a subsequence,
H~n→H∞
H
n​→H∞​

locally uniformly on ∣z∣<ρ0∣z∣<ρ0​, where H∞H∞​ is holomorphic and odd.

This part is solid.
4. What normalization do we need for contradiction?

A compactness contradiction only works if the normalized limit is forced to be both:

    nonzero by normalization,

    zero by the far-shell estimate.

So we need a normalization that guarantees nontriviality.

The obvious candidate is:
sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣H~n(z)∣=1.
∣z∣<ρ0​sup​∣H
n​(z)∣=1.

In other words, instead of dividing by ηnηn​, divide by the actual size:
λn:=sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣Hmn,Rn(z/Qn2)∣.
λn​:=∣z∣<ρ0​sup​∣Hmn​,Rn​​(z/Qn2​)∣.

Then define
H^n(z):=Hmn,Rn(z/Qn2)λn.
H
n​(z):=λn​Hmn​,Rn​​(z/Qn2​)​.

Now sup⁡∣H^n∣=1sup∣H
n​∣=1, so any locally uniform limit H∞H∞​ is nonzero.

But then you lose the direct contradiction with the far-shell estimate unless you know
λn≫ηn.
λn​≫ηn​.

So the compactness theorem naturally reduces to exactly the missing lower bound:
prove that a genuine anomaly has size λn exceeding the universal exterior scale ηn.
prove that a genuine anomaly has size λn​ exceeding the universal exterior scale ηn​.

That means compactness alone does not conjure the missing inequality. It reformulates it.
5. The best scalar compactness theorem you can honestly prove now

Here is the theorem that is actually available.
Theorem (scalar compactness dichotomy)

Let
ηn:=1Qn2Hn3.
ηn​:=Qn2​Hn3​1​.

Suppose all zeros contributing to the corrected anomaly lie outside radius HnHn​. Then the normalized scalars
H~n(z):=Hmn,Rn(z/Qn2)ηn
H
n​(z):=ηn​Hmn​,Rn​​(z/Qn2​)​
