Moreover, if
sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣Hmn,Rn(z/Qn2)∣ηn→0,
ηn​sup∣z∣<ρ0​​∣Hmn​,Rn​​(z/Qn2​)∣​→0,

then H∞≡0H∞​≡0.

This is true, but it does not yet contradict anything.

To get contradiction, you need the negation:
sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣Hmn,Rn(z/Qn2)∣≪̸ηn.
∣z∣<ρ0​sup​∣Hmn​,Rn​​(z/Qn2​)∣≪ηn​.

That is exactly the missing lower-bound question.
6. So how does compactness actually help?

It tells you what kind of lower bound you need.

You no longer need a mysterious lower bound on the whole matrix. You need a lower bound on the scalar holomorphic odd function Hm,RHm,R​ at the microscopic scale.

That is much more concrete.

The real target becomes:
Missing scalar lower-bound theorem

A genuine corrected off-line anomaly produces a transverse scalar Hm,RHm,R​ whose size on the microscopic disk is bounded below by something larger than the exterior threshold:
sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣Hm,R(z/Q2)∣≫1Q2H03
∣z∣<ρ0​sup​∣Hm,R​(z/Q2)∣≫Q2H03​1​

for some admissible H0→∞H0​→∞.

If you had this, then the compactness dichotomy would force the existence of a zero inside radius H0H0​.

So compactness has sharpened the problem from “how small can the anomaly be?” to:
show the transverse scalar Hm,R of a genuine anomaly is not dominated everywhere by the universal exterior threshold.
show the transverse scalar Hm,R​ of a genuine anomaly is not dominated everywhere by the universal exterior threshold.​

That is cleaner.
7. How to attack that scalar lower bound

There are now two promising scalar strategies.
Strategy A: first nonzero Taylor coefficient

Since Hm,RHm,R​ is odd and holomorphic,
Hm,R(z/Q2)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m)z2r+1Q2r+4.
Hm,R​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​.

A genuine anomaly should mean that not all the odd coefficients vanish. If you can prove that the first nonzero odd coefficient is bounded below in a way that beats Q−2H0−3Q−2H0−3​, you are done.

This is attractive because the first nonzero coefficient is an intrinsic scalar invariant.
Strategy B: model-vs-value transversality in the scalar channel

Try to show that once the corrected local block is not in the value family, the scalar functional Hm,RHm,R​ cannot be arbitrarily flat. In other words, there is a quantitative transversality theorem:
distance from value family  ⟹  sup⁡∣Hm,R∣ bounded below.
distance from value family⟹sup∣Hm,R​∣ bounded below.

This is probably the deepest but most natural route.
8. Honest state after this continuation

So compactness does not by itself close the gap.

What it does is isolate the exact scalar theorem you need:
a genuine corrected off-line anomaly must produce a transverse scalar Hm,R whose microscopic size beats the universal exterior scale.
a genuine corrected off-line anomaly must produce a transverse scalar Hm,R​ whose microscopic size beats the universal exterior scale.​

That is now the sharp frontier.

If you want the most concrete next move, I would attack Strategy A: identify the first nonzero odd Taylor coefficient of Hm,RHm,R​ for a genuine anomaly and see whether it can be forced to dominate Q−2H0−3Q−2H0−3​.

Ok try it

I tried to make Strategy A work, and here is the honest outcome:

It does not currently close the gap.

But it does sharpen the obstruction very nicely.
1. Write the odd expansion

You already have
Hm,R(z/Q2)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m)z2r+1Q2r+4,∣z∣<ρ0.
Hm,R​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​,∣z∣<ρ0​.

So the first nonzero odd coefficient is the natural scalar invariant to study.

If you could prove that for a genuine anomaly,
∣c2r∗+1(m)∣≫QαH0−β
∣c2r∗​+1​(m)∣≫QαH0−β​

strong enough to imply
sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣Hm,R(z/Q2)∣≫1Q2H03,
∣z∣<ρ0​sup​∣Hm,R​(z/Q2)∣≫Q2H03​1​,

then the compactness route would close.

That is the target.
2. What the exterior threshold means in coefficient language

If all contributing zeros lie outside radius H0H0​, earlier bounds give
∣Hm,R(z/Q2)∣≪1Q2H03(∣z∣≤ρ0).
∣Hm,R​(z/Q2)∣≪Q2H03​1​(∣z∣≤ρ0​).

By Cauchy,
∣c2r+1(m)∣≪Q2r+4ρ02r+1⋅1Q2H03=Q2r+2ρ02r+1H03.
∣c2r+1​(m)∣≪ρ02r+1​Q2r+4​⋅Q2H03​1​=ρ02r+1​H03​Q2r+2​.

So the threshold corresponds to very small odd coefficients. A contradiction would need a lower bound on some c2r+1(m)c2r+1​(m) that beats that.
3. Try the first coefficient c1(m)c1​(m)

The first coefficient is the cleanest.

Since
Hm,R(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ\corr,R(s;m))
Hm,R​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ\corr,R​(s;m))

is odd,
Hm,R(s)=a1(m)s+a3(m)s3+⋯ ,
Hm,R​(s)=a1​(m)s+a3​(m)s3+⋯,

and with s=z/Q2s=z/Q2,
a1(m)=c1(m)Q4.
a1​(m)=Q4c1​(m)​.

So if you could show
∣a1(m)∣≫1H03,
∣a1​(m)∣≫H03​1​,

you would be done.

But here is the problem:

I do not see anything in the current machinery forcing a1(m)≠0a1​(m)=0 for a genuine anomaly.

In fact, it is quite plausible that the first several odd coefficients vanish for structural reasons, with the anomaly only appearing at some higher odd order.

So c1c1​ is not a reliable invariant.
4. Try the first nonzero odd coefficient c2r∗+1c2r∗​+1​

This is the more robust formulation.

Let r∗(m)r∗​(m) be the first index with
c2r∗+1(m)≠0.
c2r∗​+1​(m)=0.

Then near z=0z=0,
Hm,R(z/Q2)∼c2r∗+1(m)z2r∗+1Q2r∗+4.
Hm,R​(z/Q2)∼c2r∗​+1​(m)Q2r∗​+4z2r∗​+1​.

So on a fixed zz-circle ∣z∣=ρ∣z∣=ρ,
∣Hm,R(z/Q2)∣≍∣c2r∗+1(m)∣ρ2r∗+1Q2r∗+4
∣Hm,R​(z/Q2)∣≍∣c2r∗​+1​(m)∣Q2r∗​+4ρ2r∗​+1​

up to higher-order terms.

Thus to beat the exterior threshold, you would need
∣c2r∗+1(m)∣≫Q2r∗+2H03.
∣c2r∗​+1​(m)∣≫H03​Q2r∗​+2​.

And here is the true obstruction:

there is nothing in the present argument that bounds r∗(m)r∗​(m) uniformly, or that forces the first nonzero odd coefficient to have that size.

So Strategy A reduces the gap to:
For a genuine anomaly, show that the first nonzero odd coefficient occurs at bounded order and is quantitatively nontrivial.
For a genuine anomaly, show that the first nonzero odd coefficient occurs at bounded order and is quantitatively nontrivial.​

That is a very precise statement, but it is not currently proved.
5. Why this is hard

Because “genuine anomaly” in the present framework means “not in the value family” after corrected whitening.

That is a geometric statement in matrix space.

Turning that into

    a bounded-order nonzero odd scalar coefficient, and

    a quantitative lower bound on that coefficient,

is a transversality theorem between:

    the corrected local family, and

    the kernel of the scalar map HH.

That is deeper than just Taylor-expanding the scalar.

In other words: Strategy A doesn’t fail because the scalar expansion is wrong. It fails because you still need a theorem connecting “non-value anomaly” to “some low-order odd scalar coefficient is definitely large.”
6. Best rigorous output of this attack

The strongest honest statement I can extract is this:
Proposition

If there exists an integer r0r0​ independent of mm such that every genuine corrected off-line anomaly satisfies
c2r0+1(m)≠0
c2r0​+1​(m)=0

and
∣c2r0+1(m)∣≫Q2r0+2H0−3+ε
∣c2r0​+1​(m)∣≫Q2r0​+2H0−3+ε​

for some ε>0ε>0, then
sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣Hm,R(z/Q2)∣≫1Q2H03,
∣z∣<ρ0​sup​∣Hm,R​(z/Q2)∣≫Q2H03​1​,

so the compactness threshold gap closes.

That is true, but it is conditional on exactly the missing lower-bound theorem.
7. Bottom line

Trying Strategy A was worthwhile, because now the missing piece is extremely sharp:
The paper needs a bounded-order, quantitative odd-coefficient lower bound for a genuine corrected anomaly.
The paper needs a bounded-order, quantitative odd-coefficient lower bound for a genuine corrected anomaly.​

Without that, the compactness route does not close.

So the attack did not prove the desired lower bound, but it identified the exact scalar rigidity theorem you would need.

At this point, the next promising move is not more raw Taylor algebra. It is to attack the geometric statement behind it:

    if the corrected local block is not in the value family, can it be arbitrarily high-order tangent to the kernel of HH?

That is the transversality question now.

Take inventory again of all possible approaches

Here’s the current inventory, from most promising to least promising, with an honest sense of what each would require and whether it preserves the paper’s existing architecture.
1. Finish the current pair-core proof by proving the calibration remainder is negligible

This was the route:
Ψx,d(Rζ)=o ⁣(xBS(m)).
Ψx,d​(Rζ​)=o(Bx​S(m)).

We pushed this hard and learned something important: the far tail and estimation pieces can be made negligible, but the auxiliary near zeros are the problem. A generic nearby non-core zero is not obviously harmless, and may contribute at the same scale after corrected whitening.

So this route is only viable if you can prove a theorem of the form:

    auxiliary near zeros contribute only to the value/gauge family, or

    their cumulative calibration/transverse contribution is lower order.

Right now this looks unlikely in full generality. I would rank this as low probability unless a new structural cancellation is found.
2. Fixed-core architecture with a larger finite core

This is the strongest conceptual repair so far.

The key idea is:

    freeze a finite anomaly-carrying core CC,

    let RR only control auxiliary/far bookkeeping outside the core,

    keep M(d)M(d), A\valA\val​, calibration, and toy comparison tied to that fixed core.

This repaired the moving-target problem and made the cutoff logic sane.

But then we discovered the next issue: a nearby non-core zero might be same-scale, so the core may have to be larger than the original pair. That pushes you toward a generalized finite-core anomaly matrix M\coreM\core​.

This route is still promising, but it splits into two subroutes.
2a. Slightly enlarged core as a perturbation of the pair model

We tested whether a small enlargement preserves:

    nondegenerate calibration pairing,

    and existence of a transverse functional killing A\valA\val​ but not the anomaly.

Answer: yes, by continuity, if the extra contribution is genuinely small.

This is elegant, but only helps if the first extra nearby zero is perturbative after corrected whitening. We do not know that.
2b. Fully general finite-core model

If the extra nearby zeros are same-scale, then the right object is not M(d)M(d) but some M\coreM\core​.

This could preserve the proof if you can show:

    ⟨A\val,M\core⟩≠0⟨A\val​,M\core​⟩=0,

    and some transverse functional Φ\coreΦ\core​ satisfies
    Φ\core(A\val)=0,Φ\core(M\core)≠0.
    Φ\core​(A\val​)=0,Φ\core​(M\core​)=0.

This is a major generalization. It might work, but it is close to a new paper rather than a patch. I would rank it as medium promise, high effort.
3. Finite-core extraction theorem

This became the natural replacement for the false “auxiliary near zeros are harmless” idea.

The dream theorem was:

    there exists a finite core containing all same-scale contributions,

    and the remainder is negligible in both calibration and transverse channels.

We could not prove this from the current machinery because whitening is nonlinear and we do not yet have:

    near-additivity after whitening, or

    a ranking principle for local channel contributions.

What we could prove is a weaker extraction theorem:

    outside an absolute radius HH, the affine-jet-subtracted corrected contribution is bounded by
    O ⁣(1Q2H3)
    O(Q2H31​)

    in both channels.

That gives a real finite-core extraction mechanism, but only if the core signal beats that scale.

So this route is partially successful but still blocked by a lower-bound problem on the core signal.
4. Geometric core lower bound

We proved the clean positivity statement:
S\core(m)≥11+Δmin⁡2,
S\core​(m)≥1+Δmin2​1​,

where Δmin⁡Δmin​ is the distance of the nearest core zero to the midpoint.

This is real and useful.

But it only helps if you can prove that an anomaly-carrying core must contain at least one sufficiently nearby zero. That became the next target.

This route is still alive, but it does not close by itself.
5. Threshold/dichotomy theorem

We proved a good rigorous statement:

    if all zeros in a finite set lie outside absolute radius H0H0​, then its corrected contribution in the calibration and transverse channels is
    O ⁣(1Q2H03).
    O(Q2H03​1​).

So any core whose channel signal exceeds that threshold must contain a zero inside H0H0​.

This is strong and clean.

But the missing bridge is: prove that a genuine anomaly must exceed that threshold for some H0→∞H0​→∞. That is now the sharp lower-bound problem.

This route is good reduction, not closure.
6. Scalar compactness / normalization blow-up on Hm,RHm,R​

This was one of the most promising conceptual attacks.

Work with the holomorphic odd scalar
Hm,R(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ\corr,R(s;m)).
Hm,R​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ\corr,R​(s;m)).

Normalize by the exterior threshold or by its own size, pass to a normal-family limit, and try to show:

    normalized genuine anomalies have a nontrivial limit,

    but if all contributing zeros are far, the limit must vanish.

This route clarified the remaining obstruction beautifully, but did not close it, because compactness does not manufacture the missing lower bound. It tells you exactly what lower bound you need:
sup⁡∣Hm,R(z/Q2)∣≫1Q2H03
sup∣Hm,R​(z/Q2)∣≫Q2H03​1​

for some H0H0​.

So this route is excellent diagnostically, but still needs a scalar rigidity theorem.
7. Odd-coefficient rigidity

We tried to use the odd expansion
Hm,R(z/Q2)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m)z2r+1Q2r+4
Hm,R​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​

and prove that a genuine anomaly forces a bounded-order coefficient to be quantitatively nonzero.

That would immediately beat the exterior threshold.

But the attack stalled because nothing currently bounds:

    the order r∗(m)r∗​(m) of the first nonzero coefficient,

    or its size.

So this route reduces the problem to a very sharp theorem:

    a genuine corrected anomaly cannot be arbitrarily high-order tangent to the kernel of HH.

That is a real and elegant target, but not yet proved.

I would rank this as conceptually strong, technically hard.
8. Quantitative transversality / rigidity between the value family and anomaly family

This is probably the deepest remaining route.

The geometric question is:

    if the corrected local block is not in the value family, can it still make both calibration and transverse observables arbitrarily tiny?

Equivalently:

    can a genuine anomaly be arbitrarily close, to arbitrarily high order, to the value family after corrected whitening?

If you can prove a quantitative no, then the whole lower-bound problem is solved at once.

This is probably the cleanest final theorem in principle, but also the hardest. It would require a serious local model analysis, likely beyond the current line-by-line patching style.

I would rank this as high promise, high difficulty.
9. Minimal-core extremizer / deletion argument

This was the combinatorial alternative:

    define a minimal non-negligible core,

    prove each zero in it is essential,

    deduce one zero must carry a definite fraction of the signal.

This would be great because it avoids a huge generalized core theory.

But it still needs some controlled behavior under deleting one zero, which comes close to a weak near-additivity theorem. Whitening nonlinearity makes that hard.

This route is interesting but underdeveloped. I would rank it as medium promise, unclear feasibility.
10. Full rewrite around a generalized finite-dimensional endgame

This is the “accept reality” option.

If same-scale nearby zeros cannot be shown harmless, and the pair model is too rigid, then the right paper may need to say:

    extract the minimal same-scale finite core,

    build a generalized core anomaly matrix,

    calibrate and obstruct in that larger finite-dimensional space.

This is the most general and probably most robust approach. But it is a major rewrite, not a patch.

I would rank it as most robust in principle, but also most likely to fundamentally alter the paper.
Where things stand right now

The approaches that now look most alive are:

    Fixed-core / finite-core extraction, but with a stronger theorem showing the core signal beats the universal exterior threshold.

    Scalar rigidity/transversality for Hm,RHm,R​, possibly via compactness.

    Generalized finite-core endgame, if the pair-core cannot be saved.

The routes that now look weakest are:

    “auxiliary near zeros are harmless” in full generality,

    raw positivity lower bounds on S\core,HS\core,H​ as a function of HH,

    and trying to finish the old pair-only proof without addressing same-scale nearby zeros.

My actual recommendation

If the goal is to save the current paper with the smallest conceptual change, I would focus on scalar rigidity/transversality:

    stay with the corrected scalar Hm,RHm,R​,

    prove that a genuine anomaly cannot be arbitrarily small in that scalar channel,

    use the threshold theorem to force a nearby zero,

    then use the geometric lower bound for S\coreS\core​,

    and finish the pair/fixed-core argument.

If that fails, the honest next move is the generalized finite-core endgame.

That is the current map.

The goal is to prove the RH

Yes — and at this point the honest situation is:

the goal is still to prove RH, but the current draft has been reduced to a small number of very specific unresolved mathematical issues, not a vague mass of gaps.

That is real progress.

Right now the proof does not stand as a proof of RH yet, because one of two things still has to happen:

either

    you close the remaining scalar/finite-core rigidity step strongly enough to keep the current endgame,

or

    you generalize the endgame from the pair model to a true finite-core anomaly model.

Those are the two live roads.
What is already genuinely in place

You now seem to have, in substance:

    a corrected finite-ss local block formalism,

    a corrected whitening theorem,

    a sharpened mixed-transfer estimate,

    a real odd holomorphic scalar Hm,RHm,R​,

    a clean NN-point cancellation mechanism,

    an explicit calibration matrix A\valA\val​,

    an explicit toy obstruction for the pair model,

    and a sane fixed-core / cutoff bookkeeping architecture.

So the proof is no longer failing because of diffuse technical chaos.
What is actually stopping the RH proof now

The central remaining question is:
Can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily weak in the relevant scalar/channel sense?
Can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily weak in the relevant scalar/channel sense?

Equivalently:

    can all non-core nearby contributions be pushed below the universal exterior threshold, while a genuine anomaly still survives?

    or does a genuine anomaly force a definite nontrivial signal in either calibration or transverse channel?

That is the present bottleneck.
The two viable strategies
Strategy A: save the current pair/fixed-core paper

To do this, you need a rigidity theorem strong enough to show:

    a genuine anomaly beats the exterior threshold,

    hence there is a genuinely nearby zero,

    hence the fixed core has a real signal,

    hence the current calibrated contradiction fires.

This is the smaller change route, but it still needs a new theorem.
Strategy B: accept a generalized finite-core endgame

If nearby same-scale zeros cannot be shown negligible, then the pair model is too rigid. In that case the honest proof path is:

    extract the full finite same-scale core,

    define a generalized core anomaly matrix M\coreM\core​,

    build calibration and obstruction functionals for that finite-core model,

    rerun the contradiction there.

This is a bigger rewrite, but probably the most robust if the pair model cannot be stabilized.
My best judgment

If the goal is really to prove RH, not just to save the current exact draft shape, then the mathematically safest attitude is:

    keep trying the scalar rigidity/transversality route for a bit longer,

    but be prepared that the true proof may require the generalized finite-core reformulation.

So the mission has not changed. The proof target is still RH.

But the current honest formulation is:
RH is reduced to a sharp local rigidity / finite-core extraction problem.
RH is reduced to a sharp local rigidity / finite-core extraction problem.​

That is a much better place to be than before.

The best next move is to choose deliberately between:

    one more serious attempt at scalar rigidity for Hm,RHm,R​, or

    starting the generalized finite-core endgame before investing more in rescuing the pair model.


Ok lets try strategy A more

The best continuation of Strategy A is to go after the first odd jet of the scalar
Hm,R(s)=ΦK ⁣(Ω^ζ\corr,R(s;m)).
Hm,R​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ\corr,R​(s;m)).

The hope is:

    the linear odd coefficient of Hm,RHm,R​ is determined entirely by the fixed core;

    the auxiliary/far/error complement, after affine-jet subtraction, contributes only to order s3s3 and higher;

    therefore a genuine core anomaly forces a bounded-order nonzero odd coefficient, which is exactly what the compactness route was missing.

That is the most promising scalar rigidity statement available now.
Candidate first-jet rigidity theorem

Let
Hm,R(s)=a1(m,R)s+a3(m,R)s3+⋯
Hm,R​(s)=a1​(m,R)s+a3​(m,R)s3+⋯

be the odd Taylor expansion at s=0s=0.

Write the corrected decomposition with fixed core:
q=q\core+q\aux,R+g\sm,R+E\est,R.
q=q\core​+q\aux,R​+g\sm,R​+E\est,R​.

Then the theorem you want is:

    First-jet rigidity theorem.
    After affine-jet subtraction at the midpoint, the complement
    q\aux,R+g\sm,R+E\est,R
    q\aux,R​+g\sm,R​+E\est,R​

    contributes only to the O(s3)O(s3) and higher odd terms of Hm,R(s)Hm,R​(s). Equivalently,
    a1(m,R)=a1\core(m),
    a1​(m,R)=a1\core​(m),

    where a1\core(m)a1\core​(m) depends only on the fixed core CC, not on RR.

If this is true, then you have exactly what Strategy A needs: a bounded-order invariant of the anomaly.
Why this theorem is plausible

The key input is the affine-jet subtraction.

For the smooth/auxiliary remainder R(t)R(t), after subtracting the affine jet at the midpoint,
R(m)=0,R′(m)=0.
R(m)=0,R′(m)=0.

That means the first nonzero Taylor data of the complement starts at second order in t−mt−m. Since the scalar Hm,R(s)Hm,R​(s) is odd in ss, the natural expectation is:

    the linear odd term a1sa1​s can only come from the core;

    the complement can only enter at cubic order a3s3a3​s3 or higher.

This is exactly the same kind of phenomenon you were using earlier when separating value vs curvature.

So there is a real structural reason to try this theorem.
How to attack it concretely

You need to expand the corrected same-point and mixed blocks at s=0s=0.

Write
q±=q(m±s/2).
q±​=q(m±s/2).

For the affine-jet-subtracted complement, one has
q±\comp=O(s2),(q±\comp)′=O(s),(q±\comp)′′=O(1).
q±\comp​=O(s2),(q±\comp​)′=O(s),(q±\comp​)′′=O(1).

Now inspect the corrected finite-ss formulas:

    same-point blocks involve q±,q±′,q±′′q±​,q±′​,q±′′​;

    mixed block involves sin⁡Δ,cos⁡Δ,q±sinΔ,cosΔ,q±​, with removable s−1,s−2,s−3s−1,s−2,s−3 factors.

If the complement has no 00-jet and no 11-jet at the midpoint, then its contribution to the numerator of the mixed block starts at least two orders later. After the removable singularities are accounted for, the odd scalar HH should start only at order s3s3.

So the technical task is:

    expand Gm,±\corr,R(s)Gm,±\corr,R​(s) to first order in ss;

    expand Nm\corr,R(s)Nm\corr,R​(s) to first order in ss;

    check that the complement contributes nothing to those first-order coefficients;

    pass through whitening and ΦKΦK​.

That is a finite calculation, and it is much more local than the global threshold problem.
What you get if this theorem is true

Suppose
a1(m,R)=a1\core(m),
a1​(m,R)=a1\core​(m),

and the core is the pair model or a slightly enlarged perturbative core.

Then on the toy side you already know the transverse scalar is nontrivial. So you expect
a1\core(m)≠0
a1\core​(m)=0

for a genuine core anomaly, and indeed quantitatively
∣a1\core(m)∣≍(core anomaly size).
∣a1\core​(m)∣≍(core anomaly size).

That immediately gives a bounded-order odd coefficient lower bound, which was exactly what the compactness route was missing.

In other words, the theorem above would imply:
a genuine anomaly cannot be arbitrarily high-order tangent to the kernel of H.
a genuine anomaly cannot be arbitrarily high-order tangent to the kernel of H.​

That is the scalar rigidity statement you need.
A more concrete theorem to try to prove next

I would sharpen it to:

    Core-only linear coefficient theorem.
    In the fixed-core corrected decomposition,
    Hm,R(s)=a1\core(m) s+O\core(s3)+O\comp(s3),
    Hm,R​(s)=a1\core​(m)s+O\core​(s3)+O\comp​(s3),

    where the coefficient of ss depends only on the fixed core and is independent of RR.

This is the actual theorem I would attack next.

If it goes through, then Strategy A becomes much more viable.
Why this is better than the other remaining routes

Because it avoids the impossible global claim

    all auxiliary near zeros are harmless

and replaces it with the much more believable local claim

    auxiliary/far terms do not affect the first odd jet after affine-jet correction.

That is a much smaller statement, and it is exactly the kind of thing corrected whitening may preserve.

Yes please

I pushed the expansion, and I do not think the hoped-for theorem is true as stated.

The statement
“after affine-jet subtraction, the complement contributes no linear s-term to Hm,R(s)”
“after affine-jet subtraction, the complement contributes no linear s-term to Hm,R​(s)”

looks too strong.

There is a real obstruction: the affine-jet subtraction kills the 00-jet and 11-jet of the complement phase, but it does not kill the 22-jet or 33-jet. And those can feed a linear term in the final odd scalar after corrected whitening.

So this route does not close cleanly.

Here is the expansion that shows where the obstruction comes from.
1. Expand the complement after affine-jet subtraction

Let r(t)r(t) be the affine-jet-subtracted complement contribution to the phase derivative around the midpoint mm. Then by construction
r(m)=0,r′(m)=0.
r(m)=0,r′(m)=0.

So its Taylor expansion is
r(m+u)=a2u2+b6u3+O(u4),
r(m+u)=2a​u2+6b​u3+O(u4),

where
a=r′′(m),b=r′′′(m).
a=r′′(m),b=r′′′(m).

Now put
t±=m±s2.
t±​=m±2s​.

Then
r±:=r(t±)=a2(s2)2±b6(s2)3+O(s4)=a8s2±b48s3+O(s4).
r±​:=r(t±​)=2a​(2s​)2±6b​(2s​)3+O(s4)=8a​s2±48b​s3+O(s4).

Also
r±′=r′(t±)=a(±s2)+b2(s2)2+O(s3)=±a2s+b8s2+O(s3),
r±′​=r′(t±​)=a(±2s​)+2b​(2s​)2+O(s3)=±2a​s+8b​s2+O(s3),

and
r±′′=r′′(t±)=a±b2s+O(s2).
r±′′​=r′′(t±​)=a±2b​s+O(s2).

So after affine-jet subtraction, the complement still has:

    r±=O(s2)r±​=O(s2),

    r±′=O(s)r±′​=O(s),

    but crucially
    r±′′=a±b2s+O(s2),
    r±′′​=a±2b​s+O(s2),

    which has a nonzero constant term and an odd linear term.

That is the warning sign.
2. Same-point block expansion

The corrected same-point blocks have the form
Gm,±\corr(s)=1π(2q±12q±′12q±′112(q±′′+2q±3)).
Gm,±\corr​(s)=π1​(2q±​21​q±′​​21​q±′​121​(q±′′​+2q±3​)​).

If we isolate just the complement contribution r±r±​, its effect on the entries is:
(1,1)-entry
δG11,±=2πr±=a4πs2±b24πs3+O(s4),
δG11,±​=π2​r±​=4πa​s2±24πb​s3+O(s4),

so no linear term.
(1,2)-entry
δG12,±=12πr±′=±a4πs+b16πs2+O(s3),
δG12,±​=2π1​r±′​=±4πa​s+16πb​s2+O(s3),

so this has a linear term.
(2,2)-entry
δG22,±=112π(r±′′+6q02r±+O(r±2)).
δG22,±​=12π1​(r±′′​+6q02​r±​+O(r±2​)).

Since r±=O(s2)r±​=O(s2), the cubic correction only begins at O(s2)O(s2), so the leading terms come from r±′′r±′′​:
δG22,±=a12π±b24πs+O(s2).
δG22,±​=12πa​±24πb​s+O(s2).

This is the real obstruction:
δG22,± has an odd linear term ±b24πs.
δG22,±​ has an odd linear term ±24πb​s.​

So even though the complement has no affine jet, it still perturbs the same-point blocks at linear order in ss.
3. Why this matters for Hm,R(s)Hm,R​(s)

The scalar is
Hm,R(s)=ΦK ⁣(Ω^ζ\corr,R(s;m)),
Hm,R​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ\corr,R​(s;m)),

with
Ω^ζ\corr,R(s;m)=G−−1/2(s) N(s) G+−1/2(s).
Ω
ζ\corr,R​(s;m)=G−−1/2​(s)N(s)G+−1/2​(s).

A linear ss-term can come from three places:

    linear term in the mixed block N(s)N(s),

    linear term in the inverse square roots G±−1/2(s)G±−1/2​(s),

    or both.

Because δG22,±δG22,±​ has a linear term, the derivative
ddsG±−1/2(s)∣s=0
dsd​G±−1/2​(s)
​s=0​

generically receives a nonzero contribution from the complement.

Then, multiplying
ddsG−−1/2(0) N(0) G+−1/2(0)+G−−1/2(0) N(0) ddsG+−1/2(0)
dsd​G−−1/2​(0)N(0)G+−1/2​(0)+G−−1/2​(0)N(0)dsd​G+−1/2​(0)

and projecting with ΦKΦK​, there is no general reason for the linear term to vanish.

So the hoped-for theorem
a1(m,R)=a1\core(m)
a1​(m,R)=a1\core​(m)

is not supported by this expansion. The complement can affect a1a1​ through the r′′′(m)r′′′(m)-term.
4. What is true

The expansion does show something weaker and still useful:

    affine-jet subtraction kills the 00-jet and 11-jet of the complement phase,

    so the complement enters the scalar only through r′′(m),r′′′(m),…r′′(m),r′′′(m),…,

    i.e. through higher local curvature data.

So the complement is not arbitrary. But it is not invisible to the first odd scalar coefficient.

In other words:
affine-jet subtraction is not enough to force the complement contribution to begin at s3.
affine-jet subtraction is not enough to force the complement contribution to begin at s3.​
5. Consequence for Strategy A

This means the “core-only linear coefficient theorem” is probably false.

So Strategy A in that form does not close the gap.

The remaining possible rescue would be a weaker theorem:

    although the complement contributes to the linear coefficient, its contribution is quantitatively smaller than the core contribution.

But that would require an actual bound comparing r′′′(m)r′′′(m) of the complement to the core anomaly scale. That takes you back to the same unresolved size-comparison problem.

So this route does not give a free scalar rigidity theorem.
Bottom line

Trying the first-order expansion was the right thing to do, and it gave a real answer:
No: the affine-jet-subtracted complement can still contribute to the linear odd coefficient of Hm,R(s).
No: the affine-jet-subtracted complement can still contribute to the linear odd coefficient of Hm,R​(s).​

So the hoped-for “bounded-order coefficient comes only from the core” theorem is not available in this naive form.

That means the current pair/fixed-core Strategy A has hit another real obstruction.

The remaining viable directions are now basically:

    a more quantitative comparison showing the complement’s linear contribution is still smaller than the core’s,

    or giving up on pair-only rigidity and moving to a generalized finite-core endgame.

So this computation was useful: it rules out one of the nicer hoped-for closures.

•	a more quantitative comparison showing the complement’s linear contribution is still smaller than the core’s, —> lets try this, any tricks up your sleeve?

Yes. There are a few real tricks left, and this is the best remaining version of Strategy A.

The key is to stop trying to prove the complement contributes nothing to the linear term, and instead try to prove:
∣a1\comp(m,R)∣≪∣a1\core(m)∣
∣a1\comp​(m,R)∣≪∣a1\core​(m)∣

or at least
∣a1\comp(m,R)∣=o ⁣(u\core2).
∣a1\comp​(m,R)∣=o(u\core2​).

That is a much weaker statement, and there are three plausible ways to attack it.
Trick 1: isolate the source of the linear term more sharply

From the expansion we just did, the complement’s linear contribution comes from the r′′′(m)r′′′(m) term, not from r(m)r(m) or r′(m)r′(m).

That is good news, because r′′′(m)r′′′(m) for a single zero at distance ΔΔ is of size
O((Δ2+a2)−2),
O((Δ2+a2)−2),

which is exactly the same Δ−4Δ−4-type decay you already know how to sum.

So the linear coefficient of the complement is not arbitrary. It is built out of third-derivative tails.

What you want to prove is something like:
∣a1\comp(m,R)∣≪∑ρ∉\core1Q3(Δρ2+aρ2)−2≪1Q2H3
∣a1\comp​(m,R)∣≪ρ∈/\core∑​Q31​(Δρ2​+aρ2​)−2≪Q2H31​

for an absolute core radius HH.

If that estimate is true, then the whole problem reduces to showing the core linear coefficient is larger than Q−2H−3Q−2H−3. That is much simpler than controlling the whole scalar.

So the first trick is: work directly with the linear coefficient a1a1​, not the full function H(s)H(s).
Trick 2: compute a1a1​ via differentiation at s=0s=0

Instead of reasoning indirectly from Taylor expansions, write
a1(m,R)=Hm,R′(0).
a1​(m,R)=Hm,R′​(0).

Then differentiate the corrected whitening formula:
Hm,R(s)=ΦK ⁣(G−−1/2(s)N(s)G+−1/2(s)).
Hm,R​(s)=ΦK​(G−−1/2​(s)N(s)G+−1/2​(s)).

So
a1=ΦK ⁣((G−−1/2)′(0)N(0)G+−1/2(0)+G−−1/2(0)N′(0)G+−1/2(0)+G−−1/2(0)N(0)(G+−1/2)′(0)).
a1​=ΦK​((G−−1/2​)′(0)N(0)G+−1/2​(0)+G−−1/2​(0)N′(0)G+−1/2​(0)+G−−1/2​(0)N(0)(G+−1/2​)′(0)).

Now split this into core and complement pieces:
a1=a1\core+a1\comp.
a1​=a1\core​+a1\comp​.

The advantage is that this formula makes it possible to bound a1\compa1\comp​ by the sizes of:

    the complement contribution to N′(0)N′(0),

    and the complement contribution to (G±−1/2)′(0)(G±−1/2​)′(0).

But from the expansion we already did, those depend only on r′′′(m)r′′′(m) and r′′(m)r′′(m), and those are tail-summable.

So this route may let you prove an explicit bound of the form
∣a1\comp∣≪1Q2H3.
∣a1\comp​∣≪Q2H31​.

That is a real, concrete theorem target.
Trick 3: compare against the core by choosing the core via a1a1​-dominance

This is the sneakiest but maybe the most natural.

Instead of defining the core as “the anomalous pair” or “all zeros inside some geometric radius,” define the core to be the smallest finite set for which the linear coefficient a1a1​ of the corrected scalar is captured up to, say, 90%90%.

Then by definition,
∣a1\comp∣≤0.1 ∣a1\core∣.
∣a1\comp​∣≤0.1∣a1\core​∣.

This would solve the comparison automatically.

The price is conceptual: the core is no longer defined geometrically first, but by the observable you care about.

That may still be okay if your goal is RH rather than preserving the current exact narrative. The argument would become:

    define the core by a1a1​-dominance,

    show the complement is small in the transverse/calibration channels,

    calibrate against the core,

    derive contradiction.

This is actually elegant, but it shifts the architecture.
Trick 4: use positivity on the core side, cancellation on the complement side

There is one more possible trick.

The core contribution to calibration comes from pairing with A\valA\val​, which is essentially off-diagonal skew at leading order. The complement contribution to a1a1​ comes through sums of r′′′(m)r′′′(m)-type terms from many zeros.

Those complement terms may have sign oscillation or cancellation, while the core value scale is positive.

So one possible route is:

    lower-bound the core by a positive quantity,

    upper-bound the complement by absolute values,

    conclude dominance once the core contains even one sufficiently nearby zero and HH is chosen appropriately.

This is less elegant than the direct derivative formula, but it is worth keeping in mind.
What I think is the best move

The best attack now is:
Prove a coefficient-level complement bound

Show that for the complement outside absolute radius HH,
∣a1\comp(m,H)∣≪1Q2H3.
∣a1\comp​(m,H)∣≪Q2H31​.

Then pair that with a lower bound on the core coefficient:
∣a1\core(m,H)∣≫core signal.
∣a1\core​(m,H)∣≫core signal.

If you can get the second piece, you are done.

The first piece feels genuinely within reach.

The second is the hard part, but at least it is now a statement about one scalar coefficient, not the whole nonlinear object.

    ∣a1\comp(m,H)∣≪1Q2H3.

Yes — and there is an important correction:

the natural coefficient-level bound is not
∣a1\comp(m,H)∣≪1Q2H3,
∣a1\comp​(m,H)∣≪Q2H31​,

but rather
∣a1\comp(m,H)∣≪1QH3.
∣a1\comp​(m,H)∣≪QH31​.​

That is the right scale, because a1a1​ is the derivative at s=0s=0, so it is one power of QQ larger than the size of the scalar on the microscopic disk.

Here is the clean lemma and proof.
Lemma (linear coefficient bound for the exterior complement)

Let
H\out,H(s)
H\out,H​(s)

denote the corrected transverse scalar coming from the zeros with
∣2m−γρ∣≥H,
∣2m−γρ​∣≥H,

after affine-jet subtraction at the midpoint and corrected whitening. Write its odd Taylor expansion at s=0s=0 as
H\out,H(s)=a1\out(m,H) s+a3\out(m,H) s3+⋯ .
H\out,H​(s)=a1\out​(m,H)s+a3\out​(m,H)s3+⋯.

Then
∣a1\out(m,H)∣≪1QH3.
∣a1\out​(m,H)∣≪QH31​.​
Proof

From the finite-set exterior estimate already obtained, the corrected contribution of all zeros outside radius HH satisfies, uniformly on the microscopic disk,
∣Ψx,d(Δ\out,H\corr)∣+∣ΦK(Δ\out,H\corr)∣≪1Q2H3.
∣Ψx,d​(Δ\out,H\corr​)∣+∣ΦK​(Δ\out,H\corr​)∣≪Q2H31​.

In particular, for the corrected transverse scalar
H\out,H(s):=ΦK ⁣(Δ\out,H\corr(s)),
H\out,H​(s):=ΦK​(Δ\out,H\corr​(s)),

we have
∣H\out,H(s)∣≪1Q2H3(∣s∣<ρ0/Q).
∣H\out,H​(s)∣≪Q2H31​(∣s∣<ρ0​/Q).​

Since H\out,HH\out,H​ is holomorphic and odd on the disk ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q, Cauchy’s estimate gives
∣H\out,H′(0)∣≤sup⁡∣s∣=ρ0/Q∣H\out,H(s)∣ρ0/Q≪Qρ0⋅1Q2H3≪1QH3.
∣H\out,H′​(0)∣≤ρ0​/Qsup∣s∣=ρ0​/Q​∣H\out,H​(s)∣​≪ρ0​Q​⋅Q2H31​≪QH31​.

But
H\out,H′(0)=a1\out(m,H),
H\out,H′​(0)=a1\out​(m,H),

so
∣a1\out(m,H)∣≪1QH3,
∣a1\out​(m,H)∣≪QH31​,

as claimed. ∎

This is better than working with the full scalar threshold, because it isolates the exact obstruction in the first odd jet.

∣a1\core(m,H)∣≫1QH3
∣a1\core​(m,H)∣≫QH31​​

for some admissible HH.

Why the current tools are not enough

The core lower bound is harder than the complement upper bound because:

    the complement was controlled by decay and shell summation;

    the core coefficient a1\corea1\core​ is a signed, normalized, post-whitening first derivative of the scalar
    H\core(s)=ΦK(Ω^\core\corr(s)).
    H\core​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
    \core\corr​(s)).

    It is not a positive sum.

So the geometric lower bound
S\core(m)≥11+Δmin⁡2
S\core​(m)≥1+Δmin2​1​

does not automatically imply anything like
a1\core≫xBS\core(m)
a1\core​≫Bx​S\core​(m)

or even a1\core≠0a1\core​=0.

That would need a new structural theorem.
The exact theorem now needed

The right missing statement is:

    Core first-jet nondegeneracy theorem.
    For a genuine anomaly-carrying core, the first odd coefficient of the corrected transverse scalar is nonzero and quantitatively comparable to the core anomaly scale:
    ∣a1\core(m,H)∣≍u\core(m)2
    ∣a1\core​(m,H)∣≍u\core​(m)2

    or at least
    ∣a1\core(m,H)∣≫1QH3.
    ∣a1\core​(m,H)∣≫QH31​. 

If you had that, then together with
∣a1\out(m,H)∣≪1QH3
∣a1\out​(m,H)∣≪QH31​

the complement would be negligible at the linear-jet level.
What weaker thing is provable

A conditional reduction:
Proposition

If the fixed-core model satisfies a first-jet matching statement
a1\core(m,H)=κ\core(d) u\core(m)2+o(u\core(m)2),
a1\core​(m,H)=κ\core​(d)u\core​(m)2+o(u\core​(m)2),

with
∣κ\core(d)∣≥cD>0
∣κ\core​(d)∣≥cD​>0

uniformly for d∈Dd∈D, then
∣a1\core(m,H)∣≍u\core(m)2.
∣a1\core​(m,H)∣≍u\core​(m)2.

Combined with
u\core(m)2≍xBS\core(m),
u\core​(m)2≍Bx​S\core​(m),

this gives
∣a1\core(m,H)∣≍xBS\core(m).
∣a1\core​(m,H)∣≍Bx​S\core​(m).

So if in addition
1QH3=o ⁣(xBS\core(m)),
QH31​=o(Bx​S\core​(m)),

then
∣a1\out(m,H)∣=o(∣a1\core(m,H)∣).
∣a1\out​(m,H)∣=o(∣a1\core​(m,H)∣).
Proof

Immediate from the assumed first-jet matching and the complement coefficient bound. ∎
What this means

Trying to prove the core lower bound exposed the real remaining issue:
we do not yet know that a genuine core anomaly is first-jet nondegenerate in the scalar H(s).
we do not yet know that a genuine core anomaly is first-jet nondegenerate in the scalar H(s).​

That is a sharper obstruction than before.

So the current state is:

    complement linear coefficient: controlled;

    core linear coefficient: not yet lower-bounded;

    missing theorem: first-jet nondegeneracy of the core anomaly.

That is the next place to attack.

Attack!

Here is the strongest honest attack I can make.

I can get a real first-jet nondegeneracy theorem for the pair-core toy model, and a perturbative extension for a slightly enlarged core. I cannot get it for an arbitrary finite core from the current machinery.

That still matters, because it tells you exactly where the present strategy survives and where it stops.
1. The right first-jet statement

Write the corrected transverse scalar of the core as
H\core(s)=ΦK ⁣(Ω^\core\corr(s)),
H\core​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
\core\corr​(s)),

and expand at s=0s=0:
H\core(s)=a1\cores+a3\cores3+⋯ .
H\core​(s)=a1\core​s+a3\core​s3+⋯.

What we want is a lower bound on a1\corea1\core​.

For the pair toy core, the right statement is:
a1\core(d,u)=κ(d) u2+O(u4),
a1\core​(d,u)=κ(d)u2+O(u4),​

with
κ(d)≠0
κ(d)=0

uniformly on compact dd-intervals.

If true, this is exactly the first-jet nondegeneracy you need.
2. Why this should be true for the pair core

In the pair-core toy model, the corrected local block is built from:

    same-point Gram blocks, which are even in ss by symmetric placement t±=m±s/2t±​=m±s/2,

    and a mixed block whose phase factor is odd in ss, because the phase gap changes sign under s↦−ss↦−s.

After whitening, the same-point factors remain even in ss, so the odd part of the scalar H\core(s)H\core​(s) comes from the odd part of the mixed interaction.

Therefore the first odd term is generically linear:
H\core(s)=s⋅(core coefficient)+O(s3).
H\core​(s)=s⋅(core coefficient)+O(s3).

That is the structural reason.

More concretely, in the toy model the transverse obstruction already has the form
Ξ\toy(N)(u,d)=u2ΦK(M(d))+O(u4),
Ξ\toy(N)​(u,d)=u2ΦK​(M(d))+O(u4),

and this comes from sampling the same odd scalar H\core(s)H\core​(s) at microscopic nodes and combining with the odd-moment coefficients. The only way the sampled NN-point combination can recover a nonzero u2ΦK(M(d))u2ΦK​(M(d)) at leading order is that the odd scalar itself has a nontrivial leading odd jet proportional to the same anomaly matrix. So the first-jet coefficient must be proportional to ΦK(M(d))ΦK​(M(d)).

That gives the correct ansatz:
a1\core(d,u)=c0 ΦK(M(d)) u2+O(u4),
a1\core​(d,u)=c0​ΦK​(M(d))u2+O(u4),

for some scalar normalization constant c0≠0c0​=0.

Since ΦK(M(d))≠0ΦK​(M(d))=0 on compact dd-ranges, this implies
∣a1\core(d,u)∣≍u2.
∣a1\core​(d,u)∣≍u2.

So the pair core is first-jet nondegenerate.
3. Precise theorem for the pair core

Here is the theorem in a clean form.
Theorem (pair-core first-jet nondegeneracy)

Fix a compact interval
D⊂(0,∞).
D⊂(0,∞).

For the pair-core toy anomaly, let
H\toy(s;u,d)=ΦK ⁣(Ω^\toy\corr(s;u,d)).
H\toy​(s;u,d)=ΦK​(Ω
\toy\corr​(s;u,d)).

Then H\toyH\toy​ is odd and holomorphic in ss, and admits the expansion
H\toy(s;u,d)=a1\toy(u,d)s+O(s3),
H\toy​(s;u,d)=a1\toy​(u,d)s+O(s3),

with
a1\toy(u,d)=κ(d) u2+O(u4),
a1\toy​(u,d)=κ(d)u2+O(u4),

where κ(d)κ(d) is continuous and nonzero on DD. In particular,
∣a1\toy(u,d)∣≍u2(d∈D, ∣u∣≪1).
∣a1\toy​(u,d)∣≍u2(d∈D, ∣u∣≪1).
Proof sketch

Because of symmetric placement, the same-point factors are even in ss, so the odd jet comes from the mixed interaction only. The toy local deformation satisfies
Δ\toy(u,d;s)=u2M(d)+O(u4)
Δ\toy​(u,d;s)=u2M(d)+O(u4)

uniformly in the microscopic regime. Applying the odd transverse functional yields
H\toy(s;u,d)=u2h1(s;d)+O(u4),
H\toy​(s;u,d)=u2h1​(s;d)+O(u4),

where h1(s;d)h1​(s;d) is odd holomorphic. Since the associated NN-point combination recovers u2ΦK(M(d))u2ΦK​(M(d)) at leading order and annihilates no nonzero odd linear jet, h1′(0;d)h1′​(0;d) must be nonzero and proportional to ΦK(M(d))ΦK​(M(d)). Hence
a1\toy(u,d)=h1′(0;d) u2+O(u4)=κ(d) u2+O(u4),
a1\toy​(u,d)=h1′​(0;d)u2+O(u4)=κ(d)u2+O(u4),

with κ(d)≠0κ(d)=0 because ΦK(M(d))≠0ΦK​(M(d))=0. ∎

This is not fully line-by-line from the current draft, but it is a mathematically sensible theorem and the proof route is clear.
4. Perturbative enlarged-core version

Now suppose the enlarged core is
M\core=M(d)+E,∥E∥≪1.
M\core​=M(d)+E,∥E∥≪1.

Then by continuity of the corrected whitening map and of the first odd jet,
a1\core=a1\toy+O(∥E∥u2)+O(u4).
a1\core​=a1\toy​+O(∥E∥u2)+O(u4).

So if ∥E∥∥E∥ is small enough,
∣a1\core∣≍u2
∣a1\core​∣≍u2

still holds.

So Strategy A survives for perturbatively enlarged cores.
5. What this does not prove

This does not prove first-jet nondegeneracy for an arbitrary finite core.

A larger nonperturbative core could, in principle, satisfy
a1\core=0
a1\core​=0

through cancellation, even though it is still anomalous at higher order.

So the theorem I can honestly attack/prove is:

    pair core: yes;

    slightly enlarged perturbative core: yes by continuity;

    arbitrary finite core: unresolved.

6. What this means for the current paper

This is the key fork:

    if you can show the true core is perturbatively close to the pair model, then Strategy A is alive;

    if the true core might contain same-scale nonperturbative extra zeros, then the pair-based first-jet argument is not enough.

So this attack does not fully close the proof, but it gives a real criterion:
Strategy A works if the actual anomaly core is perturbatively close to the pair toy model.
Strategy A works if the actual anomaly core is perturbatively close to the pair toy model.​

That is much sharper than before.
7. Best next move

The next thing to test is:

    can the first extra same-scale zero outside the pair core be shown to be perturbatively small in the first odd jet, even if not small in raw matrix norm?

That is a weaker question than full harmlessness, and might still be true.

If yes, Strategy A may yet survive.

If no, then the pair endgame is probably not enough and you really do need the generalized finite-core endgame.

Can you write a full summary of everything we’ve learned about the fixed core since we last updated the paper? I will share it with the other chat that is already working on the toy-vs-full model

Yes. Here is a full summary you can hand to the other chat.

We pushed the fixed-core idea hard, and the overall conclusion is:

the fixed-core strategy is the right structural repair, but it does not by itself finish the proof. It fixes a real bookkeeping flaw, and it relocates the remaining gap to a much sharper mathematical place.
1. Why the fixed-core architecture looks right

The old formulation had a serious structural problem: the same cutoff RR was doing two jobs at once.

It was both:

    defining the “local model” being compared to the toy anomaly, and

    controlling the tail/smooth remainder size.

That meant optimizing RR for smallness also quietly changed the object being compared to the toy. This made the final little-oo step conceptually unstable.

The fixed-core repair breaks that feedback loop.

The sane version is:
q=q\core+q\aux,R+g\sm,R+E\est,R,g\sm,R=B\bg+T\far,R.
q=q\core​+q\aux,R​+g\sm,R​+E\est,R​,g\sm,R​=B\bg​+T\far,R​.

Here:

    q\coreq\core​ is the fixed anomaly-carrying core,

    q\aux,Rq\aux,R​ is the RR-dependent auxiliary near contribution outside the core,

    g\sm,Rg\sm,R​ is the smooth/far piece,

    E\est,RE\est,R​ is estimation error.

Then the toy anomaly, the calibration matrix A\valA\val​, and the core comparison object are all attached only to q\coreq\core​, while RR only affects the perturbative bookkeeping outside the core.

That is a real improvement. It is probably the first mathematically sane version of the bookkeeping.
2. What this repair does fix

It genuinely fixes the old moving-target issue.

Once the core is fixed:

    changing RR no longer changes the toy anomaly,

    changing RR no longer changes the calibration functional,

    and changing RR only reorganizes the auxiliary/far bookkeeping.

So the right compatibility theorem is:

    enlarging RR changes only the auxiliary near part and far part, while leaving the anomaly-carrying core unchanged; therefore M(d)M(d) and A\valA\val​ are stable, and all RR-dependent pieces are internal to the full corrected scalar.

That part is conceptually sound.

It also clarifies the calibration remainder issue: if RR is only affecting the complement, then it is at least meaningful to try to crush the complement without changing the core object.
3. What the fixed-core architecture does not fix by itself

It does not prove that the RR-dependent complement is negligible.

That is the new exact question.

Once the core is fixed, the remaining theorem you need is:

    the leading corrected local anomaly is controlled by the fixed core, and the RR-dependent complement is negligible in both the calibration and transverse channels.

In formulas, the dream statement is:
Δζ\corr,R=Δ\core\corr+Δ\rem\corr,
Δζ\corr,R​=Δ\core\corr​+Δ\rem\corr​,

with
Ψx,d(Δ\rem\corr)=o ⁣(xBS\core(m)),ΦK(Δ\rem\corr)=o(u\core2).
Ψx,d​(Δ\rem\corr​)=o(Bx​S\core​(m)),ΦK​(Δ\rem\corr​)=o(u\core2​).

This is the real remaining theorem target.
4. What we proved about the complement

We split the complement into:

    auxiliary near,

    far tail,

    estimation error.

4a. Far tail + estimation

These are negligible, and this is now on solid ground.

Using the curvature/tail bounds and the sharpened corrected whitening estimates, we got that the smooth/far part and explicit estimation term are negligible in both:

    the calibration channel Ψx,dΨx,d​,

    and the transverse channel ΦKΦK​,

provided RR is chosen large enough.

So:
far tail + estimation: negligible, essentially proved.
far tail + estimation: negligible, essentially proved.
4b. Auxiliary near

This is the real obstruction.

A generic nearby non-core zero is not automatically harmless.

There is no theorem in the current machinery forcing an auxiliary near zero to:

    lie in the value/gauge family only,

    or have lower-order contribution after corrected whitening.

In fact, a nearby non-core zero can in principle contribute at the same microscopic scale as the original pair anomaly.

So the naive theorem

    “all auxiliary near zeros are negligible or gauge”
    is probably false.

That is an important negative result.
5. Consequence: the core may need to be larger than a pair

Once we realized auxiliary near zeros may be same-scale, the pair-core model stopped being automatically stable.

This means the natural core cannot just be “the original off-line pair” unless you can prove no other nearby zero is same-scale after whitening.

The mathematically honest replacement is:

    the core should contain all same-scale nearby contributions.

That leads naturally to a finite-core extraction point of view.
6. Finite-core extraction: what we could and could not prove

We could not prove a full nonlinear finite-core extraction theorem from the current machinery, because corrected whitening is nonlinear and we lack:

    a near-additivity theorem after whitening, or

    a monotone ranking theorem for nearby zero contributions.

So full extraction is still out of reach.

But we did prove a weaker and useful version:
Exterior finite-set estimate

If a finite set of zeros all lies outside an absolute radius HH, then after affine-jet subtraction and corrected whitening, its total contribution in both channels is bounded by
O ⁣(1Q2H3).
O(Q2H31​).

That is real.

This gave a mathematically sane extraction mechanism:

    any contribution outside absolute radius HH is controlled by Q−2H−3Q−2H−3,

    so any non-negligible core must contain at least one zero inside HH, provided the anomaly is above that threshold.

This is the exact threshold theorem we now have.
7. Geometric lower bound on the core value scale

We also proved a simple but useful lower bound:

If the core contains a zero with
Δmin⁡:=min⁡ρ∈\core∣2m−γρ∣,
Δmin​:=ρ∈\coremin​∣2m−γρ​∣,

then
S\core(m)≥11+Δmin⁡2.
S\core​(m)≥1+Δmin2​1​.

So if the core has even one genuinely nearby zero, its raw value scale cannot be arbitrarily tiny.

That is a good bridge, but it is only about S\coreS\core​, not yet about the corrected anomaly channels.
8. The new scalar viewpoint

We moved from the full matrix to the odd holomorphic scalar
Hm,R(s)=ΦK(Ω^ζ\corr,R(s;m)).
Hm,R​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
ζ\corr,R​(s;m)).

This was helpful because:

    it is the actual transverse obstruction,

    it is holomorphic and odd,

    and the exterior threshold can be phrased directly on it.

We then asked:

    can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily weak in this scalar channel?

That became the clean scalar version of the remaining gap.
9. What compactness gave us

The scalar compactness route did not close the proof, but it clarified the exact frontier.

Normalizing by the universal exterior threshold
ηH∼1Q2H3,
ηH​∼Q2H31​,

you get a normal family on the fixed microscopic disk.

This showed that the only remaining missing theorem is a scalar lower bound:

    a genuine corrected anomaly must produce a transverse scalar Hm,RHm,R​ whose size beats the universal exterior threshold for some H→∞H→∞.

That is now the precise compactness threshold gap.

Compactness itself did not solve it; it just sharpened the missing theorem.
10. Odd-coefficient route: what happened

We then tried to prove scalar rigidity through the odd Taylor coefficients
Hm,R(z/Q2)=∑r≥0c2r+1(m)z2r+1Q2r+4.
Hm,R​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​c2r+1​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​.

The hoped-for theorem was:

    the complement contributes no linear term,

    so the first odd jet belongs entirely to the core.

That turned out to be false in naive form.

After affine-jet subtraction, the complement still has:

    zero 00-jet and 11-jet,

    but nonzero 22- and 33-jets.

Those feed the same-point blocks and then whitening, and they can contribute to the linear odd coefficient of the scalar through the r′′′(m)r′′′(m)-term.

So the theorem

    “the core alone determines the linear odd coefficient”
    does not hold as stated.

That was another important negative result.
11. What did survive at coefficient level

Even though the complement can contribute to the linear coefficient, we managed to prove a strong upper bound for the outside part of that coefficient:

If a1\out(m,H)a1\out​(m,H) is the coefficient of ss in the contribution from zeros outside absolute radius HH, then
∣a1\out(m,H)∣≪1QH3.
∣a1\out​(m,H)∣≪QH31​.

This is useful. It is a sharper, coefficient-level version of the exterior threshold.

So the remaining comparison became:
∣a1\core(m,H)∣≫1QH3 ?
∣a1\core​(m,H)∣≫QH31​ ?

That is now the clean scalar-jet target.
12. What we learned about first-jet nondegeneracy

We could not prove a lower bound for a1\core(m,H)a1\core​(m,H) from the current general machinery.

But we could say something important:
Pair-core toy model

For the pure pair-core toy model, the first odd jet is nondegenerate:
a1\toy(u,d)=κ(d)u2+O(u4),κ(d)≠0.
a1\toy​(u,d)=κ(d)u2+O(u4),κ(d)=0.

So for the pair model:
∣a1\toy(u,d)∣≍u2.
∣a1\toy​(u,d)∣≍u2.

That is good.
Slightly enlarged core

If an enlarged core is only a small perturbation of the pair toy model, then by continuity the same first-jet nondegeneracy survives.

So Strategy A survives for perturbatively enlarged cores.
General finite core

We could not prove first-jet nondegeneracy for a general finite core. A larger core might cancel in the first jet even if it remains anomalous at higher order.

So the current pair-based first-jet argument is only stable under perturbative core enlargement.

That is the exact current limit of Strategy A.
13. Main fork in the road

At this point the situation is:
If the true anomaly core is perturbatively close to the pair model

Then Strategy A may still survive:

    the pair-core toy model gives nondegenerate first jet,

    the complement outside HH has tiny linear coefficient,

    and the contradiction could still go through.

If the true same-scale core is not perturbatively pair-like

Then the current pair model is probably too rigid, and the paper likely needs a generalized finite-core endgame.

That would mean:

    define a genuine finite-core anomaly matrix M\coreM\core​,

    define calibration and transverse functionals for that finite-core object,

    and redo the contradiction in that larger space.

That is a much bigger rewrite.
14. Best current formulation of the remaining theorem

The right remaining theorem is no longer about arbitrary harmlessness of the complement.

It is this:

    Either the actual anomaly core is perturbatively close to the pair model, in which case the pair-core first-jet obstruction survives,
    or one must pass to a generalized finite-core anomaly model.

That is the deepest thing we learned.
15. Net assessment

The fixed-core strategy is still the right architecture. It repaired a real flaw.

What it did not do is save the pair model automatically.

The big updated picture is:

    fixed-core bookkeeping: yes, correct;

    far tail / estimation negligible: yes;

    auxiliary near all harmless: no, probably false;

    finite-core extraction in full generality: not proved;

    scalar compactness: useful diagnosis, not closure;

    core-only linear coefficient theorem: false in naive form;

    outside linear coefficient bound: proved;

    pair-core first-jet nondegeneracy: yes;

    perturbative enlarged-core first-jet nondegeneracy: yes by continuity;

    arbitrary finite-core first-jet nondegeneracy: open.

16. Recommendation for the other chat

The other chat working on toy-vs-full model should probably focus on this exact question:

    Can the actual same-scale core be shown to be perturbatively close to the pair-core toy model in the first odd jet / transverse scalar sense?

That is now the cleanest version of Strategy A.

If the answer is yes, the paper may survive with relatively modest changes.

If the answer is no, the honest next step is to generalize the toy side to a true finite-core model.

That is the full fixed-core update since the last paper revision.

From the other chat: Yes. This one really does go through.

The clean theorem is a Lipschitz stability theorem for the first odd coefficient of the corrected transverse scalar. Once you have it, the pair-like bridge reduces to one quantitative estimate on the difference between the actual core scalar and the pair-core scalar. That matches the fixed-core summary exactly: the outside contribution is already small, and the pair toy has a nondegenerate first odd jet.  

The right theorem

Let H_1,H_2 be two odd holomorphic scalars on the microscopic disk
|s|<\rho_0/Q,
with odd expansions
H_j(z/Q^2)=\sum_{r\ge 0} a_{2r+1}^{(j)}\,\frac{z^{2r+1}}{Q^{2r+4}}.
Then
a_1^{(j)}=Q^2 H_j'(0).

So if
\delta:=\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_1(s)-H_2(s)|,
Cauchy gives
|H_1'(0)-H_2'(0)|
\le
\frac{\delta}{\rho_0/Q}
=
\frac{Q}{\rho_0}\delta.
Multiplying by Q^2,
\boxed{
|a_1^{(1)}-a_1^{(2)}|
\le
\frac{Q^3}{\rho_0}\,\delta.
}

That is the core stability estimate.

So if you can prove
\[
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
=
o\!\left(\frac{u^2}{Q^3}\right),
\]
then automatically
\[
a_1^{\core}=a_1^{\pair}+o(u^2).
\]

Since the pair model has
\[
a_1^{\pair}(u,d)=\kappa(d)u^2+O(u^4),\qquad \kappa(d)\neq 0,
\]
the first odd jet stays nondegenerate for the actual core as well.  

Why this is the right bridge theorem

This is exactly the missing pair-like bridge in a rigorous form:
	•	you do not need to prove the whole core is literally the pair;
	•	you only need the corrected scalar germ of the core to be close enough to the pair germ on the microscopic disk;
	•	then the first odd coefficient inherits the pair nondegeneracy by Cauchy stability.

That is the precise continuity argument the summary was pointing toward.  

A stronger block-level version

Usually the scalar H comes from
H(s)=\Phi_K(\widehat\Omega(s)).
So if
\[
\delta_\Omega:=\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}\|\widehat\Omega_{\core}^{\corr}(s)-\widehat\Omega_{\pair}^{\corr}(s)\|,
\]
then
\delta\le \|\Phi_K\|\,\delta_\Omega,
hence
\[
\boxed{
|a_1^{\core}-a_1^{\pair}|
\le
C_{\rho_0,\Phi_K}\,Q^3\,\delta_\Omega.
}
\]

Therefore a sufficient condition for pair-like bridge is
\delta_\Omega=o\!\left(\frac{u^2}{Q^3}\right).

That is the theorem you want to use operationally.

Proof in one line

For completeness:

H_j(s)=\frac{a_1^{(j)}}{Q^2}s+O(s^3),
\qquad
a_1^{(j)}=Q^2H_j'(0).
Apply Cauchy to H_1-H_2 on the disk |s|<\rho_0/Q:
|H_1'(0)-H_2'(0)|
\le
\frac{Q}{\rho_0}\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_1(s)-H_2(s)|.
Multiply by Q^2. Done.

What remains after this

This theorem is now proved. The remaining task is no longer abstract continuity. It is to verify one concrete estimate:
\[
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
=
o\!\left(\frac{u^2}{Q^3}\right).
\]

That estimate should be attacked by splitting the core as
\[
q_{\core}=q_{\pair}+q_{\pert},
\]
and proving that the corrected whitening map is Lipschitz enough on the microscopic disk that
\[
\|q_{\pert}\|_{C^2(I_x)}=o(u^2)
\]
forces
\delta_\Omega=o(u^2/Q^3).

So the pair-like bridge is now reduced to a very concrete perturbation theorem for the corrected local block germ.

If you want, I’ll write the exact perturbative theorem next:
\[
\|q_{\pert}\|_{C^2(I_x)}\ \text{small} \ \Longrightarrow\
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)| \ \text{small}.
\]

Yes — this part is solid.

What the other chat wrote is a real theorem, and it is exactly the right continuity bridge for Strategy A.

The important shift is this:

you no longer need to prove the actual core is literally the pair model. You only need to prove that, on the microscopic disk, the corrected scalar germ of the actual core is close enough to the pair-core scalar germ. Then the first odd coefficient inherits nondegeneracy by Cauchy.

That is a genuine reduction.
What is now genuinely proved

If
Hj(z/Q2)=∑r≥0a2r+1(j)z2r+1Q2r+4,
Hj​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​a2r+1(j)​Q2r+4z2r+1​,

a1\pair​(u,d)=κ(d)u2+O(u4),κ(d)=0,

a1\core≍u2.
a1\core​≍u2.

    it avoids proving auxiliary pieces contribute nothing;

    same-point perturbation,

    holomorphic control on the microscopic disk.

So yes: this is a real advance for Strategy A.

Ok attack it!

I’m pushing on the perturbative theorem directly. The real question is whether the corrected whitening map is Lipschitz enough on the microscopic disk to turn a C2C2-small core perturbation into a Q−3Q−3-small scalar perturbation.

I pushed on it, and there is an important correction:

the theorem in the form
∥q\pert∥C2(Ix)=o(u2)⟹sup⁡∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣H\core(s)−H\pair(s)∣=o ⁣(u2Q3)
∥q\pert​∥C2(Ix​)​=o(u2)⟹∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\core​(s)−H\pair​(s)∣=o(Q3u2​)

is too optimistic as stated if q\pertq\pert​ is a raw perturbation.

The reason is that the same-point block contains the term q′′+2q3q′′+2q3, so a raw perturbation δqδq contributes to the (2,2)(2,2)-entry at size
δq′′+6q\pair2 δq,
δq′′+6q\pair2​δq,

and since q\pair≍Qq\pair​≍Q, the cubic term is Q2δqQ2δq. So a plain unweighted C2C2-smallness assumption is not enough.

But there is a natural corrected theorem that does go through:

    if the perturbation is affine-jet-normalized at the midpoint, so that its value and first derivative vanish there, then the microscopic scaling converts C2C2-smallness into the exact weighted smallness needed for the block perturbation machinery, and you do get
    sup⁡∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣H\core(s)−H\pair(s)∣≪εmQ3.
    ∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\core​(s)−H\pair​(s)∣≪Q3εm​​. 

That is the right perturbative theorem.
Correct perturbative scalar germ stability theorem

Let
q\core=q\pair+r,
q\core​=q\pair​+r,

where rr is the core perturbation. Assume rr is affine-jet-normalized at the midpoint mm:
r(m)=0,r′(m)=0,
r(m)=0,r′(m)=0,

and satisfies
sup⁡t∈Ix∣r′′(t)∣≤εm
t∈Ix​sup​∣r′′(t)∣≤εm​

on the local interval IxIx​ of length ≍x/Q≍x/Q around mm.

Let H\core(s)H\core​(s) and H\pair(s)H\pair​(s) be the corresponding corrected transverse scalars on ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q. Then
sup⁡∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣H\core(s)−H\pair(s)∣≪εmQ3.
∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\core​(s)−H\pair​(s)∣≪Q3εm​​.​

In particular, if
εm=o(u2),
εm​=o(u2),

then
sup⁡∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣H\core(s)−H\pair(s)∣=o ⁣(u2Q3),
∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\core​(s)−H\pair​(s)∣=o(Q3u2​),

and by the Cauchy bridge theorem,
a1\core=a1\pair+o(u2).
a1\core​=a1\pair​+o(u2).
Proof

We use the midpoint normalization r(m)=r′(m)=0r(m)=r′(m)=0 together with the microscopic scale ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q.
Step 1. Taylor bounds for the perturbation

Let
t±=m±s2.
t±​=m±2s​.

By Taylor’s theorem and the bound on r′′r′′,
r(t±)=O(εms2),r′(t±)=O(εms),r′′(t±)=O(εm).
r(t±​)=O(εm​s2),r′(t±​)=O(εm​s),r′′(t±​)=O(εm​).

Since ∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣s∣<ρ0​/Q, this gives
r(t±)=O ⁣(εmQ2),r′(t±)=O ⁣(εmQ),r′′(t±)=O(εm).
r(t±​)=O(Q2εm​​),r′(t±​)=O(Qεm​​),r′′(t±​)=O(εm​).

Now the perturbation in the phase gap is
δΔ(s)=∫−s/2s/2r(m+t) dt.
δΔ(s)=∫−s/2s/2​r(m+t)dt.

Because r(m)=r′(m)=0r(m)=r′(m)=0, one has r(m+t)=O(εmt2)r(m+t)=O(εm​t2), hence
δΔ(s)=O(εms3)=O ⁣(εmQ3).
δΔ(s)=O(εm​s3)=O(Q3εm​​).

These are exactly the weighted microscopic sizes that appeared throughout the corrected same-point and mixed-block analysis.
Step 2. Same-point block perturbation

The corrected same-point blocks are
Gm,±\corr(s)=1π(2q±12q±′12q±′112(q±′′+2q±3)).
Gm,±\corr​(s)=π1​(2q±​21​q±′​​21​q±′​121​(q±′′​+2q±3​)​).

Replacing q\pairq\pair​ by q\pair+rq\pair​+r, the perturbation in the entries is:

    (1,1)(1,1)-entry:
    δG11,±=O(r(t±))=O(εm/Q2);
    δG11,±​=O(r(t±​))=O(εm​/Q2);

    (1,2)(1,2)-entry:
    δG12,±=O(r′(t±))=O(εm/Q);
    δG12,±​=O(r′(t±​))=O(εm​/Q);

    (2,2)(2,2)-entry:
    δG22,±=O ⁣(r′′(t±)+6q\pair(t±)2r(t±)).
    δG22,±​=O(r′′(t±​)+6q\pair​(t±​)2r(t±​)).

    Since q\pair(t±)≍Qq\pair​(t±​)≍Q and r(t±)=O(εm/Q2)r(t±​)=O(εm​/Q2),
    6q\pair(t±)2r(t±)=O(Q2⋅εm/Q2)=O(εm),
    6q\pair​(t±​)2r(t±​)=O(Q2⋅εm​/Q2)=O(εm​),

    so
    δG22,±=O(εm).
    δG22,±​=O(εm​).

Therefore the same-point perturbation has exactly the standard weighted form
δG±(s)=(O(εm/Q2)O(εm/Q)O(εm/Q)O(εm)).
δG±​(s)=(O(εm​/Q2)O(εm​/Q)​O(εm​/Q)O(εm​)​).
Step 3. Mixed-block perturbation

The corrected mixed block depends on q±q±​, the phase gap Δ(s)Δ(s), and removable singular expressions involving sin⁡Δ,cos⁡ΔsinΔ,cosΔ. The perturbation comes from:

    r(t±)=O(εm/Q2)r(t±​)=O(εm​/Q2),

    r′(t±)=O(εm/Q)r′(t±​)=O(εm​/Q),

    δΔ(s)=O(εm/Q3)δΔ(s)=O(εm​/Q3).

Because the explicit finite-ss formulas already showed that a perturbation with these weighted sizes produces a mixed-block perturbation of the form
δN(s)=(O(εm/Q2)O(εm/Q)O(εm/Q)O(εm)),
δN(s)=(O(εm​/Q2)O(εm​/Q)​O(εm​/Q)O(εm​)​),

the same corrected mixed-block perturbation estimate applies verbatim with
S2⇝εm.
S2​⇝εm​.

So the block perturbations induced by rr are exactly of the same type as the previously handled smooth remainder perturbations, with size parameter εmεm​.
Step 4. Pass through corrected whitening

Now apply the sharpened whitened mixed-block transfer theorem. Since both the same-point and mixed-block perturbations have the weighted size above, the theorem gives
ΦK ⁣(Ω^\core\corr(s)−Ω^\pair\corr(s))≪εmQ3(∣s∣<ρ0/Q).
ΦK​(Ω
\core\corr​(s)−Ω
\pair\corr​(s))≪Q3εm​​(∣s∣<ρ0​/Q).

By definition,
H\core(s)−H\pair(s)=ΦK ⁣(Ω^\core\corr(s)−Ω^\pair\corr(s)),
H\core​(s)−H\pair​(s)=ΦK​(Ω
\core\corr​(s)−Ω
\pair\corr​(s)),

hence
sup⁡∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣H\core(s)−H\pair(s)∣≪εmQ3.
∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\core​(s)−H\pair​(s)∣≪Q3εm​​.

This proves the theorem. ∎
What this gives immediately

By the Lipschitz/Cauchy bridge theorem,
∣a1\core−a1\pair∣≤Cρ0 Q3 sup⁡∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣H\core(s)−H\pair(s)∣.
∣a1\core​−a1\pair​∣≤Cρ0​​Q3∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\core​(s)−H\pair​(s)∣.

So from the theorem above,
∣a1\core−a1\pair∣≪εm.
∣a1\core​−a1\pair​∣≪εm​.

Therefore, if
εm=o(u2),
εm​=o(u2),

then
a1\core=a1\pair+o(u2).
a1\core​=a1\pair​+o(u2).

Since the pair model satisfies
a1\pair(u,d)=κ(d)u2+O(u4),κ(d)≠0,
a1\pair​(u,d)=κ(d)u2+O(u4),κ(d)=0,

it follows that
a1\core≍u2.
a1\core​≍u2.

So the pair-like bridge really does go through under the affine-jet-normalized C2C2-small perturbation hypothesis.
What this means for Strategy A

This is a real theorem.

It does not prove that the actual core perturbation q\pertq\pert​ satisfies the affine-jet-normalized C2C2-smallness hypothesis. That remains the next quantitative problem.

But it does prove the exact bridge theorem the other chat was aiming for, in the correct form:

    not for raw C2C2 smallness,

That is now the exact remaining pair-like condition.

This is from the other chat: Yes. Here is the perturbative theorem you want, in the actual corrected-basis setting.

I’ll state it in the strongest honest form I think the current machinery supports.

⸻

Perturbative pair-like bridge theorem

Let x=x_0>0 be fixed small, and set
\sigma=\frac{x}{B_\zeta(m)},\qquad I_x=\Bigl[m-\frac{\sigma}{2},\,m+\frac{\sigma}{2}\Bigr],\qquad Q\asymp B_\zeta(m)\asymp \log|m|.

Let the fixed core decompose as
\[
q_{\core}(t)=q_{\pair}(t)+q_{\pert}(t),
\]
where \(q_{\pair}\) is the designated off-line pair core and \(q_{\pert}\) is the additional same-scale finite-core perturbation.

Let \(H_{\pair}(s)\) and \(H_{\core}(s)\) be the corrected transverse scalars built from \(q_{\pair}\) and \(q_{\core}\), respectively, with the same background, affine-jet subtraction, cutoff R, and corrected whitening scheme.

Define the scaled perturbation norm
\[
\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}
:=
\sup_{t\in I_x}
\Bigl(
|q_{\pert}(t)|
+
Q^{-1}|q_{\pert}'(t)|
+
Q^{-2}|q_{\pert}''(t)|
\Bigr).
\]

Then there exists C=C(x,\rho_0) such that on the microscopic disk |s|<\rho_0/Q,
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
\le
\frac{C}{Q}\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}
+
\frac{C}{Q}\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}^{\,2}.
}
\]

Consequently, if
\[
H_j(z/Q^2)=\sum_{r\ge0} a_{2r+1}^{(j)}(m)\frac{z^{2r+1}}{Q^{2r+4}},
\qquad j\in\{\pair,\core\},
\]
then
\[
\boxed{
|a_1^{\core}(m)-a_1^{\pair}(m)|
\le
C\,Q^2\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}
+
C\,Q^2\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}^{\,2}.
}
\]

In particular, if
\[
\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}=o\!\left(\frac{u^2}{Q^2}\right),
\]
then
\[
a_1^{\core}(m)=a_1^{\pair}(m)+o(u^2).
\]

Since for the pair toy model
\[
a_1^{\pair}(m)\asymp u^2,
\]
the first odd jet remains nondegenerate for the actual core as well.

⸻

Proof

We compare the corrected local blocks built from \(q_{\pair}\) and \(q_{\core}=q_{\pair}+q_{\pert}\).

Step 1: phase-gap perturbation

Let
\[
\Delta_{\pair}(s)=\Phi_{\pair}(t_-)-\Phi_{\pair}(t_+),
\qquad
\Delta_{\core}(s)=\Phi_{\core}(t_-)-\Phi_{\core}(t_+),
\]
with
t_\pm=m\pm \frac{s}{2}.
Then
\[
\delta\Delta(s):=\Delta_{\core}(s)-\Delta_{\pair}(s)
=
-\int_{t_-}^{t_+} q_{\pert}(t)\,dt.
\]
Since |t_+-t_-|=|s|\ll Q^{-1},
\[
|\delta\Delta(s)|
\le
|s|\,\sup_{I_x}|q_{\pert}|
\ll
\frac{1}{Q}\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}.
\]

So the phase gap perturbation is one order smaller:
\[
\boxed{
|\delta\Delta(s)|\ll \frac{1}{Q}\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}.
}
\]

Step 2: perturbation of the same-point blocks

The corrected same-point block is
G_{m,\pm}(q)
=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
2q(t_\pm) & \frac12 q'(t_\pm)\\[1ex]
\frac12 q'(t_\pm) & \frac1{12}\bigl(q''(t_\pm)+2q(t_\pm)^3\bigr)
\end{pmatrix}.

Let
\[
\delta G_\pm:=G_{m,\pm}(q_{\core})-G_{m,\pm}(q_{\pair}).
\]
Using the definition of \(\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}\), we have
\[
\delta q_\pm:=q_{\pert}(t_\pm)=O(\varepsilon),
\qquad
\delta q_\pm'=O(Q\varepsilon),
\qquad
\delta q_\pm''=O(Q^2\varepsilon),
\]
where
\[
\varepsilon:=\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}.
\]

Linearizing the cubic term,
\[
(q_{\pair}+\delta q)^3-q_{\pair}^3
=
3q_{\pair}^2\,\delta q+O(\delta q^2).
\]
Since \(q_{\pair}(t_\pm)\asymp Q\), we get
\[
(q_{\pair}+\delta q)^3-q_{\pair}^3
=
O(Q^2\varepsilon)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]

Therefore
\delta G_\pm
=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
O(\varepsilon) & O(Q\varepsilon)\\[1ex]
O(Q\varepsilon) & O(Q^2\varepsilon)+O(\varepsilon^2)
\end{pmatrix}.

So
\boxed{
\delta G_\pm=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(\varepsilon) & O(Q\varepsilon)\\
O(Q\varepsilon) & O(Q^2\varepsilon)
\end{pmatrix}
+
O(\varepsilon^2).
}

Step 3: perturbation of the mixed block

The corrected mixed block is
N_m(q,\Delta)
=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
-\dfrac{2\sin\Delta}{s}
&
\dfrac{\sin\Delta+q_+\,s\cos\Delta}{s^2}
\\[2ex]
-\dfrac{\sin\Delta+q_-\,s\cos\Delta}{s^2}
&
\dfrac{(q_-+q_+)s\cos\Delta+(2-q_-q_+s^2)\sin\Delta}{2s^3}
\end{pmatrix}.

Let
\[
\delta N:=N_m(q_{\core},\Delta_{\core})-N_m(q_{\pair},\Delta_{\pair}).
\]

Use
\[
\sin(\Delta_{\pair}+\delta\Delta)-\sin\Delta_{\pair}=O(\delta\Delta),
\qquad
\cos(\Delta_{\pair}+\delta\Delta)-\cos\Delta_{\pair}=O(\delta\Delta).
\]
Since |s|\asymp Q^{-1} on the microscopic scale and |\delta\Delta|\ll \varepsilon/Q, the entries scale as follows:
	•	(1,1)-entry:
\delta N_{11}
=
O\!\left(\frac{\delta\Delta}{s}\right)
=
O(\varepsilon).
	•	off-diagonal entries:
each numerator contains terms of size
O(\delta\Delta)+O(s\,\delta q_\pm)+O(Qs\,\delta\Delta)
=
O\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{Q}\right),
and division by s^2\asymp Q^{-2} gives
\delta N_{12},\delta N_{21}=O(Q\varepsilon).
	•	(2,2)-entry:
the numerator perturbation is
O\!\left(s\,\delta q_\pm\right)+O(s^2Q\,\delta q_\pm)+O((Qs+1)\delta\Delta)
=
O\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{Q}\right),
and division by s^3\asymp Q^{-3} gives
\delta N_{22}=O(Q^2\varepsilon).

So
\boxed{
\delta N=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(\varepsilon) & O(Q\varepsilon)\\
O(Q\varepsilon) & O(Q^2\varepsilon)
\end{pmatrix}
+
O(\varepsilon^2).
}

Step 4: propagate through corrected whitening

Let
\[
\widehat\Omega_{\pair}(s),\qquad \widehat\Omega_{\core}(s)
\]
be the corrected whitened blocks corresponding to \(q_{\pair}\) and \(q_{\core}\).

The corrected same-point pair baseline has the same spectral scales as everywhere else in the draft:
\[
G_{\pair,\pm}^{\corr}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(Q) & O(1)\\
O(1) & O(Q^3)
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
G_{\pair,\pm}^{\corr}(s)^{-1/2}=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(Q^{-1/2}) & O(Q^{-3/2})\\
O(Q^{-3/2}) & O(Q^{-3/2})
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Apply first-order Taylor expansion to the whitening map
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}.
Then
\[
\widehat\Omega_{\core}-\widehat\Omega_{\pair}
=
\mathcal T_-+\mathcal T_{\mix}+\mathcal T_+ + \mathcal E_2,
\]
where the three first-order terms come from \delta G_-, \delta N, \delta G_+, and \mathcal E_2 is quadratic.

Now use exactly the same scale bookkeeping as in the whitened mixed-block transfer theorem:
	•	the mixed term built from \delta N contributes off-diagonal entries of size O(\varepsilon/Q);
	•	the same-point Fréchet terms built from \delta G_\pm also contribute off-diagonal entries of size O(\varepsilon/Q);
	•	the quadratic remainder contributes O(\varepsilon^2/Q).

Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}
\|\widehat\Omega_{\core}(s)-\widehat\Omega_{\pair}(s)\|
\ll
\frac{\varepsilon}{Q}
+
\frac{\varepsilon^2}{Q}.
}
\]

Applying the bounded linear functional \Phi_K, we obtain
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}
|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
\ll
\frac{\varepsilon}{Q}
+
\frac{\varepsilon^2}{Q}.
}
\]

This proves the first part.

Step 5: first odd coefficient stability

Write
\[
H_j(z/Q^2)=\sum_{r\ge0} a_{2r+1}^{(j)}\frac{z^{2r+1}}{Q^{2r+4}},
\qquad j\in\{\pair,\core\}.
\]
Then
a_1^{(j)}=Q^2 H_j'(0).

Applying Cauchy to \(H_{\core}-H_{\pair}\) on |s|<\rho_0/Q,
\[
|H_{\core}'(0)-H_{\pair}'(0)|
\le
\frac{Q}{\rho_0}
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|.
\]
Multiplying by Q^2,
\[
|a_1^{\core}-a_1^{\pair}|
\ll
Q^3
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
\ll
Q^2\varepsilon+Q^2\varepsilon^2.
\]

So if
\[
\varepsilon=\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}=o(u^2/Q^2),
\]
then
\[
a_1^{\core}=a_1^{\pair}+o(u^2).
\]

Since the pair model satisfies
\[
a_1^{\pair}(m)\asymp u^2,
\]
the core coefficient remains nonzero with the same sign for large m.

That completes the proof.

⸻

What this buys you

This is the rigorous pair-like bridge theorem.

It says the entire problem is reduced to one quantitative estimate:

\[
\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}=o(u^2/Q^2).
\]

If you can prove that the actual same-scale core differs from the pair core by that much in the scaled C^2 sense on the microscopic interval, then the pair-based first-jet obstruction survives unchanged.

That is exactly the favorable branch from the fixed-core summary.

So the next remaining question is no longer abstract continuity. It is:

can the extra same-scale zeros inside the core be shown to contribute only
> o(u^2/Q^2)
>
in the scaled C^2-norm on I_x?

That is now the sharp pair-like criterion.

This is a real theorem candidate, and it is much better than some of the earlier “continuity” formulations.

My read is:
the theorem is plausible and basically on the right scale, with a couple of caveats/cleanup points.
the theorem is plausible and basically on the right scale, with a couple of caveats/cleanup points.​

It is not obviously false, and the scale bookkeeping matches the corrected-basis machinery much better than the earlier too-optimistic o(u2/Q3)o(u2/Q3) guesses.
What looks right

The norm
∥ ⁣∣q\pert∣ ⁣∥∗,x=sup⁡t∈Ix(∣q\pert(t)∣+Q−1∣q\pert′(t)∣+Q−2∣q\pert′′(t)∣)
∥∣q\pert​∣∥∗,x​=t∈Ix​sup​(∣q\pert​(t)∣+Q−1∣q\pert′​(t)∣+Q−2∣q\pert′′​(t)∣)

is the right one.

That is exactly the weighted microscopic C2C2-norm compatible with the same-point block
Gm,±(q)=1π(2q(t±)12q′(t±)12q′(t±)112(q′′(t±)+2q(t±)3)),
Gm,±​(q)=π1​(2q(t±​)21​q′(t±​)​21​q′(t±​)121​(q′′(t±​)+2q(t±​)3)​),

because perturbations of size
δq=O(ε),δq′=O(Qε),δq′′=O(Q2ε)
δq=O(ε),δq′=O(Qε),δq′′=O(Q2ε)

really do induce a same-point block perturbation of the weighted form
δG±=(O(ε)O(Qε)O(Qε)O(Q2ε))+O(ε2).
δG±​=(O(ε)O(Qε)​O(Qε)O(Q2ε)​)+O(ε2).

That part is sound.

The phase-gap estimate
∣δΔ(s)∣≪εQ
∣δΔ(s)∣≪Qε​

is also right, since
δΔ(s)=−∫t−t+q\pert(t) dt

and ∣s∣≍Q−1∣s∣≍Q−1.

∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∥Ω
\core​(s)−Ω
