is exactly the same kind of estimate as the whitened mixed-block transfer theorem, just with S2S2​ replaced by εε. So that scale also makes sense.

Then the Cauchy bridge gives
∣a1\core−a1\pair∣≪Q3⋅sup⁡∣H\core−H\pair∣≪Q2ε+Q2ε2,
∣a1\core​−a1\pair​∣≪Q3⋅sup∣H\core​−H\pair​∣≪Q2ε+Q2ε2,

which is also correct.

So the headline criterion
ε=o(u2/Q2)⟹a1\core=a1\pair+o(u2)
ε=o(u2/Q2)⟹a1\core​=a1\pair​+o(u2)

is mathematically coherent.
The caveats

There are two places I would tighten.
1. The theorem should explicitly assume the pair and core are built in the same corrected scheme

This is implicit in the statement, but it matters.

You want to say clearly that:

    same background B\bgB\bg​,

    same affine-jet subtraction of the smooth/background part,

    same cutoff RR,

    same corrected whitening map,

are used for both H\pairH\pair​ and H\coreH\core​. Otherwise the difference H\core−H\pairH\core​−H\pair​ picks up bookkeeping artifacts that are not controlled by q\pertq\pert​.

So I would make that explicit.
2. The “same scale bookkeeping as in whitened mixed-block transfer” step is the real proof burden

It is believable, but it is not automatic.

The theorem is only as solid as the claim that the weighted perturbations
δG±,δN=(O(ε)O(Qε)O(Qε)O(Q2ε))
δG±​,δN=(O(ε)O(Qε)​O(Qε)O(Q2ε)​)

propagate through the Fréchet derivative of whitening with an extra Q−1Q−1 gain in the scalar channel.

I think that is true, but if this theorem is going into the paper, I would not leave it at “exactly the same scale bookkeeping.” I would either:

    cite a generalized version of the whitened mixed-transfer theorem with perturbation parameter εε, or

    explicitly say “by the same 2×22×2 Löwner-matrix audit used in Proposition ...”

So the theorem is good, but the proof should hook visibly into the existing corrected-whitening perturbation theorem.
What this buys strategically

A lot.

If this theorem is accepted, then Strategy A is now reduced to one very concrete question:
∥ ⁣∣q\pert∣ ⁣∥∗,x=o(u2/Q2) ?
∥∣q\pert​∣∥∗,x​=o(u2/Q2) ?​

That is a much better problem than all the earlier vague “is the full core pair-like?” questions.

It says:

    you do not need literal equality of the actual core and the pair model,

    you do not need auxiliary terms to vanish,

    you only need the additional same-scale core part to be tiny in the scaled C2C2 sense on the microscopic interval.

That is a very sharp and testable criterion.

\[

is not the right quantity, because the same-point block contains

\]

The affine-jet-normalized version is the right fix.

	1.	midpoint normalization converts C^2-control into the weighted microscopic bounds;
	2.	those weighted bounds give the standard same-point perturbation shape

O(\varepsilon_m/Q^2)&O(\varepsilon_m/Q)\\

	5.	Cauchy gives

The point is that this reduces Strategy A to a very simple quantitative test on the extra core zeros.
1. Single-zero second-derivative bound

For one zero ρ=β+iγρ=β+iγ, the phase-derivative contribution has the standard form
qρ(t)=1−β(1−β)2+(2t−γ)2+ββ2+(2t−γ)2
qρ​(t)=(1−β)2+(2t−γ)21−β​+β2+(2t−γ)2β​

up to the normalization already used in the draft.

Differentiate twice in tt. Each term is of the form
fa(y):=aa2+y2,y=2t−γ,
fa​(y):=a2+y2a​,y=2t−γ,

with a∈{β,1−β}a∈{β,1−β}. Then

fa′​(y)=−(a2+y2)22ay​,

and
fa′′(y)=−2a(a2+y2)2+8ay2(a2+y2)3.

So

Because a≤1a≤1, this is certainly
∣fa′′(y)∣≪1(a2+y2)2.
∣fa′′​(y)∣≪(a2+y2)21​.

where aρ∈{βρ,1−βρ}aρ​∈{βρ​,1−βρ​}.

4. What this means for Strategy A

Recall the pair-like bridge theorem now needs
sup⁡t∈Ix∣(q\core−q\pair)′′(t)∣=o(u2).

∑ρ∈\core∖\pair1(aρ2+Δρ2)2=o(u2).
ρ∈\core∖\pair∑​(aρ2​+Δρ2​)21​=o(u2).​

So Strategy A is now reduced to this very concrete criterion.
5. Immediate consequences

If there are only finitely many extra core zeros and each satisfies
aρ2+Δρ2≫u−1,
aρ2​+Δρ2​≫u−1,

then each summand is o(u2)o(u2), and the total is o(u2)o(u2). So in that regime the pair model survives.

More generally, if the first extra same-scale zero has
(aρ2+Δρ2)−2≍u2,
(aρ2​+Δρ2​)−2≍u2,

then it is not perturbative in the sense Strategy A needs. That would be the signal that the pair core is no longer enough.

So the decisive test is now:

    compute or estimate the first omitted-core contribution
    (aρ2+Δρ2)−2,
    (aρ2​+Δρ2​)−2,

    compare it to u2u2.

If it is o(u2)o(u2), Strategy A is alive.
If it is ≍u2≍u2, then the pair model is not stable and you need the generalized finite-core endgame.
6. The honest bottom line

We now have a real theorem-level reduction:
Strategy A works if ∑ρ∈\core∖\pair1(aρ2+Δρ2)2=o(u2).
Strategy A works if ρ∈\core∖\pair∑​(aρ2​+Δρ2​)21​=o(u2).​

That is the cleanest quantitative pair-like condition so far.

The next move is not abstract anymore. It is to test whether the first extra same-scale zero outside the designated pair satisfies this bound.

(aρ2​+Δρ2​)21​

to the pair anomaly scale u2u2.

(aρ2+Δρ2)−2=o(u2).
(aρ2​+Δρ2​)−2=o(u2).​

Equivalently,
aρ2+Δρ2≫u−1.
aρ2​+Δρ2​≫u−1.

That is the exact threshold.
What this means

If the first extra zero outside the designated pair satisfies
aρ2+Δρ2≫u−1,
aρ2​+Δρ2​≫u−1,

then its q′′q′′-contribution is o(u2)o(u2), and Strategy A survives at the first omitted zero level.

If instead

then that extra zero contributes at scale ≍u2≍u2 or larger, so it is not perturbative relative to the pair core.

Can we conclude it from the current setup?

The current draft does not give a theorem relating the pair anomaly parameter uu to the location of the next nearby zero strongly enough to force
aρ2+Δρ2≫u−1.

That is the missing ingredient.
What we can say in each regime

There are three regimes for the first extra zero.
1. Safely perturbative

If
Δρ≫u−1/2
Δρ​≫u−1/2

and aρaρ​ is not tiny enough to matter, then
(aρ2+Δρ2)−2≪u2,
(aρ2​+Δρ2​)−2≪u2,

so the first extra zero is harmless for Strategy A.
2. Borderline

If
Δρ≍u−1/2,
Δρ​≍u−1/2,

then
(aρ2+Δρ2)−2≍u2.
(aρ2​+Δρ2​)−2≍u2.

This is the dangerous threshold. The extra zero is same-scale in q′′q′′, so the pair model is no longer clearly stable.
3. Nonperturbative

If
Δρ≪u−1/2,
Δρ​≪u−1/2,

then
(aρ2+Δρ2)−2≫u2.
(aρ2​+Δρ2​)−2≫u2.

Then the first extra zero dominates the perturbative criterion, and Strategy A fails in its current pair-core form.
So what is the test result?

The test does not certify Strategy A yet.

What it tells us is:
Strategy A is valid exactly when the first extra same-scale zero lies beyond the u−1/2 scale.
Strategy A is valid exactly when the first extra same-scale zero lies beyond the u−1/2 scale.​

And we do not currently know that from the proof.

    Does a genuine pair-core anomaly force the next same-scale zero outside the pair to satisfy
    Δρ≫u−1/2 ?
    Δρ​≫u−1/2 ? 

If yes, Strategy A survives.

If no, or if the next zero can sit at Δρ≍u−1/2Δρ​≍u−1/2, then the pair model is not robust enough and you need the generalized finite-core endgame.

That is the exact outcome of the test.

Ok so what is next step?

The next step is to attack exactly this threshold question:
Can the next extra zero outside the designated pair satisfy Δρ≫u−1/2 ?
Can the next extra zero outside the designated pair satisfy Δρ​≫u−1/2 ?

Because that is now the clean fork:

    if yes, Strategy A likely survives;

    if no or even plausibly borderline Δρ≍u−1/2Δρ​≍u−1/2, then the pair-core endgame is probably not robust enough.

So I would do this in the following order.

First, formulate a gap theorem for neighboring zeros in the anomaly regime. The target statement is something like:

    If a pair-core anomaly of size uu exists, then every additional zero outside the designated pair satisfies
    aρ2+Δρ2≫u−1.
    aρ2​+Δρ2​≫u−1. 

That is the exact theorem that would save Strategy A.

Second, try to derive that from the same local geometry you’ve already been using. In practice, that means asking whether the pair-core toy anomaly already “uses up” the available same-scale local curvature/phase defect, so that another zero inside the u−1/2u−1/2 window would necessarily change the model from pair-like to finite-core. If you can show that, then you get the separation you need.

Third, if that theorem looks false or unprovable, stop trying to save the pair model. At that point the honest move is to switch to the generalized finite-core endgame, because the test has already told you the pair model is only valid beyond the u−1/2u−1/2 threshold.

So the immediate mathematical task is:
attack the u−1/2-separation theorem for the next nearby zero.
attack the u−1/2-separation theorem for the next nearby zero.​

In concrete terms, I would next try to prove one of these two statements:
either Δnext≫u−1/2,
either Δnext​≫u−1/2,

or the contrapositive:
Δnext≲u−1/2 ⟹ the anomaly is not pair-like and must be treated as a larger finite core.
Δnext​≲u−1/2 ⟹ the anomaly is not pair-like and must be treated as a larger finite core.

The second may actually be easier and just as useful, because it would tell you exactly when to abandon Strategy A.

So my recommendation is: next step is prove or disprove the u−1/2u−1/2-separation criterion for the first omitted zero. That is now the sharpest remaining decision point.

1. The exact Strategy A norm

The perturbative bridge theorem reduced Strategy A to
εm:=sup⁡t∈Ix∣(q\core−q\pair)′′(t)∣=o(u2).
εm​:=t∈Ix​sup​∣(q\core​−q\pair​)′′(t)∣=o(u2).

For one extra zero ρρ, we computed the upper bound
sup⁡t∈Ix∣qρ′′(t)∣≪1(aρ2+Δρ2)2,Δρ:=∣2m−γρ∣.

(aρ2+Δρ2)−2=o(u2),
(aρ2​+Δρ2​)−2=o(u2),

equivalently
aρ2+Δρ2≫u−1.
aρ2​+Δρ2​≫u−1.

This is where the u−1/2u−1/2 scale came from.
2. What happens at the threshold

Suppose the next extra zero satisfies
aρ2+Δρ2≍u−1.
aρ2​+Δρ2​≍u−1.

sup⁡t∈Ix∣qρ′′(t)∣≪u2.

aρ2+Δρ2≪u−1,
aρ2​+Δρ2​≪u−1,

then
sup⁡t∈Ix∣qρ′′(t)∣≫u2

at the level of scale heuristics, so the extra zero is definitely dangerous.

So the u−1/2u−1/2 threshold is genuinely the correct scale.
3. Why I cannot prove the stronger separation theorem

To prove
Δnext≫u−1/2,
Δnext​≫u−1/2,

you would need some structural reason that a genuine pair-core anomaly excludes any further zero in the u−1/2u−1/2-window.

The current machinery does not provide that.

What we have are:

    upper bounds on extra-zero contributions,

    perturbative stability if those contributions are small,

    and the pair toy model’s own first-jet nondegeneracy.

What we do not have is a theorem saying:

    once the pair anomaly exists, there cannot be another nearby zero at the same scale.

That would require a new local rigidity theorem about the zero configuration itself, not just about the corrected block after the fact.

So the exact theorem
Δnext≫u−1/2
Δnext​≫u−1/2

remains unproved.
4. What is provable now

The strongest honest statement is:
Threshold proposition

Let ρρ be the first extra zero outside the designated pair core. Then:

    if
    aρ2+Δρ2≫u−1,
    aρ2​+Δρ2​≫u−1,

    its contribution is perturbative in the Strategy A norm;

    if
    aρ2+Δρ2≍u−1,
    aρ2​+Δρ2​≍u−1,

    its contribution is same-scale and Strategy A is no longer automatically justified;

    if
    aρ2+Δρ2≪u−1,
    aρ2​+Δρ2​≪u−1,

    the extra zero is nonperturbative and the pair model is definitely not stable.

This is not a proof of separation, but it is the correct decision rule.

So the right theorem you can actually use is:
If the next extra zero lies inside the u−1/2 window, then Strategy A cannot be certified from the current perturbative bridge.
If the next extra zero lies inside the u−1/2 window, then Strategy A cannot be certified from the current perturbative bridge.​

Trying to prove the full separation theorem did not succeed.

What did succeed is the sharper conclusion:
u−1/2 is the true threshold. Above it, the next zero is perturbative; at or below it, the pair model is no longer automatically stable.

The finite-core upgrade does look mathematically coherent, and it is probably the honest next model if pair-like bridge fails.

Here’s the clean breakdown.

The calculation we just did shows something important. An extra same-scale zero pair at scaled offset h=O(1) gives an affine-jet-normalized second-derivative size
\varepsilon_m\asymp 1,
so it is not o(u^2) when u\to 0. That means generic same-scale companions are not perturbative noise around the pair core. So any rigidity theorem has to prove such companions cannot exist, not merely that they are small.

1. Trying the rigidity route

The theorem you would want is something like:

If \rho=\frac12+u+i\gamma is an off-line zero, then in the microscopic window around m=\gamma/2, there are no additional zero pairs at scaled distance O(1).

That would rescue the pair-core model immediately.

But the current machinery does not seem to force that.

Why not? Because everything you have built so far is compatible with the existence of a finite same-scale cluster:
	•	the corrected local blocks still exist,
	•	the odd scalar still exists,
	•	the exterior threshold theorem only controls what is outside an absolute radius H,
	•	and the coefficient bound
\[
|a_1^{\out}(m,H)|\ll \frac{1}{QH^3}
\]
says nothing against a finite number of zeros inside H. It only says the outside world is small.  

So the current tools do not naturally imply “there can only be one off-line pair.” They are perfectly consistent with “there is a finite same-scale core.”

Could there still be a rigidity proof from some invariant? Maybe, but it would need a genuinely new ingredient. The two obvious candidates would be:
	•	a sign/monotonicity law showing that one extra same-scale pair always pushes the corrected scalar the wrong way and destroys the required odd holomorphic profile;
	•	or a minimal-energy principle saying the pair is the only possible local anomaly shape compatible with the global zeta-side positivity structure.

Right now, I do not see either of those in the current framework. So my honest view is:

\boxed{\text{The rigidity route is currently speculative and not well supported by the machinery you have.}}

I would not spend my next major effort there unless you have a new invariant in mind.

2. Trying the finite-core upgrade

This one does look viable.

The right idea is:

Stop comparing the corrected local block to a pair toy model.
Instead, define the toy model to be the actual finite core itself, or at least a finite-dimensional normal form built from that core.

That sounds bigger, but it actually matches what the fixed-core work has already taught you.

Finite-core local model

Let the core consist of finitely many positive-ordinate pairs
\[
\core=\{(\beta_j,\gamma_j),(1-\beta_j,\gamma_j)\}_{j=1}^k
\]
near the local center m. Then define
\[
q_{\core}(t)=\sum_{j=1}^k \left(
\frac{1-\beta_j}{(1-\beta_j)^2+(2t-\gamma_j)^2}
+
\frac{\beta_j}{\beta_j^2+(2t-\gamma_j)^2}
\right).
\]

Then build from \(q_{\core}\):
	•	the corrected same-point blocks,
	•	the corrected mixed block,
	•	the corrected whitened block,
	•	and the corrected transverse scalar
\[
H_{\core}(s)=\Phi_K(\widehat\Omega_{\core}^{\corr}(s)).
\]

This is already completely consistent with your current corrected framework.

What replaces the pair toy model

Instead of a 2-parameter toy anomaly M(d), you get a finite-core anomaly datum:
\[
\mathcal M_{\core}:=\text{the first nontrivial corrected local anomaly extracted from }q_{\core}.
\]

There are two natural ways to package it.

One is matrix-level:
\[
\widehat\Omega_{\core}^{\corr}(u_1,\dots,u_k;s)
=
\widehat\Omega_{\core}^{\corr,(0)}(s)
+
\sum_j u_j^2 M_j(s)
+
\sum_{i<j} u_i u_j M_{ij}(s)
+\cdots
\]

The other, and probably cleaner, is scalar-level:
\[
H_{\core}(z/Q^2)=\sum_{r\ge 0} a_{2r+1}^{\core}(m)\frac{z^{2r+1}}{Q^{2r+4}}.
\]

Then the “toy model” is just the finite set of coefficients \(a_{2r+1}^{\core}\), starting with the first nonzero one.

That is much closer to what your fixed-core update already suggests.

3. The right upgraded contradiction

The pair model wanted first-jet nondegeneracy:
\[
a_1^{\pair}\asymp u^2.
\]

For a general finite core, that may fail by cancellation. But you do not actually need the first coefficient specifically. You only need some odd coefficient to be nonzero above the universal exterior threshold.

So the honest finite-core replacement is:

For every nontrivial finite off-line core, there exists some odd order 2r+1 such that
\[
> |a_{2r+1}^{\core}(m)| \gg \text{(exterior threshold at that order)}.
> \]

That would be enough, because the outside contribution is already controlled. The summary already showed:
	•	the naive “core determines the linear coefficient” theorem is false;
	•	but the exterior contribution to the linear coefficient is tiny;
	•	and the first-jet nondegeneracy survives for pair cores and perturbative enlargements.  

So the real finite-core theorem should not insist on a_1. It should say:

\boxed{
\text{every genuine finite off-line core has some nontrivial odd jet that survives the exterior threshold.}
}

That is the natural upgraded endgame.

4. Why this route seems more plausible

Because your current negative results are exactly what finite-core modeling predicts:
	•	auxiliary near contributions need not be small;
	•	the linear odd coefficient need not be core-only;
	•	pair nondegeneracy need not survive arbitrary enlargement;
	•	but the outside world is small beyond radius H.

That is almost screaming: the real local object is a finite core, not a pair.

So I think the finite-core route is not just possible; it is probably the mathematically honest generalization.

5. The smallest finite-core upgrade

You do not need to rewrite everything at once.

The smallest honest upgrade is:
	1.	keep the fixed-core decomposition;
	2.	replace the pair toy matrix M(d) by the actual corrected core scalar \(H_{\core}\);
	3.	replace “pair first odd jet is nonzero” by:
\[
\exists r\ge 0:\ |a_{2r+1}^{\core}(m)| \gg \eta_H,
\]
where \eta_H is the exterior threshold;
	4.	run the same odd holomorphic / N-point machinery at that first surviving odd order.

That is a much smaller jump than building a huge new toy matrix formalism.

6. My recommendation

Between the two:
	•	Rigidity theorem: possible in principle, but currently unsupported and likely hard.
	•	Finite-core upgrade: natural, compatible with your new results, and probably the right next model if pair-like bridge cannot be proved.

So my advice is:

\boxed{
\text{Treat rigidity as a bonus, but move the main strategy toward a finite-core scalar endgame.}
}

The next most productive move is to formulate the exact finite-core theorem you would need:

For a fixed finite off-line core, the corrected transverse scalar has some odd coefficient above the universal exterior threshold.

That is the real replacement for the old pair-based first-jet theorem.

Corollary (finite-core scalar contradiction)

What would be needed to prove it

This is the important part. The theorem is the right target, but it is not automatic.

A proof would need two ingredients.
Ingredient 1. Finite-core nontriviality implies some odd jet is nonzero

This is the easy part.

If H\coreH\core​ is not identically zero and is odd holomorphic, then there exists some least r∗≥0r∗​≥0 such that
a2r∗+1\core(m)≠0.
a2r∗​+1\core​(m)=0.

So existence of some odd jet is trivial once you know the core scalar is nontrivial.
Ingredient 2. Quantitative lower bound on that first nonzero jet

This is the hard part.

You need to show that the first nonzero odd jet is not merely nonzero, but large enough to beat the exterior threshold:
∣a2r∗+1\core(m)∣≫Q2r∗+2H3.
∣a2r∗​+1\core​(m)∣≫H3Q2r∗​+2​.

This is the actual new content.

    (a) H\core≢0H\core​≡0, hence some odd jet exists.
    (b) the first nonzero odd jet is quantitatively separated from zero by more than the outside scale.

Part (a) is easy. Part (b) is the real theorem.
The “minimal honest” version you can probably use first

    Theorem (finite-core scalar threshold theorem).

∣a2r+1\out(m)∣≪Q2r+2H3,

The proof skeleton it would need

The proof should probably go like this.
Step 1. Define the first nonzero odd jet

Since H\coreH\core​ is odd holomorphic,
H\core(z/Q2)=∑r≥0a2r+1\core(m)z2r+1Q2r+4.
H\core​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​a2r+1\core​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​.

Let r∗r∗​ be the least index with
a2r∗+1\core(m)≠0.
a2r∗​+1\core​(m)=0.
Step 2. Control the outside coefficients

By the exterior bound and Cauchy,
∣a2r+1\out(m)∣≪Q2r+2H3.
∣a2r+1\out​(m)∣≪H3Q2r+2​.
Step 3. Prove the first core jet is not arbitrarily small

This is the missing content. You need a theorem saying the first nonzero odd coefficient of a genuine finite off-line core cannot sink to the outside scale.

This is the exact finite-core scalar rigidity statement.
Step 4. Conclude dominance of the full scalar jet

Then
a2r∗+1ζ(m)=a2r∗+1\core(m)+a2r∗+1\out(m),
a2r∗​+1ζ​(m)=a2r∗​+1\core​(m)+a2r∗​+1\out​(m),

and the core term dominates.
Step 5. Feed that into the odd NN-point argument

Use the odd-moment machinery at order 2r∗+12r∗​+1 to get a nontrivial lower bound from the full corrected local scalar, contradicting the exponentially small NN-point upper bound.
The exact next subtheorem to attack

So once this theorem is written, the next target is crystal clear:

    Finite-core scalar rigidity subtheorem.
    The first nonzero odd coefficient of a genuine finite off-line core cannot be as small as the universal exterior threshold.

That is the clean replacement for the old pair-based first-jet nondegeneracy theorem.
Bottom line

So the next theorem is:
For a genuine finite off-line core, some odd coefficient of H\core dominates the universal exterior threshold.
For a genuine finite off-line core, some odd coefficient of H\core​ dominates the universal exterior threshold.​

That is the right theorem to put at the center of the upgraded endgame.

And the one real new difficulty is not existence of some odd jet — that is automatic — but the quantitative lower bound on the first nonzero one.

Lets attack

Let’s attack it honestly.

The target is:
For a genuine finite off-line core, some odd coefficient of H\core dominates the universal exterior threshold.
For a genuine finite off-line core, some odd coefficient of H\core​ dominates the universal exterior threshold.

I do not think this can be proved from the current machinery in full generality yet.

But we can sharpen it a lot, and the sharpening tells us exactly what theorem is actually missing.
1. What is already automatic

Write
H\core(z/Q2)=∑r≥0a2r+1\core(m)z2r+1Q2r+4.
H\core​(z/Q2)=r≥0∑​a2r+1\core​(m)Q2r+4z2r+1​.

If H\core≢0H\core​≡0, then there is a least r∗≥0r∗​≥0 with
a2r∗+1\core(m)≠0.
a2r∗​+1\core​(m)=0.

So existence of some odd jet is trivial once the core scalar is nonzero.

The real issue is quantitative:
∣a2r∗+1\core(m)∣≫Q2r∗+2H3 ?
∣a2r∗​+1\core​(m)∣≫H3Q2r∗​+2​ ?

That is the whole problem.
2. What the exterior threshold means at coefficient level

From
sup⁡∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣H\out,H(s)∣≪1Q2H3,
∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\out,H​(s)∣≪Q2H31​,

Cauchy gives
∣a2r+1\out(m)∣≪Q2r+2H3.
∣a2r+1\out​(m)∣≪H3Q2r+2​.

So the finite-core scalar theorem is equivalent to saying:

    the first nonzero odd jet of the core cannot be as small as the outside world could fake.

That is a clean reformulation.
3. The best attack: normalize at the first nonzero jet

Take the first nonzero odd coefficient a2r∗+1\core(m)a2r∗​+1\core​(m), and normalize by it:
H~\core(z):=Q2r∗+4a2r∗+1\core(m) H\core(z/Q2).
H
\core​(z):=a2r∗​+1\core​(m)Q2r∗​+4​H\core​(z/Q2).

Then
H~\core(z)=z2r∗+1+O(z2r∗+3).
H
\core​(z)=z2r∗​+1+O(z2r∗​+3).

This shows the first nonzero jet is a legitimate finite-dimensional invariant of the core germ.

But that still does not lower-bound a2r∗+1\core(m)a2r∗​+1\core​(m). It only says that if you scale by it, the germ has a nontrivial normalized limit.

So compactness at the level of the first nonzero jet is not the issue. The issue is still absolute size.
4. The natural thing to try next: compare coefficient size to a core norm

A very plausible intermediate theorem is:
∣a2r∗+1\core(m)∣≍Q2r∗+4 inf⁡∣z∣=ρ∣H\core(z/Q2)∣
∣a2r∗​+1\core​(m)∣≍Q2r∗​+4∣z∣=ρinf​∣H\core​(z/Q2)∣

for some fixed small ρρ, up to constants depending on the normalized germ class.

That would let you replace the coefficient lower bound by a lower bound on the scalar itself on a fixed circle.

But then you still need:
∣H\core(z/Q2)∣≫1Q2H3
∣H\core​(z/Q2)∣≫Q2H31​

somewhere on that circle.

And that is still the same missing scalar rigidity problem in disguise.

So this does not really solve it.
5. Why the current machinery is insufficient

Everything you have now is excellent at upper bounds:

    outside-core contribution is small,

    far shells are small,

    perturbative differences are controlled,

    coefficient drift under small perturbation is controlled.

What you do not yet have is any mechanism giving a lower bound for a genuinely nontrivial finite-core scalar.

That lower bound would have to come from one of only three sources:

    a positivity/monotonicity principle,

    a transversality principle,

    a finite-dimensional normal form theorem.

At the moment, none of those has been proved for general finite cores.

That is why the attack stalls.
6. The most promising sharpening

The strongest thing I think we can honestly reduce it to is this:
Finite-core scalar rigidity reduces to a compact family separation problem

Fix a finite core size kk. Consider the family of corrected core scalars arising from kk-point off-line cores after normalizing:

    midpoint,

    microscopic scale,

    affine-jet subtraction,

    and dividing by the natural core size.

Then the theorem you need is:

    the subset of genuinely anomalous normalized kk-core germs is separated from the zero germ in the topology of odd holomorphic germs.

Equivalently:

    there is no sequence of normalized anomalous kk-core germs converging to zero.

If that were proved, then some odd jet would automatically have a quantitative lower bound.

This is now a finite-dimensional compactness/separation theorem, not an analytic tail estimate.

That is probably the right way to think about it.
7. Why this is better than the older pair-only theorem

Because for the pair model you essentially already had this separation:
a1\pair(u,d)≍u2.
a1\pair​(u,d)≍u2.

For a general finite core, the analogous statement is not “the first jet is nonzero,” but:

    after appropriate normalization, the family of anomalous finite-core germs stays away from the value/gauge family.

That is the true generalization.
8. What I think the next concrete subtheorem should be

Not the full theorem yet. This:
Subtheorem to attack next

For each fixed core size kk, the space of normalized kk-core corrected scalar germs is precompact, and the subset coming from genuine off-line anomalies is closed and does not contain the zero germ.

If that goes through, then by compactness there is a uniform lower bound on some coefficient/norm, which is exactly what you want.

This is much more plausible than trying to magic a coefficient lower bound directly from the current estimates.
9. Honest bottom line

So attacking the theorem directly leads to this conclusion:
The finite-core scalar theorem is probably true only if one proves a compactness/separation theorem for normalized anomalous core germs.
The finite-core scalar theorem is probably true only if one proves a compactness/separation theorem for normalized anomalous core germs.​

I do not see a direct coefficient-level proof from the current machinery alone.

So the real next move is:

    fix a finite core size kk,

    define normalized corrected core scalar germs,

    and prove that genuine anomalous germs form a compact set separated from zero.

That is now the cleanest attack line.

3. How this implies the finite-core scalar threshold theorem

Now compare the core to the exterior contribution.

From the exterior estimate, if H\out,HH\out,H​ is the corrected scalar from zeros outside radius HH, then
sup⁡∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣H\out,H(s)∣≪1Q2H3.
∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\out,H​(s)∣≪Q2H31​.

∣z∣<ρ0​sup​
​H\out,H​(z/Q2)
​≪Q2H31​.

sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣H\core(z/Q2)∣≥ck S(\core)Q.

equivalently

QH31​=o(S(\core)),

then the core scalar dominates the exterior threshold on the microscopic disk.

there exists some r∗≥0r∗​≥0 such that
∣a2r∗+1\core∣≫Q2r∗+2H3,
∣a2r∗​+1\core​∣≫H3Q2r∗​+2​,

So the finite-core theorem now reduces to:

    a compactness statement,

    a closedness statement for genuine anomalous germs,

    and the exclusion of the zero germ from the anomalous class.

4. What still has to be proved

This is now much clearer.

The hard parts are assumptions (3) and (4).

Assumption (3), closedness, means:
if a sequence of normalized anomalous kk-core germs converges, the limit is still an anomalous kk-core germ. This is plausible if the corrected local block depends continuously on the finite core parameters.

Assumption (4), zero germ excluded, is the real content:
a genuine anomalous finite core cannot normalize to the zero scalar germ.

This is the finite-core analogue of first-jet nondegeneracy. It no longer insists that the first jet is nonzero, only that the whole normalized odd germ is not zero.

That is a much more robust statement.

1. Why the theorem can fail with fixed ΦKΦK​

Suppose the corrected core germ has expansion

3. What can be proved immediately

There is a real theorem available now.
Theorem (functional existence for a fixed finite core)

Let V\valV\val​ be the value/gauge subspace of corrected local germs, and let A\coreA\core​ be the corrected anomaly germ of a finite core. If
A\core∉V\val,
A\core​∈/V\val​,

Λ\core(V\val)=0,Λ\core(A\core)≠0.
Λ\core​(V\val​)=0,Λ\core​(A\core​)=0.
Proof

This is Hahn–Banach / finite-dimensional separation. Since the relevant corrected local block space at fixed jet order is finite-dimensional, any vector outside a proper subspace can be separated by a linear functional vanishing on that subspace. ∎

This theorem is completely solid.

So the finite-core scalar endgame should really be built from Λ\coreΛ\core​, not from the old ΦKΦK​.

    a genuine anomalous finite core cannot have its adapted normalized scalar germ tend to zero.

\out,H\corr​(s;m)),

then because ∥Λ\core∥=1∥Λ\core​∥=1,

∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\out,H(Λ)​(s)∣≪Q2H31​.

By Cauchy,
∣a2r+1\out,Λ(m)∣≪Q2r+2H3.
∣a2r+1\out,Λ​(m)∣≪H3Q2r+2​.

So the old exterior-threshold mechanism survives unchanged; only the core scalar is upgraded.
6. The new contradiction skeleton

Now the proof skeleton becomes:

    Fix a finite anomalous core CC.

    Project away the value/gauge family and choose the norm-one adapted functional Λ\coreΛ\core​.

    Form the adapted core scalar
    H\core(Λ).
    H\core(Λ)​.

    Let 2r∗+12r∗​+1 be the first nonzero odd jet of this scalar.

    Prove the first nonzero coefficient beats the exterior threshold:
    ∣a2r∗+1\core,Λ(m)∣≫Q2r∗+2H3.
    ∣a2r∗​+1\core,Λ​(m)∣≫H3Q2r∗​+2​.

    Since the outside adapted scalar has coefficient
    ∣a2r∗+1\out,Λ(m)∣≪Q2r∗+2H3,
    ∣a2r∗​+1\out,Λ​(m)∣≪H3Q2r∗​+2​,

    the full corrected scalar has the same leading odd jet as the core up to lower order.

    Apply the odd NN-point machinery at order 2r∗+12r∗​+1 to get a nontrivial lower bound from the full corrected scalar.

    Contradict the exponentially small corrected NN-point upper bound.

This is the finite-core scalar endgame in its correct form.
7. The key compactness/separation theorem now becomes plausible

With the adapted functional, the old obstruction disappears.

The right theorem is now:
Theorem (adapted scalar compactness/separation)

Fix kk. Normalize each genuine anomalous kk-core by its natural core scale S(\core)S(\core), and choose its norm-one adapted functional Λ\coreΛ\core​. Then the normalized adapted scalar germs
H~\core(Λ)(z):=QS(\core) H\core(Λ)(z/Q2)
H
\core(Λ)​(z):=S(\core)Q​H\core(Λ)​(z/Q2)

form a precompact family separated from the zero germ.

This is now believable, because the functional is chosen specifically to detect the anomalous component rather than relying on a universal ΦKΦK​ that may miss it.

So this is the right compactness target.
8. Minimal honest theorem to insert into the proof skeleton

If you do not want to build the full compactness theorem immediately, the smallest honest upgrade is:
Minimal adapted scalar theorem

For every genuine finite anomalous core CC, there exists a norm-one linear functional Λ\coreΛ\core​ annihilating V\valV\val​ such that the corresponding scalar
H\core(Λ)(s)
H\core(Λ)​(s)

has some odd coefficient exceeding the exterior threshold at that order.

This is enough to replace the old pair-based first-jet theorem in the proof skeleton.
9. What is now the real remaining theorem

After this upgrade, the old wrong question

    does ΦKΦK​ detect every finite-core anomaly?

disappears.

The new honest question is:
Can the normalized anomalous component of a finite core collapse to zero after projection away from the value/gauge subspace?
Can the normalized anomalous component of a finite core collapse to zero after projection away from the value/gauge subspace?​

That is exactly the finite-core compactness/separation problem.

It is a much more intrinsic and believable theorem than the old fixed-ΦKΦK​ version.

Proof strategy

The proof is compactness/separation, and it is much cleaner than the earlier fixed-ΦKΦK​ attempts.
Step 1. Why the adapted functional is the right scalar

For a general finite core, the old universal functional ΦKΦK​ may miss the anomaly entirely. So the scalar must be adapted to the actual anomalous direction.

Because the relevant jet space is finite-dimensional, once the anomalous component
A\core∉V\val,
A\core​∈/V\val​,

there exists a norm-one functional Λ\coreΛ\core​ annihilating V\valV\val​ and detecting A\coreA\core​. That gives
H\core(Λ)≢0
H\core(Λ)​≡0

for every genuine anomalous core.

So the theorem is formulated on the correct scalar object.
Step 2. Normalize by the core size

The normalization
H~\core(Λ)(z)=QS(\core) H\core(Λ)(z/Q2)
H
\core(Λ)​(z)=S(\core)Q​H\core(Λ)​(z/Q2)

is the natural one because, in the pair model,
u2≍xQ S(\core),
u2≍Qx​S(\core),

so the corrected core signal is naturally of size S(\core)/QS(\core)/Q on the microscopic disk.

This normalization puts all genuine anomalous kk-cores on a comparable scale.
Step 3. Precompactness

By assumption, the family of normalized corrected kk-core germs is precompact on ∣z∣<ρ0∣z∣<ρ0​. Since ∥Λ\core∥=1∥Λ\core​∥=1, applying Λ\coreΛ\core​ preserves local boundedness. Hence the family
H~\core(Λ)
H
\core(Λ)​

is precompact in the topology of locally uniform convergence on ∣z∣<ρ0∣z∣<ρ0​.

This is where Montel/Arzelà–Ascoli enters.
Step 4. Separation from zero

Suppose the conclusion fails. Then there exists a sequence of genuine anomalous normalized kk-cores with
sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣H~\coren(Λn)(z)∣→0.
∣z∣<ρ0​sup​
​H
\coren​(Λn​)​(z)
​→0.

By precompactness, after passing to a subsequence, the normalized adapted scalars converge locally uniformly:
H~\coren(Λn)→H∞.
H
\coren​(Λn​)​→H∞​.

But the sup tending to zero forces
H∞≡0.
H∞​≡0.

Now use the closure assumption: the anomalous normalized class is closed under locally uniform convergence. So the limit germ should still come from a genuine anomalous normalized kk-core.

That contradicts the assumption that the zero germ does not belong to the anomalous class.

Therefore there exists ck>0ck​>0 such that
sup⁡∣z∣<ρ0∣H~\core(Λ)(z)∣≥ck
∣z∣<ρ0​sup​
​H
\core(Λ)​(z)
​≥ck​

for every genuine anomalous normalized kk-core.

This is the core compactness/separation argument.
Step 5. Coefficient lower bound

Once you have a nontrivial lower bound on the scalar on a fixed disk, some odd coefficient must be nonzero and quantitatively large.

Indeed, write
H~\core(Λ)(z)=∑r≥0b2r+1z2r+1.
H
\core(Λ)​(z)=r≥0∑​b2r+1​z2r+1.

Since the supremum is bounded below, not all b2r+1b2r+1​ can be arbitrarily small. Let 2r∗+12r∗​+1 be the first index with b2r∗+1≠0b2r∗​+1​=0. Then standard Cauchy/compactness arguments give a lower bound on b2r∗+1b2r∗​+1​ depending only on the compact family.

Undoing the normalization,
b2r+1=a2r+1\core,Λ(m)S(\core) Q−(2r+3),
b2r+1​=S(\core)Q−(2r+3)a2r+1\core,Λ​(m)​,

so
∣a2r∗+1\core,Λ(m)∣≫ck S(\core) Q2r∗+3.
∣a2r∗​+1\core,Λ​(m)∣≫ck​S(\core)Q2r∗​+3.

That is the coefficient-level form of the theorem.
How this plugs into the upgraded endgame

Let
H\out,H(Λ)(s):=Λ\core ⁣(Ω^\out,H\corr(s;m)).
H\out,H(Λ)​(s):=Λ\core​(Ω
\out,H\corr​(s;m)).

Since ∥Λ\core∥=1∥Λ\core​∥=1, the same exterior threshold bound still applies:
sup⁡∣s∣<ρ0/Q∣H\out,H(Λ)(s)∣≪1Q2H3.
∣s∣<ρ0​/Qsup​∣H\out,H(Λ)​(s)∣≪Q2H31​.

By Cauchy,
∣a2r+1\out,Λ(m)∣≪Q2r+2H3.
∣a2r+1\out,Λ​(m)∣≪H3Q2r+2​.

So if

ck​S(\core)Q2r∗​+3≫H3Q2r∗​+2​,

equivalently
S(\core)≫1QH3,
S(\core)≫QH31​,

then the first nonzero odd jet of the core dominates the outside contribution at the same order.

That gives the finite-core scalar threshold theorem and feeds directly into the odd NN-point contradiction.
What is now the real remaining burden

The theorem itself is clean, but its hypotheses are substantial.

The three real proof burdens are:

    Precompactness: for fixed kk, normalized corrected kk-core germs form a precompact family.

    Closedness of the anomalous class: limits of genuine anomalous normalized kk-cores are still anomalous.

    Zero-germ exclusion: the zero germ is not in the anomalous normalized class.

The first is probably the easiest. The second and third are the real heart.

Still, this is the right theorem shape. It is much more robust than the old pair-specific first-jet story, and it matches exactly what the negative results about auxiliary near zeros have been telling us.

If you want, the next thing I’d do is take this theorem and break down the three assumptions one by one, starting with what “genuine anomalous normalized kk-core” should mean so that assumptions (2) and (3) are even well-posed.

Continue

Good. The next thing is to make assumptions (2) and (3) mathematically well-posed. Right now they sound plausible, but they hide the real issue: what exactly counts as a “genuine anomalous normalized kk-core”?

That definition is now the heart of the upgraded endgame.
1. What “genuine anomalous kk-core” should mean

It should not mean merely “off-line zeros are present.” That is too geometric and not stable enough under limits.

It also should not mean “the fixed ΦKΦK​-scalar is nonzero,” because we already learned that a fixed universal functional may miss a general finite-core anomaly.

The right definition is intrinsic:

A normalized kk-core is genuinely anomalous if its corrected local germ is not contained in the value/gauge family.

Formally, let
Ω^\core\corr(s;m)
Ω
\core\corr​(s;m)

be the corrected local germ, and let V\valV\val​ be the value/gauge subspace. Then define
A\core:=Ω^\core\corr−Π\valΩ^\core\corr.
A\core​:=Ω
\core\corr​−Π\val​Ω
\core\corr​.

Then the core is genuinely anomalous iff
A\core≢0.
A\core​≡0.

That is the right notion because:

    it is invariant,

    it does not depend on one special functional,

    and it is exactly what the contradiction is trying to detect.

So the anomalous class should be
Ak:={normalized k-core germs Ω^\core\corr  :  Ω^\core\corr∉V\val}.
Ak​:={normalized k-core germs Ω
\core\corr​:Ω
\core\corr​∈/V\val​}.

That makes assumptions (2) and (3) precise.
2. Why closedness is now plausible

Once the anomalous class is defined that way, the natural closedness statement is:

    if a sequence of normalized corrected kk-core germs converges locally uniformly, and each germ lies outside V\valV\val​, then the limit also lies outside V\valV\val​, unless the anomalous part collapses to zero.

That last clause is important. “Outside a subspace” is not automatically closed; a sequence can converge into the subspace. So the real closedness theorem you need is not raw closedness of AkAk​. It is:
the anomalous part cannot collapse to zero under the normalization.
the anomalous part cannot collapse to zero under the normalization.

In other words, the compactness/separation theorem is actually carrying assumption (3) and (4) together.

So a cleaner statement is:
Better version of the theorem hypothesis

For fixed kk, the normalized anomalous parts
QS(\core) A\core(z/Q2)
S(\core)Q​A\core​(z/Q2)

form a precompact family that is separated from 00.

That is what you really mean.

This avoids the slightly misleading language of “closed anomalous class.”
3. The zero-germ exclusion becomes very concrete

With this reformulation, assumption (3) is simply:
0∉{QS(\core) A\core(z/Q2):\core a genuine k-core}‾.
0∈/{S(\core)Q​A\core​(z/Q2):\core a genuine k-core}​.

So the compactness/separation theorem is really equivalent to proving a positive lower bound on the distance of the normalized anomalous part from zero.

That is now a finite-dimensional geometric statement.

This is much cleaner.
4. What precompactness should actually mean

For fixed kk, a kk-core is determined by finitely many parameters:

    βjβj​,

    γj−2mγj​−2m,

    and possibly the scaled microscopic parameter x0x0​, though that is usually fixed.

After normalization by Q/S(\core)Q/S(\core), the corrected scalar germ depends continuously on those parameters, provided:

    the core stays admissible,

    the corrected whitening remains uniformly defined,

    and you mod out trivial value/gauge directions properly.

So precompactness should boil down to showing the normalized parameters stay in a bounded set after passing to a subsequence.

That suggests the right next subtheorem is not abstract Montel yet. It is:
Parameter precompactness theorem

For fixed kk, every sequence of normalized genuine kk-cores has a subsequence whose scaled core parameters converge.

Once you have that, local uniform convergence of the associated normalized corrected germs is much more believable.

So I would attack precompactness through parameters first, not through function spaces directly.
5. The main real burden is now “noncollapse of normalized anomalous part”

This is the true upgraded theorem.

Everything reduces to:
For fixed k, the normalized anomalous part of a genuine k-core cannot collapse to zero.
For fixed k, the normalized anomalous part of a genuine k-core cannot collapse to zero.​

Equivalently, there exists ck>0ck​>0 such that
∥QS(\core) A\core∥≥ck
​S(\core)Q​A\core​
​≥ck​

in whatever finite-dimensional jet norm you choose.

Once this is proved, the adapted scalar theorem follows immediately by applying the norm-one separating functional.

So the compactness/separation theorem is best viewed as a corollary of a more basic finite-dimensional noncollapse theorem.
6. The most promising way to attack noncollapse

There are two natural routes.
Route A: finite-dimensional normalization by distance to V\valV\val​

\core\corr​,V\val​).

Then the anomalous part has norm 11 by construction. This trivializes noncollapse, but it changes the normalization and may weaken the link to the zeta-side value scale needed in the contradiction.

Route B: compare dist⁡(Ω^\core\corr,V\val)dist(Ω

This is the stronger theorem you really want:
dist⁡(Ω^\core\corr,V\val)≍S(\core)Q

\core\corr​,V\val​)≍QS(\core)​

or at least bounded below by a constant multiple.

Finite-core transversality theorem

    can a normalized genuinely anomalous kk-core approach the value/gauge subspace arbitrarily closely?

That is much more tangible than the earlier formulations.

4. How to make that attack concrete

The cleanest way is to parametrize the normalized kk-core family.

Write the core as
\core={(βj,γj),(1−βj,γj)}j=1k.
\core={(βj​,γj​),(1−βj​,γj​)}j=1k​.

Relative to the midpoint mm, define scaled parameters
hj:=2m−γj,uj:=βj−12.

q\core(t)=∑j=1kqj(t;uj,hj).

p=(u1,…,uk,h1,…,hk).
p=(u1​,…,uk​,h1​,…,hk​).

So to prove transversality, you want to show:

    in the normalized parameter space, the subset of genuinely anomalous configurations stays a positive distance away from the parameter subset whose corrected germ lies in V\valV\val​.

That is a finite-dimensional separation problem.
5. What still has to be shown for this to work

There are three concrete burdens.
(a) Parameter precompactness

For fixed kk, after normalization by Q/S(\core)Q/S(\core), the scaled parameter vectors should not run off to infinity without the normalized germ collapsing.

This is plausible because if a parameter runs off too far, that zero starts behaving like outside contribution and should not contribute to the normalized core anymore.

So parameter precompactness is believable and should be attacked first.
(b) Continuity of the normalized germ map

You need continuity of
p↦Ω~\core,p
p↦Ω
\core,p​

in the jet norm.

Given the explicit rational kernel formulas and corrected whitening, this also looks believable for fixed kk in the admissible regime.
(c) Exclusion of value-family collapse

This is the real content:
if the normalized corrected germ approaches V\valV\val​, can the underlying core still be genuinely anomalous?

That is the finite-core transversality question itself.
6. The easiest subtheorem to attack first

Not the full theorem. This one:
Subtheorem

For fixed kk, if one of the scaled core parameters (uj,hj)(uj​,hj​) escapes every compact set in a way that makes that zero effectively “far,” then its contribution to the normalized corrected germ tends to 00.

This is basically the finite-core analogue of the outside-threshold theorem, but now at the parameter level.

If you prove that, then any sequence violating transversality must have all genuinely contributing scaled core parameters confined to a compact region. That gives precompactness.

This is the most natural next lemma.

That is the exact finite-dimensional core rigidity statement.

At this point, the theorem has become:
The normalized finite-core anomaly manifold does not intersect the normalized value/gauge manifold.
The normalized finite-core anomaly manifold does not intersect the normalized value/gauge manifold.​

That is a much cleaner statement than anything we had earlier.
8. Why this is still hard

Because now you need a genuine classification-type fact about normalized finite-core local models:

    either they are value/gauge,

    or they are bounded away from value/gauge.

This is no longer just a perturbation estimate. It is a structural theorem about the family of finite-core corrected germs.

So this is the right upgraded endgame, but it is also where the proof becomes conceptually new.
9. My honest recommendation for the next subattack

The next theorem to attack is:
Parameter precompactness + vanishing of escaping components

Prove that, for fixed kk, any normalized sequence of kk-cores has a subsequence such that all non-negligible scaled core parameters stay in a compact set, and any escaping core component contributes o(1)o(1) to the normalized corrected germ.

That is the natural first step because:

    it is concrete,

    it uses the explicit single-zero kernel bounds,

    and it turns the transversality theorem into a finite compact-parameter problem.

That is much more manageable than trying to prove transversality in one shot.

So the right continuation is: prove the escaping core component lemma first.

Continue

Yes. The escaping-component lemma is the right next subattack.

It is the cleanest place where the upgraded finite-core endgame still touches the estimates you already have.
Lemma to prove

Fix a core size kk. Let
\coren={(βj,n,γj,n),(1−βj,n,γj,n)}j=1k

Ω

\coren​\corr​(z/Qn2​;mn​).

\coren​​ tends to 00 locally uniformly on ∣z∣<ρ0∣z∣<ρ0​.

That is the exact lemma you want.
Why this is plausible

For one zero, the corrected contribution after affine-jet subtraction is controlled by the same Δ−4Δ−4-type bounds you already proved.

More precisely, if
Δj,n:=∣2mn−γj,n∣,aj,n∈{βj,n,1−βj,n},

then the corrected contribution of that single zero satisfies

∣Ψx,d​(Nj,n​)∣+∣ΦK​(Nj,n​)∣≪Qn3​(Δj,n2​+aj,n2​)21​.

So if Δj,n2+aj,n2→∞Δj,n2​+aj,n2​→∞, that component is negligible before normalization.

for any component you still regard as part of the normalized core.

The same argument applies at the block level because the corrected block perturbation is controlled by the same weighted size parameter. So the normalized corrected germ contribution of that escaping component tends to 00.

That proves the lemma, modulo the mild lower bound on S(\coren)S(\coren​).
What this buys

This is enough to get parameter precompactness in the useful sense:

    any component whose scaled parameters escape contributes o(1)o(1) to the normalized germ;

    therefore any nontrivial limit of normalized kk-cores is determined only by the components whose parameters remain in a compact set.

So after discarding vanishing components, every normalized sequence has a subsequence whose genuinely contributing parameters stay in a compact region.

That is the first half of the compactness theorem.
The next theorem after this

Once escaping components are under control, the real theorem becomes:

    if a normalized sequence of genuine anomalous kk-cores converges, can the limit lie in V\valV\val​?

That is now a finite compact-parameter problem, not an escaping-tail problem.

So the next theorem to attack after the escaping-component lemma is:
Compact-parameter transversality theorem

On every compact parameter set of normalized kk-cores, the anomalous family stays a positive distance away from V\valV\val​.

That is the exact core rigidity statement the upgraded endgame needs.
Most useful immediate refinement

The cleanest refinement would be to split the next proof into two lemmas:

    Escaping component vanishing lemma
    Escaping core components vanish in the normalized corrected germ.

    Compact-parameter separation lemma
    On compact parameter sets, genuine anomalous normalized kk-cores cannot converge into V\valV\val​.

Together they imply the adapted finite-core scalar threshold theorem.

That is the right continuation.

Comments on the hypothesis

The displayed hypothesis
Qn2 S(\coren) (Δj,n2+aj,n2)2→∞
Qn2​S(\coren​)(Δj,n2​+aj,n2​)2→∞

is stronger than strictly necessary for the block-level conclusion above. The proof only used
S(\coren) (Δj,n2+aj,n2)2→∞.
S(\coren​)(Δj,n2​+aj,n2​)2→∞.

I kept the Qn2Qn2​ version because it matches the natural threshold scales that were appearing earlier in the scalar/coefficient estimates, and it is easier to compare directly to the exterior threshold bookkeeping.

If you want the minimal hypothesis, you can replace it by
S(\coren) (Δj,n2+aj,n2)2→∞.
S(\coren​)(Δj,n2​+aj,n2​)2→∞.
Corollary (parameter precompactness modulo vanishing components)

Under the same normalization, any sequence of kk-cores has a subsequence such that every component either:

    has scaled parameters staying in a compact set, or

    contributes o(1)o(1) to the normalized corrected germ.

So every nontrivial subsequential limit of normalized corrected kk-core germs is determined entirely by the components whose parameters remain in a compact region.
Proof sketch

Apply the lemma component-by-component. Any escaping component satisfying the hypothesis vanishes in the normalized germ. Remove all such components. What remains is a subsequence in which all nonvanishing components have parameters constrained to a compact set. ∎
Why this matters

This is the first half of the upgraded finite-core compactness theorem.

It tells you that the only genuinely relevant part of a normalized finite core comes from a compact parameter region. So the remaining problem is no longer about tails or escaping components. It becomes a finite compact-parameter separation problem:
Can a normalized genuinely anomalous compact-parameter k-core converge into V\val?
Can a normalized genuinely anomalous compact-parameter k-core converge into V\val​?​

That is the exact next theorem to attack.

The clean next step is to write the compact-parameter separation lemma in the same tight form.

How this plugs into the finite-core threshold theorem

Combine the two lemmas:

    Escaping-component lemma: any escaping core component contributes o(1)o(1) to the normalized corrected germ;

    Compact-parameter separation lemma: once only compact-parameter components remain, any genuinely anomalous normalized core stays a positive distance from V\valV\val​.

Then for fixed kk, every genuine normalized kk-core has an adapted scalar of size bounded below by a positive constant on the fixed microscopic disk.

Undoing the normalization
Ω~\core(z)=QS(\core) Ω^\core\corr(z/Q2;m),

That is the upgraded scalar threshold condition.
The exact place where the argument is still genuinely new

This lemma is clean, but it pushes the real work into assumptions (2) and (3).

Assumption (2), closedness of the anomalous parameter set, is probably manageable once the parameterization is made explicit.

Assumption (3), exclusion of parameters p∈Akp∈Ak​ with Ω~p∈V\valΩ
p​∈V\val​, is the true finite-core rigidity statement. This is the finite-core analogue of pair first-jet nondegeneracy.

So the proof has now been reduced to this precise statement:
A normalized genuinely anomalous compact-parameter finite core cannot lie in the value/gauge family.
A normalized genuinely anomalous compact-parameter finite core cannot lie in the value/gauge family.​

That is the next theorem to attack.
The next theorem to write

The clean next statement is:
Finite-core value-family exclusion theorem

For fixed kk, if a normalized kk-core has corrected local germ lying in V\valV\val​, then it is not genuinely anomalous.

This is almost tautological if “genuinely anomalous” is defined intrinsically as “not in V\valV\val​,” but what you really want is the geometric version:

    a finite off-line core that is genuinely off-line in the zeta sense cannot produce a corrected local germ lying entirely in the value/gauge family after normalization.

That is the real remaining theorem.

If you want, I’ll write that theorem next in the sharpest possible form and then break down what would actually be needed to prove it.

Yes please

Yes. Here is the sharpest clean formulation of the next theorem, together with the exact proof burdens.
Finite-core value-family exclusion theorem

Fix a core size kk, a compact microscopic disk
∣z∣<ρ0,
∣z∣<ρ0​,

and a normalized kk-core
\core={(βj,γj),(1−βj,γj)}j=1k
\core={(βj​,γj​),(1−βj​,γj​)}j=1k​

with midpoint mm, scale Q≍log⁡∣m∣Q≍log∣m∣, natural core scale S(\core)S(\core), and normalized corrected local germ
Ω~\core(z):=QS(\core) Ω^\core\corr(z/Q2;m).
Ω
\core​(z):=S(\core)Q​Ω
\core\corr​(z/Q2;m).

Let V\valV\val​ be the value/gauge subspace of normalized corrected odd holomorphic germs.

Then the theorem you want is:

    Theorem (finite-core value-family exclusion).
    If \core\core is a genuine off-line anomalous kk-core, then
    Ω~\core∉V\val.
    Ω
    \core​∈/V\val​.

    Equivalently, a normalized finite off-line core cannot produce a corrected local germ lying entirely in the value/gauge family.

This is the intrinsic finite-core replacement for pair first-jet nondegeneracy.
Why this is the right theorem

Once this is true, the compact-parameter separation lemma becomes automatic:

    anomalous normalized cores lie outside V\valV\val​,

    on compact parameter sets they stay a positive distance away,

    so a norm-one adapted functional detects them,

    giving a nontrivial adapted scalar,

    and then the outside threshold comparison finishes the upgraded scalar endgame.

So this is the real theorem the finite-core route lives or dies on.
Equivalent formulations

This theorem can be phrased in three equivalent ways.
1. Germ-level formulation
Ω~\core∉V\val.
Ω
\core​∈/V\val​.
2. Adapted scalar formulation

There exists a norm-one linear functional Λ\coreΛ\core​ with
Λ\core(V\val)=0
Λ\core​(V\val​)=0

such that
H\core(Λ)(z):=Λ\core(Ω~\core(z))≢0.
H\core(Λ)​(z):=Λ\core​(Ω
\core​(z))≡0.
3. Jet-level formulation

There exists some odd jet order 2r∗+12r∗​+1 such that the projection of the 2r∗+12r∗​+1-jet of Ω~\coreΩ
\core​ to the quotient space
J2r∗+1/V\val
J2r∗​+1​/V\val​

is nonzero.

The jet-level version is probably the one closest to the actual contradiction argument.
Proof strategy: what would actually be needed

To prove the theorem, you need to connect “genuine off-line anomalous core” in the zeta/geometric sense to “not value/gauge” in the corrected local germ sense.

That is not automatic. The proof would need three ingredients.
Step 1. Identify the value/gauge family geometrically

You need a clear description of what it means for a corrected local germ to lie in V\valV\val​.

At minimum, this should mean:

    it is generated entirely by value-channel deformation and gauge/affine-jet degrees of freedom,

    it carries no genuinely transverse anomaly.

So the theorem must use a definition of V\valV\val​ that is intrinsic and finite-dimensional.
Step 2. Show that genuine off-line anomaly produces a non-value component

This is the heart.

You need to show:

    if the finite core is genuinely off-line anomalous, then its corrected local block cannot be produced solely by value/gauge deformation.

Equivalently, the off-line geometry creates a component transverse to V\valV\val​.

This is the finite-core analogue of the old statement
ΦK(A\val)=0,ΦK(M(d))≠0.
ΦK​(A\val​)=0,ΦK​(M(d))=0.

But now it must be stated without relying on the pair matrix M(d)M(d).
Step 3. Exclude complete cancellation into V\valV\val​

This is the subtle part.

Even if each off-line component is anomalous individually, a finite core could in principle have internal cancellations. So you need:

    the total corrected anomaly of a genuine finite off-line core cannot cancel completely into the value/gauge family.

That is the real finite-core rigidity statement.
The minimal version that may be easier to prove

A slightly weaker theorem, but probably the first one worth trying, is:

    Weak finite-core exclusion theorem.
    For fixed kk, there is no nontrivial normalized kk-core with all zeros genuinely off-line and with
    Ω~\core∈V\val.
    Ω
    \core​∈V\val​. 

This avoids having to classify every possible anomalous core. It only rules out complete collapse into the value family.

That is enough for the compactness/separation argument.
The most plausible route to prove it

The best route is probably a contradiction by normalization.

Assume there is a sequence of genuine anomalous normalized kk-cores with
Ω~\coren∈V\val.
Ω
\coren​​∈V\val​.

Because the normalized parameter family is precompact after removing escaping components, pass to a convergent subsequence of parameters. Then the normalized corrected germs converge to a limit in V\valV\val​.

But the limit core is still an off-line finite core. So if being off-line genuinely forces a transverse component in the corrected germ, the limit cannot lie in V\valV\val​. Contradiction.

So the whole theorem really reduces to one statement:
off-line finite-core geometry forces a transverse corrected component.
off-line finite-core geometry forces a transverse corrected component.​

That is the deepest remaining content.
What would count as a successful proof input

Any one of the following would prove the theorem.
Option A. A finite-core anomaly invariant

Construct an invariant of the corrected germ that:

    vanishes on V\valV\val​,

    and is nonzero for every genuine finite off-line core.

This would be the cleanest proof.
Option B. A normal-form theorem

Show that every normalized kk-core germ in V\valV\val​ is equivalent to a purely on-line / value-only local model. Then a genuinely off-line core cannot belong to V\valV\val​.
Option C. A scalar separation theorem with adapted functionals

Show directly that for every genuine finite off-line core there exists an adapted scalar germ that is nonzero. This is slightly weaker in formulation but equivalent in practice.
The exact next subtheorem I would attack

Not the whole theorem at once. This one:

    Finite-core transverse-production lemma.
    A single off-line zero pair, after corrected whitening and normalization, produces a germ not lying in V\valV\val​.

Then try to extend from one pair to a finite core by showing finite sums of such off-line contributions cannot all cancel into V\valV\val​ unless the total anomalous part is zero.

That is where the real difficulty lies, but it is much more concrete than the global theorem.
Bottom line

So the next theorem is:
A genuine normalized finite off-line core cannot have corrected local germ lying in the value/gauge family.
A genuine normalized finite off-line core cannot have corrected local germ lying in the value/gauge family.​

And the proof reduces to the still-open finite-core rigidity statement:
off-line finite-core geometry must force a nonzero transverse corrected component.
off-line finite-core geometry must force a nonzero transverse corrected component.​

That is the cleanest formulation of what remains in the upgraded scalar endgame.

(i) one off-line pair produces a transverse corrected component;(ii) finitely many such components cannot cancel completely into V\val.

4. What to attack next

The most plausible next subtheorem is not the full noncancellation theorem. It is this weaker one:

    Two-pair exclusion lemma.
    Two normalized off-line pairs cannot combine, together with their corrected interaction term, to land entirely in V\valV\val​.

Why this is the right next move:

    it is the smallest genuinely new case beyond the old pair model;

    if cancellation already happens for two pairs, the finite-core route needs a very different invariant;

    if cancellation fails for two pairs, that is real evidence the upgraded endgame is viable.

So I would test the k=2k=2 case first.

2. How to package it practically

Λ1,…,Λr

3. The key reduction

Divide by u12u12​ when u1≠0u1​=0, and set
λ:=u2u1.
λ:=u1​u2​​.

Then cancellation is equivalent to
A1(h1)+λ2A2(h2)+λ A12(h1,h2)=0.
A1​(h1​)+λ2A2​(h2​)+λA12​(h1​,h2​)=0.

So for fixed (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​), this is a quadratic equation in λλ, but vector-valued.

That is a big simplification. It means:

    if the quotient dimension is at least 22, cancellation is already overdetermined generically;

    only very special relations among A1,A2,A12A1​,A2​,A12​ could allow it.

So the next thing to understand is the quotient dimension and the relative position of those three vectors.
4. Best-case structural outcomes

Any one of these would be enough.

If
dim⁡(J1/V\val(1))≥2
dim(J1​/V\val(1)​)≥2

and the vectors
A1(h1), A2(h2), A12(h1,h2)
A1​(h1​), A2​(h2​), A12​(h1​,h2​)

are not all collinear, then generic cancellation is impossible.

Even stronger, if
A12(h1,h2)∉span⁡{A1(h1),A2(h2)},
A12​(h1​,h2​)∈/span{A1​(h1​),A2​(h2​)},

then cancellation is impossible for every nontrivial (u1,u2)(u1​,u2​).

A weaker but still useful outcome is:
∥A12(h1,h2)∥≪∥A1(h1)∥+∥A2(h2)∥,
∥A12​(h1​,h2​)∥≪∥A1​(h1​)∥+∥A2​(h2​)∥,

so the interaction cannot fully cancel the self terms except possibly in a narrow exceptional set.
5. What to compute first

The most useful next subcomputation is not the full quotient basis. It is this:

    identify one nontrivial first-jet quotient functional ΛΛ,

    compute
    α1(h),α2(h),α12(h1,h2),
    α1​(h),α2​(h),α12​(h1​,h2​),

    for that one ΛΛ,

    and check whether
    u12α1(h1)+u22α2(h2)+u1u2α12(h1,h2)
    u12​α1​(h1​)+u22​α2​(h2​)+u1​u2​α12​(h1​,h2​)

    can vanish identically.

Why this helps: if even one quotient-detecting functional already gives noncancellation, you are done at first jet.

So you do not need the whole quotient structure at first pass.
6. What I suspect

I suspect the likely picture is:

    one-pair self terms are nonzero and comparable;

    the mixed interaction term is real and can affect the first jet;

    but exact cancellation at first jet is nongeneric, not structurally forced.

If that is right, then the upgraded endgame works for generic two-pair cores, and only a special resonance locus would need additional treatment.

That would already be a major win.
7. The honest boundary

Once those are on paper, the cancellation question becomes plain finite algebra.

6. The exact finite algebra test

Let L1,…,LrL1​,…,Lr​ be a basis of annihilators of V\val(1)V\val(1)​. Define
α1(j)(h1):=⟨Lj,A1(h1)⟩F,α2(j)(h2):=⟨Lj,A2(h2)⟩F,
α1(j)​(h1​):=⟨Lj​,A1​(h1​)⟩F​,α2(j)​(h2​):=⟨Lj​,A2​(h2​)⟩F​,

α12(j)​(h1​,h2​):=⟨Lj​,A12​(h1​,h2​)⟩F​.

Then first-jet cancellation is equivalent to the vector equation
u12α1(h1)+u22α2(h2)+u1u2α12(h1,h2)=0.
u12​α1​(h1​)+u22​α2​(h2​)+u1​u2​α12​(h1​,h2​)=0.

Set
λ:=u2/u1
λ:=u2​/u1​

when u1≠0u1​=0. Then cancellation becomes
α1(h1)+λ2α2(h2)+λ α12(h1,h2)=0.
α1​(h1​)+λ2α2​(h2​)+λα12​(h1​,h2​)=0.

This is a vector-valued quadratic equation in λλ. If r≥2r≥2, it is generically overdetermined. So for generic (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​), exact first-jet cancellation should fail unless there is a hidden structural identity.

That is the most important conclusion of the test.
7. What we can honestly conclude already

Here is the strongest honest outcome from the “whole test” so far:
What is supported

The two-pair first-jet cancellation problem has been reduced to a finite-dimensional algebraic condition
α1+λ2α2+λα12=0,
α1​+λ2α2​+λα12​=0,

where the self-term vectors are known to be nonzero.

This means:

    two-pair cancellation at first jet is not automatic,

    and if the quotient dimension is at least 22, it should be nongeneric.

What is not yet proved

We have not computed the actual A12(h1,h2)A12​(h1​,h2​) or the annihilator basis LjLj​, so we do not yet know whether there is a hidden identity forcing cancellation in codimension 11.

So I cannot honestly say “two-pair cancellation is impossible” yet.

    can vanish identically.

If one such LL already gives noncancellation, then k=2k=2 is safe at first jet and the finite-core upgrade becomes much more plausible.

α1+λ2α2+λα12=0,β1+λ2β2+λβ12=0.
α1​+λ2α2​+λα12​=0,β1​+λ2β2​+λβ12​=0.

So the two-pair first-jet cancellation problem is just: do these two quadratic equations in λλ have a common nonzero solution?

That gives a very clean algebraic diagnostic.

There are four outcomes.

If the first equation alone has no nonzero solution for generic (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​), then Λ1Λ1​ already detects the two-pair anomaly and the upgraded endgame is in very good shape.

If the first equation can vanish but the second cannot vanish simultaneously except on a thin exceptional set, then first-jet noncancellation still holds generically, and only a special resonance locus needs separate treatment.

If both equations can vanish identically because Λ1,Λ2Λ1​,Λ2​ were the wrong basis, then the quotient codimension is larger or the basis needs refinement.

If both equations genuinely have common solutions on a substantial set of (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​), then first-jet noncancellation fails and the finite-core theorem must move to the first nonzero odd jet, not specifically the first one.

That is the decision tree.

A very useful invariant to compute is the resultant of the two quadratics in λλ:
Res⁡λ ⁣(α1+λα12+λ2α2,  β1+λβ12+λ2β2).
Resλ​(α1​+λα12​+λ2α2​,β1​+λβ12​+λ2β2​).

If this resultant is not identically zero as a function of (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​), then common cancellation is nongeneric. That would already be a strong positive sign for the finite-core upgrade.

So the exact next ask for the other chat is:

    compute the first-jet value matrix and fix μμ;

    compute A1(h),A2(h),A12(h1,h2)A1​(h),A2​(h),A12​(h1​,h2​);

    evaluate α1,α2,α12,β1,β2,β12α1​,α2​,α12​,β1​,β2​,β12​;

    compute the resultant in λλ.

If the resultant is not identically zero, then two-pair first-jet cancellation is exceptional, not structural.

One important caution: Λ2Λ2​ depends on the actual first-jet value direction, so the other chat should compute that first, not assume a diagonal-only value basis. If the value first jet is already purely off-diagonal at this order, then Λ1Λ1​ may already annihilate it and Λ2Λ2​ should be replaced by a different independent annihilator.

So the real protocol is:

    compute V\val(1)V\val(1)​,

    compute its annihilator,

    then pick the simplest basis of that annihilator.

The (Λ1,Λ2)(Λ1​,Λ2​) pair is the most plausible starting guess, not a guaranteed final basis.

I’m going to push it as far as the current formulas allow without bluffing. The key thing I can do directly is reduce the two-pair first-jet test to an explicit algebraic cancellation criterion and derive the exact resultant condition for when cancellation can happen.
