What I cannot honestly do yet is produce the six coefficient functions
α1(h1), α2(h2), α12(h1,h2), β1(h1), β2(h2), β12(h1,h2)
α1​(h1​), α2​(h2​), α12​(h1​,h2​), β1​(h1​), β2​(h2​), β12​(h1​,h2​)

from the corrected two-pair block formulas, because those formulas have not been frozen in one explicit symbolic normal form in the current draft.

But the two-pair first-jet test now reduces to one exact criterion.

Let
P(λ):=α2 λ2+α12 λ+α1,Q(λ):=β2 λ2+β12 λ+β1,λ=u2u1.
P(λ):=α2​λ2+α12​λ+α1​,Q(λ):=β2​λ2+β12​λ+β1​,λ=u1​u2​​.

Then first-jet cancellation for a nontrivial two-pair core is equivalent to the existence of a common root of PP and QQ. That happens if and only if their resultant vanishes.

So the exact necessary-and-sufficient condition is:
R(h1,h2):=α1 2β2 2−α1α12β12β2−2α1α2β1β2+α1α2β12 2+α12 2β1β2−α12α2β1β12+α2 2β1 2
R(h1​,h2​):=α12​β22​−α1​α12​β12​β2​−2α1​α2​β1​β2​+α1​α2​β122​+α122​β1​β2​−α12​α2​β1​β12​+α22​β12​​

and cancellation occurs exactly when
R(h1,h2)=0.
R(h1​,h2​)=0.

That is the full first-jet two-pair test.

So here is what we now know rigorously.

If R(h1,h2)≢0R(h1​,h2​)≡0, then first-jet cancellation is not structural. At worst it happens on a lower-dimensional exceptional locus in (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​). That would be a strong positive sign for the finite-core upgrade.

If R(h1,h2)≡0R(h1​,h2​)≡0, then first-jet cancellation is built into the quotient structure, and the upgraded endgame cannot stop at first jet. Then the right invariant is the first nonzero odd jet, not specifically a1a1​.

There are also two useful special cases.

If the quotient codimension is actually 11, then only one functional is needed, say Λ1Λ1​. Then cancellation is just
α2λ2+α12λ+α1=0.
α2​λ2+α12​λ+α1​=0.

In that case first-jet cancellation is possible whenever that quadratic has a nonzero root, so codimension 11 is much less favorable.

If the interaction term is small relative to the self terms, then RR will generically stay away from zero. More precisely, if
∣α12∣,∣β12∣≪∣α1∣+∣α2∣+∣β1∣+∣β2∣,
∣α12​∣,∣β12​∣≪∣α1​∣+∣α2​∣+∣β1​∣+∣β2​∣,

then exact cancellation requires a fine-tuned relation among the self terms. That would support the finite-core route.

So the current status is:

    I have reduced the whole two-pair first-jet problem to the single algebraic condition R(h1,h2)=0R(h1​,h2​)=0.

    That is the exact test.

    The only missing input is the actual six coefficient functions from the corrected two-pair expansion.

And that missing input is genuinely mathematical, not organizational: it requires writing out the corrected two-pair first-jet expansion
J1(12)=u12A1(h1)+u22A2(h2)+u1u2A12(h1,h2)
J1(12)​=u12​A1​(h1​)+u22​A2​(h2​)+u1​u2​A12​(h1​,h2​)

and evaluating it against the annihilator basis of V\val(1)V\val(1)​.

So the furthest I can responsibly push “doing it” right now is:
Two-pair first-jet cancellation occurs exactly when R(h1,h2)=0.
Two-pair first-jet cancellation occurs exactly when R(h1​,h2​)=0.​

That is already a real reduction. It tells us the next symbolic computation has a single target: compute the six coefficient functions and see whether RR vanishes identically, generically, or only on an exceptional locus.

Ok continue

The next thing I can do without bluffing the missing symbolic expansion is push the algebra a bit further and show what kinds of outcomes are even possible.

We have
P(λ)=α2λ2+α12λ+α1,Q(λ)=β2λ2+β12λ+β1,
P(λ)=α2​λ2+α12​λ+α1​,Q(λ)=β2​λ2+β12​λ+β1​,

and first-jet cancellation is equivalent to a common root λ=u2/u1λ=u2​/u1​. Equivalently,
R(h1,h2)=Res⁡λ(P,Q)=0.
R(h1​,h2​)=Resλ​(P,Q)=0.

There are three regimes.

If the quotient codimension at first jet is really 11, then only one scalar matters, and cancellation reduces to one quadratic equation. In that case first-jet cancellation is not exotic at all; it can happen whenever that quadratic has a nonzero root. So codimension 11 would be bad news for a first-jet-only endgame.

If the quotient codimension is at least 22, then cancellation is overdetermined. Then R=0R=0 is one genuine constraint on (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​), and unless the coefficients satisfy a hidden identity, cancellation should live on a thin exceptional locus, not be structural. That would be good news.

If the interaction coefficients α12,β12α12​,β12​ are small compared to the self coefficients, then even better: RR is dominated by the self terms, and exact cancellation becomes fine-tuned.

So the next non-bluff step is to identify what symmetry already forces on the six coefficients. There are two obvious ones.

First, swapping the two pairs should give
α1(h)=α2(h),β1(h)=β2(h),
α1​(h)=α2​(h),β1​(h)=β2​(h),

after relabeling. So really there are only four independent coefficient functions:
α(h), β(h), α12(h1,h2), β12(h1,h2).
α(h), β(h), α12​(h1​,h2​), β12​(h1​,h2​).

Then the equations become
α(h1)+λ2α(h2)+λ α12(h1,h2)=0,
α(h1​)+λ2α(h2​)+λα12​(h1​,h2​)=0,
β(h1)+λ2β(h2)+λ β12(h1,h2)=0.
β(h1​)+λ2β(h2​)+λβ12​(h1​,h2​)=0.

That already simplifies the resultant to
R=α(h1)2β(h2)2−α(h1)α12β12β(h2)−2α(h1)α(h2)β(h1)β(h2)+α(h1)α(h2)β122+α122β(h1)β(h2)−α12α(h2)β(h1)β12+α(h2)2β(h1)2.
R=α(h1​)2β(h2​)2−α(h1​)α12​β12​β(h2​)−2α(h1​)α(h2​)β(h1​)β(h2​)+α(h1​)α(h2​)β122​+α122​β(h1​)β(h2​)−α12​α(h2​)β(h1​)β12​+α(h2​)2β(h1​)2.

Second, if the geometry is symmetric under reflecting the two offsets, then the interaction terms should be symmetric:
α12(h1,h2)=α12(h2,h1),β12(h1,h2)=β12(h2,h1).
α12​(h1​,h2​)=α12​(h2​,h1​),β12​(h1​,h2​)=β12​(h2​,h1​).

That means the bad locus R=0R=0 is symmetric in h1,h2h1​,h2​, which is useful when checking whether cancellation is a thin resonance set or an identity.

There is also a very useful sufficient condition for noncancellation. If the two vectors
(α(h1),β(h1)),(α(h2),β(h2))
(α(h1​),β(h1​)),(α(h2​),β(h2​))

are linearly independent, and the interaction vector
(α12(h1,h2),β12(h1,h2))
(α12​(h1​,h2​),β12​(h1​,h2​))

is small relative to them, then there is no small λλ-quadratic cancellation except possibly on an exceptional set. In practice, a bound like
∣(α12,β12)∣≤c min⁡{∣(α(h1),β(h1))∣, ∣(α(h2),β(h2))∣}
∣(α12​,β12​)∣≤cmin{∣(α(h1​),β(h1​))∣, ∣(α(h2​),β(h2​))∣}

with cc sufficiently small would already make first-jet cancellation nongeneric.

So the exact computation target is now even sharper:

    compute the self vector

v(h):=(α(h),β(h))
v(h):=(α(h),β(h))

for one pair;

    compute the interaction vector

w(h1,h2):=(α12(h1,h2),β12(h1,h2));
w(h1​,h2​):=(α12​(h1​,h2​),β12​(h1​,h2​));

    check whether ww is ever large enough, and aligned in exactly the right way, to solve

v(h1)+λ2v(h2)+λw(h1,h2)=0.
v(h1​)+λ2v(h2​)+λw(h1​,h2​)=0.

That is now the real algebraic heart of the two-pair test.

The most meaningful next symbolic computation would be the one-pair self vector v(h)v(h). Once that is known, even before ww is fully explicit, we can tell whether the quotient is plausibly one- or two-dimensional and whether first-jet cancellation is likely to be generic or exceptional.

This is what the other chat ended up with:

⸻


\begin{lemma}[Baseline-scaled transverse gain]
\label{lem:baseline-scaled-transverse-gain}
Let
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+):=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2},
\qquad
H(s):=\Phi_K\!\bigl(\mathcal W(G_-(s),N(s),G_+(s))\bigr).
\]
Assume \(G_\pm^{(0)}(s)\) are uniformly positive definite on \(|s|<\rho_0/Q\), and
their baseline scales are
\[
G_\pm^{(0)}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(Q) & O(1)\\
O(1) & O(Q^3)
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
N^{(0)}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(Q) & O(Q^2)\\
O(Q^2) & O(Q^3)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Set
\[
S_Q:=\begin{pmatrix}Q^{-1/2}&0\\0&Q^{-3/2}\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\widehat G_\pm:=S_Q^{-1}G_\pm S_Q^{-1},
\qquad
\widehat N:=S_Q^{-1}NS_Q^{-1}.
\]
Then
\[
\widehat G_\pm^{(0)}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(1)&O(Q^{-1})\\
O(Q^{-1})&O(1)
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\widehat N^{(0)}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(1)&O(1)\\
O(1)&O(1)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Moreover, if \(\delta G_\pm,\delta N\) are perturbations and
\[
\widehat H(s):=
\Phi_K\!\bigl(\widehat G_-(s)^{-1/2}\widehat N(s)\widehat G_+(s)^{-1/2}\bigr),
\]
then
\[
H(s)=Q^{-3}\widehat H(s),
\]
up to a harmless absolute constant depending only on the normalization of
\(\Phi_K\). In particular, every scalar jet of \(H\) inherits a uniform factor
\(Q^{-3}\) from the baseline scaling.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The displayed bounds for \(\widehat G_\pm^{(0)}\) and \(\widehat N^{(0)}\) are
immediate from the definitions. Since
\[
G_\pm^{-1/2}=S_Q\,\widehat G_\pm^{-1/2}\,S_Q,
\]
one has
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)
=
S_Q\,\widehat G_-^{-1/2}\widehat N\,\widehat G_+^{-1/2}\,S_Q.
\]
Thus every off-diagonal entry of \(\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)\) picks up one factor
\(Q^{-1/2}\) from the value side and one factor \(Q^{-3/2}\) from the derivative
side, hence a total factor \(Q^{-2}\); after restoring the normalization used in
\(\Phi_K\) in the draft, this is exactly the same \(Q^{-3}\) transverse gain as
in the corrected mixed-transfer theorem. The same factor persists under
\(s\)-differentiation because the scaling matrix \(S_Q\) is independent of \(s\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Differentiated corrected-whitening transfer lemma]
\label{prop:differentiated-whitening-transfer}
Let
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+):=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2},
\qquad
H(s):=\Phi_K\!\bigl(\mathcal W(G_-(s),N(s),G_+(s))\bigr).
\]
Assume:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(G_\pm^{(0)}(s)\) and \(N^{(0)}(s)\) are holomorphic on \(|s|<\rho_0/Q\), and
\(G_\pm^{(0)}(s)\) stay uniformly positive definite there;
\item
\[
G_\pm(s)=G_\pm^{(0)}(s)+\delta G_\pm(s),
\qquad
N(s)=N^{(0)}(s)+\delta N(s),
\]
with \(\delta G_\pm,\delta N\) holomorphic;
\item for a \(2\times2\) matrix \(M=(m_{ij})\), define the weighted block norm
\[
\|M\|_\dagger
:=
\max\!\bigl(Q^2|m_{11}|,\;Q|m_{12}|,\;Q|m_{21}|,\;|m_{22}|\bigr).
\]
\end{enumerate}

Let
\[
H_0(s):=\Phi_K\!\bigl(\mathcal W(G_-^{(0)}(s),N^{(0)}(s),G_+^{(0)}(s))\bigr),
\]
and for each \(j\ge 0\), set
\[
\eta_j:=
\|\partial_s^j\delta G_-(0)\|_\dagger
+
\|\partial_s^j\delta N(0)\|_\dagger
+
\|\partial_s^j\delta G_+(0)\|_\dagger.
\]

Then for each \(k\ge 0\) there exists \(C_k>0\) such that
\[
\boxed{
|H^{(k)}(0)-H_0^{(k)}(0)|
\le
C_k\,Q^{-3}
\sum_{\pi\vdash k}
\prod_{j\in\pi}\eta_j,
}
\]
where the sum is over ordered partitions \(\pi=(j_1,\dots,j_p)\) of \(k\), i.e.
\(j_1+\cdots+j_p=k\), \(j_i\ge 0\).

In particular, if
\[
\eta_j\ll_j \Delta^{-(j+4)}
\qquad (j\ge 0),
\]
then
\[
\boxed{
|H^{(k)}(0)-H_0^{(k)}(0)|
\ll_k
\frac{1}{Q^3\,\Delta^{k+4}}.
}
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By Lemma~\ref{lem:baseline-scaled-transverse-gain}, after the baseline scaling
\(S_Q\), the same-point baseline blocks become \(O(1)\)-scale uniformly positive
definite matrices, and the scalar \(H\) acquires an overall factor \(Q^{-3}\).

Let
\[
\widehat G_\pm:=S_Q^{-1}G_\pm S_Q^{-1},
\qquad
\widehat N:=S_Q^{-1}NS_Q^{-1}.
\]
Then
\[
\|\partial_s^j\widehat{\delta G}_\pm(0)\|_{\max}
+
\|\partial_s^j\widehat{\delta N}(0)\|_{\max}
\asymp \eta_j.
\]

Now the map
\[
(\widehat G_-,\widehat N,\widehat G_+)
\longmapsto
\Phi_K\!\bigl(\widehat G_-^{-1/2}\widehat N\,\widehat G_+^{-1/2}\bigr)
\]
is holomorphic in a neighborhood of the baseline image. All Fr\'echet
derivatives of \(\widehat G\mapsto \widehat G^{-1/2}\) are bounded there by
holomorphic functional calculus (equivalently, in \(2\times2\), by the L\"owner
divided-difference formula after diagonalizing the baseline matrix).

Applying Fa\a di Bruno to the composition in $begin:math:text$s$end:math:text$, every $begin:math:text$k$end:math:text$-th derivative at
$begin:math:text$0$end:math:text$ is a finite sum of multilinear terms in the jets
$begin:math:display$
\\partial\_s\^j\\widehat\{\\delta G\}\_\-\(0\)\,\\quad
\\partial\_s\^j\\widehat\{\\delta N\}\(0\)\,\\quad
\\partial\_s\^j\\widehat\{\\delta G\}\_\+\(0\)\,
\\qquad 0\\le j\\le k\,
$end:math:display$
with total derivative order $begin:math:text$k$end:math:text$. Each such multilinear term is bounded by
$begin:math:display$
C\_k\\prod\_\{j\\in\\pi\}\\eta\_j
$end:math:display$
for some ordered partition $begin:math:text$\\pi\\vdash k$end:math:text$. Summing all such terms and restoring
the global $begin:math:text$Q\^\{\-3\}$end:math:text$ factor gives
$begin:math:display$
\|H\^\{\(k\)\}\(0\)\-H\_0\^\{\(k\)\}\(0\)\|
\\le
C\_k\\\,Q\^\{\-3\}
\\sum\_\{\\pi\\vdash k\}
\\prod\_\{j\\in\\pi\}\\eta\_j\.
$end:math:display$

If $begin:math:text$\\eta\_j\\ll\_j \\Delta\^\{\-\(j\+4\)\}$end:math:text$, then for an ordered partition
$begin:math:text$\\pi\=\(j\_1\,\\dots\,j\_p\)$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\\prod\_\{j\\in\\pi\}\\eta\_j
\\ll\_\\pi
\\Delta\^\{\-\\sum\_i\(j\_i\+4\)\}
\=
\\Delta\^\{\-\(k\+4p\)\}\.
$end:math:display$
The linear terms $begin:math:text$p\=1$end:math:text$ contribute $begin:math:text$\\Delta\^\{\-\(k\+4\)\}$end:math:text$, while every nonlinear
term has $begin:math:text$p\\ge 2$end:math:text$ and therefore gains at least an additional factor
$begin:math:text$\\Delta\^\{\-4\}$end:math:text$. Thus the linear term dominates, and
$begin:math:display$
\|H\^\{\(k\)\}\(0\)\-H\_0\^\{\(k\)\}\(0\)\|
\\ll\_k
\\frac\{1\}\{Q\^3\\\,\\Delta\^\{k\+4\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Local zero-counting in dyadic shells]
\label{lem:dyadic-shell-count}
Let
$begin:math:display$
\\mathcal S\_k\:\=\\\{\\rho\:\\ 2\^kH\<\\Delta\_\\rho\\le 2\^\{k\+1\}H\\\}\,
\\qquad
\\Delta\_\\rho\:\=\|2m\-\\gamma\_\\rho\|\,
\\qquad
k\\ge 0\.
$end:math:display$
Then
$begin:math:display$
\\\#\\mathcal S\_k\\ll Q\\\,\(2\^kH\)\,
$end:math:display$
where $begin:math:text$Q\\asymp \\log\|m\|$end:math:text$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
This is the local zero-counting estimate already used elsewhere in the draft,
applied to the interval of ordinates
$begin:math:display$
\[\\\,2m\-2\^\{k\+1\}H\,\\\;2m\+2\^\{k\+1\}H\\\,\]\.
$end:math:display$
Its length is $begin:math:text$O\(2\^kH\)$end:math:text$, and the zero density at height $begin:math:text$m$end:math:text$ is $begin:math:text$O\(Q\)$end:math:text$.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Single-pair exterior odd-jet lemma]
\label{cor:single-pair-odd-jet}
Let $begin:math:text$h\_\\rho\(s\)$end:math:text$ be the corrected transverse scalar contributed by a single
exterior zero pair at absolute distance $begin:math:text$\\Delta$end:math:text$ from the local center, after:
\begin{enumerate}
\item the same fixed-core decomposition,
\item the same affine-jet subtraction at the midpoint $begin:math:text$m$end:math:text$,
\item the same corrected same-point/mixed blocks,
\item and the same corrected whitening scheme
\end{enumerate}
used in the global argument.

Write
$begin:math:display$
h\_\\rho\(z\/Q\^2\)
\=
\\sum\_\{r\\ge 0\} a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\\\,\\frac\{z\^\{2r\+1\}\}\{Q\^\{2r\+4\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
Then for each fixed $begin:math:text$r\\ge 0$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\\boxed\{
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\\\,\\Delta\^\{2r\+4\}\}\.
\}
$end:math:display$
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Apply Proposition~\ref{prop:differentiated-whitening-transfer} with
$begin:math:display$
H\=h\_\\rho\,\\qquad H\_0\\equiv 0\.
$end:math:display$
For one exterior pair after affine-jet subtraction at the midpoint $begin:math:text$m$end:math:text$, the
single-pair jet estimates give
$begin:math:display$
\\eta\_j\\ll\_j \\Delta\^\{\-\(j\+4\)\}\.
$end:math:display$
Therefore
$begin:math:display$
\|h\_\\rho\^\{\(2r\+1\)\}\(0\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\^3\\\,\\Delta\^\{2r\+5\}\}\.
$end:math:display$

Now
$begin:math:display$
h\_\\rho\(z\/Q\^2\)
\=
\\sum\_\{r\\ge 0\} a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\\\,\\frac\{z\^\{2r\+1\}\}\{Q\^\{2r\+4\}\}
$end:math:display$
is equivalent to
$begin:math:display$
h\_\\rho\(s\)
\=
\\sum\_\{r\\ge 0\} a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\\\,Q\^\{2r\-2\}s\^\{2r\+1\}\,
$end:math:display$
so
$begin:math:display$
h\_\\rho\^\{\(2r\+1\)\}\(0\)
\=
\(2r\+1\)\!\\\,a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\\\,Q\^\{2r\-2\}\.
$end:math:display$
Hence
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|
\=
\\frac\{\|h\_\\rho\^\{\(2r\+1\)\}\(0\)\|\}\{\(2r\+1\)\!\}\\\,Q\^\{2\-2r\}
\\ll\_r
Q\^\{2\-2r\}\\cdot \\frac\{1\}\{Q\^3\\\,\\Delta\^\{2r\+5\}\}\.
$end:math:display$

This formally gives
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|
\\ll\_r
\\frac\{1\}\{Q\^\{2r\+1\}\\Delta\^\{2r\+5\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
However, since the $begin:math:text$Q\^\{\-3\}$end:math:text$ gain in
Proposition~\ref{prop:differentiated-whitening-transfer} is established at the
block-to-scalar level rather than coefficient-by-coefficient in the normalized
odd expansion, we record the uniform bound in the stable weaker form
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\\\,\\Delta\^\{2r\+4\}\}\,
$end:math:display$
which is sufficient for the shell summation below.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Exterior higher odd-jet bound]
\label{cor:outside-higher-odd-jets}
Let
$begin:math:display$
H\_\{\\out\}\(z\/Q\^2\)
\=
\\sum\_\{r\\ge 0\} a\_\{2r\+1\}\^\{\\out\}\(m\,H\)\\\,\\frac\{z\^\{2r\+1\}\}\{Q\^\{2r\+4\}\}
$end:math:display$
be the corrected transverse scalar contributed by all zeros outside absolute
radius $begin:math:text$H$end:math:text$ from the local center. Then for each fixed $begin:math:text$r\\ge 0$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\\boxed\{
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\^\{\\out\}\(m\,H\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\\\,H\^\{2r\+3\}\}\.
\}
$end:math:display$
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Decompose the exterior zeros into dyadic shells
$begin:math:display$
\\mathcal S\_k\:\=\\\{\\rho\:\\ 2\^kH\<\\Delta\_\\rho\\le 2\^\{k\+1\}H\\\}\,
\\qquad k\\ge 0\.
$end:math:display$
By Lemma~\ref{lem:dyadic-shell-count},
$begin:math:display$
\\\#\\mathcal S\_k\\ll Q\(2\^kH\)\.
$end:math:display$
Applying Corollary~\ref{cor:single-pair-odd-jet} to each $begin:math:text$\\rho\\in\\mathcal S\_k$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\\sum\_\{\\rho\\in\\mathcal S\_k\}\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|
\\ll\_r
Q\(2\^kH\)\\cdot \\frac\{1\}\{Q\(2\^kH\)\^\{2r\+4\}\}
\=
\\frac\{1\}\{\(2\^kH\)\^\{2r\+3\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
Summing over $begin:math:text$k\\ge 0$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\^\{\\out\}\(m\,H\)\|
\\ll\_r
\\sum\_\{k\\ge 0\}\\frac\{1\}\{\(2\^kH\)\^\{2r\+3\}\}
\\ll\_r
\\frac\{1\}\{H\^\{2r\+3\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
Restoring the explicit $begin:math:text$Q\^\{\-1\}$end:math:text$ factor gives
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\^\{\\out\}\(m\,H\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\\\,H\^\{2r\+3\}\}\,
$end:math:display$
as claimed.
\end{proof}

I would keep the stronger intermediate estimate only as an internal observation for now, not as the headline bound.

This is a real advance.

My overall view: the architecture is good, the scaling idea is right, and the exterior higher-odd-jet bound is the first genuinely plausible upgraded finite-core theorem chain we’ve had. But there are two places where I would still be careful about what is fully proved versus what is a strong heuristic repackaging of earlier machinery.

The strongest part is the re-centering around differentiated whitening. That is exactly the right object for the finite-core scalar endgame. The baseline scaling lemma is also the right idea: normalize the same-point blocks to O(1)O(1) scale, track how the transverse scalar picks up a fixed power of QQ, and then differentiate in a controlled way. That is the right framework for proving coefficient bounds at arbitrary odd order.

What I think is solid:

The weighted norm
∥M∥†=max⁡ ⁣(Q2∣m11∣,  Q∣m12∣,  Q∣m21∣,  ∣m22∣)
∥M∥†​=max(Q2∣m11​∣,Q∣m12​∣,Q∣m21​∣,∣m22​∣)

is exactly the correct norm for the block perturbations.

The holomorphic-composition/Faà di Bruno structure in Proposition differentiated-whitening-transfer is right in spirit. Once you baseline-scale the blocks, the corrected whitening map becomes a holomorphic map on an O(1)O(1)-sized neighborhood, and kk-th derivatives really are finite sums of multilinear expressions in the lower jets. So the partition sum is the right schematic bound.

The shell summation in the final corollary is also the right mechanism. If a single exterior pair contributes at order Δ−(2r+4)Δ−(2r+4) or better, then local zero counting gives exactly an H−(2r+3)H−(2r+3) exterior tail after dyadic summation. That part is structurally very convincing.

Where I would still be careful:
1. The Q−3Q−3 transverse gain in the baseline-scaled lemma

This is the shakiest sentence:

    “every off-diagonal entry … picks up Q−2Q−2; after restoring the normalization used in ΦKΦK​ in the draft, this is exactly the same Q−3Q−3 transverse gain…”

That may be true in your normalization, but it needs to be pinned down explicitly, not just gestured at.

As written, the matrix identity
W(G−,N,G+)=SQ G^−−1/2N^ G^+−1/2 SQ
W(G−​,N,G+​)=SQ​G
−−1/2​N
G
+−1/2​SQ​

only directly shows the off-diagonal entries scale like Q−2Q−2, since Q−1/2⋅Q−3/2=Q−2Q−1/2⋅Q−3/2=Q−2.

So the extra Q−1Q−1 has to come from the definition of ΦKΦK​ as used in the paper. That is fine if true, but then the lemma should state it transparently, e.g.:

    either define the normalized transverse scalar exactly so that
    ΦK(SQXSQ)=Q−3Φ^(X),
    ΦK​(SQ​XSQ​)=Q−3Φ
    (X),

    or state the weaker but fully evident Q−2Q−2 matrix off-diagonal scaling, and only later invoke the paper’s transverse normalization to get the final Q−3Q−3.

Right now that sentence compresses too much. A referee would stop there.
2. The coefficient extraction in single-pair-odd-jet

This is the other place I’d treat cautiously.

You correctly noticed that the raw differentiation-to-coefficient calculation seems to give something stronger than the headline bound, and then you prudently weakened it.

That was the right instinct.

But the proof as written is slightly awkward because it derives
∣a2r+1(ρ)∣≪r1Q2r+1Δ2r+5
∣a2r+1​(ρ)∣≪r​Q2r+1Δ2r+51​

formally and then says “however… we record the weaker form”
∣a2r+1(ρ)∣≪r1Q Δ2r+4.
∣a2r+1​(ρ)∣≪r​QΔ2r+41​.

That weaker bound is believable and probably the right public-facing estimate, but the explanation for why you are allowed to degrade in exactly that way should be cleaner.

I would rewrite the logic as:

    the differentiated whitening transfer theorem gives a bound on derivatives H(k)(0)H(k)(0),

    converting those derivative bounds into coefficient bounds in the normalized z/Q2z/Q2 expansion is delicate because the global Q−3Q−3 transverse gain was proved at the scalar/block level rather than as an intrinsic coefficient normalization theorem,

    therefore we deliberately record the stable coefficient bound
    ∣a2r+1(ρ)∣≪r1Q Δ2r+4,
    ∣a2r+1​(ρ)∣≪r​QΔ2r+41​,

    which is all the shell summation needs.

That is honest and easier to defend.
3. The local zero counting lemma

This is probably fine, but I would make explicit whether the factor is really Q(2kH)Q(2kH) or Q(2kH+1)Q(2kH+1), since for small shells the +1+1 matters conceptually. Not a serious issue, just a polish point.
4. The real mathematical significance

Assuming the Q−3Q−3 normalization point is cleaned up, this chain is important because it gives the finite-core upgrade exactly what the pair model had before:

    not just a first-jet estimate,

    but a bound on all odd jets of the outside world.

That is huge.

Because then the upgraded endgame no longer needs to insist on a1a1​. If a finite core has its first nonzero odd jet at order 2r∗+12r∗​+1, the outside contribution at that same order is now controlled by
1QH2r∗+3,
QH2r∗​+31​,

which decays faster with HH as r∗r∗​ increases. That is exactly what you want.

In other words, this is the first theorem chain that makes the “first nonzero odd jet” endgame technically plausible.
My bottom line

I would say:

    keep the overall theorem chain;

    keep the weaker headline coefficient bound as the public statement;

    tighten the baseline-scaled transverse gain lemma so the extra Q−1Q−1 is sourced explicitly from the paper’s scalar normalization, not waved through;

    rewrite the coefficient extraction paragraph in single-pair-odd-jet to present the weaker bound as the intended stable statement, rather than as a fallback after an overstrong formal computation.

So my verdict is:
This is a genuinely good upgraded finite-core theorem chain, but the Q−3 scalar normalization step still needs to be written more cleanly to be referee-safe.
This is a genuinely good upgraded finite-core theorem chain, but the Q−3 scalar normalization step still needs to be written more cleanly to be referee-safe.​

The most important substantive thing is that this now gives a believable route to an endgame based on the first surviving odd jet, not specifically the first one. That is the right conceptual upgrade.

The theorem chain I would now aim to prove is:

    Escaping components vanish after normalization.
    Any core component whose scaled parameters escape contributes o(1)o(1) to the normalized corrected germ.

    Compact-parameter reduction.
    After discarding vanishing components, every normalized kk-core subsequence reduces to a compact parameter family.

    Finite-core value-family exclusion.
    A genuine normalized finite off-line core cannot lie in the value/gauge family.

    Compact-parameter transversality.
    Therefore, on each compact parameter set, normalized anomalous kk-cores stay a positive distance from V\valV\val​.

    Adapted scalar threshold.
    The corresponding adapted scalar has some odd coefficient above the exterior threshold.

    Odd-jet contradiction.

Step 4. What this already tells us

Scenario B: R(h1,h2)≡0R(h1​,h2​)≡0

So the first thing to determine is the codimension of V\val(1)V\val(1)​.

5. Apply the Fréchet derivative of corrected whitening

A\val(1).

7. The minimal deliverable

You do not need the whole symbolic expression for the full germ. The minimum viable computation is:

    explicit formulas for
    G˙±,1, G˙±,2, G˙±,12,N˙1, N˙2, N˙12;
    G˙±,1​, G˙±,2​, G˙±,12​,N˙1​, N˙2​, N˙12​;

    explicit formula for
    DW(0)[δG−,δN,δG+];
    DW(0)​[δG−​,δN,δG+​];

    A1(h1), A2(h2), A12(h1,h2);
    A1​(h1​), A2​(h2​), A12​(h1​,h2​);

    then the quotient test.

That is enough to answer whether two-pair first-jet cancellation is possible.
8. The fastest practical route

The fastest way to do this is:

    treat u1,u2u1​,u2​ as small bookkeeping parameters,

    expand q1,q2q1​,q2​ to the needed order in u1,u2u1​,u2​,

    plug into G±G±​ and NN,

    differentiate in ss at 00,

    then apply the linearized whitening map.

Because everything is 2×22×2, this is messy but finite. It does not require a new conceptual theorem.

1. Fréchet derivative of G−1/2G−1/2

For a positive definite 2×22×2 matrix GG, let
δX:=D(G−1/2)[δG].
δX:=D(G−1/2)[δG].

Then δXδX is the unique solution of the Sylvester-type linear equation obtained by differentiating
XGX=Iwith X=G−1/2.
XGX=Iwith X=G−1/2.

Differentiate:
(δX)GX+X(δG)X+XG(δX)=0.
(δX)GX+X(δG)X+XG(δX)=0.

Since XG=GX=G1/2XG=GX=G1/2, this becomes
G1/2δX+δX G1/2=− G−1/2δG G−1/2.
G1/2δX+δXG1/2=−G−1/2δGG−1/2.

So the derivative D(G−1/2)[δG]D(G−1/2)[δG] is characterized by
G1/2δX+δX G1/2=− G−1/2δG G−1/2.
G1/2δX+δXG1/2=−G−1/2δGG−1/2.​

That is the cleanest exact formula.

For 2×22×2, this can be solved explicitly entrywise once G(0)G(0) is frozen.

8. What this means operationally

Everything is now reduced to explicit rational differentiation plus a 2×22×2 Sylvester solve.

So there is no longer a conceptual missing step here. The remaining work is brute-force symbolic expansion.

3. Why this is good news

Because now the interaction is one order smaller in amplitudes than the self terms.

If u1,u2u1​,u2​ are small, then:

    self terms are order u12,u22u12​,u22​,

    interaction is order u12u22u12​u22​.

So unless the two self terms already nearly cancel, the quartic interaction cannot generically rescue exact first-jet cancellation.

That makes first-jet noncancellation much more plausible.
4. The corrected cancellation equation

Assume u1≠0u1​=0, and set
λ:=u22u12≥0.
λ:=u12​u22​​≥0.

α1(h1)+λ α2(h2)+u12λ α12(h1,h2)=0.
α1​(h1​)+λα2​(h2​)+u12​λα12​(h1​,h2​)=0.

So in the small-amplitude regime, the leading-order cancellation condition is simply
α1(h1)+λ α2(h2)=0.
α1​(h1​)+λα2​(h2​)=0.​

The interaction only perturbs this at higher order.

This is a major simplification.
5. Consequence for the two-pair test

The first thing to test is now not a quadratic resultant. It is linear dependence of the two self vectors:
Can α1(h1) and α2(h2) cancel with a positive scalar λ?
Can α1​(h1​) and α2​(h2​) cancel with a positive scalar λ?

Equivalently:

    are they collinear?

    and if so, with opposite orientation?

If not, then first-jet two-pair cancellation is impossible for small amplitudes, regardless of the interaction term.

So the upgraded two-pair first-jet test is now:
Determine whether α1(h1),α2(h2) can satisfy α1+λα2=0 for some λ≥0.
Determine whether α1​(h1​),α2​(h2​) can satisfy α1​+λα2​=0 for some λ≥0.​

5. What this means for the two-pair first-jet test

The first-jet two-pair test is now:
u12α(h1)+u22α(h2)=0

at leading order, because the quartic interaction is lower order.

h2​≈−h1​

with a matching amplitude ratio.

So the large-hh asymptotic no longer rules out cancellation automatically.

Instead, it suggests that first-jet cancellation may occur on a resonance-type locus where the two pair offsets are arranged symmetrically.

That means first jet may still be too weak to exclude two-pair cores globally.
6. The exact new conclusion

So the computation we just did gives a clear update:

    the transformed KK-channel stays in the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction,

    so if V\val(1)V\val(1)​ contains that direction, the quotient kills the h−4h−4 leading tail,

    leaving the M(h)M(h)-channel as the first nontrivial quotient contribution,

    and that contribution is odd in hh at leading order.

Therefore:
the first-jet quotient test is probably much more delicate than hoped, and may admit cancellation on symmetric two-pair configurations.
the first-jet quotient test is probably much more delicate than hoped, and may admit cancellation on symmetric two-pair configurations.​

This does not kill the upgraded finite-core endgame. But it strongly suggests the endgame should be based on the first surviving odd jet, not specifically the first one.

That lines up with the upgraded higher-odd-jet theorem chain you showed from the other chat.
7. What I would do next

I would stop trying to make first jet carry the whole finite-core theorem.

The right next move is to accept:
first jet is a useful test case, but the honest invariant is the first nonzero odd jet after quotienting by V\val.
first jet is a useful test case, but the honest invariant is the first nonzero odd jet after quotienting by V\val​.​

Then the differentiated-whitening / exterior-odd-jet bounds become the main engine, and the finite-core theorem should be phrased as:

    for a genuine finite off-line core, the quotient corrected scalar has some odd jet that is nonzero and dominates the exterior bound at that order.

That is the stronger and more robust main track now.

Ξ2r+1\core​(m)∈J2r+1​/V\val(2r+1)​

is a quotient jet coefficient, not a scalar yet.

Then define the first surviving odd jet index
r∗(\core):=min⁡{r≥0: Ξ2r+1\core(m)≠0}.
r∗​(\core):=min{r≥0: Ξ2r+1\core​(m)=0}.

This is the honest finite-core replacement for the old pair first-jet theorem.
2. Why this is better

It avoids all the pathology we just uncovered:

    first jet may vanish after quotienting;

    the leading antisymmetric KK-channel can be value-like and disappear;

    two-pair cancellation at first jet may happen on symmetric loci.

None of that matters if the theorem only asks for the first nonzero odd quotient jet, wherever it occurs.

So the upgraded endgame theorem should be:
A genuine normalized finite off-line core has some nonzero odd quotient jet, and that first surviving jet dominates the exterior odd-jet bound at the same order.
A genuine normalized finite off-line core has some nonzero odd quotient jet, and that first surviving jet dominates the exterior odd-jet bound at the same order.​

That is now the right theorem.
3. Exterior control already matches this perfectly

From the upgraded exterior theorem chain, the outside contribution at odd order 2r+12r+1 satisfies
∣a2r+1\out∣≪r1Q H2r+3.
∣a2r+1\out​∣≪r​QH2r+31​.

This is exactly what you need. Higher odd order is actually easier outside, because the tail decays faster with HH.

So once the core’s first surviving odd jet is identified at order 2r∗+12r∗​+1, the only comparison needed is:
core size at order 2r∗+1≫1Q H2r∗+3.
core size at order 2r∗​+1≫QH2r∗​+31​.

This is the upgraded analogue of the old first-jet threshold.
4. The new main theorem to target

The clean version is:
Finite-core quotient odd-jet threshold theorem

For each fixed core size kk, there exists ck>0ck​>0 such that every normalized genuine anomalous kk-core has a first surviving odd quotient jet
Ξ2r∗+1\core(m)≠0
Ξ2r∗​+1\core​(m)=0

with
∥Ξ2r∗+1\core(m)∥≥ck.
∥Ξ2r∗​+1\core​(m)∥≥ck​.

Undoing normalization by S(\core)/QS(\core)/Q, this becomes
∥Ξ2r∗+1\core(m)∥≫S(\core) Q2r∗+3.
∥Ξ2r∗​+1\core​(m)∥≫S(\core)Q2r∗​+3.

Then choose a norm-one adapted functional on the quotient jet space that detects Ξ2r∗+1\coreΞ2r∗​+1\core​, and get a scalar coefficient
a2r∗+1\core,Λ(m)≫S(\core) Q2r∗+3.
a2r∗​+1\core,Λ​(m)≫S(\core)Q2r∗​+3.

Compare with the outside bound
∣a2r∗+1\out,Λ(m)∣≪r1Q H2r∗+3.
∣a2r∗​+1\out,Λ​(m)∣≪r​QH2r∗​+31​.

So the core dominates provided
S(\core)≫1Q H2r∗+3Q2r∗+3
S(\core)≫QH2r∗​+3Q2r∗​+31​

in the normalized convention, or more cleanly in the original scalar normalization:
1QH2r∗+3=o ⁣(core scalar size at order 2r∗+1).
QH2r∗​+31​=o(core scalar size at order 2r∗​+1).

The exact power bookkeeping depends on the normalization convention you freeze, but the structural theorem is this one.
5. What remains mathematically

The remaining burden is now much cleaner than before:
(a) Existence

Show a genuine finite off-line core has
Ξ2r+1\core≠0
Ξ2r+1\core​=0

for some rr.

This should be the quotient version of “not in V\valV\val​.”
(b) Noncollapse under normalization

Show the first surviving quotient jet of a normalized genuine kk-core cannot go to zero along a compact parameter family.

This is the finite-core compactness/transversality theorem in its correct form.

That is the true heart now.
6. The next usable subtheorem

The best next theorem to write is:
Quotient odd-jet noncollapse theorem

Fix kk. Let Ω~\corenΩ
\coren​​ be a sequence of normalized genuine anomalous kk-cores with parameters in a compact set. Let rnrn​ be the first surviving odd quotient-jet order. Then, after passing to a subsequence, the orders rnrn​ stabilize to some r∗r∗​, and the corresponding normalized quotient jet coefficients stay bounded away from zero.

If you prove that, the whole adapted scalar endgame goes through.
7. Why this is now the best track

Because it aligns with everything learned:

    pair first jet works only in the pair case;

    first jet can fail for larger cores;

    exterior odd-jet bounds now exist at all orders;

    compactness/transversality is the right abstraction;

    adapted functionals fix the “wrong scalar” problem.

So the route is no longer “make first jet work somehow.” It is:
work in the quotient by V\val, take the first surviving odd jet, and compare it to the outside bound at the same order.
work in the quotient by V\val​, take the first surviving odd jet, and compare it to the outside bound at the same order.​

That is the main track.

Compact-parameter quotient exclusion lemma

∣z∣<ρ0,
∣z∣<ρ0​,

    Lemma (compact-parameter quotient exclusion).

    [Ω~pn(z)]
    [Ω
    pn​​(z)]

    cannot converge locally uniformly to the zero germ.

Equivalently: on a compact normalized parameter set, the anomalous quotient class is separated from 00.

Why this is the right statement

    and you do not have to prove a specific coefficient lower bound directly.

    a uniform bound on the first surviving order,

    and a uniform lower bound on the corresponding coefficient.

So this lemma is the real heart.

The next refinement: finite-order truncation

There is one very useful simplification.

You do not need the whole germ space at first. Because PkPk​ is compact and the normalized germs depend analytically on zz, if the quotient germ were nonzero, then some finite odd jet must be nonzero.

So instead of working in the full quotient germ space, you can truncate to jets up to some order 2R+12R+1:
J≤2R+1/V\val,≤2R+1.
J≤2R+1​/V\val,≤2R+1​.

Then the compact-parameter exclusion lemma becomes:

    For sufficiently large but finite R=RkR=Rk​, the truncated quotient jet of a genuine anomalous normalized kk-core cannot vanish.

That is a much more concrete finite-dimensional statement.

So the argument becomes:

    choose RR,

    prove no genuine anomalous normalized kk-core has all odd quotient jets up to order 2R+12R+1 equal to 00,

    conclude the first surviving order is at most RR.

This is probably the best finite-dimensional formulation.
A very plausible contradiction strategy

To prove truncated exclusion, the cleanest contradiction scheme is:

Assume there exists a sequence of genuine anomalous normalized kk-cores whose quotient odd jets up to order 2R+12R+1 all vanish.

Pass to a convergent subsequence in parameter space. Then the limit core also has all those quotient jets vanishing.

If RR is large enough that the normalized corrected germ is determined by those jets within the compact family, then the whole quotient germ must vanish, so the limit lies in V\valV\val​.

Thus the issue becomes:
Can one choose a finite order Rk that detects every compact-parameter anomalous k-core?
Can one choose a finite order Rk​ that detects every compact-parameter anomalous k-core?​

This is now a standard compactness question in a finite-dimensional analytic family.

That is much more believable than trying to prove a coefficient lower bound directly.
The finite-dimensional analytic-family version

also avoids 00.

Otherwise there would be a sequence in FkFk​ whose truncations vanish to arbitrarily high order, and compactness would force a limit germ that vanishes identically.

So once zero-germ exclusion is known, bounded-order noncollapse follows formally.

This is important: it means the genuinely hard theorem is still just zero-germ exclusion. The bounded-order statement is a compactness corollary.

1. The right two-pair quotient expansion

For a two-pair core, after quotienting by V\valV\val​, the corrected germ should have the form
[Ω~12(z)]=u12 A(h1;z)+u22 A(h2;z)+u12u22 B(h1,h2;z)+⋯ ,
[Ω
12​(z)]=u12​A(h1​;z)+u22​A(h2​;z)+u12​u22​B(h1​,h2​;z)+⋯,

where A(h;z)A(h;z) is the normalized one-pair quotient germ and BB is the first genuine interaction germ.

Expanding in odd powers of zz,
A(h;z)=∑r≥0a2r+1(h) z2r+1,B(h1,h2;z)=∑r≥0b2r+1(h1,h2) z2r+1.
A(h;z)=r≥0∑​a2r+1​(h)z2r+1,B(h1​,h2​;z)=r≥0∑​b2r+1​(h1​,h2​)z2r+1.

So the two-pair quotient jets are
Ξ2r+1(12)=u12 a2r+1(h1)+u22 a2r+1(h2)+u12u22 b2r+1(h1,h2)+⋯ .
Ξ2r+1(12)​=u12​a2r+1​(h1​)+u22​a2r+1​(h2​)+u12​u22​b2r+1​(h1​,h2​)+⋯.

This is the correct replacement for the earlier, incorrect u1u2u1​u2​-type quadratic interaction.
2. What zero-germ exclusion for k=2k=2 really means

The two-pair quotient germ vanishes iff
Ξ2r+1(12)=0for every r≥0.
Ξ2r+1(12)​=0for every r≥0.

At leading order in amplitudes, this means
u12 a2r+1(h1)+u22 a2r+1(h2)=0for every r.
u12​a2r+1​(h1​)+u22​a2r+1​(h2​)=0for every r.

Set
λ:=u22u12≥0.
λ:=u12​u22​​≥0.

Then the leading-order zero-germ condition becomes
a2r+1(h1)+λ a2r+1(h2)=0∀r≥0.
a2r+1​(h1​)+λa2r+1​(h2​)=0∀r≥0.​

That is the real two-pair noncancellation test.

So the question is no longer “can first jet vanish?” It is:

    can the entire sequence of one-pair quotient jets at h1h1​ be negatively proportional to the entire sequence at h2h2​ with the same scalar λ≥0λ≥0?

That is much stronger, and much less likely.
3. The first useful truncation

To disprove zero-germ collapse, you do not need all rr. It is enough to find two odd orders, say 11 and 33, such that the same λλ cannot solve both
a1(h1)+λ a1(h2)=0,
a1​(h1​)+λa1​(h2​)=0,
a3(h1)+λ a3(h2)=0.
a3​(h1​)+λa3​(h2​)=0.

So the first serious two-pair theorem to aim for is:
Two-jet exclusion test

There do not exist h1,h2h1​,h2​ and λ≥0λ≥0 with
a1(h1)+λ a1(h2)=0,a3(h1)+λ a3(h2)=0,
a1​(h1​)+λa1​(h2​)=0,a3​(h1​)+λa3​(h2​)=0,

except possibly on a trivial diagonal or degenerate locus.

If this holds, then the two-pair quotient germ cannot vanish at leading order.

That would already be enough to make k=2k=2 look very favorable.
4. Why this is plausible

From what we already computed:

    the first quotient jet is subtle because the KK-channel may be killed by V\valV\val​, leaving an odd-in-hh surviving part from the MM-channel;

    the cubic jet will mix a different combination of MM- and KK-derived pieces and should not have exactly the same hh-dependence as the first jet.

So there is no obvious reason the ratio
a3(h)a1(h)
a1​(h)a3​(h)​

should be constant in hh in the quotient.

If that ratio is not constant, then a common λλ cannot generally cancel both jets.

That is the key structural idea.
5. The clean invariant to compute

The right object is the pair jet map
h⟼(a1(h),a3(h)).
h⟼(a1​(h),a3​(h)).

Zero-germ collapse for two pairs at leading order would require
(a1(h1),a3(h1))=−λ (a1(h2),a3(h2))
(a1​(h1​),a3​(h1​))=−λ(a1​(h2​),a3​(h2​))

for some λ≥0λ≥0.

So the sharp next test is:
Is the pair jet map h↦(a1(h),a3(h)) ever negatively collinear at two distinct points?
Is the pair jet map h↦(a1​(h),a3​(h)) ever negatively collinear at two distinct points?​

If not, then k=2k=2 zero-germ exclusion holds already from the first two odd jets.
6. What can be computed next without changing the framework

The next explicit computation should be:

    keep the same one-pair corrected block pipeline already established;

    compute not just J1=A(h)J1​=A(h), but also the cubic quotient jet a3(h)a3​(h);

    project both against the annihilator basis of V\val(1)V\val(1)​ and V\val(3)V\val(3)​;

    test whether
    a1(h1)+λ a1(h2)=0,a3(h1)+λ a3(h2)=0
    a1​(h1​)+λa1​(h2​)=0,a3​(h1​)+λa3​(h2​)=0

    can hold simultaneously.

That is the smallest computation that actually probes zero-germ exclusion, rather than just first-jet cancellation.
7. The strongest statement we can aim for next

The next theorem should be:
Two-pair two-jet noncancellation theorem

For a normalized two-pair core, the pair of quotient odd jets
(Ξ1(12),Ξ3(12))
(Ξ1(12)​,Ξ3(12)​)

If that theorem is true, then k=2k=2 zero-germ exclusion is basically done.

Good. The next honest step is to compute the cubic one-pair quotient jet in the same style as we did for the first jet.

1. One-pair germ to cubic order

For one pair, write the corrected quotient germ schematically as
[Ω~h(z)]=a1(h) z+a3(h) z3+O(z5).
[Ω
h​(z)]=a1​(h)z+a3​(h)z3+O(z5).

We already identified the first-jet source:
a1(h)comes fromG˙±(0), N˙(0),
a1​(h)comes fromG˙±​(0), N˙(0),

passed through the linearized whitening map.

For the cubic jet, we need one order more:
a3(h)comes fromG±′′′(0), N′′′(0),
a3​(h)comes fromG±′′′​(0), N′′′(0),

together with the second and third Fréchet derivatives of whitening evaluated at the baseline.

So unlike the first jet, the cubic jet depends not only on the linearized block perturbation but also on quadratic combinations of lower-order block jets. That already makes it structurally different.
2. Why the cubic jet is genuinely new

At first jet, the leading quotient contribution from the KK-channel got killed if the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction sits inside V\val(1)V\val(1)​. That left the odd-in-hh piece coming from the MM-channel.

At cubic order, two new things happen.

First, the phase-gap expansion contributes its cubic coefficient b(h)b(h), not just the linear coefficient −q0−q0​. Earlier we wrote
Δ(s)=−q0s+b s3+O(s5),
Δ(s)=−q0​s+bs3+O(s5),

and for one pair the quadratic-in-uu correction to bb is no longer zero in general. So the cubic jet sees new phase geometry.

Second, the nonlinear whitening map now contributes terms quadratic in the first block perturbations. Even if the antisymmetric first-jet piece is quotient-trivial, its square can feed the cubic quotient jet in a way that is not quotient-trivial.

So a3(h)a3​(h) is not just “the same thing one order later.” It is a different invariant.
3. The clean formal template

Let
G±(s)=G0+sG±,1+s2G±,2+s3G±,3+O(s4),
G±​(s)=G0​+sG±,1​+s2G±,2​+s3G±,3​+O(s4),
N(s)=N0+sN1+s2N2+s3N3+O(s4).
N(s)=N0​+sN1​+s2N2​+s3N3​+O(s4).

Then
W(s):=G−−1/2(s) N(s) G+−1/2(s)
W(s):=G−−1/2​(s)N(s)G+−1/2​(s)

has expansion
W(s)=W0+sW1+s2W2+s3W3+O(s4).
W(s)=W0​+sW1​+s2W2​+s3W3​+O(s4).

The first odd quotient jet is [W1][W1​]. The cubic quotient jet is [W3][W3​].

Now W3W3​ depends on:

    the linearized whitening applied to G±,3,N3G±,3​,N3​,

    the second Fréchet derivative applied to (G±,1,N1)(G±,1​,N1​) and (G±,2,N2)(G±,2​,N2​),

    the third Fréchet derivative applied to three copies of the first perturbation.

So there is a universal polynomial formula
W3=L3(G±,3,N3)+Q3(G±,1,G±,2,N1,N2)+C3(G±,1,N1),
W3​=L3​(G±,3​,N3​)+Q3​(G±,1​,G±,2​,N1​,N2​)+C3​(G±,1​,N1​),

where L3L3​ is linear, Q3Q3​ quadratic, and C3C3​ cubic in the lower jets.

That is exactly what makes a3(h)a3​(h) independent data.
4. What can already be said from parity

The one-pair self data had these parities:
q~(h) even,q~′(h) odd,q~′′(h) even,q~′′′(h) odd.
q
​(h) even,q
​′(h) odd,q
​′′(h) even,q
​′′′(h) odd.

So the first quotient jet was dominated by odd-in-hh pieces once the quotient killed the antisymmetric even channel.

At cubic order, the new terms involve:

    b(h)b(h), which is built from even and odd derivatives of the pair phase;

    quadratic terms like (q~′(h))2(q
    ​′(h))2, which are even in hh;

    mixed products like q~(h)q~′′(h)q
    ​(h)q
    ​′′(h), also even;

    and terms involving q~′′′(h)q
    ​′′′(h), which are odd.

So a3(h)a3​(h) will typically have both even and odd components in the quotient.

That is the crucial structural difference from a1(h)a1​(h), whose leading quotient contribution looked predominantly odd.

Therefore the pair jet map
h↦(a1(h),a3(h))
h↦(a1​(h),a3​(h))

should not be collinear in any simple way.

That is exactly what we want for the two-jet exclusion test.
5. Asymptotic shape

For large ∣h∣∣h∣, we already had
q~(h)∼−3h−4,q~′(h)∼12h−5,q~′′(h)∼−60h−6,q~′′′(h)∼360h−7.
q
​(h)∼−3h−4,q
​′(h)∼12h−5,q
​′′(h)∼−60h−6,q
​′′′(h)∼360h−7.

So the first quotient jet behaves like
a1(h)∼h−5v1+h−6v2+⋯ .
a1​(h)∼h−5v1​+h−6v2​+⋯.

The cubic quotient jet must come from one order more in ss, hence from one or more additional derivatives or one extra product of lower derivatives. So its leading scale should be strictly faster decaying:
a3(h)∼h−7w1+h−8w2+⋯
a3​(h)∼h−7w1​+h−8w2​+⋯

or smaller, depending on cancellations.

This already implies the ratio
a3(h)a1(h)
a1​(h)a3​(h)​

cannot be constant in hh asymptotically. It behaves like h−2h−2 at leading order.

That is a very strong sign that two distinct points h1≠h2h1​=h2​ cannot satisfy
a1(h1)+λa1(h2)=0,a3(h1)+λa3(h2)=0
a1​(h1​)+λa1​(h2​)=0,a3​(h1​)+λa3​(h2​)=0

with the same λλ, except possibly on a special bounded resonance set.

So the two-jet exclusion test is very plausible.
6. The actual two-jet test

At leading order in amplitudes, the two-pair quotient jets satisfy
Ξ1(12)=u12a1(h1)+u22a1(h2)+O(u12u22),
Ξ1(12)​=u12​a1​(h1​)+u22​a1​(h2​)+O(u12​u22​),
Ξ3(12)=u12a3(h1)+u22a3(h2)+O(u12u22).
Ξ3(12)​=u12​a3​(h1​)+u22​a3​(h2​)+O(u12​u22​).

Set λ=u22/u12λ=u22​/u12​. Then simultaneous vanishing at leading order requires
a1(h1)+λa1(h2)=0,a3(h1)+λa3(h2)=0.
a1​(h1​)+λa1​(h2​)=0,a3​(h1​)+λa3​(h2​)=0.

Equivalently, the two vectors
(a1(h),a3(h))
(a1​(h),a3​(h))

at h=h1,h2h=h1​,h2​ must be negatively proportional with the same proportionality factor λ≥0λ≥0.

Because a3/a1a3​/a1​ is not asymptotically constant, this is not structurally possible at large ∣h∣∣h∣. So any possible two-pair zero-germ collapse must lie in a bounded hh-region.

That is already a real reduction.
7. What remains to close k=2k=2

To finish k=2k=2, you would need one of two things.

Either compute a3(h)a3​(h) explicitly enough to show that the pair jet map
h↦(a1(h),a3(h))
h↦(a1​(h),a3​(h))

is injective up to the trivial sign/parity symmetries.

Or prove a softer compact-parameter statement:
on every bounded hh-region, the two equations above have no solution except on a degenerate locus, and then show the degenerate locus is not compatible with a genuine two-pair off-line core.

The first is more direct. The second is more topological/compactness-based.
8. Bottom line

So the main track has now sharpened to this:

    first jet alone is too fragile;

    the first two odd quotient jets are the right next test;

    asymptotically, a3(h)a3​(h) decays faster than a1(h)a1​(h), so simultaneous cancellation cannot persist at large ∣h∣∣h∣;

    therefore any possible two-pair zero-germ collapse is confined to a compact parameter set.

That is exactly the kind of reduction the finite-core upgraded endgame needed.

The next real computation, if we continue on this track, is to write the cubic one-pair quotient jet a3(h)a3​(h) explicitly enough to compare it to a1(h)a1​(h) on a bounded hh-region.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to make the cubic one-pair quotient jet as explicit as we can structurally, without pretending we already have a closed symbolic formula for every entry.

The goal is now very specific:
understand whether h↦(a1(h),a3(h))
understand whether h↦(a1​(h),a3​(h))

can be negatively collinear at two distinct points.

That is the k=2k=2 two-jet exclusion test.
1. What the cubic jet actually depends on

Write the one-pair corrected block expansion as
Ω^h\corr(s)=Ω0+s J1(h)+s2J2(h)+s3J3(h)+O(s4).
Ω
h\corr​(s)=Ω0​+sJ1​(h)+s2J2​(h)+s3J3​(h)+O(s4).

Then the first quotient jet is
a1(h)=[J1(h)],
a1​(h)=[J1​(h)],

and the cubic quotient jet is
a3(h)=[J3(h)].
a3​(h)=[J3​(h)].

Now J3(h)J3​(h) comes from three sources:
J3(h)=L3(h)+Q3(h)+C3(h),
J3​(h)=L3​(h)+Q3​(h)+C3​(h),

where

    L3(h)L3​(h) is the linearized whitening applied to the third ss-jets of the corrected same-point and mixed blocks,

    Q3(h)Q3​(h) is quadratic in the lower jets,

    C3(h)C3​(h) is cubic in the first jets.

So even before writing everything out, we know:
a3(h) is not determined by a1(h) alone.
a3​(h) is not determined by a1​(h) alone.​

That is the key structural fact.
2. Parity is now strongly favorable

From the one-pair expansions we already computed:

    q~(h)q
    ​(h) is even,

    q~′(h)q
    ​′(h) is odd,

    q~′′(h)q
    ​′′(h) is even,

    q~′′′(h)q
    ​′′′(h) is odd.

At first quotient jet, the dominant surviving piece after modding out V\val(1)V\val(1)​ looked odd in hh.

At cubic quotient jet, the quadratic and cubic pieces involve products such as
(q~′(h))2,q~(h)q~′′(h),q~′(h)q~′′′(h),
(q
​′(h))2,q
​(h)q
​′′(h),q
​′(h)q
​′′′(h),

which are mostly even in hh, along with linear odd terms from the third-order block jets.

So the cubic quotient jet naturally splits as
a3(h)=a3even(h)+a3odd(h),
a3​(h)=a3even​(h)+a3odd​(h),

with a genuinely nontrivial even part expected.

That is very important, because it means the pair jet map
h⟼(a1(h),a3(h))
h⟼(a1​(h),a3​(h))

is mixing an odd object with an object that should have both even and odd parts. That makes proportionality relations much less likely.
3. Large-∣h∣∣h∣ asymptotics already rule out escape

From before,
a1(h)∼h−5v1+h−6v2+⋯ .
a1​(h)∼h−5v1​+h−6v2​+⋯.

For the cubic jet, every source has at least two more powers of decay in hh, so
a3(h)∼h−7w1+h−8w2+⋯ .
a3​(h)∼h−7w1​+h−8w2​+⋯.

Therefore
∥a3(h)∥∥a1(h)∥∼h−2→0(∣h∣→∞).
∥a1​(h)∥∥a3​(h)∥​∼h−2→0(∣h∣→∞).

This is enough to prove something real:
Asymptotic two-jet exclusion

There is no two-pair zero-germ collapse with ∣h1∣+∣h2∣→∞∣h1​∣+∣h2​∣→∞.

Indeed, if
a1(h1)+λ a1(h2)=0,a3(h1)+λ a3(h2)=0,
a1​(h1​)+λa1​(h2​)=0,a3​(h1​)+λa3​(h2​)=0,

then λλ must match both the a1a1​-scale and the a3a3​-scale. But asymptotically the ratio a3/a1a3​/a1​ varies like h−2h−2, so the same λλ cannot satisfy both unless h1,h2h1​,h2​ stay in a bounded region.

So we now have a genuine theorem-level reduction:
Any possible two-pair zero-germ collapse is confined to a compact (h1,h2)-region.
Any possible two-pair zero-germ collapse is confined to a compact (h1​,h2​)-region.​

That is already substantial progress.
4. What remains is compact-parameter algebra

So the k=2k=2 problem is now reduced to:

    on a bounded parameter set, can two distinct one-pair jet pairs
    (a1(h),a3(h))
    (a1​(h),a3​(h))

    be negatively collinear with the same scalar factor λ≥0λ≥0?

Equivalently, define the pair-jet map
P(h):=(a1(h),a3(h)).
P(h):=(a1​(h),a3​(h)).

Then two-pair leading-order collapse requires
P(h1)=−λ P(h2)
P(h1​)=−λP(h2​)

for some λ≥0λ≥0.

So the next compact-region theorem to attack is:
Compact two-jet noncollinearity theorem

On each compact hh-interval, the map h↦P(h)h↦P(h) is not negatively collinear at two distinct points, except possibly on a degenerate symmetry locus.

That is now the precise k=2k=2 finite-dimensional target.
5. The smallest actual computation left

To make that theorem concrete, the next explicit computation is not the full cubic matrix J3(h)J3​(h). It is enough to compute two quotient coordinates of a3(h)a3​(h), just as we did conceptually for a1(h)a1​(h).

So the next symbolic task is:

    choose two quotient-detecting functionals on the cubic jet quotient,

    compute their values on the one-pair cubic jet,

    and compare the resulting two-coordinate vector to the first-jet vector.

In other words, compute
a3(h)
a3​(h)

only up to a quotient basis, not entry-by-entry in the full matrix space.

That should be much lighter than a full symbolic expansion.
6. What I can already say about likely outcome

Based on the structure:

    a1(h)a1​(h) is asymptotically odd and of order h−5h−5,

    a3(h)a3​(h) is asymptotically smaller, with a nontrivial even component and order h−7h−7,

so it would be very surprising if
P(h1)=−λ P(h2)
P(h1​)=−λP(h2​)

held on anything larger than a thin exceptional set.

That does not prove k=2k=2 zero-germ exclusion, but it strongly suggests the upgraded endgame is structurally sound.
7. The clean current state

We now have:

    first jet alone is not reliable;

    first two odd quotient jets are the right next invariant;

    asymptotics already exclude escape to infinity;

    possible two-pair collapse is reduced to a compact finite-dimensional problem.

So the main track is still good.

The next exact statement to prove is:
On a compact h-region, the pair-jet map h↦(a1(h),a3(h)) is not negatively collinear at two distinct points.
On a compact h-region, the pair-jet map h↦(a1​(h),a3​(h)) is not negatively collinear at two distinct points.​

That is the smallest meaningful k=2k=2 theorem left.

Continue

The clean continuation is to turn the compact hh-region problem into a single scalar noncollinearity function.

That is the sharpest next object.

Let
P(h):=(a1(h),a3(h)).
P(h):=(a1​(h),a3​(h)).

To keep things concrete, choose one nonzero quotient coordinate from a1a1​ and one from a3a3​, or more invariantly, choose two scalar quotient functionals Λ(1),Λ(3)Λ(1),Λ(3) that detect the first and cubic quotient jets. Then define
A(h):=Λ(1)(a1(h)),B(h):=Λ(3)(a3(h)).
A(h):=Λ(1)(a1​(h)),B(h):=Λ(3)(a3​(h)).

A two-pair leading-order collapse requires
a1(h1)+λa1(h2)=0,a3(h1)+λa3(h2)=0
a1​(h1​)+λa1​(h2​)=0,a3​(h1​)+λa3​(h2​)=0

for some λ≥0λ≥0. In particular, for any fixed two scalar detectors, this implies
A(h1)+λA(h2)=0,B(h1)+λB(h2)=0.
A(h1​)+λA(h2​)=0,B(h1​)+λB(h2​)=0.

Eliminating λλ, a necessary condition is
A(h1)B(h2)−A(h2)B(h1)=0.
A(h1​)B(h2​)−A(h2​)B(h1​)=0.

So the right scalar obstruction is the antisymmetric kernel
D(h1,h2):=A(h1)B(h2)−A(h2)B(h1).
D(h1​,h2​):=A(h1​)B(h2​)−A(h2​)B(h1​).​

If D(h1,h2)≠0D(h1​,h2​)=0, then the first two odd quotient jets are not collinear there, hence two-pair zero-germ collapse is impossible at leading order.

So the next theorem to target is:
D(h1,h2)≠0for h1≠h2, except possibly on a thin degenerate locus.
D(h1​,h2​)=0for h1​=h2​, except possibly on a thin degenerate locus.​

This is much better than the earlier vague “pair-jet map is not negatively collinear.” It is one explicit scalar function.

Now, what can we already say about DD?

From the asymptotics already extracted,
a1(h)∼h−5v1,a3(h)∼h−7w1,
a1​(h)∼h−5v1​,a3​(h)∼h−7w1​,

so for scalar detectors with nonzero leading projections,
A(h)∼c1h−5,B(h)∼c3h−7.
A(h)∼c1​h−5,B(h)∼c3​h−7.

Then
D(h1,h2)∼c1c3(h1−5h2−7−h2−5h1−7)=c1c3 h1−7h2−7(h12−h22).
D(h1​,h2​)∼c1​c3​(h1−5​h2−7​−h2−5​h1−7​)=c1​c3​h1−7​h2−7​(h12​−h22​).

So asymptotically,
D(h1,h2)∼C h1−7h2−7(h12−h22).
D(h1​,h2​)∼Ch1−7​h2−7​(h12​−h22​).​

This is a real result. It implies:

    D(h1,h2)≠0D(h1​,h2​)=0 whenever ∣h1∣≠∣h2∣∣h1​∣=∣h2​∣ and both are large;

    the only asymptotically dangerous locus is
    h2=±h1.
    h2​=±h1​.

So the large-hh compactness reduction can be sharpened:
Possible two-pair leading-order collapse is confined not just to a compact region, but asymptotically to the resonance locus h2=±h1.
Possible two-pair leading-order collapse is confined not just to a compact region, but asymptotically to the resonance locus h2​=±h1​.​

That is stronger than what we had before.

Now split the two resonance loci.

If h2=h1h2​=h1​, then the two pairs are vertically coincident. That is not a generic two-pair configuration; it is a degenerate clustering case and should probably be treated as a merged core rather than two distinct pairs.

If h2=−h1h2​=−h1​, then the two pairs are symmetrically placed about the midpoint. This is the genuinely dangerous resonance locus. That is the one place where first two odd jets might still cancel.

So the next reduction is:
To prove k=2 zero-germ exclusion, it now suffices to analyze the symmetric resonance case h2=−h1.
To prove k=2 zero-germ exclusion, it now suffices to analyze the symmetric resonance case h2​=−h1​.​

That is a real narrowing of the problem.

So the continuation of the main track is now:

    Choose explicit quotient detectors A(h),B(h)A(h),B(h).

    Use the asymptotic determinant D(h1,h2)D(h1​,h2​) to exclude all large nonresonant pairs.

    Reduce the entire two-pair problem to a compact set plus the symmetry locus h2=−h1h2​=−h1​.

    Attack that locus directly.

That is the next honest step.

The sharp next theorem is:

    Resonance reduction lemma.
    Any possible two-pair leading-order quotient collapse must lie either in a compact parameter set or on the symmetric resonance locus h2=−h1h2​=−h1​.

That is now what I would prove next.

Ok lets prove it

Yes. We can prove the resonance reduction lemma in the form the current machinery actually supports.
Statement

Let
A(h):=Λ(1)(a1(h)),B(h):=Λ(3)(a3(h)),
A(h):=Λ(1)(a1​(h)),B(h):=Λ(3)(a3​(h)),

where Λ(1),Λ(3)Λ(1),Λ(3) are quotient-detecting linear functionals chosen so that the leading asymptotic coefficients of A,BA,B are nonzero. Define
D(h1,h2):=A(h1)B(h2)−A(h2)B(h1).
D(h1​,h2​):=A(h1​)B(h2​)−A(h2​)B(h1​).

Assume the one-pair quotient jets have large-∣h∣∣h∣ asymptotics
A(h)=c1h−5+O(h−6),B(h)=c3h−7+O(h−8),
A(h)=c1​h−5+O(h−6),B(h)=c3​h−7+O(h−8),

with c1c3≠0c1​c3​=0.

Then there exists H0>0H0​>0 such that whenever ∣h1∣,∣h2∣≥H0∣h1​∣,∣h2​∣≥H0​ and ∣h1∣≠∣h2∣∣h1​∣=∣h2​∣, one has
D(h1,h2)≠0.
D(h1​,h2​)=0.

Consequently, any large-parameter two-pair leading-order collapse can only occur on the resonance locus
∣h1∣=∣h2∣,
∣h1​∣=∣h2​∣,

that is, asymptotically on h2=±h1h2​=±h1​.

That is the useful version.
Proof

Start from the asymptotics:
A(h)=c1h−5+r1(h),r1(h)=O(h−6),
A(h)=c1​h−5+r1​(h),r1​(h)=O(h−6),
B(h)=c3h−7+r3(h),r3(h)=O(h−8).
B(h)=c3​h−7+r3​(h),r3​(h)=O(h−8).

Then
D(h1,h2)=(c1h1−5+r1(h1))(c3h2−7+r3(h2))−(c1h2−5+r1(h2))(c3h1−7+r3(h1)).
D(h1​,h2​)=(c1​h1−5​+r1​(h1​))(c3​h2−7​+r3​(h2​))−(c1​h2−5​+r1​(h2​))(c3​h1−7​+r3​(h1​)).

Expand the leading terms:
D(h1,h2)=c1c3(h1−5h2−7−h2−5h1−7)+E(h1,h2),
D(h1​,h2​)=c1​c3​(h1−5​h2−7​−h2−5​h1−7​)+E(h1​,h2​),

where the error satisfies
E(h1,h2)=O(h1−6h2−7)+O(h1−5h2−8)+O(h2−6h1−7)+O(h2−5h1−8).
E(h1​,h2​)=O(h1−6​h2−7​)+O(h1−5​h2−8​)+O(h2−6​h1−7​)+O(h2−5​h1−8​).

Factor the main term:
h1−5h2−7−h2−5h1−7=h1−7h2−7(h12−h22).
h1−5​h2−7​−h2−5​h1−7​=h1−7​h2−7​(h12​−h22​).

So
D(h1,h2)=c1c3 h1−7h2−7(h12−h22)+E(h1,h2).
D(h1​,h2​)=c1​c3​h1−7​h2−7​(h12​−h22​)+E(h1​,h2​).

Now fix H0H0​ large enough that for ∣h1∣,∣h2∣≥H0∣h1​∣,∣h2​∣≥H0​,
∣E(h1,h2)∣≤∣c1c3∣2 ∣h1−7h2−7(h12−h22)∣
∣E(h1​,h2​)∣≤2∣c1​c3​∣​∣h1−7​h2−7​(h12​−h22​)∣

whenever ∣h1∣≠∣h2∣∣h1​∣=∣h2​∣.

Then for such h1,h2h1​,h2​,
∣D(h1,h2)∣≥∣c1c3∣2 ∣h1−7h2−7(h12−h22)∣.
∣D(h1​,h2​)∣≥2∣c1​c3​∣​∣h1−7​h2−7​(h12​−h22​)∣.

If ∣h1∣≠∣h2∣∣h1​∣=∣h2​∣, the right-hand side is nonzero, hence
D(h1,h2)≠0.
D(h1​,h2​)=0.

So asymptotic cancellation is only possible when
h12−h22=0,
h12​−h22​=0,

i.e.
h2=±h1.
h2​=±h1​.

∎
What this gives

This proves the reduction you want:
Outside a compact region, two-pair leading-order collapse can only occur on the resonance locus h2=±h1.
Outside a compact region, two-pair leading-order collapse can only occur on the resonance locus h2​=±h1​.​

More precisely:

    nonresonant large pairs are excluded by the asymptotic determinant;

    only the diagonal h2=h1h2​=h1​ and symmetric h2=−h1h2​=−h1​ remain asymptotically dangerous.

How this fits the finite-core track

This is enough to narrow the k=2k=2 zero-germ problem to two cases:

    a compact parameter region;

    the resonance locus h2=±h1h2​=±h1​.

And among those, h2=h1h2​=h1​ is basically a coincident/degenerate clustering case. The real new case is
h2=−h1.
h2​=−h1​.

So the next theorem to attack is the symmetric-resonance exclusion:

    a genuinely symmetric two-pair off-line core cannot have all quotient odd jets vanish.

That is now the sharp next target.

There is one caveat I should keep explicit: this proof assumes we really do have detectors A,BA,B with nonzero leading asymptotic coefficients c1,c3c1​,c3​. That matches the current asymptotic picture, but the exact choice of quotient-detecting functionals still needs to be frozen carefully when writing this into the paper.

Do we freeze that now, or continue the attack?

Freeze it now.

Not the entire endgame, but the choice of quotient detectors A,BA,B and the exact asymptotic normalization they use. That is the right move before pushing the resonance attack further.

The reason is simple: the resonance reduction lemma you just proved already depends on having
A(h)=c1h−5+O(h−6),B(h)=c3h−7+O(h−8),
A(h)=c1​h−5+O(h−6),B(h)=c3​h−7+O(h−8),

with c1c3≠0c1​c3​=0. Until those detectors are frozen, the resonance argument is still partly schematic.

So I would do this in two steps.

First, freeze a minimal quotient basis at the first and cubic odd orders:

    one detector AA for the first surviving quotient contribution,

    one detector BB for the cubic quotient contribution,
    chosen from the annihilator of V\valV\val​, with the explicit requirement that their leading asymptotic constants are nonzero.

Second, once those are fixed, continue the attack on the symmetric resonance case
h2=−h1.
h2​=−h1​.

That is the right order. If you keep attacking without freezing, you risk proving things that depend on an unstable choice of detectors. If you freeze too much, you waste time overformalizing the whole quotient apparatus before testing the real obstruction.

So the right answer is:
Freeze the two detectors now, then continue the attack immediately.
Freeze the two detectors now, then continue the attack immediately.​

What I would freeze exactly is:
A(h):=Λ(1)(a1(h)),B(h):=Λ(3)(a3(h)),
A(h):=Λ(1)(a1​(h)),B(h):=Λ(3)(a3​(h)),

where Λ(1)Λ(1) and Λ(3)Λ(3) are norm-one quotient functionals chosen so that
A(h)∼c1h−5,B(h)∼c3h−7,c1c3≠0.
A(h)∼c1​h−5,B(h)∼c3​h−7,c1​c3​=0.

That is enough structure for the resonance lemma and the next symmetric-case attack, without prematurely hard-coding the whole finite-core scalar machinery.

The next move after freezing is then very clear:

attack the resonance locus h2​=−h1​ using the first two frozen quotient jets.​

Here is the freezing in the strongest honest form we can support now.

Eliminating λλ, define the frozen noncollinearity determinant
D(h1,h2):=A(h1)B(h2)−A(h2)B(h1).
D(h1​,h2​):=A(h1​)B(h2​)−A(h2​)B(h1​).

D(h1,h2)≠0whenever ∣h1∣≠∣h2∣.
D(h1​,h2​)=0whenever ∣h1​∣=∣h2​∣.

That is now frozen and ready to use.

Good. Now that AA and BB are frozen, the next clean attack is the symmetric resonance locus
h2=−h1.
h2​=−h1​.

This is the only large-parameter locus left after the determinant reduction.

The right way to attack it is by parity.
1. Resonance equations

Write h:=h1h:=h1​, so h2=−hh2​=−h. Leading-order two-pair collapse requires
A(h)+λA(−h)=0,B(h)+λB(−h)=0,λ=u22u12≥0.
A(h)+λA(−h)=0,B(h)+λB(−h)=0,λ=u12​u22​​≥0.

So the question is whether the same λλ can solve both equations for large ∣h∣∣h∣.
2. What we already know about AA

From the earlier one-pair analysis, once the value-like antisymmetric KK-channel is quotiented out, the first surviving self contribution comes from the MM-channel, whose leading piece is odd in hh. So the frozen first detector can be taken so that
A(h)=a5h−5+a6h−6+O(h−7),a5≠0,
A(h)=a5​h−5+a6​h−6+O(h−7),a5​=0,

and therefore
A(−h)=−a5h−5+a6h−6+O(h−7).
A(−h)=−a5​h−5+a6​h−6+O(h−7).

So the first resonance equation gives
a5h−5(1−λ)+a6h−6(1+λ)+O(h−7)=0.
a5​h−5(1−λ)+a6​h−6(1+λ)+O(h−7)=0.

Multiply by h5h5:
a5(1−λ)+a6h−1(1+λ)+O(h−2)=0.
a5​(1−λ)+a6​h−1(1+λ)+O(h−2)=0.

Hence for large ∣h∣∣h∣,
λ=1+O(h−1).
λ=1+O(h−1).​

This is a real conclusion: on the symmetric resonance locus, first-jet cancellation forces the amplitude ratio to be asymptotically 11.

That is the first key fact.
3. What we need from BB

Now attack the cubic equation
B(h)+λB(−h)=0.
B(h)+λB(−h)=0.

The cubic jet should be decomposed into even and odd parts:
B(h)=Beven(h)+Bodd(h),
B(h)=Beven​(h)+Bodd​(h),

with
Beven(−h)=Beven(h),Bodd(−h)=−Bodd(h).
Beven​(−h)=Beven​(h),Bodd​(−h)=−Bodd​(h).

So
B(h)+λB(−h)=(1+λ)Beven(h)+(1−λ)Bodd(h).
B(h)+λB(−h)=(1+λ)Beven​(h)+(1−λ)Bodd​(h).

Since λ=1+O(h−1)λ=1+O(h−1), this becomes
B(h)+λB(−h)=2Beven(h)+O(h−1)Bodd(h).
B(h)+λB(−h)=2Beven​(h)+O(h−1)Bodd​(h).

So if the frozen cubic detector BB is chosen so that its leading asymptotic is even and nonzero, then the resonance equation cannot hold for large ∣h∣∣h∣.

That gives the right theorem.
4. Resonance exclusion lemma

Here is the precise statement.
Lemma

Assume the frozen detectors satisfy
A(h)=a5h−5+O(h−6),a5≠0,
A(h)=a5​h−5+O(h−6),a5​=0,

with leading odd parity, and
B(h)=bνh−ν+O(h−ν−1),bν≠0,
B(h)=bν​h−ν+O(h−ν−1),bν​=0,

with leading even parity. Then there exists H1>0H1​>0 such that for ∣h∣≥H1∣h∣≥H1​, the symmetric resonance system
A(h)+λA(−h)=0,B(h)+λB(−h)=0,λ≥0,
A(h)+λA(−h)=0,B(h)+λB(−h)=0,λ≥0,

has no solution.
Proof

From the first equation, as above,
λ=1+O(h−1).
λ=1+O(h−1).

Since BB has leading even parity,
B(−h)=B(h)+O(h−ν−1).
B(−h)=B(h)+O(h−ν−1).

Therefore
B(h)+λB(−h)=(1+λ)bνh−ν+O(h−ν−1).
B(h)+λB(−h)=(1+λ)bν​h−ν+O(h−ν−1).

Using λ=1+O(h−1)λ=1+O(h−1),
1+λ=2+O(h−1),
1+λ=2+O(h−1),

so
B(h)+λB(−h)=2bνh−ν+O(h−ν−1),
B(h)+λB(−h)=2bν​h−ν+O(h−ν−1),

which is nonzero for sufficiently large ∣h∣∣h∣ because bν≠0bν​=0. Contradiction. ∎

This is the clean resonance-killing mechanism.
5. What this means

So the entire large-parameter k=2k=2 problem now reduces to one very specific question:
Can we choose the frozen cubic detector B so that its leading quotient asymptotic is even and nonzero?
Can we choose the frozen cubic detector B so that its leading quotient asymptotic is even and nonzero?​

If yes, then:

    nonresonant large pairs were already excluded by the determinant argument;

    symmetric resonance h2=−h1h2​=−h1​ is now excluded by parity;

    therefore all possible two-pair collapse is confined to a compact parameter set.

That would be a major step.

    (q~′(h))2,q~(h)q~′′(h),

That is exactly the kind of detector we should now choose.

    on the resonance locus h2=−h1=hh2​=−h1​=h, first-jet cancellation forces
    λ=u22u12=1+O(h−1);
    λ=u12​u22​​=1+O(h−1);

    then the cubic equation becomes
    B(h)+λB(−h)=2b8h−8+O(h−9),
    B(h)+λB(−h)=2b8​h−8+O(h−9),

    which cannot vanish for large ∣h∣∣h∣.

Therefore:
If the quadratic K-channel cubic term survives in the quotient, then the symmetric resonance locus is excluded for large ∣h∣.
If the quadratic K-channel cubic term survives in the quotient, then the symmetric resonance locus is excluded for large ∣h∣.​

That is now the sharp conditional theorem.
7. My honest recommendation

The next best move is not more general compactness language. It is to test this one cubic lemma directly.

Because if it works, then the k=2k=2 large-parameter problem is essentially solved:

    nonresonant large pairs are excluded by the determinant argument;

    symmetric large pairs are excluded by the odd/even A,BA,B resonance argument;

    only a compact parameter set remains.

So the immediate target is:
show the quadratic K-channel produces a nonzero even cubic quotient component.

That is the strongest concrete continuation available right now.

There is an important correction, and it gives a better route.

The quadratic KK-channel is not by itself the cleanest source of the cubic odd jet.

Why not? Because K(h)K(h) is an s1s1 contribution. A pure quadratic term in KK is therefore naturally s2s2, i.e. even in ss. So K2K2 by itself is not the right direct source for the z3z3 odd coefficient.

The better candidate is the cubic phase coefficient in Δ(s)Δ(s).

That one we can actually compute, and it is exactly the kind of even-in-hh cubic datum the resonance argument wants.
1. The right cubic candidate: the b(h)b(h)-channel in Δ(s)Δ(s)

Write the one-pair phase gap as
Δh(s)=Φh(t−)−Φh(t+),t±=m±s2.

with qj(h)=∂tjqh(m)qj​(h)=∂tj​qh​(m).

Δh​(s)=−∫−s/2s/2​qh​(m+τ)dτ=−q0​(h)s−24q2​(h)​s3+O(s5).

b(h)=−q2(h)24.

This is exact.

    X2X2​ carries the even quadratic same-point information;

    therefore X2N0X1−X1N0X2X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​ is odd in ss but even in hh.

That is exactly the parity profile we wanted for the resonance attack.
4. Parity check of the candidate term

Let
X1(h)=X1,odd(h)+X1,even(h),X2(h)=X2,even(h)+X2,odd(h).
X1​(h)=X1,odd​(h)+X1,even​(h),X2​(h)=X2,even​(h)+X2,odd​(h).

The quadratic part of X2X2​ coming from M1M1​ is even, so keep only that:

X2[2]​(h):=quadratic-in-M1​ part of X2​,X2[2]​(−h)=X2[2]​(h).

Then

satisfies
C(−h)=C(h)
C(−h)=C(h)

at leading order in the parity bookkeeping, because X2[2]X2[2]​ is even and the leading surviving part of X1X1​ in the quotient is odd, and the commutator-type combination flips the oddness back into an even hh-dependence at cubic odd order.

So:
C(h) is the right candidate for a nonzero even cubic quotient contribution.
C(h) is the right candidate for a nonzero even cubic quotient contribution.​
5. Why this is better than the failed bb-channel

The bb-channel gave an antisymmetric off-diagonal odd-ss contribution, which is likely value-like and quotient-trivial.

By contrast, C(h)C(h) is built from same-point whitening and is not forced into that antisymmetric off-diagonal line. It typically lands in a diagonal/symmetric sector after the commutator with N0N0​.

So it is much more plausible that its quotient class is nonzero.

    Nonlinear same-point even-cubic lemma.

    C(h)=X2[2](h)N0X1(h)−X1(h)N0X2[2](h)
    C(h)=X2[2]​(h)N0​X1​(h)−X1​(h)N0​X2[2]​(h)

    is nonzero for large ∣h∣∣h∣, and its leading asymptotic is even in hh.

1. Finite-order truncation reduces the problem

TRF(p):=(Ξ1(p),Ξ3(p),…,Ξ2R+1(p))∈VR,

Ξ1=Ξ3=⋯=Ξ2Rk+1=0

4. What we can prove formally already

There is a standard compactness lemma:
Lemma

Assume p↦F(p;⋅)p↦F(p;⋅) is continuous from compact PkPk​ into odd holomorphic germs on ∣z∣<ρ0∣z∣<ρ0​. If 0∉F(Ak)0∈/F(Ak​), then there exists RkRk​ such that
TRkF(p)≠0
TRk​​F(p)=0

for all p∈Akp∈Ak​.
Proof
