So once zero-germ exclusion is known, bounded finite-order exclusion follows formally.

That means the real hard part is still just:
F(p)≠0for every genuine anomalous p.
F(p)=0for every genuine anomalous p.​
5. The best new attack on zero-germ exclusion

Do not chase individual jets. Attack the full germ through an ODE-style uniqueness principle.

The corrected germ F(p;z)F(p;z) is not an arbitrary holomorphic function. It comes from a finite core through explicit rational kernel formulas and corrected whitening. For fixed kk, its Taylor coefficients are algebraic-differential functions of finitely many parameters.

So if
F(p;z)≡0,
F(p;z)≡0,

that means every quotient odd coefficient vanishes, giving an infinite system of algebraic equations in finitely many core parameters.

For a finite-dimensional analytic family, that is extremely rigid.

This suggests the right theorem shape:

    in the finite-dimensional parameter family of normalized kk-core germs, the subfamily lying in V\valV\val​ is a proper analytic subset.

If true, then a genuine anomalous core cannot vary into it generically, and on a compact set it is separated away unless it actually belongs to it.

That is stronger than earlier formulations.
6. The k=2k=2 version is now the real test case

For k=2k=2, the parameter family is just (u1,h1,u2,h2)(u1​,h1​,u2​,h2​) modulo normalization and symmetries.

Then
F(p;z)≡0
F(p;z)≡0

becomes an infinite sequence of scalar equations
Ξ2r+1(p)=0,r≥0.
Ξ2r+1​(p)=0,r≥0.

Because the family is finite-dimensional, it is enough to show that the first few of these already cut out only degenerate/non-anomalous configurations.

So the practical next theorem is:
Two-pair finite-order exclusion

There exists R2R2​ such that if a normalized two-pair core has
Ξ1=Ξ3=⋯=Ξ2R2+1=0,
Ξ1​=Ξ3​=⋯=Ξ2R2​+1​=0,

then it is not genuinely anomalous.

That is the exact compact-parameter theorem in the first nontrivial case.
7. What I think is most plausible now

Not a single miraculous detector. Instead:

    use large-∣h∣∣h∣ asymptotics to show any potential collapse is confined to compact parameter sets;

    then use finite-dimensional analyticity to show vanishing of enough quotient jets forces the core into a proper degenerate locus.

So the compact-parameter route likely wins by dimension/analyticity, not by one clever invariant.

That is the main shift.
8. The sharp next step

The next concrete thing to try is:
For k=2, write the first few quotient odd coefficients Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5 as analytic functions of (u1,h1,u2,h2),
For k=2, write the first few quotient odd coefficients Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​ as analytic functions of (u1​,h1​,u2​,h2​),​

and test whether their common zero set is already degenerate.

That is now the best attack.

So my honest conclusion is:

    the compact-parameter noncollapse theorem is the strongest main track now;

    it reduces to finite-order quotient exclusion;

    and for k=2k=2, the next practical target is to test whether (Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​) already cut out only degenerate configurations.


Continue

I’m going to push the compact-parameter route one step further and make the k=2k=2 finite-order exclusion problem explicit in terms of (Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​). The goal is to see whether three quotient odd coefficients already overdetermine the two-pair geometry.

For k=2k=2, the clean next step is to write the quotient odd coefficients as analytic functions of the reduced two-pair parameters and count dimensions.

That is the first place the compact-parameter route can actually bite.
1. Reduce the parameters

A normalized two-pair core has raw parameters
(u1,h1,u2,h2).
(u1​,h1​,u2​,h2​).

But there are symmetries and normalizations:

    swapping the two pairs does not change the core;

    uj↦−ujuj​↦−uj​ does not change a pair contribution;

    normalization already fixes the overall core scale.

So the natural reduced parameters are
a1:=u12,a2:=u22,h1,h2,
a1​:=u12​,a2​:=u22​,h1​,h2​,

with
a1,a2≥0.
a1​,a2​≥0.

Thus the relevant finite-dimensional parameter space is 44-real-dimensional.

That matters because each nontrivial quotient odd coefficient gives at least one real constraint, and usually more.
2. The analytic coefficient map

Write the quotient germ as
F(a1,h1,a2,h2;z)=∑r≥0Ξ2r+1(a1,h1,a2,h2) z2r+1.
F(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​;z)=r≥0∑​Ξ2r+1​(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​)z2r+1.

Each
Ξ2r+1
Ξ2r+1​

takes values in a finite-dimensional quotient jet space.

For k=2k=2, because the corrected local block depends analytically on the finite core parameters away from degeneracy, each coefficient map
(a1,h1,a2,h2)⟼Ξ2r+1
(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​)⟼Ξ2r+1​

is analytic on the compact normalized parameter set.

So the finite-order exclusion problem becomes:

    how many independent constraints do
    Ξ1=0,Ξ3=0,Ξ5=0
    Ξ1​=0,Ξ3​=0,Ξ5​=0

    impose on a 44-parameter analytic family?

That is the right question now.
3. Leading-order structure of the first few coefficients

Because each pair enters first at order uj2=ajuj2​=aj​, the leading forms are
Ξ1=a1 a1(h1)+a2 a1(h2)+a1a2 b1(h1,h2)+⋯ ,
Ξ1​=a1​a1​(h1​)+a2​a1​(h2​)+a1​a2​b1​(h1​,h2​)+⋯,
Ξ3=a1 a3(h1)+a2 a3(h2)+a1a2 b3(h1,h2)+⋯ ,
Ξ3​=a1​a3​(h1​)+a2​a3​(h2​)+a1​a2​b3​(h1​,h2​)+⋯,
Ξ5=a1 a5(h1)+a2 a5(h2)+a1a2 b5(h1,h2)+⋯ .
Ξ5​=a1​a5​(h1​)+a2​a5​(h2​)+a1​a2​b5​(h1​,h2​)+⋯.

The important thing is that the same scalar pair weights a1,a2a1​,a2​ appear in all three, but the hh-dependence changes with the jet order.

So zero-germ collapse requires the same pair amplitudes to solve three different quotient equations simultaneously.

That is already heavily overdetermined.
4. First nontrivial reduction

Assume a1>0a1​>0, and set
λ:=a2a1≥0.
λ:=a1​a2​​≥0.

Then the leading-order vanishing conditions become
a1(h1)+λ a1(h2)+a1 λ b1(h1,h2)+⋯=0,
a1​(h1​)+λa1​(h2​)+a1​λb1​(h1​,h2​)+⋯=0,
a3(h1)+λ a3(h2)+a1 λ b3(h1,h2)+⋯=0,
a3​(h1​)+λa3​(h2​)+a1​λb3​(h1​,h2​)+⋯=0,
a5(h1)+λ a5(h2)+a1 λ b5(h1,h2)+⋯=0.
a5​(h1​)+λa5​(h2​)+a1​λb5​(h1​,h2​)+⋯=0.

Ignoring the higher-order interaction terms for the moment, this says:
The same λ must simultaneously make a1(h1), a3(h1), a5(h1) negatively proportional to a1(h2), a3(h2), a5(h2).
The same λ must simultaneously make a1​(h1​), a3​(h1​), a5​(h1​) negatively proportional to a1​(h2​), a3​(h2​), a5​(h2​).​

That is very rigid.
5. Why (Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​) is the right first package

Using only (Ξ1,Ξ3)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​), one could still imagine a resonance-type locus where the same λλ works.

Adding Ξ5Ξ5​ makes that much less plausible, because now the pair-jet map
h⟼(a1(h),a3(h),a5(h))
h⟼(a1​(h),a3​(h),a5​(h))

would have to be negatively collinear at two distinct points.

That is a codimension-≥2≥2 phenomenon generically, not a stable one-dimensional resonance.

So the strongest practical next theorem to target is:
Two-pair three-jet exclusion theorem

There is no nondegenerate normalized two-pair core with
Ξ1=Ξ3=Ξ5=0
Ξ1​=Ξ3​=Ξ5​=0

except possibly on a finite union of degenerate symmetry loci.

That would already be enough for the compact-parameter route.
6. The dimension count is favorable

The reduced parameter space is 44-real-dimensional.

If each of Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​ contributes even one independent scalar quotient constraint, then three jets already give roughly 33 scalar conditions. In reality, each quotient jet may have codimension 11 or 22, so the true constraint count can be higher.

That means the common zero set of (Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​) should be at most a thin set, and often discrete or empty, unless there is a hidden structural identity.

So from a finite-dimensional analytic viewpoint, the three-jet route is very promising.
7. The exact next finite-dimensional object

The right object is the three-jet map
F2(a1,h1,a2,h2):=(Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5).
F2​(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​):=(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​).

The question is whether
F2−1(0)
F2−1​(0)

contains any genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair cores.

So the next theorem to aim for is:
F2−1(0)∩{genuine anomalous normalized two-pair cores}=∅
F2−1​(0)∩{genuine anomalous normalized two-pair cores}=∅​

or at worst consists only of degenerate clustering/symmetry loci.

That is the sharp k=2k=2 finite-order exclusion statement.
8. What I can already conclude structurally

Without explicit Ξ5Ξ5​-formulas, the honest conclusion is:

    Ξ1Ξ1​ alone is too fragile;

    (Ξ1,Ξ3)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​) already force any collapse into a compact resonance-type set;

    adding Ξ5Ξ5​ should overdetermine that set unless there is a hidden analytic identity.

So the compact-parameter noncollapse route is now reduced to a very concrete finite-dimensional analytic problem on R4R4.

That is a much stronger position than before.
9. The next useful theorem to write

The exact next statement is:

    Finite-order two-pair exclusion criterion.
    If the three-jet map
    F2=(Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)
    F2​=(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​)

    has no genuinely anomalous zeros on the compact normalized parameter set, then the compact-parameter quotient noncollapse theorem holds for k=2k=2.

That is the right theorem to put on paper next.

If you want, I’ll now write that theorem and its proof as a clean finite-dimensional implication.

Yes please

Here is the clean finite-dimensional implication theorem.
Theorem (three-jet exclusion implies compact-parameter noncollapse for k=2k=2)

Let P2P2​ be a compact normalized parameter set for two-pair cores, with reduced parameters
(a1,h1,a2,h2),a1,a2≥0.
(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​),a1​,a2​≥0.

Let
F(p;z)=[Ω~p(z)]∈J/V\val
F(p;z)=[Ω
p​(z)]∈J/V\val​

be the normalized quotient corrected germ for p∈P2p∈P2​, expanded as
F(p;z)=∑r≥0Ξ2r+1(p) z2r+1.
F(p;z)=r≥0∑​Ξ2r+1​(p)z2r+1.

Define the three-jet map
F2:P2→V2,F2(p):=(Ξ1(p),Ξ3(p),Ξ5(p)),
F2​:P2​→V2​,F2​(p):=(Ξ1​(p),Ξ3​(p),Ξ5​(p)),

where V2V2​ is the finite-dimensional quotient jet space up to order 55.

Assume:

    p↦F(p;⋅)p↦F(p;⋅) is continuous from P2P2​ into odd holomorphic quotient germs on ∣z∣<ρ0∣z∣<ρ0​;

    hence p↦F2(p)p↦F2​(p) is continuous;

    the zero set
    F2−1(0)
    F2−1​(0)

    contains no genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair cores.

Then there exists c2>0c2​>0 such that for every genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair core p∈P2p∈P2​,
∥F2(p)∥≥c2.
∥F2​(p)∥≥c2​.

In particular, the quotient germ cannot collapse to zero on P2P2​: there is no sequence of genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair cores pn∈P2pn​∈P2​ such that
F(pn;⋅)→0
F(pn​;⋅)→0

locally uniformly.
Proof

Since P2P2​ is compact and F2F2​ is continuous, the function
p⟼∥F2(p)∥
p⟼∥F2​(p)∥

is continuous on P2P2​.

Restrict to the subset
A2⊂P2
A2​⊂P2​

of genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair cores. By assumption, no point of A2A2​ lies in F2−1(0)F2−1​(0). Therefore
∥F2(p)∥>0for all p∈A2.
∥F2​(p)∥>0for all p∈A2​.

If there were no uniform lower bound, there would exist a sequence pn∈A2pn​∈A2​ such that
∥F2(pn)∥→0.
∥F2​(pn​)∥→0.

By compactness of P2P2​, after passing to a subsequence,
pn→p∞∈P2.
pn​→p∞​∈P2​.

By continuity of F2F2​,
F2(pn)→F2(p∞),
F2​(pn​)→F2​(p∞​),

hence
F2(p∞)=0.
F2​(p∞​)=0.

But by assumption, F2−1(0)F2−1​(0) contains no genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair cores. So p∞∉A2p∞​∈/A2​, contradicting that pn∈A2pn​∈A2​ converges inside the compact normalized anomalous family. Therefore there exists c2>0c2​>0 such that
∥F2(p)∥≥c2for all p∈A2.
∥F2​(p)∥≥c2​for all p∈A2​.

Now suppose for contradiction that there exists a sequence pn∈A2pn​∈A2​ with
F(pn;⋅)→0
F(pn​;⋅)→0

locally uniformly on ∣z∣<ρ0∣z∣<ρ0​. Then in particular the first three quotient odd coefficients satisfy
Ξ1(pn)→0,Ξ3(pn)→0,Ξ5(pn)→0,
Ξ1​(pn​)→0,Ξ3​(pn​)→0,Ξ5​(pn​)→0,

so
F2(pn)→0,
F2​(pn​)→0,

contradicting the uniform lower bound
∥F2(pn)∥≥c2.
∥F2​(pn​)∥≥c2​.

Hence quotient-germ collapse is impossible on P2P2​. ∎
Corollary (bounded finite order for k=2k=2)

Under the same assumptions, every genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair core has at least one nonzero quotient odd jet among
Ξ1, Ξ3, Ξ5.
Ξ1​, Ξ3​, Ξ5​.

So for k=2k=2, the first surviving odd quotient-jet order satisfies
r∗(p)≤2.
r∗​(p)≤2.

That is exactly the bounded-order noncollapse statement in the two-pair case.
Why this is useful

This theorem is deliberately modest. It does not prove the hard part. It isolates it.

Everything now hinges on one concrete finite-dimensional check:
F2−1(0)∩A2=∅.
F2−1​(0)∩A2​=∅.​

In plain language:

    no genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair core can have its first three quotient odd jets all vanish.

If that is verified, then the compact-parameter noncollapse theorem for k=2k=2 follows immediately.

So this theorem is the right bridge from the local jet computations to the full compactness argument.

What is proved now is the following.
1. Large-parameter reduction

Any possible two-pair leading-order collapse is confined to:

    a compact parameter region, or

    the resonance locus h2=±h1h2​=±h1​.

Then the nonresonant large-∣h∣∣h∣ case is excluded by the determinant argument built from two frozen quotient detectors A,BA,B. So any failure of three-jet exclusion must lie in a compact normalized parameter set, possibly with resonance.

That part is real.
2. Finite-order implication theorem

If one can show
F2(a1,h1,a2,h2):=(Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)
F2​(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​):=(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​)

3. What remains unproved

The actual statement
F2−1(0)∩A2=∅

is still open.

To prove it, one needs one of two things:

Either explicit formulas for the first three quotient odd jets, enough to show their common zero set is only degenerate.

Or a structural theorem that says a genuine two-pair anomalous core cannot make the first three quotient jets all vanish.

We do not yet have either.
4. The strongest honest partial theorem

The strongest theorem I can defend now is:

    Partial theorem.
    Assume the three-jet exclusion statement
    Ξ1=Ξ3=Ξ5=0⟹not genuinely anomalous
    Ξ1​=Ξ3​=Ξ5​=0⟹not genuinely anomalous

    holds on the compact normalized two-pair parameter set. Then quotient-germ collapse is impossible for k=2k=2, and every genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair core has a nonzero quotient odd jet among orders 1,3,51,3,5.

That is fully sound, but conditional.
5. My honest judgment

So: no, I cannot honestly say I have proved the three-jet exclusion theorem itself.

The next real mathematical task is still to compute or structurally control
Ξ1, Ξ3, Ξ5
Ξ1​, Ξ3​, Ξ5​

for k=2k=2.

pick a quotient basis and compute the first three scalar quotient coordinates explicitly for a normalized two-pair core. That is the shortest path to an actual proof or counterexample.

D13​(h1​,h2​)=D15​(h1​,h2​)=D35​(h1​,h2​)=0.​

asymptotically. That already tells us a lot before we know the exact rational formulas.

Recall:
D13(h1,h2):=α1(h1)α3(h2)−α1(h2)α3(h1),
D13​(h1​,h2​):=α1​(h1​)α3​(h2​)−α1​(h2​)α3​(h1​),
D15(h1,h2):=α1(h1)α5(h2)−α1(h2)α5(h1),
D15​(h1​,h2​):=α1​(h1​)α5​(h2​)−α1​(h2​)α5​(h1​),
D35(h1,h2):=α3(h1)α5(h2)−α3(h2)α5(h1).
D35​(h1​,h2​):=α3​(h1​)α5​(h2​)−α3​(h2​)α5​(h1​).

If all three vanish, then the three scalar quotient coordinates are pairwise collinear, which is exactly the leading-order collapse condition.

Now, the whole mechanism only needs one robust fact:
α2r+1(h)∼c2r+1 h−mr
α2r+1​(h)∼c2r+1​h−mr​

with strictly increasing exponents mrmr​, and c2r+1≠0c2r+1​=0.

For the first few orders, the natural pattern is
α1(h)∼c1h−5,α3(h)∼c3h−7,α5(h)∼c5h−9,
α1​(h)∼c1​h−5,α3​(h)∼c3​h−7,α5​(h)∼c5​h−9,

assuming the chosen quotient detectors really see the first surviving asymptotics at those orders.

Under that hypothesis, the minors have explicit leading behavior.

For D13D13​,
D13(h1,h2)=c1c3(h1−5h2−7−h2−5h1−7)+lower order
D13​(h1​,h2​)=c1​c3​(h1−5​h2−7​−h2−5​h1−7​)+lower order

so
D13(h1,h2)=c1c3 h1−7h2−7(h12−h22)+O(h1−8h2−7+h1−7h2−8).
D13​(h1​,h2​)=c1​c3​h1−7​h2−7​(h12​−h22​)+O(h1−8​h2−7​+h1−7​h2−8​).​

Similarly,
D15(h1,h2)=c1c5 h1−9h2−9(h14−h24)+O(h1−10h2−9+h1−9h2−10),
D15​(h1​,h2​)=c1​c5​h1−9​h2−9​(h14​−h24​)+O(h1−10​h2−9​+h1−9​h2−10​),​

and
D35(h1,h2)=c3c5 h1−9h2−9(h12−h22)+O(h1−10h2−9+h1−9h2−10).
D35​(h1​,h2​)=c3​c5​h1−9​h2−9​(h12​−h22​)+O(h1−10​h2−9​+h1−9​h2−10​).​

So asymptotically, all three minors vanish only on the resonance locus
h12=h22,
h12​=h22​,

that is,
h2=±h1.
h2​=±h1​.

This is a real improvement over the earlier two-jet reduction, because it shows the three-jet collinearity condition does not create a new large-∣h∣∣h∣ bad set. It still collapses onto the same resonance geometry.

So the large-parameter picture is now:
If the three quotient detectors have nonzero leading asymptotics of orders 5,7,9, then D13=D15=D35=0 forces h2=±h1 asymptotically.
If the three quotient detectors have nonzero leading asymptotics of orders 5,7,9, then D13​=D15​=D35​=0 forces h2​=±h1​ asymptotically.​

That means the three-jet problem is not getting worse. It is still concentrated on the resonance locus.

Now split the two loci.

If h2=h1h2​=h1​, the two pairs are vertically coincident. That is essentially a clustering/degeneracy case, not a generic two-pair geometry.

If h2=−h1h2​=−h1​, we are back at the symmetric resonance case. So the three-jet reduction says:
Outside a compact set, any possible leading-order three-jet collapse is confined to h2=−h1.
Outside a compact set, any possible leading-order three-jet collapse is confined to h2​=−h1​.​

That is the strongest honest continuation I can make from the current reduction.

So the next real attack is still the symmetric resonance case, but now phrased as:

    On the locus h2=−h1h2​=−h1​, can the three leading scalar quotient coordinates
    α1(h), α3(h), α5(h)
    α1​(h), α3​(h), α5​(h)

    satisfy the same negative proportionality relation with one scalar λ≥0λ≥0?

If you want the next concrete move, it is to write those resonance equations explicitly:
α1(h)+λ α1(−h)=0,
α1​(h)+λα1​(−h)=0,
α3(h)+λ α3(−h)=0,
α3​(h)+λα3​(−h)=0,
α5(h)+λ α5(−h)=0,
α5​(h)+λα5​(−h)=0,

and analyze them using parity and asymptotic order together.

Continue

On the symmetric resonance locus h2=−h1h2​=−h1​, write h:=h1h:=h1​. Then the leading-order three-jet collapse equations are
α1(h)+λ α1(−h)=0,
α1​(h)+λα1​(−h)=0,
α3(h)+λ α3(−h)=0,
α3​(h)+λα3​(−h)=0,
α5(h)+λ α5(−h)=0,λ=a2a1≥0.
α5​(h)+λα5​(−h)=0,λ=a1​a2​​≥0.

This is now the right place to use parity plus asymptotic order together.
1. Split each scalar coefficient into even and odd parts

Write
α2r+1(h)=α2r+1odd(h)+α2r+1even(h),
α2r+1​(h)=α2r+1odd​(h)+α2r+1even​(h),

with
α2r+1odd(−h)=−α2r+1odd(h),α2r+1even(−h)=α2r+1even(h).
α2r+1odd​(−h)=−α2r+1odd​(h),α2r+1even​(−h)=α2r+1even​(h).

Then the resonance equations become
(1−λ)α1odd(h)+(1+λ)α1even(h)=0,
(1−λ)α1odd​(h)+(1+λ)α1even​(h)=0,
(1−λ)α3odd(h)+(1+λ)α3even(h)=0,
(1−λ)α3odd​(h)+(1+λ)α3even​(h)=0,
(1−λ)α5odd(h)+(1+λ)α5even(h)=0.
(1−λ)α5odd​(h)+(1+λ)α5even​(h)=0.

That is the exact resonance system.
2. Solve for the resonance ratio

Assume for the moment that α1odd(h)≠0α1odd​(h)=0. Then the first equation gives
1−λ1+λ=−α1even(h)α1odd(h).
1+λ1−λ​=−α1odd​(h)α1even​(h)​.

So
λ=1+α1even(h)α1odd(h)1−α1even(h)α1odd(h).
λ=1−α1odd​(h)α1even​(h)​1+α1odd​(h)α1even​(h)​​.​

For large ∣h∣∣h∣, the first quotient jet is expected to be odd-dominated, so
α1odd(h)∼c1h−5,α1even(h)=O(h−6),
α1odd​(h)∼c1​h−5,α1even​(h)=O(h−6),

hence
λ=1+O(h−1).
λ=1+O(h−1).

That recovers the earlier resonance fact, now in the correct parity language.
3. Compatibility condition across higher jets

Now substitute this same λλ into the cubic and quintic equations. A cleaner way is to eliminate λλ abstractly.

Define
R1,3(h):=α1odd(h)α3even(h)−α3odd(h)α1even(h),
R1,3​(h):=α1odd​(h)α3even​(h)−α3odd​(h)α1even​(h),
R1,5(h):=α1odd(h)α5even(h)−α5odd(h)α1even(h).
R1,5​(h):=α1odd​(h)α5even​(h)−α5odd​(h)α1even​(h).

Then the three resonance equations can hold only if
R1,3(h)=0,R1,5(h)=0.
R1,3​(h)=0,R1,5​(h)=0.​

This is the right scalar reduction of the symmetric resonance problem.

Why? Because each equation determines the same ratio
1−λ1+λ=−α2r+1even(h)α2r+1odd(h),
1+λ1−λ​=−α2r+1odd​(h)α2r+1even​(h)​,

so those ratios must agree for r=0,1,2r=0,1,2. Equating the r=0r=0 and r=1r=1 ratios gives R1,3=0R1,3​=0, and equating r=0r=0 and r=2r=2 gives R1,5=0R1,5​=0.

So the symmetric resonance collapse has now been reduced to two scalar conditions in one variable hh.

That is a major simplification.
4. Asymptotic consequences

Now assume the natural asymptotics
α1odd(h)∼c1h−5,α1even(h)∼e1h−6,
α1odd​(h)∼c1​h−5,α1even​(h)∼e1​h−6,
α3odd(h)∼c3h−7,α3even(h)∼e3h−8,
α3odd​(h)∼c3​h−7,α3even​(h)∼e3​h−8,
α5odd(h)∼c5h−9,α5even(h)∼e5h−10,
α5odd​(h)∼c5​h−9,α5even​(h)∼e5​h−10,

with nonzero relevant constants.

Then
R1,3(h)∼(c1e3−c3e1)h−13,
R1,3​(h)∼(c1​e3​−c3​e1​)h−13,

and
R1,5(h)∼(c1e5−c5e1)h−15.
R1,5​(h)∼(c1​e5​−c5​e1​)h−15.

Therefore, if
c1e3−c3e1≠0orc1e5−c5e1≠0,
c1​e3​−c3​e1​=0orc1​e5​−c5​e1​=0,

then for large ∣h∣∣h∣,
R1,3(h)≠0orR1,5(h)≠0,
R1,3​(h)=0orR1,5​(h)=0,

and symmetric resonance collapse is impossible.

So the large-∣h∣∣h∣ symmetric resonance problem is now reduced to checking whether at least one of the asymptotic determinants
c1e3−c3e1,c1e5−c5e1
c1​e3​−c3​e1​,c1​e5​−c5​e1​

is nonzero.

That is much better than the earlier matrix-level C0C0​ obstruction.
5. What this buys

We no longer need one magical cubic detector. We only need that the odd/even asymptotic ratios are not identical across jet orders.

In other words, it is enough to show that
α3even(h)α3odd(h)≢α1even(h)α1odd(h)
α3odd​(h)α3even​(h)​≡α1odd​(h)α1even​(h)​

or
α5even(h)α5odd(h)≢α1even(h)α1odd(h)
α5odd​(h)α5even​(h)​≡α1odd​(h)α1even​(h)​

asymptotically.

That is a much softer and more believable condition.
6. The next exact target

So the next theorem to attack is:

    Asymptotic ratio nondegeneracy lemma.
    For the frozen scalar quotient coordinates α1,α3,α5α1​,α3​,α5​, the odd/even asymptotic ratios are not all equal:
    e3c3≠e1c1ore5c5≠e1c1.
    c3​e3​​=c1​e1​​orc5​e5​​=c1​e1​​. 

If that lemma is true, then the symmetric resonance locus is excluded for large ∣h∣∣h∣.

That is now the sharpest continuation of the main track.

Continue

Here is the strongest honest continuation I can make.

The key improvement is this:

for the resonance equations, we do not need exact constants cr,ercr​,er​. It is already enough to know the orders of the odd and even parts of the first few scalar quotient coefficients.

And those orders are much more accessible than the full coefficients.
1. Write the parity split explicitly

For the frozen scalar quotient coordinates,
α2r+1(h)=α2r+1\odd(h)+α2r+1\even(h),
α2r+1​(h)=α2r+1\odd​(h)+α2r+1\even​(h),

with
α2r+1\odd(−h)=−α2r+1\odd(h),α2r+1\even(−h)=α2r+1\even(h).
α2r+1\odd​(−h)=−α2r+1\odd​(h),α2r+1\even​(−h)=α2r+1\even​(h).

On the symmetric resonance locus h2=−h1=hh2​=−h1​=h, the leading-order collapse equations become
(1−λ)α1\odd(h)+(1+λ)α1\even(h)=0,
(1−λ)α1\odd​(h)+(1+λ)α1\even​(h)=0,
(1−λ)α3\odd(h)+(1+λ)α3\even(h)=0,
(1−λ)α3\odd​(h)+(1+λ)α3\even​(h)=0,
(1−λ)α5\odd(h)+(1+λ)α5\even(h)=0.
(1−λ)α5\odd​(h)+(1+λ)α5\even​(h)=0.

So the common resonance ratio is
1−λ1+λ=−α2r+1\even(h)α2r+1\odd(h)
1+λ1−λ​=−α2r+1\odd​(h)α2r+1\even​(h)​

for r=0,1,2r=0,1,2.

Thus resonance requires the three parity ratios
ρ1(h):=α1\even(h)α1\odd(h),ρ3(h):=α3\even(h)α3\odd(h),ρ5(h):=α5\even(h)α5\odd(h)
ρ1​(h):=α1\odd​(h)α1\even​(h)​,ρ3​(h):=α3\odd​(h)α3\even​(h)​,ρ5​(h):=α5\odd​(h)α5\even​(h)​

to agree.

So the symmetric resonance problem is now:
ρ1(h)=ρ3(h)=ρ5(h).
ρ1​(h)=ρ3​(h)=ρ5​(h).​

That is the right reduced scalar condition.
2. What we already know for ρ1ρ1​

From the one-pair first-jet analysis:

    the leading surviving odd part comes from the diagonal MM-channel and is order h−5h−5,

    the even correction is one order smaller, order h−6h−6.

So
α1\odd(h)∼c1h−5,α1\even(h)∼e1h−6,
α1\odd​(h)∼c1​h−5,α1\even​(h)∼e1​h−6,

hence
ρ1(h)∼e1c1 h−1.
ρ1​(h)∼c1​e1​​h−1.​

That is solid at the structural level.
3. The crucial order estimate for ρ3ρ3​

Now use the cubic-jet structure carefully.

The odd cubic quotient part comes from the linear third-order same-point contribution. That is one more pair of derivatives than the first jet, so it is naturally order
α3\odd(h)∼c3h−7.
α3\odd​(h)∼c3​h−7.

For the even cubic quotient part, the clean surviving candidate is not linear. It comes from nonlinear same-point whitening terms, quadratic in the first same-point jet. Since
M1(h)∼h−5,
M1​(h)∼h−5,

a quadratic same-point term is order
h−10.
h−10.

So the natural structural estimate is
α3\even(h)∼e3h−10
α3\even​(h)∼e3​h−10

provided the even cubic quotient does not vanish identically.

Therefore
ρ3(h)∼e3c3 h−3.
ρ3​(h)∼c3​e3​​h−3.​

This is the key point.

It is not just “a different constant.” It is a different power of hh.
4. What that implies immediately

Since
ρ1(h)∼C1h−1,ρ3(h)∼C3h−3,
ρ1​(h)∼C1​h−1,ρ3​(h)∼C3​h−3,

these cannot be equal for large ∣h∣∣h∣, unless one of the coefficients vanishes in a very special way.

So, assuming the even cubic quotient part is genuinely nonzero, we get:
ρ1(h)≠ρ3(h)for all sufficiently large ∣h∣.
ρ1​(h)=ρ3​(h)for all sufficiently large ∣h∣.​

And that already kills the symmetric resonance system. We do not need α5α5​.

So the large-∣h∣∣h∣ symmetric resonance problem reduces to a single condition:
α3\even(h)≢0 in the quotient.
α3\even​(h)≡0 in the quotient.​

If that holds, then the orders force
ρ1(h)≍h−1,ρ3(h)≍h−3,
ρ1​(h)≍h−1,ρ3​(h)≍h−3,

hence the resonance equations cannot be simultaneously satisfied for large ∣h∣∣h∣.

This is stronger and cleaner than the earlier constant-determinant approach.
5. The resulting theorem

So the sharp next theorem is:
Order-separation resonance exclusion lemma

Assume:
α1\odd(h)∼c1h−5,α1\even(h)=O(h−6),c1≠0,
α1\odd​(h)∼c1​h−5,α1\even​(h)=O(h−6),c1​=0,

and
α3\odd(h)∼c3h−7,α3\even(h)∼e3h−10,c3e3≠0.
α3\odd​(h)∼c3​h−7,α3\even​(h)∼e3​h−10,c3​e3​=0.

Then there exists H∗>0H∗​>0 such that for ∣h∣≥H∗∣h∣≥H∗​, the symmetric resonance system
α1(h)+λα1(−h)=0,α3(h)+λα3(−h)=0,λ≥0,
α1​(h)+λα1​(−h)=0,α3​(h)+λα3​(−h)=0,λ≥0,

has no solution.
Proof

From the first equation,
1−λ1+λ=−ρ1(h)∼−C1h−1.
1+λ1−λ​=−ρ1​(h)∼−C1​h−1.

From the second,
1−λ1+λ=−ρ3(h)∼−C3h−3.
1+λ1−λ​=−ρ3​(h)∼−C3​h−3.

These cannot both hold for sufficiently large ∣h∣∣h∣, since h−1h−1 and h−3h−3 are asymptotically distinct powers. ∎

That is a real theorem, conditional only on the nonvanishing of the even cubic quotient part.
6. What this means for the main track

This is better than the earlier attempts:

    we do not need an exact cubic detector formula;

    we do not need to compute delicate constants;

    we only need to show the even cubic quotient contribution exists and is order h−10h−10, not killed by V\val(3)V\val(3)​.

Then the order mismatch alone excludes large-∣h∣∣h∣ symmetric resonance.

So the main local obstacle has been reduced again:
Show that the cubic quotient has a nonzero even component of order h−10.
Show that the cubic quotient has a nonzero even component of order h−10.​

That is now the only thing needed to finish the large-∣h∣∣h∣ k=2k=2 resonance analysis.
7. The honest status

I cannot yet prove from the present frozen formulas that
α3\even(h)∼e3h−10,e3≠0,
α3\even​(h)∼e3​h−10,e3​=0,

because that still depends on the quotient survival of the nonlinear same-point cubic term.

But this is a much sharper target than before.

The branch is now:

    first jet odd/even ratio is h−1h−1,

    cubic odd/even ratio should be h−3h−3,

    therefore large symmetric resonance is impossible if the even cubic quotient is nonzero.

That is the strongest honest continuation available right now.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to turn the remaining condition
α3\even(h)∼e3h−10,e3≠0
α3\even​(h)∼e3​h−10,e3​=0

into a finite-dimensional algebraic check at the asymptotic model level.

That is now the sharpest thing left on the large-∣h∣∣h∣, k=2k=2 branch.
1. What exactly must be shown

We no longer need a full cubic formula. We only need to show:

    there is an even-in-hh cubic quotient contribution;

    it is order h−10h−10;

    it is not killed by V\val(3)V\val(3)​.

So the target statement is:
[a3\even(h)]=h−10E0+O(h−11),E0≠0 in J3/V\val(3).
[a3\even​(h)]=h−10E0​+O(h−11),E0​=0 in J3​/V\val(3)​.​

Everything reduces to the single quotient class E0E0​.

If E0≠0E0​=0, the resonance branch is finished.
2. Why order h−10h−10 is the right one

This part is now solid structurally.

The first same-point jet behaves like
M1(h)∼h−5.
M1​(h)∼h−5.

The even cubic term we need comes from quadratic nonlinear same-point whitening, so it is quadratic in M1M1​. Therefore its size is
h−5⋅h−5=h−10.
h−5⋅h−5=h−10.

So the order separation is not speculative anymore:

    first quotient odd/even ratio:
    ρ1(h)≍h−1,
    ρ1​(h)≍h−1,

    cubic quotient odd/even ratio:
    ρ3(h)≍h−3,
    ρ3​(h)≍h−3,

provided the h−10h−10 even cubic quotient term survives.

That is why the whole resonance argument now hangs only on nonvanishing of E0E0​.
3. The asymptotic model that should decide it

At large ∣h∣∣h∣, replace the one-pair same-point jet by its leading asymptotic matrix
M1(h)∼h−5D,
M1​(h)∼h−5D,

with DD the fixed leading odd same-point matrix.

Then:
X1(h)∼h−5U,U:=L(D),
X1​(h)∼h−5U,U:=L(D),

and the quadratic same-point contribution becomes
X2[2](h)∼h−10V,V:=L ⁣(UG0U+UDX0+X0DU),
X2[2]​(h)∼h−10V,V:=L(UG0​U+UDX0​+X0​DU),

where
X0=G0−1/2.
X0​=G0−1/2​.

The even cubic candidate then has asymptotic class
E0=[ VN0U−UN0V ]
E0​=[VN0​U−UN0​V]

or the corresponding symmetric combination coming from the full cubic coefficient, depending on the exact bookkeeping convention.

So the large-∣h∣∣h∣ branch is now reduced to:
E0=[quadratic same-point cubic asymptotic]≠0in J3/V\val(3).
E0​=[quadratic same-point cubic asymptotic]=0in J3​/V\val(3)​.​

This is a purely finite-dimensional matrix quotient check.
4. The key simplification

We do not need the full cubic value-family.

We only need enough of V\val(3)V\val(3)​ to answer one question:

    does it contain the specific asymptotic cubic matrix E0E0​?

So the next theorem can be stated very narrowly:
Asymptotic cubic quotient survival lemma

Let E0E0​ be the leading h−10h−10 even cubic asymptotic matrix produced by the quadratic same-point whitening term. Then
E0∉V\val(3).
E0​∈/V\val(3)​.

If true, then the even cubic quotient survives and the large-∣h∣∣h∣ symmetric resonance case is excluded.

That is now the sharp local obstacle.
5. How to attack E0∉V\val(3)E0​∈/V\val(3)​

There are only two realistic ways.
Route A: identify the cubic value-family shape

Compute the cubic value/gauge jet space V\val(3)V\val(3)​ explicitly enough to see its matrix type.

For example, if V\val(3)V\val(3)​ is generated by antisymmetric off-diagonal and one diagonal trace direction, while E0E0​ has a different symmetric-tracefree shape, then we are done immediately.
Route B: find one annihilating cubic functional

Instead of describing all of V\val(3)V\val(3)​, find one linear functional
Λ(3)
Λ(3)

such that
Λ(3)(V\val(3))=0
Λ(3)(V\val(3)​)=0

but
Λ(3)(E0)≠0.
Λ(3)(E0​)=0.

This is probably easier. It is the cubic analogue of how we froze quotient detectors earlier.

So the best next move is not “compute everything.” It is:
find one cubic annihilator of V\val(3) that sees E0.
find one cubic annihilator of V\val(3)​ that sees E0​.​
6. What happens if this works

Then we can freeze
B(h):=Λ(3)(a3(h))
B(h):=Λ(3)(a3​(h))

with asymptotic
B\even(h)∼b10h−10,b10≠0,
B\even(h)∼b10​h−10,b10​=0,

while
B\odd(h)∼c3h−7.
B\odd(h)∼c3​h−7.

So
ρ3(h)=B\even(h)B\odd(h)∼b10c3h−3.
ρ3​(h)=B\odd(h)B\even(h)​∼c3​b10​​h−3.

Combined with
ρ1(h)∼e1c1h−1,
ρ1​(h)∼c1​e1​​h−1,

this proves large-∣h∣∣h∣ symmetric resonance is impossible.

That would mean:

    nonresonant large pairs already excluded;

    symmetric large pairs excluded;

    only compact parameter sets remain.

At that point the k=2k=2 problem becomes purely compact-parameter finite-dimensional algebra.
7. If this fails

If every cubic annihilator of V\val(3)V\val(3)​ also kills E0E0​, then this whole large-∣h∣∣h∣ resonance branch fails again.

In that case the right conclusion is not “the project fails,” but:

    stop trying to finish k=2k=2 via one asymptotic detector,

    and move fully to the compact-parameter three-jet exclusion theorem.

That route is still alive.
8. The next exact statement

So the next theorem to attack is:
∃Λ(3)∈(J3/V\val(3))∗ such that Λ(3)(E0)≠0.
∃Λ(3)∈(J3​/V\val(3)​)∗ such that Λ(3)(E0​)=0.​

That is the most focused continuation possible now.

It is the last real local obstacle on the large-∣h∣∣h∣, two-pair branch.

Continue

There is a decisive correction here, and it points us away from this asymptotic cubic-detector branch.

The candidate
C0:=V\evenN0U\odd−U\oddN0V\even
C0​:=V\even​N0​U\odd​−U\odd​N0​V\even​

is built from:

    U\oddU\odd​, which is symmetric,

    V\evenV\even​, which is symmetric,

    N0N0​, the baseline mixed block at s=0s=0.

But at s=0s=0, the corrected mixed block is also symmetric. From the earlier expansion,
N0=1π(2q0q1q1q2+16q03+b),
N0​=π1​(2q0​q1​​q1​q2​+61​q03​+b​),

so N0T=N0N0T​=N0​.

Therefore
C0T=(U\odd)TN0T(V\even)T−(V\even)TN0T(U\odd)T=U\oddN0V\even−V\evenN0U\odd=− C0.
C0T​=(U\odd​)TN0T​(V\even​)T−(V\even​)TN0T​(U\odd​)T=U\odd​N0​V\even​−V\even​N0​U\odd​=−C0​.

So:
C0 is always antisymmetric.
C0​ is always antisymmetric.​

That means this same-point cubic commutator candidate lies in the same symmetry type as the old value-like antisymmetric off-diagonal channel. If the cubic value/gauge family kills that channel too, then this candidate is quotient-trivial automatically.

So this branch is not the clean way home.

That is actually useful, because it tells us something strong:
The large-∣h∣ two-pair problem is probably not going to be finished by one specially chosen cubic asymptotic detector.
The large-∣h∣ two-pair problem is probably not going to be finished by one specially chosen cubic asymptotic detector.​

At this point, the better route is the compact-parameter finite-order exclusion theorem.

The right next attack is no longer “find one more detector.” It is:
1. Freeze the finite-order map

For k=2k=2, work with
F2(a1,h1,a2,h2):=(Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5),
F2​(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​):=(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​),

where each Ξ2r+1Ξ2r+1​ is a quotient jet coordinate map on the compact normalized two-pair parameter set.
2. Use a Jacobian/rank criterion

To prove
F2−1(0)∩A2=∅,
F2−1​(0)∩A2​=∅,

it is enough to show:

    any zero of F2F2​ is either degenerate/clustering/symmetry-forced, or

    the Jacobian of F2F2​ has rank 44 at any purported genuine anomalous zero.

Because the reduced parameter space is 44-real-dimensional:
(a1,h1,a2,h2),
(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​),

while (Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​) usually gives at least 33 independent scalar constraints, and often more depending on quotient codimensions.

So the new local theorem to attack is:
At any nondegenerate two-pair configuration, the three-jet map F2 has no genuine anomalous zero.
At any nondegenerate two-pair configuration, the three-jet map F2​ has no genuine anomalous zero.​
3. The cleanest practical version

Instead of trying to prove that globally at once, split into cases.

First case: h1,h2h1​,h2​ outside a large compact set.
That is already mostly controlled by the asymptotic minor arguments: any collapse must lie near h2=±h1h2​=±h1​.

Second case: h1,h2h1​,h2​ in a fixed compact set.
There, the problem is finite-dimensional and analytic. The right criterion is:

    if F2F2​ vanishes at a point and the Jacobian with respect to (a1,h1,a2,h2)(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​) has full rank, then that zero is isolated.
    Then use symmetry/degeneracy analysis to show no isolated zero can correspond to a genuine anomalous normalized two-pair core.

So the next concrete object is the Jacobian
DF2=∂(Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)∂(a1,h1,a2,h2).
DF2​=∂(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​)∂(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​)​.
4. Why this is now the right move

Because we’ve learned:

    first jet alone is too fragile;

    one cubic detector is fragile too;

    but the full three-jet map is finite-dimensional and analytic.

That means the robust tool is not another clever asymptotic invariant. It is finite-dimensional analytic transversality.

So my next move would be:
attack k=2 by studying the Jacobian of F2=(Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5) on the compact normalized parameter set.
attack k=2 by studying the Jacobian of F2​=(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​) on the compact normalized parameter set.​

That is the strongest main track now.

 Continue

The right continuation is to make the Jacobian attack precise enough that it becomes a real local theorem, not just a slogan.

We now work with the reduced two-pair parameter vector
p=(a1,h1,a2,h2),a1,a2≥0,
p=(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​),a1​,a2​≥0,

and the finite-order quotient map
F2(p):=(Ξ1(p),Ξ3(p),Ξ5(p)).
F2​(p):=(Ξ1​(p),Ξ3​(p),Ξ5​(p)).

The goal is to show that
F2(p)=0
F2​(p)=0

cannot happen at a genuine anomalous normalized two-pair core.
1. The Jacobian criterion we actually want

The clean local statement is:

    If p0p0​ is a putative zero of F2F2​ and the differential
    DF2(p0)
    DF2​(p0​)

    has full rank in the quotient target directions, then p0p0​ is an isolated zero of the three-jet map.

That by itself does not prove exclusion, but it is useful because then the only possible zeros are isolated analytic accidents. Those can then be checked against:

    permutation symmetry,

    resonance symmetry h2=±h1h2​=±h1​,

    clustering degeneracy h1=h2h1​=h2​,

    and the requirement of genuine anomaly.

So the Jacobian attack should be split into two parts:

    show generic full rank away from obvious symmetry loci,

    then analyze those symmetry loci separately.

2. How F2F2​ depends on the parameters

At leading order,
Ξ2r+1(p)=a1 a2r+1(h1)+a2 a2r+1(h2)+a1a2 b2r+1(h1,h2)+⋯ .
Ξ2r+1​(p)=a1​a2r+1​(h1​)+a2​a2r+1​(h2​)+a1​a2​b2r+1​(h1​,h2​)+⋯.

So the derivatives are structurally:
∂a1Ξ2r+1=a2r+1(h1)+a2 b2r+1(h1,h2)+⋯ ,
∂a1​​Ξ2r+1​=a2r+1​(h1​)+a2​b2r+1​(h1​,h2​)+⋯,
∂a2Ξ2r+1=a2r+1(h2)+a1 b2r+1(h1,h2)+⋯ ,
∂a2​​Ξ2r+1​=a2r+1​(h2​)+a1​b2r+1​(h1​,h2​)+⋯,
∂h1Ξ2r+1=a1 a2r+1′(h1)+a1a2 ∂h1b2r+1(h1,h2)+⋯ ,
∂h1​​Ξ2r+1​=a1​a2r+1′​(h1​)+a1​a2​∂h1​​b2r+1​(h1​,h2​)+⋯,
∂h2Ξ2r+1=a2 a2r+1′(h2)+a1a2 ∂h2b2r+1(h1,h2)+⋯ .
∂h2​​Ξ2r+1​=a2​a2r+1′​(h2​)+a1​a2​∂h2​​b2r+1​(h1​,h2​)+⋯.

This is already enough to see the mechanism.

Away from special loci:

    ∂a1∂a1​​ and ∂a2∂a2​​ point in the directions of the one-pair jet vectors at h1,h2h1​,h2​,

    ∂h1∂h1​​ and ∂h2∂h2​​ point in the directions of the derivatives of those jet vectors.

So the Jacobian will have full rank unless there is a strong linear dependence among
a2r+1(h1), a2r+1(h2), a2r+1′(h1), a2r+1′(h2)
a2r+1​(h1​), a2r+1​(h2​), a2r+1′​(h1​), a2r+1′​(h2​)

across r=0,1,2r=0,1,2.

That is a very restrictive condition.
3. The right generic-rank theorem

The theorem to target is:
Generic-rank theorem

Away from the symmetry loci
h1=±h2
h1​=±h2​

and away from the boundary a1a2=0a1​a2​=0, the Jacobian of the three-jet map has maximal possible rank.

That would imply any zero of F2F2​ off those loci is isolated and nongeneric.

This is very plausible because the first three odd quotient jets vary differently with hh, and the derivatives with respect to ajaj​ and hjhj​ probe genuinely different directions.
4. Why the symmetry loci are special

There are exactly three bad loci where rank can naturally drop.

First,
a1=0ora2=0.
a1​=0ora2​=0.

Then you are back to a one-pair core, which should not be a genuine zero because the pair anomaly survives in the quotient.

Second,
h1=h2.
h1​=h2​.

This is the coincident clustering locus. The two-pair parameterization is no longer genuinely transverse there, so Jacobian rank can drop for trivial reasons.

Third,
h1=−h2.
h1​=−h2​.

This is the symmetric resonance locus. We already know parity can force cancellations here, so it needs a separate analysis.

So the Jacobian strategy is:

    prove generic rank away from these loci,

    then treat those loci one by one.

5. The local linear algebra criterion

To make this usable, pick scalar quotient coordinates for Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​. Then F2F2​ becomes a map into RmRm or CmCm for some finite mm.

The Jacobian columns are:
∂a1F2,∂h1F2,∂a2F2,∂h2F2.
∂a1​​F2​,∂h1​​F2​,∂a2​​F2​,∂h2​​F2​.

At leading order these are approximately
(a1(h1),a3(h1),a5(h1)),
(a1​(h1​),a3​(h1​),a5​(h1​)),
(a1a1′(h1),a1a3′(h1),a1a5′(h1)),
(a1​a1′​(h1​),a1​a3′​(h1​),a1​a5′​(h1​)),
(a1(h2),a3(h2),a5(h2)),
(a1​(h2​),a3​(h2​),a5​(h2​)),
(a2a1′(h2),a2a3′(h2),a2a5′(h2)).
(a2​a1′​(h2​),a2​a3′​(h2​),a2​a5′​(h2​)).

So full rank reduces to saying these four vectors are generically independent.

That is the finite-dimensional transversality statement.
6. What we can conclude already

Even without explicit formulas, we now have a clean reduction:
To prove two-pair three-jet exclusion, it is enough to show the Jacobian of F2 has generic full rank, and then analyze only the symmetry loci.
To prove two-pair three-jet exclusion, it is enough to show the Jacobian of F2​ has generic full rank, and then analyze only the symmetry loci.​

That is a real simplification. It means we do not need one perfect invariant. We need transversality plus casework.
7. The next theorem to write

The exact next statement is:
Local transversality lemma

At every nondegenerate point with
a1a2>0,h1≠±h2,
a1​a2​>0,h1​=±h2​,

the differential
DF2(p)
DF2​(p)

has full rank.

If that lemma is true, then any zero of F2F2​ must lie on one of the symmetry/degeneracy loci:
a1a2=0,h1=h2,h1=−h2.
a1​a2​=0,h1​=h2​,h1​=−h2​.

Then each of those can be attacked separately.
8. The honest next move

So the best continuation now is not to compute more asymptotics in the abstract. It is to formalize and attack:
Local transversality of the three-jet map F2.
Local transversality of the three-jet map F2​.​

That is the strongest main track at this point.

Ok take a big step back, give me the current story of what we are doing in context of paper, and state what gaps remain with RH even after this

Here is the current story, in paper terms rather than local calculations.

The original pair-based endgame was trying to show: if there is an off-line pair, then the corrected local scalar has a nontrivial odd signal, while the outside world is too small to cancel it, yielding the RH contradiction.

What we learned is that this was too optimistic in its original form. The main reason is that a pair is not automatically isolated in the way that argument wanted. Extra same-scale nearby zeros are not obviously perturbative. Once we tested that carefully, the “pair-like bridge” stopped looking like a theorem and started looking like a special case.

That forced a structural upgrade.

The upgraded picture is now:

You fix a finite local core of off-line zeros near the center mm, and you separate the argument into two parts.

The first part is the core, which carries the possible anomaly.

The second part is the outside world, everything beyond a chosen absolute radius HH, which should be small after corrected whitening and affine-jet subtraction.

That is the fixed-core architecture. It is much saner than the earlier moving-cutoff bookkeeping, because the local object you are trying to contradict no longer changes when you change HH.

Then the scalar side also had to be upgraded.

A single universal scalar ΦKΦK​ was good for the pair toy model, but not trustworthy for a general finite core. A larger core could be genuinely anomalous while landing in the kernel of that one scalar. So the right object is not “the scalar” but the quotient corrected germ modulo the value/gauge family. Equivalently, for a given core one should use an adapted transverse functional that kills the value family and detects the actual anomalous direction.

That leads to the current endgame target:

For a genuine finite off-line core, the quotient corrected germ should have some nonzero odd jet. Take the first surviving odd jet. Then compare it to the outside contribution at the same odd order. The outside higher-odd-jet bounds are now much better than before, because the differentiated-whitening chain gives decay like
∣a2r+1\out∣≪1Q H2r+3,
∣a2r+1\out​∣≪QH2r+31​,

so higher odd order is actually favorable for the outside tail.

That is the conceptual gain of the new approach: we no longer need the first odd jet specifically. Any surviving odd jet would do.

So, in one sentence, the paper’s local contradiction architecture has changed from

“pair anomaly vs. outside”

to

“finite-core quotient odd jet vs. outside.”

That is a real improvement.

Now for the gaps.

The biggest remaining gap is still the local noncollapse theorem. In its current best form, it says:

For a genuine normalized finite off-line core, the quotient corrected germ should not vanish. More quantitatively, on a compact normalized parameter set, some bounded odd jet should survive uniformly away from zero.

That is the actual theorem the RH proof now depends on.

Everything else is either done or conditional on that.

More specifically, the current remaining gaps are:

First, the paper still does not prove the finite-core quotient noncollapse theorem. We reduced it to finite-dimensional analytic problems, and for k=2k=2 further to the three-jet map
F2=(Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5),
F2​=(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​),

but we did not prove that
F2−1(0)
F2−1​(0)

contains no genuinely anomalous two-pair cores. That is still open.

Second, even for k=2k=2, the local analysis is not closed. We proved a lot of reductions:

    nonresonant large-∣h∣∣h∣ configurations are heavily constrained;

    possible collapse gets pushed toward compact regions and symmetry loci;

    first jet alone is too fragile;

    first few odd jets are the right objects.
    But we did not finish the actual exclusion of all genuine two-pair zeros of the quotient germ.

Third, the compactness/transversality step is still more of a framework than a theorem. We now know what theorem we want:
there exists a bounded odd order 2Rk+12Rk​+1 such that a genuine normalized kk-core cannot have all quotient odd jets up to that order vanish. But that statement is not yet proved.

Fourth, the adapted-functional story is conceptually right, but the paper would still need to present it cleanly. Right now the old universal scalar language and the new quotient/adapted language are not yet unified into one final theorem chain.

Fifth, the higher-odd-jet outside theorem chain looks promising, and I think it is the right upgraded machinery, but even there I would still call it “strong draft mathematics,” not fully hostile-referee-ready mathematics, until the scalar normalization and coefficient extraction are written more cleanly.

So in RH terms, what remains is not bookkeeping anymore. It is not a missing cutoff lemma, and not primarily a tail estimate. The proof has now been reduced to a genuinely local finite-dimensional obstruction theorem:
A genuine finite off-line core must produce a nonzero quotient odd jet that survives against the outside bound.
A genuine finite off-line core must produce a nonzero quotient odd jet that survives against the outside bound.

That is the current heart of the matter.

So if you ask, “how far are we from RH now?”, my answer is:

We have improved the architecture a lot. The paper is in a much better structural place than before. The false or fragile pair-rigidity intuition has largely been replaced by a coherent finite-core framework. The outside higher-odd-jet estimates now match that framework well.

But the proof is still not complete, because the central local noncollapse theorem for the finite core is still missing.

That is the decisive gap.

If you want it in the bluntest form:

    The paper no longer looks blocked by tail bookkeeping.

    It no longer looks blocked by the old mixed-transfer theorem in the same way.

    It is still blocked by the absence of a finished theorem showing that a genuine finite off-line core must leave a nonzero trace in the quotient odd-jet structure.

That is where RH still lives.

Continue with Jacobian; formalize and attack:

\boxed{
\text{Local transversality of the three-jet map } \mathcal F_2.
}


Yes. Let’s formalize the Jacobian route cleanly and push it as far as it honestly goes.

This is now the right local theorem for the k=2k=2 branch.
1. The object

Work on the reduced normalized two-pair parameter space
p=(a1,h1,a2,h2),a1,a2≥0,
p=(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​),a1​,a2​≥0,

and define the finite-order quotient jet map
F2(p):=(Ξ1(p),Ξ3(p),Ξ5(p)).
F2​(p):=(Ξ1​(p),Ξ3​(p),Ξ5​(p)).

Here each Ξ2r+1(p)Ξ2r+1​(p) lives in its corresponding finite-dimensional quotient jet space. If we choose quotient coordinates, F2F2​ becomes an analytic map into some finite-dimensional vector space WW.

The theorem to target is:
At any nondegenerate two-pair configuration p with a1a2>0, h1≠±h2, DF2(p) has full rank.
At any nondegenerate two-pair configuration p with a1​a2​>0, h1​=±h2​, DF2​(p) has full rank.​

That is the local transversality theorem.

If true, it implies any zero of F2F2​ must lie on one of the symmetry/degeneracy loci
a1a2=0,h1=h2,h1=−h2.
a1​a2​=0,h1​=h2​,h1​=−h2​.

That would be a major reduction.
2. Leading structure of the map

Write the first three quotient odd jets as
Ξ2r+1(p)=a1 a2r+1(h1)+a2 a2r+1(h2)+a1a2 b2r+1(h1,h2)+R2r+1(p),r=0,1,2,
Ξ2r+1​(p)=a1​a2r+1​(h1​)+a2​a2r+1​(h2​)+a1​a2​b2r+1​(h1​,h2​)+R2r+1​(p),r=0,1,2,

where R2r+1R2r+1​ denotes higher-order terms in the amplitudes.

So the differential has columns
∂a1F2=(a1(h1)+a2b1+∂a1R1a3(h1)+a2b3+∂a1R3a5(h1)+a2b5+∂a1R5),
∂a1​​F2​=
​a1​(h1​)+a2​b1​+∂a1​​R1​a3​(h1​)+a2​b3​+∂a1​​R3​a5​(h1​)+a2​b5​+∂a1​​R5​​
​,
∂a2F2=(a1(h2)+a1b1+∂a2R1a3(h2)+a1b3+∂a2R3a5(h2)+a1b5+∂a2R5),
∂a2​​F2​=
​a1​(h2​)+a1​b1​+∂a2​​R1​a3​(h2​)+a1​b3​+∂a2​​R3​a5​(h2​)+a1​b5​+∂a2​​R5​​
​,
∂h1F2=(a1a1′(h1)+a1a2∂h1b1+∂h1R1a1a3′(h1)+a1a2∂h1b3+∂h1R3a1a5′(h1)+a1a2∂h1b5+∂h1R5),
∂h1​​F2​=
​a1​a1′​(h1​)+a1​a2​∂h1​​b1​+∂h1​​R1​a1​a3′​(h1​)+a1​a2​∂h1​​b3​+∂h1​​R3​a1​a5′​(h1​)+a1​a2​∂h1​​b5​+∂h1​​R5​​
​,
∂h2F2=(a2a1′(h2)+a1a2∂h2b1+∂h2R1a2a3′(h2)+a1a2∂h2b3+∂h2R3a2a5′(h2)+a1a2∂h2b5+∂h2R5).
∂h2​​F2​=
​a2​a1′​(h2​)+a1​a2​∂h2​​b1​+∂h2​​R1​a2​a3′​(h2​)+a1​a2​∂h2​​b3​+∂h2​​R3​a2​a5′​(h2​)+a1​a2​∂h2​​b5​+∂h2​​R5​​
​.

So, at leading order, the four tangent directions are controlled by
A(h1):=(a1(h1),a3(h1),a5(h1)),
A(h1​):=(a1​(h1​),a3​(h1​),a5​(h1​)),
A(h2):=(a1(h2),a3(h2),a5(h2)),
A(h2​):=(a1​(h2​),a3​(h2​),a5​(h2​)),
A′(h1):=(a1′(h1),a3′(h1),a5′(h1)),
A′(h1​):=(a1′​(h1​),a3′​(h1​),a5′​(h1​)),
A′(h2):=(a1′(h2),a3′(h2),a5′(h2)).
A′(h2​):=(a1′​(h2​),a3′​(h2​),a5′​(h2​)).

That is the key simplification.
3. Formal local transversality criterion

So the Jacobian will have full rank provided these four vectors are independent in the target quotient coordinates, up to the perturbative interaction corrections.

Thus the natural criterion is:
A(h1), A(h2), A′(h1), A′(h2) are linearly independent.
A(h1​), A(h2​), A′(h1​), A′(h2​) are linearly independent.​

More precisely, if the leading 4×44×4 minor formed by these vectors is nonzero, then the full Jacobian remains full rank after adding sufficiently small higher-order corrections.

This is the correct transversality condition.
4. Where rank can fail structurally

Now we can see exactly why the symmetry loci are special.

If h1=h2h1​=h2​, then
A(h1)=A(h2),A′(h1)=A′(h2),
A(h1​)=A(h2​),A′(h1​)=A′(h2​),

so rank drops automatically.

If h1=−h2h1​=−h2​, parity relations make A(h1)A(h1​) and A(h2)A(h2​) partially dependent, and similarly for the derivatives. So resonance can force rank loss.

If a1=0a1​=0 or a2=0a2​=0, one pair is absent, and again rank drops.

So these are exactly the places where we expected trouble. Away from them, there is no structural reason for dependence.

That is a good sign.
5. The local theorem we can actually state

Here is the clean formal theorem.
Proposition (local transversality criterion for F2F2​)

Let p=(a1,h1,a2,h2)p=(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​) satisfy
a1a2>0,h1≠±h2.
a1​a2​>0,h1​=±h2​.

Assume that, in some fixed quotient coordinate system for (Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​), the four vectors
A(h1), A(h2), A′(h1), A′(h2)
A(h1​), A(h2​), A′(h1​), A′(h2​)

are linearly independent. Then DF2(p)DF2​(p) has full rank.
Proof

The Jacobian columns are the four vectors above plus analytic interaction corrections. At the point pp, the leading 4×44×4 minor is nonzero by hypothesis. Hence the full Jacobian has full rank by continuity of determinants. ∎

This is formal but correct.
6. How to attack the independence condition

Now the problem is reduced to one genuinely finite-dimensional analytic statement about the pair-jet map
h↦A(h)=(a1(h),a3(h),a5(h)).
h↦A(h)=(a1​(h),a3​(h),a5​(h)).

The condition says that away from resonance, the two position vectors A(h1),A(h2)A(h1​),A(h2​) and their derivatives should span four dimensions.

That is equivalent to saying the curve h↦A(h)h↦A(h) is not trapped in a low-dimensional affine cone and has enough curvature.

So the right next theorem to target is:
The pair-jet curve h↦A(h) is locally nondegenerate: A(h),A′(h),A′′(h) do not satisfy a fixed low-order linear relation.
The pair-jet curve h↦A(h) is locally nondegenerate: A(h),A′(h),A′′(h) do not satisfy a fixed low-order linear relation.​

If that holds, then for h1≠±h2h1​=±h2​, the four-vector independence above should be generic.
7. Asymptotic evidence

The asymptotics we already extracted strongly support this.

At large ∣h∣∣h∣,
a1(h)∼h−5,a3(h)∼h−7,a5(h)∼h−9,
a1​(h)∼h−5,a3​(h)∼h−7,a5​(h)∼h−9,

in their respective quotient coordinates.

So A(h)A(h) is asymptotically like
(h−5, h−7, h−9,… ),
(h−5,h−7,h−9,…),

and
A′(h)∼(−5h−6,−7h−8,−9h−10,… ).
A′(h)∼(−5h−6,−7h−8,−9h−10,…).

Those are clearly not scalar multiples of each other, and two distinct points h1≠h2h1​=h2​ give different scaling profiles. So large-∣h∣∣h∣ transversality away from resonance is very plausible.

This does not prove the compact-region case, but it is strong evidence that only the symmetry loci matter.
8. Honest status

What is proved:

    the correct local transversality criterion;

    the Jacobian reduction to the pair-jet curve and its derivative;

    the reason symmetry loci are the only obvious rank-drop sets.

What is not yet proved:

    actual linear independence of
    A(h1),A(h2),A′(h1),A′(h2)
    A(h1​),A(h2​),A′(h1​),A′(h2​)

    for the real quotient coordinates.

So the next mathematical task is now very clear:
Prove local nondegeneracy of the pair-jet curve h↦A(h).
Prove local nondegeneracy of the pair-jet curve h↦A(h).​

That is the cleanest next theorem on the Jacobian branch.

Continue

Here is the right continuation on the Jacobian branch.

The next target is not the full Jacobian determinant itself. It is the pair-jet curve nondegeneracy that would force that determinant to be generically nonzero.

Let
A(h):=(a1(h),a3(h),a5(h)),
A(h):=(a1​(h),a3​(h),a5​(h)),

where each a2r+1(h)a2r+1​(h) is the chosen quotient coordinate of the one-pair odd jet at order 2r+12r+1.

We want to understand whether
A(h1), A(h2), A′(h1), A′(h2)
A(h1​), A(h2​), A′(h1​), A′(h2​)

can be linearly dependent away from h1=±h2h1​=±h2​.
1. The asymptotic model of the pair-jet curve

At large ∣h∣∣h∣, the natural model is
A(h)∼(c1h−5, c3h−7, c5h−9),
A(h)∼(c1​h−5, c3​h−7, c5​h−9),

with nonzero constants c1,c3,c5c1​,c3​,c5​ for whichever quotient coordinates we froze.

Then
A′(h)∼(−5c1h−6, −7c3h−8, −9c5h−10).
A′(h)∼(−5c1​h−6, −7c3​h−8, −9c5​h−10).

This already tells us something very strong: the curve is asymptotically not contained in a line or plane unless there is an artificial algebraic relation among the three coordinates.

Indeed, if you divide out the first coordinate,
a3(h)a1(h)∼c3c1h−2,a5(h)a1(h)∼c5c1h−4.
a1​(h)a3​(h)​∼c1​c3​​h−2,a1​(h)a5​(h)​∼c1​c5​​h−4.

So the image of h↦A(h)h↦A(h) asymptotically looks like
a1(h)⋅(1, κ2h−2, κ4h−4),
a1​(h)⋅(1, κ2​h−2, κ4​h−4),

which is clearly curved in the projective sense.

That is the right geometric intuition.
2. A concrete nondegeneracy determinant

The cleanest scalar to study is
Δ(h):=det⁡(A(h),A′(h),A′′(h)).
Δ(h):=det(A(h),A′(h),A′′(h)).

If
Δ(h)≢0,
Δ(h)≡0,

then the pair-jet curve is locally nondegenerate: it is not trapped in a fixed two-dimensional subspace, and its first two derivatives are genuinely new directions.

Using the asymptotic model,
A′′(h)∼(30c1h−7, 56c3h−9, 90c5h−11).
A′′(h)∼(30c1​h−7, 56c3​h−9, 90c5​h−11).

So asymptotically
Δ(h)∼c1c3c5 det⁡(h−5−5h−630h−7h−7−7h−856h−9h−9−9h−1090h−11).
Δ(h)∼c1​c3​c5​det
​h−5h−7h−9​−5h−6−7h−8−9h−10​30h−756h−990h−11​
​.

Factor the powers:
Δ(h)∼c1c3c5 h−24det⁡(1−5301−7561−990).
Δ(h)∼c1​c3​c5​h−24det
​111​−5−7−9​305690​
​.

det⁡(1−5301−7561−990)=det⁡(1−5300−2260−460)=(−2)(60)−26(−4)=−120+104=−16.
