So
Δ(h)∼−16 c1c3c5 h−24.
Δ(h)∼−16c1​c3​c5​h−24.​

This is a real result at the asymptotic-model level.

Therefore, provided the chosen quotient coordinates actually have nonzero leading coefficients c1,c3,c5c1​,c3​,c5​,
Δ(h)≠0 for all sufficiently large ∣h∣.
Δ(h)=0 for all sufficiently large ∣h∣.​

That means the pair-jet curve is asymptotically locally nondegenerate.
3. Why this matters for the Jacobian

If the curve is locally nondegenerate, then for nearby but distinct h1,h2h1​,h2​, the four vectors
A(h1), A(h2), A′(h1), A′(h2)
A(h1​), A(h2​), A′(h1​), A′(h2​)

should generically span a large space.

More concretely, write
h2=h1+ε.
h2​=h1​+ε.

Then
A(h2)=A(h1)+εA′(h1)+ε22A′′(h1)+⋯ ,
A(h2​)=A(h1​)+εA′(h1​)+2ε2​A′′(h1​)+⋯,
A′(h2)=A′(h1)+εA′′(h1)+⋯ .
A′(h2​)=A′(h1​)+εA′′(h1​)+⋯.

So the span of
A(h1),A(h2),A′(h1),A′(h2)
A(h1​),A(h2​),A′(h1​),A′(h2​)

contains the span of
A(h1),A′(h1),A′′(h1),
A(h1​),A′(h1​),A′′(h1​),

up to lower-order errors.

Thus the nonvanishing of Δ(h1)Δ(h1​) is exactly the right local signal that the Jacobian should have maximal rank near nonresonant points.

That is the bridge from curve nondegeneracy to transversality.
4. The resulting local theorem, asymptotically

So we get the following conditional asymptotic theorem:
Asymptotic local transversality theorem

Assume the frozen quotient coordinates satisfy
a1(h)∼c1h−5,a3(h)∼c3h−7,a5(h)∼c5h−9,c1c3c5≠0.
a1​(h)∼c1​h−5,a3​(h)∼c3​h−7,a5​(h)∼c5​h−9,c1​c3​c5​=0.

Then the pair-jet curve h↦A(h)h↦A(h) satisfies
det⁡(A,A′,A′′)≠0
det(A,A′,A′′)=0

for all sufficiently large ∣h∣∣h∣. Consequently, the Jacobian of the three-jet map F2F2​ is asymptotically full rank away from the resonance loci h1=±h2h1​=±h2​.

This is not yet the full compact-parameter theorem, but it is a real large-∣h∣∣h∣ transversality result.
5. What remains after this

This means the k=2k=2 problem is being squeezed from both sides.

Large nonresonant region: controlled by determinant/minor and now pair-jet-curve transversality.

Large resonant region: still delicate, but already heavily constrained by the parity-ratio analysis.

What remains is the compact parameter set, especially near:
h1=h2,h1=−h2,
h1​=h2​,h1​=−h2​,

and possibly small amplitudes.

So the next theorem to attack is no longer “generic transversality” in the large. That we now basically have at the asymptotic-model level.

The next theorem is:
Compact-region transversality or exclusion near the symmetry loci.
Compact-region transversality or exclusion near the symmetry loci.​

In practical terms, that means a local analysis near
h1=h2andh1=−h2.
h1​=h2​andh1​=−h2​.
6. The sharp next move

The cleanest next step is to linearize around the symmetry loci.

For example, set
h2=h1+ε
h2​=h1​+ε

near the coincident locus, or
h2=−h1+ε
h2​=−h1​+ε

near the symmetric resonance locus, and expand the three-jet map in εε.

That will tell us whether those loci are genuine zero sets or only apparent degeneracies.

So the next real target is:
Symmetry-locus expansion theorem

Expand F2F2​ to first order in the transverse displacement from
h2=h1orh2=−h1,
h2​=h1​orh2​=−h1​,

and show the transverse derivative is nonzero unless the configuration is degenerate.

That is the strongest next continuation of the Jacobian track.

Continue

Let’s continue on exactly that branch.

The next useful move is to expand the three-jet map transversely to the two symmetry loci
h2=h1andh2=−h1.
h2​=h1​andh2​=−h1​.

That is the right local test of whether those loci are genuine zero sets or just places where rank drops for trivial symmetry reasons.
1. Near the coincident locus h2=h1h2​=h1​

Write
h1=h,h2=h+ε.
h1​=h,h2​=h+ε.

Then the leading-order two-pair quotient jets are
Ξ2r+1=a1 a2r+1(h)+a2 a2r+1(h+ε)+a1a2 b2r+1(h,h+ε)+⋯ .
Ξ2r+1​=a1​a2r+1​(h)+a2​a2r+1​(h+ε)+a1​a2​b2r+1​(h,h+ε)+⋯.

Expand in εε:
a2r+1(h+ε)=a2r+1(h)+ε a2r+1′(h)+O(ε2),
a2r+1​(h+ε)=a2r+1​(h)+εa2r+1′​(h)+O(ε2),

and similarly
b2r+1(h,h+ε)=b2r+1(h,h)+O(ε).
b2r+1​(h,h+ε)=b2r+1​(h,h)+O(ε).

So
Ξ2r+1=(a1+a2)a2r+1(h)+ε a2 a2r+1′(h)+a1a2 b2r+1(h,h)+O(ε2)+⋯ .
Ξ2r+1​=(a1​+a2​)a2r+1​(h)+εa2​a2r+1′​(h)+a1​a2​b2r+1​(h,h)+O(ε2)+⋯.

This is the key local model.

So the transverse derivative at the coincident locus is
∂εΞ2r+1∣ε=0=a2 a2r+1′(h)+a1a2 ∂2b2r+1(h,h)+⋯ .
∂ε​Ξ2r+1​
​ε=0​=a2​a2r+1′​(h)+a1​a2​∂2​b2r+1​(h,h)+⋯.

At leading order in amplitudes, that is just
a2 a2r+1′(h).
a2​a2r+1′​(h).

Therefore:
Near h2=h1, the transverse direction is controlled by the derivative of the one-pair jet curve.
Near h2​=h1​, the transverse direction is controlled by the derivative of the one-pair jet curve.​

So unless
a1′(h)=a3′(h)=a5′(h)=0
a1′​(h)=a3′​(h)=a5′​(h)=0

simultaneously, the coincident locus is not a stable zero set of the three-jet map.

That is a real reduction.
2. Near the symmetric resonance locus h2=−h1h2​=−h1​

Now write
h1=h,h2=−h+ε.
h1​=h,h2​=−h+ε.

Then
a2r+1(h2)=a2r+1(−h+ε)=a2r+1(−h)+ε a2r+1′(−h)+O(ε2).
a2r+1​(h2​)=a2r+1​(−h+ε)=a2r+1​(−h)+εa2r+1′​(−h)+O(ε2).

So the leading-order jets become
Ξ2r+1=a1 a2r+1(h)+a2 a2r+1(−h)+ε a2 a2r+1′(−h)+a1a2 b2r+1(h,−h)+O(ε2)+⋯ .
Ξ2r+1​=a1​a2r+1​(h)+a2​a2r+1​(−h)+εa2​a2r+1′​(−h)+a1​a2​b2r+1​(h,−h)+O(ε2)+⋯.

Split into odd/even parts:
a2r+1(h)=a2r+1\odd(h)+a2r+1\even(h),
a2r+1​(h)=a2r+1\odd​(h)+a2r+1\even​(h),

so
a2r+1(−h)=−a2r+1\odd(h)+a2r+1\even(h).
a2r+1​(−h)=−a2r+1\odd​(h)+a2r+1\even​(h).

Hence on the exact symmetric locus ε=0ε=0,
Ξ2r+1=(a1−a2)a2r+1\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a2r+1\even(h)+a1a2 b2r+1(h,−h)+⋯ .
Ξ2r+1​=(a1​−a2​)a2r+1\odd​(h)+(a1​+a2​)a2r+1\even​(h)+a1​a2​b2r+1​(h,−h)+⋯.

That is the exact local resonance normal form.

This is much better than the earlier scalar ratio version, because it works directly at the quotient-vector level.

So the leading symmetric-resonance cancellation conditions are:
(a1−a2)a1\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a1\even(h)≈0,
(a1​−a2​)a1\odd​(h)+(a1​+a2​)a1\even​(h)≈0,
(a1−a2)a3\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a3\even(h)≈0,
(a1​−a2​)a3\odd​(h)+(a1​+a2​)a3\even​(h)≈0,
(a1−a2)a5\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a5\even(h)≈0.
(a1​−a2​)a5\odd​(h)+(a1​+a2​)a5\even​(h)≈0.

This is the right finite-dimensional resonance system.
3. The transverse derivative to resonance

Now differentiate in εε. At ε=0ε=0,
∂εΞ2r+1=a2 a2r+1′(−h)+a1a2 ∂2b2r+1(h,−h)+⋯ .
∂ε​Ξ2r+1​=a2​a2r+1′​(−h)+a1​a2​∂2​b2r+1​(h,−h)+⋯.

At leading order in amplitudes this is
a2 a2r+1′(−h).
a2​a2r+1′​(−h).

So:
Near h2=−h1, the transverse escape from the resonance locus is controlled to first order by the derivative of the one-pair jet curve evaluated at −h.
Near h2​=−h1​, the transverse escape from the resonance locus is controlled to first order by the derivative of the one-pair jet curve evaluated at −h.​

That means even if exact symmetry creates partial cancellation, moving off the resonance locus should generically restore a nonzero three-jet signal unless the derivative data are also specially aligned.

That is another real reduction.
4. What this implies for the Jacobian

Now we can state the symmetry-locus transversality test.

At a point on either symmetry locus, the Jacobian columns corresponding to h1,h2h1​,h2​ reduce to combinations of
A′(h),A′(−h),
A′(h),A′(−h),

while the a1,a2a1​,a2​ columns reduce to combinations of
A(h),A(−h).
A(h),A(−h).

So the Jacobian can only fail badly if the four vectors
A(h), A(−h), A′(h), A′(−h)
A(h), A(−h), A′(h), A′(−h)

are specially dependent.

Away from exact parity collapse, that is still a strong condition.

Thus the right next local theorem is:
On the symmetry loci, the transverse derivative of F2 is nonzero unless the one-pair jet curve and its derivative satisfy an additional special dependence relation.
On the symmetry loci, the transverse derivative of F2​ is nonzero unless the one-pair jet curve and its derivative satisfy an additional special dependence relation.​

This means the symmetry loci are not automatically zero sets. They are only potential zero sets if another finite-dimensional condition holds.
5. The clean finite-dimensional obstruction

So the whole k=2k=2 local problem has now been reduced to checking whether there exist (a1,a2,h)(a1​,a2​,h) such that the three vectors
(a1−a2)a1\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a1\even(h),
(a1​−a2​)a1\odd​(h)+(a1​+a2​)a1\even​(h),
(a1−a2)a3\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a3\even(h),
(a1​−a2​)a3\odd​(h)+(a1​+a2​)a3\even​(h),
(a1−a2)a5\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a5\even(h)
(a1​−a2​)a5\odd​(h)+(a1​+a2​)a5\even​(h)

all vanish, and their transverse derivatives along εε vanish compatibly.

That is now a one-variable finite-dimensional algebra problem along each symmetry locus.

This is a much smaller problem than where we started.
6. What is proved, and what is not

What is now genuinely established structurally:

    off the symmetry loci, local transversality reduces to nondegeneracy of the pair-jet curve;

    near h2=h1h2​=h1​, transverse escape is controlled by A′(h)A′(h);

    near h2=−h1h2​=−h1​, transverse escape is controlled by A′(−h)A′(−h);

    so the symmetry loci are not automatically persistent zero sets.

What is still not proved:

    that the symmetry-locus algebra system has no genuinely anomalous solutions;

    equivalently, that the pair-jet curve and its parity split do not satisfy the necessary dependence relations.

7. The next theorem to attack

The sharp next statement is:
Symmetry-locus exclusion lemma

On each symmetry locus h2=±h1h2​=±h1​, the reduced one-variable three-jet system has no genuinely anomalous solutions except possibly degenerate clustering/boundary points.

That is now the smallest meaningful theorem left on the Jacobian branch.

And the nice part is: it is genuinely one-dimensional in hh, not four-dimensional anymore.

That is the right next continuation.

Continue

Good. We can now reduce the Jacobian branch one step further.

At this point the k=2k=2 problem is no longer really four-dimensional. Along each symmetry locus it becomes a one-variable problem in hh, with one amplitude ratio parameter.

That is the right place to attack.
1. Symmetric locus h2=−h1h2​=−h1​

Set
h1=h,h2=−h,μ:=a1−a2,ν:=a1+a2.
h1​=h,h2​=−h,μ:=a1​−a2​,ν:=a1​+a2​.

Then the leading-order three quotient jets become
Ξ2r+1=μ a2r+1\odd(h)+ν a2r+1\even(h)+a1a2 b2r+1(h,−h)+⋯ .
Ξ2r+1​=μa2r+1\odd​(h)+νa2r+1\even​(h)+a1​a2​b2r+1​(h,−h)+⋯.

Ignoring the higher interaction term for the moment, the vanishing condition is
μ a2r+1\odd(h)+ν a2r+1\even(h)=0,r=0,1,2.
μa2r+1\odd​(h)+νa2r+1\even​(h)=0,r=0,1,2.

Since (μ,ν)≠(0,0)(μ,ν)=(0,0), this means that for a given hh, the odd and even parts must be collinear with the same scalar ratio across the first three odd orders.

Equivalently, define
ρ2r+1(h):=−a2r+1\even(h)a2r+1\odd(h)
ρ2r+1​(h):=−a2r+1\odd​(h)a2r+1\even​(h)​

in any fixed scalar quotient coordinate where the denominator is nonzero. Then resonance requires
ρ1(h)=ρ3(h)=ρ5(h)=μν.
ρ1​(h)=ρ3​(h)=ρ5​(h)=νμ​.

So the symmetric-locus problem is reduced to:
Can the three parity ratios ρ1(h),ρ3(h),ρ5(h) coincide?
Can the three parity ratios ρ1​(h),ρ3​(h),ρ5​(h) coincide?​

That is now a one-variable obstruction problem.
2. Why this is promising

From the asymptotic structure already extracted:

    a1\odd(h)a1\odd​(h) is order h−5h−5,

    a1\even(h)a1\even​(h) is order h−6h−6,

so
ρ1(h)≍h−1.
ρ1​(h)≍h−1.

For cubic order, assuming the even cubic quotient really survives, we have

    a3\odd(h)a3\odd​(h) order h−7h−7,

    a3\even(h)a3\even​(h) order h−10h−10,

so
ρ3(h)≍h−3.
ρ3​(h)≍h−3.

And similarly, the quintic ratio should decay even faster:
ρ5(h)≍h−5
ρ5​(h)≍h−5

under the same structural pattern.

So asymptotically these three ratios live on different scales:
ρ1(h)∼C1h−1,ρ3(h)∼C3h−3,ρ5(h)∼C5h−5.
ρ1​(h)∼C1​h−1,ρ3​(h)∼C3​h−3,ρ5​(h)∼C5​h−5.

That immediately implies they cannot all agree for large ∣h∣∣h∣, unless some coefficient vanishes unexpectedly.

So the large-∣h∣∣h∣ symmetric-locus case is already morally dead once the even cubic and quintic quotient parts survive.
3. Coincident locus h2=h1h2​=h1​

Now set
h1=h,h2=h.
h1​=h,h2​=h.

Then the leading three jets are
Ξ2r+1=(a1+a2)a2r+1(h)+a1a2 b2r+1(h,h)+⋯ .
Ξ2r+1​=(a1​+a2​)a2r+1​(h)+a1​a2​b2r+1​(h,h)+⋯.

So unless the interaction term precisely cancels the common one-pair direction, the coincident locus is not a zero set.

At leading order in small amplitudes, this is even simpler:
Ξ2r+1≈(a1+a2)a2r+1(h).
Ξ2r+1​≈(a1​+a2​)a2r+1​(h).

Since a1+a2>0a1​+a2​>0 for a genuine two-pair core, this can vanish only if the one-pair quotient jet itself vanishes. But that would contradict the one-pair nondegeneracy built into the whole framework.

So the coincident locus is much less threatening than the symmetric one. It looks more like a degenerate clustering parameterization than a genuine obstruction.
4. The true remaining local theorem

So the sharp local theorem is now:
Symmetric-locus ratio exclusion theorem

There is no hh in the compact normalized range such that
ρ1(h)=ρ3(h)=ρ5(h)
ρ1​(h)=ρ3​(h)=ρ5​(h)

for a genuinely anomalous two-pair core.

That is the one-dimensional core problem on the Jacobian branch.

Everything else has been reduced away:

    off the symmetry loci, generic transversality should rule out zeros;

    on h1=h2h1​=h2​, the leading map is basically one-pair and should not vanish;

    on h1=−h2h1​=−h2​, the whole problem is whether these parity ratios can coincide.

5. The cleanest next attack

The next thing to do is not more abstract Jacobian talk. It is to turn the ratio problem into scalar equations.

Define
R13(h):=ρ1(h)−ρ3(h),R15(h):=ρ1(h)−ρ5(h).
R13​(h):=ρ1​(h)−ρ3​(h),R15​(h):=ρ1​(h)−ρ5​(h).

Then the symmetric-locus obstruction reduces to
R13(h)=0,R15(h)=0.
R13​(h)=0,R15​(h)=0.​

So if one can show these two one-variable functions do not vanish simultaneously on the compact normalized range except at degenerate points, then the symmetric resonance locus is excluded.

That is now the sharpest next theorem.
6. Where RH still depends on this

Even after all these reductions, RH is still not proved because we still do not have the final local theorem that a genuine finite off-line core must leave a nonzero quotient odd-jet trace.

On the k=2k=2 branch, that has now been reduced to either:

    proving the three-jet map F2F2​ has no genuine anomalous zeros, or more specifically

    proving the symmetry-locus ratio system above has no genuine anomalous solutions.

That is a much smaller target than where we started, but it is still open.

The next exact move is to formalize R13,R15R13​,R15​ and attack whether they can have common zeros on the compact normalized hh-range.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to make R13R13​ and R15R15​ more explicit in terms of asymptotic orders, because that already gives a strong obstruction.

Recall the symmetric-locus ratios
ρ1(h)=α1\even(h)α1\odd(h),ρ3(h)=α3\even(h)α3\odd(h),ρ5(h)=α5\even(h)α5\odd(h),
ρ1​(h)=α1\odd​(h)α1\even​(h)​,ρ3​(h)=α3\odd​(h)α3\even​(h)​,ρ5​(h)=α5\odd​(h)α5\even​(h)​,

up to the harmless overall sign convention from the resonance equations.

We defined
R13(h):=ρ1(h)−ρ3(h),R15(h):=ρ1(h)−ρ5(h).
R13​(h):=ρ1​(h)−ρ3​(h),R15​(h):=ρ1​(h)−ρ5​(h).

A symmetric-resonance collapse can only happen if
R13(h)=R15(h)=0.
R13​(h)=R15​(h)=0.

So the right next step is to understand the asymptotics of these two one-variable functions.
1. Asymptotic orders

From the current structure, the natural orders are
α1\odd(h)∼c1h−5,α1\even(h)∼e1h−6,
α1\odd​(h)∼c1​h−5,α1\even​(h)∼e1​h−6,

so
ρ1(h)∼e1c1h−1.
ρ1​(h)∼c1​e1​​h−1.

For cubic order, provided the even cubic quotient survives,
α3\odd(h)∼c3h−7,α3\even(h)∼e3h−10,
α3\odd​(h)∼c3​h−7,α3\even​(h)∼e3​h−10,

so
ρ3(h)∼e3c3h−3.
ρ3​(h)∼c3​e3​​h−3.

And at quintic order, the same pattern suggests
α5\odd(h)∼c5h−9,α5\even(h)∼e5h−14,
α5\odd​(h)∼c5​h−9,α5\even​(h)∼e5​h−14,

hence
ρ5(h)∼e5c5h−5.
ρ5​(h)∼c5​e5​​h−5.

So asymptotically,
R13(h)∼e1c1h−1−e3c3h−3,
R13​(h)∼c1​e1​​h−1−c3​e3​​h−3,
R15(h)∼e1c1h−1−e5c5h−5.
R15​(h)∼c1​e1​​h−1−c5​e5​​h−5.

This is already very strong.

If e1/c1≠0e1​/c1​=0, then for large ∣h∣∣h∣,
R13(h)∼e1c1h−1,R15(h)∼e1c1h−1,
R13​(h)∼c1​e1​​h−1,R15​(h)∼c1​e1​​h−1,

so neither can vanish.

If e1=0e1​=0 but e3≠0e3​=0, then
R13(h)∼−e3c3h−3,
R13​(h)∼−c3​e3​​h−3,

so again it cannot vanish for large ∣h∣∣h∣.

So in all generic cases, the simultaneous zero set of (R13,R15)(R13​,R15​) is automatically compact.

That is a real conclusion:
Any common zero of R13,R15 is confined to a compact h-interval.
Any common zero of R13​,R15​ is confined to a compact h-interval.​

This sharpens the resonance reduction further.
2. What this means

The large-∣h∣∣h∣ symmetric resonance problem is essentially solved structurally.

The only remaining issue is the compact hh-range.

So the k=2k=2 symmetric-locus problem is now:

    on a fixed compact interval in hh, can the two one-variable analytic functions R13(h)R13​(h) and R15(h)R15​(h) vanish simultaneously at a genuinely anomalous configuration?

That is much smaller than the original local problem.
3. Finite-dimensional consequence

Because R13R13​ and R15R15​ are analytic in hh on the compact normalized range, their common zero set is either:

    discrete,

    or they share a common analytic factor.

So there are only two possibilities.

If they do not share a common factor, then any common zeros are isolated. Then one can try to rule them out by checking degeneracy or incompatibility with the full three-jet equations.

If they do share a common factor, then that indicates a hidden structural identity among the parity ratios. That would be a serious warning sign, but it is also a very concrete thing to test.

So the next theorem to target is:
Compact resonance analyticity lemma

On the normalized compact hh-range, the analytic functions R13R13​ and R15R15​ do not vanish simultaneously except possibly at isolated degenerate points.

That is now the exact one-variable theorem left on the resonance branch.
4. What is still missing

To actually prove that lemma, you still need more explicit information about the first three scalar quotient coordinates than we currently have. In particular, you need enough control on the coefficients cr,ercr​,er​ and the lower-order corrections to know that R13,R15R13​,R15​ are not identically zero and do not share a hidden factor.

So this is where the current branch still stops short of a full proof.

But the reduction is now very sharp:

    the full k=2k=2 local problem has been reduced to the three-jet map;

    off the symmetry loci, generic transversality should rule out zeros;

    on h1=h2h1​=h2​, the map looks essentially one-pair and should not vanish;

    on h1=−h2h1​=−h2​, the problem reduces to two one-variable analytic equations
    R13(h)=0,R15(h)=0.
    R13​(h)=0,R15​(h)=0.

That is the current endpoint of the Jacobian/resonance reduction.
5. Bottom line

So the next concrete mathematical target is now:
Show that R13 and R15 do not have a common nondegenerate zero on the compact normalized h-range.
Show that R13​ and R15​ do not have a common nondegenerate zero on the compact normalized h-range.​

That is the smallest honest local theorem left on this branch.

And even after all this, the global RH gap remains the same in substance: we still do not have the finished finite-core quotient noncollapse theorem. For k=2k=2, it has now been reduced to a compact one-variable analytic exclusion problem along the resonance locus, which is much better, but still not done.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to turn the compact resonance problem into a Wronskian-type nonidentity test.

Right now the symmetric-locus branch has been reduced to the two one-variable analytic functions
R13(h)=ρ1(h)−ρ3(h),R15(h)=ρ1(h)−ρ5(h),
R13​(h)=ρ1​(h)−ρ3​(h),R15​(h)=ρ1​(h)−ρ5​(h),

and a genuinely dangerous symmetric two-pair collapse would require
R13(h)=R15(h)=0.
R13​(h)=R15​(h)=0.

So the key question is no longer “can they have isolated zeros?” Of course they can. The key question is:

    are these identities forced by structure, or are any common zeros only accidental isolated points?

That is the right place to use an analytic-independence test.
1. Reduce to one function pair

Instead of working with R13,R15R13​,R15​, define
f(h):=ρ1(h)−ρ3(h),g(h):=ρ1(h)−ρ5(h).
f(h):=ρ1​(h)−ρ3​(h),g(h):=ρ1​(h)−ρ5​(h).

Then a bad symmetric-resonance point is just a common zero of ff and gg.

If ff and gg are analytically independent, then their common zero set on a compact interval is discrete. That would already be good, because then the remaining obstruction is only a finite set of isolated points, not a structural resonance family.

So the next theorem to aim for is:
f and g are not identically dependent on the compact normalized h-range.
f and g are not identically dependent on the compact normalized h-range.​
2. The right scalar test: a Wronskian-type determinant

The clean test is
W(h):=f(h)g′(h)−g(h)f′(h).
W(h):=f(h)g′(h)−g(h)f′(h).

If
W≢0,
W≡0,

then ff and gg are not functionally proportional on any interval. In particular, they cannot share a nontrivial common analytic factor except possibly isolated zeros.

That is exactly what we want.

So the next local obstruction can be reduced to:
W(h)≢0.
W(h)≡0.​

That is the right one-variable analytic criterion.
3. Why this is promising from the asymptotics

We already have the asymptotic orders
ρ1(h)∼C1h−1,ρ3(h)∼C3h−3,ρ5(h)∼C5h−5,

at least under the natural survival assumptions for the even quotient parts.

Therefore

g′(h)∼−C1​h−2+5C5​h−6.

Then
W(h)=f(h)g′(h)−g(h)f′(h).

That is very strong.

It means that asymptotically the resonance-ratio functions ff and gg are already analytically independent.

So they cannot share a common factor on the large-∣h∣∣h∣ tail.

That matches all the earlier reductions.
4. What this buys

This tells us that any common zeros of R13R13​ and R15R15​ on the symmetric locus are not part of a structural identity. They are, at worst, isolated points in the compact normalized hh-range.

So the large-∣h∣∣h∣ resonance branch is effectively reduced to a finite set of compact exceptional points.

That is a real improvement over the earlier story.
5. The new exact target

So the next theorem to formalize is:
Resonance-ratio independence lemma

Assume the first three frozen scalar quotient coordinates satisfy the natural asymptotics above. Then the Wronskian
W(h)=R13(h)R15′(h)−R15(h)R13′(h)

is not identically zero. Consequently, R13R13​ and R15R15​ have only isolated common zeros on the compact normalized hh-range.

This is the clean one-variable analytic theorem that should replace the earlier, more vague common-zero language.
6. What still remains even after this

Even if this goes through, you still do not have full k=2k=2 exclusion. You only get that any bad symmetric-resonance points are isolated compact exceptions.

To finish k=2k=2, you would still need one more step:

    either rule out those isolated points directly,

    or show they are degenerate/non-anomalous.

So this does not close RH. But it shrinks the remaining k=2k=2 obstruction to a finite exceptional set problem.

7. Bottom line

Why this is the right criterion

Because along the symmetric locus, the vanishing equations are parameterized by the two amplitudes through (μ,ν)(μ,ν). Those give at most a two-dimensional family of tangent directions inside jet space.

If the transverse derivative gives a third independent direction, then moving off the locus destroys the zero immediately.

So the whole theorem reduces to one rank statement:
rank⁡[M(h∗)∣T(h∗)]=3.
rank[M(h∗​)∣T(h∗​)]=3.​

That is the sharp next exact target.
What I can prove about it now

I cannot prove the rank is 33 at every exceptional point from the formulas we currently have.

What I can say is:

    M(h∗)M(h∗​) already encodes three different odd orders 1,3,51,3,5, so its rows are not naturally proportional.

    T(h∗)T(h∗​) involves derivatives of the one-pair jet curve, which generically are not locked into the same two-dimensional resonance image.

    Therefore failure of rank 33 is itself an additional nongeneric algebraic condition.

So even if the exceptional set EE is finite, the truly dangerous subset where the augmented rank fails should be even smaller.

That is a real reduction.
The precise lemma

Here is the clean statement you can use.
Lemma (exceptional-point transversality criterion)

Let h∗h∗​ be a common zero of the resonance functions R13,R15R13​,R15​. Suppose the corresponding resonance matrix M(h∗)M(h∗​) has a one-dimensional kernel generated by some nonzero (μ,ν)(μ,ν), and suppose the transverse derivative vector T(h∗)T(h∗​) satisfies
T(h∗)∉Im⁡M(h∗).
T(h∗​)∈/ImM(h∗​).

Then h∗h∗​ does not extend to a genuine zero of the full three-jet map F2F2​ off the symmetric locus.
Proof

Along the symmetric locus, the vanishing equations are encoded by M(h∗)(μ,ν)T=0M(h∗​)(μ,ν)T=0. Moving transversely changes the three-jet map by the vector T(h∗)T(h∗​) to first order. If T(h∗)T(h∗​) is not in the image of M(h∗)M(h∗​), then no infinitesimal change in the amplitude parameters can cancel the transverse perturbation. Hence the zero does not persist off the symmetric locus. ∎

This is fully sound.
What remains

So the “next exact target” has now been reduced one more step.

To finish the symmetric branch, it is enough to check, for each h∗∈Eh∗​∈E,
rank⁡[M(h∗)∣T(h∗)]=3.
rank[M(h∗​)∣T(h∗​)]=3.

That is a finite list of finite-dimensional linear algebra checks.

I cannot do those checks numerically or symbolically yet because we still do not have explicit formulas for the quotient coordinates of
a1,a3,a5anda1′,a3′,a5′
a1​,a3​,a5​anda1′​,a3′​,a5′​

at the actual exceptional points.

But the branch is now completely localized:
The remaining k=2 symmetric-locus problem is a finite set of augmented-rank checks.
The remaining k=2 symmetric-locus problem is a finite set of augmented-rank checks.​

That is the furthest honest push on this exact target.

How do we get the formulas for the quotient coordinates?

You get them by fixing the quotient basis after you freeze the jet spaces, not before.

Right now “quotient coordinates” sounds abstract, but in practice it is a finite linear-algebra recipe. The whole thing is mechanical once you decide which jet order you are working at.

Here is the clean pipeline.
1. Freeze the finite jet space at order 2r+12r+1

Take the corrected local block germ
Ω^\corr(s),
Ω
\corr(s),

expand it at s=0s=0, and keep only the coefficient of s2r+1s2r+1. Call that matrix jet
J2r+1∈M2(C).
J2r+1​∈M2​(C).

So for r=0,1,2r=0,1,2, the raw jet spaces are just copies of M2(C)M2​(C):
J1≅M2(C),J3≅M2(C),J5≅M2(C).
J1​≅M2​(C),J3​≅M2​(C),J5​≅M2​(C).

The quotient coordinates are coordinates on
J2r+1/V\val(2r+1),
J2r+1​/V\val(2r+1)​,

where V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)​ is the value/gauge subspace at that jet order.

So the first real job is: compute V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)​ explicitly.
2. Compute the value/gauge jet subspace V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)​

This comes from the pure value-family model, not from the anomalous core.

Concretely:

    take the corrected block formulas,

    plug in only the value/gauge deformation parameters,

    expand to order s2r+1s2r+1,

    collect the resulting matrices.

Their span is V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)​.

So for each order 1,3,51,3,5, you need a finite list of generator matrices:
B1(2r+1),…,Bmr(2r+1)∈M2(C),
B1(2r+1)​,…,Bmr​(2r+1)​∈M2​(C),

with
V\val(2r+1)=span⁡{B1(2r+1),…,Bmr(2r+1)}.
V\val(2r+1)​=span{B1(2r+1)​,…,Bmr​(2r+1)​}.

This is the step people tend to skip, and then the quotient stays fuzzy forever.
3. Choose the quotient coordinates as annihilators

Once V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)​ is explicit, the cleanest quotient coordinates are not basis vectors of the quotient itself, but linear functionals that annihilate V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)​.

Pick any inner product on M2(C)M2​(C), usually Frobenius:
⟨X,Y⟩=tr⁡(X∗Y).
⟨X,Y⟩=tr(X∗Y).

Then compute the orthogonal complement
(V\val(2r+1))⊥.
(V\val(2r+1)​)⊥.

Choose a basis
L1(2r+1),…,Ldr(2r+1)
L1(2r+1)​,…,Ldr​(2r+1)​

for that complement.

Then the quotient coordinates are simply
Λj(2r+1)(X):=⟨Lj(2r+1),X⟩,j=1,…,dr.
Λj(2r+1)​(X):=⟨Lj(2r+1)​,X⟩,j=1,…,dr​.

These automatically kill the value family, so they descend to coordinates on the quotient.

That is the clean answer to “how do we get the quotient coordinates?”
4. Apply them to the actual anomalous jet

Now take the actual corrected jet matrix J2r+1(p)J2r+1​(p) for your core parameters pp. The quotient coordinates are just
ξj(2r+1)(p):=Λj(2r+1)(J2r+1(p)).
ξj(2r+1)​(p):=Λj(2r+1)​(J2r+1​(p)).

So for r=0,1,2r=0,1,2, your three finite-order quotient maps are
Ξ1(p)=(ξ1(1)(p),…,ξd0(1)(p)),
Ξ1​(p)=(ξ1(1)​(p),…,ξd0​(1)​(p)),
Ξ3(p)=(ξ1(3)(p),…,ξd1(3)(p)),
Ξ3​(p)=(ξ1(3)​(p),…,ξd1​(3)​(p)),
Ξ5(p)=(ξ1(5)(p),…,ξd2(5)(p)).
Ξ5​(p)=(ξ1(5)​(p),…,ξd2​(5)​(p)).

That is all a quotient coordinate is.
5. In practical symbolic terms

For each odd order 2r+12r+1:

    Compute the jet matrix J2r+1\valJ2r+1\val​ for each pure value/gauge generator.

    Form the matrix whose columns are the vectorized J2r+1\valJ2r+1\val​ generators.

    Compute its nullspace in the dual. Those nullspace vectors are your annihilators Lj(2r+1)Lj(2r+1)​.

    Compute the actual anomalous jet J2r+1(p)J2r+1​(p).

    Dot it with each Lj(2r+1)Lj(2r+1)​.

That gives explicit formulas.

If you were doing this in a CAS, it would literally be:

    vectorize 2×22×2 matrices into C4C4,

    build the value-family span matrix,

    compute the left nullspace,

    apply to the actual jet vectors.

That is the whole recipe.
6. What you need from the paper formulas

To make this concrete, you need exactly three inputs.

First, the corrected jet matrix J2r+1(p)J2r+1​(p) for the actual core.

Second, the corrected jet matrices for the pure value/gauge deformations at the same order.

Third, a fixed normalization convention for the jets so you are not mixing scaled and unscaled versions.

Once those are frozen, the quotient coordinates are not mysterious anymore. They are just linear algebra.
7. The minimal version to start with

You do not need the full quotient basis for all orders at once.

The fastest path is:

    do order 11 first,

    compute V\val(1)V\val(1)​,

    compute its annihilator basis,

    get explicit Ξ1Ξ1​.

Then do the same at order 33, then order 55.

That will already tell you whether the three-jet map
F2=(Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)
F2​=(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​)

has enough independent coordinates to make the Jacobian strategy viable.
8. The actual bottleneck

So the real bottleneck is not “how do we define quotient coordinates?”

It is:
compute the pure value/gauge jet subspaces V\val(1),V\val(3),V\val(5) explicitly.
compute the pure value/gauge jet subspaces V\val(1)​,V\val(3)​,V\val(5)​ explicitly.​

Once those are in hand, the quotient coordinates follow automatically.

1. What V\val(1)V\val(1)​ is

2. What the value/gauge generators are

At first odd order, the only directions that should contribute are the infinitesimal deformations that change the local model without introducing a genuine off-line anomaly.

    the value-channel deformation itself;

    the affine/gauge normalization freedoms that survive corrected whitening.

3. How to compute each Bℓ(1)Bℓ(1)​

G±\val(s;θ),N\val(s;θ).
G±\val​(s;θ),N\val(s;θ).

So the whole problem is just:

    compute δℓG±(s)δℓ​G±​(s),

4. What this should look like structurally

From everything we already learned, the first value jet is very likely dominated by the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction. So I expect one generator to produce something of the shape
B\val(1)∼(0−110)
B\val(1)​∼(01​−10​)

up to lower-order diagonal corrections.

The gauge directions may contribute diagonal or symmetric-off-diagonal corrections.

So in practice V\val(1)V\val(1)​ is probably either:

    one-dimensional, spanned by the antisymmetric off-diagonal matrix, or

    two-dimensional, spanned by that plus one diagonal/gauge direction.

That is exactly what you want to settle explicitly.
5. Once you have V\val(1)V\val(1)​, get the quotient coordinates

(x11​x21​​x12​x22​​)⟼(x11​,x12​,x21​,x22​).

vec(B1(1)),…,vec(Bm(1)).

L1(1),…,Ld1(1).
L1(1)​,…,Ld1​(1)​.

These are the quotient coordinates:
Λj(1)(X):=⟨Lj(1),vec(X)⟩.
Λj(1)​(X):=⟨Lj(1)​,vec(X)⟩.

ξj(1)​(p)=Λj(1)​(J1​(p)).

Good. The next thing to do is pin down the first value/gauge generator matrices Bℓ(1)Bℓ(1)​ in the most explicit form we can justify.

This is where the quotient story becomes concrete.
1. A minimal model for the value/gauge family at first odd order

At first odd order, the value/gauge family should come from infinitesimal deformations that do not introduce a genuine off-line anomaly. In practice, there are only a few such directions:

    a value-channel deformation of the local scalar data,

    an affine midpoint/gauge renormalization coming from how the corrected basis is fixed,

    possibly one more normalization direction if the corrected whitening convention preserves a residual diagonal freedom.

So the right first move is to write
δG±(s)=±s AG+O(s2),δN(s)=s AN+O(s2),
δG±​(s)=±sAG​+O(s2),δN(s)=sAN​+O(s2),

for each infinitesimal value/gauge generator.

The ±s±s structure in δG±δG±​ is forced by the symmetric placement
t±=m±s2.
t±​=m±2s​.

Then the first value/gauge jet is
B(1)=DW(G0,N0,G0)[−AG,AN,+AG].
B(1)=DW(G0​,N0​,G0​)​[−AG​,AN​,+AG​].

So the whole problem is: what matrix shapes can AGAG​ and ANAN​ have for pure value/gauge motion?
2. The generic shapes of AGAG​ and ANAN​

From the corrected same-point formula
Gm,±(q)=1π(2q±12q±′12q±′112(q±′′+2q±3)),
Gm,±​(q)=π1​(2q±​21​q±′​​21​q±′​121​(q±′′​+2q±3​)​),

a pure value deformation changes:

    q±q±​ with odd first ss-jet,

    q±′q±′​ with even midpoint data but odd transport,

    q±′′q±′′​ similarly.

So the first same-point generator should have the general symmetric shape
AG=(g11g12g12g22).
AG​=(g11​g12​​g12​g22​​).

From the corrected mixed block, the first odd jet we already computed has the generic structure
N˙(0)=(0n−n0)
N˙(0)=(0−n​n0​)

for the anomaly side, and the value-family first odd jet has the same symmetry type to leading order.

So the first mixed value/gauge generator should have the generic antisymmetric shape
AN=(0n−n0).
AN​=(0−n​n0​).

This is the first key simplification:
At first odd order, AG is symmetric and AN is antisymmetric.
At first odd order, AG​ is symmetric and AN​ is antisymmetric.​

That already narrows V\val(1)V\val(1)​ a lot.
3. Apply the linearized whitening map to these generic shapes

Recall
DW(G0,N0,G0)[δG−,δN,δG+]=δX− N0X0+X0 δN X0+X0N0 δX+,
DW(G0​,N0​,G0​)​[δG−​,δN,δG+​]=δX−​N0​X0​+X0​δNX0​+X0​N0​δX+​,

with
X0=G0−1/2,δX±=L(δG±).
X0​=G0−1/2​,δX±​=L(δG±​).

At first odd order for a value/gauge generator,
δG−=−AG,δG+=+AG,δN=AN,
δG−​=−AG​,δG+​=+AG​,δN=AN​,

so
δX−=−L(AG),δX+=+L(AG).
δX−​=−L(AG​),δX+​=+L(AG​).

Therefore
B(1)=−L(AG)N0X0+X0ANX0+X0N0L(AG).
B(1)=−L(AG​)N0​X0​+X0​AN​X0​+X0​N0​L(AG​).

That is the exact symbolic formula for a first value/gauge generator matrix.

So the first value/gauge space is spanned by matrices of the form
B(1)(AG,AN)=X0ANX0+X0N0L(AG)−L(AG)N0X0,
B(1)(AG​,AN​)=X0​AN​X0​+X0​N0​L(AG​)−L(AG​)N0​X0​,​

with AGAG​ symmetric and ANAN​ antisymmetric of the allowed value/gauge types.

That is the first explicit formula you wanted.
4. The most likely basis generators

Because symmetric 2×22×2 matrices are 33-dimensional and antisymmetric ones are 11-dimensional, a natural trial basis is:

For the mixed antisymmetric direction,
AN(1)=(01−10),AG(1)=0.
AN(1)​=(0−1​10​),AG(1)​=0.

For the symmetric same-point directions,
AG(2)=(1000),AN(2)=0,
AG(2)​=(10​00​),AN(2)​=0,
AG(3)=(0110),AN(3)=0,
AG(3)​=(01​10​),AN(3)​=0,
AG(4)=(0001),AN(4)=0.
AG(4)​=(00​01​),AN(4)​=0.

Then compute
Bj(1):=B(1)(AG(j),AN(j)),j=1,2,3,4.
Bj(1)​:=B(1)(AG(j)​,AN(j)​),j=1,2,3,4.

The actual value/gauge subspace V\val(1)V\val(1)​ will be the span of whichever of these are genuinely realized by the paper’s value-family model. In practice, it will almost certainly be a proper subspace of the full 4-generator trial span.

But this basis is enough to get started algebraically.
5. What this tells you immediately

The first generator gives
B1(1)=X0(01−10)X0.
B1(1)​=X0​(0−1​10​)X0​.

Since X0X0​ is symmetric,
X0(01−10)X0
X0​(0−1​10​)X0​

is still antisymmetric. So the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction is definitely present in the candidate value-family space.

That strongly supports the earlier conclusion that the old skew detector is not a quotient detector at first order.

The other generators produce symmetric and diagonal corrections through the commutator-like term
X0N0L(AG)−L(AG)N0X0.
X0​N0​L(AG​)−L(AG​)N0​X0​.

So V\val(1)V\val(1)​ is likely spanned by:

    one antisymmetric direction,

    possibly one or two symmetric/diagonal directions.

This is exactly what we needed to know qualitatively.
6. How to get the annihilator basis

Now vectorize the Bj(1)Bj(1)​ into C4C4. For example,
(x11x12x21x22)↦(x11,x12,x21,x22).
(x11​x21​​x12​x22​​)↦(x11​,x12​,x21​,x22​).

Form the matrix
B(1)=[vec(B1(1)) vec(B2(1)) vec(B3(1)) vec(B4(1))].
B(1)=[vec(B1(1)​) vec(B2(1)​) vec(B3(1)​) vec(B4(1)​)].

Then compute the left nullspace:
L∈ker⁡((B(1))⊤).
L∈ker((B(1))⊤).

Each basis vector LL in that nullspace gives a quotient coordinate
Λ(1)(X)=L⋅vec(X).
Λ(1)(X)=L⋅vec(X).

That is the full symbolic procedure.

    you can compute explicit first quotient coordinates Ξ1Ξ1​,

Good. The cubic value/gauge space V\val(3)V\val(3)​ is the next real object, because once that is explicit, the Jacobian branch becomes concrete.

Here is the exact symbolic setup.
1. What V\val(3)V\val(3)​ is

At cubic odd order, take the pure value/gauge corrected block
Ω^\val\corr(s),
Ω
\val\corr​(s),

expand
Ω^\val\corr(s)=Ω0+sJ1\val+s2J2\val+s3J3\val+O(s4),
Ω
\val\corr​(s)=Ω0​+sJ1\val​+s2J2\val​+s3J3\val​+O(s4),

and define
V\val(3):=span⁡{J3\val from all infinitesimal value/gauge generators}.
V\val(3)​:=span{J3\val​ from all infinitesimal value/gauge generators}.

So the task is to compute the cubic coefficient matrix J3\valJ3\val​ for each value/gauge generator.
2. Expand the value/gauge block data to cubic order

For each infinitesimal value/gauge generator θℓθℓ​, write
G±(s;θ)=G0±sAG,1(ℓ)+s2AG,2(ℓ)±s3AG,3(ℓ)+O(s4,θ2),
G±​(s;θ)=G0​±sAG,1(ℓ)​+s2AG,2(ℓ)​±s3AG,3(ℓ)​+O(s4,θ2),
N(s;θ)=N0+sAN,1(ℓ)+s2AN,2(ℓ)+s3AN,3(ℓ)+O(s4,θ2).
N(s;θ)=N0​+sAN,1(ℓ)​+s2AN,2(ℓ)​+s3AN,3(ℓ)​+O(s4,θ2).

The parity pattern in ss is forced by the symmetric placement:

    same-point side: odd powers flip sign between ++ and −−,

    mixed side: each coefficient has its own symmetry type.

So for each generator you need the six matrices
AG,1(ℓ), AG,2(ℓ), AG,3(ℓ),AN,1(ℓ), AN,2(ℓ), AN,3(ℓ).
AG,1(ℓ)​, AG,2(ℓ)​, AG,3(ℓ)​,AN,1(ℓ)​, AN,2(ℓ)​, AN,3(ℓ)​.

That is the raw cubic data.
3. Expand G−1/2G−1/2 to cubic order

Write
X±(s):=G±(s)−1/2=X0±sX1+s2X2±s3X3+O(s4),X0=G0−1/2.
X±​(s):=G±​(s)−1/2=X0​±sX1​+s2X2​±s3X3​+O(s4),X0​=G0−1/2​.

These are determined recursively from
X±(s)G±(s)X±(s)=I.
X±​(s)G±​(s)X±​(s)=I.

The coefficient equations are:

At order ss,
G01/2X1+X1G01/2=−X0AG,1X0,
G01/2​X1​+X1​G01/2​=−X0​AG,1​X0​,

so
X1=L(AG,1).
X1​=L(AG,1​).

At order s2s2,
G01/2X2+X2G01/2=−(X1AG,1X0+X1G0X1+X0AG,2X0+X0AG,1X1),
G01/2​X2​+X2​G01/2​=−(X1​AG,1​X0​+X1​G0​X1​+X0​AG,2​X0​+X0​AG,1​X1​),

so
X2=L(X1AG,1X0+X1G0X1+X0AG,2X0+X0AG,1X1).
X2​=L(X1​AG,1​X0​+X1​G0​X1​+X0​AG,2​X0​+X0​AG,1​X1​).

At order s3s3,
G01/2X3+X3G01/2=−(X2AG,1X0+X2G0X1+X1AG,2X0+X1AG,1X1

+X1G0X2+X0AG,3X0+X0AG,2X1+X0AG,1X2),
+X1​G0​X2​+X0​AG,3​X0​+X0​AG,2​X1​+X0​AG,1​X2​),

so
X3=L(that RHS).
X3​=L(that RHS).

This is completely explicit once AG,1,AG,2,AG,3AG,1​,AG,2​,AG,3​ are known.
4. Compute the cubic corrected jet J3\valJ3\val​

Now expand
W(s)=X−(s) N(s) X+(s).
W(s)=X−​(s)N(s)X+​(s).

The s3s3-coefficient is
J3\val=−X3N0X0+X2N0X1−X1N0X2+X0N0X3
J3\val​=−X3​N0​X0​+X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​+X0​N0​X3​
−X2AN,1X0+X1AN,1X1−X0AN,1X2−X1AN,2X0+X0AN,2X1+X0AN,3X0.
−X2​AN,1​X0​+X1​AN,1​X1​−X0​AN,1​X2​−X1​AN,2​X0​+X0​AN,2​X1​+X0​AN,3​X0​.

That is the exact symbolic formula.

So for each value/gauge generator θℓθℓ​, you get one cubic generator matrix
Bℓ(3):=J3\val,(ℓ).
Bℓ(3)​:=J3\val,(ℓ)​.

Then
V\val(3)=span⁡{B1(3),…,Bm(3)}.
V\val(3)​=span{B1(3)​,…,Bm(3)​}.
5. Structural simplifications

You do not need to compute everything blindly. The symmetry types help a lot.

At cubic order:

    AN,1AN,1​ is typically antisymmetric,

    AN,2AN,2​ is typically symmetric/diagonal,

    AN,3AN,3​ may again have mixed symmetry, but the bb-channel we checked sits in antisymmetric off-diagonal.

On the same-point side:

    AG,1AG,1​ is symmetric,

    AG,2AG,2​ is symmetric,

    AG,3AG,3​ is symmetric.

Since LL preserves symmetry for symmetric input, all
X1, X2, X3
X1​, X2​, X3​

are symmetric.

So every cubic value/gauge generator is built out of:

    symmetric XjXj​,

    symmetric N0N0​,

    antisymmetric/symmetric AN,jAN,j​.

That means the cubic value-family is not arbitrary in M2(C)M2​(C); its image has strong symmetry restrictions. That is exactly what you later exploit to find quotient annihilators.
6. How to get the cubic quotient coordinates

Exactly as at first order:

    Vectorize each Bℓ(3)∈M2(C)Bℓ(3)​∈M2​(C) into C4C4.

    Form the matrix
    B(3)=[vec(B1(3)) ⋯ vec(Bm(3))].
    B(3)=[vec(B1(3)​) ⋯ vec(Bm(3)​)].

    Compute the left nullspace
    L∈ker⁡((B(3))⊤).
    L∈ker((B(3))⊤).

    Each basis vector Lj(3)Lj(3)​ gives a cubic quotient coordinate
    Λj(3)(X)=Lj(3)⋅vec(X).
    Λj(3)​(X)=Lj(3)​⋅vec(X).

Then for the actual anomalous cubic jet J3(p)J3​(p),
ξj(3)(p)=Λj(3)(J3(p)).
ξj(3)​(p)=Λj(3)​(J3​(p)).

Those are the explicit cubic quotient coordinates.
7. Why this matters for the Jacobian branch

Once V\val(1)V\val(1)​ and V\val(3)V\val(3)​ are explicit, you can compute:

    first quotient coordinates Ξ1Ξ1​,

    cubic quotient coordinates Ξ3Ξ3​,

    and later quintic Ξ5Ξ5​.

Then the three-jet map
F2=(Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)
F2​=(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​)

is a concrete analytic map in (a1,h1,a2,h2)(a1​,h1​,a2​,h2​), not an abstract one.

At that point:

    the Jacobian can be written,

    the resonance functions R13,R15R13​,R15​ can be written,

    the Wronskian can be written,

    and the exceptional-point rank checks become actual finite computations.

8. The bottleneck now

The true bottleneck is:
Determine the actual realized value/gauge generators (AG,1,AG,2,AG,3;AN,1,AN,2,AN,3) from the paper’s corrected value model.
Determine the actual realized value/gauge generators (AG,1​,AG,2​,AG,3​;AN,1​,AN,2​,AN,3​) from the paper’s corrected value model.​

Once those are frozen, V\val(3)V\val(3)​ is just algebra.

That is the next concrete subtask on this path.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to pin down what the realized cubic value/gauge generators must look like, even before we know their exact coefficients.

That is enough to narrow V\val(3)V\val(3)​ substantially.
1. What can actually generate cubic value jets

At cubic order, a value/gauge generator can only come from three sources:

the cubic same-point transport,
AG,3,
AG,3​,

the quadratic interaction of lower same-point jets through the inverse-square-root expansion,
X2(AG,1,AG,2)andX3(AG,1,AG,2,AG,3),
X2​(AG,1​,AG,2​)andX3​(AG,1​,AG,2​,AG,3​),

and the cubic mixed-block value terms,
AN,3,
AN,3​,

together with lower mixed terms AN,1,AN,2AN,1​,AN,2​ coupled to X1,X2X1​,X2​.

So V\val(3)V\val(3)​ is spanned by matrices of the form
J3\val=S3(AG,1,AG,2,AG,3)+M3(AN,1,AN,2,AN,3),
J3\val​=S3​(AG,1​,AG,2​,AG,3​)+M3​(AN,1​,AN,2​,AN,3​),

where S3S3​ is the same-point part and M3M3​ the mixed part.

The useful thing is that symmetry already restricts these a lot.
2. Symmetry types at cubic order

Because X1,X2,X3X1​,X2​,X3​ all come from symmetric AG,jAG,j​, they are symmetric matrices.

Because N0N0​ is symmetric, the same-point contribution
−X3N0X0+X2N0X1−X1N0X2+X0N0X3
−X3​N0​X0​+X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​+X0​N0​X3​

is always of the form
symmetric+commutator of symmetric matrices through N0.
symmetric+commutator of symmetric matrices through N0​.

That means its antisymmetric part is very restricted: it only appears through commutator-type pieces like
X2N0X1−X1N0X2.
X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​.

On the mixed side:

    AN,1AN,1​ is antisymmetric,

    AN,2AN,2​ is symmetric,

    AN,3AN,3​ can have both, but the linear bb-channel showed up antisymmetric.

So the cubic value-family can only contain:

a symmetric/diagonal sector from same-point transport and symmetric mixed terms,

and an antisymmetric sector from commutator-type and odd mixed terms.

This is good, because it suggests V\val(3)V\val(3)​ is probably not all of M2M2​, and likely low-dimensional.
3. A minimal trial spanning set for V\val(3)V\val(3)​

Without pretending we know exact coefficients, the right trial generators are:
Same-point cubic generators

Take three symmetric prototypes
S1=(1000),S2=(0110),S3=(0001).
S1​=(10​00​),S2​=(01​10​),S3​=(00​01​).

Feed each one through the cubic same-point operator
S3(Sj):=−X3(Sj)N0X0+X2(Sj)N0X1(Sj)−X1(Sj)N0X2(Sj)+X0N0X3(Sj),
S3​(Sj​):=−X3​(Sj​)N0​X0​+X2​(Sj​)N0​X1​(Sj​)−X1​(Sj​)N0​X2​(Sj​)+X0​N0​X3​(Sj​),

where X1,X2,X3X1​,X2​,X3​ are built from that symmetric generator in the recursive Sylvester scheme.
Mixed cubic generators

Take the antisymmetric prototype
J=(01−10),
J=(0−1​10​),

and the symmetric ones S1,S2,S3S1​,S2​,S3​, and form
M3(J,Sj):=−X2(Sj)JX0+X1(Sj)JX1(Sj)−X0JX2(Sj),
M3​(J,Sj​):=−X2​(Sj​)JX0​+X1​(Sj​)JX1​(Sj​)−X0​JX2​(Sj​),

plus the pure cubic mixed generator
X0AN,3X0
X0​AN,3​X0​

with AN,3AN,3​ taken over the realized value-family cubic mixed directions.

So a practical trial spanning family for V\val(3)V\val(3)​ is
{S3(S1),S3(S2),S3(S3),M3(J,S1),M3(J,S2),M3(J,S3),X0AN,3X0}.
{S3​(S1​),S3​(S2​),S3​(S3​),M3​(J,S1​),M3​(J,S2​),M3​(J,S3​),X0​AN,3​X0​}.

In practice many of these will be dependent, but this gives an explicit finite set to reduce.
4. What to do with that set

Vectorize each trial generator into C4C4, stack them as columns, and row-reduce.

That tells you two things immediately:

the actual dimension of V\val(3)V\val(3)​,

and an explicit annihilator basis for the cubic quotient.

That is exactly what we need for Ξ3Ξ3​, and later for the Jacobian and resonance functions.
5. The key payoff

Once you have an annihilator basis
L1(3),…,Ld3(3),
L1(3)​,…,Ld3​(3)​,

the cubic quotient coordinates are just
ξj(3)(p)=Lj(3)⋅vec(J3(p)).
ξj(3)​(p)=Lj(3)​⋅vec(J3​(p)).

Then the parity-split scalar functions
α3\odd(h),α3\even(h)
α3\odd​(h),α3\even​(h)

become explicit, because they are just the odd/even parts of these coordinates evaluated on the one-pair cubic jet.

That is what finally makes
R13(h), R15(h), W(h)
R13​(h), R15​(h), W(h)

actual formulas rather than formal placeholders.
6. The most important next subtask

The next concrete thing to attack is not all of V\val(3)V\val(3)​ at once.

It is:
Compute the dimension and annihilator of the trial cubic value-family span generated by S1,S2,S3,J.
Compute the dimension and annihilator of the trial cubic value-family span generated by S1​,S2​,S3​,J.​

Even if this is only a trial span, it will tell us whether the cubic quotient is plausibly one-dimensional, two-dimensional, or larger.

That in turn tells us whether the Jacobian branch has enough room to work.
7. Honest status

I still cannot produce the exact matrices S3(Sj)S3​(Sj​) and M3(J,Sj)M3​(J,Sj​) numerically because we have not frozen the actual baseline G0,N0G0​,N0​ and the realized paper-specific value-model coefficients.

But the route is now completely explicit:

    choose trial generators,

    push them through the cubic whitening recursion,

    row-reduce,

    extract annihilators.

That is the next place where the argument becomes computational instead of conceptual.

7. Why this matters immediately

Even before you know the exact paper-realized value-model coefficients, this trial computation answers two big structural questions:

That would tell us whether the Jacobian/resonance branch still looks viable.

    which is antisymmetric off-diagonal;

    for j=2j=2, X1(2)X1(2)​ is symmetric off-diagonal, X2(2)X2(2)​ is diagonal, and a direct check again gives an antisymmetric off-diagonal matrix.

So in this diagonal-baseline minimal trial model, the cubic value-family generators we have actually computed all land in the antisymmetric off-diagonal line.

That is the real result:
In the minimal diagonal-baseline trial model, the cubic value-family span collapses to the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction.
In the minimal diagonal-baseline trial model, the cubic value-family span collapses to the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction.​

This explains why our earlier cubic-detector attempts kept falling back into the same symmetry type.

It also tells us something important about strategy.

If the actual paper-realized cubic value-family behaves like this model, then the cubic quotient could still be large, because many anomalous cubic terms may survive outside this line. But it also means that trying to isolate one cubic detector by hand is fragile, because the value-family itself may already be concentrated in that same antisymmetric channel.

So the Jacobian branch still makes sense, but the next thing to do is not keep guessing cubic detectors. It is to compare the actual anomalous cubic jet against this very small trial value-family span.

In practical terms, the next exact subtask is:
Take the actual anomalous cubic jet J3(p) and test whether its vectorized form is ever confined to the antisymmetric off-diagonal line together with the first and quintic jets.
Take the actual anomalous cubic jet J3​(p) and test whether its vectorized form is ever confined to the antisymmetric off-diagonal line together with the first and quintic jets.​

That is now the sharper local test.

    V\val(1)V\val(1)​ is likely small and contains the skew direction;

    in the diagonal-baseline minimal trial model, V\val(3)V\val(3)​ also looks very small, essentially the skew direction again;

    therefore the three-jet map may still have enough quotient room to be transverse, but you now need to compare it against a specific very low-dimensional value-family model, rather than an unknown large one.

That is progress, even if it is not yet a proof.

4. The next exact local theorem

So the next theorem to attack is:
Cubic non-skew lemma

For a genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair core, the cubic jet J3(p)J3​(p) is not purely antisymmetric off-diagonal. Equivalently, at least one of
J3(p)11,J3(p)22,J3(p)12+J3(p)21
J3​(p)11​,J3​(p)22​,J3​(p)12​+J3​(p)21​

is nonzero.

If that lemma holds, then the cubic quotient is automatically nonzero in the trial model.

That would be a major win.
5. Why this is plausible

Because the actual anomalous cubic jet receives contributions from:

    same-point transport terms,

    mixed-block cubic terms,

    nonlinear whitening terms.

We already saw many value-family trial generators collapse into the skew direction. But that does not mean the anomalous jet does too.

In fact, the anomalous same-point contributions M1,M2,M3M1​,M2​,M3​ naturally generate diagonal and symmetric pieces. So unless there is an unexpected cancellation, a genuine anomaly should not produce a purely skew cubic matrix.

So this is a plausible and testable next statement.
6. A sharper matrix decomposition

Decompose any 2×22×2 matrix as
X=tr⁡X2I⏟trace+X+XT2−tr⁡X2I⏟symmetric traceless+X−XT2⏟skew.
X=trace
2trX​I​​+symmetric traceless
2X+XT​−2trX​I​​+skew
2X−XT​​​.

If the cubic value-family is really only skew, then the quotient is just the trace plus symmetric-traceless part.

So a still cleaner cubic quotient basis is:
Λ\tr(3)(X)=X11+X22,
Λ\tr(3)​(X)=X11​+X22​,
Λ\diff(3)(X)=X11−X22,
Λ\diff(3)​(X)=X11​−X22​,
Λ\sym(3)(X)=X12+X21.
Λ\sym(3)​(X)=X12​+X21​.

These three kill the skew line and split the quotient into natural symmetry sectors.

That is probably the best cubic coordinate choice to freeze next.

τ3​(p)=δ3​(p)=σ3​(p)=0 ?

5. What this means

This is a serious warning.

It means the trial model is too symmetric/commutative to generate cubic quotient information. So the nice Jacobian story with explicit cubic quotient coordinates does not close in this simplified model.

There are only two ways out:

Either the actual baseline/value model is less commutative than this trial model, so V\val(3)V\val(3)​ and the anomalous cubic jet are richer than this simplified calculation suggests.

Or cubic order is genuinely too low, and the first reliable quotient information appears at quintic order.
6. Strategic consequence

This strongly suggests:
Do not rely on cubic quotient transversality in the minimal model.
Do not rely on cubic quotient transversality in the minimal model.​

So the next serious options are:

    compute the actual V\val(3)V\val(3)​ and the actual anomalous cubic jet, not the minimal diagonal approximation, and see whether cubic quotient survives there;

    or, more robustly, shift the finite-order exclusion/Jacobian branch to
    (Ξ1,Ξ5,Ξ7)or(Ξ1,Ξ5)
    (Ξ1​,Ξ5​,Ξ7​)or(Ξ1​,Ξ5​)

    instead of (Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​).

Given what we just found, I think the second is now more plausible.

    cubic quotient can collapse completely in the minimal symmetric model;

Can we somehow consider all odd orders?

Yes. In fact, I think that is probably the right abstraction now.

The clean object is not any fixed odd order, and not even a finite list like (1,3,5)(1,3,5). It is the entire odd quotient germ.

Write the corrected local germ modulo the value/gauge family as
F\core(z)∈G\odd/V\val,
F\core​(z)∈G\odd​/V\val​,

with expansion
F\core(z)=∑r≥0Ξ2r+1\corez2r+1.
F\core​(z)=r≥0∑​Ξ2r+1\core​z2r+1.

Then the real invariant is its order of vanishing
ν(\core):=min⁡{r≥0: Ξ2r+1\core≠0},
ν(\core):=min{r≥0: Ξ2r+1\core​=0},

with the convention ν(\core)=∞ν(\core)=∞ if the quotient germ vanishes identically.

That is exactly the “consider all odd orders” version.

There are three ways to use it.

First, the soft version. Instead of proving a specific jet survives, prove that the quotient germ is not identically zero:
F\core≠0.
F\core​=0.

Then some odd order survives automatically. This is the cleanest conceptual statement, and it matches the fixed-core upgrade very well.

Second, the compactness version. On a compact normalized parameter set, try to prove that ν(\core)ν(\core) is uniformly bounded:
ν(\core)≤Rk
ν(\core)≤Rk​

for all genuine kk-cores. That is the bounded-order noncollapse theorem we kept circling around. This is stronger, because it turns “some odd order” into “some odd order among finitely many.”

Third, the generating-function version. Instead of looking at coefficients one by one, choose an odd test functional LL and consider a scalar odd germ
H\core(z):=L(F\core(z)).
H\core​(z):=L(F\core​(z)).

Then you study the whole odd function at once, and the first surviving odd coefficient is just its vanishing order at 00. This is often cleaner than juggling separate jet coordinates.

So yes, considering all odd orders is not only possible, it is probably the right endgame language.

Where it helps most is exactly where the current branch became fragile. We kept finding that:

    first jet can be too weak,

    cubic can collapse in symmetric models,

    a hand-picked detector can become value-like.

The whole odd germ avoids that. You stop betting on one special order and instead ask:
Can a genuine finite off-line core make the entire odd quotient germ vanish?
Can a genuine finite off-line core make the entire odd quotient germ vanish?​

That is the right local theorem.

If you can prove the answer is no, then RH reduces to one more step: compare the first surviving odd order of the core against the outside bound at that same order. Since the outside contribution gets smaller at higher order,
∣a2r+1\out∣≪1QH2r+3,
∣a2r+1\out​∣≪QH2r+31​,

this is actually compatible with the “all odd orders” viewpoint.

The catch is that “consider all odd orders” by itself is too weak for contradiction. To finish, you still need one of these stronger statements:

    a bounded-order theorem: the first surviving odd order is uniformly bounded on compact normalized parameter sets;

    or a quantitative noncollapse theorem: the whole quotient germ stays a positive distance from zero in some Banach norm, which would imply such a bound by compactness.

That is the real reason we kept reducing to finite-order maps. Not because all odd orders are wrong, but because RH needs a uniform finite-order witness eventually.

So my view is:
Yes — the right master object is the full odd quotient germ, and the right theorem is that it cannot vanish identically for a genuine finite core.
Yes — the right master object is the full odd quotient germ, and the right theorem is that it cannot vanish identically for a genuine finite core.​

Then the technical problem becomes:
upgrade “not identically zero” to “some uniformly bounded odd order survives on compact normalized families.”
upgrade “not identically zero” to “some uniformly bounded odd order survives on compact normalized families.”​

That is the clean global formulation of everything we have been doing.

The most natural next formal statement is:

    Odd-germ nonvanishing theorem.
    For every genuine normalized finite off-line core, the quotient odd germ F\coreF\core​ is not identically zero.

And then:

    Compact bounded-order corollary.
    On each compact normalized kk-core parameter set, there exists RkRk​ such that F\coreF\core​ has some nonzero odd coefficient among orders ≤2Rk+1≤2Rk​+1.

That is, I think, the right way to reorganize the local part of the paper now.

So how do we attack it?

Attack it in two layers.

The first layer is the right theorem to aim for. The second is the route that has the best chance of proving it.

The theorem you want is not “some specific jet is nonzero.” It is:
F\core≢0in G\odd/V\val
F\core​≡0in G\odd​/V\val​

for every genuine normalized finite off-line core.

Then, by compactness on a fixed kk-core parameter set, that upgrades to a bounded-order statement automatically.

So the attack should be:
1. Prove zero-germ exclusion, not jet nondegeneracy

Assume for contradiction that a genuine normalized finite core satisfies
F\core≡0.
F\core​≡0.

That means every odd quotient jet vanishes. So the corrected local germ lies entirely in the value/gauge family.

This is much stronger than any finite-order cancellation. It says the anomalous core is locally indistinguishable from pure value motion to all odd orders.

That is the contradiction you want to attack.
2. Use finite-dimensional analyticity of the core family

For fixed core size kk, the normalized finite core depends on finitely many real parameters. Call them p∈Pkp∈Pk​.

The map
p↦Fp(z)
p↦Fp​(z)

is an analytic family of odd germs.

So the set
Zk:={p: Fp≡0}
Zk​:={p: Fp​≡0}

is an analytic subset of finite-dimensional parameter space.

That is powerful, because it means you do not need to defeat infinitely many conditions one by one. You need to show that this analytic subset contains no genuine anomalous point.

So the first real move is:
Show that Zk is contained in the degenerate/value-like locus.
Show that Zk​ is contained in the degenerate/value-like locus.​

That is the master reduction.
3. Split parameter space into escape and compact parts

You already learned this is the right split.

If some core component escapes in scaled coordinates, its contribution vanishes in the normalized germ. So escaping components are harmless.

That means any genuine obstruction to zero-germ exclusion lives in a compact normalized parameter family.

So the attack becomes:

    prove escaping components disappear;

    reduce to compact parameter sets;

    on compact sets, use analyticity and dimension.

This is already much better than fighting all configurations at once.
4. For compact families, use minimal-counterexample order

Suppose zero-germ exclusion fails. Then among all bad compact-parameter cores, pick one with minimal core size kk, and inside that family, consider a point where the order of contact with V\valV\val​ is maximal.

This is useful because if the whole odd germ vanishes, then every finite truncation vanishes too. You can try to derive structural consequences from the lowest nontrivial interaction between components.

In practice, for a minimal bad kk, one of two things should happen:

    either the core splits into smaller independent subcores, contradicting minimality;

    or the parameters satisfy a rigid resonance system, which should force degeneracy.

So the right contradiction format is:
Assume a minimal genuine bad core with F\core≡0, and exploit minimality.
Assume a minimal genuine bad core with F\core​≡0, and exploit minimality.​
5. Use symmetry-type decomposition, not a single scalar

A big lesson from the recent computations is that one detector can fail for symmetry reasons.

So decompose the odd germ into symmetry sectors:

    trace,

    symmetric traceless,

    skew.

Then ask whether all sectors can vanish to all odd orders.

This is much stronger than any one scalar cancellation. In the trial cubic model, for example, the value-family seemed concentrated in the skew sector. That suggests the real obstruction is:

    can a genuine anomalous core force the non-skew sectors to vanish to all odd orders?

That is much less plausible.

So one concrete attack is:
Project the odd germ into symmetry sectors and try to show at least one sector must survive.
Project the odd germ into symmetry sectors and try to show at least one sector must survive.​
6. Attack with the full pair-jet curve, not isolated orders

For k=2k=2, define the full one-pair quotient odd germ curve
h↦Fh(z).
h↦Fh​(z).

Then two-pair vanishing would mean
a1Fh1(z)+a2Fh2(z)+a1a2Gh1,h2(z)+⋯≡0.
a1​Fh1​​(z)+a2​Fh2​​(z)+a1​a2​Gh1​,h2​​(z)+⋯≡0.

At leading order in amplitudes, this says the whole odd germ Fh1Fh1​​ must be negatively proportional to Fh2Fh2​​.

That is much stronger than matching Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​. It means the two pair-germs are proportional as analytic germs.

So for k=2k=2, the right attack is:
Show that h↦Fh(z) is not projectively constant as an odd germ.
Show that h↦Fh​(z) is not projectively constant as an odd germ.​

Equivalently, find two odd orders whose coefficient ratios vary with hh. That was the spirit of the R13,R15R13​,R15​ and Wronskian reductions, but now phrased at the germ level.

If the projective germ curve is nonconstant, two different hh's cannot cancel to all odd orders except on isolated degenerate loci.
7. The best technical route: analytic-subset plus transversality

So the practical strategy is:

First, define
Zk={p:Fp≡0}.
Zk​={p:Fp​≡0}.

Second, show ZkZk​ is analytic.

Third, show its tangent space cannot contain the genuine anomalous directions away from obvious degeneracies. That is the transversality part.

In other words, at a putative bad point p∗p∗​, compute the differential
DpFp∣p=p∗.
Dp​Fp​
​p=p∗​​.

If the image of that differential is not contained in V\valV\val​, then p∗∉Zkp∗​∈/Zk​ as a stable point.

So the exact local attack is:
At any putative bad point, show the parameter derivative of the odd germ leaves V\val.
At any putative bad point, show the parameter derivative of the odd germ leaves V\val​.​

That is the infinite-order analogue of the Jacobian idea.
8. For k=2k=2, the concrete plan is now clear

If you want the best shot at an actual theorem soon, do this:

Take the symmetric-locus reduction h2=−h1h2​=−h1​, but phrase it at the full-germ level. Define the resonance family
R(h,λ):=Fh(z)+λF−h(z).
R(h,λ):=Fh​(z)+λF−h​(z).

Then ask:
R(h,λ)≡0 ?
R(h,λ)≡0 ?

Attack this by proving the germ-ratio
F−h(z)/Fh(z)
F−h​(z)/Fh​(z)

cannot be constant in zz unless the configuration is degenerate.

That is much cleaner than chasing one more finite jet.
9. What I think is the best overall attack

If I had to choose one route now, I would do this:
Prove zero-germ exclusion by showing the projective odd-germ curve of a finite core is nonconstant, and then use compactness to get bounded surviving order.
Prove zero-germ exclusion by showing the projective odd-germ curve of a finite core is nonconstant, and then use compactness to get bounded surviving order.​

In practice that means:

    stop betting on cubic;

    use all odd orders conceptually;

    use finite-dimensional analyticity of the parameter family;

    and use transversality of the full odd germ, not one detector.

The most concrete next theorem to try is:

    Projective germ nonconstancy for k=2k=2.
    The one-pair quotient odd germ Fh(z)Fh​(z) is not projectively constant in hh. Hence a two-pair symmetric resonance cannot make the full quotient odd germ vanish except on isolated degenerate loci.

That is the cleanest attack I see now.
