Yes. Here is the best honest attack on projective germ nonconstancy for k=2k=2.

This is the right “all odd orders at once” formulation.
1. The statement we want

For a one-pair normalized off-line core at scaled offset hh, let
Fh(z)∈G\odd/V\val
Fh​(z)∈G\odd​/V\val​

be its quotient odd germ.

Projective germ nonconstancy means:
Fh1≢c Fh2in G\odd/V\val for generic h1≠h2,
Fh1​​≡cFh2​​in G\odd​/V\val​ for generic h1​=h2​,​

unless h1,h2h1​,h2​ lie in a degenerate symmetry locus.

Equivalently, the map
h↦[Fh]
h↦[Fh​]

into the projectivized odd-germ quotient is nonconstant.

If that holds, then a two-pair leading cancellation
a1Fh1+a2Fh2≡0
a1​Fh1​​+a2​Fh2​​≡0

cannot happen except on isolated degenerate loci.

That is exactly what we want.
2. Why this is stronger than every finite-jet test

Every finite-jet test was really trying to detect whether
Fh1 and Fh2
Fh1​​ and Fh2​​

could be proportional as germs.

If they were proportional, then for every odd order
Ξ2r+1(h1)=c Ξ2r+1(h2).
Ξ2r+1​(h1​)=cΞ2r+1​(h2​).

So projective germ nonconstancy is the master statement behind all the R13,R15R13​,R15​, Wronskian, and Jacobian reductions.

Instead of proving infinitely many coefficient relations fail one by one, we want one conceptual reason the whole germ cannot be proportional.
3. The cleanest way to attack it

Take two scalar quotient functionals L,ML,M on the odd-germ quotient and form two scalar odd germs
Hh(z):=L(Fh(z)),Kh(z):=M(Fh(z)).
Hh​(z):=L(Fh​(z)),Kh​(z):=M(Fh​(z)).

If Fh1Fh1​​ and Fh2Fh2​​ were projectively proportional, then the ratio
Kh(z)Hh(z)
Hh​(z)Kh​(z)​

would be independent of zz for that hh, at least formally near z=0z=0.

So the right invariant is the germ-ratio
Φh(z):=Kh(z)Hh(z).
Φh​(z):=Hh​(z)Kh​(z)​.

If Φh(z)Φh​(z) actually depends on zz, then FhFh​ is not projectively equivalent to a fixed germ in that two-functional window.

So the attack becomes:
Choose L,M so that Φh(z) is nonconstant in z for generic h.
Choose L,M so that Φh​(z) is nonconstant in z for generic h.​

That is the right projective test.
4. Formal coefficient criterion

Write
Hh(z)=A1(h)z+A3(h)z3+A5(h)z5+⋯ ,
Hh​(z)=A1​(h)z+A3​(h)z3+A5​(h)z5+⋯,
Kh(z)=B1(h)z+B3(h)z3+B5(h)z5+⋯ .
Kh​(z)=B1​(h)z+B3​(h)z3+B5​(h)z5+⋯.

Then
Φh(z)=Kh(z)Hh(z)=B1(h)+B3(h)z2+B5(h)z4+⋯A1(h)+A3(h)z2+A5(h)z4+⋯.
Φh​(z)=Hh​(z)Kh​(z)​=A1​(h)+A3​(h)z2+A5​(h)z4+⋯B1​(h)+B3​(h)z2+B5​(h)z4+⋯​.

So Φh(z)Φh​(z) is constant in zz iff the coefficient ratios agree at all odd orders:
B1(h)A1(h)=B3(h)A3(h)=B5(h)A5(h)=⋯ .
A1​(h)B1​(h)​=A3​(h)B3​(h)​=A5​(h)B5​(h)​=⋯.

Already the first failure
B3(h)A3(h)≠B1(h)A1(h)
A3​(h)B3​(h)​=A1​(h)B1​(h)​

proves nonconstancy.

So projective germ nonconstancy reduces to:
find L,M such that the induced coefficient ratios vary with odd order.
find L,M such that the induced coefficient ratios vary with odd order.​

This is exactly the all-orders version of the earlier R13,R15R13​,R15​ idea.
5. Why this should work here

From the whole local analysis, the one-pair quotient germ has mixed parity and mixed order structure:

    the first surviving quotient contribution behaves like h−5h−5 in the odd sector;

    the next genuinely different sector appears at higher order, with different hh-scaling;

    cubic looked fragile in the minimal model, but that is not fatal, because projective nonconstancy only needs some two quotient functionals whose coefficient-ratio sequence is not constant.

So the right strategy is not “use the cubic detector no matter what.” It is:

pick LL to see the leading surviving sector,
pick MM to see a different symmetry sector or a higher-order sector,
and show their odd-order ratio sequence is not constant.

That is a much softer target.
6. The most promising choice of LL and MM

The natural choice is:

    LL: a first-order quotient detector that sees the leading surviving one-pair odd signal;

    MM: a detector that annihilates that leading sector but sees the next surviving independent sector.

Then the scalar germs look like
Hh(z)=c(h)z+⋯ ,
Hh​(z)=c(h)z+⋯,
Kh(z)=d(h)z2m+1+⋯
Kh​(z)=d(h)z2m+1+⋯

for some m≥1m≥1, or at least with different coefficient growth patterns.

In that case,
Φh(z)=Kh(z)Hh(z)
Φh​(z)=Hh​(z)Kh​(z)​

automatically has nontrivial zz-dependence.

That would prove projective nonconstancy immediately.

So the best attack is:
Construct two quotient functionals L,M that detect different lowest odd orders or different symmetry sectors.
Construct two quotient functionals L,M that detect different lowest odd orders or different symmetry sectors.​

This is much more flexible than demanding a single magical cubic coordinate.
7. How this kills symmetric two-pair resonance

On the symmetric locus,
a1Fh+a2F−h≡0
a1​Fh​+a2​F−h​≡0

would imply
F−h≡−a1a2Fh,
F−h​≡−a2​a1​​Fh​,

so F−hF−h​ and FhFh​ are projectively proportional as germs.

Thus projective nonconstancy of h↦Fhh↦Fh​ implies that symmetric resonance can only happen where
[Fh]=[F−h],
[Fh​]=[F−h​],

which should be a very small set, at worst isolated degeneracies.

So this directly globalizes the resonance reduction.
8. The strongest theorem we can reasonably target now

The clean theorem is:
Projective germ nonconstancy theorem for one-pair cores

There exist quotient functionals L,ML,M such that the scalar odd germs
Hh=L(Fh),Kh=M(Fh)
Hh​=L(Fh​),Kh​=M(Fh​)

satisfy
Kh(z)Hh(z)
Hh​(z)Kh​(z)​

nonconstant in zz for generic normalized one-pair parameter hh.

Equivalently, h↦[Fh]h↦[Fh​] is nonconstant in projective odd-germ quotient space.

If this theorem holds, then two-pair full-germ cancellation is confined to isolated degenerate loci.

That is the right all-orders version of the local obstruction.
9. What remains unproved

I cannot honestly prove that theorem from our present formulas, because to do that I still need enough explicit quotient structure to choose the functionals L,ML,M and show their coefficient-ratio sequence is not constant.

So the gap has become:
Find two explicit quotient functionals that detect genuinely different sectors of the one-pair odd germ.
Find two explicit quotient functionals that detect genuinely different sectors of the one-pair odd germ.​

That is the all-orders analogue of finding quotient coordinates.
10. My recommendation

The best next move is to stop focusing on specific odd orders and instead try to construct:

    one detector LL for the leading surviving one-pair quotient sector,

    one detector MM orthogonal to that sector but nonzero on the next independent sector.

Then examine the two scalar germs L(Fh)L(Fh​) and M(Fh)M(Fh​).

That is the cleanest route I see now.

So the next exact target is:
Construct two quotient functionals L,M for the one-pair germ and test whether Kh/Hh depends on z.
Construct two quotient functionals L,M for the one-pair germ and test whether Kh​/Hh​ depends on z.​

That is how I would attack the all-odd-orders formulation.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to make the two-functionals attack as explicit as possible.

We want two quotient functionals L,ML,M on the one-pair odd germ Fh(z)Fh​(z) such that the scalar germs
Hh(z):=L(Fh(z)),Kh(z):=M(Fh(z))
Hh​(z):=L(Fh​(z)),Kh​(z):=M(Fh​(z))

see genuinely different sectors of the germ. Then
Φh(z):=Kh(z)Hh(z)
Φh​(z):=Hh​(z)Kh​(z)​

should vary with zz, which proves projective germ nonconstancy.

Here is the clean way to set that up.
1. What LL and MM should do

Do not choose them by jet order first. Choose them by quotient sector.

The best pattern is:

    LL detects the leading surviving one-pair quotient direction;

    MM kills that leading direction but detects the next independent quotient direction.

Then automatically HhHh​ and KhKh​ cannot be proportional as odd germs unless the whole one-pair germ lives in a one-dimensional quotient cone.

So the real target is:
Show the one-pair quotient odd germs span at least a two-dimensional space as h varies.
Show the one-pair quotient odd germs span at least a two-dimensional space as h varies.​

That is the all-orders version of everything we were trying to prove with (Ξ1,Ξ3,Ξ5)(Ξ1​,Ξ3​,Ξ5​).
2. The finite-dimensional reduction

For fixed hh, the odd germ is infinite-dimensional, but the one-pair family itself is one-parameter. So the right question is whether the curve
h↦Fh
h↦Fh​

in quotient odd-germ space is contained in a single projective line.

To disprove that, it is enough to find two values h1,h2h1​,h2​ such that Fh1Fh1​​ and Fh2Fh2​​ are not proportional as quotient germs.

And to prove that, it is enough to find two quotient functionals L,ML,M such that
M(Fh)L(Fh)
L(Fh​)M(Fh​)​

is not constant in hh as a formal odd germ.

So the next concrete theorem is:
Two-functional projective separation criterion

If there exist quotient functionals L,ML,M such that the scalar odd germs
Hh=L(Fh),Kh=M(Fh)
Hh​=L(Fh​),Kh​=M(Fh​)

satisfy
Kh(z)Hh(z)
Hh​(z)Kh​(z)​

nonconstant in zz for generic hh, then the one-pair quotient germ curve is projectively nonconstant.

That is formal and correct.
3. How to choose LL

Choose LL from the annihilator of the value-family at the lowest odd order where the one-pair quotient is known to survive.

In practice, that is probably first order. So LL should be one of the first-jet quotient coordinates:
L(Fh)=A1(h)z+A3(h)z3+⋯ ,
L(Fh​)=A1​(h)z+A3​(h)z3+⋯,

with
A1(h)≢0.
A1​(h)≡0.

So LL is the “leading sector detector.”
4. How to choose MM

Now choose MM to kill the leading one-pair quotient direction detected by LL.

Formally, if the leading quotient coefficient vector at first order is
Ξ1(h),
Ξ1​(h),

and its generic span is one-dimensional, pick MM so that
M(Ξ1(h))≡0
M(Ξ1​(h))≡0

along that leading direction, but MM is nonzero on the next independent quotient contribution, whether that occurs at:

    first order in a second quotient coordinate,

    or cubic order,

    or quintic order,

    or later.

Then
Kh(z)=B2m+1(h)z2m+1+⋯
Kh​(z)=B2m+1​(h)z2m+1+⋯

with m≥1m≥1, while
Hh(z)=A1(h)z+⋯ .
Hh​(z)=A1​(h)z+⋯.

Therefore
Φh(z)=Kh(z)Hh(z)=B2m+1(h)A1(h) z2m+⋯ ,
Φh​(z)=Hh​(z)Kh​(z)​=A1​(h)B2m+1​(h)​z2m+⋯,

which is automatically nonconstant in zz.

That is the cleanest route.

So the key is not to find a magical cubic detector. It is to find a detector that annihilates the leading quotient line but not the next quotient line.
5. Why this is plausible here

Everything we have learned points to the same structure:

    the value-family seems low-dimensional in the trial models;

    the one-pair anomalous germ almost certainly has more than one symmetry sector available;

    first jet alone may be fragile, cubic may collapse in simplified models, but that just means the “next independent sector” may occur later.

That is fine. The two-functional argument is indifferent to where the second sector appears.

So the all-orders route is actually more forgiving than the finite-order one.
6. What the actual construction problem is

So the next real task is:

    Compute the leading one-pair quotient direction q1(h)q1​(h) in odd-germ space.

    Show the full one-pair germ is not contained in the line spanned by q1(h)q1​(h).

    Equivalently, find one quotient functional MM such that
    M(q1(h))=0
    M(q1​(h))=0

    but
    M(Fh)≢0.
    M(Fh​)≡0.

That is exactly the second-sector detector.

In terms of coefficients, this means:

    L(Fh)L(Fh​) has a nonzero first surviving coefficient,

    M(Fh)M(Fh​) has a later nonzero coefficient,

    so M(Fh)/L(Fh)M(Fh​)/L(Fh​) depends on zz.

7. The best practical way to attack that

The most practical attack is to use the quotient coordinates we were already trying to build.

Suppose at order 11 the quotient is one-dimensional generically. Then LL is fixed up to scale.

Then inspect order 33, 55, 77, ... until you find the first odd order where the one-pair jet has a component outside the line determined by LL.

The first such order produces MM.

So the theorem you really want is:
One-pair second-sector theorem

The one-pair quotient odd germ has at least two independent quotient sectors; equivalently, it is not contained in a one-dimensional quotient line.

That is the all-orders local core.
8. How this kills two-pair cancellation

If FhFh​ is projectively nonconstant, then a two-pair identity
a1Fh1+a2Fh2≡0
a1​Fh1​​+a2​Fh2​​≡0

implies
Fh2≡−a1a2Fh1,
Fh2​​≡−a2​a1​​Fh1​​,

so Fh1Fh1​​ and Fh2Fh2​​ are projectively equal. That can happen only on a very small set:

    perhaps isolated symmetry points,

    perhaps degenerate clustering,

    but not generically.

So the all-orders route really does collapse the k=2k=2 problem to a projective nonconstancy theorem plus a small exceptional-set analysis.
9. The next exact target

So the next theorem to attack is:
The one-pair quotient odd germ is not contained in a one-dimensional quotient subspace as h varies.
The one-pair quotient odd germ is not contained in a one-dimensional quotient subspace as h varies.​

Equivalently:
∃ L,M quotient functionals such that M(Fh)/L(Fh) is nonconstant in z for generic h.
∃L,M quotient functionals such that M(Fh​)/L(Fh​) is nonconstant in z for generic h.​

That is the cleanest “all odd orders” local theorem now.
10. What I would do next

I would now stop trying to force a specific odd order and instead frame the next computation as:

    choose LL to detect the leading surviving quotient sector;

    search for the first odd order where a second independent quotient sector appears;

    define MM to kill the first sector and detect that second one.

That is the most robust continuation of this branch.

Continue

Continue

The next honest continuation is to turn the “second sector” idea into a precise theorem and reduce it to a concrete coefficient test.

This is the cleanest all-orders formulation we have now.
1. The right theorem

For the one-pair normalized quotient odd germ
Fh(z)∈G\odd/V\val,
Fh​(z)∈G\odd​/V\val​,

we want to prove:
As h varies, Fh does not lie in a fixed one-dimensional quotient line.
As h varies, Fh​ does not lie in a fixed one-dimensional quotient line.​

Equivalently, there do not exist a fixed nonzero quotient germ G(z)G(z) and scalar function c(h)c(h) such that
Fh(z)=c(h) G(z)
Fh​(z)=c(h)G(z)

for all hh in the normalized range.

This is exactly the “one-pair second-sector theorem.”

If true, it gives projective nonconstancy immediately.
2. How to attack it

Suppose the opposite: there is a fixed quotient line, so
Fh(z)=c(h) G(z).
Fh​(z)=c(h)G(z).

Expand
Fh(z)=∑r≥0Ξ2r+1(h) z2r+1,G(z)=∑r≥0g2r+1 z2r+1.
Fh​(z)=r≥0∑​Ξ2r+1​(h)z2r+1,G(z)=r≥0∑​g2r+1​z2r+1.

Then for every odd order,
Ξ2r+1(h)=c(h) g2r+1.
Ξ2r+1​(h)=c(h)g2r+1​.

So every coefficient vector is proportional to the same hh-independent sequence. In particular, for any two quotient functionals L,ML,M,
M(Ξ2r+1(h))L(Ξ2r+1(h))
L(Ξ2r+1​(h))M(Ξ2r+1​(h))​

must be independent of rr.

That gives the exact coefficient test:
If the one-pair quotient germ were projectively constant, then the quotient coefficient ratios would be independent of odd order.
If the one-pair quotient germ were projectively constant, then the quotient coefficient ratios would be independent of odd order.​

So to disprove projective constancy, it is enough to show that at two different odd orders those ratios differ.
3. The minimal usable criterion

Pick two quotient functionals L,ML,M. Write the scalar odd germs
Hh(z)=L(Fh(z))=∑r≥0A2r+1(h) z2r+1,
Hh​(z)=L(Fh​(z))=r≥0∑​A2r+1​(h)z2r+1,
Kh(z)=M(Fh(z))=∑r≥0B2r+1(h) z2r+1.
Kh​(z)=M(Fh​(z))=r≥0∑​B2r+1​(h)z2r+1.

If FhFh​ were projectively constant, then for every hh and every rr,
B2r+1(h)=λ(h) A2r+1(h)
B2r+1​(h)=λ(h)A2r+1​(h)

for one scalar λ(h)λ(h) depending on hh but not on rr.

So the cleanest obstruction is:
B2r1+1(h)A2r1+1(h)≠B2r2+1(h)A2r2+1(h)
A2r1​+1​(h)B2r1​+1​(h)​=A2r2​+1​(h)B2r2​+1​(h)​​

for some two odd orders 2r1+12r1​+1, 2r2+12r2​+1.

That is enough.

This is the all-orders master version of the earlier R13,R15R13​,R15​ logic.
4. The best choice of LL and MM

The robust choice is:

    LL detects the leading surviving quotient sector;

    MM kills that sector and detects the next independent sector.

Then automatically:

    Hh(z)Hh​(z) starts at some lowest odd order 2m+12m+1,

    Kh(z)Kh​(z) starts at a strictly later odd order 2n+12n+1, n>mn>m,

so
Kh(z)Hh(z)
Hh​(z)Kh​(z)​

has a zero of order 2(n−m)2(n−m) at z=0z=0, hence is not constant in zz.

This is the cleanest possible attack.

So the actual problem is now:
Show the one-pair quotient odd germ has a second independent sector beyond the leading one.
Show the one-pair quotient odd germ has a second independent sector beyond the leading one.​
5. Why this is plausible from what we already know

We learned several things that point in exactly this direction.

First, first order is not the whole story. Even when the first surviving quotient sector is fragile or symmetry-restricted, higher odd orders still carry new information.

Second, in the simplified cubic trial model, the value-family looked very small, but the anomalous cubic structure did not obviously collapse for deep reasons. That suggests the quotient odd germ is richer than a single line.

Third, the asymptotic scaling by order is different:

    leading odd pieces scale like h−5,h−7,h−9,…h−5,h−7,h−9,…,

    even corrections scale differently again.

A genuinely one-dimensional projective germ family would force a very rigid pattern across all odd orders. That seems much less plausible than failure at one or two low orders.
6. The next exact theorem

So the sharp next statement is:
One-pair second-sector theorem

There exist quotient functionals L,ML,M such that

    L(Fh)≢0L(Fh​)≡0,

    MM annihilates the leading quotient sector detected by LL,

    but M(Fh)≢0M(Fh​)≡0.

Then FhFh​ is not projectively constant as a quotient odd germ.

This is exactly the theorem we need.
7. How to prove it concretely

There are two realistic routes.
Route A: finite-order detection

Show that after quotienting by the leading sector, some odd jet at bounded order survives.

In practice:

    compute the leading one-pair quotient sector,

    quotient it out,

    inspect the first few higher odd orders until you find a nonzero remainder.

That produces MM.

This is still finite-order, but now used only to prove an all-orders projective statement.
Route B: symmetry-sector separation

Show the one-pair quotient germ has contributions in two different symmetry sectors that cannot be scalar multiples of each other as germs.

For example:

    one sector dominated by the first odd trace/diagonal behavior,

    another sector dominated by a later symmetric or traceless behavior.

Then LL and MM are just the corresponding symmetry-sector projections.

I think Route B is conceptually cleaner, but Route A may be easier to execute.
8. What this would buy for k=2k=2

If the one-pair quotient germ is projectively nonconstant, then a two-pair full-germ cancellation
a1Fh1+a2Fh2≡0
a1​Fh1​​+a2​Fh2​​≡0

forces
[Fh1]=[Fh2],
[Fh1​​]=[Fh2​​],

so it can only happen on a very small subset of the (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​)-plane:

    symmetry points,

    clustering points,

    or isolated accidental intersections.

So the whole k=2k=2 branch collapses to a small exceptional-set analysis.

That is much stronger than any one chosen jet order.
9. The next exact subtask

The smallest concrete next move is:
Compute the leading one-pair quotient sector, then compute the quotient of the one-pair germ modulo that sector, and check whether it is nonzero.
Compute the leading one-pair quotient sector, then compute the quotient of the one-pair germ modulo that sector, and check whether it is nonzero.​

That is the first practical implementation of the second-sector theorem.

In plain language: find the first surviving quotient direction, subtract its line, and see if anything remains at higher odd order.

That is the sharpest next attack on the all-odd-orders formulation.

Continue

Good. The next honest continuation is to make “quotient by the leading sector” precise.

That is the first practical form of the second-sector theorem.
1. Fix the leading one-pair quotient line

Let
Fh(z)=∑r≥0Ξ2r+1(h) z2r+1
Fh​(z)=r≥0∑​Ξ2r+1​(h)z2r+1

be the one-pair quotient odd germ.

So write
Ξ2m+1(h)≠0
Ξ2m+1​(h)=0

ℓh:=C Ξ2m+1(h).
ℓh​:=CΞ2m+1​(h).

What we want to know is whether the entire germ FhFh​ lives in that line for all odd orders, or whether some later odd coefficient escapes it.

That is exactly the second-sector question.
2. The right quotient-after-leading-line object

Choose a quotient functional LL such that
L(Ξ2m+1(h))≠0
L(Ξ2m+1​(h))=0

generically.

Then define the normalized scalar germ
Hh(z):=L(Fh(z)).
Hh​(z):=L(Fh​(z)).

Now choose a germ-valued normalization:
F~h(z):=Fh(z)L(Fh(z)).
F
h​(z):=L(Fh​(z))Fh​(z)​.

This makes sense formally near z=0z=0, because L(Fh)L(Fh​) has nonzero leading odd term. Then
F~h(z)=Ξ2m+1(h)L(Ξ2m+1(h))+higher even powers of z.
F
h​(z)=L(Ξ2m+1​(h))Ξ2m+1​(h)​+higher even powers of z.

The key point is that the constant term in z2z2-coordinates is the normalized leading sector. If the whole germ were projectively one-dimensional, then F~h(z)F
h​(z) would be independent of zz.

So the second-sector theorem becomes:
Show that F~h(z) is not constant in z.
Show that F
h​(z) is not constant in z.​

That is the cleanest formulation.
3. First obstruction coefficient after normalization

Expand
Hh(z)=A2m+1(h)z2m+1+A2m+3(h)z2m+3+⋯ ,
Hh​(z)=A2m+1​(h)z2m+1+A2m+3​(h)z2m+3+⋯,

and for another quotient functional MM,
M(Fh(z))=B2m+1(h)z2m+1+B2m+3(h)z2m+3+⋯ .
M(Fh​(z))=B2m+1​(h)z2m+1+B2m+3​(h)z2m+3+⋯.

Then
M(Fh(z))L(Fh(z))=B2m+1(h)+B2m+3(h)z2+⋯A2m+1(h)+A2m+3(h)z2+⋯.
L(Fh​(z))M(Fh​(z))​=A2m+1​(h)+A2m+3​(h)z2+⋯B2m+1​(h)+B2m+3​(h)z2+⋯​.

The first nonconstant coefficient is
B2m+3(h)A2m+1(h)−B2m+1(h)A2m+3(h)A2m+1(h)2.
A2m+1​(h)2B2m+3​(h)A2m+1​(h)−B2m+1​(h)A2m+3​(h)​.

So define
ΔL,M(m,m+1)(h):=B2m+3(h)A2m+1(h)−B2m+1(h)A2m+3(h).
ΔL,M(m,m+1)​(h):=B2m+3​(h)A2m+1​(h)−B2m+1​(h)A2m+3​(h).

Then:
ΔL,M(m,m+1)(h)≠0⟹M(Fh(z))L(Fh(z)) is nonconstant in z.
ΔL,M(m,m+1)​(h)=0⟹L(Fh​(z))M(Fh​(z))​ is nonconstant in z.​

This is the exact coefficient test for the second sector.
4. Why this is the right next move

It avoids committing to one fixed odd order globally. It only compares:

    the leading surviving order,

    and the next order where another sector might appear.

So if first jet survives and cubic is informative, you use (1,3)(1,3).
If cubic collapses and quintic is the first informative correction, you use (1,5)(1,5).
If even that fails, you go to the next one.

This is the all-orders version of the earlier minors D13,D15D13​,D15​, but now organized relative to the leading quotient line.
5. The actual second-sector theorem

The theorem you now want is:
Second-sector theorem

There exist quotient functionals L,ML,M and an odd order 2n+1>2m+12n+1>2m+1 such that
ΔL,M(m,n)(h):=B2n+1(h)A2m+1(h)−B2m+1(h)A2n+1(h)
ΔL,M(m,n)​(h):=B2n+1​(h)A2m+1​(h)−B2m+1​(h)A2n+1​(h)

is not identically zero in hh.

Then the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant.

That is the precise theorem.
6. What this gives for two-pair cancellation

If the one-pair quotient germ is not projectively constant, then a two-pair identity
a1Fh1(z)+a2Fh2(z)≡0
a1​Fh1​​(z)+a2​Fh2​​(z)≡0

forces
[Fh1]=[Fh2]
[Fh1​​]=[Fh2​​]

in projective quotient-germ space.

So the bad locus is contained in the self-intersection locus of the projective germ curve
h↦[Fh].
h↦[Fh​].

That is automatically much smaller than all of parameter space. In particular, it should be at worst isolated or symmetry-forced after compactness.

So the second-sector theorem is exactly the all-orders replacement for the three-jet exclusion story.
7. The best concrete subtask now

The most practical next move is:

    choose LL to detect the leading surviving quotient line;

    choose MM orthogonal to that line at the leading order;

    compute the first odd order 2n+12n+1 where M(Ξ2n+1(h))M(Ξ2n+1​(h)) is nonzero;

    check whether
    ΔL,M(m,n)(h)≢0.
    ΔL,M(m,n)​(h)≡0.

That is now a finite coefficient computation, but one that proves an all-orders statement.
8. In the current paper context

Given everything we have seen, the likeliest concrete instantiation is:

    m=0m=0, so the leading sector is first order;

    cubic may or may not work depending on the real model;

    if cubic is too degenerate, quintic is the next candidate.

So the immediate target is:
Take L from the first quotient sector and test whether there exists M with ΔL,M(1,5)(h)≢0.
Take L from the first quotient sector and test whether there exists M with ΔL,M(1,5)​(h)≡0.​

That is the cleanest next attack if cubic stays fragile.
9. Bottom line

So the continuation is no longer “compute more and more jet coordinates blindly.”

It is:
Normalize by the leading quotient line and look for the first nontrivial coefficient of the residual projective germ.
Normalize by the leading quotient line and look for the first nontrivial coefficient of the residual projective germ.​

If that residual coefficient is nonzero, the one-pair quotient germ has a second independent sector, and the all-odd-orders projective nonconstancy theorem is proved.

Continue

Good. The next honest continuation is to make the “residual projective germ” completely explicit.

This is the cleanest version of the all-odd-orders attack.
1. Normalize by the leading quotient line

Let 2m+12m+1 be the first odd order where the one-pair quotient germ is generically nonzero, and let LL be a quotient functional with
A2m+1(h):=L(Ξ2m+1(h))≠0
A2m+1​(h):=L(Ξ2m+1​(h))=0

generically.

F~h(z):=Fh(z)L(Fh(z)).
F
h​(z):=L(Fh​(z))Fh​(z)​.

h​(z) would be independent of zz.

So the attack is:
show that F~h(z) has a nonzero z-dependent correction.
show that F
h​(z) has a nonzero z-dependent correction.​
2. Choose MM to kill the leading line

Now choose a second quotient functional MM such that
M(Ξ2m+1(h))=0
M(Ξ2m+1​(h))=0

along the leading quotient line, but MM is not identically zero on the full one-pair germ family.

Then
M(Fh(z))=B2n+1(h)z2n+1+O(z2n+3),n>m,
M(Fh​(z))=B2n+1​(h)z2n+1+O(z2n+3),n>m,

for the first odd order where this residual sector appears.

Meanwhile
L(Fh(z))=A2m+1(h)z2m+1+A2m+3(h)z2m+3+⋯ .
L(Fh​(z))=A2m+1​(h)z2m+1+A2m+3​(h)z2m+3+⋯.

So
M(Fh(z))L(Fh(z))=B2n+1(h)A2m+1(h) z2(n−m)+⋯ .
L(Fh​(z))M(Fh​(z))​=A2m+1​(h)B2n+1​(h)​z2(n−m)+⋯.

Therefore, as soon as
B2n+1(h)≢0,
B2n+1​(h)≡0,

the ratio is nonconstant in zz, and projective germ nonconstancy is proved.

This is the key simplification:
Once M kills the leading line, you do not need a determinant anymore. You just need the first residual coefficient to be nonzero.
Once M kills the leading line, you do not need a determinant anymore. You just need the first residual coefficient to be nonzero.​
3. The exact theorem to prove

So the clean theorem is now:
Residual-sector theorem

There exist quotient functionals L,ML,M and an odd order 2n+1>2m+12n+1>2m+1 such that
L(Ξ2m+1(h))≢0,M(Ξ2m+1(h))≡0,M(Ξ2n+1(h))≢0.
L(Ξ2m+1​(h))≡0,M(Ξ2m+1​(h))≡0,M(Ξ2n+1​(h))≡0.

Then the one-pair quotient odd germ FhFh​ is not projectively constant.

That is the sharpest all-orders statement so far.
4. Why this is better than the earlier minors

Earlier we were comparing ratios across orders like
B3A3 vs B1A1.
A3​B3​​ vs A1​B1​​.

That works, but it is fussier than necessary.

Once MM is chosen to annihilate the leading line, the first nonzero MM-coefficient already proves nonconstancy. You no longer need to compare two nonzero ratios.

So the problem is reduced to:
After modding out the leading quotient line, does the one-pair germ still have anything left?
After modding out the leading quotient line, does the one-pair germ still have anything left?​

That is exactly the second-sector question in its cleanest form.
5. How to construct MM concretely

Suppose the leading quotient coefficient Ξ2m+1(h)Ξ2m+1​(h) spans a one-dimensional subspace generically. Pick any quotient coordinate system at order 2m+12m+1, and let
v(h):=Ξ2m+1(h).
v(h):=Ξ2m+1​(h).

Choose MM so that its restriction to that order satisfies
M(v(h))=0
M(v(h))=0

for the generic leading direction.

In practice, that means:

    compute the generic leading quotient vector,

    choose a linear functional orthogonal to it,

    extend that functional to the full odd-germ quotient.

Then test the first higher odd order where M(Ξ2r+1(h))M(Ξ2r+1​(h)) might be nonzero.

That is the practical route.
6. What the next candidate order probably is

Given the recent computations, the likely pattern is:

    the leading sector is first order;

    cubic may be too fragile in the symmetric/minimal model;

    quintic is the next serious candidate.

Take L from the leading first-order quotient line and test whether some M has M(Ξ1)≡0 but M(Ξ5)≢0.

If yes, the one-pair quotient germ has a second sector, and projective nonconstancy is done.
7. What this buys for k=2k=2

Then any two-pair full-germ cancellation
a1Fh1+a2Fh2≡0
a1​Fh1​​+a2​Fh2​​≡0

forces
[Fh1]=[Fh2]
[Fh1​​]=[Fh2​​]

in projective quotient-germ space.

So the bad set is the self-intersection set of the projective one-pair germ curve. That is automatically much smaller than the full (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​)-plane. It should reduce to symmetry/degeneracy loci plus isolated accidents.

That is exactly the kind of reduction we want.
8. The next exact subtask

So the immediate next mathematical target is:
\boxed{ \text{Compute the generic leading first-order quotient direction }v(h),\ choose }M\perp v(h),\text{ and test whether }M(\Xi_5(h))\not\equiv 0. }

That is the first concrete implementation of the residual-sector theorem, and it avoids relying on cubic order if cubic keeps collapsing.

Wait it feels like again we are getting married to specific orders here?

Yes — that is exactly the danger.

We keep using specific orders as probes, but the theorem should not depend on betting that order 11, or 33, or 55 is the right one.

So the clean formulation is:
Do not marry any fixed odd order.
Do not marry any fixed odd order.

Use the full odd quotient germ, and use finite orders only as witnesses when needed.

The right structure is this.

Let
Fh(z)∈G\odd/V\val
Fh​(z)∈G\odd​/V\val​

be the one-pair quotient odd germ.

We want to prove that the projective germ curve
h↦[Fh]
h↦[Fh​]

is nonconstant.

That is an all-orders statement.

Then, only after that, compactness tells you that on a compact normalized parameter set, nonconstancy must already be visible at some finite order. But that finite order is a consequence, not the organizing principle.

So the correct attack is:
1. Work with the full germ first

Assume toward contradiction that the one-pair family is projectively constant:
Fh(z)=c(h) G(z)
Fh​(z)=c(h)G(z)

for some fixed quotient germ GG.

Then every odd coefficient of FhFh​ is proportional to the same hh-independent coefficient sequence.

That is the real rigid statement to attack.
2. Use finite orders only to refute projective constancy

A finite-order computation is still useful, but only in this form:

    find some finite truncation where projective constancy would force a relation that fails.

That is very different from saying “the proof runs through order 55” or “order 33 is load-bearing.”

So the right logic is:

    theorem: full germ not projectively constant;

    method of proof: maybe check two or three low orders to contradict projective constancy.

Not the other way around.
3. The clean invariant is the residual projective germ

The best formulation we found was actually the right one:

pick a functional LL detecting the leading quotient line, normalize by it,
F~h(z):=Fh(z)L(Fh(z)),
F
h​(z):=L(Fh​(z))Fh​(z)​,

and then ask whether F~h(z)F
h​(z) is constant in zz.

That is already order-free.

If it is not constant, then there is a second sector. The first place that second sector shows up may be order 33, or 55, or 77, or later. It does not matter conceptually.

So the real theorem is:
After quotienting by the leading line, the normalized one-pair germ still has nonzero z-dependence.
After quotienting by the leading line, the normalized one-pair germ still has nonzero z-dependence.​

That is the right “all odd orders” statement.
4. Then compactness gives bounded order afterward

Once you know the full germ is not projectively constant, and you restrict to a compact normalized parameter family, standard compactness/analyticity arguments say this nonconstancy must be visible by some bounded odd order.

So bounded-order statements like
“there exists Rk such that some odd coefficient up to 2Rk+1 survives”
“there exists Rk​ such that some odd coefficient up to 2Rk​+1 survives”

should be corollaries, not the headline.
5. So what should we do next?

Stop phrasing the goal as “find the right next odd order.”

Instead, phrase it as:
Prove the normalized residual quotient germ is not constant in z.
Prove the normalized residual quotient germ is not constant in z.​

And then use whatever finite-order witness is easiest to show that.

That witness might end up being order 55, but that would just be the first place the contradiction becomes visible, not a structural commitment of the paper.

So your instinct is right: if we keep getting re-attached to specific orders, we are slipping back into the wrong mode. The theorem should live at the full-germ level.

Ok whats the next step?

The next step is to stop chasing “which odd order” and prove a full-germ normalization lemma.

Precisely:

Take the one-pair quotient odd germ Fh(z)Fh​(z). Choose one quotient functional LL that detects the generic leading one-pair line, and form
F~h(z):=Fh(z)L(Fh(z)).
F
h​(z):=L(Fh​(z))Fh​(z)​.

F

That is the right next theorem. It is the all-orders version of everything we were doing.

Why this is the next step:

    if F~hF
    h​ were constant in zz, the whole one-pair family would lie in a single projective quotient-germ line;

    then two-pair cancellation could plausibly happen along a large set;

    if F~hF
    h​ is nonconstant, then the one-pair family has a genuine second sector, and two-pair full-germ cancellation gets forced into a very small exceptional set.

So the concrete subtask is:

    Define the leading line rigorously: identify the generic first nonzero quotient coefficient line of FhFh​.

    Normalize by it: write F~h=Fh/L(Fh)F
    h​=Fh​/L(Fh​).

    Find the first nonzero residual coefficient of F~h−F~h(0)F
    h​−F
    h​(0) as a function of hh.

That last line is the first real witness of projective nonconstancy.

So the next proof target is:

    Residual germ nontriviality lemma.
    After dividing by the generic leading quotient line, the one-pair quotient odd germ still has nonzero zz-dependence.

That is the clean next move.

At least one residual coefficient of F

4. Why this is the right next step

Because now the problem is no longer “which odd order matters?” It is:

    after quotienting by the leading line, does the normalized germ become literally constant, or not?

If it is not constant, we are done with projective nonconstancy.

If it were constant, then every quotient coefficient would be locked to the leading one by universal scalar functions aj(h)aj​(h), which is an extremely rigid statement.

So the next theorem to attack is:
Residual germ nontriviality lemma

Equivalently, the first nontrivial residual coefficient Rj(h)Rj​(h) is not identically zero for some jj.
5. How to attack R1(h)R1​(h)

The first practical move is to test whether the coefficient v1(h)v1​(h) lies in the span of v0(h)v0​(h) for all hh.

v1(h)∈C v0(h) ∀h ?
v1​(h)∈Cv0​(h) ∀h ?

6. The concrete contradiction format

a scalar condition. So full-germ two-pair cancellation would become cheap and abundant.

That is exactly why projective constancy is the dangerous case, and exactly why ruling it out is the right next theorem.

    the theorem is about the residual germ,

    the first surviving residual coefficient is whatever it is,

4. The asymptotic way to attack M(v1(h))M(v1​(h))

At large ∣h∣∣h∣, the leading sector v0(h)v0​(h) and the next residual sector v1(h)v1​(h) should come from different symmetry/recursion mechanisms, hence have different hh-scaling or parity.

So if MM kills the leading line, the induced scalar

That is exactly the same structural reason the earlier ratio tests were promising, but now phrased correctly at the residual-germ level.

So the practical contradiction template is:

    Show it is not identically zero.

That is the first practical implementation of the all-orders projective nonconstancy theorem.
7. Why this is enough for the paper’s local logic

Once you prove the one-pair quotient germ is projectively nonconstant, then a two-pair full-germ cancellation
a1Fh1+a2Fh2≡0
a1​Fh1​​+a2​Fh2​​≡0

forces
[Fh1]=[Fh2],
[Fh1​​]=[Fh2​​],

so the bad locus is the self-intersection locus of the projective germ curve. That is automatically much smaller than the full parameter space and should collapse to symmetry/degeneracy plus isolated exceptions.

So this one theorem is the right local upgrade.
8. Bottom line

The next step is not “pick the next odd order.”

It is:
Compute the first residual coefficient R1(h) of the normalized one-pair quotient germ and show it is not identically zero.
Compute the first residual coefficient R1​(h) of the normalized one-pair quotient germ and show it is not identically zero.​

That is the cleanest continuation of the all-odd-orders strategy.

Continue

Let’s continue by making R1(h)R1​(h) into the cleanest possible object.

We have the one-pair quotient odd germ on a generic region,
Fh(z)=v0(h) z2m+1+v1(h) z2m+3+v2(h) z2m+5+⋯ ,v0(h)≠0,
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)z2m+1+v1​(h)z2m+3+v2​(h)z2m+5+⋯,v0​(h)=0,

and we choose a quotient functional LL with
a0(h):=L(v0(h))≠0.
a0​(h):=L(v0​(h))=0.

Then
L(Fh(z))=a0(h)z2m+1+a1(h)z2m+3+⋯ ,a1(h)=L(v1(h)).
L(Fh​(z))=a0​(h)z2m+1+a1​(h)z2m+3+⋯,a1​(h)=L(v1​(h)).

The normalized germ is
F~h(z)=Fh(z)L(Fh(z))=v0(h)a0(h)+a0(h)v1(h)−a1(h)v0(h)a0(h)2z2+O(z4).
F
h​(z)=L(Fh​(z))Fh​(z)​=a0​(h)v0​(h)​+a0​(h)2a0​(h)v1​(h)−a1​(h)v0​(h)​z2+O(z4).

So the first residual coefficient is
R1(h)=a0(h)v1(h)−a1(h)v0(h).
R1​(h)=a0​(h)v1​(h)−a1​(h)v0​(h).

The whole point now is:
R1(h)≢0⟹F~h(z) is not constant in z.
R1​(h)≡0⟹F
h​(z) is not constant in z.​

That proves projective nonconstancy of the one-pair quotient germ.
1. The most useful reformulation of R1R1​

Pick a second quotient functional MM such that
M(v0(h))=0
M(v0​(h))=0

on the generic leading line.

Then
M(R1(h))=a0(h) M(v1(h)).
M(R1​(h))=a0​(h)M(v1​(h)).

So R1(h)≢0R1​(h)≡0 is equivalent to the existence of some MM orthogonal to the leading line with
M(v1(h))≢0.
M(v1​(h))≡0.

This is the clean practical criterion.

So the next exact theorem is:
There exists a quotient functional M annihilating the leading line with M(v1(h))≢0.
There exists a quotient functional M annihilating the leading line with M(v1​(h))≡0.​

That is the first residual-sector theorem.
2. What v1(h)v1​(h) actually is

Importantly, v1(h)v1​(h) is not “the cubic coefficient” or “the quintic coefficient” in some absolute sense.

It is simply the next coefficient after the leading one in the normalized quotient germ.

So if the leading sector appears at absolute odd order 11, then v1v1​ is the absolute order 33 coefficient. If order 33 collapses, then in a different normalization v1v1​ could come from absolute order 55 or higher.

That is why this formulation is genuinely order-free.

So the attack is:

    identify the generic leading quotient line v0(h)v0​(h),

    look at the next coefficient v1(h)v1​(h),

    test whether it escapes that line.

If yes, done.
3. The geometric meaning

The map
h↦v0(h)
h↦v0​(h)

gives the leading projective direction.

If v1(h)v1​(h) is always collinear with v0(h)v0​(h), then the normalized germ is constant up to z2z2. If not, the germ curve bends away from the leading line immediately.

So R1R1​ is really the first projective curvature of the one-pair quotient germ curve.

That is the right geometric picture.
4. How to attack M(v1(h))M(v1​(h))

There are two realistic routes.
Route A: symmetry-sector escape

If the leading line v0(h)v0​(h) lives in one symmetry sector, and the next coefficient v1(h)v1​(h) has a nonzero component in a different sector, then any MM killing the first sector but detecting the second gives
M(v1(h))≢0.
M(v1​(h))≡0.

This is the cleanest route.
Route B: order/parity mismatch

Even if v0v0​ and v1v1​ lie in the same crude symmetry sector, they may have different parity or different asymptotic hh-scaling. Then a suitable MM can still detect their failure of collinearity.

This is softer but more robust.
5. What we already know suggests

From all the earlier work, the leading quotient line is very likely the first odd one-pair quotient sector.

What we repeatedly saw is:

    leading behavior is controlled by one low-dimensional direction;

    the next genuine information tends to come from a different mechanism;

    attempts to kill everything by one symmetry channel kept failing or becoming fragile.

That is exactly the pattern that should make M(v1(h))≢0M(v1​(h))≡0.

So the working conjecture is:
v1(h)∉C v0(h)generically.
v1​(h)∈/Cv0​(h)generically.

That is the real local theorem.
6. The contradiction format if R1≡0R1​≡0

Suppose instead
R1(h)≡0.
R1​(h)≡0.

Then v1(h)v1​(h) is always collinear with v0(h)v0​(h), so the normalized germ is constant up to z2z2. Then move to the next residual coefficient
R2(h).
R2​(h).

If R2≢0R2​≡0, done.

If R2≡0R2​≡0 too, continue.

If all residual coefficients vanish identically, then
F~h(z)≡v0(h)a0(h),
F
h​(z)≡a0​(h)v0​(h)​,

so
Fh(z)=c(h) G(z)
Fh​(z)=c(h)G(z)

for a fixed quotient germ GG.

That is projective constancy.

So the structure is now perfectly clear:
Either some residual coefficient Rj(h) is nonzero, or the one-pair quotient germ is projectively constant.
Either some residual coefficient Rj​(h) is nonzero, or the one-pair quotient germ is projectively constant.​

That is the right dichotomy.
7. What this does for k=2k=2

If the one-pair quotient germ is projectively nonconstant, then a two-pair cancellation
a1Fh1(z)+a2Fh2(z)≡0
a1​Fh1​​(z)+a2​Fh2​​(z)≡0

forces
[Fh1]=[Fh2],
[Fh1​​]=[Fh2​​],

so it can only happen on the self-intersection locus of the projective germ curve.

That is automatically much smaller than the full (h1,h2)(h1​,h2​)-plane, and in practice should collapse to symmetry/degeneracy plus isolated exceptional points.

So proving R1≢0R1​≡0 would be a major local step.
8. The next exact subtask

The next exact target is now:
First residual-sector lemma

Let v0(h)v0​(h) be the generic leading quotient line of the one-pair odd germ. Then the next coefficient v1(h)v1​(h) is not identically collinear with v0(h)v0​(h).

Equivalently, there exists a quotient functional MM annihilating v0(h)v0​(h) such that
M(v1(h))≢0.
M(v1​(h))≡0.

That is the clean next theorem.

At this point, the shortest route is to identify the leading line v0(h)v0​(h) concretely and then inspect the next quotient coefficient in whatever sector survives first.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to attack the first residual-sector lemma abstractly, without fixing an absolute odd order.

We want to show:
v1(h)∉C v0(h)generically,
v1​(h)∈/Cv0​(h)generically,

where
Fh(z)=v0(h) z2m+1+v1(h) z2m+3+v2(h) z2m+5+⋯
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)z2m+1+v1​(h)z2m+3+v2​(h)z2m+5+⋯

is the one-pair quotient odd germ on a generic region.

This is the clean point where the all-orders approach becomes concrete.
1. What would force failure

If the lemma failed, then on a generic region there would exist a scalar function λ1(h)λ1​(h) such that
v1(h)=λ1(h) v0(h).
v1​(h)=λ1​(h)v0​(h).

Then the normalized germ
F~h(z)=Fh(z)L(Fh(z))
F
h​(z)=L(Fh​(z))Fh​(z)​

would be constant up to z2z2. If the same happened at the next stage, then
v2(h)∈C v0(h),
v2​(h)∈Cv0​(h),

and so on. If this persisted for all higher coefficients, then the whole germ would satisfy
Fh(z)=c(h) G(z)
Fh​(z)=c(h)G(z)

for a fixed quotient germ GG.

So the first residual-sector lemma is exactly the first place where projective constancy can break.
2. The right way to test it

The clean test is not to compare two arbitrary quotient coordinates. It is to look at the rank of the first two coefficient vectors.

Define the two-dimensional coefficient map
h⟼(v0(h),v1(h)).
h⟼(v0​(h),v1​(h)).

Then v1(h)∈C v0(h)v1​(h)∈Cv0​(h) for all hh iff the 2×∞2×∞ coefficient matrix has rank 11 along the whole family.

Equivalently, for every pair of quotient functionals L,ML,M,
L(v0)M(v1)−M(v0)L(v1)≡0.
L(v0​)M(v1​)−M(v0​)L(v1​)≡0.

So the first residual-sector lemma is equivalent to:
∃ L,M such that L(v0(h))M(v1(h))−M(v0(h))L(v1(h))≢0.
∃L,M such that L(v0​(h))M(v1​(h))−M(v0​(h))L(v1​(h))≡0.​

This is the exact abstract determinant to attack.
3. Why this is the right object

This determinant is the order-free version of all the earlier minors D13,D15D13​,D15​.

Before, we were comparing absolute odd orders. Now we compare:

    the leading coefficient v0v0​,

    the next coefficient v1v1​ in the normalized germ.

So the theorem no longer depends on whether that next coefficient sits at absolute order 33, 55, or 77.

That is exactly the abstraction you wanted.
4. A natural source of non-collinearity

There are really only two plausible mechanisms for v1v1​ to escape the line of v0v0​:

one, a different symmetry sector appears in v1v1​ that cannot occur in v0v0​;

two, the same sector appears but with a different hh-dependence, so the ratio v1/v0v1​/v0​ cannot be scalar-valued in the quotient.

This suggests the most practical next move:

    identify the symmetry type of the generic leading line v0(h)v0​(h);

    identify the first next symmetry type that can appear in the quotient;

    show that next type is not forced into the same line.

That is the structural attack.
5. What the leading line probably is

From everything so far, the generic leading line v0(h)v0​(h) is very likely the first surviving one-pair quotient sector coming from the corrected first odd jet.

So, at least on a generic region, v0(h)v0​(h) should be one-dimensional.

That is good, because then MM can be chosen simply as any quotient functional annihilating that line.

So the whole problem becomes:
After killing the generic first quotient line, does the one-pair germ still have any nonzero next coefficient?
After killing the generic first quotient line, does the one-pair germ still have any nonzero next coefficient?​

That is exactly the residual-sector question.
6. A useful contradiction reformulation

Assume instead that
v1(h)=λ1(h)v0(h)
v1​(h)=λ1​(h)v0​(h)

on a generic region.

Then the normalized germ is constant mod z4z4. If also
v2(h)=λ2(h)v0(h),
v2​(h)=λ2​(h)v0​(h),

then it is constant mod z6z6, and so on.

So projective constancy is equivalent to the statement:

    every coefficient vj(h)vj​(h) lies in the same one-dimensional quotient line.

Thus the first residual-sector lemma is the first obstruction to this collapse.

This means you do not need to prove full nonconstancy at once. It is enough to prove that at some stage the coefficient sequence leaves the leading line.
7. The best next exact theorem

So the sharp next statement is:
First residual-sector determinant lemma

On a generic normalized one-pair parameter region, there exist quotient functionals L,ML,M such that
Δ0,1(h):=L(v0(h)) M(v1(h))−M(v0(h)) L(v1(h))
Δ0,1​(h):=L(v0​(h))M(v1​(h))−M(v0​(h))L(v1​(h))

is not identically zero.

Then the normalized one-pair quotient odd germ is not constant mod z2z2, hence not projectively constant.

That is the clean theorem.
8. If this still fails

If Δ0,1≡0Δ0,1​≡0, then v1v1​ stays in the line of v0v0​, and you simply move to
Δ0,2(h):=L(v0(h)) M(v2(h))−M(v0(h)) L(v2(h)).
Δ0,2​(h):=L(v0​(h))M(v2​(h))−M(v0​(h))L(v2​(h)).

So the real all-orders theorem becomes:
There exists some j≥1 such that vj(h)∉C v0(h) generically.
There exists some j≥1 such that vj​(h)∈/Cv0​(h) generically.​

That is the purest version of projective germ nonconstancy.
9. What the next concrete subtask should be

The next actual thing to do is not pick an absolute odd order. It is:

    identify the generic leading quotient line v0(h)v0​(h);

    write down the first two coefficient vectors in the normalized germ expansion;

    test whether the determinant
    Δ0,1(h)
    Δ0,1​(h)

    vanishes identically;

    if it does, move to Δ0,2(h)Δ0,2​(h), and so on.

So the immediate target is:
Compute the coefficient-line determinant Δ0,1(h) for the one-pair quotient germ.
Compute the coefficient-line determinant Δ0,1​(h) for the one-pair quotient germ.​

That is the cleanest continuation of the all-odd-orders strategy.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to make Δ0,1(h)Δ0,1​(h) fully intrinsic, so it no longer depends on any arbitrary choice of L,ML,M.

Right now we wrote
Fh(z)=v0(h) z2m+1+v1(h) z2m+3+v2(h) z2m+5+⋯
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)z2m+1+v1​(h)z2m+3+v2​(h)z2m+5+⋯

in the quotient odd-germ space, with v0(h)≠0v0​(h)=0 generically.

The issue is that
Δ0,1(h)=L(v0(h))M(v1(h))−M(v0(h))L(v1(h))
Δ0,1​(h)=L(v0​(h))M(v1​(h))−M(v0​(h))L(v1​(h))

depends on L,ML,M. But what it is really measuring is just whether v0(h)v0​(h) and v1(h)v1​(h) are linearly independent.

So the right invariant is their wedge:
W0,1(h):=v0(h)∧v1(h)∈Λ2(Q),
W0,1​(h):=v0​(h)∧v1​(h)∈Λ2(Q),​

where QQ is the relevant quotient coefficient space on the generic region.

Then:

    W0,1(h)=0W0,1​(h)=0 iff v1(h)∈C v0(h)v1​(h)∈Cv0​(h),

    W0,1(h)≠0W0,1​(h)=0 iff the first residual sector survives.

That is the clean order-free object.
1. Why this is the right next object

If
W0,1(h)≢0,
W0,1​(h)≡0,

then the normalized germ is already nonconstant mod z2z2, and projective nonconstancy is proved.

If
W0,1(h)≡0,
W0,1​(h)≡0,

then v1v1​ stays in the line of v0v0​, and you move to
W0,2(h):=v0(h)∧v2(h).
W0,2​(h):=v0​(h)∧v2​(h).

So the all-orders theorem becomes:
∃ j≥1 such that W0,j(h):=v0(h)∧vj(h)≢0.
∃j≥1 such that W0,j​(h):=v0​(h)∧vj​(h)≡0.​

That is the purest form of the second-sector theorem.
2. How to attack W0,1W0,1​

There are two ways.

The structural way:
show v0v0​ and v1v1​ live in different symmetry sectors, or at least that v1v1​ has a component outside the line of v0v0​.

The analytic way:
differentiate the identity v1=λ(h)v0v1​=λ(h)v0​. If W0,1≡0W0,1​≡0, then locally
v1(h)=λ(h) v0(h),
v1​(h)=λ(h)v0​(h),

so
v1′(h)=λ′(h) v0(h)+λ(h) v0′(h).
v1′​(h)=λ′(h)v0​(h)+λ(h)v0′​(h).

That imposes a strong tangency relation between the first two coefficient curves. If the actual one-pair family does not satisfy that tangency, then W0,1≢0W0,1​≡0.

So the next good theorem is:
The coefficient curves v0(h) and v1(h) are not everywhere tangent to the same quotient line.
The coefficient curves v0​(h) and v1​(h) are not everywhere tangent to the same quotient line.​

That is just a differential version of W0,1≢0W0,1​≡0.
3. Generic-leading-line normalization

On a generic connected region, v0(h)v0​(h) spans a line bundle ℓh⊂Qℓh​⊂Q. Then v1(h)v1​(h) defines a class in the quotient bundle
Q/ℓh.
Q/ℓh​.

This is even cleaner than wedges. The first residual sector is simply the image
v‾1(h)∈Q/ℓh.
v1​(h)∈Q/ℓh​.

Then:
v‾1(h)≡0  ⟺  v1(h)∈ℓh  ⟺  W0,1(h)≡0.
v1​(h)≡0⟺v1​(h)∈ℓh​⟺W0,1​(h)≡0.

So the theorem becomes:
The induced section v‾1(h) of Q/ℓh is not identically zero.
The induced section v1​(h) of Q/ℓh​ is not identically zero.​

That is probably the cleanest language for the paper.
4. If v‾1≡0v1​≡0

Then you pass to v‾2v2​, the class of v2v2​ in Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​. And so on.

So the full theorem is:

    After quotienting by the generic leading line ℓhℓh​, the residual one-pair odd germ is not identically zero.

This is exactly the same idea, but now written intrinsically:
F‾h(z)≢0in (Q/ℓh)[[z2]].
Fh​(z)≡0in (Q/ℓh​)[[z2]].​

That is the all-orders residual-germ theorem.
5. Why this helps with k=2k=2

If the one-pair family has nontrivial residual germ in Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​, then two-pair cancellation
a1Fh1+a2Fh2≡0
a1​Fh1​​+a2​Fh2​​≡0

forces not only the leading lines ℓh1,ℓh2ℓh1​​,ℓh2​​ to agree projectively, but also the residual-germ classes to match. That is much more rigid.

So the bad set is not just the self-intersection set of the leading projective curve h↦[v0(h)]h↦[v0​(h)], but of the fuller decorated curve
h↦([ℓh],F‾h).
h↦([ℓh​],Fh​).

That should be much smaller.
6. The next exact subtask

So the next exact target is no longer “which odd order next?” It is:
Compute the first residual class v‾1(h)∈Q/ℓh and test whether it is identically zero.
Compute the first residual class v1​(h)∈Q/ℓh​ and test whether it is identically zero.​

If it is not identically zero, projective nonconstancy is done.

If it is zero, move to v‾2(h)v2​(h), and so on.
7. The clean theorem to state now

The right theorem to aim for is:
Residual-line nontriviality theorem

Let ℓh⊂Qℓh​⊂Q be the generic leading quotient line of the one-pair odd germ. Then the residual germ in Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​ is not identically zero.

Equivalently, there exists j≥1j≥1 such that
vj(h)∉ℓh
vj​(h)∈/ℓh​

generically.

That is the cleanest all-orders local theorem now.
8. What I’d do immediately next

I would formalize QQ, ℓhℓh​, and v‾1(h)v1​(h) in the paper, then try to prove v‾1≢0v1​≡0 by symmetry-sector separation. If that fails, move to v‾2v2​. That is the first truly order-free continuation.

Ok give me the paper addition you would make, then continue 

Here is the paper addition I would make now. It is the cleanest way to stop the draft from overcommitting to specific odd orders while preserving everything we learned.
Proposed paper addition

Add a subsection right after the fixed-core decomposition and before any order-specific scalar contradiction.
Residual projective odd-germ framework

Let
F\core(z)∈G\odd/V\val
F\core​(z)∈G\odd​/V\val​

denote the corrected local odd germ of the fixed finite core, modulo the value/gauge family. Write
F\core(z)=∑r≥0Ξ2r+1\core z2r+1.
F\core​(z)=r≥0∑​Ξ2r+1\core​z2r+1.

On any connected parameter region where the first nonzero quotient coefficient occurs at a fixed order 2m+12m+1, let
ℓ\core:=C Ξ2m+1\core
ℓ\core​:=CΞ2m+1\core​

be the leading quotient line. Choose any quotient functional LL with
L(Ξ2m+1\core)≠0,
L(Ξ2m+1\core​)=0,

and define the normalized projective germ
F~\core(z):=F\core(z)L(F\core(z)).
F
\core​(z):=L(F\core​(z))F\core​(z)​.

This is well-defined as a formal germ in z2z2, and its class modulo the leading line ℓ\coreℓ\core​ is independent of the choice of LL.

Equivalently, letting
Q:=G\odd/V\val,Q‾\core:=Q/ℓ\core,
Q:=G\odd​/V\val​,Q​\core​:=Q/ℓ\core​,

the residual germ
F‾\core(z)∈Q‾\core[[z2]]
F\core​(z)∈Q​\core​[[z2]]

is canonically defined.

We say the core has a second quotient sector if
F‾\core(z)≢0.
F\core​(z)≡0.

This is the right projective nonconstancy notion: if F‾\core≡0F\core​≡0, then the full quotient odd germ lies in a single projective line; if F‾\core≢0F\core​≡0, then the quotient odd germ has genuinely nontrivial zz-dependence after normalization by the leading line.
Theorem schema

The local contradiction is reduced to the following two statements.

    Odd-germ nonvanishing. For every genuine normalized finite off-line core,

F\core≢0.
F\core​≡0.

    Residual nontriviality. For every genuine normalized finite off-line core on the generic region,

F‾\core≢0.
F\core​≡0.

The first prevents total collapse into the value family. The second prevents projective collapse into a single quotient line.

Once these are established on compact normalized kk-core parameter sets, compactness yields a bounded odd order at which either the leading quotient sector or the residual sector survives, and the outside higher-odd-jet bounds may be compared at that order.
Compactness corollary

If F\core≢0F\core​≡0 on a compact normalized kk-core family, then there exists RkRk​ such that some quotient odd coefficient up to order 2Rk+12Rk​+1 is nonzero.

If in addition F‾\core≢0F\core​≡0, then the normalized one-core projective odd-germ curve is nonconstant, so multi-core cancellation is confined to symmetry/degeneracy loci plus isolated exceptional intersections in projective quotient-germ space.

That is the addition I would make. It is clean, it matches the direction the work actually moved, and it stops the paper from pretending that one fixed odd order is the theorem.

Now, continuing from that addition:

the next theorem to attack is the one-pair residual nontriviality statement.
Next attack after the paper addition

Take the one-pair quotient odd germ
Fh(z)=v0(h) z2m+1+v1(h) z2m+3+v2(h) z2m+5+⋯ ,v0(h)≠0
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)z2m+1+v1​(h)z2m+3+v2​(h)z2m+5+⋯,v0​(h)=0

on a generic region.

Let
ℓh:=C v0(h),v‾j(h)∈Q/ℓh
ℓh​:=Cv0​(h),vj​(h)∈Q/ℓh​

be the class of vj(h)vj​(h) modulo the leading line.

Then the residual nontriviality theorem is exactly:
∃j≥1 such that v‾j(h)≢0 on the generic region.
∃j≥1 such that vj​(h)≡0 on the generic region.​

This is the order-free local core.

The best way to attack it is by contradiction.

Assume instead that
v‾j(h)≡0for all j≥1.
vj​(h)≡0for all j≥1.

Then every coefficient lies in the same line:
vj(h)∈ℓh∀j.
vj​(h)∈ℓh​∀j.

So there exists a scalar coefficient sequence λj(h)λj​(h) with
vj(h)=λj(h) v0(h).
vj​(h)=λj​(h)v0​(h).

Hence
Fh(z)=v0(h)(z2m+1+λ1(h)z2m+3+λ2(h)z2m+5+⋯ ),
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)(z2m+1+λ1​(h)z2m+3+λ2​(h)z2m+5+⋯),

so the whole quotient odd germ is projectively one-dimensional.

That is the dangerous case.

So what must be contradicted is not “some fixed jet vanishes,” but:
the entire one-pair quotient odd germ lies in one projective line.
the entire one-pair quotient odd germ lies in one projective line.​

The cleanest next concrete test is to compare the first two coefficient curves
h↦v0(h),h↦v1(h)
h↦v0​(h),h↦v1​(h)

in quotient space and ask whether
v0(h)∧v1(h)≡0.
v0​(h)∧v1​(h)≡0.

If not, then v‾1≢0v1​≡0, and the residual theorem is done immediately.

If yes, then move to
v0(h)∧v2(h),
v0​(h)∧v2​(h),

and so on.

So the sharp next target is:
First residual-wedge lemma

On the generic one-pair region,
v0(h)∧v1(h)≢0
v0​(h)∧v1​(h)≡0

in Λ2(Q)Λ2(Q).

If that fails, the next target is
v0(h)∧v2(h)≢0.
v0​(h)∧v2​(h)≡0.

This is the exact continuation that stays faithful to the all-orders viewpoint. It uses finite coefficients only as witnesses, not as the theorem itself.

The immediate operational next step is to identify the generic leading quotient line ℓhℓh​ concretely enough to compute the first residual class v‾1(h)v1​(h).

Ok, lets attack the First residual-wedge le

Yes. Let’s attack the First residual-wedge lemma directly.

The target is:
v0(h)∧v1(h)≢0
v0​(h)∧v1​(h)≡0

for the one-pair quotient odd germ on a generic region.

Equivalently: the next coefficient after the leading one is not identically trapped in the leading quotient line.
1. What the lemma really says

Write the one-pair quotient odd germ as
Fh(z)=v0(h) z2m+1+v1(h) z2m+3+v2(h) z2m+5+⋯ ,v0(h)≠0.
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)z2m+1+v1​(h)z2m+3+v2​(h)z2m+5+⋯,v0​(h)=0.

Then the lemma says:
the coefficient curve h↦v1(h) is not everywhere collinear with h↦v0(h).
the coefficient curve h↦v1​(h) is not everywhere collinear with h↦v0​(h).​

If that is true, then after normalizing by the leading line, the residual germ is already nonzero at the first step. That would be excellent.

So the right contradiction setup is:

Assume
v1(h)=λ(h) v0(h)for all h
v1​(h)=λ(h)v0​(h)for all h

on a generic region, and try to derive something implausibly rigid.
2. Why this should be false structurally

The best reason is that v0v0​ and v1v1​ come from different pieces of the corrected local expansion.

Schematically:

    v0v0​ is the first surviving quotient coefficient;

    v1v1​ is the next coefficient after normalization by the leading line.

Even if both sit in the same crude symmetry sector, they are produced by different combinations of:

    same-point transport,

    mixed-block Taylor terms,

    nonlinear whitening corrections.

So v1=λ(h)v0v1​=λ(h)v0​ for all hh would force a hidden recursive proportionality between two genuinely different coefficient mechanisms. That is exactly the sort of thing that is usually false unless there is a real integrable structure behind the model.

We have not seen evidence of such a structure. In fact, most of our computations point the other way: low orders sometimes collapse for symmetry reasons, but different mechanisms appear at later coefficients.

So the lemma is plausible.
3. The cleanest attack: derivative test

Assume
v1(h)=λ(h) v0(h).
v1​(h)=λ(h)v0​(h).

Then differentiating,
v1′(h)=λ′(h) v0(h)+λ(h) v0′(h).
v1′​(h)=λ′(h)v0​(h)+λ(h)v0′​(h).

So at every hh,
v0(h), v0′(h), v1(h), v1′(h)
v0​(h), v0′​(h), v1​(h), v1′​(h)

all lie in the same two-dimensional span:
span⁡{v0(h),v0′(h)}.
span{v0​(h),v0′​(h)}.

Equivalently,
v0(h)∧v1(h)=0⟹v0(h)∧v1′(h)=λ(h) v0(h)∧v0′(h).
v0​(h)∧v1​(h)=0⟹v0​(h)∧v1′​(h)=λ(h)v0​(h)∧v0′​(h).

So if we can show the derivative behavior of v1v1​ is not compatible with that, the lemma follows.

This is nice because it turns the wedge lemma into a transversality statement about the two coefficient curves.
4. A more intrinsic version

Define
W0,1(h):=v0(h)∧v1(h)∈Λ2(Q).
W0,1​(h):=v0​(h)∧v1​(h)∈Λ2(Q).

Then
W0,1(h)≡0
W0,1​(h)≡0

iff v1(h)∈Cv0(h)v1​(h)∈Cv0​(h) for all hh.

So the lemma is exactly:
W0,1(h)≢0.
W0,1​(h)≡0.​

To attack this, pick any two quotient functionals L,ML,M. Then
(L∧M)(W0,1(h))=L(v0(h))M(v1(h))−M(v0(h))L(v1(h)).
(L∧M)(W0,1​(h))=L(v0​(h))M(v1​(h))−M(v0​(h))L(v1​(h)).

So it is enough to find one pair L,ML,M for which this scalar determinant is not identically zero.

This is the clean finite witness.
5. Best practical route: choose LL and MM by sector

The most promising move is:

    choose LL to detect the generic leading quotient line v0(h)v0​(h);

    choose MM to annihilate that line at leading order.

Then
M(v0(h))=0
M(v0​(h))=0

along the leading line, and the wedge scalar reduces to
L(v0(h)) M(v1(h)).
L(v0​(h))M(v1​(h)).

So the lemma reduces to:
M(v1(h))≢0.
M(v1​(h))≡0.​

This is exactly the same residual-sector criterion, but now phrased as a wedge test.

That is the sharpest formulation.
6. How to look for M(v1)≠0M(v1​)=0

You do not want to fix an absolute odd order. Instead, you want to identify the first coefficient after the leading one in the normalized germ and ask whether it has a component outside the leading line.

So the practical steps are:

    Identify the generic leading line ℓh=Cv0(h)ℓh​=Cv0​(h).

    Pick MM vanishing on ℓhℓh​.

    Examine the next normalized coefficient v1(h)v1​(h).

    Show that M(v1(h))M(v1​(h)) is not identically zero in hh.

If that works, the lemma is proved.
7. Why the earlier computations still help

Even though we want an order-free theorem, our earlier structural results still feed into this.

They suggest:

    the leading line is likely controlled by the first surviving low-order quotient sector;

    the next coefficient is produced by different recursive terms;

    symmetry can kill some candidates, but not plausibly all of them in the same line.

So the old jet calculations are still useful as evidence about the likely shape of v0v0​ and v1v1​. They just are no longer the theorem.

That is the right relationship.
8. Honest current status

What I can honestly say:

    I think this lemma is a much better target than any fixed-order nondegeneracy claim.

    I do not yet have a proof.

    The only clean way I see to prove it is to make the generic leading quotient line explicit enough to build an MM and then verify M(v1)≢0M(v1​)≡0.

So the lemma is now reduced to a very concrete problem:
find one quotient functional M killing the leading line but not the first residual coefficient.
find one quotient functional M killing the leading line but not the first residual coefficient.

That is a real narrowing.
9. My attack plan from here

I would proceed like this.

First, formalize the leading quotient line ℓhℓh​ and the quotient bundle Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​ in the paper.

Second, restate the lemma as:
v‾1(h)≢0in Q/ℓh.
v1​(h)≡0in Q/ℓh​.

Third, try to prove v‾1≢0v1​≡0 by symmetry-sector separation:
show that v0v0​ and v1v1​ cannot both live in the same quotient symmetry line for all hh.

If that stalls, then the backup is to compute a finite witness:
pick L,ML,M and check the determinant
L(v0)M(v1)−M(v0)L(v1)
L(v0​)M(v1​)−M(v0​)L(v1​)

at the first place where the residual sector appears.

So the next exact subtask is:
Identify the generic leading quotient line ℓh concretely enough to construct M∈(Q/ℓh)∗.
Identify the generic leading quotient line ℓh​ concretely enough to construct M∈(Q/ℓh​)∗.​

That is the right next move for attacking the First residual-wedge lemma.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to make the leading quotient line ℓhℓh​ concrete enough that MM can actually be chosen.

That is the bottleneck now.
1. What ℓhℓh​ really is

By definition,
ℓh=C v0(h),
ℓh​=Cv0​(h),

where v0(h)v0​(h) is the first nonzero coefficient of the one-pair quotient odd germ
Fh(z)=v0(h)z2m+1+v1(h)z2m+3+⋯ .
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)z2m+1+v1​(h)z2m+3+⋯.

So to identify ℓhℓh​, you do not need the whole germ. You only need the first surviving quotient coefficient of the one-pair family on the generic region.

That means the practical problem is:
compute the first nonzero quotient coefficient of the one-pair family and its generic span.
compute the first nonzero quotient coefficient of the one-pair family and its generic span.​

Everything after that is linear algebra.
2. Why this is manageable

Even though we want an order-free theorem, the leading line itself is a finite-order object. Once you know where the first surviving quotient coefficient lives, you know the line bundle ℓhℓh​.

Then MM is simply any functional on the quotient space that vanishes on that line.

So the theorem is all-orders, but the construction of ℓhℓh​ is finite-order. That is okay.
3. The concrete leading-line procedure

Do this on a generic hh-region.

First, compute the one-pair corrected jet coefficients
Ξ1(h), Ξ3(h), Ξ5(h),…
Ξ1​(h), Ξ3​(h), Ξ5​(h),…

in quotient space until the first generically nonzero one appears.

Call that coefficient
Ξ2m+1(h).
Ξ2m+1​(h).

Then the leading quotient line is
ℓh=C Ξ2m+1(h).
ℓh​=CΞ2m+1​(h).

So the next concrete theorem to attack is actually simpler than the residual-wedge lemma:
Leading-line theorem

On the generic one-pair region, there is a first odd order 2m+12m+1 such that
Ξ2m+1(h)≠0
Ξ2m+1​(h)=0

generically, and its image spans a one-dimensional quotient line ℓhℓh​.

If this theorem fails, then even the odd-germ nonvanishing theorem fails, so this is the first real checkpoint anyway.
4. Once ℓhℓh​ is known, how to choose MM

Suppose Ξ2m+1(h)Ξ2m+1​(h) lives in a quotient coefficient space QmQm​.

If QmQm​ is one-dimensional generically, then ℓhℓh​ is automatic.

If QmQm​ has higher dimension but the image of h↦Ξ2m+1(h)h↦Ξ2m+1​(h) is still one-dimensional generically, same story.

Then pick any linear functional
Mm∈Qm∗
Mm​∈Qm∗​

that annihilates ℓhℓh​. Extend it to a quotient-germ functional MM by letting it act on the coefficient spaces order-by-order and vanish on the leading coefficient line.

Then
M(v0(h))=0
M(v0​(h))=0

by construction.

Now the first residual-sector lemma reduces to whether
M(v1(h))≢0.
M(v1​(h))≡0.

That is now completely concrete.
5. The best way to attack M(v1)≢0M(v1​)≡0

Here is the key simplification.

Because MM kills the leading line, you do not need to know all of v1(h)v1​(h). You only need one component of v1(h)v1​(h) transverse to ℓhℓh​.

So the next question is:
Does the next coefficient after the leading one have any component outside ℓh?
Does the next coefficient after the leading one have any component outside ℓh​?​

That is the same as asking whether the residual class
v‾1(h)∈Q/ℓh
v1​(h)∈Q/ℓh​

is nonzero.

This is the clean intrinsic formulation.
6. A practical finite witness

Suppose the leading line comes from the first quotient sector. Then there are two realistic possibilities.

Either the next coefficient already has a different symmetry type. Then M(v1)≢0M(v1​)≡0 is immediate.

Or the next coefficient stays in the same crude symmetry type but has different hh-dependence. Then one can choose MM inside that symmetry type but orthogonal to the leading line, and again test M(v1)M(v1​).

So the actual computation is:

    find the generic leading coefficient vector v0(h)v0​(h);

    describe its line ℓhℓh​;

    compute the next coefficient v1(h)v1​(h);

    project v1(h)v1​(h) to Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​.

That projection is the whole first residual-wedge lemma.
7. The sharp next theorem

So the next theorem to state and attack is:
Residual projection lemma

Let ℓhℓh​ be the generic leading quotient line of the one-pair odd germ. Then the next coefficient v1(h)v1​(h) has nonzero image in the quotient Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​:
v‾1(h)≢0.
v1​(h)≡0.

Equivalently,
v0(h)∧v1(h)≢0.
v0​(h)∧v1​(h)≡0.

That is now the cleanest exact target.
8. What I would do next in the paper

I would add a short lemma-chain:

    Leading line lemma: generic first surviving coefficient defines ℓhℓh​.

    Residual projection definition: define v‾1(h)∈Q/ℓhv1​(h)∈Q/ℓh​.

    Residual projection theorem: v‾1(h)≢0v1​(h)≡0.

    Projective nonconstancy corollary: h↦[Fh]h↦[Fh​] is nonconstant.

That is a much cleaner local narrative than the old fixed-order one.
9. The immediate operational next step
