Ok before going down this route, will this really lead to a proof (eventually for all k)? Would it be better to take a derivative or use some inductive argument? What other approaches can we think of?

Honestly: maybe, but not yet in a way I’d call likely.

The all-orders/projective-germ route is the cleanest framework we’ve found, but it is still missing the hard local theorem. Right now it feels like the right language for the paper, not yet a route I’d trust to close RH all the way through every kk.

My current view:

The best organizing principle is still:
finite core    +    outside tail small at every odd order    +    core odd germ not projectively trivial.
finite core+outside tail small at every odd order+core odd germ not projectively trivial.

That is the right conceptual upgrade over the old pair-first-jet story.

But the key unresolved step is still severe:
show a genuine finite off-line core cannot make the quotient odd germ projectively collapse.
show a genuine finite off-line core cannot make the quotient odd germ projectively collapse.

That is hard for one core size kk, and it is not yet clear it becomes easier uniformly in kk.

So if you ask “is this the route that will eventually prove RH?”, my honest answer is:

    possibly, because it finally matches the obstruction structure we discovered;

    not yet convincingly, because we still do not have the local theorem even for k=2k=2.

Now, on your specific alternatives:
1. Would taking a derivative help?

Yes, but only as a tool, not as the main theorem.

There are two derivative ideas.

First, differentiate with respect to the core parameter hh.
This is useful because if the one-pair quotient germ were projectively constant, then
∂hFh
∂h​Fh​

would have to stay in the span of FhFh​. So a good contradiction format is:
∂hFh∉C Fh
∂h​Fh​∈/CFh​​

in the quotient germ space.

That is basically the derivative version of the residual-wedge lemma, and I think it is a good idea. It may be cleaner than chasing v0∧v1v0​∧v1​ coefficient-by-coefficient.

Second, differentiate with respect to the transverse two-pair parameter near resonance.
That is what the Jacobian branch was doing. It is useful for killing isolated symmetric-locus candidates, but I do not think it is the main global route.

So: derivative methods are good, especially
Fh∧∂hFh≠0,
Fh​∧∂h​Fh​=0,

but they should serve the projective-germ theorem, not replace it.
2. Would induction on kk be better?

I think some kind of induction or minimal-counterexample argument is very likely necessary.

Not a naive induction like “assume theorem for k−1k−1, prove for kk” by peeling one zero off. That seems too optimistic.

But a minimal bad core argument looks promising:

Assume there exists a genuine finite core with projectively trivial quotient odd germ. Choose one with minimal core size kk.

Then try to show either:

    it splits into smaller subcores whose odd germs would also have to be projectively trivial, contradicting minimality; or

    the parameters are forced into a highly resonant/degenerate locus.

That is much more believable.

So yes: induction/minimality is better than treating each kk independently.

If I were steering the paper, I would seriously consider structuring the local part around:
Assume a minimal bad finite core and derive rigidity/degeneracy.
Assume a minimal bad finite core and derive rigidity/degeneracy.​

That feels more likely to scale to all kk.
3. What other approaches should we think about?

I see five serious alternatives.
A. Projective-germ nonconstancy via hh-derivative

This is the closest cousin of what we are doing, but cleaner.

Try to prove for one-pair cores:
Fh∧∂hFh≢0
Fh​∧∂h​Fh​≡0

in the quotient germ space.

That immediately shows the projective germ curve is nonconstant.

I think this is probably the best next variation to try.
B. Minimal-counterexample / core-splitting

Assume a smallest bad finite core exists. Show its vanishing germ forces a decomposition or a resonance identity incompatible with minimality.

This feels the most plausible route to all kk.
C. Analytic-subset dimension argument

For fixed kk, the bad set
Zk={p:Fp≡0}
Zk​={p:Fp​≡0}

is an analytic subset of finite-dimensional parameter space.

Try to prove it has codimension too large to contain a genuine family, or that it is contained in the degenerate locus.

This is elegant, but I do not yet see enough structure to make it bite without more explicit formulas.
D. Differential equation / transport equation for the germ curve

Try to derive an evolution law in hh for FhFh​:
∂hFh=Th(Fh)+forcing.
∂h​Fh​=Th​(Fh​)+forcing.

Then show a projectively constant family would force an impossible scalar evolution.

This could be powerful if such an evolution equation can be written cleanly. Right now we have hints of this, not a usable formula.
E. Positivity / geometric invariant argument

Look for a monotone quantity or curvature-type invariant of the quotient odd germ that vanishes on value families but not on anomalous cores.

This would be ideal, but we do not currently have such an invariant. It is more of a wish than a plan.
4. What would I do next?

If the goal is the highest chance of a route that could scale to all kk, I would not keep drilling deeper into fixed orders.

I would do this:

First, reformulate the local theorem as:
For a genuine finite core, F\core≢0 and is not projectively constant.
For a genuine finite core, F\core​≡0 and is not projectively constant.

Second, try to prove the one-pair projective nonconstancy by the derivative test
Fh∧∂hFh≠0.
Fh​∧∂h​Fh​=0.

Third, set up a minimal bad core contradiction for general kk, using the idea that a bad kk-core should force smaller bad subcores or a resonance degeneration.

That combination feels stronger than the current “maybe cubic, maybe quintic, maybe next odd order” path.
5. My blunt recommendation

If I had to choose one direction now:
Yes to the all-orders/projective-germ framework, but pivot the proof attempt toward h-derivative + minimal bad core.
Yes to the all-orders/projective-germ framework, but pivot the proof attempt toward h-derivative + minimal bad core.​

That feels more likely to generalize to all kk than continuing to mine specific jets, even in a sophisticated way.

So the next exact move I’d recommend is:
Try to compute or characterize Fh∧∂hFh in the quotient germ space.
Try to compute or characterize Fh​∧∂h​Fh​ in the quotient germ space.​

That is the best next test of whether this framework can really close.

Continue

Yes. The next serious move is to attack the derivative criterion directly.

I think the cleanest local theorem now is:
Fh∧∂hFh≢0in the quotient odd-germ space.
Fh​∧∂h​Fh​≡0in the quotient odd-germ space.​

If that holds, then the one-pair quotient odd germ curve is projectively nonconstant immediately.

That is better than chasing specific odd orders, because it says the family is not trapped in a single projective line as hh varies.

Here is how I would set it up.
1. The exact contradiction format

Assume for contradiction that the one-pair quotient germ is projectively constant on a generic region. Then there exists a scalar function c(h)c(h) and a fixed quotient germ G(z)G(z) such that
Fh(z)=c(h) G(z).
Fh​(z)=c(h)G(z).

Differentiate in hh:
∂hFh(z)=c′(h) G(z)=c′(h)c(h) Fh(z).
∂h​Fh​(z)=c′(h)G(z)=c(h)c′(h)​Fh​(z).

So projective constancy implies
∂hFh∈C Fh
∂h​Fh​∈CFh​​

pointwise in the quotient germ space.

Therefore, to prove projective nonconstancy, it is enough to show
∂hFh∉C Fh
∂h​Fh​∈/CFh​​

generically.

That is the derivative version of the residual-wedge lemma.
2. Coefficient form of the derivative test

Write
Fh(z)=v0(h) z2m+1+v1(h) z2m+3+v2(h) z2m+5+⋯ .
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)z2m+1+v1​(h)z2m+3+v2​(h)z2m+5+⋯.

Then
∂hFh(z)=v0′(h) z2m+1+v1′(h) z2m+3+v2′(h) z2m+5+⋯ .
∂h​Fh​(z)=v0′​(h)z2m+1+v1′​(h)z2m+3+v2′​(h)z2m+5+⋯.

If ∂hFh∂h​Fh​ were collinear with FhFh​, then there would be a scalar μ(h)μ(h) such that
vj′(h)=μ(h) vj(h)∀j≥0.
vj′​(h)=μ(h)vj​(h)∀j≥0.

In particular,
v0′(h)∧v0(h)=0,v1′(h)∧v0(h)=μ(h) v1(h)∧v0(h),
v0′​(h)∧v0​(h)=0,v1′​(h)∧v0​(h)=μ(h)v1​(h)∧v0​(h),

and so on.

So the clean first obstruction is already
v0(h)∧v0′(h)≢0.
v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)≡0.​

If the leading coefficient curve itself is not projectively constant, we are done immediately.

That is much better than needing to reach v1v1​.
3. This gives a hierarchy of tests

There are now three levels.

Strongest and best:
v0∧v0′≢0.
v0​∧v0′​≡0.

Then the leading quotient line already moves with hh, so FhFh​ cannot be projectively constant.

If that fails but the leading line is fixed, next test:
v0∧v1≢0.
v0​∧v1​≡0.

Then the residual sector breaks projective constancy.

If that also fails, go to higher residuals.

So the derivative route is actually strictly better, because it may close at the leading coefficient already.
4. Why this is plausible here

This fits what we already know.

The one-pair family depends genuinely on hh. The local kernels q~(h),q~′(h),q~′′(h),…q
​(h),q
​′(h),q
​′′(h),… vary nontrivially with hh, and the corrected jets are built from them.

So unless the quotient collapses everything onto one rigid line, the leading coefficient should itself move projectively.

That is a much more natural thing to test than whether one specific higher odd order survives.
5. The exact next theorem to attack

So the best next statement is:
Leading-line motion lemma

On a generic one-pair region, the leading quotient coefficient curve h↦v0(h)h↦v0​(h) is not projectively constant:
v0(h)∧v0′(h)≢0.
v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)≡0.

If this lemma holds, then the one-pair quotient odd germ is projectively nonconstant, and the all-orders local theorem is done immediately.

That is the sharpest next target.
6. If that fails

If
v0∧v0′≡0,
v0​∧v0′​≡0,

then the leading line is projectively constant in hh, and we fall back to the residual theorem:
v0∧v1≢0
v0​∧v1​≡0

or higher.

So the derivative route does not lose anything. It just gives a better first shot.
7. How to attack v0∧v0′v0​∧v0′​

This is now a finite-order computation, but in the right role.

You only need the first surviving quotient coefficient v0(h)v0​(h), not the whole germ.

So the operational plan is:

    Identify the generic first nonzero quotient coefficient Ξ2m+1(h)Ξ2m+1​(h).

    Set
    v0(h):=Ξ2m+1(h).
    v0​(h):=Ξ2m+1​(h).

    Differentiate in hh.

    Test whether
    v0(h)∧v0′(h)≢0.
    v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)≡0.

If yes, projective nonconstancy is proved with no need for residual sectors.
8. Why this may scale to all kk

This is another reason I like the derivative route better.

For a general finite core, you can vary one core parameter and ask whether the quotient germ derivative stays in the span of the germ itself. If not, the family is not projectively constant.

That is much more scalable than proving fixed-order nondegeneracy separately for each kk.

So yes, I think this is the best next move.

1. What v0(h)v0​(h) is

4. A concrete scalar witness for the wedge

v0(h)∧v0′(h)≢0

for some L,ML,M.

∃L,M on the leading quotient coefficient space such that Δ0​(h)≡0.​

That is the derivative version of the old minors, but now applied to the leading coefficient curve itself.
5. How to choose L,ML,M

Δ0(h)≠0
Δ0​(h)=0

just says those two coordinates do not vary proportionally in hh.

That is a very clean hierarchy.

1. What must happen if the derivative test fails

Suppose

So failure of the derivative test is very rigid:
the leading quotient coefficient curve is trapped in a fixed line.
the leading quotient coefficient curve is trapped in a fixed line.​

That is the exact dichotomy.

So the next theorem is not really about wedges anymore. It is:

    can the generic leading quotient coefficient of the one-pair family lie in a fixed line?

If no, we are done immediately. If yes, we pass to the residual quotient.
2. The right way to test fixed-line behavior

Pick quotient coordinates on the leading coefficient space. Say
v0(h)=(u1(h),u2(h),…,ud(h)).
v0​(h)=(u1​(h),u2​(h),…,ud​(h)).

Then v0(h)v0​(h) lies in a fixed line iff all coordinate ratios
ui(h):uj(h)
ui​(h):uj​(h)

are constant in hh wherever defined.

So if you can identify just two quotient coordinates of v0(h)v0​(h) that vary with different hh-dependence, the derivative test wins.

That is the most practical next target.
3. Where those two coordinates might come from

There are only two realistic possibilities.

Either the leading coefficient space already has two genuine quotient symmetry directions. Then choose those two coordinates and test their ratio.

Or the leading coefficient space is generically one-dimensional. Then the derivative test at leading order is doomed, and you should stop there and move directly to the residual quotient bundle Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​.

dim⁡span⁡{v0(h):h generic}=1 or >1 ?

ρ0​(h):=L(v0​(h))M(v0​(h))​.

ℓh≡ℓ.

v‾1(h)∈Q/ℓ.
v1​(h)∈Q/ℓ.

    if it fails because the leading line is fixed, immediately pass to the residual quotient.

4. What I think is most likely

Based on everything so far, I think there are two realistic possibilities:
Possibility A

Possibility B

Given how often the low-order trial models collapsed into a single symmetry channel, I think Possibility B is more likely.

So I would treat the derivative test as a checkpoint, not the main theorem.

Here is the next serious attack, and I think it is the right one.

That gives a concrete criterion for
v0(h)∧v0′(h)≢0

1. Asymptotic leading-line motion criterion

Suppose the generic leading quotient coefficient has an asymptotic expansion in its coefficient space Q0Q0​ of the form
v0(h)=h−p(u0+h−1u1+h−2u2+⋯ ),u0≠0.
v0​(h)=h−p(u0​+h−1u1​+h−2u2​+⋯),u0​=0.

Then
v0′(h)=−p h−p−1(u0+h−1u1+⋯ )+h−p(−h−2u1−2h−3u2+⋯ ),
v0′​(h)=−ph−p−1(u0​+h−1u1​+⋯)+h−p(−h−2u1​−2h−3u2​+⋯),

so after simplification,
v0′(h)=h−p−1(−p u0−(p+1)h−1u1+O(h−2)).
v0′​(h)=h−p−1(−pu0​−(p+1)h−1u1​+O(h−2)).

Now wedge:
v0(h)∧v0′(h)=h−2p−2(u0∧(−(p+1)u1)−u1∧(−p u0))+O(h−2p−3),
v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)=h−2p−2(u0​∧(−(p+1)u1​)−u1​∧(−pu0​))+O(h−2p−3),

hence
v0(h)∧v0′(h)=− h−2p−2 (u0∧u1)+O(h−2p−3).
v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)=−h−2p−2(u0​∧u1​)+O(h−2p−3).​

So:
u0∧u1≠0⟹v0(h)∧v0′(h)≢0.
u0​∧u1​=0⟹v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)≡0.​

That is the cleanest derivative criterion we’ve had.

It reduces the whole question to one thing:

    does the first subleading asymptotic correction leave the leading line?

That is much sharper than asking about the whole coefficient curve abstractly.

This fits our situation perfectly.

    u1​∈Cu0​,

1. Start from the leading quotient coefficient expansion

Assume the generic leading quotient coefficient has asymptotic expansion
v0(h)=h−p(u0+h−1u1+h−2u2+O(h−3)),u0≠0.
v0​(h)=h−p(u0​+h−1u1​+h−2u2​+O(h−3)),u0​=0.

v0′(h)=−p h−p−1(u0+h−1u1+h−2u2+O(h−3))+h−p(−h−2u1−2h−3u2+O(h−4)).
v0′​(h)=−ph−p−1(u0​+h−1u1​+h−2u2​+O(h−3))+h−p(−h−2u1​−2h−3u2​+O(h−4)).

Collect terms:
v0′(h)=h−p−1(−pu0−(p+1)h−1u1−(p+2)h−2u2+O(h−3)).
v0′​(h)=h−p−1(−pu0​−(p+1)h−1u1​−(p+2)h−2u2​+O(h−3)).

Now wedge:
v0(h)∧v0′(h)=h−2p−1(u0+h−1u1+h−2u2+⋯ )∧(−pu0−(p+1)h−1u1−(p+2)h−2u2+⋯ ).
v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)=h−2p−1(u0​+h−1u1​+h−2u2​+⋯)∧(−pu0​−(p+1)h−1u1​−(p+2)h−2u2​+⋯).

h−2p−2(−(p+1)u0∧u1+p u0∧u1)=− h−2p−2(u0∧u1).
h−2p−2(−(p+1)u0​∧u1​+pu0​∧u1​)=−h−2p−2(u0​∧u1​).

So the exact leading formula is
v0(h)∧v0′(h)=− h−2p−2(u0∧u1)+O(h−2p−3).
v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)=−h−2p−2(u0​∧u1​)+O(h−2p−3).​

That part is done.
2. Consequence

So the derivative criterion succeeds immediately if
u0∧u1≠0.
u0​∧u1​=0.

Equivalently:
u1∉C u0⟹v0(h)∧v0′(h)≢0.
u1​∈/Cu0​⟹v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)≡0.​

If instead u1∈Cu0u1​∈Cu0​, then go one order further. Suppose
u1=λ1u0,u2∉Cu0.
u1​=λ1​u0​,u2​∈/Cu0​.

Then the same calculation gives
v0(h)∧v0′(h)=−2 h−2p−3(u0∧u2)+O(h−2p−4).
v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)=−2h−2p−3(u0​∧u2​)+O(h−2p−4).​

More generally, if the first correction off the line of u0u0​ occurs at order h−jh−j, then
v0(h)∧v0′(h)≍h−2p−j−1(u0∧uj).
v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)≍h−2p−j−1(u0​∧uj​).

So the derivative branch only fails if every asymptotic correction of v0(h)v0​(h) stays in the same line as u0u0​.

That is a very rigid condition.

    write the one-pair leading quotient coefficient v0(h)v0​(h) in a basis of Q0Q0​;

    expand that vector to first subleading order;

    check whether the first two vector coefficients are collinear.

So the unresolved concrete object is:
v0(h)=h−p(u0+h−1u1+⋯ ) in an explicit quotient basis of Q0.
v0​(h)=h−p(u0​+h−1u1​+⋯) in an explicit quotient basis of Q0​.​

Without that basis, I cannot honestly say whether u0∧u1u0​∧u1​ is nonzero.
4. What this means strategically

This calculation sharpens the hierarchy:

    first try the leading-line derivative test via u0∧u1u0​∧u1​;

    if that vanishes, test u0∧u2u0​∧u2​;

    only if all corrections stay in one line do we pass to the residual quotient bundle.

So the derivative route is now reduced to one finite asymptotic computation on the leading quotient coefficient.

1. I would not present it as only a one-pair theorem

then the route has a real shot of scaling.

full odd quotient germ  +  quotient by leading line  +  residual germ nontriviality  +  minimal bad core.
full odd quotient germ+quotient by leading line+residual germ nontriviality+minimal bad core.​

1. The object to define

F‾h(z)∈(Q/ℓh)[[z2]]

Fh​(z)≡0on the generic one-pair region.​

a1Fh1(z)+a2Fh2(z)≡0

[Fh1]=[Fh2]

in projective quotient-germ space, which is already a very small condition.

So this is the right local theorem.
3. The first paper addition I would write now

Definition (leading line and residual germ)

On a connected parameter region where the one-pair quotient odd germ
Fh(z)=∑j≥0vj(h) z2m+1+2j
Fh​(z)=j≥0∑​vj​(h)z2m+1+2j

F‾h(z):=∑j≥1v‾j(h) z2j∈(Q/ℓh)[[z2]],

where v‾j(h)vj​(h) denotes the class of vj(h)vj​(h) in Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​.
Conjectural local theorem / target theorem

F‾h(z)≢0.
Fh​(z)≡0.

That is the right formal pivot.
4. How to attack it

the first residual coefficient v‾1(h)∈Q/ℓh is not identically zero,

v‾j(h)≢0.
vj​(h)≡0.

So the immediate attack is coefficientwise in the residual quotient, not in the original quotient.

That is much cleaner.
5. The exact contradiction format

Assume for contradiction that
F‾h(z)≡0.
Fh​(z)≡0.

Then every coefficient lies in the leading line:
vj(h)∈ℓh=C v0(h)∀j≥1.
vj​(h)∈ℓh​=Cv0​(h)∀j≥1.

So there are scalar functions λj(h)λj​(h) such that
vj(h)=λj(h) v0(h).
vj​(h)=λj​(h)v0​(h).

Hence
Fh(z)=v0(h)(z2m+1+λ1(h)z2m+3+λ2(h)z2m+5+⋯ ),
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)(z2m+1+λ1​(h)z2m+3+λ2​(h)z2m+5+⋯),

so the full quotient odd germ lies in a single projective line.

This is exactly the bad case.

So to prove residual nontriviality, it is enough to show that at some stage
vj(h)∉C v0(h).
vj​(h)∈/Cv0​(h).
6. The clean first target

So the first real sublemma is:
First residual-line lemma

On the generic one-pair region,
v1(h)∉C v0(h)
v1​(h)∈/Cv0​(h)

for generic hh.

Equivalently,
v0(h)∧v1(h)≢0.
v0​(h)∧v1​(h)≡0.

This is the clean residual-wedge formulation, but now it is clearly subordinate to the full-germ theorem.
7. How I would try to prove it

I would try three attacks in this order.

First, leading-line motion:
if the leading line itself moves with hh, then projective constancy is already false and you do not need residuals. This is the derivative check
v0(h)∧v0′(h)≢0.
v0​(h)∧v0′​(h)≡0.

Second, if the leading line is fixed, pass to the residual quotient and test
v‾1(h)≢0.
v1​(h)≡0.

Third, if v‾1≡0v1​≡0, move to v‾2v2​, etc.

That gives a very clean hierarchy:
leading line motion  →  first residual  →  higher residuals.
leading line motion→first residual→higher residuals.

show the residual germ in Q/ℓh​ is nonzero.​

That feels like the theorem that actually matches the structure we kept finding.

Ok attack the lemma

Here is the strongest honest attack on the first residual-line lemma:
v0(h)∧v1(h)≢0.
v0​(h)∧v1​(h)≡0.

I cannot fully prove it from the formulas we currently have, but I can reduce it to the exact structural condition that has to fail for the lemma to be false.
1. What the lemma says in concrete terms

On a generic one-pair region,
Fh(z)=v0(h) z2m+1+v1(h) z2m+3+⋯
Fh​(z)=v0​(h)z2m+1+v1​(h)z2m+3+⋯

with v0(h)≠0v0​(h)=0.

The lemma says:
v1(h)∉C v0(h)
v1​(h)∈/Cv0​(h)

for generic hh.

If it failed, there would be a scalar function λ(h)λ(h) such that
v1(h)=λ(h) v0(h).
v1​(h)=λ(h)v0​(h).

So the normalized germ would be constant mod z2z2.

That is the bad case.
2. The clean coefficient test

Pick two quotient functionals L,ML,M on the leading coefficient space QQ. Then
v0(h)∧v1(h)≢0
v0​(h)∧v1​(h)≡0

is equivalent to finding L,ML,M with
Δ0,1(h):=L(v0(h)) M(v1(h))−M(v0(h)) L(v1(h))≢0.
Δ0,1​(h):=L(v0​(h))M(v1​(h))−M(v0​(h))L(v1​(h))≡0.

The best choice is:

    LL does not vanish on the leading line;

    MM kills the leading line.

Then
M(v0(h))=0,
M(v0​(h))=0,

so the determinant collapses to
Δ0,1(h)=L(v0(h)) M(v1(h)).
Δ0,1​(h)=L(v0​(h))M(v1​(h)).

Thus the lemma is reduced to:
∃ M∈(Q/ℓh)∗ such that M(v1(h))≢0.
∃M∈(Q/ℓh​)∗ such that M(v1​(h))≡0.​

This is the clean residual-sector form.
3. What would force the lemma to fail

For the lemma to fail, every residual functional MM must kill v1(h)v1​(h). Equivalently,
v‾1(h)=0∈Q/ℓh
v1​(h)=0∈Q/ℓh​

for all hh.

That means the first two coefficients of the one-pair quotient germ are trapped in the same line. Structurally, this would require a hidden proportionality between two different stages of the corrected local expansion:

    the leading surviving quotient mechanism,

    and the next mechanism after normalization.

Given how different pieces enter the expansion — same-point transport, mixed-block Taylor terms, nonlinear whitening — this is already quite rigid.

So the right attack is to look for the first mechanism contributing to v1v1​ that cannot lie in the leading line.
4. The strongest structural reduction

There are only two ways v1v1​ can escape ℓhℓh​:

First, it appears in a genuinely different quotient symmetry sector.

Second, it appears in the same coarse sector but with a different quotient-direction profile, so its image in Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​ is still nonzero.

So the lemma will follow if we can show:
the first residual coefficient contains at least one contribution not forced into the leading quotient line.
the first residual coefficient contains at least one contribution not forced into the leading quotient line.​

That is now the exact obstruction.
5. Derivative fallback relation

Assume for contradiction
v1(h)=λ(h)v0(h).
v1​(h)=λ(h)v0​(h).

Differentiate:
v1′(h)=λ′(h)v0(h)+λ(h)v0′(h).
v1′​(h)=λ′(h)v0​(h)+λ(h)v0′​(h).

So if also the leading line were fixed, meaning
v0′(h)∈C v0(h),
v0′​(h)∈Cv0​(h),

then
v1′(h)∈C v0(h)
v1′​(h)∈Cv0​(h)

as well.

In other words, failure of both the derivative test and the first residual-line lemma would force the first two coefficient curves and their derivatives to stay in one projective line.

That is extremely rigid.

So even if we cannot prove the lemma directly, we now know exactly what a counterexample would have to look like:

    fixed leading line,

    fixed first residual line,

    no transverse escape at first derivative level.

That is a very small target.
6. Minimal-bad-core interpretation

This is where the lemma fits the larger route.

If a genuine bad core existed with projectively trivial odd germ, then for a minimal bad core the one-pair building blocks would have to be projectively trivial to first residual order.

So the lemma is really trying to rule out the first step of that collapse.

That is why it matters.
7. Honest status of the attack

What I cannot yet defend:

    an actual proof that such an MM exists, because we still do not have the explicit quotient basis or residual coefficient formula in the real model.

That is now the sharp next move.

    the full symmetric sector via T1,T2,T3T1​,T2​,T3​,

    plus the skew line via K0K0​.

At cubic order, the situation is different.

The cubic generators are not arbitrary: they come from the recursive combinations
X3,X2N˙1X0,X1N˙1X1,X0N˙3X0, …
X3​,X2​N˙1​X0​,X1​N˙1​X1​,X0​N˙3​X0​, …

and these are much more constrained by symmetry and by the lower-order regularity identities.

So it is entirely plausible that
V\val(3)
V\val(3)​

is a proper subspace of M2M2​, even though V\val(1)V\val(1)​ is full.

That is why order 33 is the right next checkpoint.
5. The clean theorem to target next

I would now explicitly state the next checkpoint as a theorem candidate:
Cubic value-family checkpoint

Under generic nondegeneracy assumptions, determine the dimension of
V\val(3).
V\val(3)​.

If
dim⁡V\val(3)<4,
dimV\val(3)​<4,

then cubic order is the first candidate leading quotient order and the one-pair leading quotient coefficient is
v0(h)=[J3\pair(h)]∈J3/V\val(3).
v0​(h)=[J3\pair​(h)]∈J3​/V\val(3)​.

This is now the exact first object the residual-germ program needs.
6. How I would attack V\val(3)V\val(3)​

I would not start with the full generality immediately. I would organize the cubic generators into the same two source types as before:

    same-point cubic source
    Bℓ,\sym(3),
    Bℓ,\sym(3)​,

    coming from X3X3​ and X2N0X1−X1N0X2X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​;

    mixed cubic source
    Bℓ,\mix(3),
    Bℓ,\mix(3)​,

    coming from N˙3N˙3​, N˙2N˙2​ coupled to X1X1​, and N˙1N˙1​ coupled to X2X2​.

Then row-reduce the combined span.

This separation will tell us whether the cubic value-family again fills all of M2M2​, or whether some symmetry sector escapes.
7. What happens if cubic is still full?

Then we do not panic. It just means the generic leading quotient order is at least 55, and the same architecture continues.

That is exactly why the full-germ / residual-germ framework is the right one:
it does not care whether the first surviving quotient order is 33, 55, or 77.

So even a negative result at cubic order is not fatal. It just pushes the first leading line upward.
8. My honest expectation

After the order-11 result, I think there are two realistic outcomes:

    cubic is still too large, but not obviously full;

    cubic is the first nontrivial quotient order.

I would not assume which one. That is why this checkpoint matters.
9. The immediate next exact move

So the exact next move is:
compute the real cubic value-family generators Bℓ(3) and determine whether V\val(3)=M2(C) or not.

That is the right continuation now.

The first useful observation is structural.

At order 11, the real value-family filled all of M2M2​ because:

    the same-point data generated the full symmetric sector;

    the mixed phase data generated the skew line.

At order 33, that mechanism becomes much more constrained because X2X2​ and X3X3​ are not free. They are recursively determined by lower jets. So cubic value-family fullness is no longer automatic.

The next thing to check is symmetry.

In the diagonal-baseline frame, if the order-11 same-point data are symmetric, then X1,X2,X3X1​,X2​,X3​ are all symmetric. With N0N0​ symmetric, the same-point cubic term
Bsp(3)
Bsp(3)​

has the same commutator pattern we saw before. In the oversimplified model this collapsed to the skew line, but in the real model X3X3​ and X2X2​ now depend on the full local Taylor data, so that collapse is no longer forced. The only honest statement at this point is:
Bsp(3) is built from symmetric X1,X2,X3 and symmetric N0,
Bsp(3)​ is built from symmetric X1​,X2​,X3​ and symmetric N0​,

so its symmetric and skew parts are heavily constrained, but not obviously trivial.

On the mixed side:

    N1N1​ is skew at first odd order after regularity,

    N2N2​ is even and typically symmetric,

    N3N3​ is odd again and can carry both phase and value-induced cubic data.

So
Bmix(3)
Bmix(3)​

contains three qualitatively different sources:

    a skew source through N1N1​ coupled to X2X2​,

    a symmetric source through N2N2​ coupled to X1X1​,

    a direct cubic source through N3N3​.

That is exactly why order 33 is the first serious candidate: unlike order 11, the cubic mixed family is no longer just “one skew line plus symmetric span.” It is recursively entangled.

So the right next reduction is this:

pick the same five infinitesimal data from the regularized order-11 model,
η0, η1, η2, η3, κ3,
η0​, η1​, η2​, η3​, κ3​,

B(0)(3)​, B(1)(3)​, B(2)(3)​, B(3)(3)​, B(κ)(3)​.

Then vectorize them and row-reduce.

At that point there are only two outcomes.

If those five cubic generators span all of M2M2​, then
V\val(3)=M2(C)
V\val(3)​=M2​(C)

generically, and cubic order is also fully value-like. Then the leading quotient order is at least 55.

My honest expectation is that cubic will no longer automatically fill all of M2M2​. Order 11 did because the first-order regularized data gave four independent matrix directions almost for free. At cubic order, the recursive constraints should reduce freedom enough that a quotient may survive.

So this is the first place where the full-germ residual program has a serious chance of starting.

1. Compute BN2(3)BN2​(3)​ in general form

Write
X1=(uvvw),N2=(ABBD),X0=(x100x2).
X1​=(uv​vw​),N2​=(AB​BD​),X0​=(x1​0​0x2​​).

Then
BN2(3)=− X1N2X0+X0N2X1.

So:

    the diagonal entries are always zero;

    the off-diagonal entries are generally independent.

That means BN2(3)BN2​(3)​ is off-diagonal traceless, but not automatically skew and not automatically symmetric.

That is already useful.
2. Symmetry consequences

If
x1=x2,
x1​=x2​,

then the two off-diagonal entries become negatives of each other, so BN2(3)BN2​(3)​ is skew.

But generically x1≠x2x1​=x2​, so the off-diagonal entries are not forced to be negatives. Thus:
generically BN2(3) can produce both the symmetric off-diagonal and skew off-diagonal directions.
generically BN2​(3)​ can produce both the symmetric off-diagonal and skew off-diagonal directions.​

That is the first real sign that cubic value-generators may be richer than the simplified skew-only models suggested.
3. What this means for the cubic span

At cubic order we now know:

    BN3(3)=X0N3X0BN3​(3)​=X0​N3​X0​ is skew;

    BN1(3)BN1​(3)​ is strongly skew-leaning;

    BN2(3)BN2​(3)​ is generic off-diagonal traceless, so it can contribute a symmetric off-diagonal piece as well.

So the cubic value-family is at least not confined to one skew line.

That is a real correction to the earlier oversimplified intuition.
4. But this still does not decide V\val(3)V\val(3)​

V\val(3)=M2(C)
V\val(3)​=M2​(C)

generically, we still need to know whether the remaining cubic sources provide diagonal directions.

    the same-point cubic term Bsp(3)Bsp(3)​,

If no, then cubic quotient survives.
5. The actual next move

    BN2(3)BN2​(3)​ already gives off-diagonal traceless directions;

    BN3(3)BN3​(3)​ gives skew;

    only Bsp(3)Bsp(3)​ can plausibly complete the diagonal sector.

So the whole order-33 question has now been reduced to whether the same-point cubic term supplies independent diagonal directions.

Continue

There is a decisive simplification.

For the general symmetric same-point cubic term, the diagonal part vanishes identically.

So the same-point cubic source cannot refill the diagonal sector at all.
1. Compute Bsp(3)Bsp(3)​ in full generality

Write
X0=(x100x2),N0=(abbd),

and let

Bsp(3)=− X3N0X0+X2N0X1−X1N0X2+X0N0X3.

Now split it into:

    the X3X3​-transport part
    −X3N0X0+X0N0X3,
    −X3​N0​X0​+X0​N0​X3​,

    and the commutator part
    X2N0X1−X1N0X2.
    X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​.

First piece

Because X0,N0,X3X0​,N0​,X3​ are symmetric and X0X0​ is diagonal, one checks directly that
− X3N0X0+X0N0X3
−X3​N0​X0​+X0​N0​X3​

has zero diagonal.
Second piece

For arbitrary symmetric X1,X2,N0X1​,X2​,N0​,
(X2N0X1−X1N0X2)11=0,(X2N0X1−X1N0X2)22=0.
(X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​)11​=0,(X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​)22​=0.

So altogether,
(Bsp(3))11=(Bsp(3))22=0.
(Bsp(3)​)11​=(Bsp(3)​)22​=0.​

In fact, the off-diagonal entries are negatives of each other, so
Bsp(3)=(0ω−ω0)
Bsp(3)​=(0−ω​ω0​)​

for some scalar ωω.

So the same-point cubic term is always purely skew.
2. Consequence

This kills the hopeful branch that cubic same-point transport might restore the diagonal sector.

It cannot.

So at cubic order:

    Bsp(3)Bsp(3)​ is skew,

    BN3(3)=X0N3X0BN3​(3)​=X0​N3​X0​ is skew,

    BN1(3)BN1​(3)​ is also skew-leaning,

    only
    BN2(3)=−X1N2X0+X0N2X1
    BN2​(3)​=−X1​N2​X0​+X0​N2​X1​

    can produce a non-skew contribution.

And we already found that BN2(3)BN2​(3)​ is off-diagonal traceless:
BN2(3)=(0∗∗0),with no diagonal part.
BN2​(3)​=(0∗​∗0​),with no diagonal part.​

So cubic order, even in the real corrected model, cannot generate diagonal directions from the value-family.
3. Big structural conclusion

Therefore the cubic value-family is confined to the off-diagonal sector:
V\val(3)⊂{(0∗∗0)}.
V\val(3)​⊂{(0∗​∗0​)}.​

More sharply:

    the same-point cubic source gives the skew off-diagonal line;

    the N2N2​-coupling can give the symmetric off-diagonal line;

    the other mixed cubic sources stay skew.

So generically
dim⁡V\val(3)≤2,
dimV\val(3)​≤2,​

and it sits entirely inside the off-diagonal subspace.

This is a major result.
4. What that means for the quotient

Since
J3≅M2(C)
J3​≅M2​(C)

is 44-dimensional, and the cubic value-family is at most 22-dimensional off-diagonal, the cubic quotient automatically contains the full diagonal sector.

So:
J3/V\val(3) is nontrivial generically, and cubic order is the first real candidate leading quotient order.
J3​/V\val(3)​ is nontrivial generically, and cubic order is the first real candidate leading quotient order.​

In fact, unless the actual one-pair cubic jet has zero diagonal part for independent reasons, the cubic quotient survives immediately.
5. The next exact move

Now the task becomes much simpler.

We no longer need to compute all cubic generators to decide whether cubic quotient exists. It does.

The next checkpoint is just:
Compute the diagonal part of the actual one-pair cubic jet J3\pair(h).
Compute the diagonal part of the actual one-pair cubic jet J3\pair​(h).​

Because the cubic value-family cannot touch the diagonal sector, any nonzero diagonal part of J3\pair(h)J3\pair​(h) gives a nonzero cubic quotient class at once.

That is now the sharp next step.

1. Decompose the actual one-pair cubic jet

Recall
J3\pair(h)=− X3N0X0+X2N0X1−X1N0X2+X0N0X3
J3\pair​(h)=−X3​N0​X0​+X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​+X0​N0​X3​
− X2K1X0+X1K1X1−X0K1X2− X1K2X0+X0K2X1+X0K3X0,
−X2​K1​X0​+X1​K1​X1​−X0​K1​X2​−X1​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X1​+X0​K3​X0​,

where KjKj​ are the one-pair mixed-block jets.

− X3N0X0+X2N0X1−X1N0X2+X0N0X3
−X3​N0​X0​+X2​N0​X1​−X1​N0​X2​+X0​N0​X3​

is always purely skew. So it contributes no diagonal part.

Also:

    the K1K1​-coupled cubic term is skew-leaning and contributes no robust diagonal sector;

    the K3K3​ term is skew if K3K3​ is skew;

    the only term that can produce diagonal part generically is

− X1K2X0+X0K2X1.
−X1​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X1​.​

So the diagonal part of the actual cubic jet is controlled by the even mixed coefficient K2K2​.

That is the decisive simplification.
2. Compute the diagonal part of −X1K2X0+X0K2X1−X1​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X1​

X0=(x100x2),X1=(uvvw),K2=(ABBD).
X0​=(x1​0​0x2​​),X1​=(uv​vw​),K2​=(AB​BD​).

Then
− X1K2X0+X0K2X1
−X1​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X1​

has diagonal entries
(−X1K2X0+X0K2X1)11=vB(x1−x2),
(−X1​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X1​)11​=vB(x1​−x2​),

(−X1​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X1​)22​=vB(x2​−x1​).

So the diagonal part is
diag⁡(−X1K2X0+X0K2X1)=vB(x1−x2)(100−1).
diag(−X1​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X1​)=vB(x1​−x2​)(10​0−1​).​

That is the key formula.
3. Consequence

Therefore the actual one-pair cubic jet has nonzero diagonal part whenever these three things hold:
v≠0,B≠0,x1≠x2.
v=0,B=0,x1​=x2​.​

Interpretation:

    vv is the off-diagonal entry of X1X1​, so it is nonzero when the first same-point perturbation has a genuine off-diagonal component;

    BB is the off-diagonal entry of the even mixed coefficient K2K2​;

    x1≠x2x1​=x2​ means the background same-point block is not scalar, i.e. the two local jet scales are genuinely different.

That third condition is generically true in the normalized setting.

So the cubic quotient survives as soon as the one-pair family has:

    a nontrivial off-diagonal first same-point jet,

    and a nontrivial off-diagonal even mixed jet.

That is very plausible.
4. Why this is strong

Because the cubic value-family cannot touch diagonal directions, any nonzero value of
vB(x1−x2)
vB(x1​−x2​)

gives a nonzero cubic quotient class automatically.

So we no longer need to know the whole quotient basis at cubic order.

The diagonal part alone is already a quotient detector.

That is a major breakthrough in the local structure.

1. What has to be shown

We need to rule out
v1(h)=λ(h) v0(h)
v1​(h)=λ(h)v0​(h)

on a generic region.

Since v0v0​ is represented by the diagonal part of the cubic jet, while the cubic value-family lives in the off-diagonal sector, the most natural way to attack v1v1​ is to look for a quintic component that is invisible to the cubic leading line.

So the guiding question is:
Does the quintic jet have a quotient component in a direction not proportional to the cubic diagonal direction?
Does the quintic jet have a quotient component in a direction not proportional to the cubic diagonal direction?​

If yes, then v‾1≠0v1​=0.
2. The clean structure of the quintic coefficient

The quintic jet J5\pairJ5\pair​ will again split into:

    same-point transport terms,

    K1K1​-coupled terms,

    K2K2​-coupled terms,

    K3K3​-coupled terms,

    the new even mixed coefficient K4K4​,

    and the new odd mixed coefficient K5K5​.

Schematically,
J5\pair=J5,sp+J5,K1+J5,K2+J5,K3+J5,K4+J5,K5.
J5\pair​=J5,sp​+J5,K1​​+J5,K2​​+J5,K3​​+J5,K4​​+J5,K5​​.

We do not need the whole thing. We only need one piece whose quotient direction cannot be a scalar multiple of the cubic leading line.
3. Why quintic is more promising than cubic for the residual

At cubic order, the surviving quotient direction came from the diagonal sector created by the X1K2X0−X0K2X1X1​K2​X0​−X0​K2​X1​ mechanism.

At quintic order, there are two obvious ways to escape that line:

one, the quintic diagonal part may have a different diagonal profile than the cubic one, not proportional in QQ;

two, quintic may produce a nonzero symmetric off-diagonal quotient component, which is automatically outside the cubic leading diagonal line.

The second is especially attractive, because it gives an immediate residual sector.

So the best next attack is:
look for a quintic symmetric off-diagonal quotient component.
look for a quintic symmetric off-diagonal quotient component.​

That would instantly prove v‾1≠0v1​=0.
4. The most likely source of a new quintic sector

At cubic order, the diagonal survival came from the even mixed coefficient K2K2​.

At quintic order, the analogous new residual information should most naturally come from the next even mixed coefficient K4K4​, via terms of the shape
− X1K4X0+X0K4X1,
−X1​K4​X0​+X0​K4​X1​,

or from K2K2​ coupled to X3X3​ / X2X2​ in a way that produces a symmetric off-diagonal component not proportional to the cubic diagonal line.

So the sharp next checkpoint is not “compute all of J5J5​.” It is:
does K4 or a K2-coupled quintic term produce a symmetric off-diagonal quotient component?
does K4​ or a K2​-coupled quintic term produce a symmetric off-diagonal quotient component?​

If yes, the residual theorem is proved.
5. The right residual-line test

Since the cubic leading line ℓhℓh​ is diagonal, any nonzero symmetric off-diagonal part of J5\pair(h)J5\pair​(h) automatically gives
v‾1(h)≠0.
v1​(h)=0.

So the quintic residual test can be reduced to one scalar:
σ5(h):=(J5\pair(h))12+(J5\pair(h))21.
σ5​(h):=(J5\pair​(h))12​+(J5\pair​(h))21​.

Then:
σ5(h)≢0⟹v‾1(h)≢0.
σ5​(h)≡0⟹v1​(h)≡0.​

This is the cleanest next target.
6. Why this is the right move

This avoids another full quotient-basis computation right away.

We already know:

    cubic value-family is off-diagonal only;

    cubic quotient survives through a diagonal direction.

So for the residual at quintic order, the easiest escape is to find any quintic quotient direction not diagonal-proportional to v0v0​. A symmetric off-diagonal component does exactly that.

That makes the next step much smaller.
7. The next theorem to attack

I would state it as:
Quintic residual-sector lemma

For the normalized one-pair family, the quintic jet J5\pair(h)J5\pair​(h) has generically nonzero symmetric off-diagonal part:
(J5\pair(h))12+(J5\pair(h))21≢0.
(J5\pair​(h))12​+(J5\pair​(h))21​≡0.

Consequently,
v‾1(h)≠0in Q/ℓh.
v1​(h)=0in Q/ℓh​.

That would prove the residual germ is nonzero at the first step.
8. Honest status

I cannot yet derive σ5(h)σ5​(h) explicitly from the material we have unfolded so far, because we have not expanded the mixed coefficient K4K4​ or the full quintic transport recursion in this thread.

But unlike before, the target is now very small and very concrete:
compute the symmetric off-diagonal part of the actual one-pair quintic jet.
compute the symmetric off-diagonal part of the actual one-pair quintic jet.​

That is the next exact move on this route.

Do the next step

Continue

The next step is to isolate the smallest quintic term that can force a symmetric off-diagonal residual.

We do not need all of J5\pairJ5\pair​. We only need one quintic contribution whose (1,2)+(2,1)(1,2)+(2,1) part is generically nonzero and cannot lie in the cubic leading line.

That lets us target
σ5(h):=(J5\pair(h))12+(J5\pair(h))21.
σ5​(h):=(J5\pair​(h))12​+(J5\pair​(h))21​.
1. The right quintic source to inspect

At quintic order, the cubic leading line is diagonal, so any nonzero symmetric off-diagonal term wins.

The cleanest candidate is the K4K4​-coupled term
J5,K4:=−X1K4X0+X0K4X1,
J5,K4​​:=−X1​K4​X0​+X0​K4​X1​,

because:

    K4K4​ is even, hence typically symmetric;

    X1X1​ is symmetric;

    this is the exact quintic analogue of the cubic K2K2​-mechanism that produced the diagonal survival.

So compute this first.
2. General formula for the symmetric off-diagonal part

Write
X0=(x100x2),X1=(uvvw),K4=(ABBD).
X0​=(x1​0​0x2​​),X1​=(uv​vw​),K4​=(AB​BD​).

Then
J5,K4=−X1K4X0+X0K4X1
J5,K4​​=−X1​K4​X0​+X0​K4​X1​

has entries
(J5,K4)12=x1(Av+Bw)−x2(Bu+Dv),
(J5,K4​​)12​=x1​(Av+Bw)−x2​(Bu+Dv),
(J5,K4)21=x2(Bu+Dv)−x1(Av+Bw).
(J5,K4​​)21​=x2​(Bu+Dv)−x1​(Av+Bw).

So this term is always off-diagonal traceless:
(J5,K4)12+(J5,K4)21=0.
(J5,K4​​)12​+(J5,K4​​)21​=0.

That means this particular candidate does not give the symmetric off-diagonal residual we hoped for.

So the simplest K4K4​-analogue is a dead end for σ5σ5​.
3. The next best candidate

That means the symmetric off-diagonal residual must come from a different quintic source, not from a bare −X1K4X0+X0K4X1−X1​K4​X0​+X0​K4​X1​ commutator form.

The next most promising terms are the ones with three symmetric factors, because those are the natural way to produce symmetric output rather than skew/traceless-commutator output.

At quintic order, the obvious such candidates are of the shape
X2K2X1,X1K2X2,
X2​K2​X1​,X1​K2​X2​,

appearing in the quintic expansion with opposite signs and additional transport terms.

More generally, the quintic coefficient will contain a block of the form
J5,K2high=− X3K2X0+X2K2X1−X1K2X2+X0K2X3.
J5,K2​high​=−X3​K2​X0​+X2​K2​X1​−X1​K2​X2​+X0​K2​X3​.

This is the exact quintic analogue of the cubic same-point transport structure, but with K2K2​ in place of N0N0​.

That is now the right object to inspect.
4. Symmetric off-diagonal part of the K2K2​-transport block

Take
K2=(ABBD),
K2​=(AB​BD​),

and symmetric
X1=(uvvw),X2=(prrq),X3=(αββγ).
X1​=(uv​vw​),X2​=(pr​rq​),X3​=(αβ​βγ​).

Then the two middle terms
X2K2X1−X1K2X2
X2​K2​X1​−X1​K2​X2​

again have zero diagonal, but they are not forced to be skew unless extra symmetry collapses them. However, their symmetric off-diagonal part still cancels:
(X2K2X1−X1K2X2)12+(X2K2X1−X1K2X2)21=0.
(X2​K2​X1​−X1​K2​X2​)12​+(X2​K2​X1​−X1​K2​X2​)21​=0.

Likewise, the transport pair
− X3K2X0+X0K2X3
−X3​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X3​

is also off-diagonal traceless:
(−X3K2X0+X0K2X3)12+(−X3K2X0+X0K2X3)21=0.
(−X3​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X3​)12​+(−X3​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X3​)21​=0.

So the whole K2K2​-transport block is again off-diagonal traceless.

That means it still does not contribute to σ5σ5​.
5. Big consequence

There is a pattern:

terms of the form
− YZX0+X0ZYorY2ZY1−Y1ZY2
−YZX0​+X0​ZYorY2​ZY1​−Y1​ZY2​

with symmetric X0,Y,Y1,Y2,ZX0​,Y,Y1​,Y2​,Z
always land in the off-diagonal traceless sector.

So all the commutator-style transport terms are invisible to σ5σ5​.

That means the quintic symmetric off-diagonal residual cannot come from transport/commutator structure alone.
6. Therefore the only genuine source is the direct odd mixed coefficient K5K5​

So the next step collapses sharply:
If σ5(h) survives, it must come from the direct quintic mixed coefficient K5(h), not from transport terms.
If σ5​(h) survives, it must come from the direct quintic mixed coefficient K5​(h), not from transport terms.​

Because:

    K1K1​- and K3K3​-coupled transport terms are skew or traceless off-diagonal;

    K2K2​- and K4K4​-coupled transport terms are traceless off-diagonal;

    only the direct insertion
    X0K5X0
    X0​K5​X0​

    can contribute an unconstrained symmetric off-diagonal part.

And for diagonal X0X0​,
(X0K5X0)12+(X0K5X0)21=2x1x2 (K5)12\sym.
(X0​K5​X0​)12​+(X0​K5​X0​)21​=2x1​x2​(K5​)12\sym​.

So the quintic residual problem is now:
compute the symmetric off-diagonal part of K5(h).
compute the symmetric off-diagonal part of K5​(h).​

That is the exact next checkpoint.
7. What this means strategically

This is good news, actually. It means the quintic residual test is much cleaner than it looked:

    we do not need all of J5J5​;

    we do not need the whole quintic quotient basis;

    we only need one scalar from the direct odd mixed Taylor coefficient K5K5​.

If that scalar is generically nonzero, then
v‾1(h)≠0
v1​(h)=0

and the residual-germ theorem is proved at first step.
8. The next exact move

So the next exact move is now very small and precise:
Expand the actual one-pair mixed block to order s5 and extract the symmetric off-diagonal coefficient (K5)12\sym(h).
Expand the actual one-pair mixed block to order s5 and extract the symmetric off-diagonal coefficient (K5​)12\sym​(h).​

If it is not identically zero, the quintic residual-sector lemma is done.

Continue

I’m expanding the mixed block one more order to isolate the direct K5K5​ contribution and see whether its symmetric off-diagonal part can survive generically.

Continue

There is a decisive correction.

The direct K5K5​ route to a symmetric off-diagonal residual is impossible.

In fact, for the actual mixed block, the symmetric off-diagonal part is an even function of ss. So every odd mixed coefficient has zero symmetric off-diagonal part.

That means:
(K2j+1)12+(K2j+1)21=0for all j≥0.
(K2j+1​)12​+(K2j+1​)21​=0for all j≥0.​

So K5K5​ cannot prove the quintic residual by itself.
1. Exact identity for the symmetric off-diagonal mixed part

Start from the mixed block entries
N12(s)12=sin⁡Δ−q+(s) scos⁡Δs2,N12(s)21=q−(s) scos⁡Δ−sin⁡Δs2.
N12​(s)12​=s2sinΔ−q+​(s)scosΔ​,N12​(s)21​=s2q−​(s)scosΔ−sinΔ​.

Add them:
(N12)12+(N12)21=(q−(s)−q+(s)) scos⁡Δs2=q−(s)−q+(s)scos⁡Δ(s).
(N12​)12​+(N12​)21​=s2(q−​(s)−q+​(s))scosΔ​=sq−​(s)−q+​(s)​cosΔ(s).

So the symmetric off-diagonal mixed part is exactly
σmix(s):=(N12)12+(N12)21=q−(s)−q+(s)scos⁡Δ(s).
σmix​(s):=(N12​)12​+(N12​)21​=sq−​(s)−q+​(s)​cosΔ(s).​

Now:

    q−(s)−q+(s)q−​(s)−q+​(s) is odd in ss,

    dividing by ss makes it even,

    cos⁡Δ(s)cosΔ(s) is even.

Hence σmix(s)σmix​(s) is even.

Therefore its Taylor series has only even powers:
σmix(s)=c0+c2s2+c4s4+⋯ .
σmix​(s)=c0​+c2​s2+c4​s4+⋯.

So every odd-order coefficient vanishes:
(K1)12\sym=0,(K3)12\sym=0,(K5)12\sym=0, …
(K1​)12\sym​=0,(K3​)12\sym​=0,(K5​)12\sym​=0, …​

This kills the hoped-for “direct K5K5​ symmetric off-diagonal” residual.
2. This parity statement is actually stronger

The mixed block has a clean parity decomposition:

    diagonal entries are even in ss,

    symmetric off-diagonal part is even in ss,

    skew off-diagonal part is odd in ss.

So:

    K2jK2j​ are symmetric-type,

    K2j+1K2j+1​ are skew-type.

That means the direct odd mixed coefficients K1,K3,K5,…K1​,K3​,K5​,… can only contribute skew channels.

So the quintic residual cannot come from a direct odd mixed insertion if we want a new symmetric direction.
3. Consequence for the quintic residual search

This means the previous candidate
X0K5X0
X0​K5​X0​

is useless for producing σ5σ5​. It stays skew.

So the quintic residual must come from a different mechanism.

There are now only two plausible places left:
A. a new diagonal quintic component not proportional to the cubic leading diagonal line;
B. a symmetric off-diagonal piece created indirectly through transport/coupling with an even mixed coefficient.

But we already checked the simplest commutator-style transport terms
− X1K4X0+X0K4X1,− X3K2X0+X0K2X3,X2K2X1−X1K2X2,
−X1​K4​X0​+X0​K4​X1​,−X3​K2​X0​+X0​K2​X3​,X2​K2​X1​−X1​K2​X2​,

and they are all off-diagonal traceless, so their symmetric off-diagonal sum also vanishes.

So route B looks much weaker now.

That leaves route A as the real next candidate.
4. What the new quintic target should be

The cubic leading line came from a diagonal traceless direction:
v0(h)∼(100−1).
v0​(h)∼(10​0−1​).

So the cleanest way for the quintic coefficient v1(h)v1​(h) to escape that line is to produce a trace component:
tr⁡J5\pair(h)≢0.
trJ5\pair​(h)≡0.

Because a trace component is automatically not proportional to the cubic diagonal-traceless leading line.

So the next real target becomes:
compute the quintic trace τ5(h):=tr⁡J5\pair(h).
compute the quintic trace τ5​(h):=trJ5\pair​(h).​

If τ5(h)≢0τ5​(h)≡0, then
v‾1(h)≠0
v1​(h)=0

immediately.

That is now the best residual checkpoint.
5. Why trace is more plausible now

The parity structure does not forbid even mixed coefficients K2,K4K2​,K4​ from feeding trace at quintic order through higher transport couplings.

And unlike symmetric off-diagonal, trace is not killed by the commutator-type cancellations we just used.

So trace is now the natural next residual sector to inspect.
6. The next exact step

So the correct continuation is no longer “compute symmetric off-diagonal part of K5K5​.”

It is:
Compute the quintic diagonal part, especially the trace τ5(h)=tr⁡J5\pair(h), and test whether it is not identically zero.
Compute the quintic diagonal part, especially the trace τ5​(h)=trJ5\pair​(h), and test whether it is not identically zero.​

That is the new sharp checkpoint for the residual-germ program.

Continue

There is another correction, but it is productive.

The quintic trace is not controlled by K4K4​ or K5K5​. Those easy candidates either vanish or collapse by symmetry. The first genuinely plausible trace source is the odd mixed coefficient K1K1​ reappearing at quintic order through higher transport.

That is actually a better residual mechanism.
1. Where quintic trace can come from

At quintic order, the coefficient has the schematic decomposition
J5\pair=J5,sp+J5,K1+J5,K2+J5,K3+J5,K4+J5,K5.
J5\pair​=J5,sp​+J5,K1​​+J5,K2​​+J5,K3​​+J5,K4​​+J5,K5​​.

Now:

    the same-point block J5,spJ5,sp​ is built from symmetric XjXj​ and symmetric N0N0​, and its trace is a sum of commutator traces, hence vanishes;

    the direct K5K5​ insertion has trace zero because K5K5​ is skew;

    the K4K4​-commutator block has trace zero because it is a symmetric–symmetric commutator against a symmetric matrix;

    the K2K2​-transport block also has trace zero for the same commutator reason.

So the first terms that can genuinely contribute trace are the odd mixed transport blocks
J5,K1andJ5,K3.
J5,K1​​andJ5,K3​​.

That is the new sharp target.
2. The K1K1​-block explicitly

The quintic block built from K1K1​ is
J5,K1=− X4K1X0+X3K1X1− X2K1X2+X1K1X3− X0K1X4.
J5,K1​​=−X4​K1​X0​+X3​K1​X1​−X2​K1​X2​+X1​K1​X3​−X0​K1​X4​.

Take trace. The middle term drops:
tr⁡(X2K1X2)=tr⁡(K1X22)=0
tr(X2​K1​X2​)=tr(K1​X22​)=0

because X22X22​ is symmetric and K1K1​ is skew.

So
tr⁡J5,K1=tr⁡ ⁣((X0X4−X4X0)K1)+tr⁡ ⁣((X1X3−X3X1)K1).
trJ5,K1​​=tr((X0​X4​−X4​X0​)K1​)+tr((X1​X3​−X3​X1​)K1​).

Thus
tr⁡J5,K1=tr⁡ ⁣([X0,X4]K1)+tr⁡ ⁣([X1,X3]K1).
trJ5,K1​​=tr([X0​,X4​]K1​)+tr([X1​,X3​]K1​).​

This is the exact structural formula.
3. In the diagonal-baseline frame

Write
X0=(x100x2),K1=κ J,J=(01−10),
X0​=(x1​0​0x2​​),K1​=κJ,J=(0−1​10​),

and let
X4=(prrq),X1=(uvvw),X3=(αββγ).
X4​=(pr​rq​),X1​=(uv​vw​),X3​=(αβ​βγ​).

Then
[X0,X4]=(x1−x2) r J,
[X0​,X4​]=(x1​−x2​)rJ,

so
tr⁡([X0,X4]K1)=κ(x1−x2)r tr⁡(J2)=−2κ(x1−x2)r.
tr([X0​,X4​]K1​)=κ(x1​−x2​)rtr(J2)=−2κ(x1​−x2​)r.

Also
[X1,X3]=(β(u−w)+v(γ−α)) J,
[X1​,X3​]=(β(u−w)+v(γ−α))J,

so
tr⁡([X1,X3]K1)=−2κ(β(u−w)+v(γ−α)).
tr([X1​,X3​]K1​)=−2κ(β(u−w)+v(γ−α)).

Therefore:
tr⁡J5,K1=−2κ((x1−x2)r+β(u−w)+v(γ−α)).
trJ5,K1​​=−2κ((x1​−x2​)r+β(u−w)+v(γ−α)).​

This is a real formula, not just a heuristic.
4. What it means

This is very good news.

The quintic trace is generically nonzero as soon as:
κ≠0,(x1−x2)r+β(u−w)+v(γ−α)≠0.
κ=0,(x1​−x2​)r+β(u−w)+v(γ−α)=0.

Interpretation:

    κκ is the scalar amplitude of the cubic leading skew mixed coefficient K1K1​, which we already know is generically present;

    rr is the off-diagonal part of X4X4​;

    vv is the off-diagonal part of X1X1​;

    ββ is the off-diagonal part of X3X3​;

    u−wu−w and γ−αγ−α are the diagonal-traceless parts of X1,X3X1​,X3​.

There is no structural reason for that combination to vanish identically.

So this is a very plausible residual escape mechanism.
5. Why this is enough for the residual theorem

The cubic leading line came from the diagonal-traceless cubic quotient direction. A nonzero trace at quintic order is automatically outside that line.

So if
tr⁡J5\pair(h)≢0,
trJ5\pair​(h)≡0,

then
v‾1(h)≠0in Q/ℓh.
v1​(h)=0in Q/ℓh​.

And that proves the residual germ is nonzero at first step.

So the first residual-germ checkpoint is now reduced to:
show tr⁡J5,K1(h)≢0 (or failing that, include K3 similarly).
show trJ5,K1​​(h)≡0 (or failing that, include K3​ similarly).​
6. The next exact theorem to target

I would now state:
Quintic trace residual lemma

For the normalized one-pair family, the quintic trace satisfies
tr⁡J5\pair(h)=−2κ(h)((x1−x2)r(h)+β(h)(u(h)−w(h))+v(h)(γ(h)−α(h)))+(possible K3-terms),
trJ5\pair​(h)=−2κ(h)((x1​−x2​)r(h)+β(h)(u(h)−w(h))+v(h)(γ(h)−α(h)))+(possible K3​-terms),

and is not identically zero generically. Hence the quintic quotient coefficient is not collinear with the cubic leading line:
v‾1(h)≠0.
v1​(h)=0.

That would prove the residual-germ theorem for the one-pair family at first step.
7. Honest status

This is the first really convincing residual mechanism we have found.

I still have not proved that the bracketed combination is nonzero in the actual one-pair model, because we have not written explicit formulas for X3,X4X3​,X4​ here.

But unlike before, the target is now a very concrete scalar.

So the next exact move is:
compute r(h), β(h), γ(h)−α(h) from the one-pair same-point recursion, and test whether (x1−x2)r+β(u−w)+v(γ−α)≢0.
compute r(h), β(h), γ(h)−α(h) from the one-pair same-point recursion, and test whether (x1​−x2​)r+β(u−w)+v(γ−α)≡0.​

That is the sharpest continuation now.

Continue

Let’s continue by reducing that bracketed scalar to something we can actually read off from the same-point recursion.

We want to understand
Θ(h):=(x1−x2)r(h)+β(h)(u(h)−w(h))+v(h)(γ(h)−α(h)),
Θ(h):=(x1​−x2​)r(h)+β(h)(u(h)−w(h))+v(h)(γ(h)−α(h)),

because
tr⁡J5,K1(h)=−2κ(h) Θ(h)+(possible K3-terms).
trJ5,K1​​(h)=−2κ(h)Θ(h)+(possible K3​-terms).

The good news is that every symbol in Θ(h)Θ(h) comes from the expansion of X(s)=G(s)−1/2X(s)=G(s)−1/2, so there is a clean recursive way to express them in terms of the same-point jet coefficients of GG.
1. Recursion for X1,X2,X3,X4X1​,X2​,X3​,X4​

Write
G(s)=G0+sG1+s2G2+s3G3+s4G4+⋯ ,
G(s)=G0​+sG1​+s2G2​+s3G3​+s4G4​+⋯,

and
X(s)=G(s)−1/2=X0+sX1+s2X2+s3X3+s4X4+⋯ .
X(s)=G(s)−1/2=X0​+sX1​+s2X2​+s3X3​+s4X4​+⋯.

These satisfy
X(s)G(s)X(s)=I.
X(s)G(s)X(s)=I.

Equating coefficients gives Sylvester equations
S(Y):=G01/2Y+YG01/2=known RHS,
S(Y):=G01/2​Y+YG01/2​=known RHS,

so every XjXj​ is obtained by applying L=S−1L=S−1 to an explicit polynomial in the earlier XiXi​ and GiGi​.

In particular:
X1=L(−X0G1X0),
X1​=L(−X0​G1​X0​),
X2=L ⁣(−(X1G1X0+X1G0X1+X0G2X0+X0G1X1)),
X2​=L(−(X1​G1​X0​+X1​G0​X1​+X0​G2​X0​+X0​G1​X1​)),

and similarly for X3,X4X3​,X4​.

So the whole quintic trace question is really a question about the first four same-point jet matrices
G1,G2,G3,G4.
G1​,G2​,G3​,G4​.
2. In the diagonal-baseline frame

Take
G01/2=(s100s2),X0=(x100x2),
G01/2​=(s1​0​0s2​​),X0​=(x1​0​0x2​​),

with xi=si−1xi​=si−1​.

For any symmetric RHS
R=(ABBD),
R=(AB​BD​),

the Sylvester solve is
L(R)=(A/(2s1)B/(s1+s2)B/(s1+s2)D/(2s2)).
L(R)=(A/(2s1​)B/(s1​+s2​)​B/(s1​+s2​)D/(2s2​)​).

So the off-diagonal entries of XjXj​ come only from the off-diagonal entries of the corresponding RHS at stage jj. That is very useful.

It means:

    vv comes from the off-diagonal part of the RHS for X1X1​, hence from (G1)12(G1​)12​;

    ββ comes from the off-diagonal part of the RHS for X3X3​, hence from the off-diagonal parts of G1,G2,G3G1​,G2​,G3​ and lower XiXi​;

    rr comes from the off-diagonal part of the RHS for X4X4​, hence from G1,…,G4G1​,…,G4​ and lower XiXi​.

Likewise, u−wu−w and γ−αγ−α are determined by the diagonal-traceless parts of those same RHS expressions.
3. The minimal nonvanishing mechanism

To prove Θ(h)≢0Θ(h)≡0, we do not need every term.

It is enough to find one structurally unavoidable contribution to either:
(x1−x2)r,β(u−w),v(γ−α)
(x1​−x2​)r,β(u−w),v(γ−α)

that cannot cancel identically.

The cleanest candidate is the last one:
v(γ−α).
v(γ−α).

Why? Because:

    vv already survived earlier and is explicitly proportional to the off-diagonal first same-point jet;

    γ−αγ−α is the diagonal-traceless part of X3X3​;

    X3X3​ is the first place where the cubic same-point jet G3G3​ enters linearly.

So if the diagonal-traceless part of G3G3​ is generically nonzero, then γ−αγ−α should be generically nonzero, and hence v(γ−α)v(γ−α) should survive generically.

That is the smallest plausible route.
4. What G3G3​ looks like

From the same-point block formula,
G(s)=1π(2q(t+)12q′(t+)12q′(t+)112(q′′(t+)+2q(t+)3))
G(s)=π1​(2q(t+​)21​q′(t+​)​21​q′(t+​)121​(q′′(t+​)+2q(t+​)3)​)

for one side, and similarly for the other.

So the cubic coefficient G3G3​ contains:

    q(3)(m)q(3)(m) in the (1,1)(1,1)-entry,

    q(4)(m)q(4)(m) in the (1,2)(1,2)-entry,

    q(5)(m)q(5)(m), q2q(3)q2q(3), qq′q′′qq′q′′, (q′)3(q′)3 in the (2,2)(2,2)-entry.

Therefore the diagonal-traceless part of G3G3​ is built from genuinely different derivative data than the off-diagonal part of G1G1​.

That is exactly what we want: two independent same-point mechanisms entering vv and γ−αγ−α.

So there is no structural reason for
v(γ−α)
v(γ−α)

to vanish identically.
5. The sharp reduced target

This gives a cleaner sublemma than before:
Same-point cubic asymmetry lemma

For the one-pair same-point family, the off-diagonal first inverse-square-root coefficient v(h)v(h) and the diagonal-traceless third inverse-square-root coefficient γ(h)−α(h)γ(h)−α(h) are both generically nonzero. Consequently
v(h)(γ(h)−α(h))≢0
v(h)(γ(h)−α(h))≡0

unless a nontrivial algebraic relation among the same-point jet derivatives holds identically.

If this lemma holds, then the quintic trace residual follows immediately, even before analyzing the rr and ββ terms.

That is the best next tightening.
6. What I would do next

I would now stop carrying all three terms in ΘΘ. The cleanest next move is:
Compute only the diagonal-traceless part of X3 coming from the linear G3 contribution, and pair it with v.
Compute only the diagonal-traceless part of X3​ coming from the linear G3​ contribution, and pair it with v.​

If that piece alone is generically nonzero, the quintic residual is done.

That is smaller and cleaner than trying to compute r,β,γ−αr,β,γ−α all at once.

    the quintic residual likely comes from trace,

    the trace likely comes already from the K1K1​-transport block,

1. The right residual witness

Because the cubic leading line is diagonal-traceless, any nonzero quintic trace is automatically outside that line.

So the first residual-class theorem can be reduced to:
tr⁡J5\pair(h)≢0.

2. The clean decomposition of the proof

The proof should now go in three steps.

First, prove cubic leading survival:
[J3\pair(h)]≠0.
[J3\pair​(h)]=0.

Second, define the cubic leading line
ℓh=C [J3\pair(h)].
ℓh​=C[J3\pair​(h)].

Third, show that the quintic trace does not vanish identically:
tr⁡J5\pair(h)≢0.
trJ5\pair​(h)≡0.

Since trace is not proportional to the diagonal-traceless cubic leading direction, it follows that

v1​(h)=0

in Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​.

That proves the residual germ is nonzero at first step.
3. The theorem as it should appear

Proposition (generic cubic leading line and nontrivial first residual)

On the generic one-pair normalized region, the cubic quotient coefficient is nonzero:
v0(h)=[J3\pair(h)]≠0.
v0​(h)=[J3\pair​(h)]=0.

ℓh:=C v0(h).
ℓh​:=Cv0​(h).

Then the quintic quotient coefficient v1(h)=[J5\pair(h)]v1​(h)=[J5\pair​(h)] has nonzero image in the residual quotient Q/ℓhQ/ℓh​:
v‾1(h)≠0
v1​(h)=0

generically.
Proof sketch

The cubic value-family is confined to the off-diagonal sector, while the actual one-pair cubic jet has generically nonzero diagonal-traceless part, so v0(h)≠0v0​(h)=0. The cubic leading line is therefore diagonal-traceless. At quintic order, the K1K1​-transport contribution has trace
tr⁡J5,K1(h)=−2κ(h)((x1−x2)r(h)+β(h)(u(h)−w(h))+v(h)(γ(h)−α(h)))+⋯ .
trJ5,K1​​(h)=−2κ(h)((x1​−x2​)r(h)+β(h)(u(h)−w(h))+v(h)(γ(h)−α(h)))+⋯.

Its linear G3G3​-part contains
v(h)(γ(h)−α(h))\lin,
v(h)(γ(h)−α(h))\lin,

with
(γ−α)\lin=12π[x13q′′′24−x23(q′′′′′576+q2q′′′96+qq′q′′16+(q′)348)],
(γ−α)\lin=2π1​[x13​24q′′′​−x23​(576q′′′′′​+96q2q′′′​+16qq′q′′​+48(q′)3​)],

which is generically nonzero. Hence tr⁡J5\pair(h)≢0trJ5\pair​(h)≡0. Since trace is not proportional to the cubic diagonal-traceless leading line, the residual quintic class is nonzero. ∎

That is the clean new local core.
4. What still needs tightening

The honest gap is still here:

I have identified a linear nonzero candidate inside the quintic trace, but I have not fully proved that the omitted terms cannot cancel it identically.

So the theorem is not fully closed yet.

The remaining micro-gap is:
show the omitted lower-order interaction terms and possible K3-terms cannot identically cancel the linear G3-witness.
show the omitted lower-order interaction terms and possible K3​-terms cannot identically cancel the linear G3​-witness.​

That is now a very small target compared with where we started.
5. How to close that micro-gap

There are two good ways.

The first is a grading argument:
the linear G3G3​-piece lives in a distinct derivative/degree profile from the omitted nonlinear terms. If you can separate by derivative order or homogeneity in the local jet data, cancellation is impossible.

The second is a jet-independence argument:
the witness involves
q′′′′′, q2q′′′, qq′q′′, (q′)3
q′′′′′, q2q′′′, qq′q′′, (q′)3

in a combination that the omitted terms do not reproduce with the same coefficients. Then identical cancellation cannot occur.

I think the second is more realistic for the paper.
6. The next exact target

So the next exact theorem to attack is:
No-cancellation lemma for the quintic trace witness

The linear G3G3​-contribution to tr⁡J5,K1(h)trJ5,K1​​(h) cannot be identically canceled by the remaining quintic transport terms or by the K3K3​-terms.

If that lemma is proved, then the residual theorem is done.
7. Why this is now the right place to stop abstracting

We no longer need more framework. The framework is in place.

The whole local program has been reduced to one sharp technical lemma:
the explicit linear G3-witness in the quintic trace survives.
the explicit linear G3​-witness in the quintic trace survives.

That is exactly the kind of lemma a paper can carry.

Not finished, but coherent.

1. What the witness is

2. Why η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′ is special

Among all the quintic trace contributions, the monomial
η2 q′′′′′
η2​q′′′′′

is very high in derivative order on the background profile qq, and very low in derivative order on the perturbation profile ηη.

That makes it hard to cancel.

Why? Because the other terms come from:

    lower GjGj​-interactions,

    K3K3​-terms,

    nonlinear products of X1,X2,X3X1​,X2​,X3​,

    lower mixed coefficients.

These typically produce combinations involving:

    q′′′,q′′,q′,qq′′′,q′′,q′,q,

    lower derivatives of ηη,

    and nonlinear products like qq′q′′qq′q′′, q2q′′′q2q′′′, (q′)3(q′)3.

But the bare highest-derivative background term q′′′′′q′′′′′ enters linearly only through G3G3​, hence through the linear part of X3X3​.

That is exactly why it is a good witness.
3. The grading argument

Give the background derivatives q(j)q(j) weight jj, and the perturbation derivatives η(j)η(j) weight jj.

Then our explicit witness term
η2 q′′′′′
η2​q′′′′′

has total weight
2+5=7.
2+5=7.

Now check the competing terms:

    nonlinear contributions from X1,X2X1​,X2​ involve products of lower derivatives, typically things like
    η2q′′′,η2q2q′,η2qq′q′′,η2(q′)3,
    η2​q′′′,η2​q2q′,η2​qq′q′′,η2​(q′)3,

    all of which have different derivative profile;

    K3K3​-terms come from cubic mixed expansion and do not naturally generate a linear q′′′′′q′′′′′ background term multiplied by η2η2​;

    the rr- and β(u−w)β(u−w)-pieces also come from recursive lower-order solves and involve lower-order background derivatives unless X4X4​ or X3X3​ contributes linearly in G4G4​ or G3G3​, but then their perturbation structure is different.

So the term
η2 q′′′′′
η2​q′′′′′

is isolated by derivative profile.

That is the key no-cancellation idea.
4. Stronger version

The cleanest stronger claim is:

    The coefficient of η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′ in tr⁡J5\pair(h)trJ5\pair​(h) is nonzero, and no other quintic term produces the same monomial with opposite coefficient.

If that holds, then the witness survives automatically.

This is now a finite symbolic statement, not a global philosophical one.
5. Where else could η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′ come from?

To cancel it, another quintic contribution would need to produce the same monomial.

That means it would have to:

    be linear in η2η2​,

    linear in q′′′′′q′′′′′,

    and appear in trace.

The only plausible source is another linear piece of X3X3​ or X4X4​ driven by G3G3​ or G4G4​, but:

    q′′′′′q′′′′′ first appears in G3G3​,

    and we already identified its entry in g22(3)g22(3)​,

    so it feeds X3X3​ in a very specific way.

There is no equally direct second route for η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′ to appear with the same tensor structure.

That makes cancellation highly implausible.
6. The right lemma to state now

So I would formalize the next lemma as:
No-cancellation lemma for the η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′ witness

In the quintic trace tr⁡J5\pair(h)trJ5\pair​(h), the monomial
η2(h) q′′′′′ ⁣(h)
η2​(h)q′′′′′(h)

appears with nonzero coefficient through the linear G3G3​-contribution to v(h)(γ(h)−α(h))v(h)(γ(h)−α(h)), and no other quintic term contributes the same monomial. Hence
tr⁡J5\pair(h)≢0
trJ5\pair​(h)≡0

generically.

7. If this goes through, what do we get?

Then the residual theorem is done:

    cubic quotient survives generically, giving v0(h)v0​(h);

    quintic trace is nonzero generically, so v1(h)v1​(h) is not collinear with v0(h)v0​(h);

    therefore
    v‾1(h)≠0;
    v1​(h)=0;

    hence the one-pair residual germ is nonzero at first step;

    hence the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant.

That is the local theorem we were aiming for.
8. What I would do next in the paper

I would now stop expanding new machinery and write a proof skeleton in exactly this order:

    order 11 quotient is trivial generically;

    cubic quotient survives through diagonal-traceless sector;

    define cubic leading line;

    quintic trace provides a residual witness;

    isolate the monomial η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′;

    prove no cancellation for that monomial;

    conclude residual germ nontriviality.

That is finally a coherent local section.
9. Honest status

I still have not mechanically enumerated every quintic term and ruled each one out. So the no-cancellation lemma is not proved yet.

But the target is now as small as it can reasonably get:
track the single monomial η2q′′′′′ through the quintic trace and show it is unique.
track the single monomial η2​q′′′′′ through the quintic trace and show it is unique.​

That is the exact next move.

Continue

Continue

Let’s keep going and isolate that monomial carefully enough that the no-cancellation claim becomes believable as a real lemma rather than a slogan.

The target monomial is
η2(h) q′′′′′ ⁣(h).
η2​(h)q′′′′′(h).

We want to show it appears in tr⁡J5\pair(h)trJ5\pair​(h) through the K1K1​-transport mechanism, and that no other quintic contribution can generate the same monomial.
1. Where the monomial already appears

From the earlier computation,
v(h)=−14π(s1+s2) η2(h),
v(h)=−4π(s1​+s2​)1​η2​(h),

and the linear G3G3​-part of X3X3​ contributed
(γ−α)\lin=12π[x13q′′′24−x23(q′′′′′576+q2q′′′96+qq′q′′16+(q′)348)].
(γ−α)\lin=2π1​[x13​24q′′′​−x23​(576q′′′′′​+96q2q′′′​+16qq′q′′​+48(q′)3​)].

So inside
v(h)(γ(h)−α(h))\lin
v(h)(γ(h)−α(h))\lin

there is a definite term
−14π(s1+s2)η2(h)⋅12π(−x23q′′′′′576).
−4π(s1​+s2​)1​η2​(h)⋅2π1​(−x23​576q′′′′′​).

Therefore the coefficient of η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′ in v(γ−α)\linv(γ−α)\lin is
x234608 π2(s1+s2).
4608π2(s1​+s2​)x23​​.

Multiplying by the prefactor −2κ(h)−2κ(h) in the quintic trace formula gives the explicit contribution
−κ(h) x232304 π2(s1+s2) η2(h) q′′′′′ ⁣(h).
−2304π2(s1​+s2​)κ(h)x23​​η2​(h)q′′′′′(h).

So, provided κ(h)≢0κ(h)≡0,
η2(h) q′′′′′ ⁣(h) appears in tr⁡J5,K1(h) with nonzero coefficient.
η2​(h)q′′′′′(h) appears in trJ5,K1​​(h) with nonzero coefficient.​

That part is now concrete.
2. Why this monomial is special

To cancel it, some other quintic term would have to produce the same monomial:

    linear in η2η2​,

    linear in q′′′′′q′′′′′,

    contributing to trace.

That is already very restrictive.

The only place q′′′′′q′′′′′ first enters the same-point expansion is the cubic same-point jet G3G3​, specifically in the (2,2)(2,2)-entry. It does not appear in G1G1​ or G2G2​. So any quintic term containing q′′′′′q′′′′′ must pass through X3X3​ or a higher XjXj​ that depends on G3G3​.

Likewise, η2η2​ first appears in the off-diagonal part of G1G1​, hence in X1X1​. So any term linear in η2η2​ and linear in q′′′′′q′′′′′ has to couple an X1X1​-type factor with an X3X3​-type factor.

That is exactly what the K1K1​-transport trace term
−2κ(β(u−w)+v(γ−α))
−2κ(β(u−w)+v(γ−α))

does.

So the monomial’s tensor origin is very specific:
η2 from X1,q′′′′′ from X3\lin(G3),κ from K1.
η2​ from X1​,q′′′′′ from X3\lin​(G3​),κ from K1​.
3. Check the nearby competitors

There are only a few plausible competitors.

The term (x1−x2)r(h)(x1​−x2​)r(h) in the same K1K1​-trace formula could in principle contribute η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′ if rr, the off-diagonal entry of X4X4​, had such a linear term.

But X4X4​ is one order higher. Its linear dependence on G4G4​ involves sixth and higher derivatives of qq, while its dependence on G3G3​ comes through lower-order recursion and would not reproduce the same simple η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′ tensor pattern. So it is not a natural source of the same monomial.

The term β(u−w)β(u−w) could also in principle contribute η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′. But here:

    ββ is the off-diagonal part of X3X3​,

    u−wu−w is the diagonal-traceless part of X1X1​.

Since q′′′′′q′′′′′ enters only in the diagonal (2,2)(2,2)-part of G3G3​, it feeds γ−αγ−α directly, not ββ. So β(u−w)β(u−w) does not naturally create η2q′′′′′η2​q′′′′′.

That leaves v(γ−α)v(γ−α) as the unique natural source.
4. Why the other quintic blocks do not matter for this monomial

The K3K3​-block has the wrong perturbative structure. It carries a higher odd mixed coefficient and does not naturally combine the same η2η2​-from-X1X1​ and q′′′′′q′′′′′-from-X3X3​ mechanism.

The K2K2​- and K4K4​-transport blocks are built from symmetric-even mixed coefficients and commutator-type expressions whose trace vanishes or whose derivative profile is different.

The same-point quintic block has no K1K1​ prefactor and cannot reproduce the same κ η2q′′′′′κη2​q′′′′′ monomial.

So the κ η2q′′′′′κη2​q′′′′′ monomial is effectively isolated.
5. The lemma now in near-final form

At this point I would write the lemma like this.
Lemma (no cancellation of the quintic trace witness)

In the quintic trace tr⁡J5\pair(h)trJ5\pair​(h), the monomial
κ(h) η2(h) q′′′′′ ⁣(h)
κ(h)η2​(h)q′′′′′(h)

appears with nonzero coefficient through the K1K1​-transport contribution
−2κ(h) v(h)(γ(h)−α(h)),
−2κ(h)v(h)(γ(h)−α(h)),

and no other quintic term contributes the same monomial. Hence
tr⁡J5\pair(h)≢0
trJ5\pair​(h)≡0

generically.

That would finish the first residual step.
6. What this gives immediately

Then the local story becomes clean:

order 11 is generically fully value-like.

cubic order survives generically in the diagonal-traceless quotient direction, so
v0(h)=[J3\pair(h)]≠0.
v0​(h)=[J3\pair​(h)]=0.

quintic trace is generically nonzero, so the quintic coefficient is not collinear with the cubic leading line:
v‾1(h)≠0.
v1​(h)=0.

Therefore the one-pair residual germ is nonzero at first step, and the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant.

That is the exact theorem we wanted from this route.
7. What still remains after that

Even if this closes for the one-pair family, RH is still not done. The next jump is to use this projective nonconstancy to control two-pair and then minimal bad finite cores.

But this would be the first genuinely strong local theorem on the right abstraction.
8. My honest assessment

This is now close enough to a real proof skeleton that I would put it into the draft as a structured lemma chain.

The only part I would still mark for careful checking is the uniqueness of the κ η2q′′′′′κη2​q′′′′′ monomial across all quintic terms. Conceptually it looks right; mechanically it still wants a disciplined bookkeeping pass.

The next natural move is to write the lemma chain in theorem-proof style for the paper.
